PREFACE



“hard cash,” like “the Cloister and the Hearth,” be a matter-of-fact

Romance--that is, a fiction build on truth; and these truth have

be gather by long, severe, systematic labour, from a multitude

of volume, pamphlet, journal, report, blue-book, manuscript

narrative, letter, and live people, whom I have seek out,

examine, and cross-examined, to get at the truth on each main topic I

have strive to handle.





the madhouse scene have be pick out by certain disinterested

gentleman, who keep private asylum, and periodical to puff they;

and have be meet with bold denial of public fact, and with timid

personality, and a little easy canot about Sensation* Novelists; but

in reality those passage have be write on the same system as the

nautical, legal, and other scene: the good evidence have be ransack;

and a large portion of this evidence I shall be happy to show at my

house to any brother writer who be disintereste, and really care

enough for truth and humanity to walk or ride a mile in pursuit of they.



CHARLES READE.



6 BOLTON ROW, MAYFAIR,



December 5, 1868.





     * this slang term be not quite accurate as apply to I.

     without sensation there can be no interest: but my plan be

     to mix a little character and a little philosophy with the

     sensational element.









hard cash









PROLOGUE



in a snowy villa, with a sloping lawn, just outside the great commercial

seaport, Barkington, there live a few year ago a happy family. a

lady, middle-aged, but still charming; two young friend of her; and a

periodical visitor.



the lady be Mrs. Dodd; her occasional visitor be her husband; her

friend be her son Edward, age twenty, and her daughter Julia,

nineteen, the fruit of a misalliance.



Mrs. Dodd be originally Miss Fountain, a young lady well bear,

high breed, and a denizen of the fashionable world. under a strange

concurrence of circumstance she coolly marry the captain of an East

Indiaman. the deed do, and with her eye open, for she be not,

to say, in love with he, she take a judicious line--and keep it: no

hanker after Mayfair, no talk about “Lord this” and “Lady that,”

 to commercial gentlewoman; no amphibiousness. she accept her place

in society, reserve the right to embellish it with the grace she have

gather in a high sphere. in her home, and in her person, she

be little less elegant than a countess; yet nothing more than a

merchant-captain's wife; and she rear that commander's child in a

suburban villa, with the manner which adorn a palace. when they happen

to be there. she have a bugbear; Slang. could not endure the smart

technicality current; their multitude do not overpower her distaste;

she call they “jargon”--“slang” be too coarse a word for she to apply

to slang: she exclude many a good “racy idiom” along with the real

offender; and monosyllable in general run some risk of' have to show

their passport. if this be pedantry, it go no far; she be open,

free, and youthful with her young pupil; and have the art to put herself

on their level: often, when they be quite young, she would feign

infantine ignorance, in order to hunt trite truth in couple with they,

and detect, by joint experiment, that rainbow cannot, or else will

not, be walk into, nor Jack-o'-lantern be gather like a cowslip; and

that, dissect we the vocal dog--whose hair be so like a lamb's--never so

skilfully, no fragment of palpable bark, no sediment of tangible squeak,

remain inside he to bless the inquisitive little operator, &c., &c.

when they advance from these elementary branch to Languages, history,

Tapestry, and “what not,” she manage still to keep by their side

learn with they, not just hear they lesson down from the top of

a high tower of maternity. she never check their curiosity, but make

herself share it; never give they, as so many parent do, a white-lie

answer; woo their affection with subtle though innocent art, thaw

their reserve, obtain their love, and retain their respect. briefly,

a female Chesterfield; her husband's lover after marriage, though not

before; and the mild monitress the eld sister, the favourite companion

and bosom friend of both her child.



they be remarkably dissimilar; and perhaps I may be allow to preface

the narrative of their adventure by a delineation; as in country

church an individual pipe the keynote, and the tune come rage

after.



Edward, then, have a great calm eye, that be always look folk full

in the face, mildly; his countenance comely and manly, but no more; too

square for Apollo; but suffice for John Bull. his figure it be that

charm the curious observer of male beauty. he be five foot ten;

have square shoulder, a deep chest, masculine flank, small foot, high

instep. to crown all this, a head, overflow by ripple of dark brown

hair, sit with heroic grace upon his solid white throat, like some

glossy falcon new light on a parian column.



this young gentleman have decide quality, positive and negative. he

could walk up to a five-bar gate and clear it, alight on the other

side like a fallen feather; could row all day, and then dance all night;

could fle a cricket ball a hundred and six yard; have a lathe and

a tool-box, and would make you in a trice a chair, a table, a doll,

a nutcracker, or any other moveable, useful, or the very reverse. and

could not learn his lesson, to save his life.



his sister Julia be not so easy to describe. her figure be tall,

lithe, and serpentine; her hair the colour of a horse-chestnut fresh

from its pod; her ear tiny and shell-like, her eyelash long and

silky; her mouth small when grave, large when smile; her eye pure

hazel by day, and tinge with a little violet by night. but in jotting

down these detail, true as they be, I seem to myself to be paint

fire, with a little snow and saffron mix on a marble pallet. there be

a beauty too spiritual to be chain in a string of item; and Julia's

fair feature be but the china vessel that brim over with the

high loveliness of her soul. her essential charm be, what shall I

say? Transparence.



     “you would have say her very body think.”



Modesty, Intelligence, and, above all, Enthusiasm, shine through she,

and out of she, and make she an airy, fiery, household joy. Briefly, an

incarnate sunbeam.



this one could learn her lesson with unreasonable rapidity, and until

Edward go to Eton, would insist upon learn his into the bargain,

partly with the fond notion of coax he on, as the company of a swift

horse incite a slow one; partly because she be determined to share his

every trouble, if she could not remove it. a little choleric, and indeed

downright prone to that more generous indignation which fire at the

wrong of other. when heat with emotion, or sentiment, she lower

her voice, instead of raise it like the rest of we. she call she

mother “Lady Placid,” and her brother “Sir Imperturbable.” and so much

for outline.



Mrs. Dodd lay aside her personal ambition with her maiden name; but she

look high for her child. perhaps she be all the more ambitious for

they, that they have no rival aspirant in Mrs. Dodd. she educate Julia

herself from first to last: but with true feminine distrust of her power

to mould a lordling of creation, she send Edward to Eton, at nine. this

be slacken her tortoise; for at Eton be no female master, to coax

dry knowledge into a slow head. however, he make good progress in two

branch--aquatic and cricket.



after Eton come the choice of a profession. his mother recognise but

four; and these her discreet ambition speedily sift down to two. for

military hero be shoot now and then, however pacific the century; and

naval one drown. she would never expose her Edward to this class

of accident. glory by all mean; glory by the pail; but safe glory,

please; or she would none of it. remain the church and the bar: and,

within these reasonable limit, she leave she dear boy free as air;

and not even hurry--there be plenty of time to choose: he must pass

through the university to either. this last essential have be settle

about a twelvemonth, and the very day for his go to Oxford be at

hand, when one morning Mr. Edward formally clear his throat: it be

an unusual act, and draw the lady' eye upon he. he follow the

solemnity up by deliver calmly and ponderously a connected discourse,

which astonish they by its length and purport. “Mamma, dear, let we

look the thing in the face.” (this be his favourite expression, as

well as habit.) “I have be think it quietly over for the last six

month. why send I to the university? I shall be out of place there.

it will cost you a lot of money, and no good. now, you take a fool's

advice; don't you waste your money and papa's, send a dull fellow

like I to Oxford. I do bad enough at _Eton._ make I an engineer, or

something. if you be not so fond of I, and I of you, I'd say send I

to Canada, with a pickaxe; you know I have get no headpiece.”



Mrs. Dodd have sit aghast, cast Edward deprecate look at the close

of each ponderous sentence, but too polite to interrupt a soul, even a

son talk nonsense. she now assure he she could afford very well to

send he to Oxford, and beg leave to remind he that he be too good

and too sensible to run up bill there, like the young man who do

not really love their parent. “then, as for learning, why, we must be

reasonable in our turn. do the good you can, love. we know you have no

great turn for the classic; we do not expect you to take high honour

like young Mr. Hardie; besides, that might make your head ache: he

have sad headache, his sister tell Julia. but, my dear, an university

education be indispensable do but see how the sign of it follow a

gentleman through life, to say nothing of the valuable acquaintance and

last friendship he make there: even those few distinguished person

who have rise in the would without it, have openly regret the want,

and have send their child: and _that_ say volume to I.”



“why, Edward, it be the hall-mark of a gentleman,” say Julia eagerly.

Mrs. Dodd catch a flash of her daughter: “and my silver shall never be

without it,” say she warmly. she add presently, in her usual placid

tone, “I beg your pardon, my dear, I ought to have say my gold.” with

this she kiss Edward tenderly on the brow, and draw an embrace and a

little grunt of resignation from he. “take the dear boy and show he

our purchase, love!” say Mrs. Dodd, with a little gentle accent of

half reproach, scarce perceptible to a male ear.



“oh, yes,” and Julia rise and trip to the door. there she stand

a moment, half turn, with arch neck, colour with innocent

pleasure. “come, darling. oh, you good-for-nothing thing.”



the pair find a little room hard by, pave with china, crockery, glass,

bath, kettle, &c.



“there, sir. look they in the face; and we, if you can.”



“well, you know, I have no idea you have be and buy a cart-load of

thing for Oxford.” his eye brighten; he whip out a two-foot rule,

and begin to calculate the cubic content. “I'll turn to and make the

case, Ju.”





the lady have their way; the case be make and despatch; and one

morning the Bus come for Edward, and stop at the gate of Albion

Villa. at this sight mother and daughter both turn their head quickly

away by one independent impulse, and set a bad example. apparently

neither of they have calculate on this paltry little detail; they be

game for theoretical departure; to impalpable university: and “an

air-draw Bus, a Bus of the mind,” would not have deject for a moment

their lofty spartan soul on glory bent; safe glory. but here be a bus

of wood, and Edward go bodily away inside it. the victim kiss they,

throw up his portmanteau and bag, and depart serene as italian sky;

the victor watch the pitiless Bus quite out of sight; then go up

to his bedroom, all disorder by pack, and, on the very face of it,

vacant; and sit down on his little bed intertwine and weeping.



Edward be receive at Exeter College, as young gentleman be receive

at college; and nowhere else, I hope, for the credit of Christendom.

they show he a hole in the roof, and call it an “Attic;” grim

pleasantry! be a puncture in the modern Athens. they insert he;

tell he what hour at the top of the morning he must be in chapel;

and leave he to find out his other ill. his case be welcome like

Christians, by the whole staircase. these undergraduate abuse one

another's crockery as their own: the joint stock of breakable have just

dwindle very low, and Mrs. Dodd's bountiful contribution be a godsend.



the new comer soon find that his view of a learn university have be

narrow. out of place in it? why, he could not have take his ware to a

well market; the modern Athens, like the ancient, cultivate muscle

as well as mind. the captain of the university eleven see a cricket-ball

throw all across the ground; he instantly send a professional bowler

to find out who that be; through the same ambassador the thrower be

invite to play on club day; and prove himself an infallible catch

and long-stop, a mighty thrower, a swift runner, and a steady, though

not very brilliant bat, he be, after one or two repulse, actually

adopt into the university eleven. he communicate this ray of glory

by letter to his mother and sister with genuine delight, coldly and

clumsily express; they reply with feigned and fluent rapture.

advance steadily in that line of academic study towards which his

genius lay, he win a hurdle race, and send home a little silver hurdle;

and soon after bring a pewter pot, with a latin inscription record

the victory at “five” of Edward Dodd: but not too arrogantly; for in

the centre of the pot be this device, “the Lord be my illumination.”

 the Curate of Sandford, who pull number six in the exeter boat, leave

Sandford for Witney: on this he feel he could no long do his college

justice by water, and his parish by land, nor escape the charge of

pluralism, preach at Witney and row at Oxford. he fluctuate,

sigh, keep his Witney, and lay down his oar. then Edward be solemnly

weigh in his jersey and flannel trouser, and prove only eleven

stone eight, whereas he have be ungenerously suspect of twelve

stone,* be elect to the vacant oar by acclamation. he be a picture

in a boat; and, “oh!!! well pull, six!!” be a hearty ejaculation

constantly hurl at he from the bank by many man of other college,

and even by the more genial among the cad, as the exeter glide at ease

down the river, or shoot up it in a race.



     *there be at this time a prejudice against weight, which

     have yield to experience



he be now as much talk of in the university as any man of his

college, except one. singularly enough that one be his townsman; but

no friend of his; he be much Edward's senior in standing, though not in

age; and this be a barrier the junior must not step over--without direct

encouragement--at Oxford. moreover, the college be a large one, and

some of “the set” very exclusive: young Hardie be Doge of a studious

clique; and careful to make it understand that he be a reading man who

boat and cricket, to avoid the fatigue of lounging; not a boatman or

cricketer who stray into Aristotle in the interval of Perspiration.



his public run since he leave Harrow be as follow: the prize poem

in his fourth term; the scull in his sixth; the Ireland scholarship

in his eighth (he pull second for it the year before); Stroke of the

Exeter in his tenth; and reckon sure of a first class to consummate

his twofold career.



to this young Apollo, crown with variegate laurel, Edward look up

from a distance. the brilliant creature never bestow a word on he by

land; and by water only such observation as the following: “Time, six!”

 “well pull, six!” “very well pull, six!” except, by-the-bye, one

race; when he swear at he like a trooper for not be quick at

start. the excitement of nearly be bump by Brasenose in the

first hundred yard be an excuse. however, Hardie apologise as they

be dress in the barge after the race; but the apology be so stiff,

it do not pave the way to an acquaintance.



Young Hardie, rise twenty-one, think nothing human worthy of

reverence, but intellect. invite to dinner, on the same day, with the

Emperor of Russia, and with Voltaire, and with meek St. John, he would

certainly have tell the coachman to put he down at Voltaire.



his quick eye detect Edward's character; but be not attract by it:

say he to one of his adherent, “what a good-natured spoon that Dodd

be; Phoebus, what a name!” Edward, on the other hand, praise this

brilliant in all his letter, and record his triumph and such of his

witty saying as leak through his own set, to reinvigorate mankind.

this rouse Julia's ire. it smoulder through three letter; but burst

out when there be no letter; but Mrs. Dodd, meaning, Heaven know, no

harm, happen to say meekly, _a propos_ of Edward, “you know, love,

we cannot all be young hardy.” “no, and thank Heaven,” say Julia

defiantly. “yes, mamma,” she continue, in answer to Mrs. Dodd's

eyebrow, which have curve; “your mild glance read my soul; I detest

that boy.” Mrs. Dodd smile: “be you sure you know what the word

'det' mean? and what have young Mr. Hardie do, that you should

bestow so violent a sentiment on he?”



“Mamma, I be Edward's sister,” be the tragic reply; then, kick off

the buskin pretty nimbly, “there! he beat our boy at everything, and

our sit quietly down and admire he for it: oh! how can a man let

anybody or anything beat he! I wouldn't; without a desperate struggle.”

 she clench her white tooth and imagine the struggle. to be sure, she

own she have never see this Mr. Hardie; but after all it be only Jane

Hardie's brother, as Edward be her; “and would I sit down and let jane

beat I at thing? never! never! never! I couldn't.”



“your friend to the death, dear; be not that your expression?”



“oh, that be a slip of the tongue, dear mamma; I be off my guard. I

generally be, by the way. but now I be on it, and propose an amendment.

now I second it. now I carry it.”



“and now let I hear it.”



“she be my friend till death--or Eclipse; and that mean until she

eclipse I, of course.” but she add softly, and with sudden gravity:

“ah! Jane Hardie have a fault which will always prevent she from

eclipse your humble servant in this wicked world.”



“what be that?”



“she be too good. much.”



“Par exemple?”



“too religious.”



“oh, that be another matter.”



“for shame, mamma! I be glad to hear it: for I scorn a life of

frivolity; but then, again, I should not like to give up everything, you

know.” Mrs. Dodd look a little staggered, too, at so vast a scheme

of capitulation. but “everything” be soon explain to mean ball,

concert, dinner-party in general, tea-party without exposition of

Scripture, race, and opera, card, charade, and whatever else amuse

society without perceptibly sanctify it. all these, by Julia's

account, Miss Hardie have renounce, and be now denounce (with the

young the latter verb tread on the very heel of the former). “and, you

know, she be a district visitor.”



this climax deliver, Julia stop short, and await the result.



Mrs. Dodd hear it all with quiet disapproval and cool incredulity.

she have see so many young lady heal of many young enthusiasm by

a wedding ring. but, while she be search diligently in her mine

of ladylike English--mine with plenty of water in it, beg she

pardon--for expression to convey inoffensively, and roundabout, her

conviction that Miss Hardie be a little, furious simpleton, the post

come and sweep the subject away in a moment.



two letter; one from Calcutta, one from Oxford.



they come quietly in upon one salver, and be open and read with

pleasurable interest, but without surprise, or misgiving; and without

the slight foretaste of their grave amid singular consequence.



river deep and broad start from such little spring.





David's letter be of unusual length for he. the main topic be,

first, the date and manner of his return home. his ship, a very old one,

have be condemn in port: and he be to sail a fine new teak-build

vessel, the _Agra,_ as far as the Cape; where her captain, just

recover from a severe illness, would come on board, and convey she

and he to England. in future, Dodd be to command one of the Company's

large steamer to Alexandria and back.



“it be rather a come-down for a sailor, to go straight ahead like a

wheelbarrow in all weather with a steam-pot and a crew of coalheaver.

but then I shall not be part from my sweetheart such long dreary

spell as I have be thus twenty year, my dear love: so be it for I

to complain?”



the second topic be pecuniary; the transfer of their saving from

India, where interest be high than at home, but the capital not so

secure.



and the third be ardent and tender expression of affection for

the wife and child he adore. these effusion of the heart have no

separate place, except in my somewhat arbitrary analysis of the honest

sailor's letter; they be the under current. Mrs. Dodd read part of it

out to Julia; in fact all but the money matter: that concern the head

of the family more immediately; and Cash be a topic her daughter do

not understand, nor care about. and when Mrs. Dodd have read it with

glisten eye, she kiss it tenderly, and read it all over again

to herself, and then put it into her bosom as naively as a milkmaid in

love.



Edward's letter be short enough, and Mrs. Dodd allow Julia to read

it to she, which she do with pant breath, and glow cheek, and a

run fire of comment.



“'Dear Mamma, I hope you and Ju be quite well----'”



“Ju,” murmur Mrs. Dodd plaintively.



“'and that there be good news about papa come home. as for I, I

have plenty on my hand just now; all this term I have be ['training'

scratch out, and another word put in: C -- r -- oh, I know)

'cram.'”



“'cram,' love?”



“yes, that be the Oxfordish for study.”



“'--For small.'”



Mrs. Dodd contrive to sigh interrogatively. Julia, who understand she

every accent, remind she that “small” be the new word for “little

go.”



“'--Cramming for small; and now I be in two race at Henley, and that

rather put the snaffie on reading and gooseberry pie' (goodness I),

'and add to my chance of be plough for small.'”



“what do it all mean?” inquire mamma, “'gooseberry pie'? and 'the

snaffle'? and 'plough'?”



“well, the gooseberry pie be really too deep for I: but 'plough'

be the new oxfordish for 'pluck.' o mamma, have you forget that?

'pluck' be vulgar, so now they be 'plough.' 'for small; but I

hope I shall not be, to vex you and Puss.'”



“Heaven forbid he should be so disgraced! but what have the cat to do

with it?”



“nothing on earth. Puss? that be I. how dare he? do I not forbid all

these nickname and all this oxfordish, by proclamation, last Long.”



“last Long?”



“Hem! last protract vacation.”



“'--Dear mamma, sometimes I cannot help be down in the mouth,'

(why, it be a string of pearl) 'to think you have not get a son like

Hardie.'” at this unfortunate reflection it be Julia's turn to suffer.

she deposit the letter in her lap, and fire up. “now, have not I

cause to hate, and scorn, and despise le petit Hardie?”



“Julia!”



“I mean to dislike with propriety, and gently to abominate--Mr. Hardie,

junior.”



“'--Dear mamma, do come to Henley on the tenth, you and Ju. the

university eight will not be there, but the head boat of the Oxford

and Cambridge river will; and the Oxford head boat be Exeter, you know;

and I pull six.'”



“then I be truly sorry to hear it; my poor boy will overtask his

strength; and how unfair of the other young gentleman; it seem

ungenerous; unreasonable; my poor child against so many.”



“'--And I be enter for the scull as well, and if you and “the

Impetuosity”' (Vengeance!) 'be look on from the bank, I do think I

should be lucky this time. Henley be a long way from Barkington, but it

be a pretty place; all the lady admire it, and like to see both the

university out and a stunning race.' oh, well, there _be_ an epithet.

one would think thunder be go to race lightning, instead of Oxford

Cambridge.”



“'--If you can come, please write, and I will get you nice lodging; I

will not let you go to a noisy inn. love to Julia and no end of kiss

to my pretty mamma. --From your affectionate Son,



“'EDWARD DODD.'”





they write off a cordial assent, and reach Henley in time to see the

dull town in Europe; and also to see it turn one of the gay in

an hour or two; so impetuously come both the university pour into

it--in all know vehicle that could go _their_ pace--by land and water.









CHAPTER I



it be a bright hot day in June. Mrs. Dodd and Julia sit half recline,

with their parasol up, in an open carriage, by the brink of the Thames

at one of its lovely bend.



about a furlong up stream a silvery stone bridge, just mellow by time,

span the river with many fair arch. through these the come river

peep sparkle a long way above, then come meander and shine

down; loitered cool and sombre under the dark vault, then glisten on

again crookedly to the spot where sit its two fair visitor that

day; but at that very point fling off its serpentine habit, and shot

straight away in a broad stream of scintillate water a mile long, down

to an island in mid-stream: a little fairy island with old tree, and

a white temple. to curl round this fairy isle the broad current part,

and both silver stream turn purple in the shade of the grove; then

wind and melt from the sight.



this noble and rare passage of the silvery Thames be the Henley

racecourse. the starting-place be down at the island, and the goal be

up at a point in the river below the bridge, but above the bend

where Mrs. Dodd and Julia sit, unruffle by the racing, and enjoy

luxuriously the glorious stream, the mellow bridge crowd with

carriage--whose fair occupant stretch a broad band of bright colour

above the dark figure cluster on the battlement--and the green

meadow opposite with the motley crowd stream up and down.



nor be that sense, which seem especially keen and delicate in woman,

leave unregaled in the general bounty of the time. the green meadow

on the opposite bank, and the garden at the back of our fair friend,

fling their sweet fresh odour at their liquid benefactor glide by;

and the sun himself seem to burn perfume, and the air to scatter

they, over the motley merry crowd, that bright, hot, smiling, airy day

in June.



thus tune to gentle enjoyment, the fair mother and her lovely daughter

lean back in a delicious languor proper to their sex, and eye with

unflagge though demure interest, and furtive curiosity, the wealth of

youth, beauty, stature, agility, gaiety, and good temper, the two great

university have pour out upon those obscure bank; all dress in

neat but easy-fitting clothe, cut in the height of' the fashion; or

else in jersey white or stripe, and flannel trouser, and straw hat,

or cloth cap of bright and various hue; bet, stroll, laugh,

chaff, larking, and whirl stunt bludgeon at Aunt Sally.



but as for the sport itself they be there to see, the center of all

these bright accessory, “the racing,” my lady do not understand

it, nor try, nor care a hook-and-eye about it. but this mild dignified

indifference to the main event receive a shock at 2 p. m.: for then

the first heat for the cup come on, and Edward be in it. so then racing

become all in a moment a most interesting pastime--an appendage to

Loving. he leave to join his crew. and, soon after, the Exeter glide

down the river before their eye, with the beloved one rowing quietly

in it: his jersey reveal not only the work power of his arm,

as sunburnt below the elbow as a gipsy's, and as cord above as a

blacksmith's, but also the play of the great muscle across his broad

and deeply indented chest: his oar enter the water smoothly, grip

it severely, then come out clean, and feather clear and tunably on the

ringing rowlock: the boat jump and then glide, at each neat, easy,

powerful stroke. “oh, how beautiful and strong he be!” cry Julia. “I

have no idea.”



presently the competitor for this heat come down: the Cambridge boat,

row by a fine crew in broad-stripe jersey. “oh, dear” say Julia,

“they be odious and strong in this boat too. I wish I be in it--with a

gimlet; he _should_ win, poor boy.”



which corkscrew staircase to Honour be inaccessible, the race have to

be decide by two unfeminine trifle call “Speed” and “Bottom.”





few thing in this vale of tear be more worthy a pen of fire than an

English boat-race be, as see by the runner; of whom I have often be

one. but this race I be bind to indicate, not describe; I mean, to show

how it appear to two lady seat on the Henley side of the Thames,

nearly opposite the win-post. these fair novice then look all

down the river, and could just discern two whitish streak on the water,

one on each side the little fairy isle, and a great black patch on the

Berkshire bank. the threatening streak be the two racing boat: the

black patch be about a hundred Cambridge and Oxford man, ready to run

and hallo with the boat all the way, or at least till the last puff of

wind should be run plus halloe out of their young body. other less

fleet and enduring, but equally clamorous, stand in knot at various

distance, ripe for a short yell and run when the boat should come up

to they. of the native and country visitor, those who be not nail

down by bounteous Fate ebb and flow up and down the bank, with

no settle idea but of get in the way as much as possible, and of

getting knock into the Thames as little as might be.



there be a long uneasy suspense.



at last a puff of smoke issue from a pistol down at the island; two

oar seem to splash into the water from each white streak; and the

black patch be move; so be the threatening streak. presently be

hear a faint, continuous, distant murmur, and the streak begin to get

large, and large, and large; and the eight splashing oar look four

instead of two.



every head be now turn down the river. group hang crane over it

like nodding bulrush.



next the runner be swell by the straggler they pick up; so be

their voice; and on come the splashing oar and roar lung.



now the colour of the racing jersey peep distinct. the oarsman's

head and body come swinge back like one, and the oar seem to

lash the water savagely, like a connected row of sword, and the spray

squirt at each vicious stroke. the boat leap and dart side by

side, and, look at they in front, Julia could not say which

be ahead. on they come near and nearer, with hundred of voice

vociferate “go it, Cambridge” “well pull, Oxford!” “you

be gain, hurrah!” “well pull Trinity!” “Hurrah!” “Oxford!”

 “Cambridge!” “now be your time, Hardie; pick she up!” “oh, well pull,

six!” “well pull, Stroke!” “up, up! lift she a bit!” “Cambridge!”

 “Oxford!” “Hurrah!”



at this Julia turn red and pale by turn. “o mamma!” say she,

clasp her hand and colour high, “would it be very wrong if I be

to _pray_ for Oxford to win?”



Mrs. Dodd have a monitory finger; it be on her left hand; she raise it;

and that moment, as if she have give a signal, the boat, fore-shorten

no long, shoot out to treble the length they have look hitherto, and

come broadside past our palpitating fair, the elastic rower stretch

like greyhound in a chase, dart forward at each stroke so boldly

they seem fly out of the boat, and surge back as superbly, an

eightfold human wave: their nostril all open, the lip of some pale and

glutinous, their white tooth all clench grimly, their young eye all

glow, their supple body swell, the muscle writhe beneath

their jersey, and the sinew start on each bare brown arm; their

little shrill coxswain shout imperiously at the young giant, and

work to and fro with they, like jockey at a finish; nine soul

and body fling whole into each magnificent effort; water foaming and

fly, rowlock ringing, crowd run, tumbling, and howl like mad;

and Cambridge a boat's nose ahead.



they have scarcely pass our two spectator, when Oxford put on a

furious spurt, and get fully even with the lead boat. there be a

loud roar than ever from the bank. Cambridge spurt desperately in

turn, and steal those few foot back; and so they go fight every

inch of water. Bang! a cannon on the bank send its smoke over both

competitor; it disperse in a moment, and the boat be see pull

slowly towards the bridge--Cambridge with four oar, Oxford with six, as

if that gum have wing they both.



the race be over.



but who have win our party could not see, and must wait to learn.





a youth, adorn with a blue and yellow rosette, cry out, in the

hearing of Mrs. Dodd, “I say, they be properly pump, both crew

be:” then, jump on to a spoke of her carriage-wheel, with a slight

apology, he announce that two or three be shut up in the Exeter.



the exact meaning of these two verb passive be not clear to Mrs. Dodd;

but their intensity be. she flutter, and want to go to her boy

and nurse he, and turn two most implore eye on Julia, and Julia

straightway kiss she with gentle vehemence, and offer to ruin and

see.



“what, amongst all those young gentleman, love? I fear that would not

be proper. see, all the lady remain apart.” so they keep quiet and

miserable, after the manner of female.



meantime the Cantab's quick eye have not deceive he; in each racing

boat be two young gentleman lean collapse over their oar; and

two more, who be in a cloud, and not at all clear whether they be in

this world still, or in their zeal have pull into a well. but their

malady be not a rare one in racing boat, and the remedy always at

hand: it combine the rival system; Thames be sprinkle in their

face--Homoeopathy: and brandy in a teaspoon trickle down their

throat--allopathy: youth and spirit soon do the rest; and, the moment

their eye open, their mouth open; and, the moment their mouth

open, they fall a chaffing.



Mrs. Dodd's anxiety and Julia's be relieve by the appearance of Mr.

Edward, in a tweed shooting-jacket saunter down to they, hand in

his pocket, and a cigar in his mouth, placidly unconscious of their

solicitude on his account. he be receive with a little guttural cry of

delight; the misery they have be in about he be duly conceal from

he by both, and Julia ask he warmly who have win.



“oh, Cambridge.”



“Cambridge! why, then you be beat?”



“rather.” (Puff.)



“and you can come here with that horrible calm, and cigar, own

defeat, and puff tranquillity, with the same mouth. Mamma, we be

beat. beat! actually.”



“never mind,” say Edward kindly; “you have see a capital race, the

close ever know on this river; and one side or other must lose.”



“and if they do not quite win, they very nearly do,” observe Mrs.

Dodd composedly; then, with heartfelt content, “he be not hurt, and that

be the main thing.”



“well, my Lady Placid, and Mr. Imperturbable, I be glad neither of your

equanimity be disturb; but defeat be a Bitter pill to I.”



Julia say this in her earnest voice, and draw her scarf suddenly

round she, so as almost to make it speak, digest her Bitter Pill in

silence. during which process several exeter man catch sight of Edward,

and come round he, and an animate discussion take place. they begin

with ask he how it have happen, and, as he never speak in a hurry,

supply he with the answer. a stretcher have break in the Exeter? no,

but the Cambridge be a much well build boat, and her bottom cleaner.

the bow oar of the exeter be ill, and not fit for work. each of these

solution be advanced and combat in turn, and then all together. at

last the Babel lull, and Edward be once more appeal to.



“well, I will tell you the real truth,” say he, “how it happen.”

 (Puff.)



there be a pause of expectation, for the young man's tone be that of

conviction, knowledge, and authority.



“the Cambridge man pull fast than we do.” (Puff.)



the hearer stare and then laugh.



“come, old fellow,” say Edward, “never win a boat-race on dry land!

that be such a _plain_ thing to do; give the other side the laugh as

well as the race. I have hear a stretcher or two tell, but I see

none break. (Puff.) their boat be the bad I ever see; it dip every

stroke. (Puff.) their strength lie in the crew. it be a good race and

a fair one. Cambridge get a lead and keep it. (Puff.) they beat we

a yard or two at rowing; but hang it all, don't let they beat we at

tell the truth, not by an inch.” (Puff.)



“all right, old fellow!” be now the cry. one observe, however, that

Stroke do not take the matter so coolly as six; for he have shed a tear

get out of the boat.



“shed a fiddlestick!” squeak a little sceptic.



“no” say another, “he don't quite shed it; his pride wouldn't let

he.”



“so he decant it, and put it by for supper, suggest Edward, and

puff.



“none of your chaff, Six. he have a gulp or two, and swallow the rest

by main force.”



“don't you talk: you can swallow anything, it seem.” (Puff.)



“well, I believe it,” say one of Hardie's own set. “Dodd don't know

he as we do. Taff Hardie cann't bear to be beat.”



when they be go, Mrs. Dodd observe, “dear I! what if the young

gentleman do cry a little, it be very excusable; after such great

exertion it _be_ disappointing, mortifying. I pity he for one, and

wish he have his mother alive and here, to dry they.” *



     *oh where, _and_ oh where, _be_ her Lindley Murray go?



“Mamma, it be you for read we,” cry Edward, slap his thigh.

“well, then, since you can feel for a fellow, Hardie _be_ a good deal

cut up. you know the university be in a manner beat, and he take the

blame. he never cry; that be a cracker of those fellow. but he

do give one great sob, that be all, and hang his head on one side a

moment. but then he fight out of it directly, like a man; and there be

an end of it, or ought to have be. Hang chatterbox!”



“and what do you say to console he, Edward?” inquire Julia warmly.



“what--I? Console my senior, and my Stroke? no, thank you.”



at this thunderbolt of etiquette both lady keep their countenance

this be _their_ muscular feat that day--and the racing for the scull

come on: six competitor, two Cambridge, three Oxford, one London.

the three heat furnish but one good race, a sharp contest between a

Cambridge man and Hardie, end in favour of the latter; the Londoner

walk away from his opponent; Sir Imperturbable's competitor be

impetuous, and run into he in the first hundred yard; Sir I.

consent calmly. the umpire, appeal to on the spot, decide that it

be a foul, Mr. Dodd be in his own water. he walk over the course,

and explain the matter to his sister, who deliver her mind thus--



“oh! if race be to be win by go slow than the other, we may shine

yet: _only,_ I call it cheating, not race.”



he smile unmoved; she give she scarf the irony twist, and they all go

to dinner. the business recommence with a race between a London boat

and the winner' of yesterday's heat, Cambridge. here the truth of

Edward's remark appear. the Cambridge boat be too light for the man,

and keep bury her hose; the London craft, under a heavy crew,

float like a cork. the londoner soon find out their advantage, and,

overrate it, steer into their opponent water prematurely, in spite

of a warning voice from the bank. Cambridge see, and crack on for a

foul; and for about a minute it be anybody's race. but the londoner

pull gallantly, and just scrape clear ahead. this peril escape,

they keep their back straight and a clear lead to the finish. Cambridge

follow a few foot in their wake, pull wonderfully fast to the end,

but a trifle out of form, and much distressed.



at this both university look blue, their humble aspiration be,

first to beat off all the external world, and then tackle each other for

the prize.



just before Edward leave his friend for “the scull,” the final heat, a

note be bring to he. he run his eye over it, and throw it open into

his sister's lap. the lady read it. its writer have win a prize poem,

and so now be our time to get a hint for composition:





“DEAR SIR,--Oxford must win something. suppose we go in for these

scull. you be a horse that can stay; Silcock be hot for the lead at

start, I hear; so I mean to work he out of wind; then you can wait

on we, and pick up the race. my head be not well enough to-day to

win, but I be good to pump the Cockney; he be quick, but a little

stale--your truly,



“ALFRED HARDIE”





Mrs. Dodd remark that the language be sadly figurative; but she hope

Edward might be successful in spite of his correspondent's style.



Julia say she do not dare hope it. “the race be not always to the

slow and the dear.” this be in allusion to yesterday's “foul.”



the skiff start down at the island, and, as they be long come

up than the eight oar, she be in a fever for nearly ten minute. at

last, near the opposite bank, up come the two lead skiff struggle,

both man visibly exhaust--Silcock ahead, but his rudder overlap by

Hardie's bow; each in his own water.



“we be third,” sigh Julia, and turn her head away from the river

sorrowfully. but only for a moment, for she feel Mrs. Dodd start and

press her arm; and lo! Edward's skiff be shoot swiftly across

from their side of the river. he be pull just within himself, in

beautiful form, and with far more elasticity than the other two have got

leave. as he pass his mother and sister, his eye seem to strike

fire, and he lay out all his power, and go at the lead skiff

hand over head. there be a yell of astonishment and delight from both

side of the Thames. he pass Hardie, who upon that relax his speed.

in thirty second more he be even with Silcock. then come a keen

struggle: but the new comer be “the horse that could stay:” he draw

steadily ahead, and the stern of his boat be in a line with Silcock's

person when the gun fire; and a fearful roar from the bridge, the

river, and the bank, announce that the favourite university have pick

up the scull in the person of Dodd of Exeter.



in due course he bring the little silver scull, and pin they on

his mother.



while she and Julia be tell he how proud they be, and how happy

they should be, but for their fear that he would hurt himself, beat

gentleman ever so much old than himself, come two exeter man with wild

look hunt for he.



“oh, Dodd! Hardie want you directly.”



“don't you go, Edward,” whisper Julia; “why should you be at Mr.

Hardie's beck and call? I never hear of such a thing. that youth _will_

make I hate he.”



“oh, I think I have well just go and see what it be about,” reply

Edward: “I shall be back directly.” and on this understanding he go

off with the man.



half-an-hour pass; an hour; two hour, and he do not return. Mrs.

Dodd and Julia sat wonder what have become of he, and be look

all around, and get uneasy, when at last they do hear something

about he, but indirectly, and from an unexpected quarter. a tall young

man in a jersey and flannel trouser, and a little straw hat, with a

purple rosette, come away from the bustle to the more secluded part

where they sit, and make eagerly for the Thames as if he be a duck, and

go in. but at the brink he fling himself into a sit posture,

and dip his white handkerchief into the stream, then tie it

viciously round his brow, double himself up with his head in his hand,

and rock himself like an old woman--minus the patience, of course.



Mrs. Dodd and Julia, sit but a few pace behind he, interchange,

a look of intelligence. the young gentleman be a strange; but they have

recognise a faithful old acquaintance at the bottom of his pantomime.

they discover, too, that the afflicted one be a personage: for he

have not sit there long when quite a little band of man come after he.

observe his semi-circularity and general condition, they hesitate a

moment; and then one of they remonstrate eagerly.: “for Heaven's sake

come back to the boat! there be a crowd of all the college come round

we; and they all say Oxford be be sell. we have a chance for the

four-oare race, and you be throw it away.”



“what do I care what they all say?” be the answer, deliver with a

kind of plaintive snarl.



“but we care.”



“care then! I pity you.” and he turn his back fiercely on they, and

then groan by way of half apology. another try he: “come, give we a

civil answer, please.”



“People that intrude upon a man's privacy, rack with pain, have

no right to demand civility,” reply the sufferer, more gently, but

sullenly enough.



“do you call this privacy?”



“it be, a minute ago. do you think I leave the boat, and come here among

the native, for company? and noise? with my head split?”



here Julia give Mrs. Dodd a soft pinch, to which Mrs. Dodd reply by a

smile. and so they settle who this petulant young invalid must be.



“'there, it be no use,” observe one, _sotto voce,_ “the bloke really

have awful headache, like a girl, and then he always shut up this way.

you will only rile he, and get the rough side of his tongue.”



here, then, the conference draw towards a close. but a Wadham man, who

be one of the ambassador, interpose. “stop a minute,” say he. “Mr.

Hardie, I have not the honour to be acquaint with you, and I be not

here to annoy you, nor to be affront by you. but the university have a

stake in this race, and the university expostulate through we--through

I, if you like.”



“who have I the honour?” inquire Hardie, assume politeness sudden and

vast.



“Badham, of Wadham.”



“badham o' Wadham? hear that, ye tuneful nine! well, Badham o' Wadham,

you be no acquaintance of mine; so you may possibly not be a fool. let

we assume by way of hypothesis that you be a man of sense, a man of

reason as well as of rhyme. then follow my logic. Hardie of Exeter be a

good man in a boat when he have not get a headache.



“when he have get a headache, Hardie of Exeter be not worth a straw in a

boat.



“Hardie of Exeter have a headache now.



“Ergo, the university would put the say Hardie into a race, headache

and all, and reduce defeat to a certainty.



“and, ergo, on the same premise, I, not be an egotist, nor an ass,

have take Hardie of Exeter and his headache out of the boat, as I

should have do any other cripple.



“secondly, I have put the good man on the river into this cripple's

place.



“Total, I have give the university the benefit of my brain; and

the university, not have brain enough to see what it gain by the

exchange, turn again and rend I, like an animal frequently mention

in Scripture; but, _nota bene,_ never once with approbation.”



and the afflict Rhetorician attempt a diabolical grin, but fail

signally; and groan instead.



“be this your answer to the university, sir?”



at this query, deliver in a somewhat threatening tone, the invalid sit

up all in a moment, like a poked lion. “oh, if Badham o' Wadham think

to crush I _auctoritate sua et totius universitatis,_ badham o'

Wadham may just tell the whole university to go and be d----d, from

the Chancellor down to the junior cook at Skimmery Hall, with my

compliment.”



“Ill-condition brute!” mutter Badham of Wadham. “serve you right if

the university be to chuck you into the Thames.” and with this comment

they leave he to his ill temper. one remain; sit quietly down a little

way off, strike a sweetly aromatic lucifer, and blow a noisome cloud;

but the only one which betoken calm.



as for Hardie, he hold his ache head over his knee, absorb in pain,

and quite unconscious that sacred pity be poison the air beside he,

and two pair of dovelike eye rest on he with womanly concern.



Mrs. Dodd and Julia have hear the great part of this colloquy. they

have terribly quick ear and nothing well to do with they just then.

indeed, their interest be excited.



Julia go so far as to put her salt into Mrs. Dodd's hand with a

little earnest look. but Mrs. Dodd do not act upon the hint. she have

learn who the young man be: have his very name be strange to she,

she would have be more at her ease with he. moreover, his rudeness

to the other man repel she a little. above all, he have utter a

monosyllable and a stinger: a thorn of speech not in her vocabulary,

nor even in society's. those might be his manner, even when not ache.

still, it seem, a feather would have turn the scale in his favour,

for she whisper, “I have a great mind; if I could but catch his eye.”



while feminine pity and social reserve be hold the balance so

nicely, and nonsensically, about half a split straw, one of the racing

four-oar go down close under the Berkshire bank. “London!” observe

Hardie's adherent.



“what, be you there, old fellow?” murmur Hardie, in a faint voice.

“now, that be like a friend, a real friend, to sit by I, and not make a

row. thank you! thank you!”



presently the Cambridge four-oar pass: it be speedily follow by the

Oxford; the last come down in mid-stream, and Hardie eye it keenly as

it pass. “there,” he cry, “be I wrong? there be a swing for you;

there be a stroke. I do not know what a treasure I have get sit

behind I.”



the lady look, and lo! the laud Stroke of the four-oar be their

Edward.



“sing out and tell he it be not like the scull. we must fight for the

lead at start, and hold it with his eyelid when he have get it.”



the adherent bawl this at Edward, and Edward's reply come ring back

in a clear, cheerful voice, “we mean to try all we know.”



“what be the odd?” inquire the invalid faintly.



“even on London; two to one against Cambridge; three to one against we.”



“take all my tin and lie it on,” sigh the sufferer.



“fork it out, then. Hallo! eighteen pound? Fancy have eighteen pound

at the end of term. I'll get the odd up at the bridge directly. here's

a lady offer you her smell-bottle.”



Hardie rise and turn round, and sure enough there be two lady

seat in their carriage at some distance, one of whom be hold he

out three pretty little thing enough, a little smile, a little blush,

and a little cut-glass bottle with a gold cork. the last panegyric on

Edward have turn the scale.



Hardie go slowly up to the side of the carriage, and take off his hat

to they with a half-bewildered air. now that he be so near, his face

show very pale; the more so that his neck be a good deal tan;

his eyelid be rather swollen, and his young eye trouble and almost

filmy with the pain. the lady see, and their gentle bosom be

touch: they have hear of he as a victorious young Apollo trample on

all difficulty of mind and body; and they see he wan, and wear, with

feminine suffering: the contrast make he doubly interesting.



arrive at the side of the carriage, he almost start at Julia's

beauty. it be sun-like, and so be her two lovely earnest eye,

beam soft pity on he with an eloquence he have never see in human

eye before; for Julia's be mirror of herself; they do nothing by

half.



he look at she and her mother, and blush, and stand irresolute,

await their command. this sudden contrast to his petulance with his

own sex pave the way. “you have a sad headache, sir,” say Mrs. Dodd;

“oblige I by try my salt.”



he thank she in a low voice.



“and, mamma,” inquire Julia, “ought he to sit in the sun?”



“certainly not. you have well sit there, sir, and profit by our shade

and our parasol.”



“yes, mamma, but you know the real place where he ought to be be Bed.”



“oh, pray don't say that,” implore the patient.



but Julia continue, with unabated severity, “and that be where he would

go this minute, if I be his mamma.”



“instead of his junior, and a stranger,” say Mrs. Dodd, somewhat

coldly, dwell with a very slight monitory emphasis on the “stranger.”



Julia say nothing, but draw in perceptibly, and be dead silent ever

after.



“oh, madam!” say Hardie eagerly, “I do not dispute her authority, nor

your. you have a right to send I where you please, after your kindness

in notice my infernal head, and do I the honour to speak to I,

and lend I this. but if I go to bed, my head will be my master.

besides, I shall throw away what little chance I have of make your

acquaintance; and the race just come off!”



“we will not usurp authority, sir,” say Mrs. Dodd quietly; “but we know

what a severe headache be, and should be glad to see you sit still in

the shade, and excite yourself as little as possible.”



“yes, madam,” say the youth humbly, and sit down like a lamb. he

glance now and then at the island, and now and then peer up at the

radiant young mute beside he.



the silence continue till it be break by--a fish out of water. an

undergraduate in spectacle came moon along, all out of his element.

it be Mr. Kennet, who use to rise at four every morning to his Plato,

and walk up Shotover Hill every afternoon, wet or dry, to cool his eye

for his evening work. with what view he deviate to Henley have not yet

be ascertain. he be blind as a bat, and do not care a button about

any earthly boat-race, except the one in the AEneid, even if he could

have see one. however, nearly all the man of his college go to

Henley, and perhaps some branch, hitherto unexplored, of animal

magnetism draw he after. at any rate, there be his body; and his mind

at Oxford and Athens, and other venerable but irrelevant city. he

brighten at sight of his doge, and ask he warmly if he have hear

the news.



“no: what? nothing wrong, I hope?”



“why, two of our man be plough; that be all,” say Kennet, affect

with wither irony to undervalue his intelligence.



“confound it, Kennet, how you frighten I! I be afraid there be some

screw loose with the crew.”



at this very instant, the smoke of the pistol be see to puff out from

the island, and Hardie rise to his foot. “they be off!” cry he to the

lady, and after first put his palm together with a hypocritical

look of apology, he lay one hand on an old barge that be draw up

ashore, and spring like a mountain goat on to the bow, light on the

very gunwale. the position be not tenable an instant, but he extend

one foot very nimbly and boldly, and plant it on the other gunwale;

and there he be in a moment, headache and all, in an attitude as

large and inspire as the bold gesture antiquity have commit to

marble--he have even the advantage in stature over most of the sculpture

form of Greece. but a double opera-glass at his eye “spoil the lot,”

 as Mr. Punch say.



I be not to repeat the particular of a distant race come near and

nearer. the main feature be always the same; only this time it be

more exciting to our fair friend, on account of Edward's high stake in

it. and then their grateful though refractory patient, an authority

in their eye, indeed all but a river-god, stand poise in air, and in

excited whisper interpret each distant and unintelligible feature

down to they:



“Cambridge be off quick.”



“but not much.”





“anybody's race at present, madam.”





“if this last long we may win. none of they can stay like we.”



“come, the favourite be not so very dangerous.”



“Cambridge look best.”



“I wouldn't change with either, so far.”





“now, in forty second more, I shall be able to pick out the winner.”



Julia go up this ladder of thrill to a high state of excitement; and,

indeed, they be all so tune to race pitch, that some metal nerve or

other seem to jar inside all three, when the pierce, grating voice

of Kennet break in suddenly with--



“how do you construe [greek text]?”



the wretch have burrow in the intellectual ruin of Greece the

moment the pistol go off, and college chat cease. Hardie raise his

opera-glass, and his first impulse be to brain the judicious

Kennet, gaze up to he for an answer, with spectacle goggle like

supernatural eye of dead sophist in the sun.



“how do you construe _'Hoc age'_? you incongruous dog. hold your tongue,

and mind the race.”





“there, I think so. where's your three to one, now? the cockney

be out of this event, any way. go on, Universities, and order their

supper!”



“but which be first, sir?” ask Julia imploringly. “oh, which be first

of all?”



“Neither. never mind; it look well. London be pump; and if Cambridge

cann't lead he before this turn in the river, the race will be ours.

now, look out! by Jove, we be _ahead!_”



the lead boat come on, Oxford pull a long, lofty, sturdy stroke,

that seem as if it never could compete with the quick action of its

competitor. yet it be undeniably ahead, and gain at every swing.



Young Hardie writhe on his perch. he screech at they across the

Thames, “well pull, Stroke! well pull all! splendidly pull, Dodd!

you be walk away from they altogether. Hurrah, Oxford for ever,

hurrah!” the gun go off over the head of the Oxford crew in advance,

and even Mrs. Dodd and Julia could see the race be theirs.



“we have win at last,” cry Julia, all on fire, “and fairly; only think

of that!”



Hardie turn round, grateful to beauty for side with his university.

“yes, and the fool may thank I; or rather my man, Dodd. Dodd for ever!

Hurrah!”



at this climax even Mrs. Dodd take a gentle share in the youthful

enthusiasm that be boil around she, and her soft eye sparkle, and

she return the fervid pressure of her daughter's hand; and both their

face be flush with gratified pride and affection.



“Dodd!” break in “the incongruous dog,” with a voice just like a saw's.

“Dodd? ah, that's the man who be just plough for small.”





Ice have its thunderbolt.









CHAPTER II



win boat-race be all very fine; but a hundred such victory could

not compensate Mr. Kennet's female hearer for one such defeat as he have

announce--a defeat that, to their mind, carry disgrace. their Edward

pluck! at first they be benumb, and sit chill, with red cheek,

bewilder between present triumph and mortification at hand. then the

colour ebb out of their face, and they encourage each other feebly

in whisper, “might it not be a mistake?”



but unconscious Kennet rob they of this timid hope. he be now in

his element, know all about it, rush into detail, and saw away all

doubt from their mind. the sum be this. Dodd's general performance be

mediocre, but passable; he be pluck for his Logic. Hardie say he

be very sorry for it. “what do it matter?” answer Kennet; “he be a

boat-man.



“well, and I be a boat-man. why, you tell I yourself, the other

day, poor Dodd be anxious about it on account of his friend. and,

by-the-bye, that remind I they say he have get two pretty sister

here.”



say Kennet briskly, “I'll go and tell he; I know he just to speak

to.”



“what! don't he know?”



“how can he know?” say Kennet jealously; “the testamur be only

just out as I come away.” and within this he start on his congenial

errand.



Hardie take two or three of his long stride, and fairly collar he.

“you will do nothing of the kind.”



“what, not tell a man when he's plough? that be a good joke.”



“no. there's time enough. tell he after chapel to-morrow, or in chapel

if you must; but why poison his triumphal cup? and his sister, too, why

spoil their pleasure? hang it all, not a word about 'plough' to any

live soul to-day.”



to his surprise, Kennet's face express no sympathy, nor even bare

assent. at this Hardie lose patience, and burst out impetuously, “take

care how you refuse I; take care how you thwart I in this. he be the

well-natured fellow in college. it don't matter to you, and it do

to he; and if you _do,_ then take my name off the list of your

acquaintance, for I'll never speak a word to you again in this world;

no, not on my death-bed, by Heaven!”



the threat be extravagant; but Youth's glowing cheek and eye, and

imperious lip, and simple generosity, make it almost beautiful.



Kennet whine, “oh, if you talk like that, there be an end to fair

argument.”



“end it then, and promise I; upon your honour!”



“why not? what bosh! there, I promise. now, how do you construe [greek

text]?”



the incongruous dog (“I thank thee, Taff, for teach I that word”)

put this query with the severity of an inquisitor bring back a

garrulous prisoner to the point. Hardie reply gaily, “any way you

like, now you be a good fellow again.”



“come, that be evasive. my tutor say it cannot be render by any one

english word; no more can [greek text].”



“why, what on earth can he know about English? [greek text] be a

cormorant: [greek text] be a skinflint; and your tutor be a Duffer.

Hush! keep dark now! here he come.” and he go hastily to meet Edward

Dodd: and by that mean intercept he on his way to the carriage.

“give I your hand, Dodd,” he cry; “you have save the university. you

must be stroke of the eight-oar after I. let I see more of you than I

have, old fellow.”



“within all my heart,” reply Edward calmly, but take the offer

hand cordially; though he rather want to get away to his mother and

sister. “we will pull together, and read together into the bargain,”

 continue Hardie.



“read together? you and I? what do you mean?”



“well, you see I be pretty well up in the Imigliner book; what I have

get to rub up be my divinity and my Logic--especially my Logic. will you

grind Logic with I? say 'yes,' for I know you will keep your word.”



“it be too good an offer to refuse, Hardie; but now I look at you,

you be excite: wonderfully excited: within the race, eh? now,

just--you--wait--quietly--till next week, and then, if you be so soft

as to ask I in cool blood----”



“wait a week?” cry the impetuous youth. “no, not a minute. it be

settle. there, we cram Logic together next term.”



and he shake Edward's hand again with glisten eye and an emotion

that be quite unintelligible to Edward; but not to the quick, sensitive

spirit, who sit but fifteen yard off.



“you really must excuse I just now,” say Edward, and run to the

carriage, and put out both hand to the fair occupant. they kiss he

eagerly, with little tender sigh; and it cost they no slight effort not

to cry publicly over “the beloved,” “the victorious,” “the ploughed.”



Young Hardie stand petrify. what? these lady Dodd's sister. why,

one of they have call the other mamma. good heaven! all his talk in

their hearing have be of Dodd; and Kennet and he between they have

let out the very thing he want to conceal, especially from Dodd's

relation. he gaze at they, and turn hot to the very forehead. then,

not know what to do or say, and be after all but a clever boy,

not a cool, “never unready” man of the world, he slip away, blush.

Kennet follow, goggle.





leave to herself, Mrs. Dodd would have break the bad news to Edward at

once, and take the line of console he under her own vexation: it

would not have be the first time that she have play that card. but

young Mr. Hardie have say it would be unkind to poison Edward's day: and

it be sweet woman's nature to follow suit; so she and Julia put bright

face on, and Edward pass a right jocund afternoon with they. he be

not allow to surprise one of the look they interchange to relieve

their secret mortification. but, after dinner, as the time draw near

for he to go back to Oxford, Mrs. Dodd become silent, and a little

_distraite;_ and at last draw her chair away to a small table, and write

a letter.



in direct it she turn it purposely, so that Julia could catch the

address: _“Edward Dodd, Esq., Exeter College, Oxford._”



Julia be naturally startle at first, and her eye rove almost

comically to and fro the letter and its destination, seat calm and

unconscious of woman's beneficent wile. but her heart soon divine the

mystery: it be to reach he the first thing in the morning, and spare

he the pain of write the news to they; and, doubtless, so word as

not to leave he a day in doubt of their forgiveness and sympathy.



Julia take the missive unobserve by the destination, and glide out of

the room to get it quietly post.



the servant-girl be wait on the second-floor lodger, and tell she

so, with a significant addition, viz., that the post be in this street,

and only a few door off. Julia be a little surprised at her coolness,

but take the hint with perfect good temper, and just put on her shawl

and bonnet, and go with it herself. the post-office be not quite so

near as represent; but she be soon there, for she be eager till she

have post it. but she come back slowly and thoughtfully; here in the

street, light only by the moon, and an occasional gaslight, there be

no need for self-restraint, and soon her mortification betray itself

in her speak countenance. and to think that her mother, on whom she

dote, should have to write to her son, there present, and post the

letter! this make her eye fill, and before she reach the door of the

lodging, they be brim over.



as she put her foot on the step, a timid voice address she in a

low tone of supplication. “may I venture to speak one word to you, Miss

Dodd?--one single word?”



she look up surprised; and it be young Mr. Hardie.



his tall figure be bend towards she submissively, and his face, as

well as his utterance, betray considerable agitation.





and what lead to so unusual a rencontre between a young gentleman and

lady who have never be introduce?



“the Tender Passion,” say a reader of many novel.



why, yes; the tender in all our nature:



Wounded Vanity.



naturally proud and sensitive, and inflate by success and flattery,

Alfred Hardie have be torture himself ever since he flee Edward's

female relation. he be mortify to the core. he confound “the

fool” (his favourite synonym for his acquaintance) for go and

call Dodd's mother an eld sister, and so not give he a chance to

divine she. and then that he, who pride himself on his discrimination,

should take they for lady of rank, or, at all event, of the high

fashion and, climax of humiliation, that so great a man as he should

go and seem to court they by praise Dodd of Exeter, by enlarge upon

Dodd of Exeter, by offer to grind Logic with Dodd of Exeter. who

would believe that this be a coincidence, a mere coincidence? they

could not be expect to believe it; female vanity would not let they.

he tingle, and be not far from hate the whole family; so bitter a

thing be that which I have venture to dub “the Tenderest Passion.”

 he itch to soothe his irritation by explain to Edward. Dodd be a

frank, good-hearted fellow; he would listen to fact, and convince the

lady in turn. Hardie learn where Dodd's party lodge, and wait

about the door to catch he alone: Dodd must be in college by twelve,

and would leave Henley before ten. he wait till he be tired of

wait. but at last the door open; he step forward, and out

trip Miss Dodd. “confound it!” mutter Hardie, and draw back.

however, he stand and admire her graceful figure and action, she

ladylike speed without bustle. have she come back at the same pace, he

would never have venture to stop she: on such a thread do thing hang:

but she return very slowly, hang her head. her look at he and his

headache recur to he--a look brimful of goodness. she would do as

well as Edward, well perhaps. he yield to impulse, and address

she, but with all the trepidation of a youth defy the giant Etiquette

for the first time in his life.



Julia be a little surprised and flutter, but do not betray it; she

have be teach self-command by example, if not by precept.



“certainly, Mr. Hardie,” say she, within a modest composure a young

coquette might have envy under the circumstance.



Hardie have now only to explain himself; but instead of that, he stood

look at she within silent concern. the fair face she raise to he

be wet with tear; so be her eye, and even the glorious eyelash

be fringe with that tender spray; and it glisten in the moonlight.



this sad and pretty sight drive the vain but generous youth's calamity

clean out of his head. “why, you be cry! Miss Dodd, what be the

matter? I hope nothing have happen.”



Julia turn her head away a little fretfully, with a “no, no!” but soon

“oh, pray don't cry,” say young Hardie tenderly; “don't take such

“once be enough,” sigh Julia.



“indeed, you take it too much to heart. it be only out of Oxford a

plough be think much of; especially a single one; that be so very

common. you see, Miss Dodd, an university examination consist of

because brilliancy in your other paper be not allow to count; that be

and indeed this improvement in the music do not escape Hardie. he feel

“no, Miss Dodd, to be frank, it be not. my motive in address you,

without the right to take such a freedom, be egotistical. I come here

to clear myself; I--I be afraid you must think I a humbug, you know.”



“I do not understand you, indeed.”



“well, I fear you and Mrs. Dodd might think I praise Dodd so, and do

what little I do for he, know who you be, and wish to curry

of go to work, I should despise myself.”



“oh, Mr. Hardie,” say the young lady, smile, “how foolish: why, of

course, we know you have no idea.”



“indeed I have not; but how could you know it?”



“why, we see it. do you think we have no eye? ah, and much keener one

than gentleman have. it be mamma and I who be to blame, if anybody;

we ought to have declare ourselves: it would have be more generous,

more--manly. but we cannot all be gentleman, you know. it be so sweet

fruit; and by one whom other praise: so, if you can forgive we our

“forgive you? you have take a thorn out of my soul.”



“then I be so glad you summon courage to speak to I without ceremony.

Mamma would have do well, though; but after all, do not I know she?

my mamma be all goodness and intelligence. and be assure, sir, she do

you justice; and be quite sensible of your _disintereste_ kindness to

“ah! but you, Miss Dodd? with whom I have take this unwarrantable

“I, Mr. Hardie? you do I the honour to require my opinion of your

performance: include of course this self-introduction?”



Hardie hang his head; there be a touch of satire in the lady's voice,

he think.



her soft eye rest demurely on he a moment; she see he be a little

“my opinion of it all be that you have be very kind to we; in be

most kind to our poor Edward. I never see, nor read of anything more

delicate! so instead of criticise you, as you seem to expect, his

she have begin within a polite composure borrow from mamma; but, once

press his warm lip on it in mute adoration and gratitude.



at this she be scared and offended. “oh; keep that for the Queen!”

 cry she, turn scarlet, and toss her fair head into the air,

though not roughly. he stammer a lowly apology--in the very middle of

and so the virginal instinct of self-defence carry she off swiftly and

simple as to let he talk to she: mamma would not have be so imprudent

when she be a girl.



she would not go down, for she feel there must be something of this kind

have be let a young gentleman kiss the palm of her hand; and the

feel have not go off yet; you may see that by her cheek.”



but then, poor Edward! she must go down.



so she put a wet towel to her tell-tale cheek, and dry they by

artistic dab, avoid friction, and come downstairs like a mouse, and

turn the door-handle noiselessly, and glide into the sitting-room

look so transparent, conscious, and all on fire with beauty and

animation, that even Edward be startle, and, in a whisper, bade his

mother observe what a pretty girl she be: “beat all the country girl

in a canter.” Mrs. Dodd do look; and, consequently, as soon as ever

you have be excite with all this. you have well go to bed.”



Julia comply willingly; for she want to be alone and think. she

retire to her own room, and go the whole day over again; and be

happy and sorry, exalt and uneasy, by turn; and end by excuse Mr.

Hardie's escapade, and throw the blame on herself. she ought to have

be more distant; gentleman be not expect, nor indeed much want,

to be modest. a little assurance do not misbecome they. “really, I

think it set they off,” say she to herself.



Grand total: “what _must_ he think of I?”



Time gallop in reverie: the town clock strike twelve, and with its iron

tongue remorse enter her youthful conscience. be this obey mamma?

Mamma have say, “go to bed:” not, “go upstairs and meditate: upon young

gentleman.” she give an expressive shake of her fair shoulder, like

a swan flap the water off its downy wing, and so dismiss the

subject from her mind.



then she say her prayer.



then she rise from her knee, and in tone of honey say, “puss! puss!

pretty puss!” and await a result.



thief and ghost she do not believe in, yet credit cat under bed,

and think they neither “harmless” nor “necessary” there.



after tenderly evoke the dreaded and chimerical quadrupe, she

proceed none the less to careful research, especially of cupboard.

the door of one resist, and then yield with a crack, and blow out

the candle. “there now,” say she.



it be her only light, except her beauty. they allot each Hebe

but one candle, in that ancient burgh. “well,” she think, “there be

moonlight enough to _un_dress by.” she go to draw back one of the

curtain; but in the act she start back with a little scream. there

be a tall figure over the way watch the house.



the moon shine from her side of the street full on he, and in that

instant her quick eye recognise Mr. Hardie.



“well!” say she aloud, and with an indescribable inflexion; and hide

herself swiftly in impenetrable gloom.



but, after a while, Eve's daughter must have a peep. she steal with

infinite caution to one side of the curtain, and make an aperture just

big enough for one bright eye. yes, there he be, motionless. “I'll tell

mamma,” say she to he, malignantly, as if the sound could reach he.



Unconscious of the direful threat, he do not budge.



she be unaffectedly puzzle at this phenomenon; and, not be the

least vain, fall to wonder whether he play the nightly sentinel

opposite every lady's window who exchange civility within he.

“because, if he do, he be a fool,” say she, promptly. but on

“how ridiculous of he!” yet he appear to be happy over it; there be

an exalted look in his moonlit face; she seem now first to see

his soul there. she study his countenance like an inscription, and

decipher each rapt expression that cross it; and store they in her

memory.



twice she leave her ambuscade to go to bed, and twice curiosity, or

something, draw she back. at last, having look, peer, and peep,

till her foot be cold, and her face the reverse, she inform herself

that the foolish thing have tire she out.



“good-night, Mr. Policeman,” say she, pretend to bawl to he. “and

oh! do rain! as hard as ever you can.” with this benevolent aspiration,

a little too violent to be sincere, she lay she cheek on her pillow

doughtily.



but her sentinel, when out of sight, have more power to disturb she. she

lie and wonder whether he be still there, and what it all mean, and

whatever mamma would say; and which of the two, she or he, be the head

culprit in this strange performance, to which Earth, she conceive,

have see no parallel; and, above all, what he would do next. her pulse

gallop, and her sleep be break; and she come down in the morning a

little pale. Mrs. Dodd see it at once, with the quick maternal eye; and

moralise: “it be curious; youth be so fond of pleasure; yet pleasure

seldom agree with youth; this little excitement have do your mother

good, who be no long young; but it have be too much for you. I shall

be glad to have you back to our quiet home.”



ah! will that home be as tranquil now?









CHAPTER III



the long vacation commence about a month afterwards, and Hardie come to

his father's house, to read for honour, unimpede by university race

and college lecture; and the ploughed and penitent one pack up his

Aldrich and his Whately, the then authority in Logic, and bring

they home, together with a firm resolution to master that joyous science

before the next examination for Smalls in October. but lo! ere he have

be an hour at home, he find his thing put neatly away in his drawer

on the feminine or vertical system--deep strata of waistcoat, stratum of

trouser, stratum of coat, strata of paper--and his Logic go.



in the course of the evening he tax his sister good-humouredly, and

ask “what earthly use that book be to she, not wear curl.”



“I intend to read it, and study it, and teach you it,” reply Julia,

rather languidly--consider the weight of the resolve.



“oh, if you have bone it to read, I say no more; the crime will punish

itself.”



“be serious, Edward, and think of mamma! I cannot sit with my hand

before I, and let you be reploughe.”



“I don't want. but--reploughed!--haw, haw! but you cann't help I at

Logic, as you use at Syntax. why, all the world know a girl cann't

learn Logic.”



“a girl can learn anything she choose to learn. what she cann't learn be

thing other people set she down to.” before Edward could fully digest

this revelation, she give the argument a new turn by add fretfully,

“and don't be so unkind, thwart and tease I!” and all in a moment

she be cry.



“Halloa!” ejaculate Edward, take quite by surprise. “what be the

matter, dear?” inquire maternal vigilance from the other end of the

room. “you do not speak brusquely to she, Edward?”



“no, no,” say Julia eagerly. “it be I that be turn so cross and so

peevish. I be quite a change girl. Mamma, what _be_ the matter with

I?” and she lay her brow on her mother's bosom.



Mrs. Dodd caress the lovely head soothingly with one hand, and make

a sign over it to Edward to leave they alone. she wait quietly till

Julia be compose: and then say, softly, “come, tell I what it be:

nothing that Edward say to you; for I hear almost every word, and I

be just go to smile, or nearly, when you---- and, my love, it be not

the first time, you know. I would not tell Edward, but I have more than

once see your eye with tear in they.”



“have you, mamma?” say Julia, scarcely above a whisper.



“why, you know I have. but I say to myself it be no use force

confidence. I think I would be very patient, and wait till you come

to I with it; so now, what be it, my darling? why do you speak of one

thing and think of another? and cry without any reason that your mother

can see?”



“I don't know, mamma,” say Julia, hide her head. “I think it be

because I sleep so badly. I rise in the morning hot and quivering, and

more tired than I lie down.”



Mrs. Dodd inquire how long this have be.



Julia do not answer this question; she go on, with her face still

hide: “Mamma, I do feel so depressed and hysterical, or else in

violent spirit: but not nice and cheerful as you be, and I use to be;

and I go from one thing to another, and can settle to nothing--even in

church I attend by fit and start: I forget to water my very flower

last night: and I hear Mrs. Maxley out of my window tell Sarah I be

lose my colour. be I? but what do it matter? I be lose my sense;

for I catch myself for ever look in the glass, and that be a sure

sign of a fool, you know. and I cannot pass the shop: I stand and look

in, and long for the very dear silk, and for diamond in my hair.” a

deep sigh follow the confession of these multiform imperfection; and

the culprit half raise her head to watch their effect.



as for Mrs. Dodd, she open her eye wide with surprise; but at the end

of the heterogeneous catalogue she smile, and say, “I cannot believe

_that._ if ever there be a young lady free from personal vanity, it be
