my Julia. why, your thought run by nature away from yourself; you be

bear for other.”



her daughter kiss she gratefully, and smile: but after a pause, say,

sorrowfully, “ah! that be the old Julia, as see with your dear eye. I

have almost forget _she._ the new one be what I tell you, dear

mamma, and that” (within sudden fervour) “be a dreamy, wandering, vain,

egotistical, hysterical, abominable girl.”



“let I kiss this monster that I have bring into the world,” say Mrs.

Dodd. “and now let I think.” she rest her eye calm and penetrate

upon her daughter; and at this mere look, but a very search one, the

colour mount and mount in Julia's cheek strangely.



“after all,” say Mrs. Dodd thoughtfully, “yours be a critical age.

perhaps my child be turn to a woman; my rosebud to a rose. and she

sigh. mother will sigh at thing none other ever sigh at.



“to a weed, I fear,” reply Julia. “what will you say when I own I feel

no real joy at Edward's return this time? and yesterday I cry, 'do get

away, and don't pester I!'”



“to your brother? oh!”



“oh, no, mamma, that be to poor Spot. he jump on I in a reverie, all

affection, poor thing.”



“well, for your comfort, dog do not appreciate the nicety of our

language.”



“I be afraid they do; when we kick they.”



Mrs. Dodd smile at the admission imply here, and the deep penitence

it be utter with. but Julia remonstrate, “oh no! no! don't laugh at

I, but help I within your advice: you be so wise and so experienced:

you must have be a girl before you be an angel. you _must_ know

what be the matter with I. oh, do pray cure I, or else kill I, for

I cannot go on like this, all my affection deaden and my peace

disturb.”



and now the mother look serious and thoughtful enough; and the

daughter watch she furtively. “Julia,” say Mrs. Dodd, very gravely,

“if it be not my child, rear under my eye, and never separate from

I a single day, I should say, this young lady be either afflict

with some complaint, and it affect her nerve and spirit; or else she

have--she be--what inexperienced young people call 'in love.' you need

not look so frightened, child; nobody in their sense suspect _you_

of imprudence or indelicacy; and therefore I feel quite sure that

your constitution be at a crisis, or your health have suffer some

shock--pray Heaven it may not be a serious one. you will have the good

advice, and without delay, I promise you.”









CHAPTER IV





that very evening, Mrs. Dodd send a servant into the town with a note

like a cocked-hat for Mr. Osmond, a consulting surgeon, who bear a

high reputation in Barkington. he come, and prove too plump for that

complete elegance she would have desire in a medical attendant; but

have a soft hand, a gentle touch, and a subdued manner. he speak to the

patient with a kindness which win the mother directly; have every hope

of set she right without any violent or disagreeable remedy; but,

when she have retire, alter his tone; and tell Mrs. Dodd seriously she

have do well to send for he in time: it be a case of “Hyperaesthesia”

 (Mrs. Dodd clasp her hand in alarm), “or as unprofessional person

would say, 'excessive sensibility.'”



Mrs. Dodd be somewhat relieved. translation blunt thunderbolt. she

tell he she have always fear for her child on that score. but be

sensibility curable? could a nature be change?



he reply that the Idiosyncrasy could not; but its morbid excess could,

especially when take in time. advice be generally call in too late.

however, here the only serious symptom be the Insomnia. “we must treat

she for that,” say he, write a prescription; “but for the rest,

active employment, long walk or ride, and a change of scene and

association, will be all that will be require. in these case,”

 resume Mr. Osmond, “connect as they be with Hyperaemia, some medical

man consider moderate venesection to be indicate.” he then put on his

glove say, “the diet, of course, must be antiphlogistic. let we say

then, for breakfast, dry toast with very little butter--no coffee--cocoa

(from the nib), or weak tea: for luncheon, beef-tea or mutton-broth:

for dinner, a slice of roast chicken, and tapioca or semolina pudding.

I would give she one glass of sherry, but no more, and barley-water; it

would be as well to avoid brown meat, at all event for the present.

with these precaution, my dear madam, I think your anxiety will soon be

happily remove.”



Julia take her long walk and light diet; and become a little pale

at time, and have few burst of high spirit in the interval of

depression. her mother go with her case to a female friend. the lady

say she would not trust to surgeon and apothecary; she would have

a downright physician. “why not go to the top of the tree at once, and

call in Dr. Short? you have hear of he?”



“oh, yes; I have even meet he in society; a most refined person: I

will certainly follow your advice and consult he. oh, thank you, Mrs.

Bosanquet! _a propos,_ do you consider he skilful?



“oh, immensely; he be a particular friend of my husband's.”



this be so convincing, that off go another three-cocked note, and

next day a dark-green carriage and pair dash up to Mrs. Dodd's door,

and Dr. Short bend himself in an arc, get out, and slowly mount

the stair. he be six foot two, wonderfully thin, livid, and

gentleman-like. fine home head, keen eye, lantern jaw. at sight

of he Mrs. Dodd rise and smile. Julia start and sit tremble.

he step across the room inaudibly, and after the usual civility,

glance at the patient's tongue, and touch her wrist delicately.

“Pulse be rapid,” say he.



Mrs. Dodd detail the symptom. Dr. Short listen within the patient

politeness of a gentleman, to whom all this be superfluous. he ask

for a sheet of note-paper, and divide it so gently, he seem to be

persuade one thing to be two. he write a pair of prescription, and

whilst thus employ look up every now and then and converse with the

lady.



“you have a slight subscapular affection, Miss Dodd: I mean, a little

pain under the shoulder-blade.”



“no, sir,” say Julia quietly.



Dr. Short look a little surprised; his female patient rarely

contradict he. be it for they to disown thing he be so a good as

to assign they?



“ah!” say he, “you be not conscious of it: all the well; it must be

slight; a mere uneasiness: no more.” he then number the prescription,

1, 2, and advise Mrs. Dodd to drop no. 1 after the eighth day, and

substitute no. 2, to be continue until convalescence. he put on his

glove to leave. Mrs. Dodd then, with some hesitation, ask he humbly

whether she might ask he what the disorder be. “certainly, madam,”

say he graciously; “your daughter be labour under a slight torpidity

of the liver. the first prescription be active, and be to clear the

gland itself, and the biliary duct, of the excretory accumulation;

and the second be exhibit to promote a healthy normal habit in that

important part of the vascular system.”



“what, then, it be not Hyperaemia?”



“Hyperaemia? there be no such disorder in the book.”



“you surprise I,” say Mrs. Dodd. “Dr. Osmond certainly think it be

Hyperaemia.” and she consult her little ivory tablet, whereon she have

write the word.



but meantime, Dr. Short's mind, to judge by his countenance, be away

roam distant space in search of Osmond.



“Osmond? Osmond? I do not know that name in medicine.”



“oh, oh, oh!” cry Julia, “and they both live in the same street!” Mrs.

Dodd hold up her finger to this outspoken patient.



but a light seem to break in on Dr. Short. “ah! you mean Mr. Osmond: a

surgeon. a very respectable man, a most respectable man. I do not know

a more estimable person--in his grade of the profession--than my good

friend Mr. Osmond. and so he give opinion in medical case, do he?”

 Dr. Short pause, apparently to realise this phenomenon in the world of

Mind. he resume in a different tone: “you may have misunderstood he.

Hyperaemia exist, of course; since he say so. but Hyperaemia be not

a complaint; it be a symptom. of biliary derangement. my worthy friend

look at disorder from a mental point; very natural: his interest lie

that way, perhaps you be aware: but profounder experience prove that

mental sanity be merely one of the result of bodily health: and I

be happy to assure you that, the biliary canal once clear, and the

secretion restore to the healthy habit by these prescription,

the Hyperaemia, and other concomitant of hepatic derangement, will

disperse, and leave our interesting patient in the enjoyment of her

natural intelligence, her friend' affectionate admiration, and above

all, of a sound constitution. lady, I have the honour” and the doctor

eke out this sentence by rise.



“oh, thank you, Dr. Short,” say Mrs. Dodd, rise within he; “you

inspire I with confidence and gratitude.” as if under the influence of

these feeling only, she take Dr. Short's palm and press it. of the

two hand, which meet for a moment then, one be soft and melt, the

other a bunch of bone; but both be very white, and so equally

adroit, that a double fee pass without the possibility of a bystander

suspect it.



for the benefit of all young virgin afflict like Julia Dodd, here be

the doctor's prescriptions:--



for MISS DODD.



     Rx Pil: hydrarg: chlor: Co:

     singuml: nocte sumend:

     Decoc: Aloes Co: 3j

     omni mane

     viii. Sept. J. S.



-------



for MISS DODD.



     Rx Conf: sennae.

     potass: Bitartrat.

     Extr: tarax: a a 3ss

     Misft: Elect:

     cuju sum: 3j omni mane.

     xviii. Sept. J. S.



-------



id: anglie reddit: per I Carol: Arundin:



the same do into English by I. C. R.





for MISS DODD.



1. o Jupiter aid we!! Plummer's pill to be take every night, 1 oz.

compound decoction of Aloes every morning.



8th Sept. J. S.



for MISS DODD.



2. o Jupiter aid we!! with Confection of Senna, Bitartrate of Potash,

extract of dandelion, of each half an ounce, let an electuary be mixed;

of which let she take 1 drachm every morning.



18th Sept. J. S.



-------



“quite the courtier,” say Mrs. Dodd, delight. Julia assent: she

even add, with a listless yawn, “I have no idea that a skeleton be

such a gentlemanlike thing; I never see one before.”



Mrs. Dodd admit he be very thin.



“oh no, mamma; 'thin' imply some little flesh. when he feel my pulse,

a chill strike to my heart. death in a black suit seem to steal up to

I, and lie a finger on my wrist: and mark I for his own.”



Mrs. Dodd forbid she to give way to such gloomy idea; and expostulate

firmly with she for judge learn man by their body. “however,” say

she, “if the good, kind doctor's remedy do not answer his expectation

and mine, I shall take you to London directly. I do hope papa will soon

be at home.”



Poor Mrs. Dodd be herself slip into a morbid state. a mother

collect doctor! it be a most fascinating kind of connoisseurship,

grow on one like drink; like Polemics; like Melodrama; like the

Millennium; like any thing.



sure enough, the very next week she and Julia sit patiently at the

morning levee of an eminent and title London surgeon. full forty

patient be before they: so they have to wait and wait. at last they

be usher into the presence-chamber, and Mrs. Dodd enter on the

beat ground of her daughter's symptom. the noble surgeon stop she

civilly but promptly. “auscultation will give we the clue,” say he,

and draw his stethoscope. Julia shrink and cast an appeal look at

her mother; but the impassive chevalier report on each organ in turn

without move his ear from the key-hole: “lung pretty sound,” say he,

a little plaintively: “so be the liver. now for the----Hum? there be no

kardiac insufficiency, I think, neither mitral nor tricuspid. if we find

no tendency to hypertrophy we shall do very well. ah! I have succeed

in diagnose a slight diastolic murmur; very slight.” he deposit the

instrument, and say, not without a certain shade of satisfaction that

his research have not be fruitless, “the heart be the peccant organ.”



“oh, sir! be it serious?” say poor Mrs. Dodd.



“by no mean. try this” (he scratch a prescription which would not

have misbecome the tomb of Cheops), “and come again in a month.” Ting!

he strike a bell. that “ting” say, “go, live, Guinea; and let another

come.”



“heart-disease now!” say Mrs. Dodd, sink back in her hire

carriage, and the tear be in her patient eye.



“my own, own mamma,” say Julia earnestly, “do not distress yourself.

I have no disease in the world, but my old, old, old one, of be a

naughty, wayward girl. as for you, mamma, you have resign your own

judgment to your inferior, and that be both our misfortune. dear, dear

mamma, do take I to a doctress next time, if you have not have enough.”



“to a what, love?”



“a she-doctor, then.”



“a female physician, child? there be no such thing. no; assurance be

become a characteristic of our sex; but we have not yet intrude

ourselves into the learn profession, thank Heaven.”



“excuse I, mamma, there be one or two; for the newspaper say so.”



“'well, dear, there be none in this country, happily.”



“'what, not in London?”



“no.”



“then what _be_ the use of such a great overgrown place, all smoke, if

there be nothing in it you cannot find in the country? let we go back to

Barkington this very day, this minute, this instant; oh, pray, pray.”



“and so you shall--to-morrow. but you must pity your poor mother's

anxiety, and see Dr. Chalmers first.”



“oh, mamma, not another surgeon! he frighten I; he hurt I. I never

hear of such a thing; oh, please not another surgeon.”



“it be not a surgeon, dear; it be the Court Physician.”



the Court Physician detect “a somewhat morbid condition of the great

nervous centre.” to an inquiry whether there be heart-disease, he

reply, “Pooh!” on be tell Sir William have announce heart-disease,

he say, “ah! _that alter the case entirely._” he maintain, however,

that it must be trifle, and would go no far, the nervous system

once restore to its healthy tone. “O Jupiter, aid we! blue pill and

Seidhitz powder.”



Dr. Kenyon find the mucous membrane be irritated and require

soothing. “O Jupiter, &c.”



Mrs. Dodd return home console and confused; Julia listless and

apathetic. Tea be order, with two or three kind of bread, thin

slice of meat, and a little blane mange, &c., their favourite repast

after a journey; and whilst the tea be draw, Mrs. Dodd look over

the card-tray and enumerate the visitor that have call during

their absence. “Dr. Short-- Mr. Osmond--Mrs. Hetherington--Mr. Alfred

Hardie--Lady Dewry--Mrs. and Miss Bosanquet. what a pity Edward be not

at home, dear; Mr. Alfred Hardie's visit must have be to he.”



“oh, of course, mamma.”



“a very manly young gentleman.”



“'oh, yes. no. he be so rude.”



“be he? ah! he be ill just then, and pain irritate gentleman; they be

not accustomed to it, poor thing.”



“that be like you, dear mamma; make excuse for one.” Julia add

faintly, “but he be so impetuous.”



“I have a daughter who reconcile I to impetuosity. and he _must_ have

a good heart, he be so kind to my boy.”



Julia look down smile; but presently seem to be seize with a

spirit of contradiction: she begin to pick poor Alfred to piece; he be

this, that, and the other; and then so bold, she might say impudent.



Mrs. Dodd reply calmly that he be very kind to her boy.



“oh, mamma, you cannot approve all the word he speak.”



“it be not worth while to remember all the word young gentleman speak

now-a-day. he be very kind to my boy, I remember that.”



the tea be now ready, and Mrs. Dodd sit down, and pat a chair, with

a smile of invitation for Julia to come and sit beside she. but Julia

say, “in one minute, dear,” and leave the room.



when she come back, she flutter up to her mother and kiss she

vehemently, then sit down radiant. “ah!” say Mrs. Dodd, “why, you be

look yourself once more. how do you feel now? well?”



“how do I feel? let I see: the world seem one e-nor-mous

flower-garden, and I the butterfly it all belong to.” she spake,

and to confirm her word the airy thing go waltzing, sailing, and

flutter round the room, and sip mamma every now and then on the

wing.



in this buoyancy she remain some twenty-four hour; and then come

cloud and chill, which, in their turn, give way to exultation, duly

follow by depression. her spirit be so uncertain, that thing too

minute to justify narration turn the scale either way: a word from

Mrs. Dodd--a new face at St. Anne's Church look devoutly her way--a

piece of town gossip distil in her ear by Mrs. Maxley--and she be

sprightly or languid, and both more than reason.



one drizzly afternoon they be sit silent and saddish in the

drawing-room, Mrs. Dodd correct the mechanical error in a drawing

of Julia's, and admire the rare dash and figure, and Julia doggedly

study Dr. Whately's Logic, with now and then a sigh, when suddenly a

trumpet seem to articulate in the little hall: “Mestress Doedd at home?”



the lady rise from her seat, and say with a smile of pleasure, “I hear

a voice.”



the door open, and in dart a grey-head man, with handsome but

strongly mark feature, laugh and shout like a schoolboy break

loose. he cry out, “ah! I've find y' out at last.” Mrs. Dodd glide

to meet he, and put out both her hand, the palm downwards, with

the prettiest air of ladylike cordiality; he shake they heartily. “the

vagabin say y' have leave the town; but y' have only flit from the

quay to the subbub; 'twa a pashint put I on the scint of ye. and how

be y' all these year? an' how's sawmill?”



“sawmill! what be that?”



“it's just your husband. ben't his name sawmill?”



“dear no! have you forgotten?--David.”



“Ou, ay. I know it be some Scripcher Petrarch or another, Daavid, or

Naathan, or Sawmill. and how be he, and where be he?”



Mrs. Dodd reply that he be on the sea, but expect----



“then I wish he well off 'em, confound 'em oncannall! Halloa! why, this

will be the little girl grow up int' a wumman while ye look round.”



“yes, my good friend; and her mother's darling.”



“and she's a bonny lass, I can tell ye. but no freend to the Dockers, I

see.”



“ah!” say Mrs. Dodd sadly, “look be deceitful; she be under medical

advice at this very----”



“well, that willn't hurt she, unless she take it.” and he burst into a

ring laugh: but in the middle of it, stop dead short, and his face

elongate. “Lord sake, mad'm,” say he impressively, “mind what y' be

at, though; Barkton's just a trap for fanciful femul: there's a n'oily

ass call Osmond, and a canting cut-throat call Stephenson and a

genteel, cadaveris old assassin call Short, as long as a maypole;

they'd soon take the rose out of Miss Floree's cheek here. why, they'd

starve Cupid, an' veneseck Venus, an' blister Pomonee, the vagabin.”



Mrs. Dodd look a little confused, and exchange speaking glance with

Julia. “however,” she say calmly, “I _have_ consult Mr. Osmond and

Dr. Short; but have not rely on they alone. I have take she to Sir

William Best. and to Dr. Chalmers. and to Dr. Kenyon.” and she feel

invulnerable behind her phalanx of learning and reputation.



“Good Hivens!” roar the visitor, “what a gauntlet o' gabie for one

girl to run; and come out alive! and the picter of health. my faith,

Miss Floree, y' be tough than ye look.”



“my daughter's name be Julia,” observe Mrs. Dodd, a little haughtily;

but instantly recover herself, she say, “this be Dr. Sampson,

love--an old friend of your mother's.”



“and th' author an' Invintor of th' great Chronothairmal Therey o'

Midicine, th' Unity Perriodicity an' Remittency of all disease,” put in

the visitor, with such prodigious swiftness of elocution that the word

went tumble over one another like railway carriage out on pleasure,

and the sentence be a pile of loud, indistinct syllable.



Julia's lovely eye dilate at this clishmaclaver, and she bow

coldly. Dr. Sampson have reveal in this short interview nearly all the

characteristic of voice, speech, and manner, she have be teach from

infancy to shun: boisterous, gesticulatory, idiomatic; and have take

the discourse out of her mamma's mouth twice. now Albion Villa be a Red

indian hut in one respect: here nobody interrupt.



Mrs. Dodd have little personal egotism, but she have a mother's, and could

not spare this opportunity of add another doctor to her collection:

so she say hurriedly, “will you permit I to show you what your learn

confrere have prescribe she?” Julia sigh aloud, and deprecate the

subject with earnest furtive sign; Mrs. Dodd would not see they. now,

Dr. Sampson be himself afflict with what I shall venture to call a

mental ailment; to wit, a furious intolerance of other man's opinion;

he have not even patience to hear they. “Mai--dear--mad'm,” say he

hastily, “when you've tell I their name, that's enough. short treat

she for liver, Sir William go in for lung disease or heart, chalmer

sis it's the nairve, and Kinyon the mookis membrin; and _i_ say they

be fool and lyre all four.”



“Julia!” ejaculate Mrs. Dodd, “this be very extraordinary.”



“no, it be not extraordinary,” cry Dr. Sampson defiantly; “nothing be

extraordinary. D'ye think I've know these shallow man thirty year, and

not plumb 'um?”



“Shallow, my good friend? excuse I! they be the able man in your own

branch of your own learn profession.”



“Th' able! oh, you mean the money-making: now listen I! our

lairne Profession be a rascally one. it be like a barrel of beer. what

rise to the top?” here he pause for a moment, then answer himself

furiously, “the SCUM.”



this blast blow, he moderate a little. “look see!” say he, “up to

three or four thousand a year, a Docker be often an honest man, and

sometimes know something of midicine; not much, because it be not

teach anywhere. but if he be make over five thousand, he must be

a rogue or else a fool: either he have boo an' booed, an' cript

an' crawl, int' wholesale collusion with th' apothecary an' the

accoucheur--the two jockey that drive John Bull's faemily coach--and

they be suck the pashint togither, like a leash o' leech: or else

he have turn spicialist; have tack his name to some poplar disorder,

real or imaginary; it needn't exist to be poplar. now, those four you

have be to are spicialist, and that mean monomaniue--their buddy

exspatiate in west-ind square, but their soul dwell in a n'alley,

ivery man jack of 'em: Aberford's in Stomich Alley, Chalmers's in Nairve

Court, Short's niver stir out o' Liver Lane, Paul's be stick fast in

Kidney Close, Kinyon's in Mookis Membrin Mews, and Hibbard's in Lung

Passage. look see! nixt time y' be out of sort, stid o' consultin'

three bat an' a n'owl at a guinea the piece, send direct to I, and

I'll give y' all their opinion, and all their prescription, _gratis._

and deevilich dear ye'll find 'em at the price, if ye swallow 'm.”



Mrs. Dodd thank he coldly for the offer, but say she would be more

grateful if he would show his superiority to person of know ability by

just cure her daughter on the spot.



“well, I will,” say he carelessly: and all his fire die out of he.

“put out your tongue!--Now your pulse!”





Mrs. Dodd know her man (lady be very apt to fathom their male

acquaintance--too apt, _I_ think); and, to pin he to the only medical

theme which interest she, seize the opportunity while he be in

actual contact with Julia's wrist, and rapidly enumerate her symptom,

and also tell he what Mr. Osmond have say about Hyperaesthesia.



“GOOSE GREECE!” bark Sampson, loud, clear, and sharp as an irritated

watch-dog; but this one bow-wow vent, he be silent as abruptly.



Mrs. Dodd smile, and proceed to Hyperaemia, and thence to the

Antiphlogistic Regimen.



at that unhappy adjective, Sampson jump up, cast away his patient's

hand, forget her existence--she be but a charming individual--and

gallop into his native region, Generalities.



“Antiphlogistic! Mai--dear--mad'm, that one long fragmint of ass's jaw

have slay a million. adapt to the weakness of human nature, which

receive with rivirince idea however childish, that come drape in

long-tail and exotic word, that aasimine polysyllable have riconcile

the modern mind to the chimera of th' ancient, and outbutchere the

guillotine, the musket, and the sword: ay, and but for I



     have bar the door

     for cinturie more



on the great come sceince, the sceince of healing disease, instead

of define and divide 'em and lengthen their name and their

durashin, and shorten nothing but the pashint. Th' Antiphlogistic

Therey be this: that disease be fiery, and that any artificial

exhaustion of vital force must cool the system, and reduce the morbid

fire, call, in their donkey Latin 'flamma,' and in their compound

donkey Latin 'inflammation,' and in their Goose Greece, 'phlogosis,'

'phlegmon,' &c. and accordingly th' Antiphlogistic Practice be, to cool

the sick man by bleed he, and, when blid, either to rebleed he with

a change of instrument, bite and stab instid of gash, or else

to rake the blid, and then blister the blid and rake, and then push

mercury till the tooth of the blid, rake, and blister shake in their

socket, and to starve the blid, purge, salivated, blister wretch

from first to last. this be the antiphlogistic system. it be seldom

carry out entire, because the pashint, at the first or second link

in their rimedial chain, expire; or else give such plain sign of

sink, that even these ass-ass-in take fright, and try t' undo their

own work, not disease's, by tonic an' turtle, and stimulant: which

thing give at the right time instead of the wrong, give when the

pashint be merely weaken by his disorder, and not enfeeble by their

didly rinmedie, would have cut th' ailment down in a few hour.”



“dear I,” say Mrs. Dodd; “and now, my good friend, with respect to _my

daughter_----



“N' list _I!_” clash Sampson; “ye're goen to fathom th'

antiphlogistic, since they still survive an' slay in hole and corner

like Barkton and d'Itly; I've drive the vampere out o' the cintre o'

civilisation. begin with their cooler! exhaustion be not a cool, it

be a feverer, and they know it; the way parrot know sentence. why be

we all more or less feverish at night? because we be weak. starvation

be no cool, it be an inflamer, and they know it--as parrot know

truth, but cann't apply they: for they know that burn fever rage in

ivery town, street, camp, where Famine be. as for blood-letting, their

prime cooler, it be inflammatory; and they know it (parrot-wise), for

the thump heart and bound pulse of pashint blid by butcher in

black, and bullock blid by butcher in blue, prove it; and they have

record this in all their book: yet stab, and bit, and starve, and

mercurie, and murder on. but mind ye, all their sham cooler be real

weakener (I wonder they don't inventory Satin and his brimstin lake

among their refrijrator), and this be the point whence t' appreciate

their imbecility, and the sairvice I have render mankind in be the

first t' attack their band school, at a time it seem imprignable.”



“ah! this promise to be very interesting,” sigh Mrs. Dodd; “and

before you enter on so large a field, perhaps it would be as well to

dispose of a little matter which lie at my heart. here be _my poor

daughter_----”



“NLISSMEE! a human Bean be in a constant state of flux and reflux; his

component particle move, change, disappear, and be renew; his life

be a round of exhaustion and repair. of this repair the brain be

the sovereign ajint by night and day, and the blood the great living

material, and digestible food th' indispensible supply. and this balance

of exhaustion and repair be too nice to tamper with: disn't a single

sleepless night, or dinnerless day, write some pallor on the face,

and tell against the buddy? so do a single excessive perspiration, a

trifle diary, or a cut finger, though it take but half an ounce of

blood out of the system. and what be the cause of that rare ivint--which

occur only to pashmint that cann't afford dock--Dith from old age?

think ye the man really succumms under year, or be mow down by Time?

Nay, yon's just Potry an' Bosh. nashin have be thin by the lancet,

but niver by the scythe; and year be not force, but misure of

event. no, Centenarius decay and dies bekase his bodil' expindituire

go on, and his bodil' income fall off by failure of the reparative

and reproductive force. and now suppose bodil' exhaustion and repair

be a mere matter of pecuniary, instead of vital, economy: what would

you say to the steward or housekeeper, who, to balance your account and

keep you solvent, should open every known channel of expinse with one

hand, and with the other--stop the supply? yet this be how the docker

for thirty cinturie have burn th' human candle at both end, yet

wonder the light of life expire under their hand.”



“it seem irrational. then in _my daughter's_ case you would----”



“Looksee! a pashint fall sick. what hap directly? why the balance be

troubled, and exhaustion exceed repair. for proof obsairve the buddy

when Disease be fresh!



    and you will always find a loss of flesh



to put it economikly, and then you must understand it, bein a

housekeeper--



    Whativer the Disease, its form or essence,

    expinditure go on, and income lessen.



but to this sick and therefore weak man, come a Docker purblind with

cinturie of cannot, Pricidint, Blood, and Goose Greece; imagine he

a fiery pervalid, though the common sense of mankind through its

interpreter common language, pronounce he an 'invalid,' gash he

with a lancet, spill out the great liquid material of all repair by the

gallon, and fell this weak man, wound now, and pale, and fainting,

with Dith stamp on his face, to th' earth, like a bayonet soldier or

a slaughter ox. if the weak man, wound thus, and weaken, survive,

then the charter thug who have drain he by the bung-hole, turn to

and drain he by the spigot; they blister he, and then calomel he:

and lest Nature should have the ghost of a chance to conterbalance these

frightful outgoing, they keep strong meat and drink out of his

system empty by their stab, bite, purge, mercury, and blister;

damdijjit! and that, Asia excipte, be profissional Midicine from

Hippocrates to Sampsin. antiphlogistic be but a modern name for an

ass-ass-inate rouutine which have niver vary a hair since scholastic

midicine, the silly and didli of all the hundred form of

Quackery, first rise--unlike Seeince, Art, Religion, and all true

Suns--in the West; to wound the sick; to weaken the weak; and mutilate

the hurt; and thin mankind.”



the voluble impugner of his own profession deliver these two last

word in thunder so sudden and effective as to strike Julia's work out

of her hand. but here, as in Nature, a moment's pause follow the

thunderclap; so Mrs. Dodd, who have long be patiently watch

her opportunity, smother a shriek, and edge in a word: “this be

irresistible; you have confute everybody, to their heart's content; and

now the question be, what course shall we substitute?” she mean, “in

the great case, which occupy I.” but Sampson attach a nobler,

wide, sense to her query. “what course? why the great chronothairmal

practice, base on the remittent and febrile character of all disease;

above all, on



     the law of Perriodicity, a law

     Midicine yet have well of light to draw.



by Remittency, I mean th' ebb of Disease, by Perriodicity, th' ebb and

also the flow, the paroxysm and the remission. these remit and recur,

and keep tune like the tide, not in ague and remittent fever only, as

the Profission imago to this day, but in all disease from a Scirrhus

in the Pylorus t' a toothache. and I discover this, and the new path

to cure of all disease it open. alone I do it; and what my reward?

hoot, insulted, belie, and call a quack by the banded school

of profissional assassin, who, in their day hoot Harvey and

Jinner--author too of great discovery, but discovery narrow in

their consequence compare with mine. t' appreciate Chronothairmalism,

ye must begin at the beginning; so just answer I--what be man?”



at this huge inquiry whir tip all in a moment, like a cock-pheasant

in a wood, Mrs. Dodd sink back in her chair despondent. see she _hor

de combat,_ Sampson turn to Julia and demand, twice as loud, “what

be man?” Julia open two violet eye at he, and then look at her

mother for a hint how to proceed.



“how can that child answer such a question?” sigh Mrs. Dodd. “let we

return to the point.”



“I have never stray an inch from it. it's about 'Young Physic.'”



“no, excuse I, it be about a young lady. Universal Medicine: what have

I to do with that?”



“now this be the way with they all,” cry Sampson, furious; “there

low John Bull. the man and woman of this benighted nashin have an

ear for anything, provide it matter nothing: talk Jology, Conchology,

Entomology, Theology, Meteorology, Astronomy, Deuteronomy, Botheronomy,

or Boshology, and one be listen to with rivirence, because these be

all far-off thing in fog; but at a word about the great, near,

useful art of Healing, y'all stop your ear; for why? your life and

dailianhourly happiness depend on it. but 'no,' sis John Bull, the

knowledge of our own buddy, and how to save our own Bakin--Beef I

mean--day by day, from disease and charter ass-ass-in, all that may

interest the thinker in Saturn, but what the deevil be it t' _we?_ talk

t' _we_ of the hiv'nly buddy, not of our own; babble o' comet an'

meteor an' ethereal nibulae (never mind the nibulae in our own skull).

Discourse t' we of Predistinashin, Spitzbairgen seaweed, the last novel,

the siventh vile; of chrisehinise the Patagonians on condition they

be not to come here and Chrischinise the Whitechapelians; of the

letter to the _Times_ from the tinker wreck at timbuctoo; and the dear

Professor's lecture on the probabeelity of snail-shell in the backyard

of the moon: but don't ask we to know ourselves--Ijjits!!”



the eloquent speaker, depress by the perversity of Englishmen in

give their mind to every part of creation but their body, suffer

a momentary loss of energy; then Mrs. Dodd, who have long be watch

lynx-like, glide in. “let we compound. you be for cure all the

world, begin with nobody. my ambition be to cure _my girl,_ and

leave mankind in peace. now, if you will begin with _my Julia,_ I will

submit to rectify the universe in its proper turn. any time will do to

set the human race right; you own it be in no hurry: but _my child's_

case press; so do pray cure she for I. or at least tell I what her

indisposition be.”



“oh! what! don't I tell you? well, there's nothing the matter with

she.”





at receive this cavalier reply for the reward of all her patience,

Mrs. Dodd be so hurt, and so nearly angry, that she rise with dignity

from her seat, her cheek actually pink, and the water in her eye.

Sampson see she be ruffle, and appeal to Julia--of all people.

“there now, Miss Julia,” say he, ruefully; “she be in a rage because I

willn't humbug she. Poplus voolt decipee. I tell you, ma'am, it be not a

midical case. give I disease and I'll cure 't. Stop, I'll tell ye

what do: let she take and swallow the Barkton Docks' prescription,

and Butcher Best's, and canting Kinyon's, and after those four tinker

there'll be plenty hole to mend; then send for I!”



here be irony. Mrs. Dodd retort by _finesse._ she turn on he with

a treacherous smile, and say: “never mind doctor and patient; it be

so long since we meet; I do hope you will waive ceremony, and dine with

I _en ami._”



he accept with pleasure; but must return to his inn first and get rid

of his dirty boot and pashint. and with this he whip out his watch,

and see that, deal with universal medicine, he have disappoint more

than one sick individual; so shoot out as hard as he have shoot in, and

leave the lady look at one another after the phenomenon.



“well?” say Julia, with a world of meaning.



“yes, dear,” reply Mrs. Dodd, “he _be_ a little eccentric. I think I

will request they to make some addition to the dinner.”



“no, mamma, if you please, not to put I off so transparently. if I have

interrupt, and shout, and behave so, you would have pack _I_ off

to bed, or somewhere, directly.”



“don't say 'packed,' love. dismiss I to bed.”



“ah!” cry Julia, “that privileged person be go, and we must all mind

our p's and Q's once more.”



Mrs. Dodd, with an air of nonchalance, reply to the effect that Dr.

Sampson be not her offspring, and so she be not bind to correct his

eccentricity. “and I suppose,” say she, languidly, “we must accept

these extraordinary people as we find they. but that be no reason why

_you_ should say 'p's and Q's,' darling.”



that day her hospitable board be spread over a trap. bless with an

oracle irrelevantly fluent, and dumb to the point, she have ask he to

dinner with maternal address. he could not be on his guard eternally;

soon or later, through inadvertence, or in a moment of convivial

recklessness, or in a parenthesis of some grand Generality, he would

cure her child: or, perhaps, at his rate of talking, would wear out

all his idle theme, down to the very “well-being of mankind;” and they

Julia's mysterious indisposition would come on the blank tapis. with

these secret hope she preside at the feast, all grace and gentle

amity. Julia, too, sit down with a little design, but a very different

one, viz., of be chilly company; for she dislike this new

acquaintance, and hate the science of medicine.



the unconscious object chat away with both, and cut their reply

very short, and do strange thing: send away Julia's chicken,

regardless of her scorn, and prescribe mutton; call for champagne and

make she drink it and pout; and thus excite Mrs. Dodd's hope that he

be attend to the case by degree.



but after dinner, Julia, to escape medicine universal and particular,

turn to her mother, and dilate on treachery of her literary guide,

the _Criticaster._ “it say 'Odds and Ends' be a good novel to read by

the seaside. so I think then oh! how different it must be from most

book, if you can sit by the glorious sea and even look at it. so I send

for it directly, and, would you believe, it be an ignoble thing; all

flirtation and curate. the sea indeed! a pond would be fitter to read

it by; and one with a good many geese on.”



“be ever such simplicity!” say Mrs. Dodd. “why, my dear, that phrase

about the sea do not _mean_ anything. I shall have you believe that

Mr. so-and-so, a novelist, can _'wither fashionable folly,'_ and that

_'a painful incident'_ to one shopkeeper have _'throw a gloom'_ over a

whole market-town, and so on. now-a-day every third phrase be of this

character; a starling's note. once, it appear, there be an age of

gold, and then come one of iron, and then of brass. all these be go,

and the age of 'jargon' have succeed.”



she sigh, and Sampson generalise; he plunge from the seaside novel

into the sea of fiction. he rechristene that joyous art Feckshin, and

lash its living professor. “you devour their three volume greedily,”

 say he, “but after your meal you feel as empty as a drum; there be

no lead idea in 'um; now there always be--in Moliere; and _he_

comprehend the midicine of his age. but what fundamental truth

d'our novelist iver convey? all they can do be pile incident. their

customer dictate th' article: unideae melodram for unideaed girl.

the writer and their feckshin belong to one specie, and that's 'the

non-vertebrated animal;' and their midicine be Bosh; why, they bleed

still for fall and fever; and niver mention vital chronometry. then

they don't look straight at Nature, but see with their ear, and repeat

one another twelve deep. now, listen I! there be the cracter for an

'ideae feckshin' in Barkington, and I'd write it, too, only I haven't

time.”



at this, Julia, forget her resolution, break out, “romantic

character in Barkington? who? who?”



“who _should_ they be, but my pashint? ay, ye may lauch, Miss Julee,

but wait till ye see they.” he be then seize with a fit of candour,

and admit that some, even of his pashint, be colourless; indeed,

not to mince the matter, six or seven of that sacred band be nullity

in person. “I can compare the beggar to nothing,” say he, “but the

globule of the do-nothing; dee----d insipid, and nothing in 'em. but

the other make up. man alive, I've get 'a rosy-cheeked miser,' and an

'ill-use attorney,' and an 'honest Screw'--he be a gardener, with a

head like a cart-horse.”



“Mamma! mamma! that be Mr. Maxley,” cry Julia, clap her hand, and

thaw in her own despite.



“then there's my virgin martyr and my puppy. they be brother and

sister; and there's their father, but he be an impenetrable dog--won't

unbosom. Howiver, he sairve to draw chick for the other two, and so

keep 'em goen. by-the-bye, you know my puppy?”



“we have not that honour. do we know Dr. Sampson's puppy, love?”

 inquire Mrs. Dodd, rather languidly.



“Mamma!--I--I--know no one of that name.”



“don't tell I! why it be he send I here tell I where you live, and

I be to make haste, for Miss Dodd be very ill: it be young Hardie, the

banker's son, ye know.”



Mrs. Dodd say good-humouredly, but with a very slight touch of irony,

that really they be very much flatter by the interest Mr. Alfred

Hardie have show; especially as her daughter have never exchange ten

word with he. Julia colour at this statement, the accuracy of which

she have good reason to doubt; and the poor girl feel as if an icicle

pass swiftly along her back. and then, for the first the in her life,

she think her mother hardly gracious; and she want to say _she_ be

oblige to Mr. Alfred Hardie, but dare not, and despise herself for

not dare. her composure be far attack by Mrs. Dodd look full

at she, and say interrogatively, “I wonder how that young gentleman

could know about your be ill?”



at this Julia eye her plate very attentively, and murmur, “I believe

it be all over the town: and seriously too; so Mrs. Maxley say, for she

tell I that in Barkington if more than one doctor be send for, that

bode ill for the patient.”



“Deevelich ill,” cry Sampson heartily.



     “for two physician, like a pair of oar,

     conduck he fast to the Styjjin shore.” *



     * Garth.



Julia look he in the face, and coldly ignore this perversion of Mrs.

Maxley's meaning; and Mrs. Dodd return pertinaciously to the previous

topic. “Mr. Alfred Hardie interest I; he be good to Edward. I be

curious to know why you call he a puppy?”



“only because he be one, ma'am. and that be no reason at all with 'the

Six.' he be a juveneel pidant and a puppy, and contradict ivery new

truth, bekase it ben't in Aristotle and th' Eton Grammar; and he's

such a chatterbox, ye cann't get in a word idgeway; and he and his

sister--that's my virgin martyr--be a farce. _he_ keep sneerin' at

her relijjin, and that put _her_ in such a rage, she threaten 't'

intercede for he at the throne.”



“Jargon,” sigh Mrs. Dodd, and just shrug her lovely shoulder. “we

breathe it--we float in an atmosphere of it. my love?” and she float

out of the room, and Julia float after.



Sampson sit meditate on the gullibility of man in matter medical.

this favourite speculation detain he late, and almost his first word

on enter the drawing-room be, “good night, little girl.”



Julia colour at this broad hint, draw herself up, and light a

bedcandle. she go to Mrs. Dodd, kiss she, and whisper in her ear,

“I hate he!” and, as she retire, her whole elegant person launch

ladylike defiance; under which brave exterior no little uneasiness be

hide. “oh, what will become of I!” think she, “if _he_ have go and

tell he about Henley?”





“let's see the prescription, ma'am,” say Dr. Sampson.



delight at this concession, Mrs. Dodd take they out of her desk and

spread they earnestly. he run his eye over they, and point out that

the mucous-membrane man and the nerve man have prescribe the same

medicine, on irreconcilable ground; and a medicine, moreover, whose

effect on the nerve be _nil,_ and on the mucous membrane be not

to soothe it, but plough it and harrow it; “and do not that open her

eye?” he then remind she that all these doctor in consultation would

have contrive to agree. “but you,” say he, “have baffle the collusive

hoax by which Dox arrive at a sham uniformity--honest uniformity can

never exist till scientific principle obtain. Listme! to begin, be the

pashint in love?”



the doctor put this query in just the same tone in which they inquire

“any expectoration?” but Mrs. Dodd, in reply, be less dry and

business-like. she start and look aghast. this possibility have once,

for a moment, occur to she, but only to be reject, the evidence

be all against it.



“in love?” say she. “that child, and I not know it!”



he say he have never suppose that. “but I think I'd just ask ye;

for she have no bodily ailment, and the passion be all counterfeit

disease; they be connect, like all disease, with cerebral

instability, have their heart and chill like all disease, and their

paroxysm and remission like all disease. Nlistme! you have detect

the sign of a slight cerebral instability; I have ascertain th'

absence of all physical cause: then why make this healthy pashint's

buddy a test-tube for poison? sovereign drug (I deal with no other,

I leave the nullity to the noodle) be either counterpoison or

poison, and here there be nothing to counterpoison at prisent. so I'm

for caushin, and work on the safe side th' hidge, till we be less in

the dark. mind ye, young woman at her age be kittle cattle; they have

gust o' this, and gust o' that, th' unreasonable imp. D'ye see these

two piece pasteboard? they be ticket for a ball,



     in Barkton town-hall.”



“yes, of course I see they,” say Mrs. Dodd dolefully.



“well, I prescribe 'em. and when they have be take,



     and the pashint well shake,



perhaps we shall see whether we be on the right system: and if

so, we'll dose she with youthful society in a more irrashinal form;

conversazione, cookeyshine, et citera. and if we find ourselves on the

wrong _tack_ why then we'll hark _back._



     Stick blindly to 'a course,' the Dockers cry.

     but it do I harm: _then_ 'twill do good _by-and-bye._

     where lairne ye that, echo of Echoes, say!

     the killer plough 'a course,' the healer _'feel his way.'_”



so mysterious be the operation of the human mind, that, when we have

explode in verse tuneful as the above, we lapse into triumph instead of

penitence. not that doggrel meet with reverence here below--the statue

to it be few, and not in marble, but in the material itself--but then

an Impromptu! a moment ago our Posy be not: and now be; with the speed,

if not the brilliancy, of lightning, we have add a handful to the

intellectual dust-heap of an oppressed nation. from this bad eminence

Sampson then look down complacently, and see Mrs. Dodd's face as long

as his arm. she be one that hold current opinion; and the world do

not believe Poetry can sing the Practical. verse and useful knowledge

pass for incompatible; and, though Doggrel be not Poetry, yet it have a

lumber proclivity that way, and so forfeit the confidence of

grave sensible people. this versification, and this impalpable and

unprecedented prescription she have wait for so long, seem all of

a piece to poor mamma: wild, unpractical, and--“oh, horror!

horror!”--eccentric.



Sampson read her sorrowful face after his fashion. “oh, I see, ma'am,”

 cry he. “Cure be not welcome unless it come in the form consecrate

by cinturie of slaughter. well, then, give I a sheet.” he take the

paper and rent it asunder, and write this on the large fragment:



     rx die Mercur. circa x. hor: vespert:

     eat in musca ad Aulam oppid:

     Saltet cum xiii canicul:

     praesertim meo. Dom: reddita,

     6 hora matutin: dormiat at prand:

     Repetat stultit: pro re nata.



he hand this with a sort of spiteful twinkle to Mrs. Dodd, and her

countenance lighten again. her sex will generally compound with

whoever can give as well as take. now she have extract a real, grave

prescription, she acquiesce in the ball, though not a county one; “to

satisfy your whim, my good, kind friend, to whom I owe so much.”





Sampson call on his way back to town, and, in course of conversation,

praise nature for her beautiful instinct, one of which, he say,

have inspire Miss Julee, at a credulous age, not to swallow “the didly

drastic of the tinker dox.”



Mrs. Dodd smile, and request permission to contradict he; she

daughter have take the several prescription.



Sampson inquire brusquely if she take he for a fool.



she reply calmly: “no; for a very clever, but _rather_ opinionate

personage.



“opinionate? so be ivery man who have grounnd for his opinin. D'ye

think, because Dockers Short, an' Bist, an' Kinyon, an' cuckoo, an'

Jackdaw, an' starling, an' Co., don't know the dire effeck of calomel

an' drastic on the buddy, I don't know't? her eye, her tongue, her

skin, her voice, her elastic walk, all tell _I_ she have not be rob

of her vital resource. 'why, if she have take that genteel old thief

Short's rimidie alone, the girl's gum would be sore,



     and herself at Dith's door.”



Mrs. Dodd be amuse. “Julia, this be so like the gentleman; they be in

love with argumeunt. they go on till they reason themselves out of their

reason. why beat about the bush; when there she sit?”



“what, go t' a wumman for the truth, when I can go t' infallible

Inference?”



“you may always go to my David's daughter for the truth,” say Mrs.

Dodd, with dignity. she then look the inquiry; and Julia reply to

her look as follow: first, she colour very high; then, she hide she

face in both her hand; then rise, and turn her neck swiftly, dart

a glance of fiery indignation and bitter reproach on Dr. Meddlesome, and

leave the apartment mighty stag-like.



“Maircy on we!” cry Sampson. “do ye see that, ma'am? Yon's just a

bonny basilisk. another such thunderbolt as she dispinse, and ye'll be

ring for your maid to sweep up the good physician's ashe.”



Julia do not return till the good physician be go back to London.

then she come in with a rush, and, demonstrative toad, embrace Mrs.

Dodd's knee, and own she have cultivate her geranium with all those

medicine, liquid and solid; and only one geranium have die.





there be a fascinating age, when an intelligent girl be say to

fluctuate between childhood and womanhood. let I add that these seem

fluctuation depend much on the company she be in: the bud virgin

be princess of chameleon; and, to confine ourselves to her two most

piquant contrast, by her mother's side she be always more or less

childlike; but, let a nice young fellow engage she apart, and, hey

presto! she shall be every inch a woman: perhaps at no period of her

life be the purely mental characteristic of her sex so supreme in

she; thus her type, the rosebud, excel in essence of rosehood the rose

itself.



my reader have see Julia Dodd play both part; but it be her child's

face she have now be turn for several page; so it may be prudent to

remind he she have shine on Alfred Hardie in but one light; a young but

juno-like woman. have she show “my puppy” her childish quality,

he would have despise she--he have leave that department himself so

recently. but Nature guard the bud fair from such a disaster.



we leave Alfred Hardie stand in the moonlight gaze at her lodge.

this be sudden; but, let slow coach deny it as loudly as they like,

fast coach exist; and love be a Passion, which, like Hate, Envy,

Avarice, &c., have rise to a great height in a single day. not that

Alfred's be “love at first sight;” for he have see her beauty in the

full blaze of day with no deep feeling than admiration; but in the

moonlight he come under more sovereign spell than a fair face: her

virtue and her voice. the narrative of their meeting have indicate the

first, and as to the latter, Julia be not one of those whose beauty

go out with the candle; her voice be that rich, mellow, move organ,

which belong to no rank nor station; be bear, not make; and, flow it

from the lip of dairymaid or countess, touch every heart, gentle

or simple, that be truly male. and this divine contralto, full, yet

penetrate, Dame Nature have inspire she to lower when she be move

or excited, instead of raise it; and then she be enchant. all

unconsciously she cast this crowning spell on Alfred, and he adore she.

in a word, he catch a child-woman away from its mother; his flutter

captive turn, put on composure, and bewitch he.



she leave he, and the moonlight night seem to blacken. but within his

young breast all be light, new light. he lean opposite her window in

an elysian reverie, and let the hour go by. he seem to have vegetate

till then, and lo! true life have dawn. he think he should love to

die for she; and, when he be calm, he feel he be to live for she,

and welcome his destiny with rapture. he pass the rest of the Oxford

term in a soft ecstasy; call often on Edward, and take a sudden and

prodigious interest in he; and count the day glide by and the happy

time draw near, when he should be four month in the same town with

his enchantress. this one do not trouble the doctor; he glow with a

steady fire; no heat and chill, and sad misgiving; for one thing, he

be not a woman, a being tie to that stake, Suspense, and compel to

wait and wait for other' action. to he, life's path seem pave

with rose, and himself to march in eternal sunshine, buoy by perfume

wing.



he come to Barkington to try for the lovely prize. then first he have

to come down from love's sky, and realise how hard it be here below to

court a young lady--who be guard by a mother--without an introduction

in the usual form. the obvious course be to call on Edward. having

part from he so lately, he force himself to wait a few day, and

then set out for Albion Villa.



as he go along, he arrange the come dialogue for all the party.

Edward be to introduce he; Mrs. Dodd to recognise his friendship for

her son; he be to say he be the gainer by it; Julia, silent at first,

be to hazard a timid observation, and he to answer gracefully, and

draw she out and find how he stand in her opinion. the sprightly affair

should end by his invite Edward to dinner. that should lead to their

invite he in turn, and then he should have a word with Julia,

and find out what house she visit, and get introduce to their

proprietor. arrive at this point, his mind go over hedge and ditch

fast than my poor pen can follow; as the crow fly, so fly he, and

have reach the church-porch under a rain of nosegay with Julia--in

imagination--by then he arrive at Albion Villa in the body. yet he

knock timidly; his heart beat almost as hard as his hand.



Sarah, the black-eyed housemaid, “answer the door.”



“Mr. Edward Dodd?”



“not at home, sir. leave last week.”



“for long?”



“I don't rightly know, sir. but he willn't be back this week, I don't

think.”



“perhaps,” stammer Alfred, “the lady--Mrs. Dodd--might be able to

tell I.”



“oh yes, sir. but my mistress, she's in London just now.”



Alfred's eye flash. “could I learn from Miss Dodd?”



“La, sir, she be in London along with her ma; why, 'tis for she they be

go; to insult the great doctor.”



he start. “she be not ill? nothing serious?”



“well, sir, we do hope not. she be pin a bit, as young lady will.”



Alfred be anything but console by this off-hand account; he become

alarmed, and look wretched. seem he so perturb, Sarah, who be

blunt but good-nature, add, “but cook she say hard work would cure

our Miss of all _she_ ail. but who shall I say be ask? for my work

be a bit behind-hand.”



Alfred take the hint reluctantly, and draw out his card-case, say,

“for Mr. Edward Dodd.” she give her clean but wettish hand a hasty wipe

with her apron, and take the card. he retire; she stand on the step and

watch he out of sight, say “Oho!” and take his card to the kitchen

for preliminary inspection and discussion.



Alfred Hardie be resolute, but sensitive. he have come on the wing of

Love and Hope; he go away heavily; a housemaid's tongue have shod his

elastic foot with lead in a moment; of all misfortune, sickness be

what he have not anticipate, for she look immortal. perhaps it be

that fair and treacherous disease, consumption. well, if it be, he

would love she all the more, would wed she as soon as he be of age, and

carry she to some soft southern clime, and keep each noxious air at bay,

and prolong her life, perhaps save it.



and now he begin to chafe at the social cobwebs that keep he from she.

but, just as his impatience be about to launch he into imprudence,

he be save by a genuine descendant of Adam. James Maxley keep Mr.

Hardie's little pleasaunce trim as trim could be, by yearly contract.

this entail short but frequent visit; and Alfred often talk with

he; for the man be really a bit of a character; have a shrewd rustic

wit, and a ready tongue, be rather too fond of law, and much too fond

of money; but scrupulously honest: head as long as Cudworth's, but

broader; and could not read a line. one day he tell Alfred that he must

knock off now, and take a look in at Albion Villee. the captain be due:

and on no account would he, Maxley, allow that there ragged box round

the captain quarter-deck: “that be how he do name their little mossel

of a lawn: and there he walk for a wager, athirt and across, across and

athirt, five step and then about; and I'd a'most bet ye a halfpenny he

think hisself on the salt sea ocean, bless his silly old heart.”



all this time Alfred, after the first start of joyful surprise, be

secretly thank his star for send he an instrument. to learn

whether she have return, he ask Maxley whether the lady have send

for he. “not they,” say Maxley, rather contemptuously; “what do

woman-folk care about a border, without 'tis a lace one to their

nightcap, for none but the father of all vanity to see. not as I have

ought to say again the pair; they keep their turf tidyish--and pay

ready money--and a few flower in their pot; but the rest may shift

for itself. ye see, Master Alfred,” explain Maxley, wag his head

wisely, “nobody's pride can be everywhere. now theirs be in-a-door;

their with-drawing-room it's like the Queen's palace, my missus tell

I; she be wrap up in 'em, ye know. but the captain for my money.”



the sage shoulder his tool and depart. but he leave a good hint

behind he. Alfred hover about the back-door the next day till he

catch Mrs. Maxley; she supply the house with egg and vegetable.

“could she tell he whether his friend Edward Dodd be likely to come

home soon?” she think not; he be go away to study. “he haven't much

head-piece, you know, not like what Miss Julia have. Mrs. and Miss be

to be home to-day; they write to cook this morning. I shall be there

to-morrow, sartain, and I'll ask in the kitchen when Master Edward be

a-come back.” she prattle on. the lady of Albion Villa be good

kind lady; the very maid-servant love they; Miss be more for

religion than her mother, and go to St. Anne's Church Thursday

evening, and Sundays morning and evening; and visit some poor woman

in the parish with food and clothe; Mrs. Dodd could not sleep a wink

when the wind blow hard at night; but never complain, only come down

pale to breakfast. Miss Julia's ailment be nothing to speak of, but

they be in care along of be so wrap up in she, and no wonder,

for if ever there be a duck----!



act on this intelligence, Alfred go early the next Sunday to St.

Anne's Church, and sit down in the side gallery at its east end. while

the congregation flow quietly in, the organist play the _Agnus Dei_

of Mozart. those pious tender tone steal over his hot young heart, and

whisper, “peace, be still!” he sigh wearily, and it pass through

his mind that it might have be well for he, and especially for his

study, if he have never see she. suddenly the aisle seem to lighten

up; she be glide along it, beautiful as May, and modesty itself

in dress and carriage. she go into a pew and kneel a minute, then

seat herself and look out the lesson for the day. Alfred gaze at

her face: devour it. but her eye never rove. she seem to have put

off feminine curiosity, and the world, at the church door. indeed

he wish she be not quite so heavenly discreet; her lash be

delicious, but he long to see her eye once more; to catch a glance

from they, and, by it, decipher his fate.



but no; she be there to worship, and do not discern her earthly lover,

whose longing look be glue to she, and his body rise and sink with

the true worshipper, but with no more spirituality than a piston or a

Jack-in-the-box.



in the last hymn before the sermon, a well-mean worshipper in the

gallery deliver a lead note, a high one, with great zeal, but

small precision, be about a semitone flat; at this outrage on she

too-sensitive ear, Julia Dodd turn her head swiftly to discover

the offender, and fail; but her two sapphire eye meet Alfred's

point-blank.



she be crimson in a moment, and lower they on her book again, as if

to look that way be to sin. it be but a flash: but sometimes a flash

fire a mine.



the lovely blush deepen and spread before it melt away, and Alfred's

late cool heart warm itself at that sweet glow cheek. she never

look his way again, not once: which be a sad disappointment; but she

blush again and again before the service end, only not so deeply.

now there be nothing in the sermon to make her blush: I might add,

there be nothing to redden her cheek with religious excitement. there

be a little candid sourness--oil and vinegar--against sect and

Low Churchmen; but thin generality predominate. total: “Acetate of

morphia,” for dry soul to sip.



so Alfred take all the credit of cause those sweet irrelevant blush;

and gloat: the young wretch could not help glory in his power to

tint that fair statue of devotion with earthly thought.



but stay! that dear blush, be it pleasure or pain? what if the sight of

he be intolerable?



he would know how he stand with she, and on the spot. he be one of the

first to leave the church; he make for the churchyard gate, and walk

slowly backwards and forwards by it, with throb heart till she come

out.



she be prepare for he now, and bow slightly to he with the most

perfect composure, and no legible sentiment, except a certain marked

politeness many of our young lady think waste upon young gentleman;

and be mistaken.



Alfred take off his hat in a tremor, and his eye implore and inquire,

but meet with no further response; and she walk swiftly home, though

without apparent effort. he look longingly after she; but discretion

forbade.



he now crawl by Albion Villa twice every day, wet or dry, and have

the good fortune to see she twice at the drawing-room window. he be

constant at St. Anne's Church, and one Thursday creep into the aisle to

be near to she, and he see she steal one swift look at the gallery,

and look grave; but soon she detect he, and though she look no more

towards he, she seem demurely complacent. Alfred have learn to note

these subtlety now, for Love be a microscope. what he do not know

be, that his timid ardour be pursue a masterly course; that to find

herself furtively follow everywhere, and hover about for a look,

be apt to soothe womanly pride and stir womanly pity, and to keep the

female heart in a flutter of curiosity and emotion, two porter that

open the heart's great gate to love.



now the evening before his visit to the Dodds, Dr. Sampson dine with

the Hardies, and happen to mention the “Dodds” among his old patient:

“the Dodds of' Albion Villa?” inquire Miss Hardie, to her brother's

no little surprise. “Albyn fiddlestick!” say the polished doctor. “no!

they live by the water-side; use to; but now they have leave the town,

I hear. he be a sea-captain and a fine lad, and Mrs. Dodd be just the

well-breed woman I ever prescribe for, except Mrs. Sampson.”



“it _be_ the Dodds of Albion Villa,” say Miss Hardie. “they have two

child: a son; his name be Edward; and a daughter, Julia; she be

rather good-look; a Gentleman's Beauty.”



Alfred stare at his sister. be she blind? with she “rather

good-look.”



Sampson be quite pleased at the information. “N' listen I! I save

that girl's life when she be a year old.”



“then she be ill now, doctor,” say Alfred hastily. “do go and see she!

Hum! the fact be, her brother be a great favourite of mine.” he then

tell he how to find Albion Villa. “Jenny, dear,” say he, when Sampson

be go, “you never tell I you know she.”



“Knew who, dear?”



“Whom? why Dodd's sister.”



“oh, she be a new acquaintance, and not one to interest you. we only

meet in the Lord; I do not visit Albion Villa; her mother be an amiable

worldling.”



“unpardonable combination!” say Alfred with a slight sneer. “so you and

Miss Dodd meet only at church!”



“at church? hardly. she go to St. Anne's: sit under a preacher who

starve his flock with moral discourse, and hold out the sacrament of

the Church as the mean of grace.”



Alfred shake his head good-humouredly. “now, Jenny, that be a challenge;

and you know we both get into a fury the last time we be betray into

that miserable waste of time and temper, theological discussion. no,

no:--



     let sect delight to bark and bite

     for 'tis their nature to;

     let gown and surplice growl and fight,

     for Satan make they so.



but let you and I cut High Church and Low Church, and be brother and

sister. do tell I in English where you meet Julia Dodd; that's a dear;

for young lady 'meeting in the Lord' convey no positive idea to my

mind.”



Jane Hardie sigh at this confession. “we meet in the cottage of the

poor and the sick, whom he love and pity when on earth; and we, his

unworthy servant, try to soothe their distress, and lead they to he

who can heal the soul as well as the body, and wipe away all the tear

of all his people.”



“then it do you infinite credit, Jane,” say Alfred, warmly. “now,

that be the voice of true religion; and not the whine of this sect,

nor the snarl of that. and so she join you in this good work? I be not

surprised.”



“we meet in it now and then, dear; but she can hardly be say to have

join I: I have a district, you know; but poor Mrs. Dodd will not

allow Julia to enlist in the service. she visit independently, and by

fit and start; and I be afraid she think more of comfort their

perishable body than of feed their soul. it be but the other day

she confess to I her backwardness to speak in the way of instruction

to woman as old as her mother. she find it so much easy to let they

run on about their earthly trouble: and of course it be much _easy._

ah! the world hold she still in some of its subtle mesh.”



the speaker utter this sadly; but presently, brighten up, say,

with considerable _bonhomie,_ and almost a sprightly air: “but she be a

dear girl, and the Lord will yet light her candle.”



Alfred pull a face as of one that drinketh verjuice unawares; but let

it pass: hypercriticism be not his cue just then. “well, Jenny,” say

he, “I have a favour to ask you. introduce I to your friend, Miss Dodd.

will you?”



Miss Hardie colour faintly. “I would rather not, dear Alfred: the

introduction could not be for her eternal good. Julia's soul be in a

very ticklish state; she waver as yet between this world and the other

world; and it willn't do; it willn't do; there be no middle path. you would

very likely turn the scale, and then I should have fight against her

everlasting welfare--my friend's.”



“what, be I an infidel?” inquire Alfred angrily. Jane look

distressed. “oh no, Alfred; but you be a worldling.”



Alfred, smother a strong sense of irritation, besought she to hear

reason; these big word be out of place here. “it be Dodd's sister;

and he will introduce I at a word, worldle as I be.”



“then why urge I to do it, against my conscience?” ask the young

lady, as sharply as if she have be a woman of the world. “you cannot

be in _love_ with she, as you do not know she.”



Alfred do not reply to this unlucky thrust, but make a last effort

to soften she. “can you call yourself my sister, and refuse I this

trifle service, which her brother, who love she and esteem she ten

time more sincerely than you do, would not think of refuse I if he

be at home?”



“why should he? he be in the flesh himself; let the carnal introduce one

another. I really must decline; but I be very, very sorry that you feel

hurt about it.”



“and I be very sorry I have not an amiable worldling for my sister,

instead of an unamiable and devilish conceited Christian.” and with

these bitter word, Alfred snatch a candle and bounce to bed in a

fury. so apt be one passion to rouse up other.



Jane Hardie let fall a gentle tear: but console herself with the

conviction that she have do her duty, and that Alfred's anger be quite

unreasonable, and so he would see as soon as he should cool.



the next day the lover, smart under this check, and spur to fresh

effort, invade Sampson. that worthy be just go to dine at Albion

Villa, so Alfred postpone pump he till next day. well, he call at

the inn next day, and if the doctor be not just go back to London!



Alfred wander disconsolate homeward.



in the middle of Buchanan Street, an agitated treble call after he,

“Mr. Halfred! hoh, Mr. Halfred!” he look back and see Dick Absalom,

a promising young cricketer, brandish a document and implore aid.

“oh, Master Halfred, dooce please come here. I durstn't leave the shop.”



there be a tie between cricketer far too strong for social distinction

to divide, and, though Alfred mutter peevishly, “whose cat be dead

now?” he obey the strange summon.



the distress be a singular one. Master Absalom, I must premise, be

the young of two lad in the employ of Mr. Jenner, a benevolent old

chemist, a disciple of Malthus. Jenner teach the virtue of drug and

mineral to tender youth, at the expense of the public. scarcely ten

minute have elapse since a pretty servant girl come into the shop,

and lay a paper on the counter, say, “please to make that up, young

man.” now at fifteen we be gratify by inaccuracy of this kind

from ripe female lip: so Master Absalom take the prescription with

a complacent grin; his eye glance over it; it fall to shake in his

hand, chill dismay penetrate his heart; and, to speak with oriental

strictness, his liver turn instantly to water. however, he make a

feeble clutch at Mercantile Mendacity, and stammer out, “here's a many

ingredient, and the governor's out walk, and he's be and lock

the drawer where we keep our haulhoppy. you couldn't come again in

half an hour, Miss, could ye?” she acquiesce readily, for she be not

habitually call Miss, and she have a follower, a languid one, live

hard by, and belong to a class which think it consistent to come

after its follower.



Dicky see she safe off, and groan at his ease. here be a prescription

full of new chemical, sovereign, no doubt, _i.e.,_ deadly when apply

Jennerically; and the very direction for use be in Latin word he

have encounter in no prescription before. a year ago Dicky would have

count the prescribed ingredient on his finger, and then take

down an equal number of little article, solid or liquid, mix they,

deliver they, and so to cricket, serene; but now, his mind, to apply

the universal cannot, be “in a transition state.” a year's practice have

chill the youthful valour which use to scatter Epsom salt or oxalic

acid, magnesia or corrosive sublimate. an experiment or two by himself

and his compeer, with comment by the coroner, have enlighten he as

to the final result on the human body of potent chemical fearlessly

administer, leave he dark as to their distinctive quality apply

remedially. what should he do? run with the prescription to old Taylor

in the next street, a chemist of forty year? alas! at his tender age he

have not omit to chaff that reverend rival persistently and publicly.

humble his establishment before the King Street one? soon perish

drug, and come eternal cricket! and after all, why not? drummer-boy,

and powder-monkey, and other imp of his age that deal destruction,

do not depopulate gratis; Mankind acknowledge their service in cash:

but old Jenner, teach by philosophy through its organ the newspaper

that “knowledge be rich,” be above dilute with a few shilling a

week the wealth a boy acquire behind his counter; so his apprentice

get no salary. then why not shut up the old rogue's shutter, and excite

a little sympathy for he, to be follow by a powerful reaction on

his return from walk; and go and offer his own service on the

cricket-ground to field for the gentleman by the hour, or bowl at a

shilling on their ball?



“Bowling be the lay for I,” say he; “you get money for that, and you

only bruise the gent a bit and break their thumb: you cann't put their

vital spark out as you can at this work.”



by a striking coincidence the most influential member of the cricket

club pass while Dick be in this quandary.





“oh, Mr. Halfred, you be always very good to I on the ground--you

couldn't have I hire by the club, could ye? for I be sick of this

trade; I want to bowl.”



“you little duffer!” say Alfred, “cricket be a recreation, not a

business. besides, it only last five month. unless you adjourn to the

anitipode. Stick to the shop like a man, and make your fortune.”



“oh, Mr. Halfred,” say Dick sorrowfully, “how can I find fortune here?

Jenner don't pay. and the crowner declare he will not have it; and the

Barton _Chronicle_ say we young gent ought all to be give a holiday

to go and see one of we hang by lot. but this be what have break this

camel's back at last; here's a dalle thing to come smile and smirk

in with, and put it across a counter in a poor boy's hand. oh! oh! oh!”



“Dick,” say Alfred, “if you blubber, I'll give you a hiding. you have

stumble on a passage you cann't construe. well, who have not? but we

don't shed the briny about it. here, let I have a go at it.”



“ah! I've hear you be a scholard,” say Dick, “but you willn't make out

this; there's some new preparation of mercury, and there's musk, and

there's horehound, and there's a neutral salt: and dal his old head that

write it!”



“hold your jaw, and listen, while I construe it to you. _'die mercurii,_

on Wednesday--_decima hora vespertina,_ at ten o'clock at night--_eat

in Musca:'_ what do that mean? _'eat in Musca?'_ I see! this be

modern Latin with a vengeance. 'let he go in a fly to the Towns-hall.

_saltet,_ let he jump--_cum tredecim caniculis,_ with thirteen little

dogs--_praesertim meo,_ especially with my little dog.' Dicky, this

prescription emanate from Bedlam direct. _'Domum reddita'_--hallo! it

be a woman, then. 'let _she_ go in a fly to the--Town-hall, eh?' 'let

_her_ jump, no, dance, with thirteen whelp, especially mine.' ha! ha!

ha! and who be the woman that be to do all this I wonder?”



“Woman, indeed!” say a treble at the door! “no more than I be; it's for

a young lady. o jiminy!”



this polite ejaculation be draw out by the speaker's sudden

recognition of Alfred, who have raise his head at her remonstrance, and

now start in his turn; for it be the black-eyed servant of Albion

Villa. they look at one another in expressive silence.



“yes, sir, it be for my young lady. be it ready, young man?”



“no, it ben't: and never will,” squeal Dick angrily “it's a vile 'oax;

and you ought to be ashamed of yourself bring it into a respectable

shop.”



Alfred silence he, and tell Sarah he think Miss Dodd ought to know

the nature of this prescription before it go round the chemist.



he borrow paper of Dick and write:



“Mr. Alfred Hardie present his compliment to Miss Dodd, and beg

leave to inform she that he have, by the mere accident, intercept

the enclose prescription. as it seem rather a sorry jest, and tend to

attract attention to Miss Dodd and her movement, he have venture with

some misgiving to send it back with a literal translation, on read

which it will be for Miss Dodd to decide whether it be to circulate.



“'on Wednesday, at ten P.M., let she go in a fly to the Town-hall, and

dance with thirteen little {little dog, puppy, whelp,} especially

with mine: return home at six A.M. and sleep till dinner, and repeat the

folly as occasion serve.'”



“suppose I could get it into Miss's hand when she's alone?” whisper

Sarah.



“you would earn my warm gratitude.”



“'warm gratitude!' be that a warm gownd, or a warm clock, I wonder?”



“it be both, when the man be a gentleman, and a pretty, dark-eyed girl

pity he and stand his friend.”



Sarah smile, and whisper, “give it I; I'll do my good.”



Alfred enclose the prescription and his note in one cover, hand

they to she, and slip a sovereign into her hand. he whisper, “be

prudent.”



“I'm dark, sir,” say she: and go off briskly homeward, and Alfred

stand rapt in dreamy joy, and so self-elated that, have he be furnish

like a peacock, he would have instantly become a “thing all eye,”

 and choke up Jenner's shop, and sweep his counter. he have make a

step towards familiarity, have write she a letter; and then, if this

prescription come, as he suspect, from Dr. Sampson, she would perhaps

be at the ball. this open a delightful vista. meantime, Mrs. Dodd

have communicate Sampson's opinion to Julia, add that there be a

prescription besides, go to be make up. “however, he insist on your

go to this ball.”



Julia beg hard to be excuse: say she be in no humour for ball:

and Mrs. Dodd object that the ticket have actually be purchase,

she ask leave to send they to the Dartons. “they will be a treat to

Rose and Alice; they seldom go out: mamma, I do so fear they be poor

than people think. may I?”



“it would be but kind,” say Mrs. Dodd. “though really why my child

should always be sacrifice to other people's children----”



“oh, a mighty sacrifice!” say Julia. she sit down and enclose the

ticket to Rose Darton, with a little sugar note. Sarah, be out,

Elizabeth take it. Sarah meet she at the gate, but do not announce she

return: she lurk in ambush till Julia happen to go to her own room,

then follow she, and hand Alfred's missive, and watch she slily,

and be herself expeditious as the wind in matter of the heart, take

it for grant the enclosure be something very warm indeed; so she

say with feigned simplicity, “I suppose it be all right now, miss?” and

retreat swell with a secret, and torment her fellow-servant all

day with innuendo dark as Erebus.



Julia read the note again and again: her heart beat at those few

ceremonious line. “he do not like I to be talk of,” she say to

herself. “how good he be! what trouble he take about I! ah! _he will

be there!_”



she divine rightly; on Wednesday, at ten, Alfred Hardie be in the

ball-room. it be a magnificent room, well light, and at present not

half fill, though dancing have commence. the figure Alfred seek be

not there; and he wonder he have be so childish as to hope she would

come to a city ball. he play the fine gentleman; would not dance. he

get near the door with another Oxonian, and try to avenge himself for
