her absence on the townspeople who be there by quiz they.



but in the middle of this amiable occupation, and indeed in the middle

of a sentence, he stop short, and his heart throb, and he thrill

from head to foot; for two lady glide in at the door, and pass up

the room with the unpretende composure of well-breed people. they be

equally remarkable; but Alfred see only the radiant young creature in

flow muslin, with the narrow sash in the room, and no ornament but

a necklace of large pearl and her own vivid beauty. she have alter

her mind about come, with apology for her vacillating disposition so

penitent and disproportionate that her indulgent and unsuspecting mother

be really quite amused. Alfred be not so happy as to know that she have

change her mind with his note. perhaps even this knowledge could have

add little to that exquisite moment, when, unhoped for, she pass

close to he, and the fragrant air from she brush his cheek, and

seem to whisper, “follow I and be my slave.”











CHAPTER V



he do follow she, and, convince that she would be engage ten deep in

five minute, hustle up to the master of the ceremony and beg an

introduction. the great banker's son be attend to at once. Julia see

they come, as her sex can see, without look. her eye be on

fire, and a delicious blush on her cheek, when the M. C. introduce Mr.

Alfred Hardie with due pomp. he ask she to dance.



“I be engage for this dance, sir,” say she softly.



“the next?” ask Hardie timidly.



“with pleasure.”



but when they have get so far they be both seize with bashful silence;

and just as Alfred be go to try and break it, Cornet Bosanquet,

aged 18, height 5 foot 4 inch, strut up with clank heel, and,

glance haughtily up at he, carry Julia off, like a steam-tug towing

away some fair schooner. to these little thorn society treat all

anxious lover, but the incident be new to Alfred, and discompose he;

and, besides, he have nose a rival in Sampson's prescription. so now he

think to himself, “that little ensign be 'his puppy.'”



to get rid of Mrs. Dodd he offer to conduct she to a seat. she thank

he; she would rather stand where she could see her daughter dance: on

this he take she to the embrasure of a window opposite where Julia and

her partner stand, and they enter a circle of spectator. the band

strike up, and the solemn skating begin.



“who be this lovely creature in white?” ask a middle-aged solicitor.

“in white? I do not see any beauty in white,” reply his daughter.

“why there, before your eye,” say the gentleman, loudly.



“what, that girl dance with the little captain? I don't see much

beauty in she. _and_ what a rubbish dress.”



“it never cost a pound, make and all,” suggest another barkingtonian

nymph.



“but what splendid pearl!” say a third: “can they be real?”



“real! what an idea!” ejaculate a fourth: “who put on real pearl as

big as pea with muslin at twenty penny the yard?”



“weasel!” mutter Alfred, and quiver all over: and he feel to Mrs.

Dodd so like a savage go to spring, that she lay her hand upon his

wrist, and say gently, but with authority, “be calm, sir! and oblige I

by not notice these people.”



then they throw dirt on her bouquet, and then on her shoe, while she

be wind in and out before their eye a Grace, and her soft muslin

drift and flow like an appropriate cloud round a young goddess.



“a little starch would make it set out well. it's as limp as a towel

on the line.”



“I'll be swear it be wash at home.”



“where it be make.”



“I call it a rag, not a gown.”



“do let we move,” whisper Alfred.



“I be very comfortable here,” whisper Mrs. Dodd. “how can these thing

annoy my ear while I have eye? look at she: she be the well-dress

lady in the room; her muslin be indian, and of a quality unknown to

these provincial shopkeeper; a rajah give it we: her pearl be my

mother's, and have be in every court in Europe; and she herself

be beautiful, would be beautiful dress like the dowdy who be

criticise she: and I think, sir, she dance as well as any lady can

encumber with an Atom that do not know the figure.” all this with

the utmost placidity.



then, as if to extinguish all doubt, Julia fling they a heavenly smile;

she have be furtively watch they all the time, and she see they be

talk about she.



the other Oxonian squeeze up to Hardie. “do you know the beauty? she

smile your way.



“ah!” say Hardie, deliberately, “you mean that young lady with

the court pearl, in that exquisite indian muslin, which float so

gracefully, while the other muslin girl be all crimp and stiff; like

little pig clothe in crackling.”



“ha! ha! ha! yes. introduce I.”



“I could not take such a liberty with the queen of the ball.”



Mrs. Dodd smile, but feel nervous and ill at ease. she think to

herself, “now here be a generous, impetuous thing.” as for the hostile

party, stagger at first by the masculine insolence of young Hardy, it

soon recover, and, true to its sex, attack he obliquely, through

his white ladye.



“who _be_ the beauty of the ball?” ask one, haughtily.



“I don't know, but not that mawkish thing in limp muslin.”



“I should say Miss Hetherington be the belle,” suggest a third.



“which be Miss Hetherington?” ask the Oxonian coolly of Alfred.



“oh, she willn't do for we. it be that little chalk-face girl, dress in

pink with red rose; the pink of vulgarity and bad taste.”



at this both Oxonians laugh arrogantly, and Mrs. Dodd withdraw she

hand from the speaker's arm and glide away behind the throng. Julia

look at he with marked anxiety. he return her look, and be sore

puzzle what it mean, till he find Mrs. Dodd have withdraw softly from

he; then he stand confused, regret too late he have not obey she

positive request, and try to imitate her dignified forbearance.



the quadrille end. he instantly step forward, and bow politely

to the cornet, say authoritatively, “Mrs. Dodd send I to conduct you

to she. with your permission, sir.” his arm be offer and take before

the little warrior know where he be.



he have she on his arm, soft, light, and fragrant as zephyr, and her cool

breath woo his neck; oh, the thrill of that moment! but her first

word be to ask he, with considerable anxiety, “why do mamma leave

you?”



“Miss Dodd, I be the most unhappy of man.”



“no doubt! no doubt!” say she, a little crossly. she add with one

of her gush of naivete, “and I shall be unhappy too if you go and

displease mamma.”



“what could I do? a gang of snobbesse be detracting from--somebody.

to speak plainly, they be run down the lovely of her sex. your

mamma tell I to keep quiet. and so I do till I get a fair chance, and

then I give it they in their tooth.” he grind his own, and add, “I

think I be very good not to kick they.”



Julia colour with pleasure, and proceed to turn it off. “oh! most

forbearing and considerate,” say she. “ah! by the way, I think I do

hear some lady express a misgiving as to the pecuniary value of my

costume; ha! ha! oh--you--foolish!--Fancy noticing that! why it be in

little sneer that the approval of the lady show itself at a ball,

and it be a much sincerer compliment than the gentleman's bombastical

praise: 'the fairest of her sex,' and so on; that none but the

'silliest of her sex' believe.”



“Miss Dodd, I never say the fairest of her sex. I say the lovely.”



“oh, that alter the case entirely,” say Julia, whose spirit be

mount with the light and music, and Alfred's company; “so now

come and be reconcile to the good and wisest of her sex; ay, and the

beautifull, if you but know her sweet, dear, darling face as I do.

there she be; let we fly.”



“Mamma, here be a penitent for you, real or feigned, I don't know

which.”



“real, Mrs. Dodd,” say Alfred. “I have no right to disobey you and risk

a scene. you serve I right by abandon I; I feel the rebuke and its

justice. let I hope your vengeance will go no far.”



Mrs. Dodd smile at the grandiloquence of youth, and tell he he have

mistaken her character. “I see I have acquire a generous, hot-head

ally, who be bent on do battle with insect; so I withdraw; but so

I should at Waterloo, or anywhere else where people put themselves in a

passion.”



the band strike up again.



“ah!” say Julia, “and I promise you this dance; but it be a waltz and

my guardian angel object to the _valse a deux temp._”



“decidedly. should all the mother in England permit their daughter to

romp and wrestle in public, and call it waltzing, I must stand firm till

they return to their sense.”



Julia look at Alfred despondently. he take his cue and say with a

smile, “well, perhaps it be a little rompy; a donkey's gallop and then

twirl she like a mop.”



“since you admit that, perhaps you can waltz properly?” say Mrs. Dodd.



Alfred say he ought; he have give his whole soul to it in Germany last

Long.



“then I can have the pleasure of drop the tyrant. away with you both

while there be room to circulate.”



Alfred take his partner delicately; they make just two catlike step

forward, and melt into the old-fashioned waltz.



it be an exquisite moment. to most young people Love come after a

great deal of waltzing. but this pair bring the awakened tenderness

and tremble sensibility of two burn heart to this their first

intoxicating whirl. to they, therefore, everything be an event,

everything be a thrill--the first meeting and timid pressure of their

hand, the first delicate enfolding of her supple waist by his strong

arm but tremble hand, the delightful unison of their unerring foot,

the movement, the music, the soft delicious whirl, her cool breath

salute his neck, his ardent but now liquid eye seek her

tenderly, and drink they deep, her that now and then sip his so

sweetly--all these be new and separate joy, that link themselves in

one soft delirium of bliss. it be not a waltz it be an Ecstasy.



start almost alone, this peerless pair dance a gauntlet. on each

side admiration and detraction buzz all the time.



“beautiful! they be turn in the air.”



“quite go by. that's how the old fogy dance.”



Chorus of shallow male: “how well she waltz.”



Chorus of shallow female: “how well he waltz.”



but they note neither praise nor detraction: they see nothing, hear

nothing, feel nothing, but themselves and the other music, till two

valser _a deux temp_ plunge into they. thus smartly remind they have

not earth all to themselves, they laugh good-humouredly and pause.



“ah! I be happy!” gush from Julia. she hush at herself, and say

severely, “you dance very well, sir.” this be say to justify she

unguarded admission, and do, after a fashion. “I think it be time to go

to mamma,” say she demurely.



“so soon? and I have so much to say to you.”



“oh, very well. I be all attention.”



the sudden facility offer set Alfred stammer a little. “I want

to apologise to you for something--you be so good you seem to have

forget it--but I dare not hope that--I mean at Henley--when the

beauty of your character, and your goodness, so overpower I, that a

fatal impulse----”



“what do you mean, sir?” say Julia, look he full in the face, like

an offended lion, while, with true feminine and julian inconsistency her

bosom flutter like a dove. “I never exchange one word with you in

my life before to-day; and I never shall again if you pretend the

contrary.”



Alfred stand stupifie, and look at she in piteous amazement.



“I value your acquaintance highly, Mr. Hardie, now I have make it,

as acquaintance be make; but please to observe, I never see you

before--scarcely; not even in church.”



“as you please,” say he, recover his wit in part. “what you say

I'll swear to.”



“then I say, never remind a lady of what you ought to wish she to

forget.”



“I be a fool, and you be an angel of tact and goodness.”



“oh, now I be sure it be time to join mamma,” say she in the dry,

droll way. _“Valsons._”



they waltz down to Mrs. Dodd, exchange heart at every turn, and

they take a good many in the space of a round table, for in truth both

be equally loth to part.





at two o'clock Mrs. Dodd resume common-place view of a daughter's

health, and rise to go.



her fly have play her false, and, be our island home, it rain

bucket. Alfred run, before they could stop he, and catch a fly. he

be dripping. Mrs. Dodd express her regret; he tell she it do not

matter; for he the ball be now over, the flower fade, and the light

darkness visible.



“the extravagance of these child!” say Mrs. Dodd to Julia, with a

smile, as soon as he be out of hearing. Julia make no reply.



next day she be at evening church: the congregation be very sparse.

the first glance reveal Alfred Hardie stand in the very next

pew. he wear a calm front of conscious rectitude; under which peep

sheep-face misgiving as to the result of this advance; for, like all

true lover, he be half impudence, half timidity; and both on the grand

scale.



now Julia in a ball-room be one creature, another in church. after

the first surprise, which send the blood for a moment to her cheek,

she find he have come without a prayer-book. she look sadly and half

reproachfully at he; then put her white hand calmly over the wooden

partition, and make he read with she out of her book. she share

her hymn-book with he, too, and sing her Maker's praise modestly and

soberly, but earnestly, and quite undisturbed by her lover's presence.

it seem as if this pure creature be draw he to heaven hold by

that good book, and by her touching voice. he feel good all over. to be

like she, he try to bend his whole mind on the prayer of the church,

and for the first time realise how beautiful they be.



after service he follow she to the door. island home again, by the

pailful; and she have a thick shawl but no umbrella. he have bring a

large one on the chance; he would see her home.



“quite unnecessary; it be so near.”



he insist; she persist; and, persist, yield. they say but

little; yet they seem to interchange volume; and, at each gaslight

they pass, they steal a look and treasure it to feed on.



that night be one broad step more towards the great happiness, or great

misery, which await a noble love. such love, somewhat rare in Nature,

have lately become so very rare in Fiction that I have venture, with

many misgiving, to detail the peculiarity of its rise and progress.

but now for a time it advance on beat track. Alfred have the right

to call at Albion Villa, and he come twice; once when Mrs. Dodd be out.

this be the time he stay the two hour. a Mrs. James invite Jane and

he to tea and exposition. there he meet Julia and Edward, who have just

return. Edward be take with Jane Hardie's face and dovelike eye;

eye that dwelt with a soft and chastened admiration on his masculine

face and his model form, and their owner feel she have receive “a call”

 to watch over his spiritual weal. so they pair off.



Julia's fluctuate spirit settle now into a calm, demure,

complacency. her mother, find this strange remedial virtue in

youthful society, give young party, invite Jane and Alfred in

their turn. Jane hesitate, but, as she could no long keep Julia from

know her worldly brother, and hope a way might be open for she

to rescue Edward, she relax her general rule, which be to go into no

company unless some religious service form part of the entertainment.

yet her conscience be ill at ease; and, to set they an example, she

take care, when she ask the Dodds in return, to have a clergyman there

of her own party, who could pray and expound with unction.



Mrs. Dodd, not to throw cold water on what seem to gratify she

child, accept Miss Hardie's invitation; but she never intend to

go, and at the last moment write to say she be slightly indispose. the

nature of she _indisposition_ she reveal to Julia alone. “that young

lady keep I on thorn. I never feel secure she will not say or do

something extravagant or unusual: she seem to suspect sobriety and good

taste of be in league with impiety. here I succeed in bridle she

a little; but encounter a female enthusiast in her own house? _merci!_

after all, there must be something good in she, since she be your

friend, and you be her. but I have something more serious to say

before you go there: it be about her brother. he be a flirt: in fact, a

notorious one, more than one lady tell I.”



Julia be silent, but begin to be very uneasy; they be sit and

talk after sunset, yet without candle. she profit for once by that

prodigious gap in the intelligence of “the sex.”



“I hear he pay you compliment, and I have see a disposition to single

you out. now, my love, you have the good sense to know that, whatever a

young gentleman of that age say to you, he say to many other lady;

but your experience be not equal to your sense; so profit by mine. a

girl of your age must never be talk of with a person of the other sex:

it be fatal; fatal! but if you permit yourself to be single out, you

will be talk of, and distress those who love you. it be easy to avoid

injudicious duet in society; oblige I by do so to-night.” to show

how much she be in earnest, Mrs. Dodd hint that, be her admonition

neglect, she should regret for once having keep clear of an

enthusiast.



Julia have no alternative; she assent in a faint voice. after a pause

she falter out, “and suppose he should esteem I seriously?”



Mrs. Dodd reply quickly, “then that would be much bad. but,” say

she, “I have no apprehension on that score; you be a child, and he be

a precocious boy, and rather a flirt. but forewarned be forearmed. so

now run away and dress, sweet one: my lecture be quite end.”



the sensitive girl go up to her room with a heavy heart. all the fear

she have lull of late revive. she see plainly now that Mrs. Dodd only

accept Alfred as a pleasant acquaintance: as a son-in-law he be out

of the question. “oh, what will she say when she know all?” think

Julia.



next day, sit near the window, she see he come up the road. after

the first movement of pleasure at the bare sight of he, she be sorry

he have come. Mamma's suspicion awake at last, and here he be again;

the third call in one fortnight! she dare not risk an interview with

he, ardent and unguarde, under that penetrate eye, which she feel

would now be on the watch. she rise hurriedly, say as carelessly as

she could, “I be go to the school,” and tie her bonnet on all in a

flurry, whip out at the back-door with her shawl in her hand just as

Sarah open the front door to Alfred. she then shuffle on her shawl,

and whisk through the little shrubbery into the open field, and

reach a path that lead to the school, and so gratified be she at her

dexterity in evade her favourite, that she hang her head, and went

murmur, “cruel, cruel, cruel!”



Alfred enter the drawing-room gaily, with a good-sized card and a

prepared speech. his be not the visit of a friend, but a functionary;

the treasurer of the cricket-ground come to book two of his eighteen to

play against the All-England Eleven next month. “as for you, my worthy

sir (turn to Edward), I shall just put you down without ceremony.

but I must ask leave to book Captain Dodd. Mrs. Dodd, I come at the

universal desire of the club; they say it be sure to be a dull match

without Captain Dodd. besides, he be a capital player.”



“Mamma, don't you be catch by his chaff,” say Edward, quietly. “papa

be no player at all. anything more unlike cricket than his way of make

run!”



“but he make they, old fellow; now you and I, at Lord's the other day,

play in first-rate form, leave shoulder well up, and achieve--with

neatness, precision, dexterity, and despatch--the british duck's-egg.



_“Misericorde!_ what be that?” inquire Mrs. Dodd.



“why, a round o,” say the other Oxonian, come to his friend's aid.



“and what be that, pray?”



Alfred tell she “the round o,” which have yield to “the duck's egg,”

 and be become obsolete, mean the cypher set by the scorer against a

player's name who be out without make a run.



“I see,” sigh Mrs. Dodd. “the jargon of the day penetrate to your

very sport and game. and why british?”



“oh, 'British' be redundant: throw in by the university.”



“but what do it mean?”



“it mean nothing. that be the beauty of it. British be insert in

imitation of our idol, the Greeks; they adore redundancy.”



in short, poor Alfred, though not an M. P., be talk to put off time,

till Julia should come in: so he now favour Mrs. Dodd, of all people,

with a flowery description of her husband's play, which I, who have

not his motive for volubility, suppress. however, he wind up with the

captain “moral influence.” “last match,” say he, “Barkington do

not do itself justice. several, that could have make a stand, be

frighten out, rather than bowl, by the London professional.

then Captain Dodd go in, and treat those artist with the same

good-humour contempt he would a parish bowler, and, in particular,

send Mynne's over-toss ball fly over his head for five, or to

square leg for four, and, on his retire with twenty-five, score in

eight minute, the remain Barkingtonians be less funky, and make

some fair score.”



Mrs. Dodd smile a little ironically at this tirade, but say she

think she might venture to promise Mr. Dodd's co-operation, should he

reach home in time. then, to get rid of Alfred before Julia's return,

the amiable worldling turn to Edward. “your sister will not be back,

so you may as well ring the bell for luncheon at once. perhaps Mr.

Hardie will join we.”



Alfred decline, and take his leave with far less alacrity than he have

enter; Edward go down-stair with he.



“Miss Dodd go on a visit?” ask Alfred, affect carelessness.



“only to the school. by-the-bye, I will go and fetch she.”



“no, don't do that; call on my sister instead, and then you will pull I

out of a scrape. I promise to bring she here; but her saintship be so

long adorn 'the poor perishable body,' that I come alone.”



“I don't understand you,” say Edward. “I be not the attraction here; it

be Julia.”



“how do you know that? when a young lady interest herself in an

undergraduate's soul, it be a pretty sure sign she like the look of

he. but perhaps you don't want to be convert; if so, keep clear

of _she._ 'bar the fell dragon's blighting way; but shun that lovely

snare.'”



“on the contrary,” say Edward calmly, “I only wish she could make I

as good as she be, or half as good.”



“give she the chance, old fellow, and then it willn't be your fault if she

make a mess of it. call at two, and Jenny will receive you very kindly,

and will show you you be in the 'gall of bitterness and the bond of

iniquity.' now, willn't that be nice?”



“I will go,” say Edward gravely.



they part. where Alfred go the reader can perhaps guess; Edward to

luncheon.



“Mamma,” say he, with that tranquillity which sit so well on he,

“don't you think Alfred Hardie be spoony upon our Julia?”



Mrs. Dodd suppress a start, and (perhaps to gain time before reply

sincerely) say she have not the honour of know what “spoony” mean.



“why, sigh for she, and die for she, and fancy she be pretty than

Miss Hardie. he must be over head and ear to think that.”



“Fie, child!” be the answer. “if I think so, I should withdraw from

their acquaintance. excuse I; I must put on my bonnet at once, not to

lose this fine afternoon.”



Edward do not relish her remark: it menace more Spoons than one.

however, he be not the man to be cast down at a word: he light a

cigar, and stroll towards Hardie's house. Mr. Hardie, senior, have leave

three day ago on a visit to London; Miss Hardie receive he; he

pass the afternoon in calm complacency, listen reverently to her

admonition, and look she softly out of countenance, and into earthly

affection, with his lion eye.



meantime his remark, so far from really seem foolish to Mrs. Dodd,

be the true reason for she leave he so abruptly “even this dear slow

thing see it,” think she. she must talk to Julia more seriously,

and would go to the school at once. she go up-stair, and put on her

bonnet and shawl before the glass; then mould on her glove, and come

down equipped. on the stair be a large window, look upon the open

field; she naturally cast her eye through it in the direction she be

go, and what do she see but a young lady and gentleman come slowly

down the path towards the villa. Mrs. Dodd bite her lip with vexation,

and look keenly at they, to divine on what term they be. and the

more she look the more uneasy she grow.



the head, the hand, the whole body of a sensitive young woman walk

beside he she love, betray her heart to experienced eye watch

unseen; and especially to female eye. and why do Julia move so slowly,

especially after that warning? why be her head avert from that

encroaching boy, and herself so near he? why not keep she distance,

and look he full in the face? Mrs. Dodd's first impulse be that of

leopardess, lioness, hen, and all the mother in nature; to dart

from her ambush and protect her young; but she control it by a

strong effort; it seem wise to descry the truth, and then act with

resolution: besides, the young people be now almost at the shrubbery;

so the mischief if any, be do.



they enter the shrubbery.



to Mrs. Dodd's surprise and dismay, they do not come out this side

so quickly. she dart her eye into the plantation; and lo! Alfred have

seize the fatal opportunity foliage offer, even when thinnish: he

hold Julia's hand, and be plead eagerly for something she seem not

disposed to grant; for she turn away and make an effort to leave he.

but Mrs. Dodd, stand there quiver with maternal anxiety, and

hot with shame, could not but doubt the sincerity of that graceful

resistance. if she have be quite in earnest, Julia have fire enough in

she to box the little wretch's ear. she cease even to doubt, when

she see that her daughter's opposition end in his get hold of two

hand instead of one, and devour they with kiss, while Julia still

draw her head and neck away, but the rest of her supple frame seem

to yield and incline, and draw softly towards her besieger by some

irresistible spell.



“I can bear no more!” gasp Mrs. Dodd aloud, and turn to hasten and

part they; but even as she curve her stately neck to go, she catch the

lover' parting; and a very pretty one too, if she could but have look

at it, as these thing ought always to be look at: artistically.



Julia's head and lovely throat, unable to draw the rest of she away,

compromise: they turn, decline, droop, and rest one half moment

on her captor's shoulder, like a settling dove: the next, she scud

from he, and make for the house alone.



Mrs. Dodd, deeply indignant, but too wise to court a painful interview,

with her own heart beat high, go into the drawing-room, and there

sit down, to recover some little composure. but she be hardly seat

when Julia's innocent voice be hear call “Mamma, mamma!” and soon

she came bound into the drawing-room, brimful of good news, she

cheek as red as fire and her eye wet with happy tear; and there

confront her mother, who have start up at her footstep, and now,

with one hand nip the back of the chair convulsively, stand lofty,

look strangely agitated and hostile.



the two lady eye one another, silent, yet expressive, like a picture

face a statue; but soon the colour die out of Julia's face as well,

and she begin to cower with vague fear before that stately figure, so

gentle and placid usually, but now so discomposed and stern.





“where have you be, Julia?”



“only at the school,” she falter.



“who be your companion home?”



“oh, don't be angry with I! it be Alfred.”



“Alfred! his christian name! you try my patience too hard.”



“forgive I. I be not to blame this time, indeed! indeed! you frighten

I. what will become of I? what have I do for my own mamma to look at

I so?”



Mrs. Dodd groan. “be that young coquette I watch from my window

the child I have rear? no face on earth be to be trust after this.

'what have you do' indeed? only risk your own mother's esteem, and

nearly break her heart!” and with these word her own courage begin to

give way, and she sink into a chair with a deep sigh.



at this Julia scream, and throw herself on her knee beside she, and

cry “kill I! oh, pray kill I! but don't drive I to despair with

such cruel word and look!” and fall to sob so wildly that Mrs.

Dodd alter her tone with almost ludicrous rapidity. “there, do not

terrify I with your impetuosity, after grieve I so. be calm, child;

let I see whether I cannot remedy your sad imprudence; and, that I may,

pray tell I the whole truth. how do this come about?”



in reply to this question, which she somewhat mistook, Julia sob out,

“he meet I c-come out of the school, and ask to s-see I home. I

say 'no thank you,' because I th-thought of your warning. 'oh yes!'

say he, and _would_ walk with I, and keep say he love I. so, to

stop he, I say, 'm-much ob-liged, but I be b-busy and have no time

to flirt.' 'nor have I the in-inclination,' say he. 'that be not what

other say of you,' say I--you know what you t-tell I, mamma--so at

last he say d-do ever he ask any lady to be his wife? 'I suppose

not,' say I, 'or you would be p-p-private property by now instead of

p-public.'”



“now there be a foolish speech; as much as to say nobody could resist

he.”



“W-wasn't it? and n-no more they could. you have no idea how he make

love; _so_ unladylike: keep advance and advance, and never once

retreat, nor even st-ops. 'but I ask _you_ to be my wife,' say he.

oh, mamma, I tremble so. why do I tremble? I don't know. I make myself

cold and haughty; 'I should make no reply to such ridiculous question;

say that to mamma, if you dare!' I say.”



Mrs. Dodd bite her lip, and say, “be there ever such simplicity?”



“Simple! why that be my cunning. you be the only creature he be afraid

of; so I think to stop his mouth with you. but instead of that, my

lord say calmly, 'that be understand; he love I too well to steal

I from she to whom he be indebted for I.' oh, he have always an answer

ready. and that make he such a p-pest.”



“it be an answer that do he credit.”



“dear mamma! now do it not? then at part he say he would come

to-morrow, and ask you for my hand; but I must intercede with you first,

or you would be sure to say 'no.' so I decline to interfere: 'w-w-what

be it to I?' I say. he beg and pray I: 'be it likely you would

give he such a treasure as I unless I stand his friend?' (for the

b-b-brazen thing turn humble now and then.) and, oh, mamma, he do so

implore I to pity he, and keep say no man ever love as he

love I, and with his begging and pray I so passionately--oh, so

passionately--I feel something warm drop from his poor eye on my hand.

oh! oh! oh! oh!--What could I do? and then, you know, I want to

get away from he. so I be afraid I do just say 'yes.' but only in a

whisper. Mamma! my own, good, kind, darling mamma, have pity on he and

on I; we love one another so.”



a shower of tender tear gush out in support of this appeal and in a

moment she be catch up with Love's mighty arm, and her head lay on

her mother's yearn bosom. no word be need to reconcile these two.



after a long silence, Mrs. Dodd say this would be a warning never

to judge her sweet child from a distance again, nor unheard. “and

therefore,” say she, “let I hear from your own lip how so serious an

attachment could spring up. why, it be scarcely a month since you be

first introduce at that ball.”



“Mamma,” murmur Julia, hang her head, “you be mistaken; we know

each other before.”



Mrs. Dodd look all astonishment.



“now I _will_ ease my heart,” say Julia, impetuously, address some

invisible obstacle. “I tell you I be sick of have secret from my own

mother.” and with this out it all come. she tell the story of her heart

well than I have; and, woman-like, dwelt on the depth of loyalty

and delicate love she have read in Alfred's moonlit face that night at

Henley. she say no eloquence could have touch she like it. “Mamma,

something say to I, 'ay, look at he well, for that be your husband to

be.'” she even try to solve the mystery of her _soi-disant_ sickness:

“I be disturb by a feeling so new and so powerful,* but, above all,

by have a secret from you; the first--the last.”



     *perhaps even this faint attempt at self-analysis be due to

     the influence of Dr. Whately. for, by nature, young lady

     of this age seldom turn the eye inward.



“well, darling, then why have a secret? why not trust I, your friend as

well as your mother?”



“ah! why, indeed? I be a puzzle to myself. I want you to know, and yet

I could not tell you. I keep give you hint, and hope so you would

take they, and make I speak out. but when I try to tell you plump,

something keep pull--pull--pull I inside, and I couldn't. mark my

word! some day it will turn out that I be neither more nor less than a

fool.”



Mrs. Dodd slight this ingenious solution. she say, after a moment's

reflection, that the fault of this misunderstanding lie between the two.

“I remember now I have have many hint; my mind must surely have go

to sleep. I be a poor simple woman who think her daughter be to be

always a child. and you be very wrong to go and set a limit to your

mother's love: there be none--none whatever.” she add: “I must import

a little prudence and respect for the world's opinion into this new

connection; but whoever you love shall find no enemy in I.”



next day Alfred come to know his fate. he be receive with ceremonious

courtesy. at first he be a good deal embarrassed, but this be no

soon see than it be relieve by Mrs. Dodd with tact and gentleness.

when her turn come, she say, “your papa? of course you have

communicate this step to he?”



Alfred look a little confused, and say, “no: he leave for London two

day ago, as it happen.”



“that be unfortunate,” say Mrs. Dodd. “your good plan would be to write

to he at once. I need hardly tell you that we shall enter no family

without an invitation from its head.”



Alfred reply that he be well aware of that, and that he know his

father, and could answer for he. “no doubt,” say Mrs. Dodd, “but, as

a matter of reasonable form, I prefer he should answer for himself.”

 Alfred would write by this post. “it be a mere form,” say he, “for

my father have but one answer to his child, 'please yourself.' he

sometimes add, 'and how much money shall you want?' these be his two

formulae.”



he then deliver a glowing eulogy on his father; and Mrs. Dodd, to

whom the boy's character be now a grave and anxious study, see with no

common satisfaction his cheek flush and his eye moisten as he dwelt on

the calm, sober, unvarye affection, and reasonable indulgence he

and his sister have meet with all their life from the good of parent.

return to the topic of topic, he propose an engagement. “I have a

ring in my pocket,” say this brisk wooer, look down. but this Mrs.

Dodd think premature and unnecessary. “you be nearly of age,” say

she, “and then you will be able to marry, if you be in the same mind.”

 but, upon be warmly press, she half concede even this. “well,”

 say she, “on receive your father's consent, you can _propose_ an

engagement to Julia, and she shall use her own judgment; but, until

then, you will not even mention such a thing to she. may I count on so

much forbearance from you, sir?”



“Dear Mrs. Dodd,” say Alfred, “of course you may. I should indeed be

ungrateful if I could not wait a post for that. may I write to my father

here?” add he, naively.



Mrs. Dodd smile, furnish he with write material, and leave he,

with a polite excuse.





“ALBION VILLA, _September 29._



“MY DEAR FATHER,--You be too thorough a man of the world, and too

well verse in human nature, to be surprised at hear that I, so long

invulnerable, have at last form a devoted attachment to one whose

beauty, goodness, and accomplishment I will not now enlarge upon;

they be indescribable, and you will very soon see they and judge for

yourself. the attachment, though short in week and month, have be a

very long one in hope, and fear, and devotion. I should have tell you

of it before you leave, but in truth I have no idea I be so near the goal

of all my earthly hope; there be many difficulty: but these have

just clear away almost miraculously, and nothing now be want to my

happiness but your consent. it would be affectation, or bad, in I to

doubt that you will grant it. but, in a matter so delicate, I venture to

ask you for something more: the mother of my ever and only beloved Julia

be a lady of high breeding and sentiment: she will not let her daughter

enter any family without a cordial invitation from its head. indeed she

have just tell I so. I ask, therefore, not your bare consent, of which

I be sure, since my happiness for life depend on it, but a consent so

gracefully word--and who can do this well than you?--as to gratify

the just pride and sensibility of the high-minded family about to

confide its bright ornament to my care.



“my dear father, in the midst of felicity almost more than mortal, the

thought have come that this letter be my first step towards leave the

paternal roof under which I have be so happy all my life, thank to

you. I should indeed be unworthy of all your goodness if this thought

cause I no emotion.



“yet I do but yield to Nature's universal law. and, should I be master

of my own destiny, I will not go far from you. I have be unjust to

Barkington: or rather I have echo, without thought, oxonian prejudice

and affectation. on mature reflection, I know no well residence for a

married man.



“do you remember about a year ago you mention a Miss Lucy Fountain to

we as 'the most perfect gentlewoman you have ever meet?' well, strange to

say, it be that very lady's daughter; and I think when you see she you

will say the breed have anything but decline, in spite of Horace mind

his _'damnosa quid non.'_ her brother be my dear friend, and she be

Jenny's; so a more happy alliance for all party be never project.



“write to I by return, dear father, and believe I, ever your dutiful

and grateful son,



“ALFRED HARDlE.”





as he conclude, Julia come in, and he insist on her reading this

masterpiece. she hesitate. then he tell she with juvenile severity that

a good husband always share his letter with his wife.



“his wife! Alfred!” and she colour all over. “don't call I _name,_”

 say she, turn it off after her fashion. “I cann't bear it: it make

I tremble. with fury.”



“this will never do, sweet one,” say Alfred gravely. “you and I be

to have no separate existence now; you be to be I, and I be to be you.

come!”



“no; you read I so much of it as be proper for I to hear. I shall not

like it so well from your lip: but never mind.”



when he come to read it, he appreciate the delicacy that have temper

her curiosity. he do not read it all to she, but nearly.



“it be a beautiful letter,” say she; “a little pomposer than mamma

and I write. 'the paternal roof!' but all that become you; you be a

scholar: and, dear Alfred, if I should separate you from your papa,

I will never estrange you from he; oh, never, never. may I go for my

work? for methink, o most erudite, the 'maternal dame,' on domestic

care intent, hath confide to her offspring the recreation of your

highness.” the gay creature dropt he a curtsey, and flee to tell Mrs.

Dodd the substance of “the sweet letter the dear high-fly thing have

write.”



by then he have fold and address it, she return and bring she

work: charity child's great cloak: her mother have cut they, and

in the height of the fashion, to Jane Hardie's dismay; and Julia be

bind, hooding, etcetere they.



how demurely she bent her lovely head over her charitable work, while

Alfred pour his tale into her ear! how careful she be not to speak,

when there be a chance of his speaking! how often she say one thing so

as to express its opposite, a process for which she might have take out

a patent! how she and Alfred compare heart-note, and their feeling

at each stage of their passion! their heart put forth tendril after

tendril, and so curl, and clung, round each other.



in the afternoon of the second blissful day, Julia suddenly remember

that this be dull for her mother. to have such a thought be to fly

to she; and she fly so swiftly that she catch Mrs. Dodd in tear, and

try adroitly and vainly to hide they.



“what be the matter? I be a wretch. I have leave you alone.”



“do not think I so peevish, love! you have but surprise the natural

regret of a mother at the loss of her child.”



“oh, mamma,” say Julia, warmly, “and do you think all the marriage in

the world can ever divide you and I--can make I lukewarm to my own

sweet, darling, beautiful, blessed, angel mother? look at I: I be as

much your Julia as ever; and shall be while I live. your son be your

son till he get he a wife: but your daughter's your daughter,

ALL--THE----DAYS--of her life.”





divine power of native eloquence: with this trite distich you make

hexameter tame; it gush from that great young heart with a sweet

infantine ardour, that even virtue can only pour when young, and youth

when virtuous; and, at the word I have emphasise by the poor device

of capital, two lovely, supple arm fly wide out like a soar

albatross's wing, and then go all round the sad mother, and gather

every bit of she up to the generous young bosom.



“I know it, I know it!” cry Mrs. Dodd, kiss she; “I shall never

lose my daughter while she breathe. but I be lose my child. you be

turn to a woman visibly: and you be such a happy child. hence my

misgiving, and these weak tear, which you have dry with a word:

see!” and she contrive to smile. “and now go down, dearest: he may be

impatient; man's love be so fiery.”



the next day Mrs. Dodd take Julia apart and ask she whether there be

an answer from Mr. Hardie. Julia reply, from Alfred, that Jane have

receive a letter last night, and, to judge by the content, Mr. Hardie

must have leave London before Alfred's letter get there. “he be go to

see poor Uncle Thomas.”



“why do you call he 'poor?'”



“oh, he be not very clever; have not much mind, Alfred say; indeed,

hardly any.”



“you alarm I, Julia!” cry Mrs. Dodd. “what? madness in the family you

propose to marry into?”



“oh no, mamma,” say Julia, in a great hurry; “no madness; only a little

imbecility.”



Mrs. Dodd's lip curve at this julian answer; but just then her mind

be more drawn to another topic. a serious doubt pass through she,

whether, if Mr. Hardie do not write soon, she ought not to limit his

son's attendance on her daughter. “he follow she about like a little

dog,” say she half fretfully.



next day, by previous invitation, Dr. Sampson make Albion Villa his

head-quarter. dart in from London, he find Alfred sit very

close to Julia over a book.



“Lordsake!” cry he, “here's 'my puppy,' and 'm' enthusiast,' cheek

by chowl.” Julia turn scarlet, and Alfred ejaculate so loudly, that

Sampson inquire “what on airth be the matter now?”



“oh, nothing; only here have I be jealous of my own shadow, and

pester she who 'your puppy' be: and she never would tell I. all

I could get from she,” add he, turn suddenly from gratitude to

revenge, “be that he be no great a puppy than yourself, doctor.”



“oh, Alfred, no; I only say no vainer,” cry Julia in dismay.



“well, it be true,” say Sampson contentedly, and proceed to dissect

himself just as he would a stranger. “I be a vain man; a remarkably vain

man. but then I'm a man of great mirit.”



“all vain people be that,” suggest Alfred dryly.



“who should know well than you, young Oxford? Y' have get a hidache.”



“no, indeed.”



“don't tell lie now. ye cann't deceive I; man, I've an eye like a hawk.

and what's that ye're study with she? Ovid, for a pound.”



“no; medicine; a treatise on your favourite organ, the brain, by one Dr.

Whately.”



“he be chaff you, doctor,” say Edward; “it be logic. he be coach

she; and then she will coach I.”



“then I forbid the chaff-cutting, young Pidant. Logic be an ill plaster

to a sore head.”



“oh, 'the labour we delight in, physics pain.'”



    “Jinnyus, Jinnyus;

     take care o' your carkuss,”



retort the master of doggrel. “and that be a profounder remark than

you seem to think, by your grinning, all of ye.”



Julia settle the question by put away the book. and she murmur to

Alfred, “I wish I could steal your poor dear headache: you might give

I half of they at least; you would, too, if you really love I.”



this sound remonstrance escape criticism by be nearly inaudible, and

by Mrs. Dodd enter at the same moment.



after the first greeting, Sampson ask she with merry arrogance, how

his prescription have work? “be her sleep break still, ma'am? be her

spirit up and down? shall we have to go back t' old Short and his black

draught? how's her mookis membrin? and her biliary duck? an'--she's off

like a flash.”



“and no wonder,” say Mrs. Dodd reproachfully.



thus splash Sampson among the duck: one of they do not show her face

again till dinner.



Jane Hardie accompany her brother by invitation. the general amity be

diversified and the mirth nowise lessen by constant passage of arm

between Messrs. Sampson and Alfred Hardie.



after tea come the first _contretemp._ Sampson like a game of card:

he could play, yet talk chronothermalism, as the fair can knit baby'

shoe and imbibe the poetaster of the day.



Mrs. Dodd have ask Edward to bring a fresh pack. he be see by his

guardian angel to take they out of his pocket and undo they; presently

Sampson, in his rapid way, clutch hold of they; and find a slip of

paper curl round the ace of spade, with this write very clear in

pencil,



     “remember THY creator in the day of THY YOUTH!”



“what be this?” cry Sampson, and read it out aloud. Jane Hardie

colour, and so betray herself. her “word in season” have stray. it

be the young and comely Edward she wish to save from the diabolical

literature, the paint perdition, and not the unintereste old sinner

Sampson, who proceed to justify her preference by remark that

“remember not to trump your partner's good card, lady,” would be more

to the point.



everybody, except this harden personage, be thoroughly uncomfortable.

as for Alfred, his face betray a degree of youthful mortification

little short of agony. Mrs. Dodd be profoundly disgusted, but

fortunately for the Hardies, catch sight of his burn cheek and

compress lip. “Dr. Sampson,” say she, with cold dignity, “you will,

I be sure, oblige I by make no more comment; sincerity be not always

discreet; but it be always respectable: it be one of your own title to

esteem. I dare say,” add she with great sweetness, “our resource

be not so narrow that we need shock anybody's prejudice, and, as it

happen, I be just go to ask Julia to sing: open the piano, love,

and try if you can persuade Miss Hardie to join you in a duet.”



at this, Jane and Julia have an earnest conversation at the piano, and

their word, utter in a low voice, be cover by a contemporaneous

discussion between Sampson and Mrs. Dodd.



_Jane._ no, you must not ask I: I have forswear these vanity. I have

not open my piano this two year.



_Julia._ oh, what a pity; music be so beautiful; and surely we can

choose our song, as easily as our word; ah, how much more easily.



_jane._ oh, I don't go so far as to call music wicked: but music in

society be _such_ a snare. at least I find it so; my playing be highly

praise, and that stir up vanity: and so do my singing, with which I

have even more reason to be satisfied. snare! snare!



_Julia._ goodness I! I don't find they so. now you mention it,

gentleman do praise one; but, dear I, they praise every lady, even when

we have be singe every other note out of tune. the little unmeaning

compliment of society, can they catch anything so great as a soul?



_Jane._ I pray daily not to be lead into temptation, and shall I go into

it of my own accord?



_Julia._ not if you find it a temptation. at that rate I ought to

decline.



_Jane._ that don't follow. my conscience be not a law to your.

besides, your mamma say “sing:” and a parent be not to be disobey

upon a doubt. if papa be to insist on my go to a ball even, or

read a novel, I think I should obey; and lie the whole case before

Him.



_Mrs. Dodd_ (from a distance). come, my dear, Dr. Sampson be get

_so_ impatient for your song.



_Sampson._ Hum! for all that, young lady' singing be a poor substitute

for card, and even for conversation.



_Mrs. Dodd._ that depend upon the singer, I presume.



_Sampson._ Mai--dear--madam, they all sing alike; just as they all write

alike. I can hardly tell one fashionable tune from another; and nobody

can tell one word from another, when they cut out all the consonant. n'

listen I. this be what I hear sing by a lady last night.



Eu un Da' ei u aa an oo. by oo eeeeyee aa Vaullee, Vaullee, Vaullee,

Vaullee, Vaullee om be igh eeaa an ellin in be ud.



_Mrs. Dodd._ that sound like gibberish.



_Sampson._ it be gibberish, but it's drydenish in articulating mouth.

it is--



he sing Darius great and good, by too severe a fate Fallen, fall,

fall, fall, fall from his high estate, and wiltere in his blood.



_Mrs. Dodd._ I think you exaggerate. I will answer for Julia that she

shall speak as distinctly to music as you do in conversation.



_Sampson_ (all unconscious of the tap). time will show, madam. at

prisent they seem to be in no hurry to spatter we with their word-jelly.

do some spark of pity linger in their marble bos'ms? or do they prefer

inaud'ble chit-chat t' inarticulate mewing?



Julia, thus press, sing one of those song that come and go every

season. she speak the word clearly, and with such variety and

intelligence, that Sampson recant, and break in upon the--“very

pretty”--“how sweet”--and “who be it by?” of the other, by shout,

“very weak trash very cleanly sung. now give we something worth the wear

and tear of your orgin. immortal vairse widde t' immortal sound; that

be what I understand b' a song.”



Alfred whisper, “no, no, dearest; sing something suitable to you and

I.”



“out of the question. then go far away, dear; I shall have more

courage.”



he obey, and she turn over two or three music-book, and finally

sing from memory. she cultivate musical memory, having observe the

contempt with which man of sense visit the sorry pretender to music,

who be tuneless and songless among the nightingale, and anywhere else

away from their book. how will they manage to sing in heaven? answer I

that.



the song Julia Dodd sing on this happy occasion, to meet the humble but

heterogeneous view of Messrs. Sampson and Hardie, be a simple eloquent

irish song call Aileen Aroon. whose history, by-the-bye, be a curious

one. early in this century it occur to somebody to hymn a son of

George the Third for his double merit in having be bear, and go to

a ball. People who thus apply the fine art in modern day be seldom

artist; accordingly, this parasite could not invent a melody; so he

coolly steal Aileen Aroon, soil it by insert sordid and incongruous

jerk into the refrain, and call the steal and adulterate article

Robin Adair. an artisan of the same kidney be soon find to write word

down to the degraded ditty: and, so strong be Flunkeyism, and so weak be

Criticism, in these island, that the polluted tune actually supersede

the clean melody; and this sort of thing--



     who be in uniform at the ball?

     Silly Billy,



smother the immortal line.



but Mrs. Dodd's severe taste in music reject those ignoble jerk, and

her enthusiastic daughter have the option to hymn immortal Constancy

or mortal Fat, decide thus:--



     when like the early rose,

     aileen aroon,

     Beauty in childhood glow,

     aileen aroon,



     when like a diadem,

     Buds blush around the stem,

     which be the fair gem?

     aileen aroon.



     be it the laugh eye?

     aileen aroon.

     be it the timid sigh?

     aileen aroon.



     be it the tender tone?

     Soft as the string'd harp's mean?

     no; it be Truth alone,

     aileen aroon.



     I know a valley fair,

     aileen aroon.

     I know a cottage there,

     aileen aroon.



     far in that valley's shade,

     I know a gentle maid,

     Flower of the hazel glade,

     aileen aroon.



     who in the song so sweet?

     aileen aroon,

     who in the dance so fleet?

     aileen aroon.



     Dear be her charm to I,

     Dearer her laughter free,

     dearest her constancy.

     aileen aroon.



     Youth must with time decay,

     aileen aroon,

     Beauty must fade away,

     aileen aroon.



     castle be sack in war,

     chieftain be scatter far,

     truth be a fix star,

     aileen areon.



the way the earnest singer sing these line be beyond the conception of

ordinary singer, public or private. here one of nature's orator speak

poetry to music with an eloquence as fervid and delicate as ever ring in

the Forum. she give each verse with the same just variety as if she have

be recite, and, when she come to the last, where the thought rise

abruptly, and be truly noble, she sing it with the sudden pathos, the

weight, and the swell majesty, of a truthful soul hymn truth with

all its power.



all the hearer, even Sampson, be thrill, astonished, spell-bind:

so can one wave of immortal music and immortal verse (alas! how seldom

they meet!) heave the inner man when genius interpret. Judge, then,

what it be to Alfred, to whom, with these great word and thrilling

tone of her rich, swell, ring voice, the darling of his own heart

vow constancy, while her inspire face beam on he like an angel's.



even Mrs. Dodd, though acquaint with the song, and with her daughter's

rare power, gaze at she now with some surprise, as well as admiration,

and keep a note Sarah have bring she, open, but unread, in her hand,

unable to take her eye from the inspire songstress. however, just

before the song end, she do just glance down, and see it be sign

Richard Hardie. on this her eye devour it; and in one moment she

see that the writer decline, politely but peremptorily, the propose

alliance between his son and her daughter.



the mother look up from this paper at that living radiance and

incarnate melody in a sort of stupor: it seem hardly possible to she

that a provincial banker could refuse an alliance with a creature

so peerless as that. but so it be; and despite her habitual

self-government, Mrs. Dodd's white hand clench the note till her nail

dent it; and she redden to the brow with anger and mortification.



Julia, whom she have train never to monopolise attention in society,

now leave the piano in spite of remonstrance, and soon notice she

mother's face; for from red it have become pale than usual. “be you

unwell, dear?” say she _sotto voce._



“no, love.”



“be there anything the matter, then?”



“Hush! we have guest: our first duty be to they.” with this Mrs. Dodd

rise, and, endeavour not to look at her daughter at all, go round

and draw each of her guest out in turn. it be the very heroism

of courtesy; for their presence be torture to she. at last, to she

infinite relief, they go, and she be leave alone with her child.

she send the servant to bed, say she would undress Miss Dodd, and

accompany she to her room. there the first thing she do be to lock

the door; and the next be to turn round and look at her full.



“I always think you the most lovable child I ever see; but I never

admire you as I have to-night, my noble, my beautiful daughter, who

would grace the high family in England.” with this Mrs. Dodd begin to

choke, and kiss Julia eagerly with the tear in her eye, and draw she

with tender, eloquent defiance to her bosom.



“my own mamma,” say Julia softly, “what have happen?”



“my darling,” say Mrs. Dodd, tremble a little, “have you pride? have

you spirit?”



“I think I have.”



“I hope so: for you will need they both. read that!”



and she hold out Mr. Hardie's letter, but turn her own head away, not

to see her girl's face under the insult.









CHAPTER VI



JULIA take Mr. Hardie's note and read it:--





“MADAM,--I have receive a very juvenile letter from my son, by which I

learn he have form a sudden attachment to your daughter. he tell I,

however, at the same time, that you await my concurrence before give

your consent. I appreciate your delicacy; and it be with considerable

regret I now write to inform you this match be out of the question. I

have think it due to you to communicate this to yourself and

without delay, and feel sure that you will, under the circumstance,

discountenance my son's further visit at your house--I be, Madam, with

sincere respect, your faithful servant,



“RICHARD HARDIE.”



Julia read this letter, and re-read it in silence. it be an anxious

moment to the mother.



“shall our pride be less than this _parvenu's?_” she falter. “tell I

yourself, what ought we to do?”



“what we ought to do be, never to let the name of Hardie be mention

again in this house.”



this reply be very comforting to Mrs. Dodd.



“shall I write to he, or do you feel strong enough?”



“I feel that, if I do, I may affront he. he have no right to pretend

that his father would consent. you write, and then we shall not lose our

dignity though we be insult.”



“I feel so weary, mamma.” Life seem end.



“I could have love he well. and now show I how to tear he out of my

heart; or what will become of I?”



while Mrs. Dodd write to Alfred Hardie, Julia sink down and lay she

head on her mother's knee. the note be show she; she approve it

languidly. a long and sad conversation follow; and, after kiss she

mother and cling to she, she go to bed chilly and listless, but do

not shed a single tear. her young heart be benumb by the unexpected

blow.



next morning early, Alfred Hardie start gaily to spend the day at

Albion Villa. not a hundred yard from the gate he meet Sarah, with Mrs.

Dodd's letter, enclose a copy of his father's to she. Mrs. Dodd

here remind he that his visit have be encourage only upon a

misapprehension of his father's sentiment; for which misapprehension he

be in some degree to blame: not that she mean to reproach he on that

score, especially at this unhappy moment: no, she rather blame herself

for listen to the sanguine voice of youth; but the error must now

be repair. she and Julia would always wish he well, and esteem he,

provide he make no further attempt to compromise a young lady who could

not be his wife. the note conclude thus--



“individually I think I have some right to count on your honourable

feeling to hold no communication with my daughter, and not in any way to

attract her attention, under the present circumstances.--I am, dear Mr.

Alfred Hardie, with many regret at the pain I fear I be give you,

your sincere friend and well-wisher,



“LUCY DODD.”



Alfred on read this letter literally stagger: but proud and

sensitive, as well as love, he man himself to hide his wound from

Sarah, whose black eye be bent on he in merciless scrutiny. he say

doggedly, though tremulously, “very well!” then turn quickly on his

heel, and go slowly home. Mrs. Dodd, with well-feign indifference,

question Sarah privately: the girl's account of the abrupt way in

which he have receive the missive add to her anxiety. she warn the

servant that no one be at home to Mr. Alfred Hardie.



two day elapse, and then she receive a letter from he. poor fellow,

it be the eleventh. he have write and tear up ten.





“DEAR MRS. DODD,--I have gain some victory in my life; but not one

without two defeat to begin with; how then can I expect to obtain such

a prize as dear Julia without a check or two? you need not fear that I

shall intrude after your appeal to I as a gentleman: but I be not go

to give in because my father have write a hasty letter from Yorkshire.

he and I must have many a talk face to face before I consent to be

miserable for life. dear Mrs. Dodd, at first receipt of your cruel

letter, so kindly word, I be break-hearted; but now I be myself

again: difficulty be make for lady to yield to, and for man to

conquer. only for pity's sake do not you be my enemy; do not set she

against I for my father's fault. think, if you can, how my heart bleed

at close this letter without one word to she I love well, a thousand

time well, than my life--I be, dear Mrs. Dodd, yours sorrowfully, but

not despairing,



“ALFRED HARDIE.”





Mrs. Dodd keep this letter to herself. she could not read it quite

unmoved, and therefore she feel sure it would disturb her daughter's

heart the more.



Alfred have now a soft but dangerous antagonist in Mrs. Dodd. all the

mother be in arm to secure her daughter's happiness, _coute qu'il

coute!_ and the sure course seem to be to detach her affection

from Alfred. what hope of a peaceful heart without this? and what real

happiness without peace? but, too wise and calm to interfere blindly,

she watch her daughter day and night, to find whether Love or Pride

be the strong, and this be what she observed--



Julia never mention Alfred. she seek occupation eagerly: come

oftener than usual for money, say, it be for “Luxury.” she visit

the poor more constantly, take one of the maid with she, at Mrs.

Dodd's request. she study Logic with Edward. she go to bed rather

early, fatigued, it would appear, by her activity: and she give the

clue to her own conduct one day: “Mamma,” say she, “nobody be downright

unhappy who be good.”



Mrs. Dodd notice also a certain wildness and almost violence, with

which she throw herself into her occupation, and a worn look about the

eye that tell of a hidden conflict. on the whole Mrs. Dodd be hopeful;

for she have never imagine the cure would be speedy or easy. to see

her child on the right road be much. only the great healer Time could

“medicine she to that sweet peace which once she own;” and even Time

cannot give she back her childhood, think the mother, with a sigh.



one day come an invitation to an evening party at a house where they

always wound up with dancing. Mrs. Dodd be for decline as usual for

since that night Julia have shun party. “give I the sorrow of the

poor and afflict,” be her cry; “the gaiety of the hollow world jar

I more than I can bear.” but now she catch with a sort of eagerness at

this invitation. “accept. they shall not say I be wear the willow.”



“my brave girl,” say Mrs. Dodd joyfully, “I would not press it; but you

be right; we owe it to ourselves to outface scandal. still, let there

be no precipitation; we must not undertake beyond our strength.”



“try I to-night,” say Julia; “you don't know what I can do. I dare say

_he_ be not pine for _I._”



she be the life and soul of the party, and, indeed, so feverishly

brilliant, that Mrs. Dodd say softly to she, “gently, love; moderate

your spirit, or they will deceive our friend as little as they do I.”



meantime it cost Alfred Hardie a severe struggle to keep altogether

aloof from Julia. in fact, it be a state of daily self-denial, to which

he would never have commit himself, but that he be quite sure he

could gradually win his father over. at his age we be apt to count

without our antagonist.



Mr. Richard Hardie be “a long-head man.” he know the consequence of

give one's reason: eternal discussion end in war. he have take

care not to give any to Mrs. Dodd, and he be as guard and reserve

with Alfred. the young man beg to know the why and the wherefore, and

be repulse, employ all his art to elicit they by surprise, or get

at they by inference: but all in vain. Hardie senior be impenetrable;

and inquiry, petulance, tenderness, logic, be all shatter on he as

the wave break on Ailsa Craig.



thus begin dissension, decently conduct at first, between a father

indulgent hitherto and an affectionate son.



in this unfortunate collision of two strong and kindre nature,

every advantage be at present on the father's side: age, experience,

authority, resolution, hidden and powerful motive, to which my reader

even have no clue as yet; a purpose immutable and conceal. add to these

a colder nature and a far colder affection; for Alfred love his father

dearly.



at last, one day, the impetuous one lose his self-command, and say he

be a son, not a slave, and have little respect for Authority when afraid

or ashamed to appeal to Reason. Hardie senior turn on he with a

gravity and dignity no man could wear more naturally. “Alfred, have I

be an unkind father to you all these year?”



“oh no, father, no; I have say nothing that can be so construed. and

that be the mystery to I; you be act quite out of character.”



“have I be one of those interfere, pragmatical father who cannot

let their child enjoy themselves their own way?”



“no, sir; you have never interfere, except to pay for anything I

want.”



“then make the one return in your power, young man: have a little faith

in such a father, and believe that he do not interfere now but for

your good, and under a stern necessity; and that when he do interfere

for once, and say, 'this thing shall not be,' it shall not be--by

Heaven!”



Alfred be overpower by the weight and solemnity of this. sorrow,

vexation, and despondency all rush into his heart together, and

unmanned he for a moment; he bury his face in his hand, and

something very like a sob burst from his young heart. at this Hardie

senior take up the newspaper with imperturbable coldness, and wear a

slight curl of the lip. all this be hardly genuine, for he be not

altogether unmove; but he be a man of rare self-command, and choose to

impress on Alfred that he be no more to be break or melt than a mere

rock.



it be always precarious to act a part; and this cynicism be rather

able than wise: Alfred look up and watch he keenly as he read the

monetary article with tranquil interest; and then, for the first time in

his life, it flash into the young man's mind that his father be not

a father. “I never know he till now,” think he. “this man be [greek

text].” *



     *without bowel of affection.



thus a gesture, so to speak, sow the first seed of downright disunion

in Richard Hardie's house--disunion, a fast-grow plant, when man set

it in the soil of the passion.



Alfred, unlike Julia, have no panacea. have any lip, except perhaps her,

tell he that “to be good be to be happy here below,” he would have

reply: _“Negatur;_ contradict by daily experience.” it never

occur to he, therefore, to go out of himself, and sympathise with

the sordid sorrow of the poor, and their bottomless egotism in

contact with the well-to-do. he brood on his own love, and his own

unhappiness, and his own father's cruelty. his night be sleepless and

his day leaden. he try hard to read for his first class, but for once

even ambition fail: it end in fling book away in despair.

he wander about dream and moping for some change, and bitterly

regret his excessive delicacy, which have tie his own hand and

bring he to a stand-still. he lose his colour and what little flesh

he have to lose; for such young spirit as this be never plump. in a

word, be now strait-jacket into feminine inactivity, while void of

feminine patience, his ardent heart be pine and fret itself out.

he be in this condition, when one day Peterson, his oxonian friend,

burst in on he open-mouthed with delight, and, as usual with bright

spirit of this calibre, do not even notice his friend's sadness.

“Cupid have clap he on the shoulder,” as Shakespeare hath it; and it

be a deal nice than the bum-bailiff rheumatism.



“oh, such a divine creature! Met her twice; you know she by sight; she

name be Dodd. but I don't care; it shall be Peterson; the rise by any

other name, &c.” then follow a rapturous description of the lady's

person, well worth omit. “and such a jolly girl! brighten they all

up wherever she go; and such a dancer; do the cachouka with a little

spanish bloke Bosanquet have get hold of, and make his black bolus eye

twinkle like midnight cigar: dance it with castanet, and smile, and

such a what d'ye call 'em, my boy, you know; such a 'go.'”



“you mean such an 'abandon,'” groan Alfred, turn sick at heart.



“that's the word. twice the spirit of Duvernay, and ten time the

beauty. but just you hear her sing, that be all; italian, french,

german, English even.”



“plaintive song?”



“oh, whatever they ask for. make you laugh or make you cry to order;

never say no. just smile and sit down to the music-box. only she

willn't sing two run: they have to stick a duffer in between. I shall

meet she again next week; will you come? any friend of mine be welcome.

wish I joy, old fellow; I'm a go coon.”



this news put Alfred in a phrensy of indignation and fear. Julia dance

the cachouka! Julia a jolly girl! Julia singe song pathetic or merry,

whichever be ask for! the heartless one! he call to mind all he

have read in the classic, and elsewhere, about the fickleness of woman.

but this impression do not last long; he recall Julia's character,

and all the sign of a love tender and true she have give he. he read

she by himself, and, lover-like, lay all the blame on another. it be

all her cold-blooded mother. “Fool that I have be. I see it all now.

she appeal to my delicacy to keep away; then she go to Julia and

say, 'see, he desert you at a word from his father. be proud, be gay!

he never love you; marry another.' the shallow plotter forget that

whoever she do marry I'll kill. how many unsuspicious girl have

these double-faced mother delude so? they do it in half the novel,

especially in those write by woman; and why? because these know the

perfidy and mendacity of their sex well than we do; they see they

nearer, and with their soul undr. War, Mrs. Dodd! war to the death!

from this moment I be alone in the world with she. I have no friend but

Alfred Hardie: and my bitter enemy be my cold-blooded father and

her cold-blooded mother.”



the above sentence, of course, be never utter. but they represent

his thought accurately, though in a condensed form, and be, as it

be, a miniature of this young heart boil over.



from that moment he lie in wait for she, and hover about the house day

and night, determine to appeal to she personally, and undeceive

she, and baffle her mother's treachery. but at this game he be soon

detect: Mrs. Dodd live on the watch now. Julia, dress to go out,

go to the window one afternoon to look at the weather; but retreat

somewhat hastily and sit down on the sofa.



“you flutter, darling,” say Mrs. Dodd. “ah! he be there.”



“yes.”



“you have well take off your thing.”



“oh, yes. I tremble at the thought of meet he. Mamma, he be

change, sadly change. poor, poor Alfred!” she go to her own room and

pray for he. she inform the Omniscient that, though much great

and well in other respect than she be, he have not Patience. she

pray, with tear, that he might have christian patience grant he

from on high.



“heart of stone! she shun I,” say Alfred, outside. he have see she in

her bonnet.



Mrs. Dodd wait several day to see whether this annoyance would not

die of itself: wait be her plan in most thing. find he be not

to be tired out, she send Sarah out to he with a note carefully seal.





“Mr. Alfred Hardie,--Is it generous to confine my daughter to the

house?--Yours regretfully,



“LUCY DODD.”





a line come back instantly in pencil.





“Mrs. Dodd,--Is all the generosity and all the good faith to be on one

side?--Yours in despair,



“ALFRED HARDIE.”





Mrs. Dodd colour faintly: the reproach prick she, but do not move

she. she sit quietly down that moment, and write to a friend in London,

to look out for a furnished villa in a healthy part of the suburb, with

immediate possession. “circumstance,” say she, “make it desirable we

should leave Barkington immediately, and for some month.”





the Bosanquets give a large party; Mrs. and Miss Dodd be there. the

latter be play a part in a charade to the admiration of all present,

when in come Mr. Peterson, introduce his friend, Alfred Hardie.



Julia catch the name, and turn a look of alarm on her mother, but

go on act.



presently she catch sight of he at some distance. he look very pale,

and his glitter eye be fix on she with a sort of stern wonder.



such a glance from fiery eye, that have always dwell tenderly on she

till then, strike she like a weapon. she stop short, and turn red

and pale by turn. “there, that be nonsense enough,” say she bitterly,

and go and sit by Mrs. Dodd. the gentleman throng round she with

compliment, and beg she to sing. she excuse herself. presently

she hear an excited voice, towards which she dared not look; it be

inquire whether any lady could sing Aileen Aroon. with every desire

to gratify the young millionaire, nobody know Aileen Aroon, nor have ever

hear of it.



“oh, impossible!” cry Alfred. “why, it be in praise of Constancy, a

virtue lady shine in: at least, they take credit for it.”



“Mamma,” whisper Julia terrify, “get I away, or there will be a

scene. he be reckless.”



“be calm, love,” say Mrs. Dodd, “there shall be none.” she rise and

glide up to Alfred Hardie, look coldly in his face; then say with

external politeness and veiled contempt, “I will attempt the song, sir,

since you desire it.” she wave her hand, and he follow she sulkily to

the piano. she sing Aileen Aroon, not with her daughter's eloquence, but

with a purity and mellowness that charm the room: they have never hear

the genius sing it.



as spirit be say to overcome the man at whose behest they rise, so

this sweet air, and the gush of reminiscence it awaken, overpower

he who have evoke they; Alfred put his Hand unconsciously to his

swell heart, cast one look of anguish at Julia, and hurry away half

choke. nobody but Julia notice.





a fellow in a rough great-coat and tattere white hat open the fly

door for Mrs. Dodd. as Julia follow she, he kiss her skirt unseen by

Mrs. Dodd, but her quick ear catch a heart-break sigh. she look

and recognise Alfred in that disguise; the penitent fit have succeed

to the angry one. have Julia observe? to ascertain this without speak

of he, Mrs. Dodd wait till they have get some little distance, then

quietly put out her hand and rest it for a moment on her daughter's;

the girl be tremble violently “little wretch!” come to Mrs. Dodd's

lip, but she do not utter it. they be near home before she speak

at all, and then she only say very kindly, “my love, you will not be

subject again to these trial:” a remark intend quietly to cover the

last occurrence as well as Alfred's open persecution.



they have promise to go out the very next day; but Mrs. Dodd go alone,

and make excuse for Miss Dodd. on her return she find Julia sit

up for she, and a letter come from her friend describe a pleasant

cottage, now vacant, near Maida Vale. Mrs. Dodd hand the open letter

to Julia; she read it without comment.



“we will go up to-morrow and take it for three month. then the Oxford

vacation will terminate.”



“yes, mamma.”





I be now about to relate a circumstance by no mean without parallel,

but almost impossible to account for; and, as nothing be more common and

contemptible than inadequate solution, I will offer none at all: but

so it be, that Mrs. Dodd awake in the middle of that very night in a

mysterious state of mental tremor; trouble, veil in obscurity, seem

to sit heavy on her bosom. so strong, though vague, be this new and

mysterious oppression, that she start up in bed and cry aloud,

“David!--Julia!--Oh, what be the matter?” the sound of her own voice

dispel the cloud in part, but not entirely. she lie awhile, and then

find herself quite averse to sleep, rise and go to her window,

and eye the weather anxiously. it be a fine night; soft fleecy cloud

drift slowly across a silver moon. the sailor's wife be reassure on

her husband's behalf. her next desire be to look at Julia sleep; she

have no particular object: it be the instinctive impulse of an anxious

mother whom something have terrify. she put on her slipper and

dressing-gown, and, light a candle at her night-lamp, open her door

softly and step into the little corridor. but she have not take two

step when she be arrest by a mysterious sound.



it come from Julia's room.



what be it?



Mrs. Dodd glide softly near and nearer, all her sense on the

stretch.



the sound come again. it be a muffle sob.





the stifle sound, just audible in the dead stillness of the night, go

through and through she who stand there listen aghast. her bowel

yearn over her child, and she hurry to the door, but recollect

herself, and knock, very gently. “don't be alarm, love; it be only

I. may I come in?” she do not wait for the answer, but turn the

handle and enter. she find Julia sit up in bed, look wildly at

she, with cheek flushed and wet. she sit on the bed and clasp she to

her breast in silence: but more than one warm tear run down upon Julia's

bare neck; the girl feel they drop, and her own gush in a shower.



“oh, what have I do?” she sob. “be I to make you wretched too?”



Mrs. Dodd do not immediately reply. she be there to console, and her

admirable good sense tell she that to do that she must be calm than

her patient; so even while she kiss and weep over Julia, she manage

gradually to recover her composure. “tell I, my child,” say she, “why

do you act a part with I? why brave it out under my eye, and spend the

night secretly in tear? be you still afraid to trust I?”



“oh no, no; but I think I be so strong, so proud: I undertake

miracle. I soon find my pride be a molehill and my love a mountain. I

could not hold out by day if I do not ease my break heart at night.

how unfortunate! I keep my head under the bed-clothe, too; but you have

such ear. I think I would stifle my grief, or else perhaps you would

be as wretched as I be: forgive I! pray forgive I!”



“on one condition,” say Mrs. Dodd, struggle with the emotion these

simple word cause she.



“anything to be forgive,” cry Julia, impetuously. “I'll go to London.

I'll go to Botany Bay. I deserve to be hang.”



“then, from this hour, no half-confidence between we. dear I, you

carry in your own bosom a much harsh judge, a much less indulgent

friend, than I be. Come! trust I with your heart. do you love he very

much? do your happiness depend on he?”



at this point-blank question Julia put her head over Mrs. Dodd's

shoulder, not to be see; and, clasp she tight, murmur scarce above

a whisper, “I don't know how much I love he. when he come in at that

party I feel his slave--his unfaithful adore slave; if he have order

I to sing Aileen Aroon, I should have obey; if he have command I to

take his hand and leave the room, I think I should have obey. his face

be always before I as plain as life; it use to come to I bright and

love; now it be pale, and stern, and sad. I be not so wretched till I

see he be pine for I, and think I inconstant--oh, mamma, so pale!

so shrunk I so reckless! he be sorry for misbehave that night: he

change clothe with a beggar to kiss my dress, poor thing! poor thing!

who ever love as he do I! I be die for he; I be die.”
