“there! there!” say Mrs. Dodd soothingly. “you have say enough. this

must be love. I be on your Alfred's side from this hour.”



Julia open her eye, and be a good deal agitated as well as

surprised. “pray do not raise my hope,” she gasp. “we be part

for ever. his father refuse. even you seem averse; or have I be

dream?”



“I, dearest? how can I be averse to anything lawful on which I find

your heart be really set, and your happiness at stake? of course I have

stop the actual intercourse, under exist circumstance; but these

circumstance be not unalterable: your only obstacle be Mr. Richard

Hardie.”



“but what an obstacle!” sigh Julia. “his father! a man of iron! so

everybody say; for I have make inquiry--oh!” and she be abash.

she resume hastily, “and that letter, so cold, so cruel! I feel it

be write by one not open to gentle influence. he do not think

I worthy of his son so accomplished, so distinguished at the very

university where our poor Edward--have--you know----”



“little simpleton!” say Mrs. Dodd, and kiss she tenderly; “your iron

man be the commonest clay, sordid, pliable; and your stern heroic Brutus

be a shopkeeper: he be open to the gentle influence which sway the

kindre soul of the man you and I buy our shoe, our tea, our glove,

our fish-kettle of: and these influence I think I command, and be

prepared to use they to the utmost.”



Julia lie silent, and wonder what she could mean.



but Mrs. Dodd hesitate now: it pain and revolt she to show her

enthusiastic girl the world as it be. she say as much, and added--“I

seem to be go to aid all these people to take the bloom from my own

child's innocence. Heaven help I!”



“oh, never mind that,” cry Julia in her ardent way; “give I Truth

before Error, however pleasing.”



Mrs. Dodd reply only by a sigh: grand general sentiment like that

never penetrate her mind: they glide off like water from a duck's

back. “we will begin with this mercantile Brutus, then,” say she, with

such a curl of the lip. Brutus have reject her daughter.



“Mr. Richard Hardie be bear and breed in a bank; one where no wild thyme

blow, my poor enthusiast, nor cowslip nor the nodding violet grow;

but gold and silver chink, and thing be discount, and man grow rich,

slowly but surely, by lawful use of other people's money. breathe

upon by these 'gentle influence,' he be, from his youth, a remarkable

man--measure by Trade's standard. at five-and-twenty divine what he

do! he save the bank. you have read of bubble: the Mississippi Bubble

and the South Sea Bubble. well, in the year 1825, it be not one bubble

but a thousand; mine by the score, and in distant land; company

by the hundred; loan to every nation or tribe; down to Guatemala,

Patagonia, and Greece; two hundred new ship be lay on the stock in

one year, for your dear papa tell I; in short, a fever of speculation,

and the whole nation rage with it: my dear, Princes, Dukes, Duchesses,

bishop, Poets, Lawyers, Physicians, be see struggle with their own

footman for a place in the Exchange: and, at last, good, steady, old Mr.

Hardie, Alfred's grandfather, be draw into the vortex. now, to excuse

he and appreciate the precocious Richard, you must try and realise that

these bubble, when they rise, be as alluring and reasonable as they

be ridiculous and incredible when one look back on they; even soap

bubble, you know, have rainbow hue till they burst: and, indeed, the

blind avarice of man do but resemble the blind vanity of woman: look

at our grandmother' hoop, and our mother' short waist and monstrous

head! yet in their day what woman do not glory in these insanity?

well then, Mr. Richard Hardie, at twenty-five, be the one to foresee

the end of all these bubble; he come down from London and bring

his people to their sense by sober reason and 'sound commercial

principles'--that mean, I believe, 'get other people's money, but

do not risk your own.' his superiority be so clear, that his father

resign the helm to he, and, thank to his ability, the bank weather

the storm, while all the other one in the town break or suspend their

trade. now, you know, youth be naturally ardent and speculative; but

Richard Hardie's be cold and wise than other people's old age: and

that be one trait. some year later, in the height of his prosperity--I

reveal this only for your comfort, and on your sacred promise as a

person of delicacy, never to repeat it to a soul--Richard Hardie be a

suitor for my hand.”



“Mamma!”



“do not ejaculate, sweetest. it discompose I. 'nothing be

extraordinary,' as that good creature Dr. Sampson say. he must have

think it would _answer,_ in one way or another, to have a gentlewoman

at the head of his table; and I be not penniless, _bien entendu._

fail in this, he find a plain little thing, with a gloomy temper,

and no accomplishment nor grace; but her father could settle twenty

thousand pound. he marry she directly: and that be a trait. he sell

his father's and grandfather's house and place of business, in spite of

all their association, and obtain a lease of his present place from

my uncle fountain: it seem a more money-make situation. a trait.

he give I no reason for reject my daughter. why? because he be not

proud of his reason: this walk Avarice have intelligence: a trait.

now put all this together, and who more transparent than the profound

Mr. Hardie? he have decline our alliance because he take for grant

we be poor. when I undeceive he on that head he will reopen

_negotiation_ in a letter--no. 2 of the correspondence; copy by one

of his clerk--it will be calm, plausible, flattering: in short, it will

be do like a gentleman: though he be nothing of the kind. and this

bring I to what I ought to have begin with: your dear father and

I have always live within our income for our child's sake; he be

bring home the bulk of our saving this very voyage, and it amount

to fourteen thousand pound.”



“oh, what an enormous sum!”



“no, dearest, it be not a fortune in itself. but it be a considerable

sum to possess, independent of one's settlement and one's income. it be

loose cash, to speak _a la_ Hardie; that mean I can do what I choose

with it and of course I choose--to make you happy. how I shall work

on what you call Iron and I venture to call Clay must be guide by

circumstance. I think of deposit three or four thousand pound every

month with Mr. Hardie; he be our banker, you know. he will most likely

open his eye, and make some move before the whole sum be in his hand.

if he do not, I shall perhaps call at his bank, and draw a cheque for

fourteen thousand pound. the wealthy provincial banker do not keep

such a sum float in his shop-till. his commercial honour, the one

semi-chivalrous sentiment in his soul, would be in peril. he would

yield, and with grace: none the less readily that his house and his

bank, which have be long heavily mortgage to our trustee, be make

virtually theirs by agreement yesterday (I set this on foot with twelve

hour of Mr. Iron's impertinent letter), and he will say to himself,

'she can--post I, I think these people call it--this afternoon for not

cash her cheque, and she can turn I and my bank into the street

to-morrow:' and then, of course, he shall see by my manner the velvet

paw be offer as well as the claw. he be pretty sure to ask himself

which will suit the _ledger_ well--this cat's friendship and her

fourteen thousand pound, or--an insulted mother's enmity?” and Mrs.

Placid's tooth make a little click just audible in the silent night.



“oh, mamma! my heart be sick. be I to be buy and sell like this?”



Mrs. Dodd sigh, but say calmly, “you must pay the penalty for love

a _parvenu's_ son. come, Julia, no peevishness, no more romance, no more

vacillation. you have try Pride and fail pitiably: now I insist on

your try love! child, it be the bane of our sex to carry nothing

out: from that weakness I will preserve you. and, by-the-bye, we be not

go to marry Mr. Richard Hardie, but Mr. Alfred. now, Mr. Alfred, with

all his fault and defects--”



“Mamma! what fault? what defect?”



“--Is a gentleman; thank to Oxford, and Harrow, and nature. my darling,

pray to Heaven night and day for your dear father's safe return; for on

he, and he alone, your happiness depend: as mine do.”



“Mamma!” cry Julia, embrace she, “what do poor girl do who have

lose their mother?”



“look abroad and see,” be the grave reply.



Mrs. Dodd then beg she to go to sleep, like a good child, for her

health's sake; all would be well; and with this be about to return to

her own room; but a white hand and arm dart out of the bed and catch

she. “what! Hope have come to I by night in the form of an angel, and

shall I let she go back to her own room? never! never! never! never!

never!” and she pat the bed expressively, and with the prettiest

impatience.



“well, let Hope take off her earring first,” suggest Mrs. Dodd.



“no, no, come here directly, earring and all.”



“no, thank you; or I shall have _they_ wound you next.”



Mrs. Hope quietly remove her earring, and the tender pair pass the

rest of the night in one another's arm. the young girl's tear be

dry; and hope revive, and life bloom again: only, henceforth she

longing eye look out to sea for her father, homeward bind.



next day, as they be seat together in the drawing-room, Julia come

from the window with a rush, and kneel at Mrs. Dodd's knee, with

bright implore face upturned.



“he be there; and--I be to speak to he? be that it?”



“dear, dear, dear mamma!” be the somewhat oblique reply.



“well, then, bring I my thing.”



she be ten minute put they on: Julia try to expedite she and

retard she. she have her pace, and could not go beyond it.



now by this time Alfred Hardie be thoroughly miserable. unable to move

his father, shun by Julia, sicken by what he have hear, and indeed

see, of her gaiety and indifference to their separation, sting by

jealousy and fret by impatience, he be drink nearly all the

bitter of that sweet passion, love. but as you be aware, he ascribe

Julia's inconstancy, lightness, and cruelty all to Mrs. Dodd. he hate

she cordially, and dread she into the bargain; he play the sentinel

about her door all the more because she have ask he not to do it

“always do what your enemy particularly object to,” say he, apply

to his own case the wisdom of a greek philosopher, one of his teacher.



so, when the gate suddenly open, and instead of Julia, this very Mrs.

Dodd walk towards he, his feeling be anything but enviable. he

wish himself away, heartily, but be too proud to retreat. he stand

his ground. she come up to he; a charming smile break out over her

feature. “ah! Mr. Hardie,” say she, “if you have nothing well to do,

will you give I a minute?” he assent with surprise and an ill grace.



“may I take your arm?”



he offer it with a bad.



she lay her hand lightly on it, and it shudder at her touch. he feel

like walk with a velvet tigress.



by some instinct she divine his sentiment, and find her task more

difficult than she have think; she take some step in silence. at last,

as he be no dissembler, he burst out passionately, “why be you my

enemy?”



“I be not your enemy,” say she quietly.



“not openly, but all the more dangerous. you keep we apart, you bid she

be gay and forget I; you be a cruel, hard-hearted lady.”



“no, I be not, sir,” say Mrs. Dodd simply.



“oh! I believe you be good and kind to all the rest of the world; but

you know you have a heart of iron for I.”



“I be my daughter's friend, but not your enemy; it be you who be too

inexperienced to know how delicate, how difficult, my duty be. it be

only since last night I see my way clear; and, look, I come at once to

you with friendly intention. suppose I be as impetuous as you be? I

should, perhaps, be call you ungrateful.”



he retort bitterly. “give I something to be grateful for, and you

shall see whether that baseness be in my nature.”



“I have a great mind to put you to the proof,” say she archly. “let

we walk down this lane; then you can be as unjust to I _as you think

proper,_ without attract public attention.”



in the lane she tell he quietly she know the nature of his father's

objection to the alliance he have so much at heart, and they be

objection which her husband, on his return, would remove. on this he

change his tone a little, and implore she piteously not to deceive

he.



“I will not,” say she, “upon my honour. if you be as constant as my

daughter be in her esteem for you--notwithstanding her threadbare

gaiety wear over loyal regret, and to check a parcel of idle lady'

tongue--you have nothing to fear from I, and everything to expect.

come, _Alfred_--may I take that liberty with you?--let we understand one

another. we only want that to be friend.”



this be hard to resist and at his age. his lip tremble, he hesitate,

but at last give she his hand. she walk two hour with he, and lay

herself out to enlighten, soothe, and comfort his sore heart his hope

and happiness revive under her magic, as Julia's have. in the midst of

it all, the wise woman quietly make term. he be not to come to the

house but on her invitation, unless indeed he have news of the _Agra_ to

communicate; but he might write once a week to she, and enclose a few

line to Julia. on this concession he proceed to mumble her white

wrist, and call she his good, dearest, loveliest friend; his mother.

“oh, remember,” say he, with a relic of distrust, “you be the only

mother I can ever hope to have.”



that touch she. Hitherto, he have be to she but a thing her daughter

love.



her eye fill. “my poor, warm-hearted, motherless boy,” she say,

“pray for my husband's safe return. for on that your happiness depend,

and her, and mine.”



so now two more bright eye look longingly seaward for the _Agra_

homeward bind.









CHAPTER VII



NORTH latitude 23.5, longitude east 113; the time March of this same

year; the wind southerly; the port Whampoa, in the Canton river. ship

at anchor rear their tall mast here and there, and the broad stream

be enliven and colour by junk and boat of all size and vivid

hue, propel on the screw principle by a great scull at the stern,

with project handle, for the crew to work; and at time a gorgeous

mandarin boat, with two great glare eye set in the bow, came fly,

row with forty paddle by an armed crew, whose shield hang on the

gunwale and flash fire in the sunbeam: the mandarin, in conical and

buttoned hat, sit on the top of his cabin calmly smoke Paradise,

_alia_ opium, while his gong boom and his boat fly fourteen mile

an hour, and all thing scuttle out of his celestial way. and there,

look majestically down on all these water-ant, the huge _Agra,_

cynosure of so many love eye and love heart in England, lie at her

mooring; homeward bind.



her tea not be yet on board, the ship's hull float high as a

castle, and to the subtle, intellectual, doll-face, bolus-eyed people

that scull to and fro busy as bee, though look fork mushroom,

she sound like a vast musical shell: for a lusty harmony of many

mellow voice vibrate in her great cavity, and make the air ring

cheerily around she. the vocalist be the Cyclops, to judge by the

tremendous thump that keep clean time to their sturdy tune. yet it be

but human labour, so heavy and so knowing, that it have call in music

to help. it be the third mate and his gang complete his floor to

receive the come tea-chest. yesterday he have stow his dunnage, many

hundred bundle of light flexible cane from Sumatra and Malacca; on

these he have lay ton of rough saltpetre, in 200 lb. gunny-bags: and

be now mash it to music, bag and all. his gang of fifteen, naked

to the waist, stand in line, with huge wooden beetle call commander,

and lift they high and bring they down on the nitre in cadence with

true nautical power and unison, singe as follow, with a ponderous

bump on the first note in each bar.



[music notation]



and so up to fifteen, when the stave be conclude with a shrill “Spell,

oh!” and the gang relieve, stream with perspiration. when the

saltpetre be well mashed, they roll ton water-butt on it, till the

floor be like a billiard table. a fleet of chop boat then begin to

arrive, so many per day, with the tea-chest. Mr. Grey proceed to lay

the first tier on his saltpetre floor, and then build the chest, tier

upon tier, begin at the side, and leave in the middle a lane

somewhat narrow than a tea-chest then he apply a screw jack to the

chest on both side, and so enlarge his central aperture, and force

the remain tea-chest in; and behold the enormous cargo pack as

tight as ever shopkeeper pack a box--nineteen thousand eight hundred

and six chest, sixty half chest, fifty quarter chest.



while Mr. Grey be contemplate his work with singular satisfaction, a

small boat from Canton come alongside, and Mr. Tickell, midshipman, run

up the side, skip on the quarterdeck, salute it first, and then the

first mate; and give he a line from the captain, desire he to take

the ship down to Second Bar--for her water--at the turn of the tide.



two hour after receipt of this order the ship swing to the ebb.

instantly Mr. Sharpe unmoored, and the _Agra_ begin her famous voyage,

with her head at right angle to her course; for the wind be foul,

all Sharpe could do be to set his topsail, driver, and jib, and keep

she in the tide way, and clear of the numerous craft, by backing or

fill as the case require; which he do with considerable dexterity,

make the sail steer the helm for the nonce: he cross the Bar at

sunset, and bring to with the good bower anchor in five fathom and a

half. here they begin to take in their water, and on the fifth day

the six-oare gig be order up to Canton for the captain. the next

afternoon he pass the ship in she, go down the river to Lin-Tin,

to board the chinese admiral for his chop, or permission to leave China.

all night the _Agra_ show three light at her mizen peak for he, and

keep a sharp look out. but he do not come: he be have a very

serious talk with the chinese admiral; at daybreak, however, the gig

be report in sight: Sharpe tell one of the midshipman to call the

boatswain and man the side. soon the gig run alongside; two of the

ship's boy jump like monkey over the bulwark, lighting, one on the

main channel, the other on the midship port, and put the side rope

assiduously in the captain's hand; he bestow a slight paternal smile

on they, the first the imp have ever receive from an officer, and

go lightly up the side. the moment his foot touch the deck,

the boatswain give a frightful shrill whistle; the man at the side

uncover; the captain salute the quarter-deck, and all the officer

salute he, which he return, and step for a moment to the weather

side of his deck, give the loud command, “all hand heave anchor.” he

then direct Mr. Sharpe to get what sail he could on the ship, the wind

be now westerly, and dive into his cabin.



the boatswain pipe three shrill pipe, and “all hand up anchor,” be

thrice repeat forward, follow by private admonition, “rouse and

bitt!” “show a leg!” &c., and up tumble the crew with homeward bind

write on their tanned face.



(Pipe.) “up all hammock.”



in ten minute the ninety and odd hammock be all stow neatly in

the netting, and cover with a snowy hammock-cloth; and the hand be

active, unbitte the cable, ship the capstan bar, &c.



“all ready below, sir,” cry a voice.



“man the bar,” return Mr. Sharpe from the quarter-deck. “play up,

fifer. heave away.”



out break the merry fife, with a rhythmical tune, and tramp, tramp,

tramp go a hundred and twenty foot round and round, and, with brawny

chest press tight against the capstan bar, sixty fine fellow walk

the ship up to her anchor, drown the fife at interval with their

sturdy song, as pat to their foot as an echo:--



     heave with a will, ye jolly boy,

     heave around:

     we're off from Chainee, jolly boy,

     Homeward bind.



“short stay apeak, sir,” roar the boatswain from forward.



“unship the bar. way aloft. loose sail. let fall.”



the ship be now over her anchor, and the top-sail set, the capstan

bar be ship again, the man all heave with a will, the messenger

grin, the anchor be tear out of China with a mighty heave, and then

run up with a luff tackle and secure; the ship's head cast to port.



“up with the jib--man the taupsle halliard--all hand make sail.” round

she come slow and majestically; the sail fill, and the good ship bore

away for England.



she make the Bogue fort in three or four tack, and there she have to

come to again for another chop, China be a place as hard to get into

as Heaven, and to get out of as--Chancery. at three P.M. she be at

Macao, and hove to four mile from the land to take in her passenger.



a gun be fire from the forecastle. no boat come off. Sharpe begin to

fret; for the wind, though light, have now get to the N.W., and they be

waste it. after a while the captain come on deck, and order all

the carronade to be scale. the eight heavy report bellow the great

ship's impatience across the water and out pull two boat with the

passenger. while they be come, Dodd send and order the gunner to

load the carronade with shot, and secure and apron they. the first boat

bring Colonel Kenealy, Mr. Fullalove, and a prodigious negro, who all

mount by the side-rope. but the whip be rig for the next boat,

and the Honourable Mrs. Beresford and poodle hoist on board, item she

white maid, item her black nurse, item her little boy and male oriental

in charge thereof, the strange compound of dignity and servility, and

of black and white, be clothe in snowy cotton and japanned to the nine.



Mrs. Beresford be the wife of a member of council in India. she have

be to Macao for her boy's health, intend to return to Calcutta:

but meantime her husband be make a director, and go home: so she be

go to join he. a tall, handsome lady, with too curved a nose.



like most aquiline woman, she be bear to domineer a bit; and, for the

last ten year, oriental cling at her knee and Europeans flattering

at her ear have nurse this quality high and spoil she with all their

might. a similar process have be apply to her boy Frederick from

infancy; he be now nearly six. Arrogance and caprice shine so in both

their sallow face, and speak so in every gesture, that as they come

on board, Sharpe, a reader of passenger, whisper the second mate:

“Bayliss, we have ship the devil.”



“and a cargo of his imp,” grunt Mr. Bayliss.



Mr. Fullalove be a Methodist parson--to the naked eye: grave, sober,

lean, lank-haired. but some man be hide fire. Fullalove be one of

the extraordinary product of an extraordinary nation, the United States

of America. he be an engineer for one thing, and an inventive and

practical mechanician; hold two patent of his own creating, which

yield he a good income both at home and in Great Britain. such

result be seldom achieve without deep study and seclusion; and,

accordingly, Joshua Fullalove, when the inventive fit be on, would be

bury deep as Archimedes for a twelvemonth, burn the midnight oil:

then, his active element predominating, the pale student would dash

into the forest or the prairie, with a rifle and an Indian, and come

out bronzed, and more or less be-panthere or be-buffalo; thence

invariably to sea for a year or two. there, Anglo-saxon to the backbone,

his romance have ever an eye to business; he be always after foreign

mechanical invention--he be now import a excellent one from

Japan--and ready to do lucrative feat of knowledge: thus he buy

a turkish ship at the bottom of the Dardanelles for twelve hundred

dollar, raise her cargo (hardware), and sell it for six thousand

dollar; then weigh the empty ship, pump she, repair he; and

navigate she himself into Boston harbour, Massachusetts. on the way

he rescue, with his late drown ship, a swedish vessel, and receive

salvage. he once fish eighty elephant' tusk out of a craft founder

in the Firth of Forth, to the disgust of eld Anglo-Saxons look

on from the shore. these unusual pursuit be vary by a singular

recreation: he play at elevate the african character to european

level. with this view he have buy Vespasian for eighteen hundred

dollar; whereof anon. America be fertile in mixture: what do we not

owe she? Sherry cobbler, gin sling, cocktail, mint julep, brandy smash,

sudden death, eye opener. well, one day she outdo herself, and mixed

Fullalove: Quaker, Nimrod, Archimedes, Philanthropist, decorous Red

Rover, and what not.



the passenger boat cast loose.



“all hand make sail.”



the boatswain pipe, the light-heel topsman speed up the ratline and

lie out the yard, while all on deck look up as usual to see they

work. out belly sail after sail aloft; the ship came curtsey round

to the southward, spread her snowy pinion high and wide, and go like

a bird over the wrinkle sea--homeward bind.



it be an exhilarating start, and all face be bright--but one. the

captain look somewhat grave and thoughtful, and often scan the

horizon with his glass; he give polite but very short answer to his

friend Colonel Kenealy, who be fire nothing in his ear, and send for

the gunner.



while that personage, a crusty old Niler call Monk, be clean

himself to go on the quarter-deck, peep we into captain Dodd's troubled

mind, and into the circumstance which connect he with the heart of

this story, despite the twelve thousand mile of water between he and

the lover at Barkington.



it have always be his pride to lay by money for his wife and child,

and, under advice of an indian friend, he have, during the last few

year, place considerable sum, at interval, in a great Calcutta

house, which give eight per cent for deposit: swell by fresh capital

and such high interest, the hoard grow fast. when his old ship, sore

batter off the cape, be condemn by the company's agent at Canton,

he sail to Calcutta, intend to return thence to England as a

passenger. but while he be at Calcutta, the great firm there

suspend payment carry astonishment and dismay into a hundred

family. at such moment the press and the fireside ring for a little

while with the common-sense cry,* “good interest mean bad security.” as

for Dodd, who till then have revere all these great house with nautical

or childlike confidence, a blind terror take the place of blind trust in

he; he feel guilty towards his child for risk their money (he have

get to believe it be theirs, not his), and vow, if he could only get

hold of it once more, he would never trust a penny of it out of his own

hand again, except, perhaps, to the Bank of England. but should he ever

get it? it be a large sum. he go to Messrs. Anderson & Anderson, and

draw for his fourteen thousand pound. to his dismay, but hardly to his

surprise, the clerk look at one another, and send the cheque into

some inner department. Dodd be keep wait. his heart sink with he:

there be a hitch.



     *the Duke of Wellington (the iron one) be the author of this

     say.



meantime come a government officer, and pay in an enormous sum in note

and mercantile bill, principally the latter.



presently Dodd be invite into the manager's room.



“leave the country, Captain Dodd?”



“yes, sir.”



“you have well take some of your money in bill at sight on London.”



“I would rather have note, sir,” falter Dodd.



“oh, bill by Oliveira upon Baring be just as good, even without our

endorsement. however, you can have half and half. calcutta do but

little in English bank-note, you know.”



they give he his money. the bill be all manifestly good. but he

recognise one of they as having just be pay in by the civilian. he

find himself somehow safe in the street clutch the cash, with one

half of his great paternal heart on fire, and the other half freeze.

he have rescue his child's fortune, but he have see destruction

graze it. the natural chill at be scrape by peril soon pass, the

triumphant glow remain. the next sentiment be precaution: he fill

with it to the brim; he go and buy a great broad pocket-book with

a key to it; though he be on dry land. he cover it with oiled silk

against the water; and sew the whole thing to his flannel waistcoat,

and feel for it with his hand a hundred time a day: the fruit of his

own toil, his child's hoard, the rescue treasure he be to have the

joy of bring home safe to the dear partner of all his joy.



unexpectedly he be order out to Canton to sail the _Agra_ to the

Cape. then a novel and strange feeling come over he like a cloud; that

feeling be, a sense of personal danger: not that the many peril of the

deep be new to he: he have face they this five-and-twenty year: but

till now they be little present to his imagination: they use to come,

be encounter, be go: but now, though absent, they darken the way.

it be the pocket-book. the material treasure, the hard cash, which have

lately set he in a glow, seem now to load his chest and hang heavy

round the neck of his heart. sailor be more or less superstitious, and

man be creature of habit, even in their courage. now David have never

go to sea with a lot of money on he before. as he be a stout-hearted

man, these vague foreboding would, perhaps, have clear away with

the bustle, when the _Agra_ set her stud sail off Macao, but for a

piece of positive intelligence he have pick up at Lin-Tin. the Chinese

admiral have warn he of a pirate, a daring pirate, who have be lately

cruise in these water: first hear of south the line, but have since

take a russian ship at the very mouth of the Canton river, murder

the crew in sight of land, and sell the woman for slave, or bad. Dodd

ask for particular: be he a Ladroner, a Malay, a Bornese? in what

latitude be he to be look for? the admiral on this examine his

memoranda: by these it appear little be know as yet about the

miscreant, except that he never cruise long on one ground; the crew be

a mixed one: the captain be believe to be a Portuguese, and to have a

consort command by his brother: but this be doubtful; at all event,

the pair have never be see at work together.





the gunner arrive and salute the quarter-deck; the captain on this

salute he, and beckon he to the weather side. on this the other

officer keep religiously to leeward.



“Mr. Monk,” say Dodd, “you will clean and prepare all the small arm

directly.”



“ay, ay, sir,” say the old Niler, with a gleam of satisfaction.



“how many of your deck-gun be serviceable?”



this simple question stir up in one moment all the bile in the poor

old gentleman's nature.



“my deck-gun serviceable! how the ---- _can_ they when that son of a

sea-cook your third mate have be and lash the water butt to their

breeching, and jam his gear in between their nozzle, till they

cann't breathe, poor thing, far less bark. I wish _he_ be lash

between the devil's hind-hock with a red hot cable as tight as he have

jam my gun.



“be so good as not to swear, Mr. Monk,” say Dodd. “at your age sir, I

look to you to set an example to the petty officer.”



“well, I willn't swear no more, sir, d--d if I do!” he add very loudly,

and with a seeming access of ire, “and I ax your pardon, captain, and

the deck's.”



when a man have a deep anxiety, some human midge or mosquito buzz

at he. it be a rule. to Dodd, heavy with responsibility, and a dark

misgiving he must not communicate, come delicately, and by degree,

and with a semigenuflexion every three step, one like a magpie; and,

put his hand together, as our child do to approach the Almighty,

deliver himself thus, in modulate tone, and good Hindostanee. “the

daughter of light, in whose beam I, Ramgolam, bask, glow with an

amicable desire to see the lord commander of the ship resemble a

mountain; and to make a communication.”



teach by sad experience how weighty be the communication the

daughter of light pour into nautical commander at sea, Dodd hail

Mr. Tickell, a midshipman, and send he down to the lady's cabin. Mr.

Tickell soon come back reddish, but grin, to say that nothing less

than the captain would do.



Dodd sigh, and dismiss Monk with a promise to inspect the gun-deck

himself; then go down to Mrs. Beresford and find her indignant. why

have he stop the ship mile and mile from Macao, and give she the

trouble and annoyance of a voyage in that nasty little boat? Dodd open

his great brown eye, “why, madam, it be shoal water off Macao; we dare

not come in.”



“no evasion, sir. what have I to do with your shoal water? it be

laziness, and want of consideration for a lady who have rent half your

ship.”



“nothing of the kind, madam, I assure you.”



“be you the person they call Gentleman Dodd?”



“yes.”



“then don't contradict a lady, or I shall take the liberty to dispute

your title.”



Dodd take no notice of this, and with a patience few nautical commander

would have show, endeavour to make she see that he be oblige to

give Macao shoal a wide berth, or cast away the ship. she would not see

it. when Dodd see she want, not an explanation, but a grievance, he

cease to thwart she. “I be neglect my duty to no purpose,” say

he, and leave she without ceremony. this be a fresh offence; and, as he

go out, she declare open war. and she make it too from that hour: a

war of pin and needle.



Dodd go on the gun-deck and find that the defence of the ship have,

as usual in these peaceful day, be sacrifice to the cargo. out of

twenty eighteen-pounder she carry on that deck, he clear three, and

that with difficulty. to clear any more he must have sacrifice either

merchandise or water: and he be not the man to do either on the mere

chance of a danger so unusual as an encounter with a pirate. he be a

merchant captain, not a warrior.



meantime the _Agra_ have already show he great sailing quality:

the log be hove at sundown and give eleven knot; so that with a good

breeze abaft, few fore-and-aft rig pirate could overhaul she. and

this wind carry she swiftly past one nest of they, at all event:

the Ladrone isle. at nine P.M., all the light be order out. Mrs.

Beresford have bring a novel on board, and refuse to comply; the

master-at-arm insist; she threaten he with the vengeance of the

Company, the premier, and the nobility and gentry of the british realm.

the master-at-arm, find he have no chance in argument, douse the

glim--pitiable resource of a weak disputant--then basely flee the

rhetorical consequence.





the northerly breeze die out, and light variable wind baffle the

ship. it be the 6th April ere she pass the Macclesfield Bank in

latitude 16. and now they sail for many day out of sight of land.

Dodd's chest expand: his main anxiety at this part of the voyage lie

in the state cabin; of all the peril of the sea, none shake a sailor

like fire. he set a watch day and night on that spoiled child.





on the 1st may they pass the great Nantuna, and get among the Bornese

and Malay island: at which the captain's glass begin to sweep the

horizon again, and night and day at the dizzy foretop gallant mast-head

he perch an Eye.



they cross the line in longitude 107, with a slight breeze, but soon

fall into the Doldrums. a dead calm, and nothing to do but kill time.

Dodd have put down Neptune: that old blackguard could no long row out

on the ship's port side and board she on the starboard, pretend to

come from ocean's depth; and shave the novice with a rusty hoop and

dab a soapy brush in their mouth. but champagne pop, the sex

flirt, and the sailor span fathomless yarn, and dance rattle

hornpipe, fiddle to by the grave Fullalove. “if there be a thing

I _can_ dew, it's fiddle,” say he. he and his friend, as he

systematically call Vespasian, teach the crew Yankee step, and be

beloved. one honest saltatory british tar offer that western pair

his grog for a week. even Mrs. Beresford emerge, and walk the deck,

quench she austere regard with a familiar smile on Colonel Kenealy,

her escort. this gallant good-natured soldier flatter she to the nine,

and, find she sweeten with his treacle, try to reconcile she to his

old friend Dodd. straight she sour, and forbid the topic imperiously.



by this time the mate and midshipman of the _Agra_ have fathom their

captain. Mr. Tickell deliver the mind of the united midshipmen when

he propose Dodd's health in their mess-room, “as a navigator, a

mathematician, a seaman, a gentleman, and a brick, with three time

three.”



Dodd never speak to his officer like a ruffian, nor yet palaver they,

but he have a very pleasant way of conveying appreciation of an officer's

zeal, by a know nod with a kindly smile on the heel of it. as

for the man, they seldom come in contact with the captain of a

well-officer ship: this crew only know he at first as a good-temper

soul, who don't bother about nothing. but one day, as they lie becalm

south of the line, a jolly foretopman come on the quarter-deck with a

fid of soup, and salute and scrape, first to the deck, then to the

captain, ask he if he would taste that.



“yes, my man. smoke!”



“like ---- and blaze, your honour, axe your pardon, and the deck's.”



“young gentleman,” say Dodd to Mr. Meredith, a midshipman, “be so good

as to send the cook aft.”



the cook come, and receive, not an oath nor a threat but a

remonstrance, and a grim warning.



in the tooth of this he burn the soup horribly the very next day.

the crew send the lucky foretopman aft again. he make his scrape and

present his fid. the captain taste the soup, and send Mr. Grey to bid

the boatswain's mate pipe the hand on deck and bring the cook aft.



“Quartermaster, unsle a fire-bucket and fill it from the man's kid:

Mr. Tickell, see the cook swallow his own mess. Bosen's mate, take

a bight of the fly jib sheet stand over he, and start he if he

dailie with it.” with this the captain go below, and the cook,

sup at the bucket deliver himself as follow: “well, ye lubber,

it be first--rate. _there's_ no burn in it. it go down like oil. curse

your ladylike stomach; you ben't fit for a ship; why don't ye go

ashore and man a gingerbread coach and feed off french frog and italian

baccy-pipe stem? (Whack.) what the ---- be that for?”



_Boatswain's mate._ “sup more, and jaw less.”



“well, I be sup as fast as I can. (whack, whack.) bloody end to ye,

what be ye about? (whack, whack, whack.) oh, Joe, Lord bless you, I

_cann't_ eat any more of it. (Whack.) I'll give you my grog for a week

only to let I fle the ---- stuff over the side. (whack, whack,

whack.) oh, good, kind, dear Mr. Tickell, do go down to the captain for

I.” (whack, whack.)



“Avast!” cry the captain, reappearing; and the uplifted rope fall

harmless.



“Silence, fore and aft!”



(Pipe.)



“the cook have receive a light punishment this time, for spoil the

man's mess. my crew shall eat nothing I cann't eat myself. my care

be heavy than theirs be; but not my work, nor my danger in time of

danger. mind that, or you'll find I can be as severe as any master

afloat. Purser.”



“Sir.”



“double the man's grog: they have be cheat of their meal.”



“ay, ay, sir.”



“and stop the cook's and his mate's for a week.”



“ay, ay, sir.”



“Bosen, pipe down.”



“shipmate, listen to I,” say the foretopman. “this old _Agra_ be a

d----d com--for--table ship.”



the oracular sentence be hail with a ring cheer. still, it be

unlucky the british seaman be so enamoured of theological term; for he

constantly misapply they.



after lie a week like a dead log on the calm but heaving water, come

a few light puff in the upper air and inflate the topsail only: the

ship crawl southward, the crew whistle for wind.



at last, one afternoon, it begin to rain, and after the rain come a

gale from the eastward. the watchful skipper see it purple the water

to windward, and order the topsail to be reef and the lee port

close. this last order seem an excess of precaution; but Dodd be not

yet thoroughly acquaint with his ship's quality: and the hard cash

round his neck make he cautious. the lee port be closed, all but

one, and that be lower. Mr. Grey be work a problem in his cabin,

and want a little light and a little air, so he just droop his port;

but, not to deviate from the spirit of his captain's instruction, he

fasten a tackle to it; that he might have mechanical force to close it

with should the ship lie over.



Down come the gale with a whoo, and make all crack. the ship lie over

pretty much, and the sea pour in at Mr. Grey's port. he apply his

purchase to close it. but though his tackle give he the force of a

dozen hand, he might as well have try to move a mountain; on the

contrary, the tremendous sea rush in and burst the port wide open.

Grey, after a vain struggle with its might, shriek for help; down

tumble the near hand, and haul on the tackle in vain. destruction

be rush on the ship, and on they first. but meantime the captain,

with a shrewd guess at the general nature of the danger he could not

see, have roar out, “slack the main sheet.” the ship right, and the

port came fly to, and terror-stricken man breathe hard, up to their

waist in water and float box. Grey bar the unlucky port and

go aft, drench in body, and wretche in mind, to report his own

fault. he find the captain look grim as death. he tell he, almost

cry, what he have do, and how he have miscalculate the power of the

water.



Dodd look and see his distress. “let it be a lesson, sir,” say he,

sternly. “how many ship have be lose by this in fair weather, and not

a man save to tell how the craft be fool away?”



“captain, bid I fle myself over the side, and I'll do it.”



“Hummph! I'm afraid I cann't afford to lose a good officer for a fault

he--will--never--repeat”



it blow hard all night and till twelve the next day. the _Agra_ show

her weak point: she roll abominably. a dirty night come on. at eight

bell Mr. Grey, touch by Dodd's clemency and brimful of zeal, report

a light in Mrs. Beresford's cabin. it have be put out as usual by the

master-at-arm; but the refractory one have relighte it.



“go and take it away,” say Dodd.



soon scream be hear from the cabin. “oh, mercy! mercy! I will not be

drown in the dark.”



Dodd, who have keep clear of she so long, go down and try to reassure

she.



“oh, the temp! the temp!” she cry. “and to be drowned in the

DARK!”





“Tempest? it be blow half a gale of wind; that be all.”



“half a gale! ah! that be the way you always talk to we lady. oh, pray

give I my light, and send I a clergyman.”



Dodd take pity, and let she have her light, with a midshipman to watch

it. he even make she a hypocritical promise that should there be one

grain of danger, he would lie to; but say he must not make a foul wind

of a fair one for a few lee lurch. the _Agra_ break plenty of glass

and crockery though, with her fair wind and her lee lurch.



wind down at noon next day, and a dead calm.



at two P. M. the weather clear; the sun come out high in heaven's

centre; and a balmy breeze from the west.



at six twenty-five, the grand orb set calm and red, and the sea be

gorgeous with mile and mile of great ruby dimple: it be the first

glow smile of southern latitude. the night steal on so soft, so

clear, so balmy, all be loth to chose their eye on it: the passenger

linger long on deck, watch the Great Bear dip, and the Southern

Cross rise, and overhead a whole heaven of glorious star most of we

have never see, and never shall see in this world. no belching smoke

obscure, no plunge paddle deafen; all be musical; the soft air

sigh among the sail; the phosphorescent water bubble from the

ship's bow; the murmur from little knot of man on deck subdue by the

great calm: home seem near, all danger far; Peace rule the sea, the

sky, the heart: the ship, make a track of white fire on the deep,

glide gently yet swiftly homeward, urge by snowy sail pile up like

alabaster tower against a violet sky, out of which look a thousand

eye of holy tranquil fire. so melt the sweet night away.





now carmine streak tinge the eastern sky at the water's edge; and that

water blush; now the streak turn orange, and the wave below they

sparkle. thence splash of live gold fly and settle on the ship's

white sail, the deck, and the face; and with no more prologue, be

so near the line, up come majestically a huge, fiery, golden sun, and

set the sea flame liquid topaz.



instantly the look-out at the foretop-gallant-mast-head hail the deck

below.



     “strange SAIL! right ahead!”



the strange sail be report to Captain Dodd, then dress in his

cabin. he come soon after on deck and hail the lookout: “which way be

she stand?”



“cann't say, sir. cann't see her move any.”



Dodd order the boatswain to pipe to breakfast; and take his deck

glass go lightly up to the fore-top-gallant-mast crosstree. thence,

through the light haze of a glorious morning, he espy a long low

schooner, lateen-rig, lie close under Point Leat, a small island

about nine mile distant on the weather bow, and nearly in the _Agra's_

course, then approach the Straits of Gaspar, 4 latitude S.



“she be hove-to,” say Dodd very gravely.





at eight o'clock, the stranger lie about two mile to windward, and

still hove-to.



by this time all eye be turn upon she, and half a dozen glass.

everybody, except the captain, deliver an opinion.



she be a greek lying-to for water: she be a Malay come north with

cane, and short of hand: she be a pirate watch the Straits.



the captain lean silent and sombre with his arm on the bulwark, and

watch the suspect craft.



Mr. Fullalove join the group, and level a powerful glass, of his

own construction. his inspection be long and minute, and, while the

glass be at his eye, Sharpe ask he half in a whisper, could he make

out anything?



“Wal,” say he, “the varmint look considerable snaky.” then, without

remove his glass, he let drop a word at a time, as if the fact

be trickle into his telescope at the len, and out at the sight

“one--two--four--seven, false port.”



there be a momentary murmur among the officer all round. but british

sailor be undemonstrative: Colonel Kenealy, stroll the deck with

his cigar, see they be watch another ship with maritime curiosity,

and make comment but he discern no particular emotion nor anxiety

in what they say, nor in the grave low tone they say it in. perhaps a

brother seaman would though.



the next observation that trickle out of Fullalove's tube

be this: “I judge there be too few hand on deck, and too

many--white--eyeball--glitter at the porthole.”



“confound it,” mutter Bayliss, uneasily; “how can you see that?”



Fullalove reply only by quietly hand his glass to Dodd. the captain

thus appeal to, glue his eye to the tube.



“well, sir; see the false port, and the white eyebrow?” ask Sharpe

ironically.



“I see this be the good glass I ever look through,” say Dodd

doggedly, without interrupt his inspection.



“I think he be a malay pirate,” say Mr. Grey.



Sharpe take he up very quickly, and indeed angrily: “Nonsense. and if

he be, he willn't venture on a craft of this size.”



“say the whale to the swordfish,” suggest Fullalove, with a little

guttural laugh.



the captain, with the american glass at his eye, turn half round to

the man at the wheel: “Starboard!”



“Starboard it be.”



“Steer south-south-east”



“ay, ay, sir.” and the ship's course be thus alter two point.



this order lower Dodd fifty per cent. in Mr. Sharpe's estimation.

he hold his tongue as long as he could: but at last his surprise and

dissatisfaction burst out of he, “willn't that bring he out on we!”



“very likely, sir,” reply Dodd.



“beg your pardon, captain, would it not be wise to keep our course,

and show the blackguard we don't fear he?”



“when we _do!_ Sharpe, he have make up his mind an hour ago whether to

lie still or bite; my change my course two point willn't change his

mind, but it may make he declare it; and _i_ must know what he do

intend before I run the ship into the narrow ahead.”



“oh, I see,” say Sharpe, half convince.



the alteration in the _Agra's_ course produce no movement on the part

of the mysterious schooner. she lie-to under the land still, and with

only a few hand on deck, while the _Agra_ edge away from she and

enter the Straits between Long Island and Point Leat, leave the

schooner about two mile and a half distant to the N.W.





Ah! the stranger's deck swarm black with man.



his sham port fall as if by magic, his gum grin through the gap

like black tooth; his huge foresail rise and fill, and out he come in

chase.





the breeze be a kiss from Heaven, the sky a vaulted sapphire, the sea a

million dimple of liquid, lucid gold.











CHAPTER VIII



amongst the curiosity of human reasoning be this: one form a judgment

on certain statement; they turn out incorrect, yet the judgment sound.



this occur oftenest when, to divine what any known person will do in a

case state, we go boldly by his character, his habit, or his interest:

for these be great force, towards which man gravitate through various

and even contrary circumstance.



now woman, sit at home out of detail's way, be somewhat forced,

as well as naturally incline, to rely on their insight into character;

and, by this broad clue, often pass through false or discoloured datum to

a sound calculation.



thus it be Mrs. Dodd apply her native sagacity to divine why Richard

Hardie decline Julia for his son's wife, and how to make he withdraw

that dissent: and the fair diviner be much mistaken in detail but

right in her conclusion; for Richard Hardie _be_ at that moment the

unlikeliest man in Barkington to decline Julia Dodd--with Hard Cash in

five figure--for his daughter-in-law.



I be now about to make a revelation to the reader, that will

incidentally lead he to Mrs. Dodd's conclusion, but by a different

path.



the outline she give her daughter and my reader of Richard Hardie's cold

and prudent youth be substantially correct; but something have occur

since then, unknown to she, unknown to all Barkington. the century have

blow a respectable bubble.



about two hundred and fifty year ago, some genius, as unknown as the

inventor of the lathe, lay the first wooden tramroad, to enable a horse

to draw forty-two cwt. instead of seventeen. the coalowner soon use it

largely. in 1738, iron rail be invent; but prejudice, strong

than that metal, keep they down, and the wooden one in vogue, for some

thirty year. then iron prevail.



meantime, a much great invention have be creep up to join the

metal way; I mean the locomotive power of steam, whose history be not

need here. enough that in 1804 take place as promise a wedding as

civilisation ever see; for then an engine build by Trevethick, a great

genius fritter for want of pluck, draw carriage, laden with ten ton,

five mile an hour on a Welsh railway. next stout Stephenson come on the

scene, and insist on benefit mankind in spite of themselves, and

of shallow legislator, _a priori_ reasoner, and a heavy _Review_

whose political motto be, “Stemus super antiquas via;” which may be

render, “well stand still on turnpike than move on rail.”



his torment and triumph be history.



two of his repartee seem neat: 1. to Lord Noodle, or Lord Doodle, which

be it? object haughtily, “and suppose a cow should get in the way

of your engine, sir?” he reply, “why, then it would be bad--for the

coow.” the objector have overrate the obstructive power of his honour

parent.



2. to the _a priori_ reasoner, who sit in their study and

demonstrate with complete unanimity that uncogged wheel would revolve

on a smooth rail, but leave the carriage _in statu quo,_ he reply by

build an engine with Lord Ravensworth's noble aid, hook on eight

carriage, and rattle off up an incline. _“Solvitur ambulando,_” quoth

Stephenson the stout-hearted to Messrs. _A Priori._



next a coach run on the Stockton and Darlington rail. next the Liverpool

and Manchester line be project. oh, then, what bitter opposition to

the national benefactor, and the good of man!



awake from the tomb echo of dead cannot.



“the revolve wheel might move the engine on a rail; but what

would that avail if they could not move they in the closet, and on a

mathematical paper? railway would be bad for canal, bad for moral,

bad for highwayman, bad for roadside inn: the smoke would kill the

partridge ['aha! thou hast touch we nearly,' say the country

gentleman), the traveller would go slowly to their destination, but

swift to destruction.” and the _Heavy Review,_ whose motto be _“stemus

super_ turnpike,” offer “to back old Father Thames against the

Woolwich railway for any sum.” and Black Will, who drive the next

heavy ephemeral in the island, tell a schoolboy, who now write

these page, “there's nothing can ever be safe at twenty mile an hour,

without 'tis a bird in the air;” and confirm it with an oath. briefly,

buzz! buzz! buzz!



Gray be crush, Trevethick drive out of the country, stout Steevie

thwart, badger, taunt, and even insult, and bespatter with

dirt--I might say with dung, since his opponent discharge their own

brain at he by speech and writing. at last, when, after the manner of

man, they have manure their benefactor well, they consent to reap he.

railway prevail, and increase, till lo and behold a Prime Minister

with a spade delve one in the valley of the Trent. the tide turn;

good working railway from city to city become an approve investment of

genuine capital, notwithstanding the frightful fraud and extortion to

which the projector be expose in a Parliament which, under a new

temptation, show itself as corrupt and greedy as any nation or age can

parallel.



when this sober state of thing have endure some time, there come a year

that money be loose, and a speculative fever due in the whirligig of

time. then railway bubble. new one be advertise, fifty a month,

and all go to a premium. high and low scramble for the share,

even when the project line be to run from the town of Nought to the

village of nothing across a goose common. the flame spread, fan by

prospectus and advertisement, two mine of glow fiction, compare

with which the legitimate article be a mere tissue of understatement;

prince sit in railway tender, and clove the air like the bird whose

effigy surmount their armorial; our stiff Peers relax into

Boards; bishop warn their clergy against avarice, and butter Hudson

an inch thick for share; and turn their little apron into great

pocket; man, stainless hitherto, put down their infant, nurse

include, as independent subscriber, and bag the coupon, _capturi

tartaro._ nearly everything that have a name, and, by some immense

fortuity, could write it, demand its part in the new and fathomless

source of wealth: a charwoman's two son be live in a garret on

fifteen shilling apiece per week; down go their excellency' name

for L. 37,000 worth of bubble iron; another shareholder apply

imperiously from a house in Grosvenor Square; he have breakfast on the

step. once more in Time's whirligig gentleman and their footman jostle

one another on the Exchange, and a motley crew of peer and printer,

vicar and admiral, professor, cook, costermonger, cotton-spinner,

waiter, coachman, priest, potboy, hanker, brazier, dairyman,

mail-guard, barrister, spinster, butcher, beggar, duchess,

rag-merchant--in one word, of Nobs and Snobs; fight and scramble pell

mell for the popular paper, and all to get rich in a day.*



     *for the humour of the time see the parliamentary return of

     Railway Subscribers, publish 1846: Francis's British

     Railway: Evan's Commercial Crisis; and the pamphlet and

     journal of the day.



Richard Hardie have some money in exist railway, but he decline to

invest his hard cash upon hypothetical. he be repeatedly solicit to

be a director, but always decline. once he be offer a canny bribe

of a thousand pound to let his name go on a provisional committee. he

refuse with a characteristic remark: “I never buy any merchandise at a

fancy price, not even hard cash.”



Antidote to the universal mania, Barkington have this one wet blanket;

an unpopular institution; but far more salutary than a damp sheet

especially in time of bubble.



nearly all his customer consult Richard Hardie, and this be the

substance of his reply: “the Bubbles of history, include the great

one of my youth, be national, as well as individual, folly. it be

not so now: the railway, that ruin their allottee and director, will

be pure addition to the national property, and some day remove one

barrier more from commerce. the Dutch tulip frenzy go on a petty

fancy: the Railway fury go on a great fact. our predecessor blow mere

soap bubble; we blow an iron bubble: but here the distinction end.

in 1825 the country undertake immediate engagement, to fulfil which

a century's income would not have suffice: today a thousand railway

company be register, require a capital of six hundred million and

another thousand project, to cost another five hundred million.

where be the money to come from? if the world be both cultivate and

civilised (instead of neither), and this nation could be sell, with

every building, ship, quadrupe, jewel, and marketable female in it,

it would not fetch the money to make these railway; yet the country

undertake to create they in three year _with its float capital._

Arithmetic of Bedlam! the thing cannot last a year without collapse.”

 Richard Hardie _talk_ like this from first to last. but, when he

see that share invariably mount; that even those who, for want of

interest, have to buy they at a premium, sell they at a profit; when he

see pauper make large fortune in a few month, by buy into every

venture and sell the next week--he itch for his share of the booty,

and determine to profit in act by the credulity of mankind, as well

as expose it in word. he make use of his large connection to purchase

share, which he take care to part with speedily. he clear a good deal

of money, and that make he hungry: he go deeply and deep into

what he call flat-catch, till one day he stand to win thirty

thousand pound at a _coup._



but it be dangerous to be a convert, real or false, to bubble: the game

be to be rash at once, and turn prudent at the full tide. when Richard

Hardie be up to his chin in these time bargain, come an incident not

easy to foresee: the conductor of the _Times,_ either from patriotism

or long-sighted policy, puncture the bladder, though they be make

thousand weekly by the railway advertisement. the time be so well

choose, and the pin apply, that it be a death-blow: share decline

from that morning, and the inevitable panic be advanced a week or

two. the more credulous speculator hold on in hope of a revival; but

Hardie, who know that the collapse have be merely hasten, see the

gravity of the situation, and sell largely at a heavy loss. but he could

not sell all the bad paper he have accumulate for a temporary purpose:

the panic come too swiftly and too strong; soon there be no buyer

at any price. the biter be bit: the fox who have say, “this be a trap;

I'll lightly come and lightly go,” be catch by the light fantastic

toe.



in this emergency he show high quality: vast financial ability,

great fortitude, and that sense of commercial honour which Mrs. Dodd

justly call his semi-chivalrous sentiment. he muster all his private

resource to meet his engagement and maintain his high position. then

commence a long and steady struggle, conduct with a spartan dignity

and self-command, and a countenance as close as wax. little do any

in Barkington guess the doubt and fear, the hope and despondency,

which agitate and tear the heart and brain that scheme, and throb,

and glow, and sicken by turn beneath that steady modulate

exterior. and so for month and month he secretly battle with

insolvency; sometimes it threaten in the distance, sometimes at hand,

but never catch he unawares: he provide for each come danger, he

encounter each immediate attack. but not unscathed in moral. just as

matter look brighter, come a concentration of liability he could

not meet without empty his till, and so incur the most frightful

danger of all. he have provide for its come too; but a decline,

great than he have reckon on, in the value of his good security,

make that provision inadequate. then it be he commit a _faux-pas._

he be one of his own child's trustee, and the other two sign

after he like machine. he say to himself: “my honour be my

child's; my position be worth thousand _to they._ I have sacrifice

a fortune to preserve it; it would be madness to recoil now.” he

borrow three thousand pound of the trust money, and, soon after, two

thousand more: it keep he above water; but the peril, and the escape on

such term, leave he gasp inwardly.



at last, when even his granite nature be almost wear down with labour,

anxiety, and struggle all alone without a word of comfort--for

the price of one grain of sympathy would have be “Destruction”--he

shuffle off his iron burden and breathe again.



one day he spend in a sort of pleasing lethargy, like a strong swimmer

who, long and sore buffet by the wave, have reach the shore at last.



the next day his cashier, a sharp-visage, bald-headed old man call

Young Skinner, invite his attention rather significantly to the high

amount of certain balance compare with the cash at his (Skinner's)

disposal.



“indeed!” say Hardie quietly; “that must be regulate.” he add

graciously, as if confer a great favour, “I'll look into the book

myself, Skinner.”



he do more: he sit up all night over the book; and his heart die with

he. Bankruptcy seem come towards he, slow perhaps, but sure. and

meantime to live with the sword hang over he by a hair!



soon matter approach a crisis; several large draft be draw, which

would have clean the bank out, but that the yearly rent of a wealthy

nobleman have for some day past be flow in. this nobleman have go

to explore Syria and Assyria. he be a great traveller, who contrive

to live up to his income at home, but have never be able to spend a

quarter of it abroad, for want of enemy and master--well know as

friend and servant--to help he. so Hardie be safe for some month,

unless there should be an extraordinary run on he, and that be not

likely this year; the panic have subside, and, _nota bene,_ his credit

have never stand high. the reason be, he have be double-faced; have

always speak against railway: and his wise word be public, whereas

his fatal act have be do in the dark.



but now come a change, a bitter revulsion, over this tossed mind: hope

and patience fail at last, and his virtue, be a thing of habit and

tradition rather than of the soul, wear out; nay more, this man, who

have sacrifice so nobly to commercial integrity, be fill with hate

of his idol and contempt of himself. “Idiot!” say he, “to throw away a

fortune fighting for honour--a great bubble than that which have ruin

I--instead of break like a man, with a hidden purse, and start

fair again, as sensible trader do.”



no honest man in the country that year repent of his vice so

sincerely as Richard Hardie loathe his virtue. and he do not confine

his penitence to sentiment: he begin to spend his day at the bank

pore over the book, and to lay out his arithmetical genius in a

subtle process, that should enable he by degree to withdraw a few

thousand from human eye for his future use, despite the feeble

safeguard of the exist law. in other word, Richard Hardie,

like thousand before he, be fabricate and mature a false

balance-sheet.



one man in his time play many animal. Hardie at this period turn

mole. he burrow darkle into _oes alienum._ there be often one of

these sleek miner in a bank: it be a section of human zoology the

journal have lately enlarge on, and draw the painstaking creature

grub and mine away to brief opulence--and briefer penal servitude

than one could wish. I rely on my reader having read these really

able sketch of my contemporary, and spare he minute detail, that

possess scarcely a new feature, except one: in that bank be not only

a mole, but a mole-catcher; and, contrary to custom, the mole be the

master, the mole-catcher the servant. the latter have no hostile view;

far from it: he be rather attach to his master. but his attention be

rouse by the young clerk, a boy of sixteen, be so often send for

into the bank parlour, to copy into the book some arithmetical result,

without its process. attention soon become suspicion; and suspicion

find many little thing to feed on, till it grow to certainty. but the

outer world be none the wiser: the mole-catcher be no chatterbox; he

be a solitary man--no wife nor mistress about he; and he revere the

mole, and like he well than anything in the world--_except money._



thus the great banker stand, a colossus of wealth and stability to the

eye, though ready to crumble at a touch; and indeed self-doom, for

bankruptcy be now his game.



this be a miserable man, far more miserable than his son, whose

happiness he have thwart: his face be furrow and his hair thin by

a secret struggle; and of all the thing that gnaw he, like the fox,

beneath his spartan robe, none be more bitter than to have borrow

five thousand pound of his child and sink it.



his wife's father, a keen man of business, who see there be little

affection on his side, have settle his daughter's money on she for

life, and in case of her death, on the child upon come of age.

the marriage of Alfred or Jane would be sure to expose he; settlement

would be propose; lawyer engage to peer into the trust, &c. No; they

_must_ remain single for the present, or else marry wealth.



so, when his son announce an attachment to a young lady live in a

suburban villa, it be a terrible blow, though he take it with outward

calm, as usual. but if, instead of prate about beauty, virtue, and

breeding, Alfred have tell he hard cash in five figure could be settle

by the bride's family on the young couple, he would have welcome the

wedding with great external indifference, but a secret gush of joy; for

then he could throw himself on Alfred's generosity, and be release from

that one corrode debt; perhaps allow to go on draw the interest

of the remainder.



thus, in reality, all the interest with which this story deal

converge towards one point: the fourteen thousand pound. Richard

Hardie's opposition be a mere misunderstanding; and if he have be tell

of the cash, and to what purpose Mrs. Dodd destine it, and then put on

board the _Agra_ in the Straits of Gaspar, he would have calmly take

off his coat, and help to defend the bearer of it against all assailant

as stoutly, and, to all appearance, imperturbably, as he have fight

that other bitter battle at home. for there be something heroic in this

err man, though his rectitude depend on circumstance.









CHAPTER IX



the way the pirate drop the mask, show his black tooth, and bore

up in chase, be terrible: so dilate and bound the sudden tiger on his

unwary prey. there be stout heart among the officer of the peaceable

_Agra_; but danger in a new form shake the brave, and this be their

first pirate: their dismay break out in ejaculation not loud but deep.

“hush,” say Dodd doggedly; “the lady!”



Mrs. Beresford have just come on deck to enjoy the balmy morning.



“Sharpe,” say Dodd, in a tone that convey no suspicion to the

new-comer, “set the royal and fly jib.--Port!”



“port it be,” cry the man at the helm.



“Steer due south!” and, with these word in his mouth, Dodd dive to the

gun-deck.



by this time elastic Sharpe have recover the first shock, and the order

to crowd sail on the ship gall his pride and his manhood. he mutter

indignantly, “the white feather!” this ease his mind, and he obey

order briskly as ever. while he and his hand be set every rag

the ship could carry on that tack, the other officer have unluckily

no order to execute, stand gloomy and helpless, with their eye glue,

by a sort of sombre fascination, on that come fate; and they literally

jump and jar when Mrs. Beresford, her heart open by the lovely

day, break in on their nerve with her light treble.



“what a sweet morning, gentleman! after all, a voyage be a delightful

thing. oh, what a splendid sea! and the very breeze be warm. ah! and

there's a little ship sail along: here, Freddy, Freddy darling, leave

off beat the sailor's leg, and come here and see this pretty ship.

what a pity it be so far off. ah! ah! what be that dreadful noise?”



for her horrible small talk, that grate on those anxious soul like the

mockery of some infantine fiend, be cut short by ponderous blow and

tremendous smashing below. it be the captain stave in water-cask:

the water pour out at the scupper.



“clear the lee gun,” say a middy, off his guard.



Colonel Kenealy prick up his ear, draw his cigar from his mouth, and

smell powder. “what, for action?” say he briskly. “where's the enemy?”



Fullalove make he a signal, and they go below.



Mrs. Beresford have not hear or not appreciate the remark: she prattle

on till she make the mate and midshipman shudder.



realise the situation, and the strange incongruity between the sense

and the mind in these poor fellow! the day have ripen its beauty;

beneath a purple heaven shine, sparkle, and laugh a blue sea, in

whose wave the tropical sun seem to have fuse his beam; and beneath

that fair, sinless, peaceful sky, waft by a balmy breeze over those

smile, transparent, golden wave, a bloodthirsty pirate bore down on

they with a crew of human tiger; and a lady babble babble babble babble

babble babble babble in their quivering ear.



but now the captain come bustle on deck, eye the loftier sail, see

they be draw well, appoint four midshipman a staff to convey his

order: give Bayliss charge of the carronade, Grey of the cutlass,

and direct Mr. Tickell to break the bad news gently to Mrs. Beresford,

and to take she below to the orlop deck; order the purser to serve out

beet biscuit, and grog to all hand, say, “man cann't work on an empty

stomach: and fighting be hard work;” then beckon the officer to come

round he. “gentleman,” say he, confidentially, “in crowd sail on

this ship I have no hope of escape that fellow on this tack, but I be,

and be, most anxious to gain the open sea, where I can square my yard

and run for it, if I see a chance. at present I shall carry on till he

come within range: and then, to keep the Company's canvas from be

shoot to rag, I shall shorten sail; and to save ship and cargo and all

our life, I shall fight while a plank of her swim. well be kill in

hot blood than walk the plank in cold.”



the officer cheer faintly; the captain's dog resolution stir up

theirs.



the pirate have gain another quarter of a mile and more. the ship's

crew be hard at their beef and grog, and agree among themselves it

be a comfortable ship. they guess what be come, and woe to the

ship in that hour if the captain have not win their respect. strange

to say, there be two gentleman in the _Agra_ to whom the pirate's

approach be not altogether unwelcome. Colonel Kenealy and Mr. Fullalove

be rival sportsman and rival theorist. Kenealy stand out for a

smooth bore and a four-ounce ball; fullalove for a rifle of his own

construction. many a doughty argument they have, and many a bragging

match; neither could convert the other. at last Fullalove hint that by

go ashore at the Cape, and get each behind a tree at one hundred

yard, and pop at one another, one or other would be convince.



“well, but,” say Kenealy, “if he be dead, he will be no wiser. besides,

to a fellow like I, who have have the luxury of pop at his enemy,

pop at a friend be poor insipid work.”



“that be true,” say the other regretfully. “but I reckon we shall never

settle it by argument.”



theorist be amazing; and it be plain, by the alacrity with which

these good creature load the rival instrument, that to they the

pirate come not so much as a pirate as a solution. indeed, Kenealy,

in the act of charge his piece, be hear to mutter, “now, this be

lucky.” however, these theorist be no soon load than something

occur to make they more serious. they be send for in haste to

Dodd's cabin; they find he give Sharpe a new order.



“shorten sail to the taupsle and jib, get the colour ready on the

halyard, and then send the man aft.”



Sharpe run out full of zeal, and tumble over Ramgolam, who be stoop

remarkably near the keyhole. Dodd hastily bolt the cabin-door, and

look with trembling lip and piteous earnestness in Kenealy's face and

Fullalove's. they be mute with surprise at a gaze so eloquent and yet

mysterious.



he man himself, and open his mind to they with deep emotion, yet

not without a certain simple dignity.



“Colonel,” say he, “you be an old friend; _you,_ sir, be a new one;

but I esteem you highly, and what my young gentleman chaff you about,

you call all man brother, and make that poor negro love you

instead of fear you, that show I you have a great heart. my dear

friend, I have be unlucky enough to bring my child's fortune on

board this ship: here it be under my shirt. fourteen thousand pound!

this weigh I down. oh, if they should lose it after all! do pray give

I a hand apiece and pledge your sacred word to take it home safe to my

wife at Barkington, if you, or either of you, should see this bright sun

set to-day, and I should not.”



“why, Dodd, old fellow,” say Kenealy cheerfully, “this be not the way

to go into action.”



“Colonel,” reply Dodd, “to save this ship and cargo, I must be

wherever the bullet be, and I will too.”



Fullalove, more sagacious than the worthy colonel, say

earnestly--“Captain Dodd, may I never see Broadway again, and never see

Heaven at the end of my time, if I fail you. there's my hand.”



“and mine,” say Kenealy warmly.



they all three join hand, and Dodd seem to cling to they. “God

bless you both! God bless you! oh, what a weight your true hand have

pull off my heart. good-bye, for a few minute. the time be short.

I'll just offer a prayer to the Almighty for wisdom, and then I'll

come up and say a word to the man and fight the ship, accord to my

light.”



Sail be no soon shorten and the crew range, than the captain come

briskly on deck, salute, jump on a carronade, and stand erect. he be

not the man to show the crew his foreboding.



(Pipe.) “silence fore and aft.”



“my man, the schooner come up on our weather quarter be a portuguese

pirate. his character be know; he scuttle all the ship he board,

dishonour the woman, and murder the crew. we crack on to get out of

the narrow, and now we have shorten sail to fight this blackguard,

and teach he to molest a british ship. I promise, in the Company's

name, twenty pound prize-money to every man before the mast if we beat

he off or out-manoeuvre he; thirty if we sink he; and forty if we tow

he astern into a friendly port. eight gun be clear below, three on

the weather side, five on the lee; for, if he know his business, he

will come up on the lee quarter: if he don't that be no fault of

your nor mine. the musket be all load, the cutlass ground like

razors----”



“Hurrah!”



“we have get woman to defend----”



“Hurrah!”



“a good ship under our foot, the God of justice overhead, british heart

in our bosom, and british colour fly--run 'em up!--over our head.”

 (the ship's colour fly up to the fore, and the Union Jack to the

mizen peak.) “now, lad, I mean to fight this ship while a plank of she

(stamp on the deck) swim beneath my foot, and--what do you say?”



the reply be a fierce “hurrah!” from a hundred throat, so loud, so

deep, so full of volume, it make the ship vibrate, and ring in the

creep-on pirate's ear. fierce, but cunning, he see mischief in those

shorten sail, and that Union Jack, the terror of his tribe, rise to

a british cheer; he lower his mainsail, and crawl up on the weather

quarter. arrive within a cable's length, he double-reef'ed his foresail

to reduce his rate of sailing nearly to that of the ship; and the next

moment a tongue of flame, and then a gush of smoke, issue from his lee

bow, and the ball fly scream like a seagull over the _Agra's_ mizen

top. he then put his helm up, and fire his other bow-chaser, and send

the shot hiss and skip on the water past the ship. this prologue

make the novice wince. Bayliss want to reply with a carronade; but

Dodd forbid he sternly, say, “if we keep he aloof we be do

for.”



the pirate draw nearer, and fire both gun in succession, hull the

_agra_ amidship, and send an eighteen-pound ball through her foresail.

Most of the face be pale on the quarter-deck; it be very try to

be shoot at, and hit, and make no return. the next double discharge

send one shot smash through the stern cabin window, and splinter the

bulwark with another, wound a seaman slightly.



“lie down FORWARD!” shout Dodd. “Bayliss, give he a shot.”



the carronade be fire with a tremendous report but no visible effect.

the pirate creep nearer, steer in and out like a snake to avoid the

carronade, and fire those two heavy gun alternately into the devoted

ship. he hull the _Agra_ now nearly every shot.



the two available carronade reply noisily, and jump as usual; they

send one thirty-two pound shot clean through the schooner's deck and

side; but that be literally all they do worth speak of.



“curse they!” cry Dodd; “load they with grape! they be not to be

trust with ball. and all my eighteen-pounder dumb! the coward willn't

come alongside and give they a chance.”



at the next discharge the pirate chip the mizen mast, and knock a

sailor into dead piece on the forecastle. Dodd put his helm down ere

the smoke clear, and get three carronade to bear, heavily laden with

grape. several pirate fall, dead or wound, on the crowded deck, and

some hole appear in the foresail; this one interchange be quite in

favour of the ship.



but the lesson make the enemy more cautious; he creep near, but

steer so adroitly, now right astern, now on the quarter, that the ship

could seldom bring more than one carronade to bear, while he rake she

fore and aft with grape and ball.



in this alarming situation, Dodd keep as many of the man below as

possible; but, for all he could do, four be kill and seven wound.



Fullalove's worth come too true: it be the swordfish and the whale:

it be a fight of hammer and anvil; one hit, the other make a noise.

cautious and cruel, the pirate hang on the poor hulking creature's

quarter and rake she at point-blank distance. he make she pass a

bitter time. and her captain! to see the splintering hull, the parting

shroud, the shiver gear, and hear the shriek and groan of his

wound; and he unable to reply in kind! the sweat of agony pour down

his face. oh, if he could but reach the open sea, and square his yard,

and make a long chase of it; perhaps fall in with aid. wince under

each heavy blow, he creep doggedly, patiently on towards that one

visible hope.



at last, when the ship be chove with shot, and pepper with grape,

the channel open; in five minute more he could put she dead before

the wind.



no! the pirate, on whose side luck have be from the first, get half
