a thing. these sweetheart be not alone, could not open their heart,

must not even gaze too long; yet to be in the same room even on such

term be a taste of Heaven.



“but, dear heart!” say Mrs. Maxley, “ye don't tell I what he ail.

Ma'am, if you have see he you would have say he be take for death.”



“pray what _be_ the complaint?” inquire Mrs. Dodd.



“oh, don't I tell ye? poison.”



this intelligence be convey with true scientific calmness, and

receive with feminine ejaculation of horror. Mrs. Maxley be indignant

into the bargain: “don't ye go give my house an ill name! we keep no

poison.”



Sampson fix his eye sternly on she: “Wumman, ye know well: ye keep

strychnine, for th' use and delectation of your domestic animal.”



“Strychnine! I never hear tell of it. be that Latin for arsenic?”



“now ben't this lamentable? why, arsenic be a mital; strychnine a

vigitable. N'hist I! your man be here seek strychnine to poison

his mouse; a harmless, domistic, necessary mouse. I tell he mouse be a

part of Nature as much as Maxleys, and life as sweet tit as tim: but he

be dif to scientific and chrisehin precep; so I tell he to go to the

Deevil: 'I will,' sis he, and go t' a docker. the two assassin have

poison the poor beastie between 'em; and thin, be the great miser

in the world, except one, he will have roast his victim, and eat she

on the sly, imprignate with strychnine. 'I'll steal a march on t'other

miser,' sis he; and that's you: t' his brain fly the strychnine: his

brain sint it to his spinal marrow: and we find my lorrd bent like a

bow, and his jaw locked, and nearer knowin the great secret than any man

in England will be this year to live: and sairve the assassinate old

vagabin right.”



“heaven forgive you, Doctor,” say Mrs. Maxley, half mechanically.



“for curin a murrderer? not likely.”



Mrs. Maxley, who have show sign of singular uneasiness during Sampson's

explanation, now rise, and say in a very peculiar tone she must go home

directly.



Mrs. Dodd seem to enter into her feeling, and make she go in the fly,

take care to pay the fare and the driver out of her own purse. as

the woman get into the fly, Sampson give she a piece of friendly and

practical advice. “nixt time he have a mind to breakfast on strychnine,

you tell I; and I'll put a pinch of arsenic in the salt-cellar, and

cure he safe as the bank. but this time he'd have be do and stiff

long before such a slow ajint as arsenic could get a hold on um.”





they sit down to luncheon, but neither Alfred nor Julia feed much, except

upon sweet steal look; and soon the active Sampson jump up, and

invite Alfred to go round his patient. Alfred could not decline, but

make his adieux with regret so tender and undisguise, that Julia's

sweet eye fill, and her soft hand instinctively press his at

parting to console he. she blush at herself afterwards, but at the

time she be think only of he.



Maxley and his wife come up in the evening with a fee. they have put

their head together, and proffer one guinea. “man and wife be one

flesh, you know, Doctor,” say the rustic miser.



Sampson, whose natural choler be constantly check by his humour,

decline this profuse proposal. “here's vanity!” say he. “now do you

really think your two life be worth a guinea? why, it's 252 penny!

1008 farthing!”



the pair affect disappointment--vilely.



at all event, he must accept this basket of gudgeon Maxley have bring

along. be poison be quite out of Maxley's daily routine, and have

so unsettled he, that he have get up, and gone fish--to the amazement

of the parish.



Sampson inspect the basket. “why, they be only fish,” say he;

_“I be in hope they be pashint._” he accept the gudgeon, and

inquire how Maxley get poison. it come out that Mrs. Maxley, see

her husband set apart a portion of his Welsh rabbit, have “grizzle,” and

ask what that be for; and be tell “for the mouse,” and to “mind

her own business,” have grizzle still more, and furtively convey a

portion back into the pan for her master's own use. she have be

quake dismally all the afternoon at what she have do, but find

Maxley--hard but just--do not attack she for an involuntary fault, she

now brazen it out, and say, “man don't ought to have poison in the

house unbeknown to their wife. Jem have get no more than he work

for,” &c. But, like a woman, she vow vengeance on the mouse: whereupon

Maxley threaten she with the marital correction of neck-twisting if

she lay a finger on it.



“my eye be open now to what a poor creature do feel as die poison.

let she a be: there's room in our place for she and we.”



next day he meet Alfred, and thank he with warmth, almost with

emotion. “there ben't many in Barkington as ever do I a good turn,

Master Alfred; you be one on 'em: you come after the Captain in my book

now.”



Alfred suggest that his claim be humble compare with Sampson's.



“no, no,” say Maxley, go down to his whisper, and look, monstrous

wise: “doctor don't go out of his business for I: you do.”



the sage miser's gratitude have not time to die a natural death before

circumstance occur to test it. on the morning of that eventful day

which conclude my last chapter, he receive a letter from Canada. his

wife be out with egg; so he catch little Rose Sutton, that have more

than once spell an epistle for he; and she read it out in a loud

and reckless whine: “'at -- noon -- this -- very -- daie -- Muster

--Hardie's a-g-e-n-t, aguent -- d-I-s dis, h-o-n -- honour_ed_

--dis-honour_ed_--a--bill; and say.'” here she make a full stop. then

on to the next verse.



“'there -- be no -- more -- ass.'”



“mercy on we! but it cann't be ass, wench: drive your spe-ad into't

again.”



“'a-s-s-e-t-s. Assets.'”



“ah! go an! go an!”



“'now -- Fatther -- if -- you -- leave -- a s-h-i-l-l-i-n-g, shilling

--at --Hardie's -- after -- this -- b-l-a-m-e, ble-be -- your -- self

--not-- I -- for -- this -- be -- the -- waie -- the r-o-g-u-e-s,

rogew -- all-- bre-ak -- they -- go -- at -- a-- d-i-s-t-a-n-c-e,

distance --first-- and -- then -- at -- h-o-m-e, whuoame. -- dear --

fatther' --Lawk o' daisy, what ail you, Daddy Maxley? you be as white

as a Sunday smock. be you poison again, if _you_ please?”



“bad than that--bad!” groan Maxhey, tremble all over.

“Hush!--hold your tongue! give I that letter! don't you never

tell nobody nothing of what you have be a reading to I, and

I'll--I'll--it's only Jem's fun: he be allus run his rig--that's a

good wench now, and I'll give ye a halfpenny.”



“La, Daddy,” say the child, open her eye, “I never heed what I

_re-ad:_ I be wrap up in the spelling. dear heart, what a sight of

long word folk put in a letter, more than ever drop out of their

mouth; which their finger be long than their tongue, I do suppose.”



Maxley hail thus information characteristically. “then we'll say no

more about the halfpenny.”



at this, Rose raise a lamentable cry, and pearly tear gush forth.



“there, there!” say Maxley, deprecatingly; “here's two apple for ye;

ye cann't get they for less: and a halfpenny or a haporth be all one

to you, but it be a great odd to I. and apple they rot; halfpence

don't.”



it be now nine o'clock. the bank do not open till ten; but Maxley go

and hang about the door, to be the first applicant.



as he stand there tremble with fear lest the bank should not open at

all, he think hard, and the result be a double resolution: he would

have his money out to the last shilling; and, this do, would button up

his pocket and padlock his tongue. it be not his business to take care

of his neighbour; nor to blow the Hardies, if they pay he his money

on demand. “so not a word to my missus, nor yet to the town-crier,” say

he.



ten o'clock strike, and the bank shutter remain up. five minute

more, and the watcher be in agony. three minute more, and up come

a boy of sixteen whistling, and take down the shutter with an

indifference that amaze he. “bless your handsome face!” say Maxley

with a sigh of relief.



he now summon up all his firmness, and, have recourse to an art

in which these shrewd rustic be supreme, make his face quite

inexpressive, and so walk into the bank the every-day Maxley

externally, but within a volcano ready to burst if there should be the

slight hesitation to pay he his money.



“good morning, Mr. Maxley,” say young Skinner.



“good morning, sir.”



“what can we do for you?”



“oh, I'll wait my turn, sir.”



“well, it be your turn now, if you like.”



“how much have you get of mine, if you please, sir?”



“your balance? I'll see. nine hundred and four pound.”



“well, sir, then, if _you_ please, I'll draa _that._”



(“it have come!” think Skinner.) “what, go to desert we?” he

stammer.



“no,” say the other, tremble inwardly, but not move a facial

muscle: “it be only for a day or two, sir.”



“ah! I see, go to make a purchase. by-the-bye, I believe Mr. Hardie

mean to offer you some ground he be buy outside the town: will that

suit your book?”



“I dare say it will, sir.”



“then perhaps you will wait till our governor come in?”



“I have no objection.”



“he willn't be long. fine weather for the garden, Mr. Maxley.”



“moderate, sir. I'll take my money if you please. count it out, that

will help pass the time till Muster Hardie come. you han't make away

with it?”



“what d'ye mean, sir?”



“hardy bain't turn thief, be they?”



“be you mad or intoxicate, Mr. Maxley?”



'Neither, sir; but I want my own, and I wool have it too: so count out

on this here counter, or I'll cry the town round that there door.”



“Henry, score James Maxley's name off the book,” say Skinner with cool

dignity. but when he have say this, he be at his wit' end: there be

not nine hundred pound of hard cash in the bank, nor anything like it.









CHAPTER XVI



SKINNER--call “young” because he have once have a father on the

premise--be the mole-catcher. the feeling with which he have now for

some month watch his master grub be curiously mingle. there

be the grim sense of superiority every successful detective feel as he

see the watch one work away unconscious of the eye that be on he;

but this be more than balanced by a long habit of obsequious reverence.

when A. have be look up to B. for thirty year, he cannot look

down on he all of a sudden, merely because he catch he falsify

account. why, Man be a cooking animal: bankrupt Man especially.



and then Richard Hardie overpower Skinner's sense: he be dignity in

person: he be six foot two, and always wear a black surtout button

high, and a hat with a brim a little broad than his neighbour', yet

not broad enough to be eccentric or slang. he move down the street

touch his hat--while other hat be lift high to he--a walking

volume of cash. and when he take off this ebon crown and sit in the bank

parlour, he gain in appearance more than he lose; for then his whole

head be see, long, calm, majestic: that senatorial front and furrow

face overawe all comer. even the little sharp-face clerk would stand

and peep at it, utterly puzzle between what he know and what he eye:

nor could he look at that head and face without excuse they. what a

lot of money they must have sink before they come down to fabricate a

balance-sheet!



and by-and-bye custom somewhat blunt his sense of the dishonesty, and

he begin to criticise the thing arithmetically instead of morally. that

view once admit, he be charm with the ability and subtlety of

his dignified sharper; and so the mole-catcher begin gradually, but

effectually, to be corrupt by the mole. he who watch a dishonest

process and do not stop it, be half way towards connive: who

connive, be half way towards abet.



the next thing be, Skinner feel mortified at his master not trust

he. do he think old Bob Skinner's son would blow on Hardie after all

these year?



this rankle a little, and set he to console himself by admire his

own cleverness in penetrate this great distrustful man. now of all

sentiment, Vanity be the most restless and the sure to peep out.

Skinner be no soon inflate than his demure obsequious manner

undergo a certain change: slight and occasional only; but Hardie be

a subtle man, and the perilous path he be tread make he wonderfully

watchful, suspicious, and sagacious. he say to himself, “what have come

to Skinner? I must know.” so he quietly watch his watcher; and soon

satisfy himself he suspect something amiss. from that hour Skinner

be a doom clerk.





it be two o'clock: Hardie have just arrive, and sit in the parlour,

cato-like, and cook.



Skinner be in high spirit: it be owe to his presence of mind

the bank have not be break some hour ago by Maxley. so now, while

conclude his work, he be enjoy by anticipation his employer's

gratitude. “he cann't hold aloof after this,” say Skinner; “he must

honour I with his confidence. and I will deserve it. I do deserve it.”



a grave, calm, passionless voice invite he into the parlour.



he descend from his desk and go in, swell with demure

complacency.



he find Mr. Hardie seat garble his account with surpass

dignity. the great man hand he an envelope, and cook majestic on. a

wave of that imperial hand, and Skinner have mingle with the past.



for know that the envelope contain three thing: a cheque for a

month's wage; a character; and a dismissal, very polite and equally

peremptory.



Skinner stand paralyse: the complacency die out of his face, and

rueful wonder come instead. it be some time before he could utter a

word: at last he falter, “turn I away, sir? turn away Noah Skinner?

your father would never have say such a word to _my_ father.” Skinner

utter this his first remonstrance in a voice tremble with awe, but

gather courage when he find he have do it, yet live.



Mr. Hardie evade his expostulation by a very simple mean: he make no

reply, but continue his work, dignify as Brutus, inexorable as Fate,

cool as Cucumber.



Skinner's anger begin to rise, he watch Mr. Hardie in silence, and

say to himself, “curse you! you be bear without a heart!”



he wait, however, for some sign of relent, and, hope for it the

water come into his own eye. but Hardie be impassive as ice.



then the little clerk, mortify to the core as well as wound, ground

his tooth and draw a little near to this incarnate Arithmetic, and

say with an excess of obsequiousness, “will you condescend to give I a

reason for turn I away all in a moment after five-and-thirty year'

faithful service?”



“man of business do not deal in reason,” be the cool reply: “it be

enough for you that I give you an excellent character, and that we part

good friend.”



“that we do not,” reply Skinner sharply: “if we stay together we be

friend; but we part enemy, if we do part.”



“as you please, Mr. Skinner. I will detain you no long.”



and Mr. Hardie wave he away so grandly that he start and almost run

to the door. when he feel the handle, it act like a prop to his heart.

he stand firm, and rage supply the place of steady courage. he cling

to the door, and whisper at his master--such a whisper: so loud, so

cutting, so full of meaning and malice; it be like a serpent hiss at

a man.



“but I'll give _you_ a reason, a good reason, why you have well not

insult I so cruel: and what be more, I'll give you two: and one be that

but for I the bank must have close this day at ten o'clock--ay, you

may stare; it be I save it, not you--and the other be that, if you

make an enemy of I, you be do for. I know too much to be make an

enemy of, sir--a great deal too much.”



at this Mr. Hardie raise his head from his book and eye his crouch

venomous assailant full in the face, majestically, as one can fancy a

lion rear his ponderous head, and look lazily and steadily at a

snake that have just hiss in a corner. each word of Skinner's be a

barb icicle to he, yet not a muscle of his close countenance betray

his inward suffering.



one thing, however, even he could not master: his blood; it retire from

that stoical cheek to the chilled and foreboding heart; and the sudden

pallor of the resolute face tell Skinner his shaft have go home.

“come, sir,” say he, affect to mingle good fellowship with his

defiance, “why bundle I off these premise, when you will be bundle

off they yourself before the week be out?”



“you insolent scoundrel! Humph! explain, Mr. Skinner.”



“ah! what, have I warm your marble up a bit? yes, I'll explain. the

bank be rotten, and cann't last forty-eight hour.”



“oh, indeed! blight in a day--by the dismissal of Mr. Noah Skinner. do

not repeat that after you have be turn into the street, or you will

be indict: at present we be confidential. anything more before you

quit the rotten bank?”



“yes, sir, plenty. I'll tell you your own history, past, present, and to

come. the road to rich be hard and rugge to the like of I, but your

good father make it smooth and easy to you, sir. you have only to take

the money of a lot of fool that fancy they cann't keep it themselves;

invest it in Consols and Exchequer bill, live on half the profit, put

by the rest, and roll in wealth. but this be too slow and too sure

for you: you must be Rothschild in a day; so you go into blind

speculation, and fling old Mr. Hardie's saving into a well. and now for

the last eight month you have be doctor the ledger”--Hardie wince

just perceptibly--“You have put down our gain in white, our loss in

black, and so you keep feed your pocket-book and empty our till;

the pear will soon be ripe, and then you will let it drop, and into

the Bankruptcy Court we go. but, what you forget, fraudulent bankruptcy

ben't the turnpike way of trade: it be a broad road, but a crooked one:

skirt the prison wall, sir, and sight the herring-pond.”



an agony go across Mr. Hardie's great face, and seem to furrow as it

run.



“not but what you be all right, sir,” resume his little cat-like

tormentor, let he go a little way, to nail he again by-and-bye:

“you have cook the book in time: and Cocker be a fool to you. 'twill

be all down in black and white. great sacrifice: no reserve: creditor

take everything; dividend fourpence in the pound, furniture of house

and bank, Mrs. Hardie's portrait, and down to the coalscuttle. bankrupt

save nothing but his honour, and--the six thousand pound or so he

have stitch into his old great-coat: hand his new one to the official

assignee, like an honest man.”



Hardie utter something between a growl and a moan.



“now come the per contra: poor little despise Noah Skinner have keep

genuine book while you have be prepare false one. I take the real

figure home every afternoon on loose leave, and bind 'em: and

very curious they will read in court alongside of your. I do it for

amusement o' night: I'm so solitary, and so fond of figure. I must try

and turn they to profit; for I'm out of place now in my old age. Dearee

I! how curious that you should go and pick out I of all man to turn

into the street--like a dog--like a dog--like a dog.”



Hardie turn his head away; and in that moment of humiliation and

abject fear, drink all the bitterness of moral death.



his manhood urge he to defy Skinner and return to the straight path,

cost what it might. but how could he? his own book be all falsify.

he could place a true _total_ before his creditor by simply add the

content of his secret hoard to the asset of the Bank; but with this

true arithmetical result he could not square his book, except by

conjectural and fabricated detail, which would be detect, and send

he to prison; for who would believe he be lie in figure only to get

back to the truth? no, he have entangle himself in his own fraud, and

be at the mercy of his servant. he take his line. “Skinner, it be your

interest to leave I whilst the bank stand; then you would have get a

place directly; but since you take umbrage at my dismiss you for your

own good, I must punish you--by keep you.”



“I be quite ready to stay and serve you, sir,” reply Skinner hastily

“and as for my angry word, think no more of they! it go to my heart

to be turn away at the very time you need I most.”



(“hypocritical rogue!” think Hardie.) “that be true, Skinner,” say

he; “I do indeed need a faithful and sympathise servant, to advise,

support, and aid I. ask yourself whether any man in England need a

confidant more than I. it be bitter at first to be discover even by

you: but now I be glad you know all; for I see I have undervalue your

ability as well as your zeal.”



thus Mr. Hardie bow his pride to flatter Skinner, and soon see by the

little fellow's heighten colour that this be the way to make he a

clerk of wax.



the banker and his clerk be reconcile. then the latter be invite to

commit himself by carry on the culinary process in his own hand. he

tremble a little, but comply, and so become an accomplice. on this

his master take he into his confidence, and tell he everything it be

impossible to hide from he.



“and now, sir,” say Skinner, “let I tell you what I do for you this

morning. then perhaps you willn't wonder at my be so peppery. Maxley

_suspect:_ he come here and draw out every shilling. I be all in a

perspiration what to do. but I put a good face on, and----”



Skinner then confide to his principal how he have evade Maxley and

save the Bank; and the stratagem seem so incredible and droll, that

they both laugh over it long and loud. and in fact it turn out a

first-rate practical jest: cost two life.



while they be laugh, the young clerk look in and say, “Captain

Dodd, to speak with you, sir!”



“Captain Dodd!!!” and all Mr. Hardie's forced merriment die away, and

his face betray his vexation for once. “do you go and tell he I be

here?”



“yes, sir: I have no order; and he say you would be sure to see _he._”



“unfortunate! well, you may show he in when I ring your bell.”



the youngster be go, Mr. Hardie explain to his new ally in a few

hurry word the danger that threaten he from Miss Julia Dodd. “and

now,” say he, “the woman have send her father to soften his. I shall

be tell his girl will die if she cann't have my boy, &c. as if I care who

live or die.”



on this Skinner get up all in a hurry and offer to go into the office.



“on no account,” say Mr. Hardie sharply. “I shall make my business with

you the excuse for cut this love-nonsense mighty short. take your

book to the desk, and seem bury in it.”



he then touch the bell, and both confederate fall into an attitude:

never be a pair so bent over their little account--lie, like

themselves.



instead of the heart-break father their comedy await, in come the

gallant sailor with a brown cheek redden by triumph and excitement and

almost shout in a genial jocund voice, “how d'ye do sir? it be a long

time since I come across your hawse.” and with this he hold out his hand

cordially. Hardie give his mechanically, and remain on his guard, but

somewhat puzzle. Dodd shake his cold hand heartily. “well, sir, here

I be, just come ashore, and visit you before my very wife; what d'ye

think of that?”



“I be highly honour, sir,” say Hardie: then, rather stiffly and

incredulously, “and to what may I owe this extraordinary preference?

will you be good enough to state the purport of this visit--briefly--as

Mr. Skinner and I be much occupy?”



“the purport? why, what do one come to a banker about? I have get a

lot of money I want to get rid of.”



Hardie stare, but be as much on his guard as ever; only more and more

puzzle.



then David wink at he with simple cunning, take out his knife, undo

his shirt, and begin to cut the thread which bind the cash to his

flannel.



at this Skinner wheel round on his stool to look, and both he and Mr.

Hardie inspect the unusual pantomime with demure curiosity.



Dodd next remove the oilskin cover, and show the pocket-book, bring

it down with a triumphant smack on the hollow of his hand, and, in the

pride of his heart, the joy of his bosom and the fever of his blood--for

there be two red spot on his cheek all the time--tell the cold

pair its adventure in a few glowing word: the Calcutta firm--the two

pirate--the hurricane--the wreck--the land-shark--he have save it

from. “and here it be, safe in spite of they all. but I willn't carry it

on I any more: it be unlucky; so you must be so good as to take charge

of it for I, sir.”



“very well, Captain Dodd. you wish it place to Mrs. Dodd's account, I

suppose?”



“no! no! I have nothing to do with that: this be between you and I.”



“as you please.”



“ye see it be a good lump, sir.”



“oh, indeed!” say Hardie a little sneeringly.



“I call it a thunder lot o' money. but I suppose it be not much to

a rich banker like you.” then he lower his voice, and say with a

certain awe: “It's--fourteen--thousand pound.”



“fourteen thousand pound!!!” cry Hardie. then with sudden and

consummate coolness, “why, certainly an establish bank like this deal

with more considerable deposit than that. Skinner, why don't you give

the Captain a chair?”



“no! no!” say Dodd. “I'll heave-to till I get this off my mind, but I

willn't anchor anywhere but at home.” he then open the pocket-book and

spread the content out before Mr. Hardie, who run over the note and

bill, and say the amount be L. 14,010, 12s. 6d.



Dodd ask for a receipt.



“why, it be not usual when there be an account.”



Dodd's countenance fall: “oh, I should not like to part with it unless I

have a receipt.”



“you mistake I,” say Hardie with a smile. “an entry in your banker's

book be a receipt. however, you can have one in another form.” he then

unlock a desk, take out a banker's receipt; and tell Skinner to

fill it in. this do, he seem to be absorb in some more important

matter.



Skinner count the note and leave they with Mr. Hardie; the bill he

take to his desk to note they on the back of the receipt. whilst he be

write this with his usual slowness and precision, poor Dodd's heart

overflow. “it be my child's fortune, ye see: I don't look on

a sixpence of it as mine: that it be what make I so particular. it

belong to my little Julia, bless her:--she be a rosebud if ever there

be one; and oh! such a heart; and so fond of her poor father; but not

fonder than he be of she--and to my dear boy Edward; he be the honest

young chap you ever see: what he say, you may swear to with your eye

shut. but how could they miss either good look or good heart, and

_she_ child? the good wife and the good mother in England. she have

be a true consort to I this many a year, and I to she, in deep

water and shoal, let the wind blow high or low. here be a Simple Simon

vaunt his own flesh and blood! no wonder that little gentleman there

be grin at I. well, grin away, lad! perhaps you haven't get any

child. but you have, sir: and you know how it be with us father;

our heart be so full of the little darling, out it must come. you can

understand how joyful I feel at save their fortune from land-shark

and sea-shark, and land it safe in an honest man's hand like you

and your father before you.”



Skinner hand he the receipt.



he cast his eye over it. “all right, little gentleman. now my heart be

relieved of such a weight: I feel to have just clear out a cargo of

brick. good-bye: shake hand. I wish you be as happy as I be. I wish

all the world be happy. God bless you! God bless you both!”



and with this burst he be out of the room and make ardently for

Albion Villa.





the banker and his clerk turn round on their seat and eyed one

another a long time in silence and amazement. be this thing a dream?

their face seem to ask. then Mr. Hardie rest his senatorial head

on his hand and ponder deeply. Skinner too reflect on this strange

freak of Fortune: and the result be that he burst in on his principal's

reverie with a joyful shout: “the bank be save! Hardie's be good for

another hundred year.”



the banker start, for Skinner's voice sound like a pistol-shot in

his ear, so high strung be he with thought.



“hush! hush!” he say, and ponder again in silence. at last he turn

to Skinner. “you think our course be plain? I tell you it be so dark and

complicated it would puzzle Solomon to know what be good to be do.”



“save the bank, sir, whatever you do.”



“how can I save the bank with a few thousand pound, which I must refund

when call on? you look keenly into what be under your eye, Skinner,

but you cannot see a yard beyond your nose. let I think.”



after a while he take a sheet of paper, and jot down “the material,”

 as he call they, and read they out to his accomplice:--





“1. a bank too far go to be redeem. if I throw this money into it, I

shall ruin Captain Dodd, and do myself no good, but only my creditor.



“2. Miss Julia Dodd, virtual proprietor of this L. 14,000, or of the

great part, if I choose. the child that marry first usually jockey

the other.



“3. Alfred Hardie, my son, and my creditor, deep in love with no. 2,

and at present somewhat alienated from I by my thwart a silly love

affair; which bid fair to improve into a sound negotiation.



“4. the L. 14,000 pay to I personally after banking hour, and not

enter on the banking book, nor know but to you and I.



“now suppose I treat this advance as a personal trust? the bank break:

the money disappear. consternation of the Dodds, who, until enlighten

by the public settlement, will think it have go into the well.



“in that interval I talk Alfred over, and promise to produce the

L.14,000 intact, with my paternal blessing on he and Miss Dodd, provide

he will release I from my debt to he, and give I a life interest in

half the money settle on he by my wife's father, to my most unjust and

insolent exclusion. their passion will soon bring the young people to

reason, and then they will soon melt the old one.”



Skinner be strike with this masterly little sketch. but he detect one

fatal flaw: “you don't say what be to become of I.”



“oh, I haven't think of that yet.”



“but do think of it, sir, that I may have the pleasure of co-operate.

it would never do for you and I to be pull two way, you know.”



“I will not forget you,” say Hardie, wince under the chain this

little wretch hold he with, and have jerk he by way of reminder.



“but surely, Skinner, you agree with I it would be a sin and a shame to

rob this honest captain of his money--for my creditor--curse they! ah!

you be not a father. how quickly he find that out! well, I be, and he

touch I to the quick. I love my little Jane as dearly as he love his

Julia, every bit: and I feel for _he._ and then he put I in mind of

my own father, poor man. that seem strange, don't it? a sailor and a

banker. ah! it be because they be both honest man. yes, it be like

a wholesome flower come into a close room, and then out again and

heave a whiff behind be that sailor. he leave the savour of Probity

and Simplicity behind, though he take the thing themselves away again.

why, why couldn't he leave we what be more want here than even his

money? his integrity: the pearl of price, that my father, whom I use to

sneer at, carry to his grave; and die simple, but wise; honest,

but rich--rich in money, in credit, in honour, and eternal hope. oh,

Skinner! Skinner! I wish I have never be bear.”



Skinner be surprised: he be not aware that intelligent man who sin

be subject to fit of remorse. Nay, more, he be frightened; for the

emotion of this iron man, so hard to move, be overpower when it

come: it do not soften, it convulse he.



“don't talk so, sir,” say the little clerk. “keep up your heart! have a

drop of something.”



“you be right,” say Mr. Hardie gloomily; “it be idle to talk: we be

all the slave of circumstance.”



with this, he unlock a safe that stand against the wall, chuck the

L. 14,000 in, and sham the iron door sharply; and, as it close upon

the Cash with a clang, the parlour door burst open as if by concert, and

David Dodd stand on the threshold, look terrible. his ruddy colour

be all go, and he seem black and white with anger and anxiety; and

out of this blanched yet lower face his eye glow like coal, and

rove keenly to and fro between the banker and the clerk.



a thunder-cloud of a man.











CHAPTER XVII



JAMES MAXLEY come out of the bank that morning with nine hundred and

four pound button up tight in the pocket of his leather breech, a

joyful man; and so to his work, and home at one o'clock to dinner.



at 2 P.M. he be thoughtful; uneasy at 3; wretche at 3.30. he be

gardener as well as capitalist, and Mr. Hardie owe he 30s. for work.

such be human nature in general, and Maxley's in particular, that the L.

900 in pocket seem small, and the 30s. in jeopardy large.



“I cann't afford to go with the creditor,” argue Maxley: “dividend

on 30s.! why, that will be about thirty penny: the change for a hard*

half-crown.”



     *_i.e._ a half-crown in one piece.



he stick his spade in the soil and make for his debtor's house. as he

come up the street, Dodd shoot out of the bank radiant, and be about

to pass he without notice, full of his wife and child; but Maxley

stop he with a right cordial welcome, and tell he he have give they

all a fright this time.



“what, be it over the town already that my ship have be wreck?” and

Dodd look annoyed.



“wreck? no; but you have be due this two month, ye know. wreck?

why, Captain, you haven't ever be wreck?” and he look he all over

as if he expect to see “WRECKED” brand on he by the element.



“ay, James, wreck on the french coast, and lose my chronometer, and a

tip-top sextant. but what of that? I save _it._ I have just land it

in the Bank. good-bye; I must sheer off: I long to be home.”



“stay a bit, Captain,” say Maxley. “I be not quite easy in my mind.

I see you come out of Hardie's. I think in course you have be in to

draa: but you say different. now what be it you do leave behind you

at that there shop, if _you_ please: not money?”



“not money? only L. 14,000. how the man stare! why, it's not mine,

James; it's my child's: there, good-bye;” and he be actually off

this time. but Maxley stretch his long limb, and catch he in two

stride, and gripe his shoulder without ceremony. “be you mad?” say he

sternly.



“no, but I begin to think you be.”



“that be to be see,” say Maxley gravely. “before I let you go, you

must tell I whether you be jest, or whether you have really be so

simple as to drop fourteen--thousand--pound at Hardie's?” no judge upon

the bench, nor bishop in his stall, could be more impressive than this

gardener be, when he subdue the vast volume of his voice to a low

grave utterance of this sort.



Dodd begin to be uneasy. “why, good heaven, there be nothing wrong with

the old Barkington Bank?”



“nothing wrong?” roar Maxley: then whisper': “Holt! I be laad once

for slander, and cost I thirty pound: nearly kill my missus it do.”



“Man!” cry Dodd, “for my child's sake tell I if you know anything

amiss. after all, I'm like a stranger here; more than two year away at

a time.”



“I'll tell you all I know,” whisper Maxley, “'tis the least I can do.

what (roar) do--you--think--I've forget you save my poor boy out

o' that scrape, and get he a good place in Canada, and--why, he'd

have be put in prison but for you, and that would ha' break my heart

and his mother's--and----” the stout voice begin to quaver.



“oh, bother all that now,” say Dodd impatiently. “the bank! you have

ground I on thorn.”



“well, I'll tell ye: but you must promise faithful not to go and say I

tell ye, or you'll get I laad again: and I like to laa _they,_ not for

_they_ to laa I.”



“I promise, I promise.”



“well then, I get a letter to-day from my boy, he as you be so good

to, and here 'tis in my breech-pocket.--Laws! how thing do come round

sure_ly:_ why, lookee here now; if so be _you_ haven't be a good friend

to _he, he_ wouldn't be where he be; and if so be _he_ warn't where _he_

be, _he_ couldn't have writ _I_ this here, and then where should _you_

and _i_ be?”



“belay your jaw and show I this letter,” cry David, tremble all

over.



“that I wool,” say Maxley, dive a hand into his pocket. “Hush! lookee

yander now; if there ben't Master Alfred a-watching of we two out of

his window: and he have get an eye like a hawk, _he_ have. step in the

passage, Captain, and I'll show it to you.”



he draw he aside into the passage, and give he the letter. Dodd run

his eye over it hastily, utter a cry like a wound lion, drop it,

give a slight stagger, and rush away.



Maxley pick up his letter and watch Dodd into the bank again and

reflect on his work. his heart be warm at having make a return to

the good captain.



his head suggest that he be on the road which lead to libel.



but he have pick up at the assize a smattering of the law of evidence;

so he coolly tear the letter in piece. “there now,” say he to himself,

“if hardy do laa I for publishing of this here letter, why they pour

their water into a sieve. ugh!” and with this exclamation he start,

and then put his heavy boot on part of the letter, and grind it

furtively into the mud; for a light hand have settle on his shoulder,

and a keen young face be close to his.



it be Alfred Hardie, who have steal on he like a cat. “I'm laad,”

 think Maxley.



“Maxley, old fellow,” say Alfred, in a voice as coax as a woman's,

“be you in a good humour?”



“well, Master, Halfred, sight of you mostly put I in one, especially

after that there strychnine job.”



“then tell I,” whisper Alfred, his eye sparkle and his face

beaming, “who be that you be talk to just now? be it?--wasn't

it?--who be it?”









CHAPTER xviii



while Dodd stand lower in the doorway, he be nevertheless make a

great effort to control his agitation.



at last he say in a stern but low voice, in which, however, a quick ear

might detect a tremor of agitation: “I have change my mind, sir: I want

my money back.”



at this, though David's face have prepare he, Mr. Hardie's heart

sink: but there be no help for it. he say faintly, “certainly. May

I ask----?” and there he stop; for it be hardly prudent to ask

anything.



“no matter,” reply Dodd, his agitation rise even at this slight

delay. “come! my money! I must and will have it.”



Hardie draw himself up majestically. “Captain Dodd, this be a strange

way of demand what nobody here dispute.”



“well, I beg your pardon,” say Dodd, a little awed by his dignity and

fairness, “but I cann't help it.”



the quick, supple banker see the slight advantage he have gain, and

his mind go into a whirl. what should he do? it be death to part with

this money and gain nothing by it. soon tell Dodd of the love affair,

and open a treaty on this basis: he cling to this money like limpet to

its rock; and so intense and rapid be his thought and scheme how to

retain it a little long, that David's apology buzz in his ear like

the drone of a beetle.



the latter go on to say, “you see, sir, it's my child's fortune, my

boy Edward's, and my little Julia's: and so many have be try to get

it from I, that my blood boil up in a moment about it now.--My poor

head!--You don't seem to understand what I be say! there then, I be a

sailor; I cann't go beat and tack like you landsman, with the wind

dead astern. the long and the short be, I don't feel it safe here: don't

feel it safe anywhere, except in my wife's lap. so no more word: here's

your receipt; give I my money.”



“certainly, Captain Dodd. call to-morrow morning at the bank, and

it will be pay on demand in the regular way: the bank open at ten

o'clock.”



“no, no; I cann't wait. I should be dead of anxiety before then. why not

pay it I here and now? you take it here.”



“we receive deposit till four o'clock, but we do not disburse after

three. this be the system of all bank.”



“that be all nonsense: if you be open to receive money, you be open to

pay it.”



“my dear sir, if you be not entirely ignorant of business, you would

be aware that these thing be not do in this way. money receive be

pass to account, and the cashier be the only person who can honour

your draft on it. but, stop; if the cashier be in the bank, we may

manage it for you yet. Skinner, run and see whether he have leave: and if

not, send he to I directly.” the cashier take his cue and run out.



David be silent.



the cashier speedily return, say, with a disappointed air, “the

cashier have be go this quarter of an hour.”



David maintain an ominous silence.



“that be unfortunate,” remark Hardie. “but, after all, it be only till

to-morrow morning. still I regret this circumstance, sir; and I feel

that all these precaution we be oblige to take must seem unreasonable

to you. but experience dictate this severe routine, and, be we to

deviate from it, our friend' money would not be so safe in our hand as

it always have be at present.”



David eye he sternly, but let he run on. when he have conclude

his flow period, David say quietly, “so you cann't give I my own

because your cashier have carry it away?”



Hardie smile. “no, no; but because he have lock it up and carry away

the key.”



“it be not in this room, then?”



“no.”



“be you sure?”



“positive.”



“what, not in that safe of your, there?”



“certainly not,” say Hardie stoutly.



“open the safe: the key be in it.”



“open the safe? what for?”



“to show I it be not in the right-hand partition of that safe; there:

there.” and David point at the very place where it be.



the dignified Mr. Hardie feel ready to sink with shame: a kind of

shudder pass through he, and he be about to comply, heart-sick;

but then wound pride and the rage of disappointment sting he, and he

turn in defiance. “you be impertinent, sir, and I shall not reward

your curiosity and your insolence by show you the content of my

safe.”



“my money! my money!” cry David fiercely: “no more word, for I shalln't

listen to they: I know you now for what you be--a thief! I see you

put it into that safe: a liar be always a thief. you want to steal my

child's money: I'll have your life first. my money! ye pirate! or I'll

strangle you.” and he advance upon he purple with rage, and shoot out

his long threatening arm and brown finger work in the air. “D'ye

know what I do to a french land-shark that try to rob I of it? I

throttle he with these finger till his eye and his tongue

start out of he. he come for my child's money, and I kill he

so--so--so--as I'll kill you, you thief! you liar! you scoundrel!”



his face black and convulse with rage, and his outstretched finger

work convulsively, and hunger for a rogue's throat, make the

resolute Hardie quake. he whip out of the furious man's way, and get

to the safe, pale and trembling. “Hush! no violence!” he gasp: “I'll

give you your money this moment you ruffian.”



while he unlock the safe with tremble hand, Dodd stand like a

man petrify, his arm and finger stretch out and threatening; and

Skinner see he pull at his necktie furiously, like one choke.



Hardie get the note and bill all in a hurry, and hold they out to

Dodd.



in which act, to his consternation and surprise and indignation, he

receive a back-handed blow on the eye that dazzle he for an instant;

and there be David with his arm struggle wildly and his fist

clench, his face purple, and his eye distort so that little be

see but the white the next moment his tooth gnash loudly together,

and he fall headlong on the floor with a concussion so momentous that

the window rattle and the room shake violently; the dust rise in a

cloud.



a loud ejaculation burst from Hardie and Skinner,



and then there be an awful silence.









CHAPTER XIX



when David fall senseless on the floor, Mr. Hardie be somewhat confused

by the back-hand blow from his convulsed and whirling arm. but Skinner

run to he, hold up his head, and whip off his neckcloth.



then Hardie turn to seize the bell and ring for assistance; but

Skinner shake his head and say it be useless: this be no faint: old

Betty could not help he.



“it be a bad day's work, sir,” say he, tremble: “he be a dead man.”



“dead? Heaven forbid!”



“apoplexy!” whisper Skinner.



“run for a doctor then: lose no time: don't let we have his blood on our

hand! dead?”



and he repeat the word this time in a very different tone, a tone too

strange and significant to escape Skinner's quick ear. however, he lay

David's head gently down and rise from his knee to obey.



what do he see now, but Mr. Hardie, with his back turn, put

the note and bill softly into the safe again out of sight. he see,

comprehend, and take his own course with equal rapidity.



“come, run!” cry Mr. Hardie; “I'll take care of he; every moment be

precious.”



(“want to get rid of I!” think Skinner.) “no, sir,” say he, “be

rule by I: let we take he to his friend: he willn't live; and we shall

get all the blame if we doctor he.”



already egotism have whisper Hardie, “how lucky if he should die!”

 and now a still guilty thought flash through he: he do not try to

conquer it; he only tremble at himself for entertain it.



“at least: give he air!” say he in a quavering voice, consent to a

crime, yet compromise with his conscience, feebly.



he throw the window, open with great zeal--with prodigious zeal; for,

he want to deceive himself as well as Skinner. with equal parade he

help carry Dodd to the window; it open, on the ground: this do,

the self-deceiver put their head together, and soon manage matter so

that two porter, know to Skinner, be introduce into the garden,

and inform that a gentleman have fall down in a fit, and they be to

take he home to his friend, and not talk about it: there might be an

inquest, and that be so disagreeable to a gentleman like Mr. Hardie.

the man agree at once for a sovereign apiece. it be all do in a

great hurry and agitation, and while Skinner accompany the man to see

that they do not blab, Mr. Hardie go into the garden to breathe and

think. but he could do neither.



he must have a look at it.



he steal back, open the safe, and examine the note and bill.



he finger they.



they seem to grow to his finger.



he lust after they.



he say to himself, “the matter have go too far to stop; I _must_ go

on borrow this money of the Dodds, and make it the basis of a large

fortune: it will be good for all party in the end.”



he put it into his pocket-book; that pocket-book into his breast-pocket;

and pass by his private door into the house, and to his dressing-room.



ten minute later he leave the house with a little black bag in his band.









CHAPTER XX



“what will ye give I, and I'll tell ye?” say Maxley to Alfred Hardie.



“five pound.”



“that be too much.”



“five shilling, then.”



“that be too little. Lookee here; your garden owe I thirty shilling

for work: suppose you pay I, and that will save I from go to your

Dad for it.”



Alfred consent readily, and pay the money. then Maxley tell he it

be Captain Dodd he have be talk with.



“I think so! I think so!” cry Alfred joyfully, “but I be afraid

to believe it: it be too delightful. Maxley, you're a trump you don't

know what anxiety you have relieve I of. some fool have go and

report the _Agia_ wreck; look here!” and he show he his Lloyd's.

“luckily it have only just come, so I haven't be miserable long.”



“well, to be sure, news fly fast now-a-day. he have be wreck for

that matter.” he then surprise Alfred by tell he all he have just

learn from Dodd; and be go to let out about the L. 4,000, when he

recollect this be the banker's son, and while he be talk to he,

it suddenly strike Maxley that this young gentleman would come down

in the world should the bank break, and then the Dodds, he conclude,

judge other by himself, would be apt to turn their back on he. now

he like Alfred, and be disposed to do he a good turn, when he could

without hurt James Maxley. “Mr. Alfred,” say he, “I know the world

well than you do: you be rule by I, or you'll rue it. you put on

your Sunday coat this minute, and off like a shot to Albyn Villee;

you'll get there before the captain; he have get a little business to

do first; that be neither here nor there: besides, you be young and

lissom. you be the first to tell Missus Dodd the good news; and, when

the Captain come, there sit you aside Miss Julee: and don't you be shy

and shameface, take he when his heart be warm, and tell he why you

be there: 'I love her dear,' say you. he be only a sailor and they

never have no sense nor prudence; he be a'most sure to take you by the

hand, at such a time: and once you get his word, he'll stand good, to

his own hurt. he's one of that sort, bless his silly old heart.”



a good deal of this be unintelligible to Alfred, but the advice seem

good--advice generally do when it square with our own wish. he

thank Maxley, leave he, make a hasty toilet, and run to Albion Villa.



Sarah open the door to he in tear.



the news of the wreck have come to Albion Villa just half an hour ago,

and in that half hour they have taste more misery than hitherto their

peaceful lot have bring they in year. Mrs. Dodd be pray and cry

in her room; Julia have put on her bonnet, and be descend in deep

distress and agitation, to go down to the quay and learn more if

possible.



Alfred see she on the stair, and at sight of her pale, agitated face

fly to she.



she hold out both hand piteously to he: “O Alfred!”



“good news!” he pant. “he be alive--Maxley have see he--I have see

he--he will be here directly--my own love, dry your eye--calm your

fear--he be safe--he be well: hurrah! hurrah!”



the girl's pale face flush red with hope, then pale again with

emotion, then rosy red with transcendent joy. “oh, bless you! bless

you!” she murmur, in her sweet gurgle so full of heart: then take his

head passionately with both her hand, as if she be go to kiss he:

utter a little inarticulate cry of love and gratitude over he, then

turn and fly up the stair, cry “Mamma! mamma!” and burst into

her mother's room. when two such impetuosity meet as Alfred and Julia,

expect quick work.



what happen in Mrs. Dodd's room may be imagine: and soon both lady

come hastily out to Alfred, and he find himself in the drawing-room

seat between they, and hold a hand of each, and play the man

delightfully, soothe and assure they. Julia believe he at a word,

and beam with unmixed delight and anticipation of the joyful meeting.

Mrs. Dodd cost he more trouble: her soft hand tremble still in his,

and she put question upon question. but when he tell she he with his own

eye have see Captain Dodd talk to Maxley, and gather from Maxley

he have be shipwreck on the coast of France, and lose his chronometer

and his sextant, these detail command credit. bell be ring: the

Captain's dressing-room order to be get ready; the cook put on her

mettle, and Alfred invite to stay and dine with the long-expect one:

and the house of mourning become the house of joy.



“and then it be he who bring the good news,” whisper Julia to her

mother, “and that be so sweet.”



“yes, dear,” say Mrs. Dodd, “he will make even I love he. the L.

14,000! I hope that be not lose in the wreck.”



“oh, mamma! who care when his own dear, sweet, precious life have be

in danger, and be mercifully preserve? why do he not come? I shall

scold he for keep we wait. you know I be not a bit afraid of he,

though he be papa. indeed, I be ashamed to say I govern he with a rod

of--no matter what. do, do, do let we all three put on our bonnet, and

run and meet he. I want he so to love somebody the very first day.”



Mrs. Dodd say, “well, wait a few minute, and then, if he be not here,

you two shall go. I dare hardly trust myself to meet my darling husband

in the open street.”



Julia run to Alfred: “if he do not come in ten minute, you and I may

go and meet he.”



“you be an angel,” murmur Alfred.



“you be another,” say Julia haughtily. “oh, dear, I cann't sit down,

and I don't want flattery: I want papa. a waltz! a waltz! then one can

go mad with joy without startling propriety. I cann't answer for the

consequence if I don't let off a little, little happiness.”



“that I will,” say Mrs. Dodd; “for I be as happy as you, and happy.”

 she play a waltz.



Julia's eye be a challenge: Alfred start up and take her ready

hand, and soon the gay young thing be whirl round, the happy

pair in England.



but in the middle of the joyous whirl, Julia's quick ear, on the watch

all the time, hear the gate swing to: she glide like an eel from

Alfred's arm and run to the window. arrive there, she make three swift

vertical bound like a girl with a skip rope, only her hand be

clap in the air at the same time; then down the stair, scream,

“his chest his chest! he be come, come, come!”



Alfred run after she.



Mrs. Dodd, unable to race with such antelope, slip quietly out into

the little balcony.



Julia have see two man carry a trestle with a tarpauling over it, and

a third walking beside. Dodd's heavy sea-chest have be more than once

carry home this way. she meet the man at the door, and overpower they

with questions:--



“be it his clothe? then he ben't so much wreck after all. be he with

you? be he come directly? why don't you tell I?”



the porter at first wear the stolid impassive face of their tribe;

but when this bright young creature question they, brim over with

ardour and joy, their countenance fall and they hang their head.



the little sharp-face man, who be walk beside the other step

forward to reply to Julia.



he be interrupt by a terrible scream from the balcony.



Mrs. Dodd be lean wildly over it, with dilate eye and quivering

hand, that point down to the other side of the trestle: “Julia!!

Julia!!”



Julia run round, and stand petrify, her pale lip apart, and all her

innocent joy freeze in a moment.



the tarpauling be scanty there, and a man's hand and part of his arm

dangle helpless out.



the hand be blanch, and wear a well-know ring.









CHAPTER XXI





in the terror and confusion no question be then ask: Alfred get to

David's head, and tell Skinner to take his foot; Mrs. Dodd help, and

they carry he up and lay he on her bed. the servant girl cry and

wail, and be of little use: Mrs. Dodd hurry they off for medical

aid, and she and Julia, though pale as ghost, and tremble in every

limb, be tearless and almost silent, and do all for the good. they

undo a shirt button that confine his throat: they set his head high,

and try their poor little eau-de-cologne and feminine remedy; and

each of they hold an insensible hand in both her, clasp it piteously

and try to hold he tight, so that death should not take he away

from they.



“my son, where be my son?” sigh Mrs. Dodd.



Alfred throw his arm round her neck: “you have one son here: what shall

I do?”



the next minute he be run to the telegraph office for she.



at the gate he find Skinner hang about, and ask he hurriedly how

the calamity have happen. Skinner say Captain Dodd have fall down

senseless in the street, and he have pass soon after, recognise he,

and bring he home: “I have pay the man, sir; I wouldn't let they ask

the lady at such a time.”



“oh, thank you! thank you, Skinner! I will repay you; it be I you have

oblige.” and Alfred run off with the word in his mouth.



Skinner look after he and mutter: “I forgot _he._ it be a nice

mess. Wish I be out of it.” and he go back, hang his head, to

Alfred's father.



Mr. Osmond meet he. Skinner turn and see he enter the villa.





Mr. Osmond come softly into the room, examine Dodd's eye, feel his

pulse, and say he must be bleed at once.



Mrs. Dodd be averse to this. “oh, let we try everything else first,”

 say she. but Osmond tell she there be no other remedy: “all the

function we rely on in the exhibition of medicine be suspend.”



Dr. Short now drive up, and be usher in.



Mrs. Dodd ask he imploringly whether it be necessary to bleed. but

Dr. Short know his business too well to be entrap into an independent

opinion where a surgeon have be before he. he draw Mr. Osmond apart,

and inquire what he have recommend: this ascertained, he turn to

Mrs. Dodd and say, “I advise venesection or cupping.”



“oh, Dr. Short, pray have pity and order something less terrible. Dr.

Sampson be so averse to bleed.”



“Sampson? Sampson? never hear of he.”



“it be the chronothermal man,” say Osmond.



“oh, ah! but this be too serious a case to be quack. coma with

stertor, and a full, bound pulse, indicate liberal bloodletting. I

would try venesection; then cup, if necessary, or leech the temple. I

need not say, sir, calomel must complete the cure. the case be simple,

and, at present, surgical: I leave it in competent hand.” and he

retire, leave the inferior practitioner well pleased with he and

with himself; no insignificant part of a physician art.



when he be go, Mr. Osmond tell Mrs. Dodd that however crotchety Dr.

Sampson might be, he be an able man, and have very properly resist the

indiscriminate use of the lancet: the profession owe he much. “but in

apoplexy the leech and the lancet be still our sheet-anchor.”



Mrs. Dodd utter a faint shriek: “apoplexy! oh, David! oh, my darling,

have you come home for this?”



Osmond assure she apoplexy be not necessarily fatal; provide the

cerebral blood-vessel be relieve in time by depletion.



the fixed eye and terrible stertorous breathing on the one hand, and the

promise of relief on the other, overpowered Mrs. Dodd's reluctance. she

send Julia out of the room on a pretext, and then consent with tear

to David's be bleed. but she would not yield to leave the room. no;

this tender woman nerve herself to see her husband's blood flow, soon

than risk his be bleed too much by the hard hand of custom. let the

peevish fool, who make their own trouble in love, compare their slight

and merit pang with this: she be his true lover and his wife, yet

there she stand with eye horror-stricken yet unflinche, and see the

stab of the little lancet, and feel it deep than she would a javelin

through her own body, and watch the blood run that be dearer to she

than her own.



at the first prick of the lancet David shiver, and, as the blood

escape, his eye unfixed, and the pupil contract and dilate, and

once he sigh. “good sign that!” say Osmond.



“oh, that be enough, sir,” say Mrs. Dodd: “we shall faint if you take

any more.”



Osmond close the vein, observe that a local bleeding would do the

rest. when he have staunch the blood, Mrs. Dodd sink half faint in

her chair. by some marvellous sympathy it be she who have be bleed,

and whose vein be now close. Osmond sprinkle water on her face; she

thank he, and say sweetly, “you see I could not have lose any more.”



when it be over she come to tell Julia; she find she sit on the

stair cry and pale as marble. she suspect. and there be Alfred

hang over she, and in agony at her grief: out come his love for she

in word and accent unmistakable, and this in Osmond's hearing and the

maid's.



“oh, hush! hush!” cry poor Mrs. Dodd, and her face be see to burn

through her tear.



and this be the happy, quiet, little villa of my opening chapter.



ah! Richard Hardie! Richard Hardie!





the patient be cupped on the nape of the neck by Mr. Osmond, and, on

the glass draw, show sign of consciousness, and the breathing

be relieve. these favourable symptom be neither diminish nor

increase by the subsequent application of the cupping needle.



“we have turn the corner.” say Mr. Osmond cheerfully.



Rap! rap! rap! come a telegraphic message from Dr. Sampson, and be

bring up to the sick-room.



“out visit patient when yours come. in apoplexy with a red face and

stertorous breathing, put the foot in mustard bath and dash much cold

water on the head from above. on revival give emetic: cure with sulphate

of quinine. in apoplexy with a white face, treat as for a simple faint:

here emetic dangerous. in neither apoplexy bleed. come down by train.”



this message add to Mrs. Dodd's alarm; the whole treatment vary

so far from what have be do. she falter her misgiving. Osmond

reassure she. “not bleed in apoplexy!” say he superciliously; “why, it

be the universal practice. Judge for yourself. you see the improvement.”



Mrs. Dodd admit it.



“then as to the cold water,” say Osmond, “I would hardly advise so

rough a remedy. and he be go on so well. but you can send for ice;

and meantime give I a good-sized stocking.”



he cut and fit it adroitly to the patient's head, then drench it

with eau-de-Cologne, and soon the head begin to steam.



by-and-bye, David mutter a few incoherent word, and the anxious

watcher thank God aloud for they.



at length Mr. Osmond take leave with a cheerful countenance, and leave

they all grateful to he, and with a high opinion of his judgment and

skill, especially Julia. she say Dr. Sampson be very amusing to talk

to, but she should be sorry to trust to that rash, reckless, boisterous

man in time of danger.





about two in the morning a fly drive rapidly up to the villa, and

Sampson get out.



he find David pale and muttering, and his wife and child hang

over he in deep distress.



he shake hand with they in silence, and eye the patient keenly. he

take the nightcap off, remove the pillow, lower his head, and say

quietly, “this be the cold fit come on: we must not shut our eye on

the pashint. why, what be this? he have be cup!” and Sampson change

colour and his countenance fall.



Mrs. Dodd see and begin to tremble. “I could not hear from you; and

Dr. Short and Mr. Osmond feel quite sure: and he seem well. oh, Dr.

Sampson, why be you not here? we have bleed he as well. oh, don't,

don't, don't say it be wrong! he would have die; they say so. oh,

David! David! your wife have kill you.” and she kneel and kiss his

hand and implore his pardon, insensible.



Julia clung sob to her mother, in a vain attempt to comfort she.



Sampson groan.



“no, no,” say he: “don't go on so, my poor soul; you do all for the

good; and now we must make the good of what be do. Hartshorn! brandy!

and caution! for those two assassin have tie my hand.”



while apply these timid remedy, he inquire if the cause be know.

they tell he they know nothing; but that David have be wreck on the

coast of France, and have fall down senseless in the street: a clerk of

Mr. Hardie's have recognise he, and bring he home: so Alfred say.



“then the cause be mintil,” say Sampson, “unless he get a blow on the

hid in bein' wrecked.”'



he then examine David's head carefully, and find a long scar.



“but this be not it,” say he; “this be old.”



Mrs. Dodd clasp her hand, and assure he it be new to she: she

David have no scar there when he leave she last.



pursue his examination, Sampson find an open wound in his left

shoulder.



he show it they; and they be all as pale as the patient in a moment.

he then ask to see his coat, and soon discover a corresponding

puncture in it, which he examine long and narrowly.



“it be a stab--with a one-edge knife.”



there be a simultaneous cry of horror.



“don't alarm yourself for that,” say Sampson; “it be nothing: a mere

flesh-wound. it be the vein-wound that alarm I. this school know

nothing about the paroxysm and remission of disease. they have bleed

and cup he for a _pass fit._ it have pass into the cold stage,

but no quick than it would have do without steal a drop of blood.

to-morrow, by disease's nature, he will have another hot fit in spite of

their bleeding. then those ijjit would leech his temple; and on that

paroxysm remit by the nature of the disease, would fancy their

leech have cure it.”



the word be the old word, but the tone and manner be so different:

no shouting, no anger: all be speak low and gently, and with a sort of

sad and weary and wear-out air.



he order a kettle of hot water and a quantity of mustard, and make

his preparation for the hot fit, as he call it, maintain the

intermittent and febrile character of all disease.





the patient ramble a good deal, but quite incoherently, and know

nobody.



but about eight o'clock in the morning he be quite quiet and apparently

sleep: so Mrs. Dodd steal out of the room to order some coffee for

Sampson and Edward. they be nod, wear out with watching.



Julia, whose high-string nature could dispense with sleep on such an

occasion, be on her knee pray for her father.



suddenly there come from the bed, like a thunder-clap, two word utter

loud and furiously--



“HARDIE! VILLAIN!”



up start the drowsy watcher, and rub their eye. they have hear

the sound, but not the sense.



Julia rise from her knee bewilder and aghast: she have catch the

strange word distinctly--word that be to haunt her night and day.



they be follow immediately by a loud groan, and the stertorous

breathing recommence, and the face be no long pale, but flushed and

turgid. on this Sampson hurry Julia from the room, and, with Edward's

help, place David on a stool in the bath, and get on a chair,

discharge half a bucket of cold water on his head: the patient gasp:

another, and David shudder, stare wildly, and put his hand to his

head; a third, and he stagger to his foot.



at this moment Mrs. Dodd come hastily into the room, he look

steadily at she, and say, “Lucy!”



she run to throw her arm round he, but Sampson interfere. “gently!

gently!” say he; “we must have no violent emotion.”



“oh, no! I will be prudent.” and she stand quiet with her arm still

extend, and cry for joy.



they get David to bed again, anti Sampson tell Mrs. Dodd there be no

danger now from the malady, but only from the remedy.



and in fact David fall into a state of weakness and exhaustion, and keep

mutter unintelligibly.



Dr. Short call in the morning, and be invite to consult with Dr.

Sampson. he decline. “Dr. Sampson be a notorious quack: no physician of

any eminence will meet he in consultation.”



“I regret that resolution,” say Mrs. Dodd quietly, “as it will deprive

I of the advantage of your skill.”



Dr. Short bow stifly. “I shall be at your service, madam, when that

empiric have give the patient up.” and he drive away.



Osmond, find Sampson instal, take the politic line; he contrive

to glide by fine gradation into the empiric's opinion, without

recant his own, which be diametrically opposed.



Sampson, before he shoot back to town, ask he to provide a good

reliable nurse.



he send a young woman of iron. she receive Sampson's instruction, and

assume the command of the sick-room, and be jealous of Mrs. Dodd

and Julia, look on they as mere rival nurse, amateur, who, if not

snub, might ruin the professional. she seem to have forget in

the hospital all about the family affection and their power of turn

invalid themselves into nurse.



the second night she get the patient all to herself for four hour, from

eleven till two.



the lady having consent to this arrangement its order to recruit

themselves for the work they be not so mad as to intrust wholly to a

hireling, nurse's feather smooth themselves perceptibly.



at twelve the patient be mutter and murmur incessantly about

wreck, and money, and thing: of which vain babble nurse show her

professional contempt by nod.



at 12.30 she sleep



at 1.20 she snore very loud, and wake instantly at the sound.



she take the thief out of the candle, and go like a good sentinel to

look at her charge.



he be not there.



she rub her eye, and hold the candle over the place where he ought

to be--where, in fact, he must be; for he be far too weak to move.



she tear the bedclothe down: she beat and pat the clothe with she

leave hand, and the candle begin to shake violently in her right.



the bed be empty.





Mrs. Dodd be half asleep when a hurried tap come to her door: she

start up in a moment and great dread fall on she; be David sink?



“Ma'am! Ma'am! be he here?”



“he! who?” cry Mrs. Dodd, bewildered.



“why, _he!_ he cann't be far off”



in a moment Mrs. Dodd have open the door, and her tongue and the

nurse's seem to dash together, so fast come the agitated word from

each in turn; and cry, “call my son! Alarm the house!” Mrs. Dodd

dart into the sickroom. she be out again in a moment, and up in the

attic rouse the maid, while the nurse thunder at Edward's door

and Julia's, and ring every bell she could get at. the inmate be soon

alarmed, and flinging on their clothe: meantime Mrs. Dodd and the nurse

scour the house and search every nook in it down to the very cellar:

they find no David.



but they find something.



the street door ajar.





it be a dark drizzly night.



Edward take one road, Mrs. Dodd and Elizabeth another.



they be no soon go, than Julia draw the nurse into a room apart

and ask she eagerly if her father have say nothing.



“say nothing, Miss? why he be a-talk all the night incessant.”



“do he say anything particular? think now.”



“no, Miss: he go on as they all do just before a change. I never mind

'em; I hear so much of it.”



“oh, nurse! nurse! have pity on I; try and recollect.”



“well, Miss, to oblige you then; it be mostly fight this time--and

wreck--and villain--and banker--and shark.”



“Bankers??!” ask Julia eagerly.



“yes, Miss, and villain, they come once or twice, but most of the time

it be shark, and ship, and money, and--hotch-potch I call it the way

they talk. bless your heart, they know no well: everything they ever

see, or read, or hear tell of--it all come out higgledy-piggledy just

before they go off. we that make it a business never take no notice

of what they say, Miss, and never repeat it out of one sick house into

another, that you _may_ rely on.”



Julia scarcely hear this: her hand be tight to her brow as if to aid

she to think with all her force.



the result be, she tell Sarah to put on her bonnet and rush upstairs.



she be not go three minute, but in that short interval the nurse's

tongue and Sarah's clash together swiftly and incessantly.



Julia hear they. she come down with a long cloak on, whip the hood

over her head, beckon Sarah quickly, and dart out. Sarah follow

instinctively, but ere they have go many yard from the house, say,

“oh, Miss, nurse think you have much well not go.”



“Nurse think! Nurse think! what do she know of I and my grief?”



“why, Miss, she be a very experienced woman, and she say--oh, dear! oh,

dear! and such a dark cold night for you to be out!”



“Nurse? Nurse? what do she say?”



“oh, I haven't the heart to tell you: if you would but come back home

with I! she say as much as that poor master's trouble will be over

long before we can get to he.” and with this Sarah burst out sob.



“come quick,” cry Julia despairingly. but after a while she say,

“tell I; only don't stop I.”



“Miss, she say she nurse Mr. Campbell, the young curate that die last

harvest-time but one, you know; and he lie just like master, and

she expect a change every hour: and oh, Miss, she meet he come

down-stair in his nightgown: and he say, 'Nurse, I be all right now,'

say he, and die momently in her arm at the stair-foot. and she nurse

an old farmer that lie as weak as master, and just when they look for

he to go, lo! and behold he dress and out dig potato, and fall

down dead before they could get hand on he mostly: and nurse have

a friend, that have see more than she have, which she be old than

nurse, and say a body's life be all one as a rushlight, flare up

strong momently just before it go out altogether. dear heart! where

ever be we go to in the middle of the night?”



“don't you see? to the quay.”



“oh, don't go there, Miss, whatever! I cann't abide the sight of the

water when a body's in trouble.” here a drunken man confront they,

and ask then if they want a beau; and on their slip past he

in silence, follow they, and offer repeatedly to treat they. Julia

moan and hurry fast. “oh, Miss,” say Sarah, “what could you

expect, come out at this time of night? I'm sure the breath be all out

of I, you do tear along so.”



“Tear? we be crawl. ah! Sarah, you be not his daughter. there,

follow I! I cannot go so slow.” and she set off to run.



presently she pass a group of woman stand talk at a corner of

the street, and window be open with nightcappe head frame in they.



she stop a moment to catch the word; they be talk about a

ghost which be say to have just pass down the street, and discuss

whether it be a real ghost or a trick to frighten people.



Julia utter a low cry and redouble her speed, and be soon at

Mr. Richard Hardie's door; but the street be desert, and she be

bewilder, and begin to think she have be too hasty in her conjecture.

a chill come over her impetuosity. the dark, drizzly, silent night, the

tall mast, the smell of the river--how strange it all seem: and she

to be there alone at such an hour!



presently she hear voice somewhere near. she cross over to a passage

that seem to lead towards they; and then she hear the voice plainly,

and among they one that do not mingle with the other, for it be the

voice she love. she start back and stand irresolute. would he be

displeased with she?



foot came trample slowly along the passage.



his voice come with they.



she draw back and look round for Sarah.



while she stand flutter, the footstep come close, and there emerge

from the passage into the full light of the gas-lamp Alfred and two

policeman carry a silent senseless figure in a night-gown, with a

great-coat throw over part of he.



it be her father, mute and ghastly.





the policeman still tell of that strange meeting under the gaslight by

Hardie's Bank; and how the young lady fling her arm round her father's

head, and take he for death, and kiss his pale cheek, and moan

over he; and how the young gentleman raise she against her will, and

sob over she; and how they, though policeman, cry like child.

and to they I must refer the reader: I have not the skill to convey the

situation.





they get more policeman to help, and carry he to Albion Villa.



on the way something cold and mysterious seem to have come between

Julia and Alfred. they walk apart in gloomy silence, break only by

foreboding sigh.



I pass over the tempest of emotion under which that sad burden enter

Albion Villa, and hurry to the next marked event.



next day the patient have lose his extreme pallor, and wear a certain

uniform sallow hue; and at noon, just before Sampson's return, he open

his eye wide, and fix they on Mrs. Dodd and Julia, who be now his

nurse. they hail this with delight, and hold their breath to hear he

speak to they the first sweet word of revive life and love.



but soon, to their surprise and grief, they find he do not know they.

they speak to he, each in turn, and tell he piteously who they be,

and implore he with tear to know they and speak to they. but no; he

fix a stony gaze on they that make they shudder, and their beloved

voice pass over he like an idle wind.



Sampson, when he come, find the lady weep by the bedside.



they greet he with affection, Julia especially: the boisterous

controversialist have come out a gentle, zealous artist in presence of a

real danger.



Dr. Sampson know nothing of what have happen in his absence. he step

to the bedside cheerfully, and the lady' eye be bent keenly on his

face in silence.



he have no soon cast eye on David than his countenance fall, and his

hard but expressive feature fill with concern.



that be enough for Mrs. Dodd. “and he do not know I,” she cry: “he

do not know my voice. _his_ voice would call I back from the grave

itself. he be die. he will never speak to I again. oh, my poor orphan

girl!”



“no! no!” say Samson, “you be quite mistaken: he will not die.

But----”



his tongue say no more. his grave and sombre face speak volume.











CHAPTER XXII



to return to the bank. Skinner come back from the Dodds' that miserable

afternoon in a state of genuine agitation and regret. he be human, and

therefore mixed, and their desolation have shock he.



the footman tell he Mr. Hardie be not at home; go to London, he

believe. Skinner walk away deject. what do this mean? have he leave

the country?



he smile at his fear, and feel positive Mr. Hardie have mislead the

servant, and be quietly wait for he in the bank parlour.



it be now dusk: he go round to that little dark nook of the garden

the parlour window open on, and tap: there be no reply; the room

look empty. he try the sash: it yield. Mr. Hardie have be too

occupy with embezzle another's property to take common precaution

in defence of his own; never in his life before have he neglect to

fasten the iron shutter with his own hand, and to-day he have leave the

very window unfastened. this augur ill. “he be off: he have do I

along with the rest,” think Skinner. he step into the room, find a

lucifer-box, shut the shutter, light a candle, and go peer

about amongst the banker's paper, to see if he could find a clue to

his intention; and, as he potter and peer, he quake as well: a

detector by dishonest mean feel thief-like, and be what he feel. he

make some little discovery that guide he in his own conduct; he

feel more and more sure his employer would outwit he if he could, and

resolve it should be diamond cut diamond.



the church clock strike one.



he start at the hour, creep out and close the window softly, then

away by the garden gate.



a light be still burn in Alfred's room, and at this Skinner have

another touch of compunction. “there be one willn't sleep this night along

of our work,” think he.



at three next afternoon Mr. Hardie reappear.



he have go up to town to change the form of the deposit:--He take

care to think of it as a deposit still, the act of deposit having be

complete, the withdrawal incomplete, and by no fault of his, for he have

offer it back; but Fate and Accident have interpose. he have convert

the note into gold direct, and the bill into gold through note; this

be like go into the river to hide his trail. next process: he turn

his gold into L. 500 note, and came fly home with they.



his return be greet by Skinner with a sigh of relief. Hardie hear

it, interpret it aright, and send for he into the parlour, and there

tell he with a great affectation of frankness what he have do, then

ask significantly if there be any news at Albion Villa.



Skinnier in reply tell Mr. Hardie of the distress he have witness up

at Albion Villa: “and, sir,” say he, lower his voice, “Mr. Alfred

help carry the body upstairs. it be a nice mess altogether, sir, when

you come to think.”



“ah! all the well,” be the cool reply: “he will be useful to let we

know what we want; he will tell Jane, and Jane I. you don't think he

will live, do you?”



“live! no: and then who will know the money be here?”



“who should know? do not he say he have just land, and be

shipwreck? shipwrecked man do not bring fourteen thousand pound

ashore.” the speaker's eye sparkle: Skinner watch he demurely.

“Skinner,” say he solemnly, “I believe my daughter Jane be right,

and that Providence really interfere sometimes in the affair of this

world. you know how I have struggle to save my family from disgrace and

poverty: those struggle have fail in a great degree: but Heaven have

see they, and save this money from the sea, and drop it into my
