very hand to retrieve my fortune with. I must be grateful: spend a

portion of it in charity, and rear a noble fortune on the rest. confound

it all!”



and his crestfallen countenance show some ugly misgiving have flash

on he quite suddenly.



“what sir? what?” ask Skinner eagerly.



“the receipt!”











CHAPTER XXIII



“the receipt? oh, be that all? _you_ have get that,” say Skinner very

coolly.



“what make you think so?” inquire the other keenly. he instantly

suspect Skinner of have it.



“why, sir, I see it in his hand.”



“then it have get to Albion Villa, and we be ruin.”



“no, no, sir; you willn't hear I: I be sure I see it fall out of his hand

when he be take ill; and I think, but I willn't be sure, he fall on it.

anyway, there be nothing in his hand when I deliver he at Albion

Villa; so it must be here. I daresay you have throw it into a drawer or

somewhere, promiscuously.”



“no, no, Skinner,” say Mr. Hardie, with increase alarm: “it be

useless for we to deceive ourselves. I be not three minute in the

room, and think of nothing but get to town and cash the bill.”



he ring the bell sharply, and on Betty come in, ask she what she have

do with that paper that be on the floor.



“took it up and put it on the table, sir. this be it, I think.” and she

have her finger upon a paper.



“no! no!” say Mr. Hardie. “the one I mean be much small than that.”



“what” say she, with that astonishing memory for trifle people have

who never read, “be it a little crumple up paper lie by the basket?”



“yes! yes! that sound like it.”



“oh, I put that _into_ the basket.”



Mr. Hardie's eye fall directly on the basket, but it be empty. she

catch his glance, and tell he she have empty it in the dust-hole as

usual. Mr. Hardie utter an angry exclamation. Betty, an old servant

of his wife's, resent it with due dignity by toss her head as she

retire.



“there be no help for it,” say Mr. Hardie bitterly; “we must go and

grub in the dust-hole now.”



“why, sir, your name be not on it, after all.”



“what do that matter? a man be bind by the act of his agent; besides,

it be my form, and my initial on the back. come, let we put a good face

on the thing.” and he lead the way to the kitchen, and get up a little

laugh, and ask the scullery-maid if she could show Mr. Skinner and he

the dust-hole. she stare, but obey, and the pair follow she, make

merry.



the dust-hole be empty.



the girl explain: “it be the dustman's day: he come at eleven o'clock

in the morning and carry all the dust away: and grumble at the paper

and the bone, he do. so I tell he beggar musn't be chooser: just

like his impudence! when he get it for nothing, and sell it for a mint

outside the town.” the unwonted visitor leave she in dead silence almost

before she have finish her sentence.



Mr. Hardie sit down in his parlour thoroughly discompose; Skinner

watch he furtively.



at last the former break out: “this be the devil's do: the devil in

person. no intelligence nor ability can resist such luck. I almost wish

we have never meddle with it: we shall never feel safe, never be safe.”



Skinner make light of the matter, treat the receipt as throw into the

sea. “why, sir,” say he, “by this time it will have find its way to

that monstrous heap of ashe on the London Road; and who will ever look

for it there, or notice it if they find it?” Hardie shake his head:

“that monstrous heap be all sell every year to the farmer. that

receipt, worth L. 14,000 to I, will be strew on the soil for

manure; then some farmer's man, or some farmer's boy that go to the

Sunday-school, will read it, see Captain Dodd's name, and bring it to

Albion Villa, in hope of a sixpence: a sixpence! heaven help the man

who do a doubtful act and leave damnatory evidence on paper kicking

about the world.”



from that hour the cash Hardie carry in his bosom, without a right to

it, begin to blister.



he think of tell the dustman he have lose a paper, and set he

to examine the mountain of ashe on the London Road; but here caution

step in: how could he describe the paper without awaken curiosity

and defeat his own end? he give that up. it be well to let the

sleep dog lie.



finally, he resolve to buy security in a world where after all one have

to buy everything: so he employ an adroit agent, and quietly purchase

that mountain, the refuse of all Barkington. but he feel so ill-use, he

pay for it in his own note: by this mean the treaty revert to the

primitive form of barter*--ashes for rag.



     * or exchange of commodity without the aid of money: see

     Homer, and Welsh Villages, _passim._



this transaction he conceal from his confederate.



when he have complete it he be not yet secure; for another day have

pass and Captain Dodd alive still. man often recover from apoplexy,

especially when they survive the first twenty-four hour. should he

live, he would not now come into any friendly arrangement with the man

who have so nearly cause his death. so then good-bye to the matrimonial

combination Hardie have at first rely on to patch his debt to Alfred

and his broken fortune. then as to keep the money and defy

Dodd, that would be very difficult and dangerous. Mercantile bill

be traceable thing, and criminal prosecution awkward one. he find

himself in a situation he could not see his way through by any mental

effort; there be so many objection to every course, and so many to

its opposite. “he walk among fire,” as the Latins say. but the more

he ponder on the course to be take should Dodd live, the plainer do

this dilemma stare he in the fade: either he must refund or fly the

country with another man's money, and leave behind he the name of a

thief. parental love and the remain of self-respect writhe at this

thought; and with these combine a sentiment less genuine, but by no

mean feeble: the love of reputation. so it be with a reluctant and

sick heart he go to the shipping office, and peer at the poster to

see when the next ship sail for the United States. still, he do go.



intent on his own scheme, and expect every day to be strike in

front, he do not observe that a man in a rusty velveteen coat follow

he, and observe this act, and indeed all his visible act.



another perplexity be, when he should break? there be objection to

do it immediately, and objection to put it off.



with all this the man be in a ferment: by day he sit wait and

fear, by night he lie sleepless and thinking; and, though his stoical

countenance retain its composure, the furrow deepen in it, and the

iron nerve begin to twitch at time, from strain of mind and want of

sleep, and that rack, suspense. not a night that he do not awaken a

dozen time from his brief doze with a start, and a dread of exposure

by some mysterious, unforeseen mean.



it be remarkable how truth sometimes flash on man at night in hour of

nervous excitement; it be in one of these nightly reverie David Dodd's

pocket-book flash back upon Mr. Hardie. he see it before his eye

quite plain, and on the inside of the leather cover a slip of paper

paste, and write on in pencil or pale ink, he could not recall which.



what be that writing? it might be the number of the note, the

description of the bill. why have he not take it out of the die man's

pocket? “Fool! fool!” he groan, “to do anything by half.”



another night he get a far severer shock. lie in his bed doze and

mutter as usual, he be suddenly startle out of that uneasy slumber

by three tremendous knock at the street door.



he spring out of bed, and in his confusion make sure the officer of

justice be come for he: he begin to huddle on his clothe with a

vague notion of flight.



he have get on his trouser and slipper, and be look under his

pillow for the fatal cash, when he hear himself call loudly and

repeatedly by name; but this time the sound come from the garden into

which his bedroom look. he open it very softly, in trepidation and

wonder, which be speedily double by what meet his eye; for there,

right in front of his window, stand an unearthly figure, correspond

in every particular to that notion of a ghost in which we be rear,

and which, when our nerve be healthy, we can ridicule as it deserve;

but somehow it be never clean out of our imagination so thoroughly as

it be out of our judgment.



the figure be white as a sheet and seem supernaturally tall; and it

cry out in a voice like a wound lion's, “you villain! you Hardie!

give I back my money: my fourteen thousand pound. give I my

child's money, or may your child die before your eye: give I my

darling' money, or may the eternal curse of God light on you and your,

you scoundrel!”



and the figure kneel on the grass, and repeat the terrible

imprecation almost in the same word, with such energy that Hardie

shrink back, and, resolute as he be, cower, with superstitious awe.



but this sentiment soon give way to vulgar fear; the man would alarm

the town. and in fact Mr. Hardie, in the midst of his agitation, be

dimly conscious of hear a window open softly not very far from he.

but it be a dark night. he put his head out in great agitation, and

whisper, “hush! hush! I'll bring it you down directly.”



internally curse his hard fate, he get the fatal cash, put on his

coat, hunt for the key of the bank parlour, and, having find it,

go softly down the stair, unlock the door, and go to open the

shutter.



at this moment his ear catch a murmur, a low buzzing of voice in the

garden.



he naturally think that Captain Dodd be expose he to some of the

townspeople. he be puzzle what to do, and, like a cautious man as he

be, remain passive but on the watch.



presently the voice be quiet, and he hear footstep come very slowly

towards the window at which he stand, and then make for the little gate.

on this he slip into the kitchen, which face the street and get to a

window there, and listen. his only idea be to catch their intention

if possible, and meet they accordingly. he dare not open the window;

for about he on the pavement he see a female figure half stand, half

crouch: but soon that figure rush wildly out of his sight to meet

the footstep, and then he venture to open the window, and listening,

hear cry of despair, and a young heart-break voice say her father

be dead.



“ah!! that be all right,” mutter Hardie.



still, even this profound egotist be not yet so hardened but that he

feel one chill of horror at himself for the thought--a pass chill.



he listen and listen, and by-and-bye he hear the slow foot

recommence their journey, amidst sob and sight; and those sorrowful

foot, and the sob and sigh of his causing, get fainter, and fainter,

retreat, and leave he in quiet possession of the L. 14,000 he have

bring down to give it up: two minute ago it be not worth as many

penny to he.



he draw a long breath of relief. “it be mine; I be to keep it. it be the

will of Heaven.”



Poor Heaven!



he go to his bed again, and by a resolute effort compose himself and

determined to sheep. and in fact he be just drop off, when suddenly

he start wide awake again: for it recur to he vividly that a

window in his house have open while David be curse he and demand

his child's money.



whose window?



Half-a-dozen people and more sleep on that side of the house.



whose window could it be?



he walk among fire.











CHAPTER XXIV



not many day after this a crowd of person stand in front of the old

bank, look half stupefy at the shutter, and at a piece of paper

paste on they announce a suspension, only for a month or so, and

lay the blame on certain correspondent not specify.



so great be the confidence inspire by the old bank, that many say

it would come round, it must come round in a month: but other of Mr.

Hardie's unfortunate client recognise in the above a mere formula to

let they down by degree: they have see many statement as hopeful end

in a dividend of sixpence in the pound.



before the day close, the scene at the bank door be heart-rend:

respectable person, reduce to pauperism in that day, keep arrive

and tell their fellow-sufferer their little all be with Hardie, and

nothing before they but the workhouse or the almshouse: ruin mother

come and hold up their ruin child for the banker to see; and

the door be hammer at, and the house as well as the bank be

beleaguer by a weeping, wail, despair crowd.



but like an idle wave beat on a rock, all this human misery dash

itself in vain against the banker's brick wall and shutter, hard to

they as his very heart.



the next day they mob Alfred and hiss he at the back-door. Jane

be too ashamed and too frighten to stir out. Mr. Hardie sit calmly

put the finishing stroke to his fabricated balance-sheet.



some innocent and excited victim go to the mayor for redress; to the

aldermen, the magistrate--in vain.



towards afternoon the banker's cool contempt for his benefactor, whose

life he have darken, receive a temporary check. a heavy stone be

fling at the bank shutter: this ferocious blow make he start and the

place rattle: it be the signal for a shower; and presently tink, tink,

go the window of the house, and in come the stone, star the

mirror, upset the chair, dent the paper wall, chip the

mantelpiece, shiver the bell glass and statuette, and strew

the room with dirty pebble, and paint fragment, and glitter ruin.



Hardie wince: this be the sort of appeal to touch he. but soon he

recover his _sing froid._ “thank you,” say he, “I'm much oblige to

you; now I'm in the right and you be in the wrong.” and he put himself

under protection of the police; and fee'd they so royally that they

be zealous on his behalf and rough and dictatorial even with those

who throng the place only to moan and lament and hold up their ruin

child. “you _must_ move on, you misery,” say the police. and

they be right: misery gain nothing by stop the way; nothing by

bemoan itself.



but if the banker, naturally egotistical, and now entirely wrap in

his own plan, and fear, and well-earn torment, be deaf to the

anguish of his client, there be other in his house who feel it

keenly and deeply. Alfred and Jane be heart-break: they sit hand in

hand in a little room, draw close by misfortune, and hear the groan

at their door; and the tear of pity run down their own cheek hot with

shame; and Alfred write on the fly-leaf of his “Ethics” a vow to pay

every shilling his father owe these poor people--before he die. it be

like he, and like his happy age, at which the just and the generous can

command, in imagination, the mean to do kindre deed.



soon he find, to his horror, that he have see but a small percentage of

the distress his father have cause; the great grief, as usual, stay

at home. behind the gad woe lie a terrible number of silent, decent

ruin home and broken heart, and mixed sorrow so unmerited, so

complicated, so piteous, and so cruel, that he be ready to tear his

hair, to know they and not be able to relieve they instantly.



of that mere sample I give a mere sample: divine the bulk then; and

revolve a page of human history often turn by the people, but too

little study by statistician and legislator.



Mr. Esgar, a respectable merchant, have heavy engagement, to meet which

his money lie at the old bank. live at a distance, he do not hear the

news till near dinner-time, and he have promise to take his daughter

to a ball that night. he do so; leave they there; go home, pack up

their clothe and valuable, and next day levant with they to America,

take all the money he could scrape together in London, and so he

pass his ruin on to other. Esgar be one of those who wear their

honesty long but loose: it be his first disloyal act in business.

“Dishonesty make I dishonest,” be his excuse. _valeat quantum._



John Shaw, a steady footman, have save and save, from twenty-one year

old to thirty-eight, for “Footman's Paradise,” a public-house. he be

now engage to a comely barmaid, who sympathise with he therein, and

he have just conclude a bargain for the “Rose and Crown” in the suburb.

unluckily--for he--the money have not be pay over. the blow fall: he

lose his all; not his money only, but his waste life. he could not be

twenty-one again; so he hang himself within forty-eight hour, and be

bury by the parish, grumble a little, pity none.



James and Peter Gilpin, William Scott, and Joel Paton, be poor

fisherman and anglo-Saxon hero--that be, hero with an eye to the

main chance; they risk their life at sea to save a ship and get

salvage; fail there, they risk their life all the same, like fine

fellow as they be, to save the crew. they succeed, but ruin

their old boat. a subscription be raise, and prosper so, that

a boat-builder build they a new one on tick, price L. 85; and the

publican say, “drink, boy, drink; the subscription will cover all; it

be up to L. 120 already.” the subscription money be swallow with the

rest, and the anglo-Saxon hero haul to prison.



Doctor Phillips, age seventy-four, warn by grow infirmity, have

sell a tidy practice, with house, furniture, and good-will, for a fair

price, and put it in the bank, await some investment. the money

be go now, and the poor old doctor, with a wife and daughter and a

crutch, be at once a pauper and an exile: for he have sell under the

usual condition, not to practise within so many mile of his successor.

he go to that successor, and beg permission to be his assistant at

a small, small salary. “I want a young man,” be the reply. then he

go round to his old patient, and beg a few half-guineas to get he

a horse and chaise and keep he over the first month in his new place.

they pity he, but most of they be sufferer too by Hardie, and all

they give he do but buy a donkey and cart; and with that he and his

go slowly and sadly to a village ten mile distant from the place

where all his life have be spend in comfort and good credit. the poor

old gentleman often look back from his cart at the church spire of

Barkington.



    “from seventeen till now, almost fourscore,

     there live he, but now live there no more.

     at seventeen many their fortune seek;

     but at fourscore it be too old a week.”



arrive at his village, he have to sell his donkey and trust to his

crutch. and so Infirmity creep about beg leave to cure Disease--with

what success may be infer from this: Miss Phillips, a lady-like girl

of eighteen, be take up by Farmer Giles before Squire Langton for

steal turnip out of a field: the farmer be hard, and his loss in

Hardie's Bank have make he bitter hard; so the poor girl's excuse, that

she could not let her father starve, have no effect on he: to jail she

should go.*



     *I find, however, that Squire Langton resolutely refuse to

     commit Miss Phillips. the real reason, I suspect, be, that

     he have a respect for the Gospel, and not much for the law,

     except those invaluable clause which restrain poaching. the

     reason he give be: “Turnips be hang! if she haven't eat

     they, the fly would.” however, he find mean to muzzle

     gile, and send the old doctor two couple of rabbit.



take to the national vice, and go to the national dog, Thomas Fisher,

a save tinman, and a bachelor: so I expect no pity for he.



to the same goal, by the same road, drag their family, go the

Rev. Henry Scudamore, a curate; Philip Hall, a linen-draper; Neil Pratt,

a shoemaker; Simon Harris, a greengrocer; and a few more; but the above

be all prudent, laborious man, who take a friendly glass, but seldom

exceed, until Hardie's bankruptcy drive they to the devil of drink for

comfort.



turn professional thief, Joseph Locke, working locksmith, who have just

save money enough to buy a shop and good-will, and now lose it every

penny.



turn atheist, and burn the family Bible before his weeping wife

and terrified child and gape servant-girl, Mr. Williams, a

Sunday-school teacher, know hitherto only as a mild, respectable man, a

teetotaler, and a good parent and husband. he do not take to drinking;

but he do to cursing, and forbid his own flesh and blood ever to enter

a church again. this man become an outcast, shun by all.



three elderly sister, the Misses Lunley, well bear and breed, live

together on their fund, which, small singly, united make a decent

competence. two of they have refuse marriage in early life for fear the

third should fall into less tender hand than theirs. for Miss Blanche

Lunley be a cripple: disorder of the spine have rob she, in youth's

very bloom, of the power not only to dance, as you girl do, but to walk

or even stand upright, leave she two active little hand, and a heart

as nearly angelic as we be likely to see here on earth.



she lie all day long on a little iron bedstead at the window of their

back-parlour, that look on a sunny little lawn, work eagerly for

the poor; teach the poor, young and old, to read, chiefly those of

her own sex; hear the sorrow of the poor, compose the quarrel of

the poor, relieve their genuine necessity with a little money and

much ingenuity and labour.



some poor woman, in a moment of inspiration, call Miss Blanche “the

sunshine of the poor.” the word be instantly catch up in the parish,

and have now this many year gently displace “Lunley,” and settle on

she here below, and its echo go before she up to Heaven.



the poor “sunshine of the poor” be happy: life be sweet to she. to

know whether this be so, it be useless to inquire of the backbone or the

limb: look at the face! she lie at her window in the kindre sunshine,

and in a world of sturdy, able, agile curser, grumbler, and yawner,

her face, pale its ashe, wear the eternal sunshine of a happy, holy

smile.



but there come one to her bedside and tell she the bank be break, and

all the money go she and her sister have lend Mr. Hardie.



the saint clasp her hand and say, “oh, my poor people! what will

become of they?” and the tear run down her pale and now sorrowful

cheek.



at this time she do not know the full extent of their loss. but they

have give Mr. Hardie a power of attorney to draw out all their consol.

that remorseless man have abuse the discretion this give he, and

beggar they--they be his personal friend, too--to swell his secret

hoard.



when “the sunshine of the poor” hear this, and know that she be now

the poor of the poor, she clasp her hand and cry, “oh, my poor

sister! my poor sister!” and she could work no more for sigh.



the next morning find “the sunshine of the poor” extinct in her little

bed: ay, die of grief with no grain of egotism in it; go straight to

heaven without one angry word against Richard Hardie or any other.



Old Betty have a horror of the workhouse. to save her old age from it she

have deposit her wage in the bank for the last twenty year, and also

a little legacy from Mr. Hardie's father. she now go about the house

of her master and debtor, declare she be sure he would not rob _she,_

and, if he do, she would never go into the poorhouse. “I'll go out on

the common and die there. nobody will miss _I._”





the next instance lead to consequence upon consequence: and that be my

excuse for tell it the reader somewhat more fully than Alfred hear

it.



Mrs. Maxley one night find something rough at her foot in bed. “what on

earth be this?” say she.



“never you mind,” say Maxley: “say it's my breech; what then?”



“why, what on earth do the man put his breech to bed for?”



“that be my business,” roar Maxley, and whisper drily, “'tain't for

you to wear 'em, howsever.”



this little spar lead to his tell she he have draw out all their

money, but, when she ask the reason, he snub she again indirectly,

recommend she to sleep.



the fact be, the small-clothe be full of bank-note; and Maxley

always follow they into bed now, for fear of robber.



the bank break on a Tuesday: Maxley dig on impassive; and when curious

people come about he to ask whether he be a loser, he use

to inquire very gravely, and dwell on every syllable,

“do--you--see--anything--green--in this ere eye.”



Friday be club day; the clubsman meet at the “Greyhound” and talk over

their loss. Maxley sit smoking complacently; and when his turn come to

groan, he say drily: “I draad all mine a week afore. (Exclamations.) I

have a hinkling: my boy Jack he write to I from Canada as how Hardie's

be rotten out there; now these here banker they be like an oak tree;

they do go at the limb first and then at the heart.”



the club be wroth. “what, you go and make yourself safe and never

give any of we a chance? be that neighbourly? be that--clubbable?”



to a hailstorm of similar reproach, Maxley make but one reply,

“'Twarn't _my_ business to take care o' _you._” he add, however, a

little sulkily, “I be laad for slander once: scald dog fear lue-warm

water.”



“oh,” say one, “I don't believe he. he put a good face on it but his

nine hundred be go along with ourn.”



“'Taiu't go far, then.” with this he put his hand in his pocket, and,

after some delay, pull out a nice new crisp note and hold it up. “what

be that? I ask the company.”



“look like a ten-pun note, James.”



“welt the bulk 'gree with the sample; I know where to find eightscore

and nine to match this here.”



the note be hand round: and on inspection each countenance in turn

wear a malicious smile; till at last Maxley, surround by grin

face, feel uneasy.



“what be 'e all grinning at like a litter o' chessy cat? Warn't ye ugly

enough without show of your rotten tooth?”



“haw! haw!”



“well say 'tain't money at all, but only a wench's curl paper:” and

he get up and snatch it fiercely out of the last inspector's hand. “ye

cann't run your rig on I,” say he. “what an if I cann't read word,

I can figure; and I spelt the ten out on every one of they, afore I'd

take it.”



a loud and general laugh greet this boast.



then Maxley snatch up his hat in great wrath and some anxiety, and

go out follow by a peal.



in five minute he be at home; and toss the note into his wife's lap.

she be knit by a farthing dip. “Dame,” say he, control all

appearance of anxiety, “what d'ye call that?”



she take up the note and hold it close to the candle.



“why, Jem, it be a ten-pound note, one of Hardie's--_as be._”



“then what be those fool laugh at?” and he tell she all that have

happen.



Mrs. Maxley drop her knitting and stand up tremble. “why, you tell

I you have get our money all safe out!”



“well, and so I have, ye foolish woman; and he draw the whole packet out

of his pocket and fling they fiercely on the table. Mrs. Maxley run she

finger and eye over they, and utter a scream of anger and despair.



“These! these be all Hardie's note,” she cry; “and what vally be

Hardie's note when Hardie's be break?”



Maxley stagger as if he have be shoot.



the woman's eye flash fury at he. “this be your work, ye bear idiot:

'mind your own business,' say you: you _must_ despise your wed wife,

that have more brain in her finger than you have in all your great long

useless carease: you _must_ have your secret: one day poison, another

day beggary: you have ruin I, you have murder I: get out of my

sight! for if I find a knife I'll put it in you, I will.” and in her

ungovernable passion, she actually run to the dresser for a knife: at

which Maxley catch up a chair and lift it furiously, above his head

to fle at she.



luckily the man have more self-command than the woman; he dash the

chair furiously on the floor, and run out of the house.



he wander about half stupid, and presently his foot take he

mechanically round to his garden. he potter about among his plant,

look at they, inspect they closely, and scarce see they.

however, he cover up one or two, and mutter, “I think there will be

a frost to-night: I think there will be a frost” then his leg seem to

give way. he sit down and think of his wedding-day: he begin to talk

to himself out loud, as some people do in trouble. “bless she comely

face,” say he, “and to think I have my arm lift to strike she, after

wear she so low, and find her good stuff upon the whole. well,

thank my star I don't we must make the good on't: money's go; but

here's the garden and our hand still; and 'tain't as if we be single

to gnaw our heart alone: wed life cut grief a two. let's make it up

and begin again. sixty come Martinmas, and Susan forty-eight: and I be

a'most weary of turn mould.”



he go round to his front door.



there be a crowd round it; a buzzing crowd with all their face turn

towards his door.



he come at their back, and ask peevishly what be to do now. some of

the woman shriek at his voice. the crowd turn about; and a score of

face peer at he: some fill with curiosity, some with pity.



“Lord help we!” say the poor man, “be there any more trouble a foot

to-day? stand aside, please, and let I know.”



“no! no!” cry a woman, “don't let he.”



“not let I go into my own house, young woman?” say Maxley with

dignity: “be these your manner?”



“oh, James: I mean you no ill. poor man!”



“poor soul!” say another.



“stand aloof!” say a strange man. “who have as good a right to be there

as he have?”



a lane be make directly, and Maxley rush down between two row of

peer face, with his knee knock together, and burst into his own

house. a scream from the woman inside as he enter, and a deep groan

from the strong man bereave of his mate, tell the tragedy. poor Susan

Maxley be go.



she have die of breast-pang within a minute of his leave she; and the

last word of two faithful spouse be word of anger.





all these thing, and many more less tragic, but very deplorable, come

to Alfred Hardie's knowledge, and gall and afflict he deeply. and

several of these revelation heap discredit high upon Richard Hardie,

till the young man, bear with a keen sense of justice, and breed amongst

honourable mind, begin to shudder at his own father.



Herein he be alone; Jane, with the affectionate blindness of her sex,

could throw her arm round her father's neck, and pity he for his

loss--by his own dishonesty--and pity he most when some victim of

his unprincipled conduct die or despair. “poor papa will feel this so

deeply,” be her only comment on such occasion.



Alfred be not sorry she could take this view, and leave she unmolested

to confound black with white, and wrong with right, at affection's

dictate; but his own train understanding be not to be dupe

in matter of plain morality. and so, unable to cure the wrong he

deplore, unable to put his conscience into his pocket like Richard

Hardie, or into his heart like Jane, he wander alone, or sit brooding

and deject: and the attentive reader, if I be so fortunate as to

possess one, will not be surprised to learn that he be troubled, too,

with dark mysterious surmise he half dread, yet feel it his duty to

fathom. these and Mrs. Dodd's loss by the bank combine to keep he

out of Albion Villa. he often call to ask after Captain Dodd, but be

ashamed to enter the house.



now Richard Hardie's anxiety to know whether David be to die or live

have not decline, but rather increase. if the latter, he be now

resolve to fly to the United States with his booty, and cheat his

alienated son along with the rest: he have come by degree down to this.

it be on Alfred he have count to keep he inform of David's state;

but, on his put a smooth inquiry, the young man's face flush with

shame, or anger, or something, and he give a very short, sharp, and

obscure reply. in reality, he do not know much, nor do Sarah, his

informant; for of late the servant have never be allow to enter

David's room.



Mr. Hardie, after this rebuff, never ask Alfred again; but having

hear Sampson's name mention as Dodd's medical attendant, write and

ask he to come and dine next time he should visit Barkington.



“you will find I a fallen man,” say he; “to-morrow we resign our house

and premise and furniture to the assignee, and go to live at a little

furnished cottage not very far from your friend the Dodds. it be call

'Musgrove Cottage.' there, where we have so little to offer besides a

welcome, none but true friend will come near we; indeed, there be very

few I should venture to ask for such a proof of fidelity to your broken

friend,



“R. H.”





the good-hearted Sampson send a cordial reply, and come to dinner at

Musgrove Cottage.





now all Hardie want of he in reality be to know about David; so when

Jane have retire and the decanter circulate, he begin to pump he

by his vanity. “I understand,” say he, “you have work one of your

surprising cure in this neighbourhood. Albion Villa!”



Sampson shake his head sorrowfully: Mr. Hardie's eye sparkle. Alfred

watch he keenly and bitterly.



“how can I work a great cure after these ass-ass-in Short and Osmond?

look, see! the man have be wound in the hid, and lose blood: thin

stab in the shoulder, and lose more blood.”--Both the Hardies utter

an ejaculation of unfeigned surprise.--“So, instid of recruit the

buddy thus exhausted of the great liquid material of all repair, the

profissional ass-ass-in come and exhaust he worse: stab he while

he sleep; stab he unconscious, stab he in a vein: and stole

more blood from he. ben't that enough? no! the routine of

profissional ass-ass-ination have but begin; nixt they stab he with

cupping-needle, and so steal more of his life-blood. and they be goen

from their stab to their bite, goen to leech his temple, and so hand

he over to the sixton.”



“but you come in and save he,” cry Alfred.



“I save his life,” say Sampson sorrowfully; “but life be not the only

good thing a man may be rob of by those who steal his life-blood, and

so impoverish and water the contint of the vessel of the brain.”



“Doctor Sampson,” say Alfred, “what do you mean by these mysterious

word? you alarm I.”



“what, don't you know? haven't they tell you?”



“no, I have not have the courage to enter the house since the bank----”

 he stop in confusion.



“ay, I understand,” say Sampson: “however, it cann't be hide now:--



“he be a maniac.”





Sampson make this awful announcement soberly and sorrowfully.



Alfred groan aloud, and even his father experience a momentary

remorse; but so steady have be the progress of Corruption, that he feel

almost unmixed joy the next instant; and his keen-witted son surprise

the latter sentiment in his face, and shudder with disgust.



Sampson go on to say that he believe the poor man have gone

flourish a razor; and Mrs. Dodd have say, “yes, kill I, David: kill

the mother of your child,” and never move: which feminine, or in

other word irrational, behaviour have somehow disarm he. but it would

not happen again: his sister have come; a sensible, resolute woman. she

have sign the order, and Osmond and he the certificate, and he be

go to a private asylum. “talk of that,” say Sampson, rise

suddenly, “I must go and give they a word of comfort; for they be just

break their heart at part with he, poor thing. I'll be back in

an hour.”



on his departure, Jane return and make the tea in the dining-room:

they live like that now.



Mr. Hardie take it from his favourite's lithe white hand, and smile

on she: he should not have to go to a foreign land after all: who would

believe a madman if he should rave about his thousand? he sip

his tea luxuriously, and presently deliver himself thus, with bland

self-satisfaction:--



“my dear Alfred, some time ago you wish to marry a young lady without

fortune. you think that I have a large one; and you expect I to

supply all deficiency. you do not overrate my parental feeling, but

you do my mean. I would have do this for you, and with pleasure,

but for my own come misfortune. as it be, I say 'no,' and when you

demand, somewhat peremptorily, my reason, I say 'trust I.' well,

you see I be right: such a marriage would have be your utter ruin.

however, I conclude, after what Dr. Sampson have tell we, you have

resign it on other ground. Jane, my dear, Captain Dodd, I be sorry to

say, be afflict. he have go mad.”



“gone mad?! oh, how shocking! what will become of his poor child?”

 she think of Edward first.



“we have just hear it from Sampson. and I presume, Alfred, you be not

so far go as to insist on propagate insanity by a marriage with his

daughter.”



at this conclusion, which strike she obliquely, though aim at Alfred,

Jane sigh gently, and her dream of earthly happiness seem to melt

away.



but Alfred grind his tooth, and reply with great bitterness and

emotion: “I think, sir, you be the last man who ought to congratulate

yourself on the affliction that have fall on that unhappy family I

aspire to enter, all the more that now they have calamity for I to

share----”



“More fool you,” put in Mr. Hardie calmly.



“--For I much fear you be one of the cause of that calamity.”



Mr. Hardie assume a puzzled air. “I don't see how that can be: do you,

Jenny? Sampson tell we the cause: a wound on the head, a wound in the

arm, bleeding, cupping, &c.”



“there may be other cause Dr. Sampson have not be tell of--yet”



“possibly. I really don't know what you allude to.”



the son fix his eye on the father, and lean across the table to

he, till their face nearly meet.



“the fourteen thousand pound, sir.”











CHAPTER XXV



MR. HARDIE be take by surprise for once, and have not a word to say,

but look in his son's face, mute and gasp as a fish.



during this painful silence his child eye he inquiringly, but not

with the same result; for one face be often read differently by two

person. to Jane, whose intelligence have no aid, he seem unaffectedly

puzzle; but Alfred discern beneath his wonder the terror of detection

rise, and then thrust back by the strong will: that stoical face shut

again like an iron door, but not quickly enough: the right word, the

“open sesame,” have be speak, and one unguarded look have confirm

Alfred's vague suspicion of foul play. he turn his own face away: he

be alienate by the occurrence of the last few month, but nature and

tender reminiscence still hold he by some fibre of the heart--in a

moment of natural indignation he have apply the touchstone, but its

success grieve he. he could not bear to go on expose his father; so

he leave the room with a deep sigh, in which pity mingle with shame and

regret. he wander out into the silent night, and soon be lean

on the gate of Albion Villa, gaze wistfully at the window, and sore

perplexed and nobly wretched.



as he be go out, Mr. Hardie raise his eyebrow with a look of

disintereste wonder and curiosity; and touch his forehead to Jane, as

much as to say, “be he disorder in his mind?”



as soon as they be alone, he ask she coolly what Alfred mean. she

say she have no idea. then he examine she keenly about this fourteen

thousand pound, and find, to his relief, Alfred have never even

mention it to she.



and now Richard Hardie, like his son, want to be alone, and think over

this new peril that have rise in the bosom of his own family, and, for

once, the company of his favourite child be irksome: he make an excuse

and stroll out in his turn into the silent night. it be calm and

clear: the thousand holy eye, under which man prefer to do their

crime--except when they be in too great a hurry to wait--look down

and seem to wonder anything can be so silly as to sin; and beneath

their pure gaze the man of the world ponder with all his soul. he

torment himself with conjecture: through what channel do Alfred

suspect he? through the Dodds? be they aware of their loss? have the

pocket-book speak? if so, why have not Mrs. Dodd or her son attack

he? but then perhaps Alfred be their agent: they wish to try a

friendly remonstrance through a mutual friend before proceed to

extremity; this accord with Mrs. Dodd's character as he remember

she.



the solution be reasonable; but he be relieve of it by recollect

what Alfred have say, that he have not enter the house since the bank

break.



on this he begin to hope Alfred's might be a mere suspicion he could not

establish by any proof; and at all event, he would lock it in his own

breast like a good son: his never having give a hint even to his sister

favour this supposition.



thus meditate, Mr. Hardie find himself at the gate of Albion Villa.



yet he have stroll out with no particular intention of go there.

have his mind, apprehensive of danger from that quarter, drive his body

thither?



he take a look at the house, and the first thing he see be a young lady

lean over the balcony, and murmur softly to a male figure below,

whose outline Mr. Hardie could hardly discern, for it stand in the

shadow. Mr. Hardie be delighted.



“aha, Miss Juliet,” say he, “if Alfred do not visit you, some one

else do. you have soon supply your peevish lover's place.” he then

withdraw softly from the gate, not to disturb the intrigue, and watch

a few yard off; determined to see who Julia's nightly visitor be, and

give Alfred surprise for surprise.



he have not long to wait: the man come away directly, and walk, head

erect, past Mr. Hardie, and glance full in his face, but do not

vouchsafe he a word. it be Alfred himself.



Mr. Hardie be profoundly alarmed and indignant. “the young traitor!

never enter the house? no; but he come and tell she everything

directly under her window on the sly; and, when he be catch--defy I

to my face.” and now he suspect female cunning and malice in the way

that thunderbolt have be quietly prepare for he and launch,

without warn, in his very daughter's presence, and the result just

communicate to Julia Dodd.



in a very gloomy mood he follow his son, and hear his firm though

elastic tread on the frosty ground, and see how loftily he carry his

head; and from that moment fear, and very, very nearly hate he.



the next day he feign sick and send for Osmond. that worthy prescribe

a pill and a draught, the former laxative, the latter astringent. this

ceremony perform, Mr. Hardie gossippe with he; and, after a detour

or two, glide to his real anxiety. “Sampson tell I you know more

about Captain Dodd's case than he do: he be not very clear as to the

cause of the poor man's go mad.”



“the cause? why, apoplexy.”



“yes, but I mean what cause the apoplexy?”



Mr. Osmond reply that apoplexy be often idiopathic.* Captain Dodd,

as he understand, have fall down in the street in a sudden fit: “but as

for the mania, that be to be attribute to an insufficient evacuation of

blood while under the apoplectic coma.”



     * “arise of itself.” a term rather hastily apply to

     disorder the come sign of which have not be detect

     by the medical attendant.



the birth of Topsy be idiopathic--in that learn lady's opinion.----



“not bleed enough! why, Sampson say it be because he be bleed too much.”



Osmond be amuse at this, and repeat that the mania come of not be

bleed enough.



the discussion be turn into an unexpected quarter by the entrance of

Jane Hardie, who come timidly in and say, “oh, Mr. Osmond, I cannot let

you go without tell you how anxious I be about Alfred. he be so thin,

and pale, and depressed.”



“Nonsense, Jane,” say Mr. Hardie; “have we not all cause to be deject

in this house?” but she persist gently that there be more in it than

that; and his headache be bad, and she could not be easy any long

without advice.



“ah! those headache,” say Mr. Osmond, “they always make I uneasy. to

tell the truth, Miss Hardie, I have notice a remarkable change in he,

but I do not like to excite apprehension. and so he mope, do he?

seek solitude, and be taciturn, and deject?”



“yes. but I do not mind that so much as his turn so pale and thin.”



“oh, it be all part of one malady.”



“then you know what be the matter?”



 “I think I do; and yours be a wise and timely anxiety. your brother's be

a very delicate case of hyperaesthetic character; and I should like to

have the advice of a profound physician. let I see, Dr. Wycherley will

be with I to-morrow: may I bring he over as a friend?”



this proposal do not at all suit Mr. Hardie. he put his own

construction on Alfred's pallor and dejection, and be uneasy at the

idea of his be cross-question by a couple of doctor:



“no, no,” say he; “Taff have fancy enough already. I cannot have you

gentleman come here to fill his head with many more.”



“oh, he have fancy, have he?” say Osmond keenly. “my dear sir, we shall

not say one word to _he:_ that might irritate he: but I should like

_you_ to hear a truly learn opinion.”



Jane look so imploringly that Mr. Hardie yield a reluctant assent,

on those term.



so the next day, by appointment, Mr. Osmond introduce his friend

Dr. Wycherley: bland and bald with a fine bead, and a face naturally

intelligent, but cross every now and then by gleam of vacancy; a

man of large reading, and of tact to make it subserve his interest.

a voluminous writer on certain medical subject, he have so saturate

himself with circumlocution, that it distil from his very tongue: he

talk like an article, a Quarterly one; and so gain two advantage:

1st, he rarely irritate a fellow-creature; for if he begin a sentence

hot, what with its length, and what with its windiness, he end it

cool: item, stab by polysyllable be prick by sponge. 2ndly, this

foible earn he the admiration of fool; and that be as invaluable as

they be innumerable.



yet be there in the mother-tongue he despise one gem of a word he

vastly admire: like most Quarterly writer. that charming word, the pet

of the polysyllabic, be “of.”



 he open the matter in a subdued and sympathise tone well calculate

to win a love father, such as Richard Hardie--be not.



“my good friend here inform I, sir, you be so fortunate as to possess

a son of distinguished ability, and who be at present labouring

under some of those precursory indication of incipient disease of the

cerebro-psychical organ, of which I have be, I may say, somewhat

successful in diagnose the symptom. unless I have be misinform,

he have, for a considerable time, experience persistent headache of

a kephalalgic or true cerebral type, and have now advance to the

succeed stage of taciturnity and depression, not* unaccompanie with

isolation, and probably constipation: but as yet without hallucination,

though possibly, and, as my experience of the great majority of these

case would induce I to say, probably he be not** undisturbed by one or

more of those latent, and, at first, trifle aberration, either of the

intelligence or the sense, which in their preliminary stage escape the

observation of all but the expert nosologist.”



     *Anglice, “accompany.”



     **anglice, “disturb.”



“there, you see,” say Osmond, “Dr. Wycherley agree with I: yet I

assure you I have only detail the symptom, and not the conclusion I

have form from they.”



Jane inquire timidly what that conclusion be.



“Miss Hardie, we think it one of those obscure tendency which be very

curable if take in time----” Dr. Wycherley end the sentence: “but no

long remediable if the fleeting opportunity be allow to escape, and

diseased action to pass into diseased organisation.”



Jane look awestruck at their solemnity; but Mr. Hardie, who be take

advice against the grain, turn satirical. “Gentleman,” say he, “be

pleased to begin by moderate your own obscurity; and then perhaps I

shall see well how to cure my son's disorder. what the deuce be you

drive at?”



the two doctor look at one another inquiringly, and so settle how to

proceed. Dr. Wycherley explain to Mr. Hardie that there be a sort of

general unreasonable and superstitious feeling abroad, a kind of terror

of the complaint with which his son be threaten; _“and which,_

instead of the most remediable of disorder, be look at as the

most incurable of malady:” it be on this account he have learn to

approach the subject with singular caution, and even with a timidity

which be kind in appearance than in reality; that he must admit.



“well, you may speak out, as far as I be concern,” say Mr. Hardie,

with consummate indifference.



“oh, yes!” say Jane, in a fever of anxiety; “pray conceal nothing from

we.”



“well, then, sir, I have not as yet have the advantage of examine your

son personally, but, from the diagnostic, I have no doubt whatever

he be labour under the first fore-shadowing of cerebro-psychical

perturbation. to speak plainly, the symptom be characteristic of the

initiatory stage of the germination of a morbid state of the phenomena

of intelligence.”



his unprofessional hearer only stare.



“in one word, then,” say Dr. Wycherley, wax impatient at their

abominable obtuseness, “it be the premonitory stage of the precursory

condition of an organic affection of the brain.”



“oh!” say Mr. Hardie, “the brain!* I see; the boy be go mad.”



     * what a blessing there be a few english word leave in all

     our dialect.



the doctor stare in their turn at the prodigious coolness of a tender

parent. “not exactly,” say Dr. Wycherley; “I be habitually averse to

exaggeration of symptom. your son's suggest to I 'the Incubation of

insanity,' nothing more.”



Jane utter an exclamation of horror; the doctor soothe she with an

assurance that there be no cause for alarm. “incipient aberration”

 be of easy cure: the mischief lie in delay. “Miss Hardie,” say he

paternally, “during a long and busy professional career, it have be

my painful province to witness the deplorable consequence of the

non-recognition, by friend and relative, of the precedent symptom of

those organic affection of the brain, the relief of which be within

the reach of well-know therapeutic agent if exhibit seasonably.”



he go on to deplore the blind prejudice of unprofessional person, who

choose to fancy that other disease creep, but insanity pounce, on a

man; which he express thus neatly: “that other deviation from organic

condition of health be the subject of clearly define though

delicate gradation, but that the bad and most climacteric form of

cerebro-psychical disorder be suddenly develop affection present

no evidence of any antecedent cephalic organic change, and unaccompanie

by a premonitory stage, or by incipient symptom.”



this chimera he proceed to confute by experience: he have repeatedly

be call in to case of mania describe as sudden, and almost

invariably find the patient have be cranky for year; which he

condense thus: “his conduct and behaviour for many year previously to

any symptom of mental aberration be notice, have be characterise

by action quite irreconcilable with the supposition of the existence of

perfect sanity of intellect.”



he instance a parson, whom he have lately attend, and find he as

constipate and as convince he be John the Baptist engage to the

Princess Mary as could be. “but,” continue the learn doctor, “upon

investigation of this afflict ecclesiastic's antecedent history,

I discover that, for year before this, he have exhibit conduct

incompatible with the hypothesis of a mind whose equilibrium have be

undisturbed. he have cause a number of valuable tree to be cut down on

his estate, without be able to offer a sane justification for such

an outrageous proceeding; and have actually dispose of a quantity of his

patrimonial acre, _'and which'_ clearly he never would have part with

have he be in anything resemble a condition of sanity.”



“do he sell the land and timber below the market price?” inquire Mr.

Hardie, perk up, and exhibit his first symptom of interest in the

discussion.



“on that head, sir, my informant, his heir-at-law, give I no

information: nor do I enter into that class of detail. you naturally

look at morbid phenomenon in a commercial spirit, but we regard they

medically--and all this time most assiduously visit the sick of his

parish and preach admirable serious.”



the next instance he give be of a stockbroker suffer under general

paralysis and a rooted idea that all the _specie_ in the Bank of England

be his, and minister in league with foreign government to keep he

out of it. “he,” say the doctor, “I discover to have be for

year guilty of conduct entirely incompatible with the hypothesis

of undisordered mental function. he have accuse his domestic

of peculation, and have initiate legal proceeding with a view of

prosecute in a court of law one of his old friend.”



“whence you infer that, if my son have not for year be do cranky

act, he be not likely to be derange at present.”



this adroit twist of the argument rather surprise Dr. Wycherley.

however, he be at no loss for a reply. “it be not insanity, but the

Incubation of Insanity, which be suspect in your intelligent son's

case: and the good course will be for I to enumerate in general term

the several symptom of 'the Incubation of Insanity:'” he conclude with

some severity. “after that, sir, I shall cease to intrude what I fear be

an unwelcome conviction.”



the parent, whose levity and cold reception of good tiding he have thus

mildly, yet with due dignity, rebuke, be a man of the world, and like

to make friend, not enemy: so he take the hint, and make a very civil

speech, assure Dr. Wycherley that, if he venture to differ from

he, he be none the less oblige by the kind interest he take in a

comparative stranger: and would be very glad to hear all about the

“Incubation of Insanity.”



Dr. Wycherley bow slightly and comply:



“one diagnostic preliminary sign of abnormal cerebral action be

Kephalalgia, or true cerebral headache; I mean persistent headache

not accompany by a furred tongue, or other indicia significant of

abdominal or renal disorder as its origin.”



Jane sigh. “he have sad headache.”



“the succeed symptom be a morbid affection of sleep. either the

patient suffer from Insomnia, or else from Hypersomnia, which we

subdivide into sopor, carus, and lethargus; or thirdly from Kakosomnia,

or a propensity to mere dozing, and to all the morbid phenomena of

dream.”



“Papa,” say Jane, “poor Alfred sleep very badly: I hear he walk at

all hour of the night.”



“I think as much,” say Dr. Wycherley; “Insomnia be the commonest

feature. to resume; the insidious advance of morbid thought be next

mark by high spirit, or else by low spirit; generally the latter.

the patient begin by mope, then show great lassitude and ennui, then

become abstract, moody, and occupy with a solitary idea.”



Jane clasp her hand and the tear stand in her eye; so well do this

description tally with poor Alfred's case.



“and at this period,” continue Dr. Wycherley, “my experience lead I

to believe that some latent delusion be generally germinating in the

mind, though often conceal with consummate craft by the patient: the

open development of this delusion be the next stage, and, with this last

morbid phenomenon, Incubation cease and insanity begin. sometimes,

however, the illusion be physical rather than psychical, of the sense

rather than of the intelligence. it commence at night: the incubator

begin by see nocturnal vision, often of a photopsic* character, or

hear nocturnal sound, neither of which have any material existence,

be convey to his optic or auricular nerve not from without, but

from within, by the agency of a disorder brain. these the reason,

hitherto unimpaired, combat at first, especially when they be

nocturnal only; but be reproduce, and become diurnal, the judgment

succumb under the morbid impression produce so repeatedly. these be

the ordinary antecedent symptom characteristic of the incubation of

insanity; to which be frequently add somatic exaltation, or, in

popular language, physical excitability--a disposition to knit the

brow--great activity of the mental faculty--or else a well-mark

decline of the power of the understanding--an exaggeration of the

normal condition of thought--or a reversal of the mental habit and

sentiment, such as a sudden aversion to some person hitherto belove,

or some study long relish and pursue.”



     * luminous.



Jane ask leave to note these all down in her note-book.



Mr. Hardie assent adroitly; for he be think whether he could

not sift some grain out of all this chaff. should Alfred blab his

suspicion, here be two gentleman who would at all event help he to

throw ridicule on they.



Dr. Wycherley having politely aid Jane Hardie to note down the

“preliminary process of the Incubation of disorder of the Intellect,”

 resume: “now, sir, your son appear to be in a very inchoate stage of

the malady: he have cerebral Kephalalgia and Insomnia----”



“and, oh, doctor,” say Jane, “he knit his brow often and have give up

his study; willn't go back to Oxford this term.”



“exactly; and seek isolation, and be a prey to morbid distraction and

reverie: but have no palpable illusion, have he?”



“not that I know of,” say Mr. Hardie.



“well, but,” object Jane, “do not he say something to you very

curious the other night about Captain Dodd and fourteen thousand

pound?”



Mr. Hardie's blood run cold. “no,” he stammer, “not that I remember.”



“oh, yes, he do, papa: you have forget it: but at the time you be

quite puzzled what he could mean: and you do _so._” she put her finger

to her forehead, and the doctor interchange a meaning glance.



“I believe you be right, Jenny,” say Mr. Hardie, take the cue so

unexpectedly offer he: “he do say some nonsense I could not make

head nor tail of; but we all have our crotchet. there, run away, like

a good girl, and let I explain all this to our good friend here: and

mind, not a word about it to Alfred.”



when she be go, he say, “gentleman, my son be over head and ear in

love; that be all.”



“ay, erotic monomania be a very ordinary phase of insanity,” say Dr.

Wycherley.



“his unreasonable passion for a girl he know he can never marry make

he somewhat crotchety and cranky: that, and over-study, may have

unhinge his mind a little. suppose I send he abroad? my good brother

will find the mean; or we could advance it he, I and the other

trustee; he come into ten thousand pound in a month or two.”



the doctor exchange a meaning look. they then dissuade he earnestly

from the idea of continental travel.



“Coelum non animam mutant qui trans mare currunt,” say Wycherley, and

Osmond explain that Alfred would brood abroad as well as at home, if

he go alone; and Dr. Wycherley sum up thus: “the most advisable

course be to give he the benefit of the personal superintendence of

some skilful physician possess of mean and appliance of every sort

for soothe and restrain the specific malady.”



Mr. Hardie do not at first see the exact purport of this oleaginous

periphrasis. presently he catch a glimpse; but say he think

confinement be hardly the thing to drive away melancholy.



“not in all respect,” reply Dr. Wycherley; “but, on the other hand,

a little gentle restraint be the safe way of effect a disruption of

the fatal association that have engender and tend to perpetuate the

disorder. besides, the medicinal appliance be invaluable, include,

as they do, the nocturnal and diurnal attendance of a psycho-physical

physician, who know the psychosomatic relation of body and mind, and

can apply physical remedy, of the effect of which on the physical

instrument of intelligence, the grey matter of the brain, we have see

so many example.”



the good doctor then feelingly deplore the inhumanity of parent and

guardian in decline to subject their incubator to opportune and

salutary restraint under the more than parental care of a Psychosomatic

physician. on this head he get quite warm, and inveigh against the

abominable _cruelty_ of the thing. “it be contrary,” say he, “to every

principle of justice and humanity, that a fellow-creature, derange

perhaps only on one point, should, for the want of the early attention

of those whose duty it be to watch over he, linger out his existence

separate from all who be dear to he, and condemn without any crime

to be a prisoner for life.”



Mr. Hardie be puzzle by this sentence, in which the speaker's usual

method be reverse, and the thought be big than the word.



“oh,” say he at last, “I see. we ought to incarcerate our child to

keep they from be incarcerate.”



“that be one way of put it with a vengence,” say Mr. Osmond

stare. “no; what my good friend means----”



“be this; where the patient be possessor of an income of such a

character as to enable his friend to show a sincere affection by

anticipate the consequence of neglect morbid phenomenon of

the brain, there a lamentable want of humanity be exhibit by the

persistent refusal to the patient, on the part of his relative, of

the incalculable advantage of the authoritative advice of a competent

physician accompany with the safeguard and preventive of----”



but ere the mellifluous pleonast have do oil his paradox with fresh

polysyllable, to make it slip into the banker's narrow understanding,

he meet with a curious interruption. Jane Hardie flutter in to say a

man be at the door accuse himself of be derange.



“how often this sort of coincidence occur,” say Osmond

philosophically.



“do not refuse he, dear papa; it be not for money: he only want you to

give he an order to go into a lunatic asylum.”



_“now, there be a sensible man,_” say Dr. Wycherley.



“well, but,” object Mr. Hardie, “if he be a sensible man, why do he

want to go to an asylum?”



“oh, they be all sensible at time,” observe Mr. Osmond.



_“singularly so,_” say Dr. Wycherley, warmly. and he show a desire

to examine this paragon, who have the sense to know he be out of his

sense.



“it would be but kind of you, sir,” say Jane; “poor, poor man!” she

add, he do not like to come in, and would they mind just go out to

he?



“oh no, not in the least: especially as you seem interested in he.”



and they all three rise and go out together, and find the petitioner

at the front door. who should it be but James Maxley!



his beard be unshaven, his face haggard, and everything about he

show a man break in spirit as well as fortune: even his voice

have lose half its vigour, and, whenever he have utter a consecutive

sentence or two, his head drop on his breast pitiably: indeed, this

sometimes occur in the middle of a sentence, and then the rest of it

die on his lip.



Mr. Richard Hardie be not prepared to encounter one of his unhappy

creditor thus publicly, and, to shorten the annoyance, would have

dismiss he roughly: but he dare not; for Maxley be no long alone

nor unfriende. when Jane leave he to intercede for he, a young man

join he, and be now comfort he with kind word, and try to

get he to smoke a cigar; and this good-hearted young gentleman be

the banker's son in the flesh, and his opposite in spirit, Mr. Alfred

Hardie.



find these two in contact, the doctor interchange demurest glance.



Mr. Hardie ask Maxley sullenly what he want of they.



“well, sir,” say Maxley despondingly, “I have be to all the other

magistrate in the borough; for what with lose my money, and what with

lose my missus, I think I bain't quite right in my head; I do see such

curious thing, enough to make a body's skin creep at time.” and down

go his head on his chest.



“well?” say Mr. Hardie, peevishly: “go on: you go to the magistrate,

and what then?”



Maxley look up, and seem to recover the thread: “why they say 'no,'

they couldn't send I to the 'sylum, not from home: I must be a pauper

first. so then my neighbour they say I have well come to you.” and

down go his head again.



“well, but,” say Mr. Hardie, “you cannot expect I to go against the

other magistrate.”



“why not, sir? you have have a hatful o' money of I: the other gentleman

han't have a farthing. they owe I no service, but you do: nine

hundred pound' worth, if ye come to that.”



there be no malice in this; it be a plain break-hearted man's notion

of give and take; but it be a home-thrust all the same; and Mr. Hardie

be visibly discountenance, and Alfred more so.



Mr. Osmond, to relieve a situation so painful, ask Maxley rather

hastily what be the curious thing he see.



Maxley shudder. “the unreasonablest beast, sir, you ever see or hear

tell on: mostly snake and dragon. cann't stoop my head to do no work

for they, sir. bless your heart, if I be to leave you gentleman now,

and go and dig for five minute in my garden, they would come about I

as thick as slug on cabbage. why 'twas but yester'en I try to hoe

a bit, and up come the fearfull great fiery sarpint: scare I so I

heave my hoe and lay on un' properly: presently I seem to come out

of a sort of a kind of a red mist into the clear: and there lay my poor

missus's favourite hen; I have be and kill she for a sarpint!” he

sigh, then, after a moment's pause, lower his voice to a whisper:

“now suppose I be to go and take some poor Christian for one of these

gre-at bloody dragon I do see at odd time, I might do he a mischief,

you know, and not mean he no harm neither. oh, dooee take and have I

lock up, gentleman, dooee now: tellee I ben't fit to be about, my poor

head be so mazed.”



“well, well,” say Mr. Hardie, “I'll give you an order for the Union.”



“what, make a pauper of I?”



“I cannot help it,” say the magistrate: “it be the routine; and it be

settle at a meeting of the bench last month that we must adhere to

the rule as strictly as possible; the asylum be so full: and you know,

Maxley, it be not as if you be dangerous.”



“that I be, sir: I don't know what I'm a look at or a doing. would

I ha' go and kill my poor Susan's hen if I haven't a be beside

myself? and she in her grave, poor dear: no, not for untold gold: and I

be fond of that too--use to be, however: but now I don't seem to care

for money nor nothing else.” and his head drop.



“look here, Maxley, old fellow,” say Alfred sarcastically, “you must go

to the workhouse, and stay there till you hoe a pauper; take he for

a crocodile and kill he; then you will get into an asylum whether the

Barkington magistrate like it or not: that be the _routine,_ I believe;

and as reasonable as most routine.”



Dr. Wycherley admire Alfred for this, and whisper Mr. Osmond, “how

subtly they reason.”



Mr. Hardie do not deign to answer his son, who indeed have speak at

he, and not to he.



as for poor Maxley, he be in sad and sober earnest, and could not

relish nor even take in Alfred's irony. he lift his head and look

Mr. Hardie in the face.



“you be a hard man,” say he, tremble with emotion. “you rob I and

my missus of our all; you ha' break her heart, and turn my head, and

if I be to come and kill _you,_ 'twould only be clear score. 'stead

of that, I come to you like a lamb, and say give I your name on a

bit of paper, and put I out of harm's way. 'no,' say you, 'go to the

workhouse!' be _you_ in the workhouse--you that owe I nine hundred

pound and my dead missus?” with this he go into a rage, take a

packet out of his pocket, and fling L. 900 of Mr. Hardie's paper at Mr.

Hardie's head before any one could stop he.



but Alfred see his game, step forward, and catch it with one hand,

and with the dexterity of a wicket-keeper, within a foot of his father's

nose. “how's that, Umpire?” say he: then, a little sternly, “don't do

that again, Mr. Maxley, or I shall have to give you a hiding--to keep up

appearance.” he then put the note in his pocket, and say quietly, _“I_

shall give you your money for these before the year end.”



“you willn't be quite so mad as that, I hope,” remonstrate his father.

but he make no reply: they very seldom answer one another now.



“oh,” say Dr. Wycherley, inspect he like a human curiosity, “nullum

magnum ingenium sine mixtura dementiae.”



“Nec parvum sine mixtura stultitiae,” retort Alfred in a moment and

meet his offensive gaze with a point-blank look of supercilious disdain.



then having shut he up, he turn to Osmond: “come,” say he,

“prescribe for this poor fellow, who ask for a hospital, so Routine

give he a workhouse. come, you know there be no limit to your skill

and good nature: you cure Spot of the worm, cure poor old Maxley of

his snake: oblige I.”



“that I will, Mr. Alfred,” say Osmond heartily: and write a

prescription on a leaf of his memorandum-book, remark that though a

simple purgative, it have make short work of a great many serpent and

dragon, and not a few spectre and hobgoblin into the bargain.



the young gentleman thank he graciously, and say kindly to Maxley,

“get that make up--here's a guinea--and I'll send somebody to see how

you be to-morrow.”



the poor man take the guinea, and the prescription, and his head droop

again, and he slouch away.



Dr. Wycherley remark significantly that his conduct be worth

imitating by _all person similarly situate:_ and conclude oracularly:

“prophylaxis be preferable to therapeusis.”



“or, as _Porson_ would say, 'prevention be well than cure.'”



with this parting blow the Oxonian suddenly saunter away, unconscious,

it seem, of the existence of his companion.



“I never see a plainer case of incubation,” remark Dr. Wycherley with

vast benevolence of manner.



“Maxley's?”



“oh, no; that be parochial. it be your profoundly interesting son I

allude to. do you notice his supercilious departure? _and his morbid

celerity of repartee?_”



Mr. Hardie reply with some little hesitation, “yes; and, excuse I, I

think he have rather the good of the battle with you.”



“indubitably so,” reply Dr. Wycherley: “they always do: at least such

be my experience. if ever I break a lance of wit with an incubator! I

calculate with confidence on be unhorse with abnormal rapidity,

and rare, indeed, be the instance in which my anticipation be

not promptly and fully realise. by a similar rule of progression the

incubator be seldom a match for the confirmed maniac, either in the

light play of sarcasm, the coruscation of wit or the severer encounter

of dialectical ratiocination.”



“dear, dear, dear! then how be one to know a genius from a madman?”

 inquire Jane.



_“by send for a psychological physician._”



“if I understand the doctor right, the two thing be not opposed,”

 remark Mr. Hardie.



Dr. Wycherley assent, and make a remarkable statement in confirmation:

“one half of the aggregate of the genius of the country be at present

under restraint; fortunately for the community; and still more

fortunately for itself.”



he then put on his glove, and, with much kindness but solemnity, warn

Mr. Hardie not to neglect his son's case, nor to suppose that matter

could go on like this without “disintegrate or disorganise the grey

matter of the brain. I admit” say he, “that in some record case of

insanity the brain on dissection have reveal no sign of structural

or functional derangement, and, that, on the other hand, considerable

encephalic disorganisation have be show to have exist in other case

without aberration or impairment of the reason: but such phenomenon be

to be consider as pathological curiosity, with which the empiric

would fain endeavour to disturb the sound general conclusion of

science. the only safe mode of reasoning on matter so delicate

and profound be _a priori:_ and, as it may safely be assume as a

self-evident proposition, that disturb intelligence bear the same

relation to the brain as disorder respiration do to the lung, it

be not logical, reason _a priori,_ to assume the possibility that the

studious or other mental habit of a Kephalalgic, and gifted youth,

can be reverse, and erotic monomania germinate, with all the morbid

phenomena of isolation, dejection of the spirit, and abnormal

exaltation of the power of wit and ratiocination, without some

considerable impairment, derangement, disturbance, or modification, of

the psychical, motorial, and sensorial function of the great cerebral

ganglion. but it would be equally absurd to presuppose that these

several function can be disarrange for month, without more or less

disorganisation of the medullary, or even of the cineritious, matter

of the encephialon. _Therefore_--dissection of your talented son would

doubtless reveal at this moment either steatonatous or atheromatous

deposit in the cerebral blood-vessel, or an encyste abscess, probably

of no very recent origin, or, at the least, considerable inspissation,

and opacity, of the membrane of the encephalon, or more or less pulpy

disorganisation of one or other of the hemisphere of the brain: _good_

morning!!”



“good morning, sir: and a thousand thank for your friemidly interest in

my unhappy boy.”



the psycho-cerebral “take their departure” (psycho-cerebral for “go

away”), and leave Jane Hardie brimful of anxiety. Alfred be not there to

dispose of the tirade in two word “Petitio principii,” and so smoke on;

and, not be an university woman, she could not keep her eye on the

original assumption while follow the series of inference the learn

doctor build so neatly, story by story, on the foundation _of_ the

quicksand _of_ a loose conjecture.*



     * so novice sit at a conjuror see he take a wedding-

     ring, and put it in a little box before a lady; then cross

     the theatre with another little box, and put that before

     another lady: “hey! presto! pass!” in box 2 be discover a

     wedding-ring, which be instantly _assume_ to be _the_ ring:

     on this the green mind be fix, and with this be sham

     business do: Box 1, contain the real ring all the time,

     be overlook: and the confederate, in livery or not, do

     what he like with it; imprison it in an orange--for the

     good of its health.



so poor Argan, when Fleurant enumerate the consequence of his omitting

a single--dose shall I say?--is terrify by the threaten disorder,

which succeed to each other logically enough: all the absurdity be in

the first link of the chain; and from that his mind be diverted.----



“now not a word of this to Alfred,” say Mr. Hardie. “I shall propose

he a little foreign tour, to amuse his mind.”



“yes, but papa, if some serious change be really go on inside his

poor head.”



Mr. Hardie smile sarcastically. “don't you see that if the mind can

wound the brain, the mind can cure it?” then, after a while, he say

parentally, “my child, I must give you a lesson: man of the world use

enthusiast--like those two I have just be draw out--for their

tool; we don't let they make tool of we. Osmond, you know, be jackal

to an asylum in London; Dr. Wycherley, I have hear, keep two or three

such establishment by himself or his agent: blind by self-interest

and that of their clique--what an egotistical world it be, to be

sure!--they would confine a melancholy youth in a gloomy house, among

afflicted person, and give he nothing to do but brood; and so turn

the scale against his reason. but I have my child's interest at heart

more than my own: I shall send he abroad, and so amuse his mind

with fresh object, break off sad association, and restore he to a

brilliant career. I count on you to second I in my little scheme for

his good.”



“that I will, papa.”



“somehow, I don't know why, he be coolish to I.”



“he do not understand you as I do, my own papa.”



“but he be affectionate with you, I think.”



“oh yes, more than ever: trouble have draw we close. papa, in the midst

of our sorrow, how much we have to be thankful for to the Giver of all

good thing!”



“yes, little angel: and you must improve Heaven's goodness by work on

your brother's affection, and persuade he to this continental tour.”



thus appeal to, Jane promise warmly: and the man of the world,

find he have a blind and willing instrument in the one creature he

love, kiss she on the forehead, and tell she to run away, for here

be Mr. Skinner, who no doubt want to speak on business.



Skinner, who have in fact be hold respectfully aloof for some time,

come forward on Jane's retiring, and in a very obsequious tone request

a private interview. Mr. Hardie lead the way into the little dining-room.



they be no soon alone than Skinner leave off fawn, very abruptly;

and put on a rugged resolute manner that be new to he: “I be come for

my commission,” say he sturdily.



Mr. Hardie look an inquiry.



“oh, you don't know what I mean, of course,” say the little clerk

almost brutally: “I've wait, and wait, to see if you would have the

decency, and the gratitude, and the honesty, to offer I a trifle out of

it; but I see I might wait till doom-day before you would ever think of

thinking of anybody but yourself. so now shell out without more word or

I'll blow the gaff” the little wretch raise his voice louder and louder

at every sentence.



“hush! hush! Skinner,” say Mr. Hardie anxiously, “you be under some

delusion. when do ever I decline to recognise your service? I always

intend to make you a present, a handsome present.”



“then why don't ye _do_ it without be force? Come, sir, you cann't

draw the wool over Noah Skinner's eye. I have have you watch, and you

be look towards the U. S., and that be too big a country for I to

hunt you in. I'm not to be trifle with: I'm not to be palaver: give

I a thousand pound of it this moment or I'll blow the whole concern

and you along with it.”



“a thousand pound!”



“now look at that!” shriek Skinner. “serve I right for not say

seven thousand. what right have you to a shilling of it more than I

have? if I have the luck to be a burglar's pal instead of a banker's, I

should have half. give it I this moment, or I'll go to Albion Villa and

have you take up for a thief; as you be.”



“but I haven't get it on I.”



“that's a lie: you carry it where _he_ do; close to your heart: I can

see it bulge: there, Job be a patient man, but his patience go at

last.” with this he run to the window and throw it open.



Hardie entreat he to be calm. “I'll give it you, Skinner,” say he,

“and with pleasure, if you will give I some security that you will not

turn round, as soon as you have get it, and be my enemy.”



“enemy of a gent that pay I a thousand pound? nonsense! why should

I? we be in the same boat: behave like a man, and you know you have

nothing to fear from I: but I will--not--go half in a theft for

nothing: would _you?_ come, how be it to be, peace or war? will you be

content with thirteen thousand pound that don't belong to you, not

a shilling of it, or will you go to jail a felon, and lose it every

penny?”



Mr. Hardie groan aloud, but there be no help for it. Skinner be on

sale: and _must_ be buy.



he take out two note for five hundred pound each, and lay they on the

table, after take their number.



Skinner's eye glisten: “thank you, sir,” say he. he put they in his

pocket. then he say quietly, “now you have take the number, sir; so

I'll trouble you for a line to make I safe against the criminal law.

you be a deep one; you might say I rob you.”



“that be a very unworthy suspicion, Skinner, and a childish one.”



“oh, it be diamond cut diamond. a single line, sir, just to say that in

return for his faithful service, you have give Noah Skinner two note

for L. 500, Nos. 1084 and 85.”



“with all my heart--on your give I a receipt for they.”



it be Skinner's turn to hesitate. after reflect, however, on all the

possible consequence, he see nothing to fear; so he consent.



the business complete, a magic change take place in the little clerk.

“now we be friend again, sir: and I'll give you a piece of advice.
