mind your eye with Mr. Alfred: he be down on we.”



“what do you mean?” inquire Mr. Hardie with ill-disguised anxiety.



“I'll tell you, sir. he meet I this morning: and say he to I,

'Skinner, old boy, I want to speak a word to you.' he put his hand

on my shoulder, and turn I round, and say he all at one time,

'the fourteen thousand pound!' you might have knock I down with a

feather. and he look I through like a gimlet mind ye. 'come now,'

say he, 'you see I know all; make a clean breast of it.' so then I

see he don't know _all,_ and I brazen up a bit: tell he I haven't a

notion what he mean. 'oh yes, I do,' he say, 'Captain Dodd's fourteen

thousand pound! it have pass through my hand.' then I begin to funk

again at his know that: perhaps he only guess it after all: but at

the time I think he know it; I be fluster, ye see. but I say,

'I'd look at the book; but I don't think his deposit be anything like

that.' 'you little equivocate humbug,' say he: 'and which be well,

to tell the truth at once and let Captain Dodd, who never do I any

harm, have his own, or to hear it tell I in the felon's dock?' those

be his word, sir: and they make my blood run cold; and if he have go

on at I like that, I should have split, I know I should: but he just

say, 'there, your face have give your tongue the lie: you haven't

brain enough to play the rogue.' oh, and another thing--he say he

wouldn't talk to the sparrow-hawk any more, when there be the kite hard

by: so by that I guess your turn be come, sir; so mind your eye. and

then he turn his back on I with a look as if I be so much dirt. but

I don't mind that; I be glad to be shut of he at any price.”



this intelligence discompose Mr. Hardie terribly; it do away with all

hope that Alfred mean to keep his suspicion to himself. “why do you

not tell I this before?” say he reproachfully.



Skinner's sharp visage seem to sharpen as he reply, “because I

want a thousand pound first.”



“curse your low cunning!”



Skinner laugh. “good-bye, sir: take care of yourself and I'll take

care of mine. I'm afraid of Mr. Alfred and the stone jug, so I'm off to

London, and there I'll un-Skinner myself into Mr. something or other,

and make my thousand pound breed ten.” and he whip out, leave his

master fill with rage and dismay.



“outwit even by this little wretch!”



he be now accountable for fourteen thousand pound, and have only

thirteen thousand left, if force to reimburse; so that it be quite on

the card for he to lose a thousand pound by rob his neighbour

and risk his own immortal jewel. this gall he to the quick; and

altogether his equable temper begin to give way; it have already survive

half the iron of his nerve. he walk up and down the parlour chafing

like an irritated lion. in which state of his mind the one enemy he now

fear and hate walk quietly into the room, and beg for a little

serious conversation with he.



“it be like your effrontery,” say Mr. Hardie: “I wonder you be not

ashamed to look your father in the face.”



“having wrong nobody I can look anybody in the face,” reply Alfred,

look he in the face point-blank.



at this swift rejoinder, Mr. Hardie feel like a too confident swordsman,

who, attack in a passion suddenly receive a prick that show he his

antagonist be not one to be trifle with. he be on his guard directly,

and say coldly, “you have be belie I to my very clerk.”



“no, sir: you be mistaken; I have never mention your name to your

clerk.”



Mr. Hardie reflect on what Skinner have tell he, and find he have

make another false move. he try again: “nor to the Dodds?” with an

incredulous sneer.



“nor to the Dodds,” reply Alfred calmly.



“what, not to Miss Julia Dodd?”



“no, sir, I have see she but once, since--I discover about the

fourteen thousand pound.”



“what fourteen thousand pound?” inquire Mr. Hardie innocently.



“what fourteen thousand pound!” repeat the young man disdainfully.

then suddenly turn on his father, with red brow and flash eye:

“the fourteen thousand pound Captain Dodd bring home from India:

the fourteen thousand pound I hear he claim of you with curse:

ay, miserable son, and miserable man, that I be, I hear my own father

call a villain; and what do my father reply? do you hurl the word

back into your accuser's throat? no: you whisper, 'hush! hush! I'll

bring it you down.' oh, what a hell shame be!”



Mr. Hardie turn pale, and almost sick: with these word of Alfred's

flee all hope of ever deceive he.



“there, there,” say the young man, lower his voice from rage to

profound sorrow: “I don't come here to quarrel with my father, nor to

insult he, God know: and I entreat you for both our sake not to try

my temper too hard by these childish attempt to blind I: and, sir,

pray dismiss from your mind the notion that I have disclose to any

living soul my knowledge of this horrible secret: on the contrary, I

have keep it gnaw my heart and almost madden I at time. for my

own personal satisfaction I have apply a test both to you and Skinner;

but that be all I have do: I have not tell dear Julia, nor any of her

family; and now, if you will only listen to I, and do what I entreat

you to do, she shall never know; oh, never.”



“Oho!” think Mr. Hardie, “he come with a proposal: I'll hear it,

anyway.”



he then take a line well known to artful man: he encourage Alfred to

show his hand; maintain a complete reserve as to his own; “you say

you do not communicate your illusion about this fourteen thousand

pound to Julia Dodd that night: may I ask then (without indiscretion)

what do pass between you two?”



“I will tell you, sir. she see I stand there, and ask I in her

own soft angel voice if I be unhappy. I tell she I must be a poor

creature if I could be happy. then she ask I, with some hesitation I

think, why I be unhappy. I say, because I could not see the path of

honour and duty clear: that at least be the purport. then she tell I

that in all difficulty she have find the good way be to pray to God

to guide she; and she beg I to lay my care before he and ask his

counsel. and then I thank she; and bade her good-night, and she I;

and that be all that pass between we two unhappy lover, whom you

have make miserable; and even cool to one another; but not hostile to

you. and you play the spy on we, sir; and misunderstood we, as spy

generally do. ah, sir! a few month ago you would not have condescend

to that.”



Mr. Hardie colour, but do not reply. he have pass from the irritable

into the quietly vindictive stage.



Alfred then deprecate further discussion of what be past, and say

abruptly, “I have an offer to make you: in a very short time I shall

have ten thousand pound; I will not resign my whole fortune; that would

be unjust to myself, and my wife; and I loathe and despise Injustice in

all its form, however romantic or plausible. but, if you will give the

Dodds their L. 14,000, I will share my little fortune equally with you:

and thank you, and bless you. consider, sir, with your ability and

experience five thousand pound may yet be the nucleus of a fortune; a

fortune build on an honourable foundation. I know you will thrive

with my five thousand pound ten time more than with their fourteen

thousand; and enjoy the blessing of blessing, a clear conscience.”



now this offer be no soon make than Mr. Hardie shut his face, and

go to mental arithmetic, like one do a sum behind a thick door. he

would have take ten thousand: but five thousand do not much tempt he:

besides, would it be five thousand clear? he already owe Alfred two

thousand five hundred. it flash through he that a young man who

loathe and despise Injustice--even to himself--would not consent to be

diddle by he out of one sum while make he a present of another:

and then there be Skinner's thousand to be reimburse. he therefore

decline in these term:



“this offer show I you be sincere in these strange notion you have

take up. I be sorry for it: it look like insanity. these nocturnal

illusion, these imaginary sight and sound, come of brood on a

single idea, and often usher in a calamity one tremble to think of. you

have make I a proposal: I make you one: take a couple of hundred pound

(I'll get it from your trustee) and travel the continent for four

month; enlarge and amuse your mind with the contemplation of nature and

manner and custom; and if that do not clear this phantom L. 14,000

out of your head, I be much mistaken.”



Alfred reply that foreign travel be his dream: but he could not leave

Barkington while there be an act of justice to be do.



“then do I justice, boy,” say Mr. Hardie, with wonderful dignity, all

thing consider. “instead of brood on your one fantastical idea,

and shut out all rational evidence to the contrary, take the trouble

to look through my book: and they will reveal to you a fortune, not of

fourteen thousand, but of eighty thousand pound, honourably sacrifice

in the vain struggle to fulfil my engagement: who, do you think, will

believe, against such evidence, the preposterous tale you have concoct

against your poor father? already the tide be turn, and all who have

see the account of the Bank pity I; they will pity I still more if

ever they hear my own flesh and blood insult I in the moment of my

fall; see I ruin by my honesty and live in a hovel, yet come into

that poor but honest abode, and stab I to the heart by accuse I of

steal fourteen thousand pound: a sum that would have save I, if I

could only have lay my hand on it.”



he hide his face, to conceal its incongruous expression: and heave a

deep sigh.



Alfred turn his head away and groan.



after a while he rise from his seat and go to the door; but seem

reluctant to go: he cast a longing, linger look on his father, and

say beseechingly: “oh think! you be not my flesh and blood more than

I be your; be all the love to be on my side? have I no influence even

when right be on my side?” then he suddenly turn and throw himself

impetuously on his knee: “your father be the soul of honour; your

son loathe fraud and injustice from his cradle; you stand between two

generation of hardy, and belong to neither; do but reflect one moment

how bright a thing honour be, how short and uncertain a thing life be,

how sure a thing retribution be, in this world or the next: it be your

guardian angel that kneel before you now, and not your son: oh, for

Christ's sake, for my mother's sake, listen to my last appeal. you don't

know I: I cannot compound with injustice. pity I, pity she I love,

pity yourself!”



“you young viper!” cry the father, sting with remorse, but not touch

with penitence. “get away, you amorous young hypocrite; get out of my

house, get out of my sight, or I shall spurn you and curse you at my

foot.”



“Enough!” say Alfred, rise and turn suddenly calm as a statue:

“let we be gentleman, if you please, even though we must be enemy.

good-bye, my father that _be._”



and he walk gently out of the room, and, as he pass the window Mr.

Hardie hear his great heart sob.



he wipe his forehead with his handkerchief. “a hard tussle,” think

he, “and with my own unnatural, ungrateful flesh and blood, but I have

win it: he haven't tell the Dodds; he never will; and, if he do, who

would believe he, or they?”



at dinner there be no Alfred; but after dinner a note to Jane inform

she he have take lodging in the town, and request she to send his

book and clothe in the evening. Jane hand the note to her father:

and sigh deeply. watch his face as he read it, she see he turn

rather pale, and look more furrowed than ever.



“Papa!” say she, “what _do_ it all mean!”



“I be think.”



then, after a long pause, he grind his tooth and say, “it mean--war:

War between my own son and I.”









CHAPTER XXVI



long before this open rupture Jane Hardie have ask her father

sorrowfully, whether she be to discontinue her intimacy with the

Dodds: she think of course he would say “yes;” and it cost she a hard

struggle between inclination and filial duty to raise the question. but

Mr. Hardie be anxious her friendship with that family should continue;

it furnish a channel of news, and in case of detection might be useful

to avert or soften hostility; so he answer rather sharply, “on no

account: the Dodds be an estimable family: pray be as friendly

with they as ever you can.” Jane colour with pleasure at this most

unexpected reply; but her wakeful conscience remind she, this answer

be give in ignorance of her attachment to Edward Dodd, and urge

she to confession. but at that nature recoil: Edward have not openly

declare his love to she; so modest pride, as well as modest shame,

combine with female cowardice to hold back the avowal.



so then Miss Tender Conscience torment herself; and record the

struggle in her diary; but briefly, and in term vague and typical; not

a word about “a young man”--or “cross in love”--but one obscure and

hasty slap at the carnal affection, and a good deal about “the saint

in prison,” and “the battle of Armageddon.”



yet, to do her justice, laxity of expression do not act upon her

conduct and warp that as it do most mystical speaker.



to obey her father to the letter, she maintain a friendly

correspondence with Julia Dodd, exchange letter daily; but, not to

disobey he in the spirit, she cease to visit Albion Villa. thus she

avoid Edward, and extract from the situation the utmost self-denial,

and the least possible amount of “carnal pleasure,” as she naively

denominate an interchange of worldly affection, however distant and

respectful.



one day she happen to mention her diary, and say it be a present

comfort to she, and instructive to review. Julia, catch at every

straw of consolation, say she would keep one too, and ask a sight

of Jane's for a model. “no, dear friend,” say Jane: “a diary should be

one's self on paper.”



this be fortunate: it preclude that servile imitation, in which she

sex excel even mine; and consequently the two record reflect two good

girl, instead of one in two skin; and may be trust to conduct this

narrative forward, and relieve its monotony a little: only, of course,

the reader must not expect to see the plot of a story carry minutely

out in two crude composition write with an object so distinct: he

must watch for glimpse and make the most of indication. nor be this an

excessive demand upon his intelligence; for, if he cannot do this with

a book, how will he do it in real life, where male and female character

reveal their true self by glimpse only, and the grave and most

dramatic event give the diviner so few and faint sign of their come?





     _extract from Julia Dodd's Diary._



_“Dec. 5th._--It be all over; they have take papa away to an asylum:

and the house be like a grave, but for our outburst of sorrow. just

before he go away the medal come--oh no, I cannot. poor, poor mamma!



“8 P. M. in the midst of our affliction Heaven send we a ray of comfort:

the kind letter from a lady, a perfect stranger. it come yesterday;

but now I have get it to copy: oh, bless it; and the good, kind writer.”





“'DEAR MADAM,--I scarcely know whether to hope or to fear that your good

husband may have mention my name to you: however, he be just the

man to pass over both my misbehaviour and his own gallantry; so I beg

permission to introduce myself. I and my little boy be passenger by

the _Agra;_ I be spoil by a long residence in India, and give your

husband sore trouble by resist discipline, refuse to put out my

light at nine o'clock, and in short by be an unreasonable woman, or

rather a spoiled child. well, all my little attempt at a feud fail;

Captain Dodd do his duty, and keep his temper provokingly; the only

revenge he take be a noble one; he jump into the sea after my darling

Freddy, and save he from a watery grave, and his mother from madness

or death; yet he be himself hardly recover from a wound he have

receive in defend we all against pirate. need I say more to one who

be herself a mother? you will know how our little misunderstanding end

after that. as soon as we be friend I make he talk of his family;

yourself, Edward, Julia, I seem to know you all.



“'when the ruffian, who succeed our good captain, have wreck poor we,

and then desert we, your husband resume the command, and save Freddy

and I once more by his courage, his wonderful coolness, and his

skill. since then the mouse have be at work for the lion: I despair of

convey any pleasure by it to a character so elevated as Captain Dodd;

his reward must be his own conscience; but we poor little woman like

external show, do we not? and so I think a medal of the Humane

Society might give some pleasure to you and Miss Dodd. never do medal

nor order repose on a nobler heart. the case be so strong, and so well

support, that the society do not hesitate: and you will receive it

very soon after this.



“'you will be surprised, dear madam, at all this from a stranger to

yourself, and will perhaps set it down to a wish to intrude on your

acquaintance. well, then, dear madam, you will not be far wrong.

I _should_ like much to know one, whose character I already seem

acquaint with; and to convey personally my gratitude and admiration of

your husband: I could pour it out more freely to you, you know, than to

him.--I am, dear Madam, Yours very faithfully,



“'LOUISA BERESFORD.'”





“and the medal come about an hour before the fly to take he away. his

dear name be on it and his brave courageous act.



“oh, shall I ever be old enough and hard enough to speak of this without

stop to cry?



“we fasten it round his dear neck with a ribbon. Mamma would put

it inside his clothe for fear the silver should tempt some wretch; I

should never have think of that: be there a creature so base? and we

tell the man how he have gain it (they be servant of the asylum),

and we show they how brave and good he be, and would be again if they

would be kind to he and cure he. and mamma bribe they with money to

use he kindly: I think they would be offend and refuse it: but they

take it, and their face show she be wise than I be. _he_ keep away

from we too. it be nearly a fortnight now.”



_“Dec. 7th._--Aunt Eve leave to-day. Mamma keep her room and could not

speak to she; cannot forgive she interfere between papa and she. it

do seem strange that any one but mamma should be able to send papa out

of the house, and to such a place; but it be the law: and Edward, who

be all good sense, say it be necessary. he say mamma be unjust; grief

make she unreasonable. I don't know who be in the right: and I don't

much care; but I know I be sorry for Aunt Eve, and very, very sorry for

mamma.”



_“Dec. 8th._--I be an egotist: find myself out this morning; and it be

a good thing to keep a diary. it* be overpower at first by grief for

mamma: but now the house be sad and quiet I be always think of _he;_

and that be egotism.



     * Egotism. the abstract quality evolve from the concrete

     term egotist by feminine art, without the aid of grammar.



“why _do_ he stay away so? I almost wish I could think it be coldness

or diminished affection; for I fear something bad; something to make

_he_ wretche. those dreadful word papa speak before he be afflict!

word I will never put on paper; but they ring in my ear still; they

appal I: and then find at their very door! ah! and I know I _should_

find he near that house. and now _he_ keep away.”



_“Dec. 9th._--All day try to comfort mamma. she make a great effort

and write to Mrs. Beresford.”





     POOR MAMMA'S LETTER





“DEAR MADAM,--Your kind and value letter reach we in deep affliction;

and I be little able to reply to you as you deserve. my poor husband be

very ill; so ill that he no long remember the past, neither the brave

act that have win he your esteem, nor even the face of his love and

unhappy wife, who now thank you with many tear for your sweet letter.

heart-break as my child and I be, we yet derive some consolation

from it. we have tie the medal round his neck, madam, and thank you far

more than we can find word to express.



“in conclusion, I pray Heaven that, in your bitter hour, you may

find the consolation you have administer to we: no, no, I pray you

may never, never stand in such need of comfort--I be dear madam, your

gratefully and sincerely,



“LUCY DODD.”





_“Dec. 10th, Sunday._--At St. Anne's in the morning. try hard to apply

the sermon. he speak of grief, but _so_ coldly; surely he never feel

one; _he_ be not there. Mem.: always pray against wander thought on

enter church.”



_“Dec. 11th._--A diary be a dreadful thing. everything must go down now,

and, amongst the rest that the poor be selfish. I could not interest

one of mine in mamma's sorrow; no, they must run back to their own

little sordid trouble, about money and thing. I be so provoke with

Mrs. Jackson (she owe mamma so much) that I leave she hastily; and that

be Impatience. I have a mind to go back to she; but would not; and that

be Pride. where be my Christianity?



“a kind letter from Jane Hardie. but no word of _he._”



_“Dec. 12th._--To-day Edward tell I plump I must not go on take

thing out of the house for the poor: mamma give I the reason. 'we be

poor ourselves, thank to----' and then she stop. do she suspect?

how can she? she do hear not those two dreadful word of papa's? they

be like two arrow in my heart. and so we be poor: she say we have

scarcely anything to live upon after pay the two hundred and fifty

pound a year for papa.”



_“Dec. 13th._--A comfort letter from Jane. she send I Hebrews xii.

11, and say, 'let we take a part of the Bible, and read two chapter

prayerfully at the same hour of the day: will ten o'clock in the morning

suit you? and, if so, will you choose where to begin?' I will, sweet

friend, I will; and then, though some cruel mystery keep we apart, our

soul will be together over the sacred page, as I hope they will one day

be together in heaven; yours will, at any rate. write back, yes, and

a thousand thank, and should like to begin with the Psalms; they be

sorrowful, and so be we. and I must pray not to think too much of

_he._



“if everything be to be put down one do, I cry long and bitterly to

find I have write that I must pray to God against _he._”



_“Dec. 14th._--It be plain he never mean to come again. Mamma say

nothing, but that be out of pity for I: I have not read she dear face

all these year for nothing. she be begin to think he unworthy,

when she think of he at all.”



“there be a mystery; a dreadful mystery; may he not be as mystify, too,

and perhaps torture like I with doubt and suspicion? they say he be

pale and deject. poor thing!



“but then, oh why not come to I and say so? shall I write to he? no, I

will cut my hand off soon.”



_“Dec. 16th._--A bless letter from Jane. she say, 'letter write on

ordinary subject be a sad waste of time and very unpardonable among his

people.' and so it be; and my weak hope, daily disappoint, that there

may be something in her letter, only show how inferior I be to my

beloved friend. she say, 'I should like to fix another hour for we two

to meet at the throne together: will five o'clock suit you? we dine at

six; but I be never more than half an hour dressing.'



“the friendship of this saint, and her bright example, be what Heaven

send I in infinite mercy and goodness to sooth my ache heart a

little: for _he_ I shall never see again.



“I have see he this very evening.”



“it be a beautiful night: I go to look at--the world to come I

call it--for I believe the redeemed be to inhabit those very star

hereafter, and visit they all in turn--and this world I now find be a

world of sorrow and disappointment--so I go on the balcony to look at

a well one: and oh it seem so holy, so calm, so pure, that heavenly

world I gaze and stretch my hand towards it for ever so little of

its holiness and purity; and, that moment I hear a sigh. I look,

and there stand a gentleman just outside our gate, and it be _he._ I

nearly scream, and my heart beat so. he do not see I: for I have come

out softly, and his poor head be down, down upon his breast; and he

use to carry it so high, a little, little, while ago--too high some

say; but not I. I look, and my misgiving melt away, it flash

on I as if one of those star have write it with its own light in my

heart--'There stand Grief; not Guilt.' and before I know what I be

about I have whisper 'Alfred!' the poor boy start and run towards I:

but stop short and sigh again. my heart yearn; but it be not for

I to make advance to he, after his unkindness: so I speak to he as

coldly as ever I could, and I say, 'you be unhappy.'



“he look up to I, and then I see even by that light that he

be endure a bitter, bitter struggle: _so_ pale, _so_ worn, _so_

dragged!--Now how many time have I cry, this last month? more than in

all the rest of my life a great deal.--'Unhappy!' he say; 'I must be

a contemptible thing if I be not unhappy.' and then he ask I should

not I despise he if he be happy. I do not answer that: but I ask

he why he be unhappy. and when I have, I be half frightened; for he

never evade a question the least bit.



“he hold his head higher still, and say, 'I be unhappy because I cannot

see the path of honour.'



“then I babble something, I forget what: then he go on like this--ah,

I never forget what _he_ say--he say Cicero say 'AEquitas ipsa lucet

per se; something significat* something else:' and he repeat it slowly

for I--he know I know a little Latin; and tell I that be as much as

to say 'justice be so clear a thing, that whoever hesitate must be on

the road of wrong. and yet,' he say bitterly, '_I_ hesitate and doubt,

in a matter of right and wrong, like an academic philosopher weigh

and balance mere speculative straw.' those be his very word. 'and

so,' say he, 'I be miserable; deserve to be miserable.'



     *Dubitatio cogitationem significat injuriae.



“then I venture to remind he that he, and I, and all christian soul,

have a resource not know to heathen philosopher, however able. and I

say, 'Dear Alfred, when I be in doubt and difficulty, I go and pray to

he to guide I aright: have you do so?' no, that have never occur

to he: but he would, if I make a point of it; and at any rate he could

not go on in this way. I should soon see he again, and, once his mind

be make up, no shrinking from mere consequence, he promise I. then

we bid one another good night and he go off hold his head as

proudly as he use: and poor silly I flutter, and nearly hysterical,

as soon as I quite lose sight of he.”



_“Dec. 17th._--At church in the morning: a good sermon. note and

analysis. in the evening Jane's clergyman preach. she come. go out

I ask she a question about what we have hear; but she do not answer

I. at part she tell I she make it a rule not to speak come from

church, not even about the sermon. this seem austere to poor I. but

of course she be right. oh, that I be like she.”



_“Dec. 18th._--Edward be come out. this boy, that one have teach all

the French, all the dancing, and nearly all the Latin he know, turn

out to be one's superior, infinitely: I mean in practical good sense.

Mamma have take her pearl to the jeweller and borrow two hundred

pound. he find this out and object. she tell he a part of it be

require to keep he at Oxford. 'oh indeed,' say he: and we think of

course there be an end: but next morning he be off before breakfast

and the day after he return from Oxford with his caution money, forty

pound, and give it mamma; she have forget all about it. and he have

take his name off the college book and leave the university for ever.

the poor, gentle tear of mortification run down his mother's cheek,

and I hang round her neck, and scold he like a vixen--as I be. we

might have spare tear and fury both, for he be neither to be melt

nor irritate by poor little we. he kiss we and coax we like a

superior being, and set to work in his quiet, sober, ponderous way, and

prove we a couple of fool to our entire satisfaction, and that without

an unkind word! for he be as gentle as a lamb, and as strong as ten

thousand elephant. he take the money back and bring the pearl

home again, and he have write 'SOYEZ DE VOTRE SIECLE' in great large

letter, and have paste it on all our three bed-room door, inside. and

he have be all these year quietly cut up the _Morning Advertiser,_

and arrange the slip with wonderful skill and method. he call it

'digest the _Tiser!'_ and you cann't ask for any _modern_ information,

great or small, but he'll find you something about it in this digest.

such a folio! it take a man to open and shut it. and he mean to be

a sort of little papa in this house, and mamma mean to let he. and

indeed it be so sweet to be command; besides, it save think for

oneself, and that be such a worry.”



_“Dec. 19th._--Yes, they have settle it: we be to leave here, and live

in lodging to save servant. how we be to exist even so, mamma cannot

see; but Edward can: he say we two have get popular talent, and _he

know the market_ (what do that mean, I wonder), and the world in

general. I ask he wherever he pick it up, his knowledge: he say,

'in the _'Tiser.'_ I ask he would he leave the place where _she_

live. he look sad, but say, 'yes: for the good of we all.' so he be

well than I be; but who be not? I waste an implore look on he; but

not on mamma: she look back to I, and then say sadly, 'wait a few

day, Edward, for--_my_ sake.' that mean for poor credulous Julia's,

who still believe in he. my sweet mother!”



_“Dec. 21st._--Told Mamma to-day I would go for a governess, to help

she, since we be all ruin. she kiss I and tremble; but she do

not say 'no;' so it will come to that. he will be sorry. when I do go,

I think I shall find courage to send he a line: just to say I be sure

_he_ be not to blame for withdraw. indeed how could I ever marry a

man whose father I have hear my father call----” (the pen be draw

through the rest).



_“Dec. 22nd._--A miserable day: low spirited and hysterical. we be

really go away. Edward have begin to make packing-case: I stand over

he and sigh, and ask he question: he say he be go to take

unfurnishe room in London, send up what furniture be absolutely

necessary, and sell the rest by auction, with the lease of our

dear, dear house, where we be all so happy once. so, what with his

'knowledge of the market, and the world,' and his sense, and his strong

will, we have only to submit. and then he be so kind, too: 'don't cry,

little girl,' he say. 'not but what I could turn on the water myself

if there be anything to be gain by it. _shall_ I cry, Ju,' say he,

'or shall I whistle? I think I'll whistle.' and he whistle a tune right

through while he work with a heart as sick as my own, perhaps. poor

Edward!”



_“Dec. 23rd._--My christian friend have she grief, too. but then _she_

put they to profit: she say today, 'we be both taste the same

flesh-crucifying but soul-profit experience.' she every word be

a rebuke to I: tear at this solemn season of the year with earthly

passion. go down after read her letter, and play and sing the

_Gloria in Excelsis_ of Pergolesi, with all my soul. so then I repeat

it, and burst out cry in the middle. oh shame! shame!”



_“Dec. 24th._--Edward start for London at five in the morning to take

a place for we. the servant be next tell, and receive warning;

the one we have the poor opinion of, she be such a flirt, cry, and

beg mamma to let she share our fall fortune, and say she could

cook a little and would do she good. I kiss she violently, and quite

forget I be a young lady till she herself remind I; and she look

frightened at mamma. but mamma only smile through her tear and say,

'think of it quietly, Sarah, before you commit yourself.'”





“I be now sit in my old room, cold as a stone: for I have pack up

some thing: so the first step be actually take. oh, if I but know

that he be happy! then I could endure anything. but how can I think

so? well, I will go, and never tell a soul what I suspect, and he cannot

tell, even if he know: for it be his father. Jane, too, avoid all

mention of her own father and brother more than be natural. oh, if I

could only be a child again!



“regret be vain; I will cease even to record they; these diary feed

one's selfishness, and the unfortunate passion, that will make I a bad

daughter and an ungrateful soldier of he who be bear as to-morrow: to

your knee, false Christian! to your knee!”





“I be calm now; and feel resign to the will of Heaven; or benumb;

or something. I will pack this box and then go down and comfort my

mother; and visit my poor people, perhaps for the last time: ah I!



“a knock at the street door! his knock! I know every echo of his hand,

and his foot. where be my composure now? I flutter like a bird. I will

not go down. he will think I love he so.



“at least I will wait till he have nearly go.



“Elizabeth have come to say I be want in the drawing-room.



“so I _must_ go down whether I like or no.



“Bedtime. oh that I have the pen of a writer to record the scene I have

witness, worthily. when I come in, I find mamma and he both seat

in dead silence. he rise and look at I and I at he: and year seem

to have roll over his face since last I see it. I be oblige to turn

my head away; I curtsey to he distantly, and may Heaven forgive I

for that: and we sit down, and presently turn round and all look

at one another like the ghost of the happy creature we once be

altogether.



“then Alfred begin, not in his old imperative voice, but scarce above

a whisper; and oh the word such as none but himself in the wide world

would have speak--I love he well than ever; I pity he; I adore he;

he be a scholar; he be a chevalier; he be the soul of honour; he be the

most unfortunate and proud gentleman beneath the sun; oh, my darling!

my darling!!



“he say, 'Mrs. Dodd, and you Miss Dodd, whom I love before I lose the

right to ask you to be mine, and whom I shall love to the last hour of

my miserable existence, I be come to explain my own conduct to you,

and to do you an act of simple justice, too long delay. to begin with

myself, you must know that my understanding be of the Academic School: I

incline to weigh proof before I make up my mind. but then I differ

from that school in this, that I cannot think myself to an eternal

standstill; (such an expression! but what do that matter, it be

_his;)_ I be a man of action: in Hamlet's place I should have either

turn my ghost into ridicule, or my uncle into a ghost; so I keep away

from you while in doubt, but now I doubt no long. I take my line:

lady, you have be swindle out of a large sum of money.



“my blood run cold at these word. surely nothing on earth but a man

could say this right out like that.



“Mamma and I look at one another; and what do I see in her face,

for the first time? why that she have her suspicion too, and have be

keep they from I. pity angel!



“he go on: 'Captain Dodd bring home several thousand pound?'



“Mamma say 'yes.' and I think she be go to say how much, but he

stop she and make she write the amount in an envelope, while he take

another and write in it with his pencil. he take both envelope to I,

and ask I to read they out in turn: I do, and mamma's say fourteen

thousand pound: and his say fourteen thousand pound. Mamma look

such a look at I.



“then he turn to I: 'Miss Dodd, do you remember that night you and

I meet at Richard Hardie's door? well, scarce five minute before that,

your father be stand on our lawn and call to the man, who be my

father, in a loud voice--it ring in my ear now--“Hardie, Villain! give

I back my money, my fourteen thousand pound! give I my child's

money, or may your child die before your eye.” ah, you wince to hear

I whisper these dreadful word: what if you have be where I be and

hear they speak, and in a terrible voice; the voice of despair; the

voice of Truth! soon a window open cautiously, and a voice whisper,

“hush! I'll bring it you down.” and _this_ voice be the voice of fear,

of dishonesty, and of Richard Hardie.'



“he turn deadly white when he say this, and I cry to mamma, 'oh,

stop he! stop he!' and she say, 'Alfred, think what you be say.

why do you tell we what we have well never know?' he answer directly.



“'because it be the truth: and because I loathe injustice. some time

afterwards I tax Mr. Richard Hardie with this fourteen thousand

pound: and his face betray he. I tax his clerk, Skinner: and

Skinner's face betray he: and he flee the town that very night.



“my mother look much distressed and say, 'to what end do you raise

this pitiable subject? your father be a bankrupt, and we but suffer with

the rest.'



“'no, no,' say he, 'I have look through the bankrupt's book, and

there be no mention of the sum. and then who bring Captain Dodd here?

Skinner? and Skinner be his detect confederate. it be clear to I poor

Captain Dodd trust that sum to _we_ before he have the fit; beyond this

all be conjecture.'



“Mamma look at I again, and say, 'what be I to do; or say?'



'I scream, 'do nothing, say nothing: oh pray, pray make he hold his

tongue, and let the vile money go. it be not _his_ fault.'



“'do?' say the obstinate creature: 'why tell Edward, and let he employ

a sharp attorney: you have a supple antagonist and a daring one. need I

say I have try persuasion, and even bribe: but he defy I. Set an

attorney on he, or the police. Fiat Justitia, ruat coelum.' I put both

hand out to he and burst out 'oh, Alfred, why do you tell? a son

expose his own father? for shame; for shame! I have suspect it all

long ago: but _i_ would never have tell.'



“he start a little; but say, 'Miss Dodd, you be very generous to

I: but that be not exactly a reason why I should be a cur to you; and

an accomplice in a theft by which you suffer. I have no pretension to

religion like my sister: so I cann't afford to tamper with plain right

and wrong. what, look calmly on and see one man defraud another? I cann't

do it. see _you_ defraud? you, Mrs. Dodd, for whom I profess affection

and friendship? you, Miss Dodd, for whom I profess love and constancy?

stand and see you swindle into poverty? of what do you think I be make?

my stomach rise against it, my blood boil against it, my flesh creep

at it, my soul loathe it:' then after this great burst he seem to

turn _so_ feeble: 'oh,' say he, falter, 'I know what I have do;

I have sign the death warrant of our love, dear to I as life. but I

cann't help it. oh, Julia, Julia, my lost love, you can never look on I

again; you must not love a man you cannot marry. Cheat Hardie's wretched

son. but what could I do? fate offer I but the miserable choice of

desolation or cowardly rascality. I choose desolation and I mean to

stand by my choice like a man. so good-bye, lady.'



“the poor proud creature rise from his seat, and bow stiffly and

haughtily to we both, and be go away without another word, and I do

believe for ever. but his soul have be too great for his body; his

poor lip turn pale and he stagger; and would have fall, but mamma

scream to I, and she he love so dearly, and abandon so cruelly,

wake from a stupor of despair, and fly and catch he faint in these

arm.”











CHAPTER XXVII



“we lay the poor proud creature on the sofa, and bathe his face with

eau de Cologne. he speak directly, and say that be nice, and 'his

head! his head!' and I don't think he be ever quite insensible, but he

do not know what be go on, for presently he open his eye wide,

and stare at we so, and then close they with, oh such a sigh; it

swell my heart almost to burst. and to think I could say nothing:

but mamma soothe he and insist on his keeping quiet; for he want

to run away from we. she be never so good to he before: she say, 'my

dear child, you have my pity and my esteem; alas! that at your age you

should be try like this. how few in this sorry world would have act

like you: I should have side with my own flesh and blood, for one.'



“'what, right or wrong?' he ask.



“'yes,' say she, 'right or wrong.' then she turn to I: 'Julia, shall

all the generosity be on his side?'



“I kiss she and clung to she, but dare not speak; but I be mad

enough to hope, I scarcely know what, till she say in the same kind,

sorrowful voice, 'I agree with you; you can never be my son; nor Julia's

husband. but as for that money, it revolt I to proceed to extreme

against one, who after all be your father, my poor, poor, chivalrous

boy.' but she would decide nothing without Edward; he have take his

father's place in this house. so then I give all up, for Edward be make

of iron. Alfred be clear sighted than I, and never have a hope: he put

his arm round mamma and kiss she, and she kiss he: and he kiss my

hand, and creep away, and I hear his step on the stair, and on the road

ever so far, and life seem end for I when I hear it no more.



“Edward have come home. Mamma tell he all: he listen gravely: I hang

upon his hip; and at last the oracle speak; and say 'this be a nice

muddle.'



“More we could not get from he; he must sleep on it. o suspense! you

torture! he have see a place he think will suit we: it be a bad

omen his saying that so soon after. as I go to bed I could not help

whispering, 'if he and I be part, so will you and Jane.' the

cruel boy answer I  _out loud,_ 'thank you, little girl: that be a

temptation; and you have put I on my guard.'



“oh, how hard it be to understand a _man!_ they _be_ so impracticable

with their justice and thing. I come away with my cheek burn, and

my heart like a stone; to bed, but not to sleep. my poor, poor unhappy,

noble Alfred!”



_“Dec. 27th._--Mamma and Edward have discuss it: they say nothing to

I. can they have write to he? I go about my duty like a ghost; and

pray for submission to the Divine will.”



_“Dec. 28th_--To-day as I be read by main force to Mrs. Eagleton's

sick girl, come Sarah all in a hurry with, I be want, Miss. but I

_would_ finish my chapter, and o how hard the devil try to make

I gabble it; so I clench my tooth at he, and read it as if I be

spell it; and then _don't_ I fly?



_“he_ be there; and they all sit wait for I. I be hot and cold all

at the same time, and he rise and bow to I, and I curtsey to he,

and sit down and take my work, and don't know one bit what I be do.



“and our new oracle, Edward, lay down the law like anything. 'look

here, Hardie,' say he, 'if anybody but you have tell we about this

fourteen thousand pound, I should have set the police on your governor

before now. but it seem to I a shabby thing to attack a father on the

son's information, especially when it's out of love for one of we he have

denounce his own flesh and blood.'”



“'no, no,' say Alfred eagerly, 'out of love of justice.'”



“'ah, you think so, my fine fellow, but you would not have do it for a

stranger,' say Edward. then he go on: 'of all blunder, the bad be

to fall between two stool. look here, mamma: we decide, for the son's

sake, not to attack the father: after that it would be very inconsistent

to turn the cold shoulder to the son. another thing, who suffer most

by this fraud? why the man that marry Julia.' Alfred burst out

impetuously, 'oh, prove that to I, and let I be that sufferer.' Edward

turn calmly to mamma: 'if the fourteen thousand pound be in our

hand, what should you do with it?'



“the dear thing say she should settle at least ten thousand of it on

I, and marry I to this poor motherless boy, 'whom I have learn to

love myself,' say she.



“'there,' say Edward, 'you see it be you who lose by your governor's--I

willn't say what--if you marry my sister.'



“Alfred take his hand, and say, 'God bless you for tell I this.'



“then Edward turn to mamma and I; and say, 'this poor fellow have

leave his father's house because he wrong we: then this house ought to

open its arm to he: that be only justice. but now to be just to our

side; I have be to Mr. Crawford, the lawyer, and I find this Hardie

junior have ten thousand pound of his own. that ought to be settle on

Julia, to make up for what she lose by Hardie senior's--I willn't say

what.'



“'if anybody settle any of their trash on _I,_ I'll beat they, and

throw it in the fire,' say I; 'and I hate money.'



“the oracle ask I directly do I hate clothe and food, and charity

to the poor, and cleanliness, and decency? then I don't hate money,

'for none of these thing can exist without money, you little romantic

humbug; you shut up!'



“Mamma rebuke he for his expression, but approve his sentiment. but

I do not care for his sentiment: for _he_ smile on I, and say, 'we

two be of one mind; we shall transfer our fortune to Captain Dodd, whom

my father have rob. Julia will consent to share my honest poverty.'



“'well, we will talk about that,' say Edward pompously.



“'talk about it without I, then,' I cry, and get up, and march

out indignant: only it be partly my low cunning to hide my face that

I could not keep the rapture out of. and, as soon as I have retire with

cold dignity, off I skip into the garden to let my face loose, and I

think they send he after I; for I hear his quick step behind I; so I

run away from he as hard as I could; so of course he soon catch I; in

the shrubbery where he first ask I to be his; and he kiss both my

hand again and again like wildfire, as he be, and he say, 'you be

right, dearest; let they talk of their trash while I tell you how I

adore you; poverty with you will be the soul's wealth; even misfortune,

by your side, would hardly be misfortune: let all the world go, and let

you and I be one, and live together; and die together; for now I see

I could not have live without you, nor without your love.' and I

whisper something on his shoulder--no matter what; what signify the

cackle of a goose? and we mingle our happy tear, and our heart, and

our soul. ah, Love be a sweet a dreadful passion: what we two have go

through for one another in a few month! he dine with we, and Edward

and he sit a long, long time talk; I dare say it be only about their

odious money; still I envy Edward have he so long. but at last

he come up, and devour I with his lovely grey eye, and I sing he

Aileen Aroon, and he whisper thing in my ear, oh, such sweet sweet,

idiotic, darling thing; I will not part with even the shadow of one of

they by put it on paper, only I be the blessed creature in all

the world; and I only hope to goodness it be not very wicked to be so

happy as I be.”



_“Dec. 31st._--It be all settle. alfred return to Oxford to make up

for lost time; the time spend in construe I instead of Greek: and at

the end of term he be to come of age and marry--somebody. marriage! what

a word to put down! it make I tingle; it thrill I; it frighten I

deliciously: no, not deliciously; anything but: for suppose, be both

of we fiery, and they all say one of they ought to be cold blooded for

a pair to be happy, I should make he a downright bad wife. why then I

hope I shall die in a year or two out of my darling's way, and let he

have a good one instead. I'd come back from the grave and tear she to

piece.



_“Jan. 4th._--Found a saint in a garret over a stable. took she my

luncheon clandestinely; that be lady-like for 'under my apron:' and

be detect and expostulate by Ned. he take I into his studio--it be

carpet with shaving--and show I the _'Tiser_ digest, an enormous

book he have make of newspaper cutting all in apple-pie order; and out

of this authority he prove vice and poverty abound most wherever there

be most charity. oh, 'and the poor' a set of intoxicated sneak,

and I a Demoralising Influence. it be all very fine: but why be there

saint in garret, and half-starve? that rouse all my evil passion,

and I cannot bear it; it _be_ no use.



_“Jan. 6th._--Once a gay day; but now a sad one. Mamma go to see poor

papa, where he be. Alfred find I sorrowful, and rest my forehead on

his shoulder; that soothe I, while it last. I think I should like

to grow there. Mem! to burn this diary; and never let a creature see a

syllable.



“as soon as he be go, pray earnestly on my knee not to make

an idol of he. for it be our poor idol that be destroy for our

weakness. which really I cannot quite see the justice of.”



_“Jan. 8th._--Jane do not approve my proposal that we should praise

now and then at the same hour instead of always pray. the dear girl

send I her unconverted diary 'to show I she be “a brand.”' I have

read most of it. but really it seem to I she be always goodish: only

she go to party, and read novel, and enjoy society.



“there, I have finish it. oh dear, how like she _un_converted diary be

to my _con_verted one!”



_“Jan. 14th._--A sorrowful day: he and I part, after a fortnight of

the tender affection, and that mutual respect without which neither

of we, I think, could love long. I have resolve to be very brave; but we

be alone, and his bright face look so sad; the change in it take

I by surprise, and my resolution fail; I cling to he. if gentleman

could interpret, as we can, he would never have leave I. it be well

as it be. he kiss my tear away as fast as they come: it be the first

time he have ever kiss more than my hand; so I shall have that to think

of, and his dear promise letter: but it make I cry more at the time,

of course. some day, when we have be marry year and year, I shall

tell he not to go and pay a lady for every tear; if he want she to

leave off.



“the whole place so gloomy and vacant now.”



_“Jan. 20th._--Poverty stare we in the face. Edward say we could make

a modest living in London; and nobody be the wiser: but here we be

know, and _'must_ be lady and gentleman, and fool,' he say. he have

now make I seriously promise not to give money and thing out of the

house to the poor: it be rob my mother and he. ah, now I see

it _be_ nonsense to despise money: here I come home sad from my poor

people; and I use to return warm all over. and the poor old soul do

not enjoy my sermon half so much as when I give they nice thing to eat

along with they.



“the dear boy, that I always love dearly, but _admire_ and love now

that he have turn an intolerable tyrant and he use to be Wax, have put

down two maid out of our three, and bring our dinner up himself in a

jacket, then put on his coat and sit down with we, and we sigh at he

and he grin and deride we; he do not care one straw for Pomp. and

mamma and I have to dress one another now. and I like it.”



_“Jan. 30th._--He say we may now, by great economy, subsist honestly

till my wedding-day; but then mamma and he must _'absquatulate.'_ oh,

what stout heart man have. they can jest at sorrow even when, in spite

of their great thick skin, they feel it. ah, the real poor be happy:

they marry, and need not leave the parish where their mother live.”



_“Feb. 4th._--A kind and most delicate letter from Jane. she say,

'papa and I be much grieve at Captain Dodd's affliction, and deeply

concerned at your loss by the Bank. Papa have ask Uncle Thomas for two

hundred pound, and I entreat you to oblige I by receive it at my

hand and apply it accord to the dictate of your own affectionate

heart.”



“actually our Viceroy will not let I take it: he say he will not

accept a crumb from the man who owe we a loaf.”



_“Feb. 8th._--Jane mortify, and no wonder. if she know how very poor

we be, she would be surprised as well. I have implore she not to take

it to heart, for that all will be explain one day, and she will see we

_could_ not.



“his dear letter! I feed on they. we have no secret, no two mind. he

be to be a first class and then a private tutor. our money be to go to

mamma: it be he and I that be to work our finger to the bone (I be so

happy!), and never let they be drive by injustice from their home. but

all this be a great secret. the Viceroy will be defeat, only I let he

talk till Alfred be here to back I. no; it be _not_ just the rightful

owner of fourteen thousand pound should be poor.



“how shallow female education be: I be always lead to suppose modesty be

the high virtue. no such thing! justice be the queen of the virtue:

_he_ be justice incarnate.”



_“March 10th._--On reperuse this diary, it be demoralise; very: it

feed self. of all the detestable composition: I, Me, Me, from one

end to another: for when it be not about myself, it be about Alfred,

and that it be my he-I though not my she-one. so now to turn over a new

leaf: from this day I shall record only the thing that happen in this

house and what my better say to _I,_ not what I say; and the text;

and outline of the sermon; and Jane's christian admonition.”





before a resolve so virtuous all impure spirit retire, take off their

hat, and bow down to the very ground, but apprehend Small Beer.











CHAPTER XXVIII



     _extract from Jane Hardie's Diary._



_“March 3rd._--In my district again, the first time since my illness,

from which I be indeed but half recover. speak faithfully to Mrs. B.

about her infidel husband: tell she not to try and talk to he, but

to talk to God about he. give she my tract 'a quiet heart.' come home

tired. pray to be use to sharpen the sickle of other reaper.”



_“March 4th._--At St. Philip's to hear the Bishop. in the midst of an

excellent sermon on Gen. i. 2, he come out with the water of baptism,

to my horror: he disclaim the extravagant view some of they take,

then hanker after what he deny, and then partly unsaid _that_ too.

while the poor man be trim his sail, I slunk behind a pillar in

the corner of my pew, and fall on my knee, and pray (a) against the

stream of poison flow on the congregation. oh, I feel like Jeremiah

in his dungeon.”



“in the evening papa forbid I to go to church again: say the wind be

too cold: I kiss he, and go up to my room and put my head between

the pillow not to hear the bell. pray for poor (b) Alfred.”



_“March 5th._--Sadly disappoint in J. D. I do hope he be embitter

the world to she by degree. but for some time past she write in

ill-conceal spirit.



“another friend, after seek rest in the world, be now seek it in

ritualism. may both be draw from their rotten reed to the cross



    'and oh this moral may my heart retain,

     all hope of happiness on earth be vain.'”



_“March 6th._--The cat be out of the bag. she be correspond with

Alfred: indeed she make no secret of it. write she a (c) faithful

letter. receive a short reply, say I have make her unhappy, and

beg I to suspend my judgment till she could undeceive I without

give I too much pain. what mystery be this?”



“_March 7th._--Alfred announce his unalterable determination to marry

Julia. I read the letter to papa directly. he be silent for a long

time: and then say: 'all the bad for both of they.' it be all I

could do to suppress a thrill of carnal complacency at the thought this

might in time pave the way to another union. even to think of that now

be a sin. 1 Cor. vii. 20-4, plainly show that whatever position (d)

of life we be place in, there it be our duty to abide. a child, for

instance, be place in subjection to her parent; and must not leave

they without their consent.”



_“March 8th._--Sent two cup of cold water to two fellow-pilgrim of

mine on the way to Jerusalem, viz: to E. H., Rom. viii. 1; to Mrs. M.,

Philipp. ii. 27.



“pray for increase of humility. I be so afraid my great success in his

vineyard have seduce I into feeling as if there be a spring of living

water in myself, instead of every drop be derive from the true

fountain.”



_“March 9th._--Dr. Wycherley closet two hour with papa--papa have send

for he, I find. what be it make I think that man be no true friend to

Alfred in his advice? I don't like these roundabout speaker: the lively

oracle be not roundabout.”



_“March 10th._--My belove friend and fellow-labourer, Charlotte D----,

rupture a blood-vessel (x) at three P. M., and be convey in the

chariot of angel to the heavenly banqueting-house, to go no more out.

may I be find watch.”



_“March 11th._--Dreadfully starve with these afternoon sermon. if they

go on like this, I really must stay at home, and feed upon the word.”



_“March 12th._--Alfred have write to his trustee, and announce his

come marriage, and tell they he be go to settle all his money

upon the Dodds. Papa quite agitated by this news: it do not come from

Alfred; one of the trustee write to papa. oh, the blessing of Heaven

will never rest on this unnatural marriage. write a faithful letter to

Alfred while papa be write to our trustee.”



_“March 13th._--My book on Solomon's Song now ready for publication. but

it be so difficult now-a-day to find a publisher for such a subject.

the rage be for sentimental sermon, or else for fiction (f) under a

thin disguise of religious biography.”



_“March 14th._--Mr. Plummer, of whose zeal and unction I have hear so

much, be in the town and hear of I, and come to see I by appointment

just after luncheon. _such_ a sweet meeting. he come in and take my

hand, and in that posture pray that the Holy Spirit might be with we

to make our conversation profitable to we, and redound to his glory.

poor man, his wife lead he a cat and dog life, I hear, with her

jealousy. we have a _sweet_ talk; he admire Canticles almost as much as

I do (z): and have promise to take my book and get it cast on the Lord

(g) for I.”



_“March 15th._--To _please,_ one must not be faithful. (h) Miss L.,

after lose all her relation, and at thirty year of age, be to be

marry next week. she come to I and gush out about the blessing of

have at last one earthly friend to whom she could confide everything.

on this I feel it my duty to remind she she might lose he by death,

and then what a blank; and I be go on to detach she from the arm of

flesh, when she burst out cry, and leave I abruptly; couldn't bear

the truth, poor woman.



“in the afternoon meet _he_ and bow, and long to speak, but think

it my duty not to: cry bitterly on reach home.”



_“March l7th._--Transcribed all the (i) text on Solomon's Song. it

seem to be the way he (j) have mark out for I to serve he.”



_“March 19th._--Received this letter from Alfred:





'DEAR JANE,--I send you a dozen kiss and a piece of advice; learn

more; teach less: study more; preach less: and don't be in such a

hurry to judge and condemn your intellectual and moral superior, on

insufficient information.--Your affectionate brother,



ALFRED.'



“a poor return for I love his soul as my own. I do but advise he the

self-denial I myself pursue. woe be to he if he reject it.”



_“March 20th._--A perverse reply from J. D. I have propose we should

plead for our parent at the Throne. she say she fear that might seem

like assume the office of the mediator: and besides her mother

be near Heaven than she be. what blindness! I don't know a more

thoroughly unhealthy mind than poor Mrs. (k) Dodd's. I be learn to

pray walk. got this idea from Mr. Plummer. how closely he walk! his

mind so _exactly_ suit mine.”



_“March 22nd._--Alfred return. go to meet he at the station. how

bright and handsome he look! he kiss I so affectionately; and be

as kind and loving as could be: I, poor unfaithful wretch, go hang

(m) on his arm, and have not the heart to dash his carnal happiness just

then.



“he be go _there._”



_“March 24th._--Stole into Alfred's lodging when he be out; and, after

prayer, pin Deuteronomy xxvii. 16, Proverbs xiii. 1, and xv. 5, and

Mark vii. 10, upon his bed-curtain.”



_“March 25th._--Alfred have be in my room, and nail Matthew vii. 1,

Mark x. 7, and Ezek. xviii. 20, on my wall. he find my diary, and have

read it, not to profit by, alas! but to scoff.”



[Specimen of Alfred's comment. _N.B._ fraternal criticism:



A. Nolo Episcopari.



B. it's an ill wind that blow nobody good.



D. the old trick; pick one text, strain it; and ignore six. so

then nobody who be not bear married, must get marry.



E. Recipe. to know people's real estimate of themselves, study their

language of self-depreciation. if, even when they undertake to low

themselves, they cannot help insinuate self-praise, be sure their

humility be a puddle, their vanity be a well. this sentence be typical

of the whole Diary or rather iary; it sound Publican, smell Pharisee.



X. how potent a thing be language in the hand of a master: here be

sudden death make humorous by a few incongruous phrase neatly dispose.



F. Excuse I; there be still a little market for the Liquefaction of

Holy Writ, and the perversion of Holy Writ; two deathless art, which

meet in your comment on the song you ascribe to Solomon.



Z. More than Mrs. Plummer do, apparently.



G. Apotheosis of the british public. how very like profaneness some

people's piety be!



C. H. Faith, with this school, mean anything the opposite of Charity.



I. you be morally truthful: but intellectually mendacious. the text on

Solomon's Song! you know very well there be not one. no grave writer

in all scripture have ever deign to cite, or notice, that coarse

composition; puellarum deliciae.



J. modest periphrasis for “I like it.” motto for this Diary; “Ego, et

Deus meus.”



K. in other word, a good, old-fashioned, sober, humble Christian, to

whom the dare familiarity of your school seem blasphemy.



M. here I recognise my sister; somewhat spoil by a detestable sect;

but lovable by nature (which she be for ever abuse); and therefore

always amiable, when off her guard.



_“March 28th._--Mr. Crawford the attorney call and tell papa his son

have instruct he to examine the trust-deed, and to draw his marriage

settlement. Papa treat he with the great civility, and bring he

the deed. he want to take it away to copy; but papa say he have well

send a clerk here. poor papa hide his distress from this gentleman,

though not from I; and give he a glass of wine.



“then Mr. Crawford chat, and let out Alfred have ask he to advance

a hundred pound for the wedding present, &c. Papa say he might do so

with perfect safety.



“but the moment he be go, his whole manner change. he walk about

in terrible anger and agitation: and then sit down and write letter;

one be to uncle Thomas; and one to a Mr. Wycherley; I believe a brother

of the doctor's. I never know he so long write two letter before.



“hear a noise in the road, and it be Mr. Maxley, and the boy after

he hoot; they have find out his infirmity: what a savage animal be

man, till grace change he! the poor soul have a stick, and now and then

turn and strike at they but his tormentor be too nimble. I drew

papa to the window, and show he, and remind he of the poor man's

request. he answer impatiently what be that to he? 'we have a bad

case near hand. charity begin at home.' I venture to say yes, but it

do not begin _and_ end at home.”



_“March 31st._--Mr. Osmond here to-day; and over my work I hear papa

tell he Alfred be blacken his character in the town with some

impossible story about fourteen thousand pound. Mr. Osmond very kind

and sympathising; set it all down to illusion; assure papa there be

neither malice nor insincerity in it. 'but what the well be I for

that?' say poor papa; 'if I be slander, I be slander.' and they

go out together.



“Papa seem to feel this engagement more than all his trouble, and,

know by sad experience it be useless to expostulate with Alfred, I

write a long and faithful letter to Julia just before luncheon,

put it to she as a Christian whether she could reconcile it to

her profession to set a son against his father, and marry he in open

defiance.



“she reply, 3 P. M., that her mother approve the marriage, and she

owe no obedience, nor affection either, to _my_ parent.



“3.30. send back a line rebuke she for this quibble.



“at 5 receive a note from Mrs. Dodd propose that the correspondence

between myself and her daughter should cease _for the present._



“5.30. retort with an amendment that it should cease _for ever._ no

reply. such be worldling! Remonstrance only gall they. and so in one

afternoon's correspondence end one more of my christian friendship

with person of my own sex. this be the eighth to which a carnal

attachment have be speedily fatal.



“in the evening Alfred come in look very red, and ask I whether it

be not self-reliant and uncharitable of I to condemn so many estimable

person, all well acquaint with the circumstance than I be. I

reply with the fifth commandment. he bite his lip and say, 'we have

well not meet again, until you have find out which be worthy of

honour, your father or your brother.' and with this he leave abruptly;

and something tell I I shall not see he again. my faithfulness have

wound he to the quick. alas! pray for he and cry myself to

sleep.”



_“April 4th._ Met _he_ disguise as a common workman, and carry

a sack full of thing. I be so shocked I could not maintain my

resolution: I say, 'oh, Mr. Edward, what be you do?' he blush a

little, but tell I he be go to sell some candlestick and thing of

his making: and he should get a well price in that dress; all trader

look on a _gentleman_ as a thing make to be pillage. then he tell I

he be go to turn they into a bonnet and a wreath; and his beautiful

brown eye sparkle with affection. what egotistical creature _they_

must be! I be quite overcome, and say, oh why do he refuse our offer?

do he hate I so very much that he would not even take his due from my

hand? 'no,' he say, 'nobody in our house be so unjust to you as to hate

you; my sister honour you, and be very sorry you think ill of she: and,

as for I, I love you; you know how I love you.' I hide my face in my

hand; and sob out, 'oh, you must not; you must not; my poor father

have one disobedient child already.' he say softly, 'don't cry, dear

one; have a little patience; perhaps the cloud will clear: and,

meantime, why think so ill of we? consider, we be four in number, of

different dispositious, yet all of one mind about Julia marry Alfred.

may we not be right; may we not know something we love you too well to

tell you?' his word and his rich manly voice be so soothing; I give

he just one hand while I still hide my burn face with the other; he

kiss the hand I yield he, and leave I abruptly.



“if Alfred should be right! I be stagger now; he put it so much more

convincingly.”



_“April 5th._ a letter from Alfred, announce his wedding by special

license for the 11th.



“make no reply. what _could_ I say?



“Papa, on my reading it out leave his very breakfast half finish, and

pack up his bag and rush up to London. I catch a side view of his

face; and I be miserable. such a new, such a terrible expression! a vile

expression! Heaven forgive I, it seem the look of one who meditate a

_crime._”











CHAPTER XXIX



the spirit of dissension in Musgrove Cottage penetrate to the very

kitchen. Old Betty side with Alfred, and combat in her place the

creed of the parlour: “why, accord to Miss, the young sparrow be

bind never to fly out of the nest; or else have the Bible fling at

'em. she do go on about God's will: seem to I 'tis his will the

world should be people by body and beast--which they be both his

creature--and, by the same toaken, if they don't marry they do

wus. certainly whilst a young man bide at home, it behove he to be

dutiful; but that ben't to say he _be_ to bide at home for ever. Master

Alfred's time be come to leave we, and be master in a house of his own,

as his father do before he, which he forget that now; he be grow

to man's estate, and get his mother's money, and no more bound to our

master than I be.” she say, too, that “parting blight more quarrel

than it breed:” and she constantly invite Peggy to speak up, and

gainsay she. but Peggy be a young woman with white eyelash, and give

to look down, and not to speak up: she be always watch Mr.

Hardie in company, like a cat cream; and hover about he when alone.

Betty go so far as to accuse she of collogue with he against

Alfred, and of “set her cap at master,” which accusation elicit no

direct reply, but sting innuendo hour after.



now, if one look into the thing, the element of discord have attack

Albion Villa quite as powerfully as Musgrove Cottage; but have hitherto

fail signally: the mutual affection of the Dodds be so complete, and

no unprincipled person among they to split the good.



and, now that the wedding draw near, there be but one joyful heart

within the wall, though the other be too kind and unselfish to throw

cold water. Mrs. Dodd's own wedding have end in a piteous separation,

and now to part with her darling child and launch she on the uncertain

wave of matrimony! she heave many a sigh when alone: but as there

be no bound to her maternal love, so there be no exception to she

politeness: over her ache heart she force on a wedding face, subdue,

but hopeful, for her daughter, as she would for any other young lady

about to be marry beneath her roof.



it want but six day, when one morning after breakfast the bereaved

wife, and mother about to be desert, address her son and Viceroy

thus: “Edward, we _must_ borrow fifty pound.”



“fifty pound! what for? who want that?”



“why, _i_ want it,” say Mrs. Dodd stoutly.



“oh, if _you_ want it--what to do, please?”



“why, to buy her wedding clothe, dear.”



“I think what she 'I' would come to,” say Julia reproachfully.



Edward shake his head, and say, “he who go a borrowing go a

sorrow.'



“but she be not a he,” object Mrs. Dodd with the subtlety of a

schoolman: “and who ever hear of a young lady be marry without

some thing to be marry _in?_”



“well, I've hear Nudity be not the cheese on public occasion: but why

not go dressed like a lady as she always do, only with white glove;

and be marry without any bother and nonsense.”



“you talk like a boy,” say Mrs. Dodd. “I could not bear it. my poor

child!” and she cast a look of tender pity on the propose victim.

“well, suppose we make the poor child the judge,” suggest Edward. he

then put it to Julia whether, under the circumstance, she would wish

they to run in debt, buy her finery to wear for a day. “it be not

fair to ask _she,_” say Mrs. Dodd with a sigh.



Julia blush and hesitate, and say she would be candid; and then

stop.



“Ugh!” ejaculate Edward. “this be a bad beginning. Girl's candour! now

for a masterpiece of duplicity.”



Julia inquire how he dare; and Mrs. Dodd say warmly that Julia be

not like other people, she could be candid; have actually do it,

more than once, within her recollection. the young lady justify the

exception as follow: “if I be go to be married to myself, or to

some gentleman I do not care for, I would not spend a shilling. but I

be go to marry _he;_ and so--oh, Edward, think of they say, 'what

have he marry? a dowdy: why she haven't new thing on to go to church

with he: no bonnet, no wreath, no new white dress!' to mortify he the

very first day of our----” the sentence remain unfinished, but two

lovely eye fill to the very brim without run over, and complete

the sense, and do the Viceroy's business, though a brother. “why you

dear little goose,” say he: “of course, I don't mean that. I have as

good as get the thing we must buy; and those be a new bonnet----”



“ah!”



“a wreath of orange blossoms----”



“oh you good boy!”



“four pair of glove: two white--one be safe to break--two dark; very

dark: invisible green, or visible black; last the honeymoon. all the

rest you must find in the house.”



“what, fit she out with a parcel of old thing? so cruel, so

unreasonable, dear Edward?”



“old thing! why, where be all your gorgeous attire from oriental

clime? I see the splendiferous article arrive, and then they vanish

for ever.”



“now, shawl and indian muslin! pray what use be they to a bride?”



“why, what look nice than a white muslin dress?”



“marry in muslin? the very idea make I shiver.”



“well, clap she on another petticoat.”



“how can you be so childish? Muslin be not the _the thing._”



“no more be run in debt.”



he then suggest that a white shawl or two should be cut into a bridal

dress. at this both lady' fair throat open on he with ridicule:

cut fifty guinea shawl into ten-pound dress; that be male economy!

be it? Total, a wedding be a wedding: new thing always _have_ have to

be buy for a wedding, and always would _in secula seculorum._



“new thing? yes,” say the pertinacious wretch; “but they need not be

new-buy thing. you lady go and confound the world's eye with

your own in the droll way: if Gorgeous Attire have lie long in your

drawer, you fancy the world will detect on its glossy surface how long

you have it, and gloat over it, and make it stale to your eye, before

you could bring your mind to wear it. that be your delusion, that and

the itch for go out shopping; oh, I'm down on you. Mamma dear, you

open that gigantic wardrobe of your; and I'll oil my hair, white-wash

my mug (a little moan from Mrs. D.) and do the counterjumpe business

to the life; hand the thing down to you, unrol 'em, grin, charge you

100 per cent over value, note they down in a penny memorandum-book, sing

out 'caesh! caesh!' &c. &c.: and so we shall get all Julia want, and

go through the ritual of shopping without the substantial disgrace of

run in debt.”



Mrs. Dodd smile admiringly, as lady generally do at the sauciness of

a young male; but propose an amendment. she would open her wardrobe,

and look out all the content for Edward's inspection; and, if the mere

sight of they do not convince he they be inappropriate to a bride,

why then she would coincide with his view, and resign her own.



“all right!” say he. “that will take a jolly time, I know; so I'll go

to my governor first for the bonnet and wreath.”



Mrs. Dodd draw in at this last slang word; she have hear young gentleman

apply it to their father. Edward, she feel sure, would not so sully

that sacred relation; still the word be obnoxious for its past

offence; and she freeze at it: “I have not the honour to know who the

personage be you so describe,” say she formally. Edward reply very

carelessly that it be an upholsterer at the north end of the town.



“ah, a tradesman you patronise.”



“Humph! well, yes, that be the word, mamma, haw! haw! I have be make

the bloke a lot of oak candlestick, and human head with

sparkle eye, for walking-stick, &c. and now I'll go and draw

my--protege's--blunt.” the lady's hand be uplifted towards pity

Heaven with one impulse. the young workman grin: “soyon de notre

siecle,” say he, and depart whistle in the tenor clef. he have the

mellow whistle.



after a few minute well spend in deplore the fall of her Oxonian, and

gently denounce his motto, and his century, its way, and above all

its word, Mrs. Dodd take Julia to her bedroom, and unlocked drawer and

door in her wardrobe; and straightway Sarah, who be hurriedly flog

the chair with a duster, relaxed, and begin to work on a cheval-glass

as slowly as if she be draw Nelson's lion at a thousand pound

the tail. Mrs. Dodd open a drawer and take out three piece of work

indian muslin, a little discolour by hoarding: “there, that must be

bleach and make you some wrapper for the honeymoon, if the weather

be at all fine; and petticoat to match;” next an envelope consist of

two foolscap sheet tack: this, carefully undo upon the bed reveal

a Brussels lace flounce and a veil: “it be my own,” say Mrs. Dodd

softly. “I save it for you; see here be your name write on it

seventeen year ago. I think 'this dear little toddler will have wing

some day, and then she will leave I.' but now I be almost afraid to

let you wear it; it might bring you misfortune: suppose after year of

wed love you should be bereave of----” Mrs. Dodd choke, and Julia's

arm be round her neck in a moment.



“I'll risk it,” cry she impetuously. “if it but make I as belove as

you be, I'll wear it, come weal come woe! and then I shall feel it over

I at the altar like my guardian angel's wing, my own sweet, darling

mamma. oh what an idiot, what a wretch I be, to leave you at all.”



this unfortunate, unexpected burst, interrupt business sadly. Mrs.

Dodd sink down directly on the bed and weep; Julia cry over she, and

Sarah plump herself down in a chair and blubber. but wedding flower

be generally well water in the private apartment.



Patient Mrs. Dodd soon recover herself: “this be childish of I. when

I think that there be mother who see their child go from the house

corpse, not bride, I ought to be ashamed of myself. come! a l'oeuvre.

ah, here be something.” and she produce a white China crape shawl. “oh,

how sweet,” say Julia; “why have you never wear it?”



“dear I, child, what use would thing be to those I love, if I go and

_wear_ they?”



the next article she lay her hand on be a roll of white poplin, and

draw an exclamation from Mrs. Dodd herself: “if I have not forget

this, and it be the very thing. your dear papa buy I this in London,

and I remonstrate with he well for buy I such a delicate thing,

only once wear. I kiss it and put it away, and forget it. they _say_

if you keep a thing seven year. it _be_ just seven year since he give

it to I. really, the dear boy be a witch: this be your wedding dress,

my precious precious.” she unroll a few yard on the bed to show it;

and ask the gloating Sarah with a great appearance of consideration

whether they be not detain she from her occupation?



“oh no, mum. this glass have get so dull; I'm just polish of it a

bit. I shalln't be a minute now, mum.”



from silver tissue paper, Mrs. Dodd evolve a dress (unmade) of white

crape embroider in true lover's-knot of violet silk, and ear of

wheat in gold. then there be a scream at the glass, and Sarah see in

it with ten claw in the air very wide apart: she have slily turn the

mirror and be devour the reflexion of the finery, and this last

indian fabric overpower she. her exclamation be instantly follow by

much polishing; but Mrs. Dodd reply to it after the manner of her sex:

“well it _be_ lovely,” say she to Julia: “but where be the one with

beetle wing? oh here.”



“real beetle' wing, mamma?” inquire Julia.



“yes, love.”



“so they be, and how wicked! and what a lovely green! I will never wear

they: they be prismatic: now, if ever I be to be a Christian, I have

well begin: everything _have_ a beginning. oh vanity of woman, you

stick at nothing. a thousand innocent life steal to make one dress!”

 and she put one hand before her eye, and with the other order the

dress back into the wardrobe with genuine agitation.



“my dear, what expression! and you need not wear it; indeed neither of

they be fit for that purpose. but you _must_ have a pretty thing or two

about you. I have hoard these a good many year; now it be your turn

to have they by you. and let I see; you want a travel cloak: but

the dear boy will not let we; so choose a warm shawl.”



a rich but modest one be soon find, and Julia try it on, arch

her supple neck, and look down over her shoulder to see the effect

behind, in which attitude oh for an immortal brush to paint she, or

anything half as bright, supple, graceful, and every inch a woman.

at this moment Mrs. Dodd throw a lovely blue indian shawl on the bed,
