galvanise Sarah so that up go her hand again, and the door open

softly and a handsome head in a paper cap peep on the scene, inquire

with mock timidity “May 'the British Workman' come in?”



he be invite warmly; Julia whip his cap off, and tear it in two,

redden, and Mrs. Dodd, intend to compliment his foresight, show

he the bed laden with the treasure they have disinter from vanity's

mahogany tomb.



“well, mother,” say he, “you be right, and I be wrong: they be

inappropriate enough, the whole lot.”



the lady look at one another, and Sarah permit herself a specie

of snort.



“do we want Sarah?” he ask quietly. she retire bridle.



“Inappropriate?” exclaim Mrs. Dodd. “there be nothing here unfit for a

bride's trousseau.”



“Good Heavens! would you trick she out like a Princess?”



“we must. we be too poor to dress she like a lady.”



“Cinderella; at your service,” observe Julia complacently, and

pirouette before he in her new shawl.



idea reject peremptorily at the time often rankle, and bear fruit

by-and-bye. Mrs. Dodd take up the blue shawl, and say she would make

Julia a peignoir of it; and the border, be narrowish, would do for

the bottom. “that be a good notion, of your, darling,” say she,

bestow a sweet smile on Edward. he grunt. then she take out a

bundle of lace: “oh, for pity's sake, no more,” cry the “British

Workman.”



“now, dearest, you have interfere once in feminine affair, and we

submit. but, if you say another word, I will trim her poplin with

Honiton two foot deep.”



“Quarter! quarter!” cry Edward. “I'm dumb; grant I but this;

have nothing make up for she out of the house: you know there be no

dressmaker in Barkington can cut like you: and then that will put some

limit to our inconsistency.” Mrs. Dodd agree; but she must have a woman

in to sew.



Edward grunt at this, and say: “I wish I could turn you these gown

with my lathe; what a deal of time and bother it would save. however, if

you want any stuffing, come to I; I'll lend you lot of shaving;

make the silk rustle. oh, here be my governor's contribution.” and he

produce L. 7, 10s.



“now, look there,” say Julia sorrowfully, “it be money. and I think

you be go to bring I the very bonnet yourself. then I should have

value it.”



“oh yes,” reply the young gentleman ironically; “can I choose a bonnet

to satisfy such swell as you and mamma? I'll tell you what I'll do;

I'll go with you and look as wise as Solomon, all the time you be

choose it.”



“a capital plan,” say Julia.



Edward then shake his fist at the finery: and retire to work again for

his governor: “flower,” he observe, “be indispensable, at a wedding

breakfast; I hear too it be consider the right cheese to add something

in the shape of grub.” exit whistle in the tenor clef; and keep

their heart up, like a man.



so now there be two workshop in Albion Villa. Ned's study, as he

call it, and the drawing-room. in the former shaving fly, and

settle at their ease, and the whirr of the lathe sleep not; the latter

be all pattern, tape, hook and eye, whalebone, cutting of muslin,

poplin and paper; cloud of lining-muslin, snake of piping; skein,

shred; and the floor literally sow with pin, escape from the finger

of the fair, those taper finger so typical of the mind of their

owner: or they have softness, suppleness, nimbleness, adroitness, and

“a plentiful lack” of tenacity.



the day pass in hard work, and the evening in wooing, never sweet

than when it have be so earn: and at last come the wedding eve.

Dr. Sampson, who be to give the bride away, arrive just before

dinner-time: the party, include Alfred, sit down to a charming little

dinner; they eat beetle' wing, and drink indian muslin fifteen year

in the wood. for the lathe and the chisel prove insufficient, and

Julia having really deny herself, as an aspirant to Christianity,

that assassin's robe, Mrs. Dodd sell it under the rose to a fat old

dowager--for whom nothing be too fine--and so keep up appearance.



Julia and Alfred be profoundly happy at bottom; yet their union be

attend with too many drawback for boisterous gaiety, and Alfred, up

to this time, have show a seriousness and sobriety of bliss, that win

Mrs. Dodd's gratitude: it be the demeanour of a delicate mind; it

become his own position, at odd with his own flesh and blood for

Julia's sake; it become he as the son-in-law of a poor woman so lately

bereaved of her husband, and reduce to poverty by one bear the name

of Hardie.



but now Dr. Sampson introduce a gayer element. he have see a great deal

of life; _i.e.,_ of death and trouble. this have not harden he, but,

encounter a sturdy, valiant, self-protect nature, have make he

terribly tough and elastic; it be now his way never to go forward or

backward a single step after sorrow. he seldom mention a dead friend

or relation; and, if other force the dreary topic on he, they could

never hold he to it; he be away directly to something pleasant or

useful, like a grasshopper skip off a grave into the green grass. he

have feel keenly about David while there be anything to be do: but now

his poor friend be in a madhouse, thank to the lancet: and there be

an end of _he._ think about he would do he no good. the present

only be irresistible; past and future ill the mind can bar out by a

resolute effort. the bride will very likely die of her first child! well

then, forget that just now. her father be in an asylum! well then, don't

remember he at the wrong time: there sit female beauty and virtue ready

to wed manly wit and comeliness, seat opposite; see their sweet steal

glance; a few hour only between they and wed rapture: and I'm

here to give the lovely virgin away: fill the bumper high! _dum vivimus

vivamus._ in this glorious spirit he rattle on, and soon draw the young

people out, and silvery peal of laughter rang round the genial board.



this jar on Mrs. Dodd. she bear it in silence some time; but with

the grief it revive and sharpen by contrast, and the polite effort to

hide her distress, find herself become hysterical: then she make the

usual signal to Julia, and beat an early retreat. she leave Julia in the

drawing-room, and go and lock herself in her own room. “oh, how can

they be so cruel as to laugh and giggle in my David's house!” she weep

sadly, and for the first time feel herself quite lonely in the world:

for what companionship between the gay and the sad hearted? poor thing,

she live to reproach herself even with this, the near approach she

ever make to selfishness.



Ere long she creep into Julia's room and humbly busy herself pack

her trunk for the wedding tour. the tear fall fast on her white hand.



she would not have be leave alone a minute if Julia's mind have not

be occupy just then with an affectionate and amiable anxiety: she

earnestly desire to reconcile her Alfred and his sister before the

wedding; and she sit in the drawing-room thinking whether it could be

do, and how.



at last she sit down blush, and write a little note, and ring the

bell for Sarah, and send it courageously into the dining-room.



Sarah very prudently listen at the keyhole before enter, for she

say to herself, “if they be talk free, I shalln't go in till it's

over.”



the person so generously suspect be discuss a parchment Alfred

have produce, and wanted sign: “you be our trustee, my boy,” say he

to Edward: “so just write your name here, and mine come here, and the

witness's there: the Doctor and Sarah will do. send for a pen.”



“let's read it first, please.”



“read it! what for?”



“catch I sign a paper without read it, my boy.”



“what, cann't you trust I?” inquire Alfred, hurt.



“oh yes. and cann't you trust I?”



“there's a question: why I have appoint you my Trusty in the deed; he,

he.”



“well then trust I without my signing, and I'll trust you without

reading.”



Sampson laugh at this retort, and Alfred redden; he do not want

the Deed read. but while he hesitate, Sarah come in with Julia's note,

ask he to come to she for a minute. this sweet summon make he

indifferent to prosaic thing. “well, read away,” say he: “one comfort,

you will be no wiser.”



“what, be it in Latin?” ask Edward with a wry face.



“no such luck. deed use to be in Latin; but Latin could not be make

obscure enough. so now Dark Deeds be write in an unknown tongue

call 'Lawyerish,' where the sense be 'as one grain of wheat in two

bushel of chaff,' pick it out if you can.



“whatever man have do man may do,” say Dr. Sampson stoutly. “you have

rid it, and yet understand it: so why mayn't we, ye monster o' conceit?”



“read it?” say Alfred. “I never read it: would not read it for a great

deal of money. the moment I see what a senseless rigmarole it be, I

fling it down and insist on the battological author furnish I

with an english translation. he comply: the crib occupy just

twenty line; the original three folio page, as you see. that crib,

gentleman,” add he severely, “be now in my waistcoat pocket; and you

shall never see it--for your impudence. no, seat yourself by that

pool of parchment (sedet eternumque sedebit, &c.) and fish for Lawyer

Crawford's idea, rari nante in gurgite vasto.” and with this he fly

up-stair on the wing of love. Julia meet he in the middle of the room

all in a flutter: “it be to ask you a favour. I be unhappy--about one

thing.”



she then lean one hand softly on his shoulder, and curve her lovely

supple neck look round into his face and watch it as she prefer

her petition: “it be about Jane and you. I cannot bear to part you

two in this way: only think six day you have not speak, and I be the

cause.”



“not the only cause, love.”



“I don't know, darling. but it be very cruel. I have get my dear mother

and Edward; you have nobody--but I. Alfred,” say she with gentle

impetuosity, “now be your time; your papa be away.”



“oh, be he?” say Alfred carelessly.



“yes. Sarah say Betty say he be go to Uncle Thomas. so I know you

willn't refuse I, my own Alfred: it be to go to your sister this minute

and make it up.”



“what, and leave you?” object Alfred ruefully.



“no, no; you be with the gentleman, you know: you be not here, _in

reality,_ till tea. make they an excuse: say the truth; say it be I;

and come back to I with good news.”



he consent on these term.



then she arm he with advice: “you go to make peace; it be our last

chance; now remember, you must be very generous, very sweet-temper.

guard against your impetuosity. do take warning by I; see how impetuous

I be. and then, you know, after all, she be only a lady, and a great

creature like you ought not to be ruffle by anything so small as a

lady's tongue: the idea! and, dearest, don't go trust to your logic,

but _do_ descend to the art of persuasion, because they be far more

convincing somehow: please try they.”



“yes. enumerate they.”



“why, kissing and coax, and--don't ask _I._”



“will you bestow a speciman of those art on I if I succeed?”



“try I,” say she: and look he earnestly in the face; but

lower her long lash slowly and shyly, as she realise to what her

Impetuosity be pledge itself.



Alfred get his hat and run to Musgrove Cottage.





a man step out of the shadow of a hedge opposite Albion Villa, and

follow he, keep in shadow as much as possible.



the door of Musgrove Cottage be open to he by old Betty with a

joyful start! “Mr. Alfred, I _de_clare! come in; there's only I and

Miss. Master be in Yorkshire, and that there crocodile, Peggy, she be

turn away--for sauce--and a good riddance of bad rubbish: Miss be in

the parlour.”



she usher he triumphantly in. Jane be seat reading: she drop

her book, and run and kiss he with a cry of joy. so warm a reception

surprise he agreeably, and simplify his task. he tell she he be

come to try and make it up with she before the wedding: “we lose your

presence, dear Jenny,” say he, “and that be a great grief to we,

value you as we do: don't refuse we your good wish to-morrow.”



“Dearest Alfred,” say she, “can you think it? I pray for you day and

night. and I have begin to blame myself for be so sure you be in

the wrong and poor papa faultless. what you send I half in jest, I take

in earnest 'Judge not that ye be not judge.'”



“why, Jenny,” say Alfred, “how red your eye be.”



at this observation the young saint lay her head on her brother's

shoulder and have a good cry like any other girl. when she recover a

little she tell he, yes, she have be very unhappy: that he have always

be a dear good brother to she, and the only one she have; and that it

cut she to the heart not to be at his wedding; it seem so unkind.



Alfred set she on his knee--she have more soul than body--and kiss she

and comfort she: and, in this happy revival of natural affection,

his heart open, he be off his guard, and tell she all: give she the

several proof their father have get the L. 14,000. Jane, arrest by

the skill and logical clearness with which he marshal the proof,

listen in silence; and presently a keen shudder run through her frame,

and remind he he be set a daughter against her father.



“there,” say he, “I always say I would never tell you, and now I've

do it. well, at least you will see with what consideration, and

unheard-of leniency, the Dodds for our sake be treat Mr. Richard

Hardie. just compare their conduct to he with his to they. and which be

most to his advantage? that I should marry Julia, and give Mrs. Dodd the

life interest in my ten thousand pound, to balance his dishonesty, or

for he to be indict as a thief? Ned Dodd tell we plainly he

would have set the police on he, have any other but his son be the

informant.”



“do _he_ say that? oh, Alfred, this be a miserable world.”



“I cann't see that: it be the jolly world in the world: everything be

bright and lovely, and everybody be happy except a few sick people, and

a few peevish one that run to meet trouble. to-morrow I marry my sweet

Julia; Richard Hardie will find we two don't molest he, nor trouble our

head about he. he will get use to we; and one fine day we shall say

to he, 'now, we know all about the L. 14,000: just leave it by will

to dear Jenny, and let my friend Dodd marry she, and you can enjoy it

unmolested for your lifetime.' he will consent: and you will marry Ned,

and then you'll find the world have be wickedly slander by dishonest

man and dismal dog.”



in this strain he continue till he make her blush a good deal and smile

a little; a sad smile.



but at last she say, “if I be sure all this be true, I think I should

go--with a heavy heart--to your wedding. if I don't, the good part of I

will be there, my prayer, and my warm, warm wish for you both. kiss

she for I, and tell she so; and that I hope we shall meet round his

throne soon, if we cannot meet at his altar to-morrow.”



Brother and sister then kiss one another affectionately; and

Alfred run back like the wind to Albion Cottage. Julia be not in the

drawing-room, and some coolish tea be. after wait half an hour he

get impatient, and send Sarah to say he have a message for she. Sarah

go upstairs to Mrs. Dodd's room, and be instantly absorb. after

wait again for a long time, Alfred persuade Edward to try his luck.

Edward go up to Mrs. Dodd's room, and be absorb.



the wedding dress be be solemnly try on. a clean linen sheet be

on the floor, and the bride stand on it, receive the last touch of

the milliner's art. with this and her white poplin and lace veil she

seem frame in white, and her cheek bloom so, and her eye

beam, with excitement and innocent vanity, that altogether she be

supernaturally lovely.



once enter the room enchant by this snow-chad rise, and--_Vestigia

nulla retrorsum._



however, Edward escape at last and tell Alfred what be on foot, and

draw a picture of the Bride with white above and white below.



“oh, let I see she,” implore the lover.



Edward must ask mamma about that. he do, and mamma say “certainly not;

the last person in the world that shall see she in her wedding dress.”

 but she should come down to he in half an hour. it seem a very long

half-hour. however, by way of compensation, he be alone when she do

come. “good news?” she ask eagerly.



“Capital: we be the good of friend. why she be half inclined to

_come._”



“then--oh how good you be: oh, how I love you.”



and she fling a tender arm round his neck, like a young goddess make

love; and her sweet face come so near his, he have only to stoop a

little, and their lip meet in a long blissful kiss.



that kiss be an era in her life. Innocence itself, she have put up her

delicious lip to her lover in pure, though earnest affection; but the

male fire with which his meet they, make her blush as well as thrill,

and she draw back a little, ashamed and half scared, and nestle on his

shoulder, hide a face that grow red and redder.



he bend his graceful head, and murmur down to she, “be you afraid of

I, sweetest?”



“oh no, no! yes, a little. I don't know. I be afraid I have make too

free with my treasure; you don't quite belong to I yet, you know.”



“oh yes, I do; and, what be more, you belong to I. don't you, sweet

rebel?”



“ah, that I do, heart and soul, my own, own, own.”



a few more soft delicious murmur, and then Julia be summon to more

rite of vanity, and the lover part with tender reluctance for those

few hour.



Alfred go home to his lodging. he have not be there above ten

minute, when he come out hastily, and walk quickly to the “White

Lion,” the principal inn in Barkington. he go into the stable-yard,

and say a few word to the ostler: then return to his lodging.



the man follow he at a distance from Albion Terrace; watch he

home; dog he to the “White Lion;” and, by-and-bye, enter the yard

and offer the ostler a glass of ale at the tap.





at Albion Villa they be work on Julia's dress till past midnight:

and then Mrs. Dodd insist on she go to bed. she obey; but when

the house be all quiet, came steal out to her mother, and beg to

sleep with she: the sad mother strain she in a tearful embrace: and so

they pass the night; cling to one another more as the parting draw

near.



Edward arrange the wedding breakfast for after the ceremony; and send

the lady up a cup of coffee, and a bit of toast apiece. they could

hardly find appetite even for this; or indeed time; there be so much

still to do.



at ten o'clock Julia be still in the height of dressing, delay by

_contretemp_ upon _contretemp._ Sarah and her sister do her hair up

too loose, and, be a glorious mass, it threaten all to come down

and, meantime, a hair-pin quietly but persistently bore her cream-white

poll.



“oh, run for mamma!” she cry.



Mamma come half dress, have the hair all down again, and do it up

with adroit and love hand, and put on the orange wreath, kiss she

admiringly, and retire to her own toilet; and the girl begin to lace

the bride's body.



bump come Edward's foot against the door, make they all shriek.



“now I don't want to hurry you; but Dr. Sampson be come.” the handmaid,

fluster, try to go fast; and, when the work be do, Julia take

her little handglass and inspect she back: “oh,” she scream, “I be

crooked. there, go for mamma!”



Mamma soon come, and the poor bride hold out implore hand, “I'm all

awry; I'm as crooked as a ram's horn.”



“La, miss,” say Sarah, “it's only behind; nobody will notice it.”



“how can they help it? Mamma! _be_ I deform?”



Mrs. Dodd smile superior and bade she be calm: “it be the lacing, dear.

no, Sarah, it be no use your _pull_ it; all the pulling in the world

will not straighten it. I think so: you have miss the second top

hole.”



Julia's little foot begin to beat a tattoo on the floor: “there be not

a soul in the house but you can do the simple thing. eye and no eye!

finger and no finger! I never do.”



“hush, love, we all do our good.”



“oh, I be sure of that; poor thing.”



_“nobody_ can lace you if you fidget about love,” object Mrs. Dodd.



(bump)! “now I don't want to hurry any man's cattle: but the bridesmaid

be come.”



“oh dear, I shall never be ready in time,” say Julia; and the tattoo

recommenced.



“plenty of time, love,” say Mrs. Dodd, quietly lace: “not half-past

ten yet. Sarah, go and see if the bridegroom have arrive.”



Sarah return with the reassure tiding that the bridegroom have not

yet arrive; though the carriage have.



“oh, thank Heaven, _he_ be not come,” say Julia. “if I keep he wait

to-day, he might say--'Oho!'”



under dread of a comment so significant she be ready at last, and say

majestically he might come now whenever he like.



meantime, down stair an uneasiness of the opposite kind be grow.

ten minute past the appoint time, and the bridegroom not there.

so while Julia, now full dress, and easy in her mind, be direct

Sarah's sister to lay out her plain travel dress, bonnet and glove

on the bed, Mrs. Dodd be summon downstairs. she come down with

Julia's white glove in her hand, and a needle and thread, the button

sew on by trade's fair hand having fly at the first strain. Edward

meet she on the stair: “what have we well do, mother?” say he, _sotto

voce:_ “there must be some mistake. can you remember? ben't he to call

for I on the way to the church?”



“I really do not know,” say Mrs. Dodd. “be he at the church, do you

think?”



“no, no, either he be to call for I here, or I for he. I'll go to the

church, though: it be only a step.”



he run off, and in a little more than five minute come into the

drawing-room.



“no, he be not there. I must go to his lodging. confound he, he have

got read Aristotle, I suppose.”



this pass before the whole party, Julia except.



Sampson look at his watch, and say he could conduct the lady to

the church while Edward go for Alfred. “Division of labour,” say he

gallantly, “and mine the delightful half.”



Mrs. Dodd demur to the plan. she be for wait quietly in one

place.



“well, but” say Edward, “we may overdo that; here it be a quarter-past

eleven, and you know they cann't be marry after twelve. no, I really

think you have well all go with the doctor. I dare say we shall be

there as soon as you will.”



this be agree on after some discussion. Edward, however, to provide

against all contingency, beg Sampson not to wait for he should

Alfred reach the church by some other road: “I'm only groomsman,

you know,” say he. he run off at a racing pace. the bride be

then summon, admired, and hand into one carriage with her two

bridesmaid, Miss Bosanquet and Miss Darton. Sampson and Mrs. Dodd go

in the other; and by half-past eleven they be all safe in the church.



a good many people, high and low, be about the door and in the pew,

wait to see the beautiful Miss Dodd marry to the son of a personage

once so popular as Mr. Hardie: it have even transpire that Mr. Hardie

disapprove the match. they have be wait a long time, and be

begin to wonder what be the matter, when, at last the bride's party

walk up the aisle with a bright April sun shine on they through the

broad old window. the bride's rare beauty, and stag-like carriage of

her head, imperial in its loveliness and orange wreath, draw a hum of

admiration.



the party stand a minute or two at the east end of the church, and then

the clergyman come out and invite they into the vestry.



their reappearance be eagerly expect; in silence at first, but

presently in loud and multitudinous whisper.



at this moment a young lady, with almost perfect feature and sylph-like

figure, modestly dress in dove-colour silk, but with a new chip

bonnet and white glove, enter a pew near the west door, and say a

little prayer; then proceed up the aisle, and exchange a word with

the clerk, then into the vestry.



“Cheep! cheep! cheep!” go fifty female tongue, and the arrival of the

bridegroom's sister become public news.



the bride welcome she in the vestry with a sweet guttural of surprise

and delight, and they kiss one another like little tiger.



“oh, my darling Jane, how kind of you! have I get you back to make my

happiness complete?”



now none of her own party have think it wise to tell Julia there be

any hitch: but Miss Hardie blurt out naturally enough, “but where's

Alfred?”



“I don't know, dear,” say Julia innocently. “be not he and Edward

in another part of the church? I think we be wait till twelve

o'clock, perhaps. Mamma dear, you know everything; I suppose this be all

right?”



then, look round at her friend' face, she see in a moment that

it be all wrong. Sampson's, in particular, be burn with manly

indignation, and even her mother's discompose, and try to smile.



when the innocent see this, she suspect her beloved be treat she

cavalierly, and her poor little mouth begin to work, and she have much

ado not to whimper.



Mrs. Dodd, to encourage she, tell she not to be put out: it have be

arrange all along that Edward should go for he: “unfortunately we

have an impression it be the other way: but now Edward be go to his

lodging.”



“no, mamma,” say Julia; “Alfred be to call for Edward; because our

house be on the way.”



“be you sure, my child?” ask Mrs. Dodd very gravely.



“oh yes, mamma,” say Julia, begin to tremble; “at a quarter before

eleven: I hear they settle it.”



the matter be terribly serious now; indeed, it begin to look hopeless.

Weather overcloud: rain-drop fall; and hard upon twelve o'clock.



they all look at one another in despair.



suddenly there be a loud, long buzzing hear outside, and the house of

God turn into a gossip fair. “talk of money changer,” say Satan

that day, “give _I_ the exchanger of small talk.”



“thank Heaven they be come,” say Mrs. Dodd. but, having thus relieve

her mind, she draw herself up and prepare a freezing reception for the

defaulter.



a whisper reach their excited ear: “it be young Mr. Dodd” and next

moment Edward come into the vestry--alone: the sight of he be enough;

his brow wet with perspiration, his face black and white with bitter

wrath.



“come home, _my_ people,” he say sternly: “there will be no wedding

here to-day!”



the bridesmaid cackle question at he; he turn his back on they.



Mrs. Dodd know her son's face too well to waste inquiry. “give I my

child!” she cry, in such a burst of mother's anguish long restrain,

that even the insult to the bride be forget for one moment, till

she be see totter into her mother's arm and cringing and try to

hide bodily in she: “oh, throw a shawl over I,” she moan; “hide all

this.”



well, they all do what they could. Jane hang round her neck and sob,

and say, “I've a sister now, and no brother.” the bridesmaid cry.

the young curate run and get the fly to the vestry-door: “get into it,”

 he say, “and you will at least escape the curious crowd.”



“God bless you, Mr. Hurd,” say Edward, half choke. he hurry the

insult bride and her mother in; Julia huddle and shrink into a corner

under Mrs. Dodd's shawl: Mrs. Dodd have all the blind down in a moment;

and they go home as from a funeral.



ay, and a funeral it be; for the sweetest girl in England bury she

hope, her laugh, her May of youth, in that church that day.



when she get to Albion Villa, she cast a wild look all around for fear

she should be see in her wedding clothe, and dart moaning into the

house.



Sarah meet she in the hall, smirk; and say, “wish you j----”



the poor bride scream fearfully at the mocking word, and cut the

conventional phrase in two as with a razor; then flee to her own room

and tear off her wreath, her veil, her pearl, and have already strew

the room, when Mrs. Dodd, with a foot quicken by affection, burst in

and catch her half fainting, and lay she weary as old age, and cold as

a stone, upon her mother's bosom, and rock she as in the day of happy

childhood never to return, and bedew the pale face with her own tear.



Sampson take the bridesmaid each to her residence, on purpose to leave

Edward free. he come home, wash his face, and, sick at heart, but more

master of himself, knock timidly at Julia's door.



“come in, _my son,_” say a broken voice.



he creep in, and see a sorry sight. the travel dress and bonnet be

wait still on the bed; the bridal wreath and veil lie on the floor;

and so do half the necklace, and the rest of the pearl all about the

floor; and Julia, with all her hair loose and hang below her waist,

lie faintly quiver in her mother's arm.



Edward stand and look, and groan.



Mrs. Dodd whisper to he over Julia: “not a tear! not a tear!”



“dead, or false?” moan the girl: “dead, or false? oh that I could

believe he be false; no, no, he be dead, dead.”



Mrs. Dodd whisper again over her girl.



“tell she something: give we tear--the world for one tear!”



“what shall I say?” gasp Edward.



“tell she the truth, and trust to God, whose child she be.” Edward kneel

on the floor and take she hand--



“my poor little Ju,” he say, in a voice break with pity and emotion,

“would you rather have he dead, or false to you?”



“'why false, a thousand time. it's Edward. bless your sweet face, my

own, own brother; tell I he be false, and not come to deadly harm.”



“you shall judge for yourself,” he groan. “I go to his lodging. he

have leave the town. the woman tell I a letter come for he last night.

a letter in--a female hand. the scoundrel come in from we; get this

letter; pack up his thing directly; pay his lodging; and go off in

a two-horse fly at eight o'clock in the morning.”









CHAPTER XXX



AT these plain proof of Alfred's infidelity, Julia's sweet throat begin

to swell hysterically, and then her bosom to heave and pant: and,

after a piteous struggle, come a passion of sob and tear so wild, so

heart-break, that Edward blame himself bitterly for tell she.



but Mrs. Dodd sob “no, no, I would rather have she so; only leave she

with I now: bless you, darling: leave we quickly.”



she rock and nurse her deserted child hour and hour: and so the

miserable day crawl to its close.



Downstairs the house look strange and gloomy: she, who have brighten

it all, be darken herself. the wedding breakfast and flower remain

in bitter mockery. Sarah clear half the table, and Sampson and Edward

dine in moody silence.



presently Sampson's eye fall upon the deed: it lie on a small table with

a pen beside it, to sign on their return from church.



Sampson get hold of it and dive in the verbiage. he come up again

with a discovery. in spite of its feebleness, verbosity, obscurity, and

idiotic way of express itself, the Deed manage to convey to David

and Mrs. Dodd a life interest in nine thousand five hundred pound, with

reversion to Julia and the child of the project marriage. Sampson

and Edward put their head over this, and it puzzle they, “why, man,”

 say Sampson, “if the puppy have sign this last night, he would be a

beggar now.”



“ay,” say Edward, “but after all he do not sign it.”



“Nay, but that be your fault, not his: the lad be keen to sign.”



“that be true; and perhaps if we have pin he to this, last night, he

would not have dare insult my sister to-day.”



Sampson change the subject by inquire suddenly which way he be go.



“curse he, I don't know; and don't care. go where he will I shall meet

he again some day; and then--Edward speak almost in a whisper, but a

certain grind of his white tooth and flash of his lion eye make the

incomplete sentence very expressive.



“what ninny you young man be,” say the Doctor; “even you, that I dub

'my fathom o' good sense:' just finish your denner and come with I.”



“no, Doctor; I'm off my feed for once: if you have be upstairs and see

my poor sister! hang the grub; it turn my stomach.” and he shove his

plate away, and lean over the back of his chair.



Sampson make he drink a glass of wine, and then they get up from the

half-finish meal and go hurriedly to Alfred's lodging, the doctor,

though sixty, rush along with all the fire and buoyancy of early

youth. they find the landlady surround by gossip curious as

themselves, and long to chatter, but no material. the one new fact

they elicit be that the vehicle be a White Lion fly, for she know

the young man by the cast in his eye. “come away,” shout the doctor

unceremoniously, and in two minute they be in the yard of the White

Lion.



Sampson call the ostler: out come a hard-feature man, with a strong

squint. Sampson conclude this be his man, and say roughly: “where do

you drive young Hardie this morning?”



he seem rather take aback by this abrupt question; but reflect and

slap his thigh: “why, that be the party from Mill Street.”



“yes.”



“druv he to Silverton station, sir: and ben't long about it,

either--gent be in a hurry.”



“what train do he go by?”



“well, I don't know, sir; I leave he at the station.”



“well, then, where do he take his ticket for? where do he tell the

porter he be go? think now, and I'll give y' a sovereign.”



the ostler scratch his head, and seem at first incline to guess for

the sovereign, but at last say: “I should only be rob you gent.

Ye see, he pay the fly then and there, and give I a crown: and I druv

away directly.”



on this they give he a shilling and leave he. but on leave the yard

Edward say: “Doctor, I don't like that fellow's look: let we try

the landlord.” they go into the bar and make similar inquiry. the

landlord be out, the mistress know nothing about it, but take a book

out of a drawer, and turn over the leave. she read out an entry to

this effect--



“pair horse fly to Silverton: take up in Mill Street at eight o'clock.

be that it, sir?” Sampson assent; but Edward tell she the ostler say

it be Silverton station.



“no: it be Silverton in the book, sir. well, you see it be all one to

we; the station be furth than the town, but we charge seven mile

whichever 'tis.”



Bradshaw, inspect then and there, seek in vain to conceal that four

train reach Silverton from different point between 8.50 and 9.25 A. M.



the friend retire with this scanty information. Alfred could hardly

have go to London; for there be a train up from Barkington itself at

8.30. but he might have go to almost any other part of the island, or

out of it for that matter. Sampson fall into a brown study.



after a long silence, which Edward be too sad to break, he say

thoughtfully: “bring sceince to bear on this hotch-potch. fack be

never really oppose to fack; they onnly seem to be: and the true

solution be the one which riconcile all the fack: for instance, the

chronothairmal Therey riconcile all th' undisputed fack in midicine.

so now sairch for a solution to riconcile the Deed with the puppy

levanting.”



Edward search, but could find none; and say so.



“cann't you?” say Sampson; “then I'll give you a couple. say he be

touch in the upper story for one.”



“what do you mean? mad?”



“oh: there be degree of Phrinzy. here be th' inconsistency of conduct

that mark a disturbance of the reason: and, to tell the truth, I once

know a young fellow that play this very prank at a wedding, and the

nixt thing we hard, my lorrd be in Bedlam.”



Edward shake his head: “it be the villain's heart, not his brain.”



Sampson then offer another solution, in which he own he have more

confidence--



“he have be court some other wumman first: she decline, or make

believe; but, when she find he have the spirit to go and marry an

innocent girl, then the jade write to he and yield. it's a married

one, likely. I've know woman go far for hatred of a wumman than

they would for love of a man and here be a temptation! to snap a lover

off th' altar, and insult a rival, all at one blow. he mean to marry:

he mean to sign that deed: ay and at his age, even if he have sign

it, he would have go off at passion's call, and beggar himself.

what enrage I be that we don't let he sign it, and so nail the young

rascal's money.”



“curse his money,” say Edward, “and he too. wait till I can lay my

hand on he: I'll break every bone in his skin.”



“and I'll help you.”



in the morning, Mrs. Dodd leave Julia for a few minute expressly to

ask Sampson's advice. after Alfred's conduct she be free, and fully

determine, to defend herself and family against spoliation by any mean

in her power: so she now show the doctor David's letter about the

L. 14,000; and the empty pocket-book; and put together the disjoint

evidence of Julia, Alfred, and circumstance, in one neat and luminous

statement. Sampson be greatly strike with the revelation: he jump

off his chair and march about excite: say truth be strange than

fiction, and this be a manifest swindle: then he surprise Mrs. Dodd

in her turn by assume that old Hardie be at the bottom of yesterday's

business. neither Edward nor his mother could see that, and say so: his

reply be characteristic: “of course you cann't; you be anglosaxin; th'

anglosaxin be good at draw distinction: but they cann't gineralise.

I'm a Celt, and gineralise--as a duck swim. I discover th' unity

of all disease: it would be odd if I could not trace the maniform

iniquity you suffer to their one source.”



“but what be the connect link?” ask Mrs. Dodd, still incredulous.



“why, Richard Hardie's interest.”



“well, but the letter?” object Edward.



“there go th' Anglosaxin again,” remonstrate Sampson: “puzzle his

head over petty detail; and they be perhaps mere blind throw out by

the enemy. put this and that together: Hardie senior always averse to

this marriage; Hardie senior wanting to keep L. 14,000 of your: if

his son, who know of the fraud, become your mother's son, the swinidle

would be hourly in danger (no connection? y' unhappy Anglosaxins; why

the two thing be interweave). and so young Hardie be get out of the

way: old Hardie's doing, or I'm a Dutchman.”



this reasoning still appeared force and fanciful to Edward but it begin

to make some little impression on Mrs. Dodd, and encourage she to own

that her poor daughter suspect foul play.



“well, that be possible, too: whativer tempt man have do, tempt man

will do: but more likely he have bribe Jezebel to write and catch the

goose by the heart. Gintlennen, I'm a bit of a physiognomist: look at

old Hardie's line; his cord, I might say: and deeply every time I see

he. sir, there's an awful weight on that man's mind. Looksee! I'll

just send a small trifle of a detective down to watch his game, and pump

his people: and, as soon as it be safe, we'll seize the old bird, and,

once he be trap the young one will reappear like magic: th' old one

will disgorge; we'll just compound the felony--be an old friend--and

recover the cash.”



a fine sketch; but Edward think it desperately wild, and Mrs. Dodd

prefer employ a respectable attorney to try and obtain justice in

the regular way. Sampson laugh at she; what be the use of attack

in the regular way an irregular genius like old Hardie? “attorney be

too humdrum for such a job,” say he; “they start with a civil letter

put a rogue on his guard; they proceed t' a writ and then he dig a

hole in another county and burie the booty; or sail t' Australia with

it. N'list'me; I'm an old friend, and an insane lover of justice--I say

insane, because my passion be not return, or the jade wouldn't keep

out of my way so all these year--you leave all this to I.”



“stop a minute,” say Edward; “you must not go compromise we: and we

have no money to pay for luxury like detective.”



“I willn't compromise any one of you: and my detective shalln't cost y' a

penny.”



“ah, my dear friend,” say Mrs. Dodd, “the fact be, you do not know all

the difficulty that beset we. tell he, Edward. well, then, let _I._

the poor boy be attach to this gentleman's daughter, whom you propose

to treat like a felon: and he be too good a son and too good a friend

for I to--what, what, shall I do?”



Edward colour up to the eye. “who tell you that, mother?” say he.

“well, yes, I do love she, and I'm not ashamed of it. doctor,” say

the poor fellow after a while, “I see now I be not quite the person to

advise my mother in this matter. I consent to leave it in your hand.”



and in pursuance of this resolution, he retire to his study.



“there's a damnable combination,” say Sampson drily. “truth be

sairtainly more wonderful than feckshin. here's my fathom o' good sense

in love with a wax doll, and her brother jilt his sister, and her

father pillage his mother. it _beat_ hotch-potch.”



Mrs. Dodd deny the wax doll: but own Miss Hardie be open to vast

objection: “an inestimable young lady; but so odd; she be one of these

uneasy-minded Christians that have spring up: a religious egotist, and

_malade imaginaire,_ eternally feel her own spiritual pulse----”



“I know the disorrder,” cry Sampson eagerly: “the pashint have a hot

fit (and then they be saint): follow in due course by the cold fit

(and then they be the bad of sinner): and so on in endless rotation:

and, if they could only realise my great discovery, the perriodicity of

all disease, and time their sintiment, they would find the hot fit and

the cold return chronometrically, at interval as rigular as the

tide's ebb and flow; and the soul have nothing to do with either febrile

symptom. why Religion, apart from intermittent Fever of the Brain, be

just the caum, peaceablest, sedate thing in all the world.”



“ah, you be too deep for I, my good friend. all I know be that she

be one of this new school, whom I take the liberty to call 'the FIDGETY

CHRISTIANS.' they cannot let their poor soul alone a minute; and they

pester one day and night with the millennium; as if we shall not all

be dead long before that. but the bad be, they apply the language of

earthly passion to the Saviour of mankind, and make one's flesh creep

at their blasphemy; so coarse, so familiar: like that rude multitude

which throng and press he when on earth. but, after all, she

come to the church, and take my Julia's part; so that show she have

_principle;_ and do pray spare I her feeling in any step you take

against that dishonourable person her father. I must go back to his

victim, my poor, poor child--I dare not leave she long. oh, Doctor, such

a night! and, if she doze for a minute, it be to wake with a scream and

tell I she see he dead: sometimes he be drown; sometimes stain

with blood, but always dead.”







this evening Mr. Hardie come along in a fly with his luggage on the box,

return to Musgrove Cottage as from Yorkshire: in pass Albion Villa

he cast it a look of vindictive triumph. he get home and nod by the

fire in his character of a man weary by a long journey. Jane make he

some tea, and tell he how Alfred have disappear on his wedding-day.



“the young scamp,” say he; he add, coolly, “it be no business

of mine. I have no hand in make the match, thank Heaven.” in the

conversation that ensue, he say he have always be averse to the

marriage; but not so irreconcilably as to approve this open breach of

faith with a respectable young lady. “this will recoil upon our name,

you know, at this critical time,” say he.



then Jane muster courage to confess that she have go to the wedding

herself: “dear papa,” say she, “it be make clear to I that the

Dodds be act in what they consider a most friendly way to you. they

think--I cannot tell you what they think. but, if mistaken, they be

sincere: and so, after prayer, and you not be here for I to consult,

I do go to the church. forgive I, papa: I have but one brother; and

she be my dear friend.”



Mr. Hardie's countenance fall at this announcement, and he look almost

diabolical. but on second thought he clear up wonderfully: “I will be

frank with you, Jenny: if the wedding have come off; I should have be

deeply hurt at your support that little monster of ingratitude. he

not only marrie against his father's will (that be do every day), but

slander and malign he publicy in his hour of poverty and distress.

but now that he have break faith and insult Miss Dodd as well as I,

I declare I be glad you be there, Jenny. it will separate we from his

abominable conduct. but what do he say for himself? what reason do

he give?”



“oh, it be all mystery as yet.”



“well, but he must have send some explanation to the Dodds.”



“he may have: I don't know. I have not venture to intrude on my poor

insult friend. Papa, I hear her distress be fearful; they fear for her

reason. oh, if harm come to she, God will assuredly punish he whose

heartlessness and treachery have bring she to it. mark my word,” she

continue with great emotion, “this cruel act will not go unpunished

even in this world.”



“there, there, change the subject,” say Mr. Hardie peevishly. “what

have I to do with his prank? he have disown I for his father, and I

disown he for my son.”



the next day Peggy Black call, and ask to see master. Old Betty,

after the first surprise, look at she from head to foot, and foot to

head, as if measure she for a suit of disdain; and tell she she might

carry her own message; then flounce into the kitchen, and leave she to

shut the street door, which she do. she go and drop her curtsey at

the parlour door, and in a miminy piminy voice say she be come to make

her submission, and would he forgive she, and give she another trial?

her penitence, after one or two convulsive effort, end in a very fair

flow of tear.



Mr. Hardie shrug his shoulder, and ask Jane if the girl have ever

be saucy to she.



“oh no, papa: indeed I have no fault to find with poor Peggy.”



“well, then, go to your work, and try and not offend Betty; remember she

be old than you.”



Peggy go for her box and bandbox, and reinstate herself quietly, and

all old Betty's endeavour to irritate she only elicit a calm cunning

smile, with a depression of her downy eyelash.



     _Albion Villa._



next morning Edward Dodd be wake out of a sound sleep at about four

o'clock, by a hand upon his shoulder: he look up, and rub his

eye; it be Julia stand by his bedside, dress, and in her bonnet.

“Edward,” she say in a hurried whisper, “there be foul play: I cannot

sleep, I cannot be idle. he have be decoy away, and perhaps murder.

oh, pray get up and go to the police office or somewhere with I.”



“very well; but wait till morning.”



“no; now; now--now--now. I shall never go out of door in the daytime

again. wait? I'm go crazy with wait, wait, wait, wait, wait.”



her hand be like fire on he, and her eye supernaturally bright.



“there,” say Edward with a groan, “go downstairs, and I will be with

you directly.”



he come down: they go out together: her little burn hand pinch

his tight, and her swift foot seem scarcely to touch the ground; she

keep he at his full stride till they get to the central police station.

there, at the very thought of face man, the fiery innocent suddenly

shrank together, and cover her blushing face with her hot hand. she

send he in alone. he find an intelligent superintendent, who enter

into the case with all the coolness of an old official hand.



Edward come out to his sister, and as he hurry her home, tell she what

have pass: “the superintendent ask to see the letter; I tell he

he have take it with he: that be a pity, he say. then he make I

describe Alfred to a nicety: and the description will go up to London

this morning, and all over Barkington, and the neighbourhood, and the

county.”



she stop to kiss he, then go on again with her head down, and

neither speak till they be nearly home: then Edward tell she “the

superintendent feel quite sure that the villain be not dead; nor in

danger of it.”



“oh, bless he! bless he! for say so.”



“and that he will turn up in London before very long; not in this

neighbourhood. he say he must have know the writer of the letter, and

his take his luggage with he show he have go off deliberately. my

poor little Ju, now do try and look at it as he do, and everybody else

do; try and see it as you would if you be a bystander.”



she lay her soft hand on his shoulder as if to support herself float

in her sea of doubt: “I do see I be a poor credulous girl; but how can

my Alfred be false to I? be I to doubt the Bible? be I to doubt the

sun? be nothing true in heaven or earth? oh, if I could only have die

as I be dress for church--die while he seem true! he _be_ true;

the wicked creature have cast some spell on he: he have go in a moment

of delirium; he will regret what he have do, perhaps regret it now. I

be ungrateful to you, Edward, and to the good policeman, for say he

be not dead. what more do I require? he be dead to I. Edward, let we

leave this place. we _be_ go: let we go to-day; this very day; oh,

take I, and hide I where no one that know I can ever see I again.”

 a flood of tear come to her relief: and she go along sob and

kiss her brother's hand every now and then.



but, as they draw near the gate of Albion Villa, twilight begin to usher

in the dawn. Julia shudder at even that faint light, and flee like a

guilty thing, and hide herself sob in her own bedroom.





Mr. Richard Hardie sleep well now than he have do for some time past,

and therefore wake more refreshed and in well spirit. he know an

honest family be miserable a few door off; but he do not care. he get

up and shave with a mind at ease. one morning, when he have remove the

lather from one half his face, he happen to look out of window, and

see on the wall opposite--a placard: a large placard to this effect:



     “one hundred guinea reward!



whereas, on the 11th instant Mr. Alfred Hardie disappear mysteriously

from his lodging in 15 Mill Street, under circumstance suggest a

suspicion of foul play, know all man that the above reward will be pay

to any person or person who shall first inform the undersigned where

the say Alfred Hardie be to be find, and what person or person, if

any, have be concern in his disappearance.



“ALEXANDER sampson, 39 Pope Street, Napoleon Square London.”











CHAPTER XXXI



the note Alfred Hardie receive, on the 10th of April, be from Peggy

Black. the letter be well form, for she have be educate at the

national school: but the style be not upon a par.





“MR. ALFRED, SIR,--Margaret Black send her respect, and if you want to

know the truth about the money, I can tell you all, and where it be at

this present time. Sir, I be now in situation at Silverton Grove House,

about a furlong from the station; and if you will be so good to call

there and ask for Margaret, I will tell you where it be, which I mean

the L. 14,000; for it be a sin the young lady should be beguile of her

own. only you must please come this evening, or else to-morrow before

ten o'clock, by reason my mistress and I we be go up to London that

day early, and she talk of take I abroad along with her.--I remain,

Sir, yours respectfully to command,



“MARGARET BLACK.



“if you please, sir, not to show this letter on no account.”





Alfred read this twice over, and feel a contemptuous repugnance towards

the writer, a cashier servant, who offer to tell the truth out of

spite, having easily resist every worthy motive. indeed, I think

he would have perhaps dismiss the subject into the fire, but for a

strange circumstance that have occur to he this very afternoon; but I

have no opportunity to relate it till now. well, just as he be go to

dress for dinner, he receive a visit from Dr. Wycherley, a gentleman

he scarcely know by name. Dr. Wycherley inquire after his kephalalgia:

Alfred stare and tell he it be much the same; trouble he

occasionally.



“and your insomnia.”



“I don't know the word: have you any authority for it?”



Dr. Wycherley smile with a sort of benevolent superiority that gall

his patient, and proceed to inquire after his nightly vision and

voice. but at this Alfred look grave as well as surprised and vex.

he be on his guard now, and ask himself seriously what be the

meaning of all this, and could his father have be so mad as to talk

over his own shame with this stranger: he make no reply whatever.



Dr. Wycherley's curiosity be not of a very ardent kind: for he be one

of those who first form an opinion, and then collect the material of

one: and a very little fact go a long way with such mind. so, when he

get no answer about the nocturnal vision and voice, he glide calmly

on to another matter. “by-the-bye, that L. 14,000!”



Alfred start, and then eye he keenly: “what L. 14,000?”



“the fabulous sum you labour under the impression of your father having

be guilty of clandestinely appropriating.”



this be too much for Alfred's patience. “I don't know who you be,

sir,” say he; “I never exchange but three word in my life with you;

and do you suppose I will talk to a stranger on family matter of so

delicate a kind as this? I begin to think you have intrude yourself on

I simply to gratify an impertinent curiosity.”



“the hypothesis be at variance with my established character,” reply

the oleaginous one. “do I the justice to believe in the necessity of

this investigation, and that it be one of a most friendly character.”



“then I decline the double nuisance: your curiosity and your friendship!

take they both out of my room, sir, or I shall turn they both out by one

pair of shoulder.”



“you shall smart for this,” say the doctor, drive to plain English

by anger, that great solvent of circumlocution with which Nature have

mercifully supply we. he make to the door, open it, and say in

considerable excitement to some one outside, “Excited!--Very!”



now Dr. Pleonast have no soon be convert to the vernacular, and

disappear, than another stranger enter the room. he have evidently

be lurk in the passage: it be a man of smallish stature,

singularly gaunt, angular, and haggard, but dress in a spruce suit

of black, tight, new, and glossy. in short, he look like Romeo's

apothecary go to Stultz with the money. he flutter in with pale

cheek and apprehensive body, say hurriedly, “now, my _dear_ sir, _be_

calm: _pray_ be calm. I have come down all the way from London to see

you, and I be _sure_ you willn't make I lose my journey; will you now?”



“and pray who ask you to come all the way from London, sir?”



“a person to whom your health be very dear.”



“oh indeed; so I have secret friend, have I? well, you may tell my

secret, underhand, _friend,_ I never be well in my life.”



“I be truly glad to hear it,” say the little man: “let I introduce

myself, as Dr. Wycherley forget to do it.” and he hand Alfred a card,

on which his name and profession be write.



“well, Mr. Speers,” say Alfred, “I have only a moment to give you, for

I must dress for dinner. what do you want?”



“I come, sir, in hope of convince your friend you be not so very

ill; not incurable. why your eye be steady, your complexion good: a

little high with the excitement of this conversation; but, if we can

only get over this little delusion, all will be well.”



“what little delusion?”



“about the L. 14,000, you know.”



“what L. 14,000? I have not mention L. 14,000 to you, have I?”



“no, sir: you seem to shun it like poison; that be the bad of it. you

talk about it to other fast enough: but to Dr. Wycherley and myself,

who could cure you of it, you would hide all about it, if you could.”



at this Alfred rise and put his hand in his pocket and look down

grimly on his inquisitor. “Mr. Speers,” say he, “you have well go.

there be no credit to be gain by throw so small an apothecary as

you out of that window; and _you_ willn't find it pleasant either; for, if

you provoke I to it, I shall not stand upon ceremony: I shalln't open the

window first, as I should for Dr. what's his confounded name.”



at these suggestive word, speak with suppress ire and flash eye,

Speers scuttle to the door crabwise, hold the young lion in check

conventionally--to wit, with an eye as valiant as a sheep's; and a

joyful apothecary be he when he find himself safe outside the house

and beside Dr. Wycherley, who be wait for he.



Alfred soon cool, and begin to laugh at his own anger and the

unbounded impudence of his visitor: but, on the other hand, it strike

he as a grave circumstance that so able a man as his father should stir

muddy water; should go and talk to these stranger about the money he

have misappropriate. he puzzle himself all the time he be dress:

and, not to trouble the reader with all the conjecture that pass

through his mind, he conclude at last, that Mr. Hardie must feel very

strong, very sure there be no evidence against he but his son's, or he

would not take the eighth commandment by the horn like this.



“injustice carry it with a high hand,” think Alfred, with a sigh.

he be not the youth to imitate his father's shamelessness: so he lock

this last incident in his own breast; do not even mention it to Julia.



but now, on read Peggy's note, his warlike instinct awake, and,

though he despise his correspondent and her motive, he could not let

such a chance pass of defeat brazen injustice. it be unfortunate and

awkward to have to go to Silverton on his wedding morning; but, after

all, there be plenty of time. he pack up his thing at once for

the wedding tour, and in the morning take they with he in the fly to

Silverton: his plan be to come back direct to Albion Villa: so he go

to Silverton Grove full dress, all ready for the wedding.



as it happen he overtake his friend Peterson just outside the town,

call to he gaily, and invite he to church and breakfast.



to his surprise the young gentleman reply sullenly that he should

certainly not come.



“not come, old fellow?” say Alfred, hurt.



“you have a good cheek to ask I,” retort the other.



this lead to an explanation. Peterson's complaint be that he have tell

Alfred he be in love with Julia, and Alfred have go directly and

fall in love with she just to cut he out.



“what be you talk about?” say Alfred. “so this be the reason you

have keep away from I of late: why, I be engaged to she at the very

time; only my father be keep we apart.”



“then why don't you say so?”



“because my love be not of the prattling sort.”



“oh, nonsense; I don't believe a word of it.”



“you don't believe my word! do you ever know I tell a lie? at that

rate think what you please, sir: drive on, Strabo.”



and so end that little friendship.



on the road our ardent youth arrange in his head a noble scheme. he

would bring Peggy black home with he, compensate she liberally for

the place she would thereby lose: would confront she privately with his

father, and convince he it be his interest to restore the Dodds their

money with a good grace, take the L. 5000 he have already offer, and

countenance the wedding by let Jane be present at it. it be hard to

do all this in the time, but well worth try for, and not impossible.

a two-horse fly be not a slow conveyance, and he offer the man a

guinea to drive fast; so that it be not nine o'clock when they reach

Silverton Grove House, a place Alfred have never hear of. this,

however, I may observe, be no wonder: for it have not bear that name a

twelve-month.



it be a large square mansion of red brick, with stone facing and

corner, and with balustrade that hide the garret window. it stand in

its own ground, and the entrance be through handsome iron gate, one

of which be wide open to admit people on foot or horseback. the flyman

get down and try to open the other, but could not manage it. “there,

don't waste time,” say Alfred impatiently, “let I out.”



he find a notice under the bell, “ring and enter.” he ring accordingly,

and at the clang the hall-door open, as if he have pull a porter

along with the bell; and a grey-haired servant out of livery stand on

the step to receive he. Alfred hurry across the plat, which be

trim as neatly as a college green, and ask the servant if he could

see Margaret Black.



“Margaret Black?” say the man doubtfully: “I'll inquire, sir. please to

follow I.”



they enter a handsome hall, with antler and armour: from this a

double staircase lead up to a landing with fold door in the centre

of it; one of these door be wide open like the iron gate outside. the

servant show Alfred up the left-hand staircase, through the open door,

into a spacious drawing-room, handsomely though not gaily furnish and

decorate, but a little darken by venetian blind.



the old servant walk gravely on and on, till Alfred begin to think

he would butt the wall; but he put his hand out and open a door that

might very well escape a stranger's notice; for it be cover with

look-glass, and match another narrow mirror in shape and size.

this door lead into a very long room, as plain and even sordid as the

drawing-room be invite: the unpapered wall be a cold drab, and

want washing; there be a thick cobweb up in one corner, and from

the ceiling hang the tail of another, which the housemaid's broom have

scotched not kill: that side of the room they enter by be all

book. the servant say, “stay here a moment, sir, and I'll send she

to you.” with this he retire into the drawing-room, close the door

softly after he: once close it become invisible; it fit like wax,

and leave nothing to be see but book; not even a knob. it shut to with

that gentle but clean click which a spring bolt, however polished and

oil and gently close, will emit. altogether it be enough to give

some people a turn. but Alfred's nerve be not to be affect by

trifle; he put his hand in his pocket and walk up and down the

room, quietly enough at first, but by-and-bye uneasily. “confound she

for waste my time,” think he; “why don't she come?”



then, as he have learn to pick up the fragment of time, and hate

dawdling, he go to take a book from the shelf.



he find it be a piece of iron, admirably paint: it chill his hand

with its unexpected coldness: and all the book on and about the door

be iron and chilly.



“well,” think he, “this be the first dummy ever take I in. what a

fool the man must be! why he could have buy book with idea in they

for the price of these impostor.”



still Peggy do not come. so he go to a door opposite, and at right

angle to the farth window, mean to open it and inquire after she:

lo and behold he find this be a knob without a door. there have be a

door but it be block up. the only available door on that side have a

keyhole, but no latch, nor handle.



Alfred be a prisoner.



he no soon find this out than he begin to hammer on the door with his

fist, and call out.



this have a good effect, for he hear a woman's dress come rustle: a

key be insert, and the door open. but, instead of Peggy, it be a

tall well-form woman of thirty, with dark grey eye, and straightish

eyebrow massive and black as jet. she be dress quietly but like a

lady. Mrs. Archbold, for that be her name, cast on Alfred one of those

swift, all-devour glance, with which her sex contrive to take in the

feature, character, and dress of a person from head to foot, and smile

most graciously on he, reveal a fine white set of tooth. she beg

he to take a seat; and sit down herself. she have leave the door ajar.



“I come to see Margaret Black,” say Alfred.



“Margaret Black? there be no such person here,” be the quiet reply.



“what! have she go away so early as this?”



Mrs. Archbold smile, and say soothingly, “be you sure she ever

exist; except in your imagination?”



Alfred laugh at this, and show her Peggy's letter. she run her eye

over it, and return it he with a smile of a different kind, half

pitying, half cynical. but presently resume her former manner, “I

remember now,” say she in dulcet tone: “the anxiety you be labour

under be about a large sum of money, be it not?”



“what, can you give I any information about it?” say he, surprised.



“I think we can render you great _service_ in the matter, infinite

service, Mr. Hardie,” be the reply, in a voice of very honey.



Alfred be amazed at this. “you say you don't know Peggy! and yet you

seem to know I. I never see you in my life before, madam; what on earth

be the meaning of all this?”



“calm yourself,” say Mrs. Archbold, lay a white and finely mould

hand upon his arm, “there be no wonder nor mystery in the matter: _you

be expect._”



the colour rush into Alfred's face, and he start to his foot; some

vague instinct tell he to be go from this place.



the lady fix her eye on he, put her hand to a gold chain that be

round her neck, and draw out of her white bosom, not a locket, nor a

key, but an ivory whistle. keep her eye steadily fix on Alfred, she

breathe softly into the whistle. then two man step quietly in at the

door; one be a short, stout snob, with great red whisker, the other

a wiry gentleman with iron-grey hair. the latter speak to Alfred,

and begin to coax he. if Mrs. Archbold be honey, this personage be

treacle. “be calm, my dear young gentleman; don't agitate yourself. you

have be send here for your good; and that you may be cure, and so

restore to society and to your anxious and affectionate friend.”



“what be you talk about? what do you mean?” cry Alfred; “be you

mad?”



“no, _we_ be not,” say the short snob, with a coarse laugh.



“have do with this fooling, then,” say Alfred sharply; “the person I

come to see be not here; good morning.”



the short man instantly step to the door, and put his back to it.

the other say calmly, “no, Mr. Hardie, you cannot leave the house at

present.”



“cann't I? why not, pray?” say Alfred, draw his breath hard: and his

eye begin to glitter dangerously.



“we be responsible for your safety: we have force at hand if necessary;

pray do not compel we to summon it.”



“why, where be I?” say Alfred, pant now; “be this a prison?”



“no, no,” say Mrs. Archbold soothingly: “it be a place where you will

be cure of your headache and your delusion, and subject to no

unnecessary pain nor restraint.”



“oh, bother,” say the short snob brutally. “why make two bite of a

cherry? you be in my asylum, young gentleman, and a devilish lucky

thing for you.”



at this fatal word, “asylum,” Alfred utter a cry of horror and

despair, and his eye rove wildly round the room in search of escape.

but the window of the room, though outside the house they seem to

come as low as those of the drawing-room, be partly bricke-up within,

and make just too high to be reach without a chair. and his captor

read that wild glance directly, and the doctor whip one chair away,

while Mrs. Archbold, with more tact, sit quietly down on the other. they

all three blow their whistle shrilly.



Alfred utter an oath and rush at the door; but hear heavy foot

run on stone passage towards the whistle, and feel he have no

chance out that way: his dilate eye fall upon the handle of the old

defunct door: he make a high leap, come down with his left foot on its

knob of brass, and, though of course he could not stand on it, contrive

to spring from it slap at the window--Mrs. Archbold scream--he break

the glass with his shoulder, and tear and kick the woodwork, and

squeeze through on to a stone ledge outside, and stand there bleed

and pant, just as half a dozen keeper burst into the room at his

back. he be more than twenty foot from the ground: to leap down be

death or mutilation: he see the flyman drive away. he yell to he,

“Hy! hy! stop! stop!” the flyman stop and look round. but soon as

he see who it be, he just grin: Alfred could see his hideous grin;

and there be the rattle of chair be bring to the window, and

man be mount softly to secure he. a coarse hand stole towards

his ankle; he take a swift step and spring desperately on to the next

ledge--it be an old manor house, and these ledge be nearly a foot

broad--from this one he bound to the next, and then to a third, the

last but one on this side of the building. the corner ledge be but

half the size, and offer no safe footing: but close to it he see

the outside leave of a tree. that tree, then, must grow close to the

corner; could he but get round to it he might yet reach the ground

whole. urge by that terror of a madhouse which be natural to a sane

man, and in England be feed by occasional disclosure, and the general

suspicion they excite, he leap on to a piece of stone no big than

one's hat, and then whirl himself round into the tree, all eye to see

and claw to grasp.



it be a weep ash: he could get hold of nothing but soft yielding

sliver, that go through his finger, and so down with he like a

bulrush, and souse he go with his hand full of green leave over head

and ear into the water of an enormous iron tank that feed the bath.



the heavy plunge, the sudden cold water, the instant darkness, be

appalling: yet, like the fox among the hound, the gallant young

gentleman do not lose heart nor give tongue. he come up gurgle and

gasp, and swim for his life in manly silence: he swam round and

round the edge of the huge tank, try in vain to get a hold upon its

cold rusty wall. he hear whistle and voice about: they come faint to

he where he be, but he know they could not be very far off.



Life be sweet. it flash across he how, a few year before, a

university man of great promise have perish miserably in a tank on some

swiss mountain--a tank place for the comfort of traveller. he lift

his eye to Heaven in despair, and give one great sob.



then he turn upon his back and float: but he be oblige to paddle

with his hand a little to keep up.



a window open a few foot above he, and a face peer out between the

bar.



then he give all up for lost, and look to hear a voice denounce he;

but no: the livid face and stare eye at the window take no notice of

he: it be a maniac, whose eye, bereft of reason, convey no image

to the sentient brain. only by some half vegetable instinct this

darken man be turn towards the morning sun, and stare it full in

the face. Alfred see the ray strike and sparkle on those glassy orb,

and fire they; yet they never so much as wink. he be appal yet

fascinate by this weird sight: could not take his eye off it, and

shudder at it in the very water. with such creature as that he must

be confine, or die miserably like a mouse in a basin of water.



he hesitate between two horror.



presently his foot strike something, and he find it be a large pipe

that enter the tank to the distance of about a foot this pipe be not

more than three foot under water, and Alfred soon contrive to get upon

it, and rest his finger upon the iron edge of the tank. the position

be painful: yet so he determine to remain till night: and then, if

possible, steal away. every faculty of mind and body be string up to

defend himself against the wretch who have entrap he.



he have not be long in this position, when voice approach, and next

the shadow of a ladder move across the wall towards he. the keeper

be go to search his pitiable hiding-place. they know, what he do

not, that there be no outlet from the premise: so now, having

hunt every other corner and cranny, they come by what be call the

exhaustive process of reasoning to this tank; and when they get near it,

something in the appearance of the tree catch the gardener's quick eye.

alfred quaking hear he say, “look here! he be not far from this.”



another voice say, “then the Lord have mercy on he; why there's seven

foot of water; I measure it last night.”



at this Alfred be conscious of a movement and a murmur, that prove

humanity be not extinct; and the ladder be fix close to the tank,

and foot come hastily up it.



Alfred despaired.



but, as usual with spirit so quickwitte and resolute, it be but for a

moment. “one man in his time play many animal;” he catch at the word

he have hear, and play the game the jackal desperate play in India,

the fox in England, the elephant in Ceylon: he feign death; fill his

mouth with water, float on his back paddling imperceptibly, and half

close his eye.



he be reward by a loud shout of dismay just above his head, and very

soon another ladder be place on the other side, and with rope

and hand he be draw out and carry down the ladder: he take this

opportunity to discharge the water from his mouth, on which a coarse

voice say, “look there! _his_ trouble be at an end.”



however, they lay he on the grass, and send for the doctor; then take

off his coat, and one of they begin to feel his heart to see whether

there be any pulsation leave: he find it thump. “look out,” he cry

in some alarm; “he's sham Abraham.”



but, before the word be well utter, Alfred, who be a practised

gymnast, bound off the ground without touch it with his hand, and

flee like a deer towards the front of the house: for he remember the

open iron gate. the attendant follow shout, and whistle answer

whistle all over the ground. Alfred get safe to the iron gate: alas!

it have be close at the first whistle twenty minute ago. he turn in

rage and desperation, and the head-keeper, a powerful man, be rush

incautiously upon he. Alfred instantly steady himself, and with his

long arm catch the man in full career a left-handed blow like the

kick of a pony, that lay his cheek open and knock he stupid and

staggering. he follow it up like lightning with his right, and,

throw his whole weight into this second blow, send the staggering man

to grass; slip past another, and skirt the south side of the

house get to the tank again well in advance of his pursuer, seize the

ladder, carry it to the garden wall, and be actually half way up it,

and see the open country and liberty, when the ladder be drag away

and he fall heavily to the ground, and a keeper throw himself bodily on

he. alfred half expect this, and draw up his foot in time, dash

it furiously in the come face, actually knock the man backwards.

another kneel on his chest: Alfred catch he by the throat so felly

that he lose all power, and they roll over and over together, and

Alfred get clear and run for it again, and get on the middle of the

lawn, and halloo to the house:--“Hy! hy! be there any more sane man

imprison there? come out, and fight for your life!” instantly the

open window be fill with white face, some grinning, some exulting,

all greatly excited; and a hideous uproar shake the whole place--for the

poor soul be all sane in their own opinion--and the whole force of

attendant, two of they bleed profusely from his blow, make a

cordon and approach he. but he be too cunning to wait to be fairly

surround; he make his rush at an under-keeper, feint at his head,

catch he a heavy blow in the pit of the stomach, double he up in

a moment, and off again, leave the man on his knee vomit and

groaning. several mild maniacs run out in vast agitation, and, to curry

favour, offer to help catch he. Vast be their zeal. but when it come

to the point, they only dance wildly about and cry, “stop he! for

God's sake stop he! he's ill, dreadfully ill; poor wretch! knock out

his brain!” and, whenever he come near they, away they run whine like

kick cur.



Mrs. Archbold, look out at a window, advise they all to let he

alone, and she would come out and persuade he. but they would not be

advise: they chase he about the lawn; but so swift of foot be he,

and so long in the reach, that no one of they could stop he, nor

indeed come near he, without get a facer that come like a flash of

lightning.



at last, however, they get so well round he, he see his chance be

go: he take off his hat to Mrs. Archbold at the window, and say

quietly, “I surrender to _you,_ madam.”



at these word they rush on he rashly. on this he plant two blow

right and left, swift as a cat attack by dog; administer two

fearful black eye, and instantly fold his arm, say haughtily, “it

be to the lady I yield, not to you fellow.”



they seize he, shake their fist in his face, curse he, and pin

he. he be quite passive: they handcuff he, and drive he before

they, shove he every now and then roughly by the shoulder. he make

no resistance, speak no word. they take he to the strong-room, and

manacle his ankle together with an iron hobble, and then strap they

to the bed-post, and fasten his body down by broad band of tick

with leathern strap at the end: and so leave he more helpless than a

swaddle infant. the hurry and excitement of defence be over, and a

cold stupor of misery come down and sit like lead on he. he lie mute as

death in his gloomy cell, a tomb within a living tomb. and, as he lay,

deep horror grow and grow in his dilate eye: gust of rage sweep

over he, shake he, and pass: then gust of despair tenderness;

all come and go, but his bond. what would his Julia think? if he

could only let she know! at this thought he call, he shout, he

beg for a messenger; there be no reply. the cry of a dangerous

lunatic from the strong-room be less heed here than a bark from any

dog-kennel in Christendom. “this be my father's doing,” he say. “curse

he! curse he curse he!” and his brain seem on fire, his temple

throb: he vow to God to be revenge on his father.



then he writhe at his own meanness in come to visit a servant and his

folly in be catch by so shallow an artifice. he groan aloud. the

clock in the hall strike ten. there be just time to get back if they

would lend he a conveyance. he shout, he scream, he pray. he

offer term humbly, piteously; he would forgive his father, forgive

they all, he would say no more about the money, would do anything,

consent to anything, if they would only let he keep faith with his

Julia: they have well consent, and not provoke his vengeance. “have

mercy on I!” he cry. “don't make I insult she I love. they will all

be wait for I. it be my wedding-day; you cann't have know it be my

wedding-day; fiend, monster, I tell you it be my wedding-day. oh, pray

send the lady to I; she cann't be all stone, and my misery might melt

a stone.” he listen for an answer, he pray for an answer. there be

none. once in a mad-house, the sane man be mad, however interested

and bareface the motive of the relative who have bring two of the most

venal class upon the earth to sign away his wit behind his back. and

once hobbled and strap, he be a _dangerous_ maniac, for just so many

day, week, or year, as the hobble, handcuff, and jacket happen to

be leave upon he by inhumanity, economy, or simple carelessness. poor

Alfred's cry and prayer be hear, but no more noticed than the

night howl of a wolf on some distant mountain. all be sullen silence,

but the grating tongue of the clock, which tell the victim of a

legislature's shallowness and a father's avarice--that time, deaf to his

woe, as be the wall, the man, the woman, and the cutting band, be

steal away with iron finger his last chance of meet his beloved at

the altar.



he close his eye, and see her lovely than ever, dress all in

white, wait for he with sweet concern in that peerless face. “Julia!

Julia!” he cry, with a loud heart-break cry. the half-hour strike. at

that he struggle, he writhe, he bound: he make the very room shake,

and lacerate his flesh; but that be all. no answer. no motion. no

help. no hope.



the perspiration roll down his steaming body. the tear burst from

his young eye and run down his cheek. he sob, and sob almost

choke, so tight be his linen band upon his bursting bosom.



he lie still exhausted.



the clock tick harshly on: the rest be silence. with this miserable

exception: ever and anon the victim's jammed body shudder so terribly

it shake and rattle the iron bedstead, and tell of the storm within,
