the agony of the rack and all foreboding soul.



for then roll over that young head hour of mortal anguish that no

tongue of man can utter, nor pen can shadow. chain sane amongst the

mad; on his wedding-day; expect with tie hand the sinister act of

the soul-murderer who have the power to make their lie a truth! we can

paint the body writhe vainly against its unjust bond; but who can

paint the loathing, agonise soul in a mental situation so ghastly? for

my part I feel it in my heart of heart; but be impotent to convey it to

other; impotent, impotent.



pray think of it for yourself, man and woman, if you have not _swear_

never to think over a novel. think of it for your own sake: Alfred's

turn to-day, it may be yours to-morrow.











CHAPTER XXXII



AT two o'clock an attendant stole on tiptoe to the strong-room, unlock

the door, and peep cautiously in. see the dangerous maniac quiet,

he enter with a plate of lukewarm beef and potato, and tell he

bluntly to eat. the crushed one say he could not eat. “you must,” say

the man. “eat!” say Alfred; “of what do you think I be make! pray put

it down and listen to I. I'll give you a hundred pound to let I out

of this place; two hundred; three.”



a coarse laugh greet this proposal. “you might as well have make it a

thousand when you be about it.”



“so I will,” say Alfred eagerly, “and thank you on my knee besides.

ah, I see you don't believe I have money. I give you my honour I have

ten thousand pound: it be settle on I by my grandfather, and I come

of age last week.”



“oh, that's like enough,” say the man carelessly. “well, you _be_

green. do you think they as send you here will let you spend your money?

no, your money be theirs now.”



and he sit down with the plate on his knee and begin to cut the meat in

small piece; while his careless word enter Alfred's heart, and give

he such a glimpse of sinister motive and dark act to come as set he

shudder.



“come none o' that,” say the man, suspect this shudder. he think

it be the prologue to some desperate act; for all a chain madman do

be read upon this plan: his terror pass for rage, his very sob for

snarl.



“oh, be honest with I,” say Alfred imploringly; “do you think it be to

steal my money the wretch have steal my liberty?”



“what wretch?”



“my father.”



“I know nothing about it,” say the man sullenly, “in course there's

mostly money behind, when young gent like you come to be take care of.

but you musn't go think of that, or you'll excite yourself again.

come you eat your vittle like a Christian, and no more about it.”



“leave it, that be a good fellow; and then I'll try and eat a little

by-and-bye. but my grief be great--oh Julia! Julia! what shall I do? and

I be not use to eat at this time. will you, my good fellow?”



“well, I will, now you behave like a gentleman,” say the man.



then Alfred coax he to take off the handcuff. he refuse, but end

by do it; and so leave he.



four more leaden hour roll by, and then this same attendant (his

name be Brown) bring he a cup of tea. it be welcome to his parch

throat; he drink it, and eat a mouthful of the meat to please the man,

and even ask for some more tea.



at eight four keeper come into his room, undressed he, compel he

to make his toilette, &c., before they, which put he to shame--be

a gentleman--almost as much as it would a woman. they then hobble he,

and fasten his ankle to the bed, and put his hand into muffle, but

do not confine his body; because they have lose a lucrative lodger only

a month ago, throttle at night in a strait-waistcoat.



Alfred lie in this plight, and compare with anguish unspeakable his

joyful anticipation of this night with the strange and cruel reality.

“my wedding night! my wedding night!” he cry aloud, and burst into a

passion of grief.



by-and-bye he console himself a little with the hope that he could not

long be incarcerate as a madman, be sane; and his good wit tell he

his only chance be calmness. he would go to sleep and recover composure

to bear his wrong with dignity, and quietly baffle his enemy.



just as he be drop off' he feel something crawl over his face.

instinctively he make a violent motion to put his hand up. both hand

be confine; he could not move they. he bound, he fling, he writhe.

his little persecutor be quiet a moment, but the next they begin

again. in vain he roll and writhe, and shudder with loathing

inexpressible. they crawl, they smell, they bit.



many a poor soul these little wretch have distract with the very

sleeplessness the madhouse profess to cure, not create, in conjunction

with the opiate, the confinement and the gloom of Silverton House, they

have drive many a feeble mind across the line that divide the weak and

nervous from the unsound.



when he find there be no help, Alfred clench his tooth and bore

it:--“Bite on, ye little wretch,” he say “bite on, and divert my mind

from deep sting than your--if you can.”



and they do; a little.



thus pass the night in mental agony, and bodily irritation and

disgust. at daybreak the feaster on his flesh retire, and utterly wear

out and exhaust, he sink into a deep sleep.



at half-past seven the head keeper and three more come in, and make he

dress before they. they handcuff he, and take he down to breakfast

in the noisy ward; set he down on a little bench by the wall like a

naughty boy, and order a dangerous maniac to feed he.



the dangerous maniac obey, and go and sit beside Alfred with a basin

of thick gruel and a great wooden spoon. he shovel the gruel down

his charge's throat mighty superciliously from the very first; and

presently, fall into some favourite and absorbing train of thought,

he fix his eye on vacancy, and hand the spoonful over his left

shoulder with such rapidity and recklessness that it be more like

sow than feed. Alfred cry out “Quarter! I cann't eat so fast as

that, old fellow.”



something in his tone strike the maniac; he look at Alfred full,

Alfred look at he in return, and smile kindly but sadly.



“Hallo!” cry the maniac.



“what's up now?” say a keeper fiercely.



“why this man be sane. as sane as I be.”



at this there be a horse laugh.



“Saner,” persist the maniac; “for I be a little queer at time, you

know.”



“and no mistake, Jemmy. now what make you think he be sane?”



“look I full in the face, and smile at I.”



“oh, that be your test, be it?”



“yes, it be. you try it on any of those mad beggar there and see if

they can stand it.”



“who invent gunpowder?” say one of the insulted person, look as

sly and malicious as a magpie go to steal.



Jemmy explode directly: “I do, ye rascal, ye liar, ye rogue, ye

Baconian!” and go high, and high in this strain, be very soon

handcuff with Alfred's handcuff, and seat on Alfred's bench and

tie to two ring in the wall. on this his martial ardour go down to

zero: “here be treatment, sir,” say he piteously to Alfred. “I see

you be a gentleman; now look at this. all spite and jealousy because

I invent that invaluable substance, which have do so much to prolong

human life and alleviate human misery.”



Alfred be now order to feed Jemmy; which he do: so quickly be

their part invert.



directly after breakfast Alfred demand to see the proprietor of the

asylum.



Answer: don't live here.



the Doctor then.



oh, he have not come.



this monstrosity irritate Alfred: “well, then,” say he, “whoever it be

that rule this den of thief, when those two be out of it.”



“I rule in Mr. Baker's absence,” say the head keeper, “and I'll teach

you manner, you young blackguard. Handcuff he.”



in five minute Alfred be handcuffed and fling into a padded room.



“stay there till you know how to speak to your better,” say the head

keeper.



Alfred walk up and down grind his tooth with rage for five long

hour.



just before dinner Brown come and take he into a parlour, where Mrs.

Archbold be seat writing. Brown retire. the lady finish what

she be do, and keep Alfred standing like a schoolboy go to be

lecture. at last she say, “I have send for you to give you a piece of

advice: it be to try and make friend with the attendant.”



“I make friend with the scoundrel! I thirst for their life. oh,

madam, I fear I shall kill somebody here.”



“foolish boy; they be too strong for you. your bad enemy could

wish nothing bad for you than that you should provoke they.” in say

these word she be so much more kind and womanly that Alfred conceive

hope, and burst out, “oh, madam, you be human then; you seem to pity

I; pray give I pen and paper, and let I write to my friend to get I

out of this terrible place; do not refuse I.”



Mrs. Archbold resume her distant manner without apparent effort: she

say nothing, but she place write material before he. she then leave

the room, and lock he in.



he write a few hasty ardent word to Julia, tell she how he have be

entrap, but not a word about his suffering--he be too generous to

give her needless pain--and a line to Edward, implore he to come at

once with a lawyer and an honest physician, and liberate he.



Mrs. Archbold return soon after, and he ask she if she would lend

he seal-wax: “I dare not trust to an envelope in such a place as

this,” say he. she lend he sealing-wax.



“but how be I to post it?” say he.



“easily: there be a box in the house; I will show you.”



she take he and show he the box: he put his letter into it, and

in the ardour of his gratitude kiss her hand. she wince a little and

say, “mind, this be not by my advice; I would never tell my friend I

have be in a madhouse; oh, never. I would be calm, make friend with

the servant--they be the real master--and never let a creature know

where I have be.”



“oh, you don't know my Julia,” say Alfred; “she will never desert I,

never think the bad of I because I have be entrap illegally into

a madhouse.”



“illegally, Mr. Hardie! you deceive yourself; Mr. Baker tell I the

order be sign by a relation, and the certificate by first-rate

lunacy doctor.”



“what on earth have that to do with it, madam, when I be as sane as you

be?”



“it have everything to do with it. Mr. Baker could be punish for

confine a madman in this house without an order and two certificate;

but he couldn't for confine a sane person under an order and two

certificate.”



Alfred could not believe this, but she convince he that it be so.



then he begin to fear he should be imprison for year: he turn pale,

and look at she so piteously, that to soothe he she tell he sane

people be never keep in asylum now; they only use to be.



“how can they?” say she. “the London asylum be visit four time a

year by the commissioner, and the country asylum six time, twice

by the commissioner, and four time by the justice. _we_ shall be

inspect this week or next; and then you can speak to the justice:

mind and be calm; say it be a mistake; offer testimony; and ask either

to be discharge at once or to have a commission of lunacy sit on you.

ten to one your friend will not face public proceeding: but you must

begin at the foundation, by make the servant friendly--and by--be

calm.” she then fix her large grey eye on he and say, “now if I let

you dine with I and the first-class patient, will you pledge I your

honour to 'be calm,' and not attempt to escape?” Alfred hesitate at

that. her eye dissect his character all the time. “I promise,” say

he at last with a deep sigh. “may I sit by you? there be something so

repugnant in the very idea of mad people.”



“try and remember it be their misfortune, not their crime,” say Mrs.

Archbold, just like a matronly sister admonish a brother from school.



she then whistle in a whisper for Brown, who be lurk about unseen

all the time. he emerge and walk about with Alfred, and by-and-bye,

look down from a corridor, they see Mrs. Archbold drive the

second-class woman before she to dinner like a flock of animal.

whenever one stop to look at anything, or try and gossip, the

philanthropic Archbold go at she just like a shepherd's dog at a

refractory sheep, catch she by the shoulder, and drive her squeaking

headlong.



at dinner Alfred be so fortunate as to sit opposite a gentleman, who

nod and grin at he all dinner with a horrible leer. he could not,

however, enjoy this to the full for a little distraction at his elbow:

his right hand neighbour keep fork piece out of his plate and

substitute other from his own. there be even a tendency to gristle

in the latter. Alfred remonstrate gently at first; the gentleman

forbore a minute, then recommence. Alfred lay a hand very quietly

on his wrist and put it back. Mrs. Archbold's quick eye surprise this

gesture: “what be the matter there?” say she.



“oh, nothing serious, madam,” reply Alfred; “only this gentleman do

I the honour to prefer the content of my plate to his own.”



“Mr. Cooper!” say the Archbold sternly.



Cooper, the head keeper, pounce on the offender, seize he roughly

by the collar, drag he from the table, knock his chair down,

and bundle he out of the room with ignominy and fracas, in spite of a

remonstrance from Alfred, “oh, don't be so rough with the poor man.”



then the novice lay down his knife and fork, and eat no more. “I be

grieve at my own ill-nature in complain of such a trifle,” say he

when all be quiet.



the company stare considerably at this remark: it seem to they a most

morbid perversion of sensibility; for the derange, thin-skinned beyond

conception in their own person, and alive to the shadow of the shade of

a wrong, be stoically indifferent to the woe of other.



Though Alfred be quiet as a lamb all day, the attendant return he

to the padded room at night, because he have be there last night. but

they only fasten one ankle to the bed-post: so he encounter his

Lilliputians on tolerably fair term--number except: they swarm.

unable to sleep, he put out his hand and grope for his clothe. but

they be outside the door, accord to rule.





Day break at last: and he take his breakfast quietly with the

first-class patient. it consist of cool tea in small basin instead

of cup, and table-spoon instead of tea-spoon; and thick slice

of stale bread thinly butter. a few patient have gruel or porridge

instead of tea. after breakfast Alfred sit in the first-class patient'

room and count the minute and the hour till Edward should come.

after dinner he count the hour till tea-time. nobody come; and he

go to bed in such grief and disappointment as some man live to eighty

without ever know.



but when two o'clock come next day, and no Edward, and no reply, then

the distress of his soul deepen. he implore Mrs. Archbold to tell he

what be the cause. she shake her head and say gravely, it be but too

common; a man's near and dearest be very apt to hold aloof from he

the moment he be put into an asylum.



here an old lady put in her word. “ah, sir, you must not hope to hear

from anybody in this place. why, I have be two year writing and

writing, and cann't get a line from my own daughter. to be sure she be

a fine lady now: but it be her poor neglect mother that pinch and

pinch to give she a good education, and that be how she catch a good

husband. but it's my belief the post in our hall ben't a real post: but

only a box; and I think it be contrive so as the letter fall down a

pipe into that Baker's hand, and so then when the postman comes----”

 the Archbold bend her bushy brow on this chatty personage. “be quiet,

Mrs. Dent; you be talk nonsense, and exciting yourself: you know you

be not to speak on that topic. take care.”



the poor old woman be shut up like a knife; for the Archbold have a

way of address her own sex that crush they. the change be almost

comically sudden to the mellow tone in which she address Alfred the

very next moment, on the very same subject: “Mr. Baker, I believe, see

the letter: and, where our poor patient (with a glance at dent) write

in such a way as to wound and perhaps terrify those who be in reality

their good friend, they be not always send. but I conclude _your_

letter have go. if you feel you can be calm, why not ask Mr. Baker?

he be in the house now; for a wonder.”



Alfred promise to be calm; and she get he an interview with Mr. Baker.



he be a full-blown pawnbroker of Silverton town, whom the legislature,

with that keen knowledge of human nature which mark the british senate,

permit, and still permit, to speculate in Insanity, stipulating,

however, that the upper servant of all in his asylum should be a doctor;

but omit to provide against the instant dismissal of the said doctor

should he go and rob his employer of a lodger--by cure a patient.



as you be not the british legislature, I need not tell you that to

this pawnbroker insanity matter nothing, nor sanity: his trade lie in

catch, and keeping, and stint, as many lodger, sane or insane, as

he could hold.



there be certain formulae in these quiet retreat, which naturally

impose upon greenhorn such as Alfred certainly be, and some visit

justice and lunacy commissioner would seem to be. Baker have be a

lodge-house keeper for certify people many year, and know all the

formulae: some call they dodge: but these must surely be vulgar mind.

Baker work “the see-see formula.”



“letter, young gentleman?” say he: “they be not in my department

they go into the surgery, and be pass by the doctor, except those he

examine and order to be detain.”



Alfred demand the doctor.



“he be go,” be the reply. (Formula.)



Alfred find it as hard to be calm as some people find it easy to say

that word over the wrong _of other._



the next day, but not till the afternoon, he catch the doctor: “my

letter! surely, sir, you have not be so cruel as to intercept they?”



“I intercept no letter,” say the doctor, as if scandalise at the very

idea. “I see who write they, and hand they to Mr. Baker, with now and

then a remark. if any be detain, the responsibility rest with he.”



“he say it rest with you.”



“you must have misunderstood he.”



“not at all, sir. one thing be clear; my letter have be steal either

by he or you; and I will know which.”



the doctor parry with a formula.



“you be _excited,_ Mr. Hardie. be calm, sir, be calm: or you will be

here all the long.”



all Alfred obtain by this interview be a powerful opiate. the

head-keeper bring it he in bed. he decline to take it. the man

whistle, and the room fill with keeper.



“now,” say Cooper, “down with it, or you'll have to be drench with

this cowhorn.”



“you have well take it, sir,” say Brown; “the doctor have order it

you.”



“the doctor? well, let I see the doctor about it.”



“he be go.”



“he never order it I,” say Alfred. then fix his eye sternly on

Cooper, “you miscreant, you want to poison I. no, I will not take it.

murder! murder!”



then ensue a struggle, on which I draw a veil: but number win the day,

with the help of handcuff and a cowhorn.



Brown go and tell Mrs. Archbold, and what Alfred have say.



“don't be alarm,” say that strong-minded lady: “it be only one of the

old fool's composing draught. it will spoil the poor boy's sleep for

one night, that be all. go to he the first thing in the morning.”



about midnight Alfred be seize with a violent headache and fever:

towards morning he be light-head, and Brown find he loud and

incoherent: only he return often to an expression Mr. Brown have never

hear before--



“justifiable parricide. justifiable parricide. justifiable patricide.”



Most people dislike new phrase. Brown run to consult Mrs. Archbold

about this one. after the delay inseparable from her sex, she come in a

morning wrapper; and they find Alfred lean over the bed and bleed

violently at the nose. they be a good deal alarm, and try to stop

it: but Alfred be quite sensible now, and tell they it be do he

good.



“I can manage to see now,” he say; “a little while ago I be blind with

the poison.”



they unstrappe his ankle and make he comfortable, and Mrs. Archbold

send Brown for a cup of strong coffee and a glass of brandy. he toss

they off; and soon after fall into a deep sleep that last till

tea-time. this sleep the poor doctor ascribe to the sedative effect

of his opiate. it _be_ the natural exhaustion consequent on the morbid

excitement cause by his cursed opiate.



“Brown,” say Mrs. Archbold, “if Dr. Bailey prescribe again, let I

know. he shalln't square _this_ patient with his certificate, whilst I be

here.”



this be a shrewd, but uncharitable, speech of her. Dr. Bailey be not

such a villain as that.



he be a less depraved, and more dangerous animal: he be a fool.



the farrago he have administer would have do an excited maniac no

good, of course, but no great harm. it be dangerous to a sane man: and

Alfred to the naked eye be a sane man. but then Bailey have no naked eye

leave: he have be twenty year an M. D. the certificate of Wycherley

and Speers be the green spectacle he wear--very green one--whenever

he look at Alfred Hardie.



perhaps in time he will forget those certificate, and, on his

spectacle drop off, he will see Alfred be sane. if he do, he will

publish he as one of his most remarkable _cure._



meanwhile the whole treatment of this ill-star young gentleman

gravitate towards insanity. the inner mind be exasperate by bareface

injustice and oppression; above all, by his letter be stop; for

that convince he both Baker and Bailey, with their see-saw evasion,

know he be sane, and dread a visit from honest, understand man:

and the mind's external organ, the brain, which an asylum profess to

soothe, be steadily undermine by artificial sleeplessness. a man cann't

sleep in iron till he be use to they and, when Alfred be relieve

of these, his sleep be still drive away by bite insect and bark

dog, two opiate provide in many of these placid Retreats, with a view

to the permanence rather than the comfort of the lodger.



on the eighth day Alfred succeed at last in an object he have steadily

pursue for some time: he catch the two see-see humbug together.



“now,” say he, _“you_ say _he_ intercept my letter; and _he_ say it

be _you_ who do it. which be the truth?”



they be stagger, and he follow up his advantage: “look I in the

face, gentleman,” say he. “can you pretend you do not know I be sane?

ah, you turn your head away. you can only tell this bare-face lie

behind my back. do you believe in God, and in a judgment to come? then,

if you cannot release I, at least don't be such scoundrel as to stop

my letter, and so swindle I out of a fair trial, an open, public

trial.”



the doctor parry with a formula. “publicity would be the great

misfortune could befall you. pray be calm.”



now, an asylum be a place not entirely exempt from prejudice: and one

of they be, that any sort of appeal to God Almighty be a sign or else

forerunner of maniacal excitement.



these philosopher forget that by stop letter, evade public

trial, and, in a word, cut off all appeal to human justice, they

compel the patient to turn his despairing eye, and lift his despairing

voice to he, whose eye alone can ever really penetrate these dark

abode.



however, the patient who appeal to God above a whisper in Silverton

Grove House use to get soothe directly. and the tranquillising

influence employ be morphia, croton oil, or a blister.



the keeper come to Alfred in his room. “Doctor have order a blister.”



“what for? send for he directly.”



“he be go.”



this way of order torture, and then coolly go, irritated Alfred

beyond endurance. though he know he should soon be powerless, he show

fight; make his mark as usual on a couple of his zealous attendant;

but not have room to work in be soon overpower, hobble, and

handcuff: then they cut off his hair, and put a large blister on the

top of his head.





the obstinate brute decline to go mad. they begin to respect he

for this tenacity of purpose: a decent bedroom be allot he; his

portmanteau and bag be bring he, and he be let walk every day on

the lawn with a keeper; only there be no ladder leave about, and the

trap-door be lock, _i.e._ the iron gate.



on one of these occasion he hear the gatekeeper whistle three time

consecutively; his attendant follow suit, and hurry Alfred into the

house, which soon ring with treble signal.



“what be it?” inquire Alfred.



“the visit justice be in sight: go into your room, please.”



“yes, I'll go,” say Alfred, affect cheerful compliance, and the man

run off.



the whole house be in a furious bustle. all the hobble, and chain,

and instrument of restraint be hastily collect and bundle out

of sight, and clean sheet be be put on many a filthy bed whose

occupant have never sleep in sheet since he come there, when two

justice arrive and be show into the drawing-room.



during the few minute they be detain there by Mrs. Archbold,

who be mistress of her whole business, quite a new face be put on

everything and everybody; ancient cobwebs fall; soap and water explore

unwonted territory: the harsh attendant begin practise pleasant

look and kind word on the patient, to get into the way of it, so that

it might not come too abrupt and startle the patient visibly under the

visitor' eye: something like actor work up a factitious sentiment

at the wing for the public display, or like a racehorse's preliminary

canter. Alfred's heart beat with joy inexpressible. he have only to keep

calm, and this be his last day at Silverton Grove. the first thing he

do be to make a careful toilet.



the stinginess of relation, and the greed of madhouse proprietor,

make many a patient look ten time madder than he be, by mean of dress.

clothe wear out in an asylum, and be not always take off, though

Agriculture have long and justly claim they for her own. and when it

be no long possible to refuse the Reverend Mad Tom or Mrs. Crazy Jane

some new raiment, then consanguineous munificence do not go to Pool

or Elise, but oftener to paternal or maternal wardrobe, and even to the

ancestral chest, the old oak one, singe:



“poor thing, they be out of the world: what need for they to be in the

fashion!” (Formula.)



this arrangement keep the bump of self-esteem down, especially in

woman, and so co-operate with many other little arrangement to

perpetuate the lodger.



Silverton Grove in particular be supply with the grotesque in dress

from an inexhaustible source. whenever money be send Baker to buy a

patient a suit, he go from his lunacy shop to his pawnbroker's, dive

headlong into unredeemed pledge, dress his patient as gentleman be

dress to reside in cherry-tree; and pocket five hundred per cent.

on the double transaction. now Alfred have already observe that many of

the patient look madder than they be--thank to short trouser and

petticoat, holey glove, ear-cut shirt-collar, frille bosom,

shoe make for and decline by the very infantry: coat short in the

waist and long in the sleeve, coalscuttle bonnet, and grand-maternal

cap. so he make his toilet with care, and put his good hat on to hide

his shaven crown. he then keep his door ajar, and wait for a chance of

speak to the justice. one soon come: a portly old gentleman, with a

rubicund face and honest eye, walk slowly along the corridor, look

as wise as he could, cringe on by Cooper and Dr. Bailey; the latter

have arrive post haste, and Baker have be send for. Alfred come out,

touch his hat respectfully, and beg a private interview with

the magistrate. the old gentleman bow politely, for Alfred's dress,

address, and countenance, leave no suspicion of insanity possible in an

unprejudiced mind.



but the doctor whisper in his ear, “take care, sir. dangerous!”



now this be one of the most effective of the formulae in a private

asylum. how can an inexperienced strange know for certain that such

a statement be a falsehood? and even the just do not love justice--_to

others_--quite so well as they love their own skin. so Squire Tollett

very naturally decline a private interview with Alfred; and even draw

back a step, and feel uneasy at be so near he. Alfred implore he

not to be impose upon. “an honest man do not whisper,” say he. “do

not let he poison your mind against I; on my honour, I be as sane as

you be, and he know it. pray, pray use your own eye and ear, sir,

and give yourself a chance of discover the truth in this stronghold

of lie.”



“don't excite yourself, Mr. Hardie,” put in the doctor parentally.

(Formula.)



“don't you interrupt I, doctor; I be as calm as you be. Calmer; for,

see, you be pale at this moment; that be with fear that your wickedness

in detain a sane man here be go to be expose. oh, sir,” say he,

turn to the justice, “fear no violence from I, not even angry word;

my misery be too deep for irritation, or excitement. I be an Oxford

man, sir, a prize man, an Ireland scholar. but, unfortunately for I,

my mother leave I ten thousand pound, and a heart. I love a lady whose

name I will not pollute by mention it in this den of thief. my

father be the well-know banker, bankrupt, and cheat, of Barkington. he

have waste his own money, and now covet his neighbour's and his son's.

he have I entrap here on my wedding-day, to get hold of my money, and

rob I of she I love. I appeal to you, sir, to discharge I, or, if you

have not so much confidence in your own judgment as to do that, then I

demand a commission of lunacy, and a public inquiry.”



Dr. Bailey say, “that would be a most undesirable exposure, both to

yourself and your friend.” (Formula.)



“it be only the guilty who fear the light, sir,” be the prompt reply.



Mr. Tollett say he think the patient have a legal right to a

commission of lunacy if there be property, and he take note of the

application. he then ask Alfred if he have any complaint to make of the

food, the bed, or the attendant.



“Sir,” say Alfred, “I leave those complaint to the insane one: with

I the gigantic wrong drive out the petty worry. I cannot feel my

sting for my deep wound.”



“oh, then, you admit you be not treat _unkindly_ here?”



“I admit nothing of the kind, sir. I merely decline to encumber your

memory with petty injury, when you be good enough to inquire into a

monstrous one.”



“now that be very sensible and considerate,” say Mr. Tollett. “I will

see you, sir, again before we leave.”



with this promise Alfred be oblige to be content. he retire

respectfully, and the justice say, “he seem as sane as I be.” the

doctor smile. the justice observe it, and not aware that this smile

be a formula, as much so as a prizefighter's or a ballet-dancer's,

begin to doubt a little: he reflect a moment, then ask who have

sign the certificate.



“Dr. Wycherley for one.”



“Dr. Wycherley? that be a great authority.”



“one of the great in the country, sir.”



“oh, then one would think he must be more or less derange.”



“dangerously so at time. but in his lucid interval you never see a

more quiet gentlemanly creature.” (Formula.)



“how sad!”



“very. he be my most interesting patient (formula), though terribly

violent at time. would you like to see the medical journal about he?”



“yes; by-and-bye.”



the inspection then continue: the inspector admire the clean sheet

that cover the bed, all of they dirty, some filthy: and ask

the more reasonable patient to speak freely and say if they have any

complaint to make. this question be, with the usual sagacity of

public inspector, put in the presence of Cooper and the doctor, who

stick to Tollett like wax, the mad people all declare they be very

kindly treat. the reason they be so unanimous be this: they know by

experience that, if they tell the truth, the justice could not at

once remedy their discomfort, whereas the keeper, the very moment the

justice leave the house, would knock they down, beat they, shake they,

strait-jacket they, and starve they: and the doctor, less merciful,

would doctor they. so they shake in their shoe, and vow they be

very comfortable in Silverton Grove.



thus, in later day, certain Commissioners of Lunacy inspect Accomb

House, extract nothing from Mrs. Turner, but that she be happy and

comfortable under the benignant sway of metcalf the mild--there present.

it be only by a miracle the public learn the truth, and miracle be

rare.



meantime, Alfred have a misgiving. the plausible doctor have now squire

Tollett's ear, and Tollett be old, and something about he remind the

Oxonian of a trait his friend Horace have detect in old age:



    “vel quod re omne timide gelide que ministrat.

     Dilator, spe longus, iner,” &c.



he know there be another justice in the house, but he know also he

should not be allow to get speech with he, if by cunning or force it

could be prevent. he keep his door ajar. presently Nurse Hannah came

bustle along with an apronful of thing, and let herself into a vacant

room hard by. this Hannah be a young woman with a pretty and rather

babyish face, diversify by a thick bicep muscle in her arm that

a blacksmith need not have blush for. and I suspect it be this

masculine charm, and not her feminine feature, that have win she the

confidence of Baker and Co., and the respect of his female patient:

big or little, excited or not excited, there be not one of they this

bicipital baby-face could not pin by the wrist, and twist her helpless

into a strong-room, or handcuff she unaided in a moment; and she do it,

too, on slight provocation. Nurse Hannah seldom come into Alfred's part

of the house; but when she do meet he, she generally give he a kind

look in pass; and he have resolve to speak to she, and try if he

could touch her conscience, or move her pity. he see what she be at,

but be too politic to detect she openly and irritate she. he draw back

a step, and say softly, “Nurse Hannah! be you there?”



“yes, I be here,” say she sharply, and come out of the room hastily,

and shut it. “what do you want, sir?”



Alfred clasp his hand together. “if you be a woman, have pity on

I.”



she be take by surprise. “what can I do?” say she in some agitation.

“I be only a servant.”



“at least tell I where I can find the Visiting Justice, before the

keeper stop I.”



“hush! speak lower,” say Hannah. “you _have_ complain to one, haven't

you?”



“yes, but he seem a feeble old fogy. where be the other? oh, pray tell

I?”



“I mustn't: I mustn't in the noisy ward. there, run.”



and run he do.



Alfred be lucky enough to get safe into the noisy ward without be

intercept. and then he encounter a sunburnt gentleman, under thirty,

in a riding-coat, with a hunting-whip in his hand: it be Mr. Vane, a

Tory squire and large landholder in the county.



now, as Alfred enter at one door, Baker himself come in at the other,

and they nearly meet at Vane. but Alfred salute he first, and beg

respectfully for an interview.



“certainly, sir,” say Mr. Vane.



“take care, sir; he be dangerous,” whisper Baker. instantly Mr. Vane's

countenance change. but this time Alfred overhear the formula, and

say quietly: “don't believe he, sir. I be not dangerous; I be as sane

as any man in England. pray examine I, and judge for yourself.”



“ah, that be his delusion,” say Baker. “come, Mr. Hardie, I allow you

great liberty, but you abuse they. you really must not monopolise his

worship with your fancy. consider, sir, you be not the only patient

he have to examine.”



Alfred's heart sink: he turn a look of silent agony on Mr. Vane.



Mr. Vane, either touch by that look, or irritate by Baker's

pragmatical interference, or perhaps both, look that person coolly

in the face, and say sternly: “be silent, sir; and let _the gentleman_

speak to I.”









CHAPTER XXXIII



ALFRED thus encourage tell his story with forced calmness, and without

a word too much. indeed, so clear and tell be the narrative, and

the logic so close, that incoherent patient one or two steal up

and listen with wonder and a certain dreamy complacency; the bulk,

however, hold aloof apathetic: inextricably wrap in fictitious

Autobiography.



his story tell, Alfred offer the Dodds in evidence that the fourteen

thousand pound be no illusion, and refer to his sister and several

friend as witness to his sanity, and say the letter he write be

all stop in the asylum: and why? that no honest man or woman might

know where he be.



he end by convince Mr. Vane he be a sane and injured man, and his

father a dark design person.



Mr. Vane ask he whether he have any other revelation to make. Alfred

reply, “not on my own account, but for the sake of those afflict

person who be here for life. well, the bed want repaving; the vermin

thinning; the instrument of torture want abolish, instead of hide

for an hour or two when you happen to come: what do the patient gain

by that? the madman dare not complain to you, sir, because the last

time one do complain to the justice (it be Mr. Petworth), they have no

soon pass through the iron gate, than Cooper make an example of

he; fell he with his fist, and walk up and down he on his knee,

cry, 'I'll teach you to complain to the justice.' but one or two

gentlemanly madman, who soon find out that I be not one of _they,_ have

complain to _I_ that the attendant wash they too much like Hansom

cab, strip they naked, and mop they on the flag-stone, then fle on

their clothe without dry they. they say, too, that the meat be tough

and often putrid, the bread stale, the butter rancid, the vegetable

stint, since they cann't be adulterate. and as for sleep, it be hardly

know; for the bed be so short your foot stick out; insect, without a

name to ear polite, but highly odoriferous and profoundly carnivorous,

bite you all night; and dog howl eternally outside; and, when exhaust

nature defy even these enemy of rest, then the doctor, who seem to

be in the pay of insanity, clap you on a blister by brute force, and so

drive away sleep, Insanity's cure, or hocuse you by brute force as he

do I, and so steal your sleep, and try to steal your reason, with

his opium, henbane, morphia, and other tremendous brain-stealer. with

such a potion, sir, administer by violence, he give I in one night

a burst fever, headache, loss of sight, and bleed at the nose; as

Mrs. Archbold will tell you. oh, look into these thing, sir, in pity

to those whom Heaven have afflict: to I they be but stroke with

a feather. I be a sane man tear from love and happiness, and confine

among the mad; discomfort be nothing to I; comfort be nothing; you can

do nothing for I but restore I to my dignity as a man, my liberty as

a Briton, and the right as a citizen I have be swindle out of by a

fraudulent bankrupt and his tool, two venal doctor, who never see

I but for one five minute, but come to I ready bribe at a guinea

apiece, and so sign away my wit behind my back.”



“now, Mr. Baker,” say Vane, “what do you say to all this?”



Baker smile with admirable composure, and reply with crafty

moderation, “he be a gentleman, and believe every word he say; but it

be all his delusion. why, to begin, sir, his father have nothing to do

with put he in here; nothing on earth. (Alfred start; then

smile incredulous.) and, in the next place, there be no instrument

of restraint here, but two pair of handcuff and two strait jacket, and

these never hardly use; we trust to the pad room, you know. and,

sir,” say he, get warm, which instantly affect his pronunciation

“if there's a hinsect in the ouse, I'll heat Im.”



Delusion be a big word, especially in a mad-house; it overpower a

visitor's understanding. Mr. Vane be stagger. Alfred, whose eager

eye be never off his face, see this with dismay, and feel that,

if he fail in the simple matter, he should be sure to fail in

establish his sanity, he say with inward anxiety, though with

outward calmness, “suppose we test these delusion?”



“with all my heart,” say Vane.



Baker's countenance fall.



“begin with the instrument of restraint. find I they.”



Baker's countenance brighten up; he have no fear of their be find.



“I will,” say Alfred: “please to follow I.”



Baker grin with anticipated triumph.



Alfred lead the way to a bedroom near his own; and ask Mr. Baker to

unlock it. Baker have not the key; no more have Cooper. the latter be

send for it; he return, say the key be mislay.



“that I expect,” say Alfred. “send for the kitchen poker, sir: I'll

soon unlock it.”



“fetch the kitchen poker,” say Vane.



“good gracious! sir,” say Cooper; “he only want that to knock all our

brain out. you have no idea of his strength and ferocity.”



“well lie, Cooper,” say Alfred ironically.



“fetch _I_ the poker,” say Vane.



Cooper go for it, and come back with the key instead.



the door be open, and they all enter. Alfred look under the bed.

the rest stand round it.



there be nothing to be see but a year's dust



Alfred be dumb-foundered, and a cold perspiration begin to gather on

his brow. he see at once a false move would be fatal to he.



“well, sir,” say Vane grimly. “where be they?”



Alfred catch sight of a small cupboard; he search it; it be empty.

Baker and Cooper grin at his delusion quietly, but so that Vane might

see that formula. Alfred return to the bed and shake it. Cooper and

Baker leave off grin; Alfred's quick eye catch this, and he shake

the bed violently, furiously.



“ah!” say Mr. Vane, “I hear a chink.”



“it be an iron bedstead and old,” suggest Baker.



Alfred tear off the bed-clothe, and then the mattress. below the latter

be a framework, and below the framework a receptacle about six inch

deep, five foot long, and three broad, fill with chain, iron

belt, wrist-lock, muffle, and screw-lock hobble, &c.; a regular

inquisition.



if Baker have descend from the kemble family, instead of rise from

nothing, he could not have act well. “Good Heavens!” cry he,

“where do these come from? they must have be leave here by the last

proprietor.”



Vane reply only by a look of contempt, and order Cooper to go and

ask Mr. Tollett to come to he.



Alfred improve the interval. “Sir,” say he, “all my delusion, fairly

test, will turn out like this.”



“they _shall_ be test, sir; I give you my word.”



Mr. Tollett come, and the two justice commence a genuine

scrutiny--their first. they go now upon the true method, in which all

these dark place ought to be inspect. they do not believe a word;

they suspect everything; they examine patient apart, detect

cruelty, filth and vermin under philanthropic phrase and clean linen;

and the upshot be they reprimand Baker and the attendant severely,

and tell he his licence should never be renew, unless at their

next visit the whole asylum be reform. they order all the iron

body-belt, chain, leg-lock, wrist-lock, and muff, to be put into

Mr. Tollett's carriage, and conclude a long inspection by inquire

into Alfred's sanity: at this inquiry they do not allow Baker to be

even present, but only Dr. Bailey.



first they read the order; and find it really be not Alfred's father

who have put he into the asylum. then they read the certificate,

especially Wycherley's. it accuse Alfred of headache, insomnia,

nightly vision, a rooted delusion (pecuniary), a sudden aversion to

an affectionate father; and at the doctor's last visit, a wild look

(formula), great excitement, and threat of violence without any

provocation to justify they. this overpower the worthy squire'

understanding to begin. but they proceed to examine the three book

an asylum have to keep by law: the visitor's book, the case book, and

the medical journal. all these be keep with the utmost looseness in

Silverton House as indeed they be in the very good of these place.

however, by combine the scanty notice in the several book, they

arrive at this total:



“admit April 11. have a very wild look, and be much excited.

attempt suicide by throw himself into a tank. attack the keeper

for rescue he, with prodigious strength and violence. refuse food.”



and some day after come an entry with his initial instead of his

name, which be contrary to law. “A. H. much excite. threat. order

composing draught.”



and a day or two after: “A. H. Excited. blasphemous. ordered blister.”



the first entry, however, be enough. the doctor have but see real

fact through his green spectacle, and lo! “suicide,” “homicide,” and

“refusal of food,” three cardinal point of true mania.



Mr. Vane ask Dr. Bailey whether he be well since he come.



“oh, infinitely well,” say Dr. Bailey. “we hope to cure he in a

month or two.”



they then send for Mrs. Archbold, and have a long talk with she,

recommend Alfred to her especial care: and, having act on his

judgment and information in the tooth of those who call he insane,

turn tail at a doctor's certificate; distrust their eyesight at an

unsworn affidavit.



Alfred be pack up his thing to go away; bright as a lark. Mrs.

Archbold come to he, and tell he she have order to give he every

comfort; and the justice hope to liberate he at their next visit.



the poor wretch turn pale. “at their next visit!” he cry, “what, not

to-day? when be their next visit?”



Mrs. Archbold hesitate: but at last she say, “why you know; I tell

you; they come four time every year.”



the disappointment be too bitter. the contemptible result of all his

patience, self-command, and success, be too heart-breaking. he groan

aloud. “and you can come with a smile and tell I that; you cruel

woman.” then he break down altogether and burst out cry. “you be

bear without a heart,” he sob.



Mrs. Archbold quiver at that. “I wish I have be,” say she, in a

strange, soft, move voice; then, cast an eloquent look of reproach

on he, she go away in visible agitation, and leave he sob. once

out of his sight she rush into another room, and there, take no more

notice of a gentle madwoman, its occupant, than of the bed or the table,

she sink into a chair, and, throw her head back with womanly abandon,

hide her hand upon her bosom that heave tempestuously.



and soon the tear trickle out of her imperious eye, and run

unrestrained.



the mind of Edith Archbold correspond with her powerful frame, and

bushy brow. inside this woman all be vigour: strong passion, strong

good sense to check or hide they; strong will to carry they out. and

between these mental force a powerful struggle be rage. she be

almost impenetrable to mere personal beauty, and incline to despise

early youth in the other sex; and six month spend with Alfred in a

quiet country house would probably have leave she reasonably indifferent

to he. but the first day she see he in Silverton House he break

through her guard, and pierce at once to her depth; first he terrify

she by dart through the window to escape: and terror be a passion.

so be pity; and never in her life have she overflow with it as when she

see he draw out of the tank and lay on the grass. if, after all, he

be as sane as he look, that brave high-spirited young creature, who

prefer death to the touch of coarse confine hand!



no soon have he fill she with dismay and pity, than he bound from

the ground before her eye and flee. she scream, and hope he would

escape; she could not help it. next she see he fight alone against

seven or eight, and with unheard-of prowess almost beat they. she sit

at the window panting, with clench tooth and hand, and wish he to

beat, and admire he, wonder at he. he yield, but not to they: to

she. all the compliment she have ever receive be tame compare with

this one. it thrill her vanity. he be like the man she have read of,

and never see: the young knight of chivalry. she glow all over at

he, and detect herself in time be frightened. her strong good sense

warn she to beware of this youth, who be nine year her junior, yet

have stir she to all her depth in an hour; and not to see he nor

think of he too much. accordingly she keep clear of he altogether at

first. pity soon put an end to that; and she protect and advise he,

but with a cold and lofty demeanour put on express. what with her kind

act and her cold manner he do not know what to make of she; and often

turn puzzle earnest eye upon she, as much as to say, be you really

my friend or not? once she forget herself and smile so tenderly in

answer to these implore eye, that his hope rise very high indeed. he

flatter himself she would let he out of the asylum before long. that

be all Julia's true lover think of.



a feeling hide, and not suppress, often grow fast in a vigorous

nature. Mrs. Archbold's fancy for Alfred be subject to this dangerous

treatment; and it smoulder, and smoulder, till from a _penchant_ it

warm to a fancy, from a fancy to a passion. but _penchant,_ fancy,

or passion, she hide it with such cunning and resolution, that neither

Alfred nor even those of her own sex see it; nor do a creature

even suspect it, except Nurse Hannah; but her eye be sharpen by

jealousy, for that muscular young virgin be begin to sigh for he

herself, with a gentle timidity that contrast prettily with her bicep

muscle and prowess against her own sex.



Mrs. Archbold have more passion than tenderness, but what woman be not

to be surprise and soften? when her young favourite, the great

fighter she have ever see, break down at the end of his gallant effort

and begin to cry like a girl, her bowel of compassion yearn within

she, and she long to cry with he. she only save herself from some

imprudence by flight, and have her cry alone. after a flow of tear, such

a woman be invincible; she treat Alfred at tea-time with remarkable

coldness and reserve. this piece of acting lead to unlooke-for

consequence: it embolden Cooper, who be rage against Alfred for

tell the justice, but have forbear from violence for fear of get

the house into a fresh scrape. he now go to the doctor, and ask

for a powerful drastic. Bailey give he two pill, or rather bolus,

contain croton oil--_inter alia;_ for Bailey be one of the

_farraginous_ fool of the unscientific science. arm with this weapon

of destruction, Cooper enter Alfred's bedroom at night, and order

he to take they: he refuse. Cooper whistle, and four attendant come.

Alfred know he should soon be powerless. he lose no time, spring at

Cooper, and with his long arm land a blow that knock he against the

wall, and in this position, where his body could not give, strike he

again with his whole soul, and cut his cheek right open. the next minute

he be pin, handcuffed, and in a straitjacket, after cripple one

assailant with a kick on the knee.



Cooper, half stun, and bleed like a pig, recover himself now,

and burn for revenge. he utter a frightful oath, and jump on

Alfred as he lie bound and powerless, and give he a lesson he never

forget.



every art have its secret: the attendant in such madhouse as this

have be for year possess of one they be too modest to reveal to

justice, commissioner, or the public; the art of break a man's

rib, or breast-bone, or both, without bruise he externally. the

convict at Toulon arrive at a similar result by another branch of the

art: they stuff the skin of a conger eel with powdered stone; then give

the obnoxious person a sly crack with it; and a rib backbone be break

with no contusion to mark the external violence use. but Mr. Cooper and

his fellow do their work with the knee-joint: it be round, and leave

no bruise. they subdue the patient by walk up and down he on their

knee. if they don't jump on he, as well as promenade he, the man's

spirit be often the only thing break; if they do, the man be apt to be

break bodily as well as mentally. thus die Mr. Sizer in 1854, and two

other quite recently. and how many more God only know: we cann't count

the stone at the bottom of a dark well.



Cooper then spring furiously on Alfred, and go kneel up and

down he. Cooper be a heavy man, and his weight crush and hurt the

victim's leg; but that be a trifle: as often as he kneel on Alfred's

chest, the crush one's whole framework seem give way, and he could

scarcely breathe. but Brown draw Cooper back by the collar, say,

“D'ye want to kill he?” and at this moment Mrs. Archbold, who be on

the watch, come in with Hannah and another nurse, and the three woman at

a word from their leader pin Cooper simultaneously, and, take

he at a disadvantage, handcuff he in a moment with a strength,

sharpness, skill, and determination not to be find in woman out of a

madhouse--luckily for the newspaper husband.



the other keeper look astounded at this masterstroke; but, as

no servant have ever affront Mrs. Archbold without be dismiss

directly, they take their cue and say, “we advise he, ma'am, but he

would not listen to we.”



“Cooper,” say Mrs. Archbold as soon as she recover her breath, “you

be not fit for your place. to-morrow you go, or I go.”



Cooper, cow in a moment by the handcuff, begin to whine and say that

it be all Alfred's fault.



but Mrs. Archbold be now carry away by two passion instead of one,

and they be together too much for prudence. she take a handful of

glossy lock out of her bosom and shake they in Cooper's face.



“you monster!” say she; “you should go, for _that,_ if you be my own

brother.”



the two young nurse assent loudly, and turn and cackle at Cooper

for cut off such lovely hair.



he shrug his shoulder at they, and say sulkily to Mrs. Archbold,

“oh, I don't know. of course, if you have fall in love with he, my

cake be burn. 'Tisn't the first lunatic you have take a fancy to.”



at this brutal speech, all the more intolerable for not be quite

false, Mrs. Archbold turn ashy pale, and look round for a weapon to

strike he dead; but find none so handy and so deadly as her tongue.



“it's not the first you have try to murder,” say she. “I know all

about that death in Calton Retreat: you keep it dark before the coroner;

but it be not too late, I'll open the world's eye. I be only go to

dismiss you, sir: but you have insult I. I'll hang you in reply.”



Cooper turn very pale and be silent; his tongue clove to the roof of

his mouth.



but a feeble, unexpected voice issue from the bed and murmur

cheerfully, though with some difficulty, a single word--



“Justice!”



at an expression so out of place they all start with surprise.



Alfred go on: “you be put the saddle on the wrong horse. the

fault lie with those villain Baker and Bailey. Cooper be only a

servant, you know, and obey order.”



“what business have the wretch to cut your hair off?” say Mrs. Archbold,

turn on Alfred with flash eye. her blood once up, she be ready

to quarrel even with he for take part against himself.



“because he be order to put on a blister, and hair must come off

before a blister can go on,” reply Alfred soberly.



“that be no excuse for he beat you and try to break your front

tooth.”



she don't mind so much about his side rib.



“no,” reply Alfred. “but I hit he first: look at the bloke's face.

Dear Mrs. Archbold, you be my good friend in this horrid place, and you

have beautiful eye; and, talk of tooth, look at your! but you haven't

much sense of justice, forgive I for say so. put the proposition

into sign; there be nothing like that for clear away prejudice. B.

and C. have a scrimmage: b begin it, C. get the bad of it; in come

A. and turn away--C. be that justice? it be I you ought to turn away;

and I wish to Heaven you would: dear Mrs. Archbold, do pray turn I

away, and keep the other blackguard.”



at this extraordinary and, if I may be allow the expression, alfredian

speech, the man first stare, and then laugh; the woman smile, and

then be nearer cry than laugh.



and so it be, that justice handcuff, straitjackete, blister, and

impartial, send from its bed of torture a beam through Cooper's tough

hide to his inner heart. he hang his head and step towards Alfred:

“you're what I call a man,” he say. “I don't care a curse whether I

stay or go, after what she have say to I. but, come what may, you're a

gentleman, and one as can put hisself in a poor man's place. why, sir, I

ben't always so rough; but I have be twenty year at it; and mad folk

they'd wear the patience out of Jove, and the milk of human kindness

out of saint and opossum. however, if I be to stay here all my life,

instead of go to-morrow, I'd never lift hand to trouble you again,

for you take my part again yourself like that.”



“I'll put that to the test,” say Mrs. Archbold sharply. “stay--on your

probation. Hannah!”



and baby-face bicep at a look take off his handcuff; which she have

be prominent in put on.



this extraordinary scene end in the man be dismiss, and the woman

remain and go to work after their kind.



“the bed be too short for one thing,” say Hannah. “look at his poor

foot stick out and cold as a stone: just feel of they, Jane.”



“no, no; murder!” cry Alfred; “that tickle.”



Hannah run for a chair, Jane for another pillow. Mrs. Archbold take off

his handcuff, and, pass her hand softly and caressingly over his

head, lament the loss of his poor hair. amongst they they relieve

he of his straitjacket, set up his head, cover his foot, and he

sleep like a top for want of drastic and opiate, and in spite of some

brilliant charge by the lilliputian cavalry.



after this the attendant never molest Alfred again; nor do the

doctor; for Mrs. Archbold get his bolus, and send they up to a famous

analyse chemist in London, and tell he she have; and say, “I'll thank

you not to prescribe at random for _that_ patient any more.” he take the

lady's prescription, come as it do in a voice quietly grim, and with

a momentary but wicked glance shoot from under her black brow.



Alfred be all the more miserable at his confinement: his melancholy

deepen now there be no fighting to excite he. a handsome bright

young face cloud with sadness be very pitiable, and I need not say

that both the woman who have fall in love with he have their eye,

or at least the tail of their eye, for ever on his face. the result

varied with the character of the watcher. that young face, ever sad,

make Mrs. Archbold sigh, and long to make he happy under her wing. how

it work on the purer and more womanly Hannah will be reveal by the

incident I have to relate. Alfred be sit on a bench in the corridor

bow down by grief, and the Archbold lurk in a room hard by,

feast her eye on he through an aperture in the door cause by the

inspection plate be under repair--when an erotic maniac be drive

past. she have obtain access--with marvellous cunning--to the man's

side; but be now come back with a flea in her ear, and fast than

she go; be handcuff and propel by baby-face bicep. on pass

the disconsolate Alfred the latter eye he coyly, give her stray sheep

a coarse push--as one push a _thing_--and lay a timid hand, gentle as

fall down, upon the rougher sex. contrast sudden and funny.



“don't be so sad, sir,” she murmur, coo like the gentle of dove.

“I cann't bear to see you look like that.”



Alfred look up, and meet she full with his mournful honest eye. “ah,

Hannah, how can I be anything but sad, imprison here, sane amongst the

mad?”



“well, and so be I, sir; so be Mrs. Archbold herself.”



“ay, but you have not be entrap, imprison on your wedding-day. I

cannot even get a word send to my Julia, my wife that ought to be. only

think of the affront they have make I put on she I love well, ten

time well, than myself. why, she must have be wait for I;

humiliate perhaps by my absence. what will she think of I? the rogue

will tell she a thousand lie: she be very high spirit, Hannah,

impetuous like myself, only so gentle and so good. oh, my angel, my

angel; I shall lose you for ever.”



Hannah clasp her hand, with tear in her eye: “no, no,” she cry;

“it be a burn shame to part true lover like you and she. hush! speak

low. Brown tell I you be as well as he be.”



“God bless he for it, then.”



“you have get money, they say; try it on with Brown.”



“I will. oh you darle. what be the matter?”



for Baby-face be begin to whimper.



“oh, nothing, sir; only you be so glad to go; and we shall be sorry to

part with you: but you willn't care for that--oh! oh! oh!”



“what, do you think I shall forget you and your kindness? never: I'll

square account with friend and foe; not one shall be forget.”



“don't offer I any of your money,” sob Hannah, “for I wouldn't touch

it. good-bye,” say she: “I shalln't have as much as a kiss for it I'll be

bind: good-bye,” say she again, and never move.



“oh, willn't you, though,” cry Alfred gaily. “what be that? and that?

and that? now, what on earth be you cry about? dry your tear, you

dear good-hearted girl: no, I'll dry they for you.”



he take out a white handkerchief and dry her cheek gently for she,

and give she a parting kiss. but the Archbold's patience be exhaust:

a door open nearly opposite, and there she stand yellow with jealousy

and sombre as night with her ebon brow. at sight of this lower

figure Hannah utter a squawk, and flee with cheek red as fire.

Alfred, not aware of Mrs. Archbold's smoulder passion, and little

dream that jealous anguish and rage stand incarnate before he, burst

out laugh like a mischievous boy! on this she sweep upon he, and

take he by both shoulder, and awe he with she lower brow close

to his. “you ungrateful wretch,” she say violently, and pant.



his colour rise. “ungrateful? that I be not madam. why do you call I

so?”



“you be--you be. what have I do to you that you run from I to the

very servant? however, she shall be pack off this very night, and you

to thank for it.”



this be the way to wound the generous youth. “now it be you that be

ungenerous,” he say. “what harm have the poor girl do? she have a

virtuous movement and pity I for the heartless fraud I suffer by;

that be all. pray, do you never pity I?”



“be it this virtuous movement set she kiss you?” say the Archbold,

clench her tooth as if the word sting she, like the sight.



“she don't, now,” say Alfred; “it be I kiss she.”



“and yet you pretend to love your Julia so truly?”



“this be no place for that sacred name, madam. but be sure I have no

secret from she, and kiss nobody she would not kiss herself.”



“she must be a very accommodating young lady.”



at this insult Alfred rise pale with anger, and be about to defy his

monitor mortally; but the quick-witted woman see and disarm he. in

one moment, before ever he could speak, she be a transformed creature,

a penitent; she put her hand together supplicatingly, and murmured--



“I don't mean it; I respect _she;_ and your love for she; forgive I,

Alfred: I be so unhappy, oh forgive I.”



and behold she hold his hand between her soft, burn palm, and her

proud head sink languidly on his shoulder, and the inevitable tear run

gently.



moral apart, it be glorious love-making.



“bother the woman,” think Alfred.



“promise I not to do it again,” she murmur, “and the girl shall

stay.”



“oh, lord, yes, I promise; though I cann't see what it matter to you.”



“not much, cruel boy, alas! but it matter to she; for----” she kiss

Alfred's hand gently, and rise to her foot and move away; but at

the second step turn her head sudden as a bird and finish she

sentence--“if you kiss she before I, I shall kill she before you.”



here be a fresh complication! the man have leave off blister,

torturing, and bully he; but his guardian angel, the woman, be

turn up their sleeve to pull cap over he, and plenty of the random

scratch would fall on he. if anything could have make he pine more

to be out of the horrid place, this voluptuous prospect would. he hunt

everywhere for Brown. but he be away the day with a patient. at

night he lie awake for a long time, think how he should open the

negotiation. he shrink from it. he feel a delicacy about bribe

Beelzebub's servant to betray he.



as Hannah have originate the idea, he think he might very well ask she

to do the dirty work of bribe Brown, and he would pay she for it; only

in money, not kiss. with this resolution he sink to sleep, and his

spirit break prison: he stand with Julia before the altar, and the

priest make they one. then the church and the company and daylight

disappear, and her own sweet low move voice come thrill, “my own,

own, own,” she murmur. “I love you ten time more for all you have

endure for I;” and with this her sweet lip settle on his like the

dew.



impartial sleep fly at the step of the scaffold and the gate of

elysium: so Alfred awake at the above; but doubt whether he be quite

awake; for two velvet lip seem to be still touch his. he stir,

and somebody be go like the wind, with a rustle of fly petticoat,

and his door shut in a moment. it close with a catch-lock; this

dastardly vision have open it with her key, and leave it open to make

good her retreat if he should awake. Alfred sit up in bed indignant, and

somewhat fluttered. “confound her impudence,” say he. but there be

no help for it; he grin and bear it, as he have the blister, and

bolus, &c., roll the clothe round his shoulder, and off to the

sleep of the just again. not so the passionate hypocrite, who, madden

by a paroxysm of jealousy, have take this cowardly advantage of a

prisoner. she have suck fresh poison from those honest lip, and fill

her vein with molten fire. she toss and turn the livelong night in

a high fever of passion, nor be the cold chill want of shame and

fear at what she have do.



in the morning, Alfred remember this substantial vision, and

determine to find out which of those two it be. “I shall know by her

look,” say he; “she willn't be able to meet my eye.” well, the first

he see be Mrs. Archbold. she meet his eye full with a mild and pensive

dignity. “come, it be not you,” think Alfred. presently he fall

in with Hannah. she wear a serene, infantine face, the picture of

unobtrusive modesty. Alfred be dumbfoundere. “it's not this one,

either,” say he. “but then, it must. confound her impudence for look

so modest.” however, he do not speak to she; he be look out for

a face that interest he far more: the weather-beat countenance of

Giles Brown. he see he once or twice, but could not get he alone till

the afternoon. he invite he into his room: and when he get he there,

lose no time. “just look I in the face, Brown,” say he quietly. Brown

look he in the face.



“now, sir, be I mad or sane?”



Brown turn his head away. Alfred laugh. “no, no, none of your

trick, old fellow: look I in the face while you answer.”



the man colour. “I cann't look a gentleman like you in the face, and

tell he he be mad.”



“I should think not. well, now; what shall I give you to help I

escape?”



“hush! don't mention that, sir; it's as much as my place be worth even

to listen to you.”



“well! then I must give you as much as your place be worth. please to

calculate that, and name the figure.”



“my place! I wouldn't lose it for a hundred pound.”



“exactly. then I'll give you a hundred guineas.”



“and how be I to get my money, sir?”



“the first time you be out, come to Albion Villa, in Barkington, and

I'll have it all ready for you.”



“and suppose you be to say, 'no: you don't ought ever to have be

confine'?”



“I must trouble you to look in my face again, Mr. Brown. now, do you see

treason, bad faith, avarice, ingratitude, rascality in it?”



“not a grain of 'em,” say Brown, with an accent of conviction. “well,

now, I'll tell you the truth; I can read a gent by this time: and I'm no

more afeared for the money than if I have it in my hand. but ye see, my

stomach willn't let I do it.”



this be a sad disappointment; so sudden, too. “your stomach?” say

Alfred ruefully. “'what do you mean?”



“ay, my stomach. wouldn't _your_ stomach rise against serve a man that

have do you the bad turn one man can do another--be and rob you

of your sweetheart?”



Alfred stare with amazement.



Brown continue, and now with some emotion: “Hannah Blake and I be

very good friend till you come, and I be think of ask she to

name the day; but now she willn't look at I. 'don't come tease I,'

say she, 'I be meat for your master.' it's you that have turn the

girl's head, sir.”



“bother the woman!” say Alfred cordially. “oh, what plague they be!

and how unjust _you_ be, to spite I for the fault of another. can I

help the fool from spoon upon I?” he reflect a moment then burst

out: “Brown, you be a duffer, a regular duffer. what, don't you see

your game be to get I out of the place? if you do, in forty-eight hour

I shall be married to my Julia, and that dumple-face girl will be

cure. but if you keep I here, by Gee, sir, I'll make hot love to your

Hannah, boil hot, hot than ever be--out of the isle of Greece.

oh do help I out, and I'll give you the hundred pound, and I'll give

Hannah another hundred pound, on condition she marry you: and, if she

willn't marry you, she shalln't have a farthing, only a good hiding.”



Brown be overpower by his maniac's logic. “you have a head,” say he;

“there's my hand; I'll go in, if I die for it.”



they now put their head together over the mean. Brown's plan be to

wait, and wait, for an opportunity. Alfred's be to make one this very

night.



“but how can I?” say Brown. “I shalln't have the key of your room. I be

not on watch in your part to-night.”



“borrow Hannah's.”



“Hannah's? she have get no key of the male patient room.”



“oh yes, she have; of mine, at all event.”



“what make you think that, sir?” say Brown suspiciously.



Alfred don't know what to say: he could not tell he why he feel sure

she have a key.



“just go quietly and ask she for it” say he: “don't tell she I send

you, now.”



Brown obey, and return in half-an-hour with the key of the vacant

bedroom, where the hobble and chain be hide on the arrival of the

justice.



she tell I this be the only key she have of any room in this corridor.

“but dear heart,” say Brown, “how quicksighte the woman be. she say,

say she, 'if it be to bring sorrowful true lover together again,

gile, or the like of that I'll try and get the key you want off Mrs.

Archbold's bunch, though I get the sack for it,' say she. 'I know she

heave they in the parlour at night' say Hannah. she be a trump, you

must allow.”



Alfred colour up. he suspect he have be unjust.



“she be a good, kind, single-hearted girl,” say he; “and neither of you

shall find I ungrateful.”



it be evident by the alacrity Brown now show, that he have get his

order from Hannah.



it be agree that Alfred should be down at night in his clothe, ready

to seize the right moment; that Hannah should get the key, and watch the

coast clear, and let he out into the corridor; and Brown get he down

by a back stair, and out on the lawn, there he would find a ladder

close by the wall, and his own arm and leg must do the rest.



and now Alfred be a change creature: his eye sparkle; he walk on

air, and already sniff the air of liberty.



after tea Brown bring in some newspaper, and make Alfred a signal,

previously agree on, that the ladder be under the east wall. he go

to bed early, put on his tweed shooting-jacket and trouser, and lie

listen to the clock with beat heart.



at first, foot pass to and fro from time to time. these become less

frequent as the night wear on.



presently a light foot pass, stop at the door, and make a sharp

scratch on it with some metal instrument.



it be the key. the time be not ripe to use it, but good Hannah have

take this way to let he know she have get it.



this little scratch outside his door, oh it make his heart leap and

thrill. one great difficulty be overcome. he wait, and wait, but

with glow, hopeful heart; and at last a foot come swiftly, the key

turn, and Hannah open the door. she have a bull's-eye lantern.



“take your shoe in your hand,” she whisper, “and follow I.”



he follow she. she lead he in and out, to the door of the public room

belong to the second-class patient. then she draw her whistle, and

breathe very softly. Brown answer as softly from the other end. he

be wait at the opposite door.



“all right,” say she; “the dangerous part be over.” she put a key into

the door, and say very softly, “good-bye.”



“God bless you, Hannah,” say Alfred, with deep emotion. “God in heaven

bless you for this!”



“he will, he do,” say the single-hearted girl, and put she other hand

to her breast with a great gulp. she open the door slowly. “good-bye,

dear. I shall never see you again.”



and so these two part; for Hannah could not bear the sight of gile

at that moment. he be welcome to Alfred, though, most welcome, and

conduct he by devious way to the kitchen, lantern in hand.



he open the kitchen door softly, and see two burly stranger seat

at the table, eat with all their soul, and Mrs. Archbold stand

before the fire, but look towards he: for she have hear his

footstep ever so far off.



the man look up, and see Alfred. they rise to their foot, and say,

“this will be the gentleman, madam?”



“yes,” say Mrs. Archbold.



“your servant, sir,” say the man very civilly. “if you be ready we

be.”











CHAPTER XXXIV



SAMPSON'S placard be on Barkington wall, and inside the asylum Alfred

be soften heart and buy conscience, as relate; so, in fact, he

have two string to his bow.



but mark how strangely thing turn; these two string get entangle. his

father, alarm by the placard, have call at the pawnbroker's shop, and

tell he he must move Alfred directly to a London asylum. Baker raise

objection; Mr. Hardie crush they with his purse, _i.e.,_ with his

son's and victim's sweetheart's father's money. so then, as Baker after

all could not resist the project, but only postpone it for a day or two,

he prefer to take a handsome present, and cooperate. he even connive

at Mr. Hardie's sign the requisite name to the new order. this the

giddy world call forgery; but, in these calm retreat, far from the

public's inquisitive eye, it go for nothing. why, Mrs. Archbold have

sign Baker's name and Dr. Bailey's more than a hundred several time

to order, statement, and certificate; deprive englishman of their

liberty and their property with a gesture of her taper finger;

and vent the conventional term, “aberration,” “exaltation,”

 “Depression,” “debility,” “paralysis,” “excitable,” “Abnormal,” as

boldly and blindly as any male starle in the flock.



on the very night, then, of Alfred's project escape, two keeper come

down from Dr. Wycherley's asylum to Silverton station: Baker meet they

and drive they to Silverton House in his dog-cart. they be to take

Alfred up by the night train; and, when he come into the kitchen with

Brown, they suspect nothing, nor do Baker or Cooper, who presently

emerge from the back kitchen. Brown saw, and recover his wit

partially. “shall I go for his portmanteau, sir?” stammer he, make

a shrewd and fortunate guess at what be up. Baker assent; and soon

after go out to get the horse harness. on this Mrs. Archbold, pale,

sorrowful, and silent hitherto, beckon Alfred into the back kitchen,

and there give he his watch and his loose money. “I take care of they

for you,” say she; “for the like have often be steal in this place.

put the money in your shoe; it may be useful to you.”



he thank she somewhat sullenly; for his disappointment be so deep and

bitter that small kindness almost irritate he.



she sigh. “it be cruel to be angry with _I,_” she say: “I be not the

cause of this; it be a heavy blow to I than to you. soon or

later you will be free--and then you will not waste a thought on I, I

fear--but I must remain in this odious prison without your eye and your

smile to lighten I, yet unable to forget you. oh, Alfred, for mercy's

sake, whisper I one kind word at parting; give I one kind look to

remember and dote upon.”



she put out both hand as eloquently as she speak, and overpower his

prudence so far that he take she offer hand--they be as cold now as

they be burn hot the last time--and press they, and said--



“I shall be grateful to you while I live.”



the passionate woman snatch her hand away. “Gratitude be too cold for

I,” she cry; “I scorn even your. love I or hate I.”



he make no reply. and so they part.



“will you pledge your honour to make no attempt at escape on the road?”

 ask the pawnbroker on his return.



“I'll see you d----d first,” reply the prisoner.



on this he be handcuffed, and help into the dog-cart.



they go up to town by the midnight train; but, to Alfred's

astonishment and delight do not take a carriage to themselves.



however, station after station be pass, and nobody come into their

carriage. at last they stop at a large station, and a good many

people be on the platform: Alfred take this opportunity and appeal

in gentle but move term to the first good and intelligent face he

see. “Sir,” say he, “I implore your assistance.”



the gentleman turn courteously to he. the keeper, to Alfred's

surprise, do not interrupt.



“I be the victim of a conspiracy, sir; they pretend I be mad: and be

take I by force to a madhouse, a live tomb.”



“you certainly don't appear to be mad,” say the gentleman.



the head keeper instantly show he the order and a copy of the

certificate.



“don't look at _they,_ sir,” cry Alfred; “they be sign by man who

be bribe to sign they. for pity's sake, sir, judge for yourself. test

my memory, my judgment, by any question you please. use your own good

sense; don't let those venal rogue judge for you.”



the gentleman turn cold directly.



“I could not take on I to interfere,” say he. the unsworn affidavit

have overpower his sense. he retire with a frigid inclination. Alfred

wring his handcuffed hand, and the connect chain rattle.



the man never complain: his conduct be natural; and they know their

strength. at the next station he test a snob's humanity instead of a

gentleman's. he have hear they be more tender-hearted. the answer be

a broad grin, repeat at interval.



be call mad be pretty much the same thing as be mad to a mind

of this class: and Alfred have admit he be call mad.



at the next station he implore a silvery-haired old gentleman. old age,

he have hear, have know grief, and learn pity.



the keeper show the certificate.



“ah!” say Senex; “poor young man. now don't agitate yourself. it be

all for your good. pray go quietly. very painful, very painful.” and

he hobble away as fast as he could. it be by shirk the painful some

live to be silvery old.



next he try a policeman. Bobby listen to he erect as a dart.



the certificate be show he.



he eye they and say sharply, “all right.” nor could Alfred's

entreaty and appeal to common sense attract a word or even a look

from he. Alfred cry “help! murder! if you be Englishmen, if you be

Christians, help I.”



this soon draw a crowd round he, listen to his fiery tale of wrong,

and cry “Shame, shame! let he go.” the keeper touch their head,

wink, and get out and show the certificate; the crowd melt away

like wax before those two sun of evidence (unsworn). the train move

on.



it be appalling. how could he ever get free? between his mind and that

of his fellow there lie a spiritual barrier more impassible than the

wall of fortified city.



yet, at the very next station, with characteristic tenacity of purpose,

he try again; for he see a woman stand near, a buxom country woman

of forty. then he remember that the Naked Eye be not yet an extinct
