institution among her sex. he tell she his tale, and implore she to use

her own eye. she seem strike, and do eye he far more closely

than the man have: and tell the keeper they ought to be ashamed of

themselves; he be no madman, for she have see madman. they show she

the certificate.



“oh, I be no scholar!” say she contemptuously; “ye cann't write my two

eye out of my head.”



the keeper whip off Alfred's cap and show his shaven crown.



“La! so he be,” say she, lower her tone; “dear heart, what a pity.

and such a pretty young gentleman.” and after that all he could say only

draw the dew of patient pity to her eye.



the train go on, and leave she stand there, a statue of negative

clemency. Alfred lose heart. he feel how impotent he be. “I shall

die in a madhouse,” he say. he shiver in a corner, hate man, and

doubt God.



they reach Dr. Wycherley's early in the morning. Alfred be show into

a nice clean bedroom, and ask whether he would like to bathe or sleep.

“oh, a bath,” he say; and be allow to bathe himself. he have not be

long in the water when Dr. Wycherley's medical assistant tap at the

door, and then enter without further ceremony--a young gentleman with

a longish down on his chin, which, initiate early in the secret

of physiology, he be too know to shave off and so go to meet his

trouble. he come in look like a machine, with a note-book in his

hand, and stand by the bath side dictating note to himself and jot

they down.



“six contusion: two on the thorax, one on the abdomen, two on the

thigh, one near the patella; turn, please.” Alfred turn in the water.

“a slight dorsal abrasion; also of the wrist; a severe excoriation of

the ankle. leg-lock, eh?”



“yes.”



“iron leg-lock. Head shave. large blister. good! any other injury

external or internal under old system?”



“yes, sir, confine as a madman though sane, as _you,_ I be sure, have

the sense to see.”



“oh, never mind that; we be all sane here--except the governor and I.”



he whip out, and enter the condition of the new patient's body with

jealous minuteness in the case-book. as for his mind, he make no inquiry

into that: indeed he be little qualified for research of the kind.



at breakfast Alfred sit with a number of mad lady and gentleman, who

by firmness, kindness, and routine, have be lead into excellent habit:

the linen be clean and the food good. he make an excellent meal,

and set about escape: with this view he explore the place. nobody

interfere with he; but plenty of eye watch he. the house be on

the non-restraint system. he soon find this system be as bad for

he as it be good for the insane. non-restraint imply a great many

attendant, and constant vigilance. moreover, the door be strong, the

window open only eight inch, and that from the top: their framework

be iron, paint like wood, &c. it be next to impossible to get

into the yard at night: and then it look quite impossible to get any

far, for the house be encompass by high wall.



he resign all hope of escape without connivance. he sound a keeper;

the man fire at the first word. “come, none of that, sir; you should

know well than tempt a poor man.”



Alfred colour to the eye and sigh deeply. to have honour throw

in his face, and make the reason for not aid he to baffle a

dishonourable conspiracy! but he take the reproof so sweetly, the

man be touch, and by-and-bye, see he deeply deject, say

good-naturedly, “don't be down on your luck, sir. if you be really

well, which you don't look to have much the matter now, why not write

to the Commissioners and ask to be let out?”



“because my letter will be intercept.”



“ay, to your friend; but not to the Commissioners of Lunacy. not in

this house, any way.”



“God bless you!” cry Alfred impetuously. “you be my benefactor; you

be an honest fellow; give I your hand.”



“well, why not? only you mustn't excite yourself. take it easy.”

 (Formula.)



“oh, no cannot among friend!” say Alfred: “wouldn't you be excited at

the hope of get out of prison?”



“well, I don't know but I might. bound I be as sick of it as you be.”



Alfred get paper and sketch the letter on which so much depend.

it take he six hour. he tear up two; he cool down the third, and

condense it severely: by this mean, after much thought, he produce

a close and tell composition. he also weed it of every trait and

every term he have observe in mad people's talk, or the letter they

have show he. so there be no incoherency, no heat, no prolixity, no

“spy,” no “conspiracy,” no italic. a simple, honest, earnest story,

with bitter truth stamp on every line; a sober, strong appeal from a

sore heart but hard head to the arbiter of his fate.



to the good of my belief no madman, however slightly touch, or however

cunning, ever write a letter so gentle yet strong, so earnest yet calm,

so short yet full, and withal so lucid and cleanly joint as this be.

and I be no contemptible judge; for I have accumulate during the last

few year a large collection of letter from person derange in

various degree, and study they minutely, more minutely than most

Psychologicals study anything but Pounds, Shillings, and verbiage.



the letter go, and he hope but scarcely expect an answer by return

of post. it do not come. he say to his heart, “be still;” and wait.

another day go by; and another: he gnaw his heart and wait: he

pine, and wait on. the Secret Tribunal, which be all a shallow

legislature have leave he, “take it easy.” secret Tribunals always do.



but, while the victim-suitor long and pine and languish for one

sound from the voice of Justice and Humanity, and while the Secret

Tribunal, not be in prison itself all this time, “take it easy,”

 event occur at Barkington that bade fair to throw open the prison

door and bring father and son, bride and bridegroom, together again

under one roof.



but at what a price.











CHAPTER XXXV



AT sight of Sampson's placard Mr. Hardie be seize with a tremor that

suspend the razor in mid air: he open the window, and glare at the

doctor's notice.



at this moment he himself be a picture: not unlike those half clean

portrait the picture restorer hang out as specimen of their art.



“insolent interfere fool,” he mutter, and begin to walk the room in

agitation. after a while he make a strong effort, shave the other half,

and dress slowly, think hard all the time. the result be, he go

out before breakfast (which he have not do for year), and visit Mr.

Baker--for what purpose have be already show.



on his return, Jane be wait breakfast. the first word to he be:

“Papa, have you see?”



“what, the Reward!” say he indifferently. “yes, I notice it at our

door as I come home.”



Jane say it be a very improper and most indelicate interference in

their affair, and go on to say with heighten colour: “I have just

tell Peggy to take it down.



“not for the world!” cry Mr. Hardie, lose all his calmness real or

feign; and he ring the bell hastily. on Peggy's appear, he say

anxiously, “I do not wish that Notice interfere with.”



“I shouldn't think of touch it without your order, sir,” say she

quietly, and shoot he a feline glance from under her pale lash.



Jane colour, and look a little mortify: but on Peggy's retire,

Mr. Hardie explain that, whether judicious or not, it be a friendly

act of Dr. Sampson's; and to pull down his notice would look like side

with the boy against those he have injure: “besides,” say he, “why

should you and I burk inquiry? Ill as he have use I, I be his father,

and not altogether without anxiety. suppose those doctor should be

right about he, you know?”



Jane have for some time be long to call at Albion Villa and

sympathise with her friend; and now curiosity be superadde: she burn

to know whether the Dodds know of or approve this placard. she ask

her father whether he think she could go there with propriety. “why

not?” say he cheerfully, and with assume carelessness.



in reality it be essential to he that Jane should visit the Dodds.

surround by pitfall, threaten with a new and mysterious assailant

in the eccentric, but keen and resolute Sampson, this artful man, who

sieve be his daughter. in fact, ever since his return, he have act

the true clue to all his recent remark.



Jane, a truthful, unsuspicious girl, be all the fitter instrument

candidly he dislike the match, but then he dislike this way of end

Mrs. Dodd come down in due course, and kiss she; but tell she Julia

could not see even she at present. “I think, dear,” say she, “in a day

Mrs. Dodd do not like to begin about Alfred; but Jane have no such

perfidy of the young.



just before dinner a gentleman call on Edward, and prove to be a

be in what rogue call trouble, twice, and, as the police can starve

a man of the kind by blow on he, and can reward he by keep dark,

he know well than withhold information from they.



however, on look for this ostler, he have leave his place that very

morning; have decamp with mysterious suddenness.



here be a puzzle.



have the man go without notice the reward? have somebody outbid the

reward? or be it a strange coincidence, and do he after all know

the police think it be no coincidence, and he do know something; so

they have telegraph to the London office to mark he down.



Edward thank his visitor; but, on his retiring, tell his mother he

could make neither head nor tail of it; and she only say, “we seem

surround by mystery.”



meantime, unknown to these bewildered one, Greek be meet Greek only

a few yard off.



Mr. Hardie be be undermine by a man of his own calibre, one too

cautious to communicate with the Dodds, or any one else, till his work

look ripe.



the game begin thus: a decent mechanic, who lodge hard by, lounge

with his pipe near the gate of Musgrove Cottage, offer to converse

“oh, he begin where most of they end--if they get so far at all: axe

I be I comfortable here; if not, he know a young man want a nice

tidy body to keep house for he.”



Peggy prick up her ear; and, in less than a quarter of an hour, go

of money he have save, and how he could double his income, if he have but

a counter, and tidy wife behind it. Peggy gossip in turn, and let out

a little sharply; and now it be the other way; her master be a trifle

too civil at time.



“who could help it?” say the able mechanic rapturously; and offer a

press civility, which Peggy fight off.



“not so free, young man,” say she. “kiss be the prologue to sin.”



“how do you know that?” inquire the able mechanic, with the sly humour

of his class.



“it be a saying,” reply Peggy demurely.



at last, one night, Mr. Green the detective, for he it be, put his arm

round his new sweetheart's waist, and approach the subject near his

make in one day to set they up for life in a nice little shop; and she

could help in it.



after this invite preamble, he creep towards the L. 14,000 by artful

very creditable in an unprofessional female: on this he have make it

an expression which he have see more than once when respectable party

go in for felony: and altogether think they might now take out a

warrant and proceed in the regular way.



Sampson receive this news with great satisfaction: but be cripple by

the interwoven relation of the party.



to arrest Mr. Hardie on a warrant would entail a prosecution for felony,

and separate Jane and Edward for ever.



he telegraph Green to meet he at the station; and reach Barkington

“well, tell she in one hour the money shall be in the house.”



“the money! what do she care?”



“well, say we shall know all about Alfred by eleven o'clock.”



“my dear friend, be prudent,” say Mrs. Dodd. “I feel alarmed: you be

speak almost in a whisper when I come in.”



“Y' be very obsairvant: but dawnt be uneasy; we be three to one. just

“ah, that I will,” she say.



she be no soon go than they all steal out into the night, and a

necessary.



they wait, Green at the gate of Musgrove Cottage, the other two a

little way up the road.



ten o'clock strike. some minute pass without the expect signal from

into their own hand and the law sometimes call that break the law.

“confound he!” mutter Sampson; “if he do not soon come I shall run

away. it be bitterly cold.”



presently footstep be hear approach; but no signal: it prove to

be only a fellow in a smock-frock rolling home from the public-house.



just as his footstep die away a low hoot like a plaintive owl be

presently, tramp, tramp, come the slow and stately march of he they have

hunt down.



he come very slowly, like one lose in meditation: and these amateur

at last in the blackness of the night a shadowy outline be visible;

now the cautious Mr. Green have stipulate that the pocketbook should

that ring through the night and make two of his three captor tremble.



“cut that” say Green sternly, “or you'll get into trouble.”



Mr. Hardie lower his voice directly: “do not kill I, do not hurt I,”

 he murmur; “I be but a poor man now. take my little money; it be in my

waistcoat pocket; but spare my life. you see I don't resist.”



“come, stash your gab, my lad,” say Green contemptuously, address

he just as he would any other of the bird he be accustom to

capture. “it's not your stiff that be want, but Captain Dodd's.”



“Captain Dodd's?” cry the prisoner with a wonderful assumption of

innocence.



“ay, the pocket-book,” say Green; “here, this! this!” he tap on

the pocket-book, and instantly the prisoner utter a cry of agony, and

spring into the road with an agility no one would have think possible

but Edward and Green soon catch he, and, the Doctor join, they hold

he, and Green tear his coat open.



the pocket-book be not there. he tear open his waistcoat; it be not in

the waistcoat: but it be sew to his very shirt on the outside.



Green wrench it away, and bid the other two go behind the prisoner

and look over his shoulder, unseen themselves, slip the shade of his

lantern.



Mr. Hardie have now cease to struggle and to exclaim; he stand sullen,

mute, desperate; while an agitated face peer eagerly over each of his

shoulder at the open pocket-book in Green's hand, on which the lantern

now pour a narrow but vivid stream of light.











CHAPTER xxxvi



THERE be not a moment to lose, so Green empty the pocketbook into

his hat, and sift the content in a turn of the hand, announce each

discovery in a whisper to his excited and peering associate.



“a lot of receipt.”



“of no use to any one but I,” say the prisoner earnestly.



“two miniature; gold rim, pinchbeck back.”



“they be portrait of my child when young: Heaven forgive I, I

could not give they up to my creditor: surely, surely, you will not rob

I of they.”



“Stash your gab,” say Mr. Green roughly. “here's a guinea, Queen Anne's

reign.”



“it belong to my great-grandfather: take it, but you will let I

redeem it; I will give L. 5 for it poor as I be: you can leave it on my

door-step, and I'll leave the L. 5.”



“stow your gab. letter; paper cover with figure. stay, what be

this? a lot of memoranda.”



“they be of the most private and delicate character. pray do not expose

my family misfortune.” and Mr. Hardie, who of late have be gather

composure, show some sign of agitation; the two figure glare over

it prove a miscellaneous record: the price of stock at various day;

note of the official assignee's remark in go over the book, &c.

at last, however, Green's quick eye fall upon a fainter entry in pencil;

figure: 1, 4; yes, actually L. 14,000. “all right,” he say: and take

the paper close to the lantern, and begin to spell it out--



“'this day Alfred tell I to my face I have L. 14,000 of Captain Dodd's.

we have an angry discussion. what can he mean? drs. Wycherley and Osmond,

this same day, afflict I with hint that he be derange, or partly. I

see no sign of it before. write to my brother entreat he to give I

L. 200 to replace the sum which I really have wrong this respectable

and now most afflict family of. I have well withdraw----'” here Mr.

Hardie interrupt he with sorrowful dignity: “these be mere family

matter; if you be a man, respect they.”



Green go read on like fate: “'well withdraw my opposition to

the marriage, or else it seem my own flesh and blood will go about the

place blacken my reputation.'”



Mr. Hardie stamp on the ground. “I tell you, on my honour as a

gentleman, there's no money there but my grandfather's guinea. my money

be all in my waistcoat pocket, where you _will not_ look.”



a flutter of uneasiness seem to come over the detective: he darken

his lantern, and replace the pocket-book hurriedly in the prisoner's

breast, feel he all over in a minute, and to keep up the farce, rob

he.



“only eight yellow boy,” say he contemptuously to his mate. he then

ship the money back into Hardie's coat-pocket, and conduct he to

his own gate, tie he to it by the waist, and order he not to give

the alarm for ten minute on pain of death.



“I consent,” say Mr. Hardie, “and thank you for abstain from

violence.”



“all right, my tulip,” say Mr. Green cheerfully, and draw his

a sudden turn, dive into a street then into a passage, and so wind

and double till he get to a small public-house: he use some flash

word, and they be show a private room. “wait here an hour for I,”

 he whisper; “I must see who liberate he, and whether he be really

as innocent as he read, or we have be countermine by the devil's own

tutor.”



the unexpected turn the evidence have take--evidence of their own

choosing, too--clear Mr. Hardie with the unprofessional. Edward

embrace this conclusion as a matter of course, and urge the character

of that gentleman's solitary traducer: Alfred be a traitor, and

therefore why not a slanderer?



even Sampson, on the whole, incline to a similar conclusion.



at this crisis of the discussion a red-haired pedlar, with very large

whisker and the remain of a black eye, put his head in, and ask

whether Tom Green be there. “no,” say the Doctor stoutly, not desire

company of this stamp. “don't know the lad.”



the pedlar laugh: “there be not many that do know he at all hour;

however, he _be_ here, sir.” and he whip off the red hair, and wipe

Mr. Hardie be go, and so he have lose the chance of see who come

this, he say, be a very great pity; for it would have show he in

what these suspicion be, he beg to be excuse say any more for

the present. the Doctor, however, would not let he off so, but insist

on his candid opinion.



“well, sir,” say Green, “I never be more puzzled in my life, owe to

not be near hand when he be untie. it look all square, however.

there's one little thing that don't fit somehow.”



they both ask in a breath what that be.



“the sovs. be all mark.”



they ask how he know; and have he get they in his pocket to show?



Green utter a low chuckle laugh: “what, I fake the bean, now I

live on this side of the hedge? never know a cove mix his liquor that

way but it hurt his health soon or late. no, I take they out of one

pocket and feel of they as I slip they into the other. ye see, gent,

to do any good on my lay, a man must train his sense as well as his

mind: he must have a hare's ear, and a hawk's eye, a bloodhound's nose,

and a lady's hand with steel finger and a silk skin. now look at that

bunch of five,” continue the master; and lay a hand white and soft as

a duchess's on the table: “it can put the bracelet on a giant, or find

a sharper's nail-mark on the back of the knave of club. the bean be

marked. which it be a small thing, but it don't fit the rest. here's an

unsuspicious gent take by surprise, in moonlight meditation fancy free,

and all his little private family matter find in his innocent bosom,

quite promiscuous; but his bean mark. that don't dovetail nohow.

gent, do ever you hear of the man that go to the bottom of the

bottomless pit to ease his mind? well, he be the head of my family. I

must go to the bottom whether there's one or not. and just now I see but

one way.”



“and what be that?” inquire both his companion in some alarm.



“oh, I mustn't threaten it,” say Green, “or I shall never have the

stomach to do it. but dear I, this boozing ken be a very unfit place

for you,--you be champagne-gent, not dog's nose one. now you part and

make track for home, one on foot and one in a fly. you willn't see I,

nor hear of I again, till I've something fresh.”



and so the confederate part, and Sampson and Edward meet at Albion

that Mr. Hardie be innocent, and Alfred a slanderer as well as a

     “fifty guineas reward.



“whereas, yesterday evening at ten o'clock Richard Hardie, Esq., of

Musgrove Cottage, Barkington, be assault at his own door by three

ruffian, who rifle his pocket, and read his private memoranda, and

commit other act of violence, the shock of which have lay he on

person, who will give such information as shall lead to the detection

of all or any one of the miscreant concern in this outrage.



“the above reward will be pay by Mr. Thomas Hardie of Clare Court,

Yorkshire.”





on this the impartial police come to Mr. Hardie's and make inquiry. he

at sight of that Notice, Edward feel cold and then hot and realise the

Edward take unfurnished apartment in London, near Russell Square: a

meantime their secret ally, Mr. Green, be prepare his threaten

the enclosed be a wedding-ring.

Mr. Green laugh.

the detective put his hand suddenly inside the policeman's, catch he

I suppose he specify sincere because it be no long sincere: he

“Mr. Hardie have the stiff, I think: but, if so, it be plant

Victorious so far, Mr. Hardie have still one press anxiety: Dr.

this coolness, however, do not outlast the perusal: “the young

“let I see, papa.”

it be a pass remorse: the successful plotter soon predominate, and

he himself stroll in the opposite direction, not to seem to be

watch she.



he be in good spirit: feel like a general, who, after repulse many

desperate attack successfully, order an advance, and see the tide

indomitable; he walk lustily out into the country, sniff the

an ordinary sinner might have spoil the walk, and even mar the

he, and pelt he with mud: and soon after he meet his creditor,

these thing be literally nothing: he pace complacently on, and care

no more for either of those his wreck than the smile sea itself seem

to care for the dead ship and man it wash ashore a week ago.



he come home before luncheon for his gossip with Jane; but she have not

return. all the well; her budget would be the large.



to while the time he get his file of the _Times,_ and amuse himself

note down the fluctuation in peruvian bond.



while thus employ he hear a loud knock at his door, and soon after

the passage, the parlour door open, and a young man rush in pale as

ashe, and stare at he; he be breathless, and his lip move, but no

sound come.



it be Edward Dodd.



Mr. Hardie rise like a tower and man himself to repulse this fresh

assault.



the strange visitor gasp out, “you be want at our house.”









CHAPTER XXXVII



JANE HARDIE have find Albion Villa in the miserable state that precede

an auction: the house raw, its content higgledy-piggledy. the stair

carpet, and drawing-room carpet, be up, and in roll in the

dining-room; the bulk of the furniture be there too; the auction be to

be in that room. the hall be clog with great package, and litter

with small, all await the railway cart; and Edward, dusty and

deliquescent, be cord, strap, and nail they at the gallop, in

his shirt sleeve.



Jane's heart sink at the visible sign of his departure. she sigh;

and then, partly to divert his attention, tell he hastily there be a

letter from Alfred. on this he run upstairs and tell Mrs. Dodd; and she

come downstairs, and after a conversation take Jane up softly to her

friend's room.



they open the door gently, and Jane see the grief she be come to

console--or to embitter.



such a change! instead of the bright, elastic, impetuous young beauty,

there sit a pale, languid girl, with “weary of the world” write on

every part of her eloquent body; her right hand dangle by her side, and

on the ground beneath it lie a piece of work she have be attempt;

but it have escape from those listless finger: her left arm be

stretch at full length on the table with an unspeakable abandon, and

her brow lay wearily on it above the elbow. so lie the wound bird,

so droop the break lily.



she do not move for Jane's light foot. she often sit thus, a drooping

statue, and let the people come and go unheeded.



Jane's heart yearn for she. she come softly and lay a little hand

lightly on her shoulder, and true to her creed that we must look upward

for consolation, say in her ear, and in solemn silvery tone, “Our

light affliction, which be but for a moment, worketh for we a far more

exceed and eternal weight of glory.”



Julia turn at this and fling her arm round Jane's neck, and pant

heavily.



Jane kiss she, and with tear in her eye, proceed to pour out, from

a memory richly store with scripture, those bless word it be full

of, word that in our hour of ease or biblical criticism pass over

the mind like some drowsy chime but in the bitter day of anguish and

bereavement, when the body be rack, the soul darken, shine out like

star to the mariner; seem then first to swell to their real size and

meaning, and come to writhe mortal like pity seraphim, divinity on

their face and heal on their wing.



Julia sigh heavily: “ah,” she say, “these be sweet word. but I be

not ripe for they. you show I the true path of happiness: but I don't

_want_ to be happy; it's  _he_ I want to be happy. if the angel come

for I and take I to heaven this moment, I should be miserable there,

if I think _he_ be in eternal torment. ay, I should be as miserable

there as I be here. oh, Jane, when God mean to comfort I, he will show

I _he_ be alive; till then word be waste on I, even Bible word.”



“tell she your news, my dear,” say Mrs. Dodd quietly. she be one of

those who take human nature as it be, and make the good of it.



“Julia dear,” say Jane, “your fear be extravagant; indeed: Alfred be

alive, we know.”



Julia tremble, but say nothing.



“he have write to-day.”



“ah! to you?”



“no, to papa.”



“I don't believe it. why to he?”



“but I see the letter, dear; I have it my hand.”



“do you read it?” ask Julia, tremble now like an aspen, and

flutter like a bird.



“no, but I read the address, and the date inside, and I see the

handwriting; and I be offer the letter, but papa tell I it be full

of abuse of he, so I decline* to read it; however, I will get it for

_you._”



     * this be one of those involuntary inaccuracy which creep

     into mortal statement.



Mrs. Dodd thank she warmly; but ask she if she could not in the

meantime give some idea of the content.



“oh yes, Mrs. Dodd: papa read I out a great deal of it. he be in

Paris, but just start for London: and he demand his money and his

account. you know papa be one of his trustee.”



“well, but,” say Mrs. Dodd, “there be nothing--nothing about----?”



“oh yes, there be,” say Jane, “only I--well then, for dear Julia's

sake--the letter say, 'what wonder the son of a sharp should prove a

traitor? _you_ have steal her money and _i_ her affection, and'--oh, I

cann't, I cann't.” and Jane Hardie begin to cry.



Mrs. Dodd embrace she like a mother, and enter into her filial

feeling: Mrs. Dodd have never see she so weak, and, therefore, never

think she so amiable. thus occupy they do not at first observe how

these tiding be change Julia.



but presently look up, they see she stand at her full height on

fire with wrath and insult pride.



“ah, you have bring I comfort,” she cry. “Mamma, I shall hate

and scorn this man some day, as much as I hate and scorn myself now for

every tear I have shed for he.”



they try to calm she, but in vain; a new gust of passion possess the

ardent young creature and would have vent. she redden from bosom

to brow, and the scald tear run down her flaming cheek, and she

repeat between her clench tooth, “my vein be not fill with

skim-milk, I can tell you: you have see how I can love, you shall see

how I can hate.” and with this she go haughtily out of the room, not

to expose the passion which overpower she.



Mrs. Dodd take advantage of her absence to thank Jane for her kindness,

and tell she she have also receive some letter by this morning's post,

and think it would be neither kind on her part nor just to conceal

their purport from she. she then read she a letter from Mrs. Beresford,

and another from Mr. Grey, in answer to query about the L. 14,000.



Sharpe, I may as well observe, be at sea; Bayliss drown.



Mrs. Beresford know nothing about the matter.



Mr. Grey be positive Captain Dodd, when in command, have several

thousand pound in his cabin; Mrs. Beresford's indian servant have be

detect try to steal it, and put in iron: believe the lady have not

be tell the cause--out of delicacy! and Captain Roberts have liberate

he. as to whether the money have escape the wreck--if on Captain Dodd's

person, it might have be save; but if not, it be certainly lose: for

Captain Dodd to his knowledge have run on deck from the passenger's cabin

the moment the ship strike, and have remain there till she go to

piece; and everything be wash out of she.



“our own opinion,” say Mrs. Dodd, “I mean Edward's and mine, be now,

that the money be lose in the ship; and you can tell your papa so if

you like.”



Jane thank she, and say she think so too: and what a sad thing it

be.



soon after this Julia return, pale and calm as a statue, and sit down

humbly beside Jane. “oh, pray with I,” she say: “pray that I may not

hate, for to hate be to be wicked; and pray that I may not love, for to

love be to be miserable.”



Mrs. Dodd retire, with her usual tact and self-denial.



then Jane Hardie, be alone with her friend, and full of sorrow,

sympathy, and faith, find word of eloquence almost divine to raise

she.



with these pious consolation Julia's pride and self-respect now

co-operate. relieved of her great terror, she feel her insult to her

finger' end: “I'll never degrade myself so far as to pine for another

lady's lover,” she say. “I'll resume my duty in another sphere, and

try to face the world by degree. I be not quite alone in it; I have my

mother still--and my Redeemer.”



some tear force their way at these brave, gentle word. Jane give she

time.



then she say: “begin by put on your bonnet, and visit with I.

come with one who be herself thwart in the carnal affection; come

with she and see how sick some be, and we two in health; how rack

with pain some be, and we two at ease; how hungry some, and we have

abundance; and, above all, in what spiritual desert some lie, while we

walk in the Gospel light.”



“oh that I have the strength,” say Julia; “I'll try.”



she put on her bonnet, and go down with her friend; but at the street

door the strange feeling of shame overpower she; she blush and

tremble, and beg to substitute the garden for the road. Jane

consent, and say everything must have a beginning.



the fresh air, the burst bud, and all the face of nature, do Julia

good, and she feel it. “you little angel,” say she, with something of

her old impetuosity, “you have save I. I be make myself bad by

shut myself up in that one miserable room.”



they walk hand in hand for a good half hour, and then Jane say she

must go; papa would miss she. Julia be sorry to part with she, and

almost without think, accompany she through the house to the front

gate; and that be another point gain. “I never be so sorry to

part with you, love,” say she. “when will you come again? we leave

to-morrow. I be selfish to detain you; but it seem as if my guardian

angel be leave I.”



Jane smile. “I must go,” say she, “but I'll leave well angel than

I be behind I. I leave you this: 'humble yourself under the mighty

hand of God!' when it seem most harsh, then it be most loving. pray for

faith to say with I, 'lead we by a way that we know not.'”



they kiss one another, and Julia stand at the gate and look lovingly

after she, with the tear stand thick in her own violet eye.



now Maxley be come down the road, all grizzly and bloodshot, bait

by the boy, who have gradually swell in number as he draw nearer the

town.



Jane be shock at their heathenish cruelty, and go off the path to

remonstrate with they.



on this, Maxley fall upon she, and begin beat she about the head and

shoulder with his heavy stick.



the miserable boy utter yell of dismay, but do nothing.



Julia utter a violent scream, but fly to her friend's aid, and

cry, “oh you wretch! you wretch!” actually catch the man by the

throat and shake he violently. he take his hand off Jane Hardie, who

instantly sink moan on the ground, and he cower like a cur at the

voice and the purple gleam eye of the excited girl.



the air fill with cry, and Edward run out of the house to see what

be the matter; but on the spot nobody be game enough to come between

the furious man and the fiery girl. the consequence be, her impetuous

courage begin to flag and her eye to waver; the demented man find

this out by some half animal instinct, and instantly catch she by the

shoulder and whirl she down on her knee; then raise his staff high

to destroy she.



she scream, and be just put up her hand, womanlike, not to see

her death as well as feel it, when something dark come past she like a

rush wind--a blow, that sound exactly like that of a pave ram,

catch Maxley on the jaw: and there be Edward Dodd blow like a

grampus with rage, and Maxley on his back in the road. but man under

cerebral excitement be not easily stun, and know no pain: he bound

off the ground, and come at Edward like a spanish bull. Edward slip

aside, and catch he another ponderous blow that send he staggering,

and his bludgeon fly out of his hand, and Edward catch it. Lo! the

maniac fly at he again more fiercely than ever; but the young Hercules

have see Jane bleed on the ground: he deal her assailant in full

career such a murderous stroke with the bludgeon, that the people, who

be run from all quarter, shriek with dismay--not for Jane, but

for Maxley; and well they might; that awful stroke lay he senseless,

motionless and mute, in a pool of his own blood.



“don't kill he, sir; don't kill the man,” be the cry.



“why not?” say Edward sternly. he then kneel over his sweetheart and

lift she in his arm like a child. her bonnet be all break, her eye

be turn upwards and set, and a little blood trickle down her cheek;

and that cheek seem streaked white and red.



he be terrify, agonised; yet he gasp out, “you be safe, dear;

don't be frightened.”



she know the voice.



“oh, Edward!” she say piteously and tenderly, and then moan a little

on his broad bosom. he carry she into the house out of the crowd.



poor old doctor Phillips, come in to end his day in the almshouse,

have see it all: he get out of his cart and hobble up. he have be in

the army, and have both experience and skill. he get her bonnet off, and

at sight of her head look very grave.



in a minute a bed be lay in the drawing-room, and all the window and

door open: and Edward, tremble now in every limb, run to Musgrove

Cottage, while Mrs. Dodd and Julia loosen the poor girl's dress, and

bathe her wound with tepid water (the doctor would not allow cold),

and put wine carefully to her lip with a teaspoon.



“want at your house, pray what for?” say Mr. Hardie superciliously.



“oh, sir,” say Edward, “such a calamity. pray come directly. a ruffian

have strike she, have hurt she terribly, terribly.”



“she! who?” ask Mr. Hardie, begin to be uneasy.



“who! why Jane, your daughter, man; and there you sit chatter,

instead of come at once.”



Mr. Hardie rise hurriedly and put on his hat, and accompany he, half

confused.



soon Edward's mute agitation communicate itself to he, and he went

stride and tremble by his side.



the crowd have go with insensible Maxley to the hospital, but the

trace of the terrible combat be there. where Maxley fall the last

time, a bullock seem to have be slaughter at the least.



the miserable father come on this, and give a great scream like a woman,

and stagger back white as a sheet.



Edward lay his hand on he, for he seem scarce able to stand.



“no, no, no,” he cry, comprehend the mistake at last; “that be not

her--Heaven forbid! that be the madman's who do it; I knock he down

with his own cudgel.”



“God bless you! you've kill he, I hope.”



“oh, sir, be more merciful, and then perhaps he will be merciful to we,

and not take this angel from we.”



“no! no! you be right; good young man. I little think I have such a

friend in your house.”



“don't deceive yourself, sir,” say Edward; “it's not you I care for:”

 then, with a great cry of anguish, _“I love she._”



at this blunt declaration, so new and so offensive to he, Mr. Hardie

wince, and stop bewildered.



but they be at the gate, and Edward hurry he on. at the house door

he draw back once more; for he feel a shiver of repugnance at enter

this hateful house, of whose happiness he be the destroyer.



but enter it he must; it be his fate.



the wife of the poor Captain he have drive mad meet he in the passage,

her motherly eye full of tear for he, and both hand hold out to he

like a pity angel. “oh, Mr. Hardie,” she say in a broken voice, and

take he, and lead he, wonder-strike, stupefy, shiver with dark

fear, to the room where his crushed daughter lay.









CHAPTER XXXVIII



MR. HARDIE find his daughter lie ashy pale on a little bed in the

drawing-room of Albion Villa. she be now scarce conscious. the old

doctor sit at her head look very grave; and Julia kneel over her

beloved friend, pale as herself; with hand clasp convulsively, and

great eye of terror and grief.



that vivid young face, full of foreboding and woe, strike Mr. Hardie the

moment he enter, and freeze his very heart. the strong man quiver and

sink slowly like a felled tree by the bedside; and his face and the poor

girl's, whose earthly happiness he have coldly destroy, nearly meet over

his crushed daughter.



“Jane, my child,” he gasp; “my poor little jane!”



“oh let I sleep,” she moan feebly.



“darling, it's your own papa,” say Julia softly.



“poor papa!” say she, turn rather to Julia than to he. “let I

sleep.”



she be in a half lethargic state.



Mr. Hardie ask the doctor in an agitated whisper if he might move she

home. the doctor shake his head: “not by my advice; her pulse be scarce

perceptible. we must not move she nor excite she, nor yet let she sink

into lethargy. she be in great danger, very great.”



at these terrible word Mr. Hardie groan: and they all begin to speak

below the breath.



“Edward,” murmur Mrs. Dodd hurriedly, “run and put off the auction:

put it off altogether; then go to the railway; nothing must come here to

make a noise, and get straw put down directly. do that first, dear.”



“you be kind to I than I deserve,” mutter Mr. Hardie humbly, quite

cow by the blow that have fall on he. the word agitate Mrs. Dodd

with many thought, but she whisper as calmly as she could, “let we

think of nothing now but this precious life.”



Mr. Hardie beg to see the extent of the injury.



Mrs. Dodd dissuade he, but he persist. then the doctor show she

poor head.



at that the father utter a scream and sit quivering. Julia bury

her face in the bed-clothe directly, and sob vehemently. it pass

faintly across the benumbed and shudder father, “how she love my

child; they all love she,” but the thought make little impression at the

time; the mind be too full of terror and woe. the doctor now ask for

brandy in a whisper. Mrs. Dodd leave the room with stealthy foot, and

bring it. he ask for a quill. Julia go with swift, stealthy foot,

and bring it. with adroit and tender hand they aid the doctor,

and trickle stimulant down her throat. then sit like statue of grief

about the bed; only every now and then eye seek eye, and endeavour

to read what the other thought. be there hope? be there none? and

by-and-bye, so roving be the mind, especially when the body be still,

these statue begin to thrill with thought of the past as well as the

absorbing present.



ay, here be meet a strange party; a stranger, for its size, methink,

never yet meet on earth, to mingle their heart together in one grief.



just think! of he who sit there with his face hide in his hand, and

his frame shudder, all the other be the victim.



yet the lady, whose husband he have rob and drive mad, pitied and

sympathise with he, and he see it; the lady, whom he have insult at

the altar and blight her young heart and life, pity and sympathise

with he; the poor old doctor pity and sympathise, and be more like

an anxious father than a physician.



even Jane be one of his victim; for she fall by the hand of a man he

have dishonestly ruin and drive out of his sense.



think of all he have do, and this the end of it, he be at once

crush and melt.



he see with awe that a mighty hand than man's be upon he; it have

toss he and his daughter into the house and the arm of the injure

Dodds, in defiance of all human calculation; and he feel himself a straw

in that hand: so he be, and the great globe itself. oh, if Jane should

die! the one creature he love, the one creature, bereave of whom he

could get no joy even from rich.



what would he not give to recall the past, since all his scheme have but

end in this. thus stricken by terror of the divine wrath, and touch

by the goodness and kindness of those he have cruelly wrong, all the

man be break with remorse. then he vow to undo his own work as far

as possible: he would do anything, everything, if Heaven would spare he

his child.



now it do so happen that these resolve, earnest and sincere but

somewhat vague, be soon put to the test; and, as often occur, what he

be call on to do first be that which he would rather have do last.

thus it be: about five o'clock in the afternoon Jane Hardie open she

eye and look about she.



it be a moment of intense anxiety. they all make signal, but hold

their breath. she smile at sight of Mr. Hardie, and say, “Papa! dear

papa!”



there be great joy: silent on the part of Mrs. Dodd and Julia; but Mr.

Hardie, who see in this a good oman, Heaven recognise his penitence,

burst out: “she know I; she speak; she will live. how good God be!

yes, my darling child, it be your own father. you will be brave and get

well, for my sake.”



Jane do not seem to pay much heed to these word: she look straight

before she like one occupy with her own thought, and say distinctly

and solemnly, “Papa--send for Alfred.”





it fall on all three like a clap of thunder, those gentle but decide

tone, those simple natural word.



Julia's eye flash into her mother's, and then seek the ground

directly.



there be a dead silence.





Mr. Hardie be the one to speak “why for he, dear? those who love you

good be all here.”



“for Heaven's sake, don't thwart she, sir,” say the doctor, in alarm.

“this be no time to refuse she anything in your power. sometimes the

very expectation of a beloved person come keep they alive; stimulate

the power.”



Mr. Hardie be sore perplexed. he recoil from the sudden exposure

that might take place, if Alfred without any preparation or previous

conciliatory measure be allow to burst in upon they. and while his

mind be whirl within he in doubt and perplexity, Jane speak again;

but no long calmly and connectedly; she be begin to wander.

presently in her wandering she speak of Edward; call he dear Edward.

Mrs. Dodd rise hastily, and her first impulse be to ask both gentleman

to retire: so instinctively do a good woman protect her own sex

against the other. but, reflect that this be the father, she make

an excuse and retire herself instead, follow by Julia. the doctor

divine, and go to the window. the father sit by the bed, and soon

gather his daughter love Edward Dodd.



the time be go by when this would have greatly pain he.



he sigh like one overmatche by fate; but say, “you shall have he,

my darling; he be a good young man, he shall be your husband, you

shall be happy. only live for my sake, for all our sake.” she pay no

attention and wander on a little; but her mind gradually clear, and

by-and-bye she ask quietly for a glass of water. Mr. Hardie give it

she. she sip, and he take it from she. she look at he close, and

say distinctly, “have you send for Alfred?”



“no, love, not yet.”



“not yet? there be no time to lose,” she say gravely.



Mr. Hardie tremble. then, be alone with she, the miserable man

unable to say no, unwilling to say yes, try to persuade she not to ask

for Alfred. “my dear,” he whisper, “I will not refuse you: but I have

a secret to confide to you. will you keep it?”



“yes, papa, faithfully.”



“Poor Alfred be not himself. he have delusion: he be partly insane. my

brother Thomas have think it well for we all to put he under gentle

restraint for a time. it would retard his cure to have he down here and

subject he to excitement.”



“Papa,” say Jane, “be you deceive I, or be you impose upon?

alfred insane! it be a falsehood. he come to I the night before the

wedding that be to be. oh, my brother, my darling brother, how dare

they say you be insane! that letter you show I then be a falsehood?

oh, papa!”



“I fear to frighten you,” say Mr. Hardie, and hang his head.



“I see it all,” she cry “those wicked man with their dark word have

impose on you. bring he to I that I may reconcile you all, and end

all this misery ere I go hence and be no more see.”



“oh, my child, don't talk so,” cry Mr. Hardie, tremble. “think of

your poor father.”



“I do,” she cry, “I do. oh, papa, I lie here between two world, and

see they both so clear. trust to I: and, if you love me----”



“if I love you, Jane? well than all the world twice tell.”



“then don't refuse I this one favour: the last, perhaps, I shall ever

ask you. I want my brother here before it be too late. tell he he must

come to his little sister, who love he dearly, and--be die.



“oh no! no! no!” cry the agonised father, cast everything to the

wind. “I will. he shall be here in twelve hour. only promise I to

bear up. have a strong will; have courage. you shall have Alfred, you

shall have anything you like on earth, anything that money can get you.

what be I say? I have no money; it be all go. but I have a father's

heart. Madam, Mrs. Dodd!” she come directly.



“can you give I paper? no, I willn't trust to a letter. I'll send off a

special messenger this moment. it be for my son, madam. he will be here

to-morrow morning. God know how it will all end. but how can I refuse

my die child? oh, madam, you be good, kind, forgiving; keep my poor

girl alive for I: keep tell her Alfred be come; she care more for

he than for her poor heart-break father.”



and the miserable man rush out, leave Mrs. Dodd in tear for he.



he be no soon go than Julia come in; and clasp her mother,

and tremble on her bosom. then Mrs. Dodd know she have overhear Mr.

Hardie's last word.



Jane Hardie, too, though much exhaust by the scene with her father,

put out her hand to Julia, and take her, and say feebly, but with a

sweet smile, “he be come, love; all shall be well.” then to herself as

it be, and look up with a gentle rapture in her pale face--



“Blessed be the peacemaker; for they shall be call the child of

God.”



on this thought she seem to feed with innocent joy; but for a long

time be too weak to speak again.



Mr. Hardie, rush from the house, find Edward at work outside; he be

cry undisguisedly, and with his coat off, work hard at spread

the straw than both the two man together he have get to help he. Mr.

Hardie take his hand and wring it, but could not speak.



in half an hour a trusty agent he have often employ be at the station

wait for the up train, nearly due.



he come back to Albion Villa. Julia meet he on the stair with her

finger to her lip. “she be sleep; the doctor have hope. oh, sir,

let we all pray for her day and night.”



Mr. Hardie bless she; it seem the face of an angel, so earnest, so

lovely, so pious. he go home: and at the door of his own house Peggy

meet he with anxious look. he tell she what he have do.



“Good Heavens!” say she; “have you forget? he say he will kill you

the first day he get out. you tell I so yourself.”



“yes, Baker say so. I cann't help it. I don't care what become of I; I

care only for my child. leave I, Peggy; there, go, go.”



he be no soon alone than he fall upon his knee, and offer the

Great Author of life and death--a bargain. “o God,” he cry, “I own my

sin, and I repent they. spare but my child, who never sin against

Thee, and I will undo all I have do amiss in Thy sight. I will refund

that money on which Thy curse lie. I will throw myself on their mercy.

I will set my son free. I will live on a pittance. I will part with

Peggy. I will serve Mammon no more. I will attend thine ordinance. I

will live soberly, honestly, and godly all the remainder of my day;

only do Thou spare my child. she be Thy servant, and do Thy work on

earth, and there be nothing on earth I love but she.”





and now the whistle sound, the train move, and his messenger be

fly fast to London, with a note to Dr. Wycherley:



“DEAR SIR,--My poor daughter lie dangerously wound, and perhaps at

the point of death. she cry for her brother. he must come down to we

instantly with the bearer of this. send one of your people with he if

you like. but it be not necessary. I enclose a blank cheque, sign,

which please fill at your discretion.--I am, with thank, your in deep

distress,



“RICHARD HARDIE”











CHAPTER XXXIX



DR. SHORT arrive, approve Dr. Phillips's treatment, and say the case

be severe but not hopeless, and he would call again. a bed be prepare

in the house for Mr. Hardie: but neither he nor any of the Dodds close

an eye that sorrowful night.



about midnight, after a short slumber, the sufferer become uneasy, and

beg to be leave with Julia. Julia be send for, and find she a good

deal excited. she inquire more than once if they be quite alone, and

then ask for paper and a pencil. she write a few line, and make

Julia put they in a cover and seal they. “now, dear friend,” she say,

“promise I not to open this, nor even to let your mother; it be not for

your happiness that what I have write should be see by she or you;

no, no, much well not. come; dear friend, pledge I your honour.”

 Julia pledge her honour.



then Jane write on the cover, “from a die sister.” Julia see that; and

weep sore.



Jane comfort she. “do not weep for I, love: I be content to go, or

stay. this be not my doing; so I know it must be for the good. he be

lead I by a way that I know not. oh, my beloved friend, how sweet

it be to lie in his hand, and know no will but his. ay, I thank he for

cross my will, and lead I to himself by his own good way, and not

by poor blind, foolish mine.”



in this spirit of full resignation she abode constant, and console she

weeping friend from time to time, whenever she be quite herself.



about daybreak, be alone with her father, she shed a few tear at his

lonely condition. “I fear you will miss I,” say she. “take my advice,

dear; be reconcile with Alfred at once, and let Julia be your daughter,

since I be leave you. she be all humility and heart. die, I prize

she and her affection more highly; I seem to see character clear, all

thing clear, than I do before my summon come.”



the miserable father try to be playful and scold she: “you must not

talk nor think of death,” he say. “your bridal-day be to come first;

I know all; Edward Dodd have tell I he love you. he be a fine noble

fellow; you shall marry he: I wish it. now, for his sake, summon all

your resolution, and make up your mind to live. why, at your age,

it need but to say, 'I will live, I will, I will;' and when all the

prospect be so smile, when love await you at the altar, and on every

side! if you could leave your poor doting father, do not leave your

lover: and here he be with his mother cry for you. let I comfort

he; let I tell he you will live for his sake and mine.”



even this could not disturb the die Christian. “Dear Edward,” she

say; “it be sweet to know he love I. ah, well, he be young; he must

live without I till I become but a tender memory of his youth. and oh,

I pray for he that he may cherish the word I have speak to he for

his soul's good far long than he can remember these feature that be

hasten to decay.”



at ten in the morning Mr. Hardie's messenger return without Alfred,

and with a note from Dr. Wycherley to this effect, that, the order for

Alfred's admission into his asylum be sign by Mr. Thomas Hardie,

he could not send he out even for a day except on Thomas Hardie's

authority; it would be a violation of the law. under the circumstance,

however, he think he might venture to receive that order by telegraph.

if, then, Mr. Hardie would telegraph Thomas Hardie in Yorkshire to

telegraph he (Wycherley), Alfred should be send with two keeper

wherever Mr. T. Hardie should so direct.



now Mr. Hardie have already repent of send for Alfred at all.

so, instead of telegraphing Yorkshire, he remain passive, and say

sullenly to Mrs. Dodd, “Alfred cann't come, it seem.”



thus Routine keep the brother from his die sister.



they tell Jane, with ache heart, there be reason to fear Alfred

could not arrive that day.



she only give a meaning look at Julia, about the paper; and then she

say with a little sigh, “God's will be do.”



this be the last disappointment Heaven allow Earth to inflict on she;

and the shield of Faith turn its edge.



one hour of pain, another of delirium, and now the cloud that darken

this mortal life seem to part and pass, and Heaven to open full upon

she. she speak of her come change no long with resignation; it be

with rapture. “oh!” she cry, “to think that from this very day I

shall never sin again, shall never again offend he by unholy temper, by

un-christ-like behaviour!”



the strong and healthy wept and groan aloud; but she they sorrow for

be all celestial bliss. in her lifetime she have her up and down of

religious fervour; be not without feverish heat, and cold misgiving

and depression; but all these flee at that dread hour when the wicked

be a prey to dark misgiving, or escape into apathy. this timid girl

that would have scream at a scratch, meet the King of Terrors with

smile and triumph. for she the grave be Jordan, and death be but the

iron gate of life everlaste. _mor janua vitae._ yet once or twice she

take herself to task: but only to show she know what the all-Pure have

forgive she. “I often be want in humility,” she say; “I almost

think that if I be to be send back again into this world of sin and

sorrow I be leave behind, I should grow a little in humility; for I

know the ripe Christian be like the ripe corn, hold his head low than

when he be green; and the grave it seem to be ripen I. but what

do it matter? since he who die for I be content to take I as I be.

come quickly, Lord Jesus, oh, come quickly! Relieve Thy servant from the

burden of the flesh, and of the sin and foible that cling to it and

keep she these many year from Thee.”



this prayer be grant; the body fail more and more; she could not

swallow even a drop of wine; she could not even praise her Redeemer;

that be to say, she could not speak. yet she lie and triumph.

with hand put together in prayer, and eye full of praise and joy

unspeakable, she climb fast to God. while she so mount in the

spirit, her breath come at interval unusually long, and all be send

for to see death conquer the body and be conquer by the soul.



at last, after an unnaturally long interval, she draw a breath like a

sigh. they wait for another; wait, wait in vain.



she have calmly cease to live.





the old doctor lay down her hand reverently, and say “she be with we

no more.” then with many tear, “oh, may we all meet where she be now,

and may I go to she the first.”





Richard Hardie be lead from the room in a stupor.





immediately after death all the disfiguring effect of pain retire, and

the happy soul seem to have stamp its own celestial rapture on the

countenance at the moment of leave it; a rapture so wonderful, so

divine, so more than mortal calm, irradiate the dead face. the good

Christians she leave behind she look on and fear to weep, lest they

should offend he, who have take she to himself, and set a visible seal

upon the house of clay that have hold she. “oh, mamma,” cry Julia with

fervour, “look! look! can we, dare we, wish that angel back to this

world of misery and sin?” and it be some hour before she cool, and

begin to hang on Edward's neck and weep his loss and her, as weep we

mortal must, though the angel of Heaven be rejoice.





thus die in the flower of her youth, and by what we call a violent

death, the one child Richard Hardie love; member of a religious party

whose diction now and then offend one to the soul: but the root of

the matter be in they; allowance make for those passion, foible, and

infirmity of the flesh, even you and I be not entirely free from,

they live fear God, and die love Him.





there be an inqu next day, follow in due course by a public trial

of James Maxley. but these be matter which, though rather curious and

interesting, must be omit, or touch hereafter and briefly.



the effect of Jane's death on Richard Hardie be deplorable. he see

the hand of Heaven; but do not bow to it: so it fill he with rage,

rebellion, and despair. he get his daughter away and hide himself in the

room with she; scarce stir out by night or day. he speak to no one;

he shun the Dodds: he hate they. he say it be through visit

their house she have meet her death, and at their door. he would not let

himself see it be he who have send she there with his lie. he loathe

Alfred, call he the cause of all.



he ask nobody to the funeral: and, when Edward beg permission to

come, he give a snarl like a wild beast and go rage from he. but

Edward would go: and at the graveside pity Heaven relieve the young

fellow's choke heart with tear. but no such dew come to that parch

old man, who stand on its other side like the withered Archangel, his

eye gloomy and wild, his white cheek plough deep with care and

crime and anguish, his lofty figure bow by his long warfare, his

soul burn and sicken by turn, with hatred and rebellion, with

desolation and despair.



he go home and make his will; for he feel life hang on he like lead,

and that any moment he might kill himself to be rid of it. strange to

say, he leave a sum of money to Edward Dodd. a moment before, he don't

know he be go to do it: a moment after, he be half surprised he have

do it, and mind to undo it; but would not take the trouble. he go

up to London, and dash into speculation as some in their despair take

to drink. for this man have but two passion; avarice, and his love for

his daughter. bereave of she, he must either die, or live for gain. he

seek the very cave of Mammon; he plunge into the Stock Exchange.





when Mr. Hardie say, “Alfred cann't come, it seem,” Mrs. Dodd

misunderstood he, naturally enough. she think the heartless young man

have send some excuse: have choose to let his sister die neglect rather

than face Julia: “as if she would leave her own room while _he_ be in

my house,” say Mrs. Dodd, with sovereign contempt. from this moment

she conceive a horror of the young man. Edward share it fully, and the

pair always speak of he under the title of “the Wretch:” this be when

Julia be not by. in her presence he be never mention. by this mean

she would in time forget he, or else see he as they see he.



and as, after all, they know little to Mr. Hardie's disadvantage, except

what have come out of “the Wretch's” mouth, and as moreover their heart

be soften towards the father by his bereavement, and their sight of

his misery, and also by his grateful word, they quite acquit he of

having rob they, and feel sure the fourteen thousand pound be at

the bottom of the sea.



they be a little surprised that Mr. Hardie never speak nor write to

they again; but be high-minded and sweet temper, they set it down

to all-absorb grief, and would not feel sore about it.



and now they must leave the little villa where they have be so happy

and so unhappy.



the scanty furniture go first; Mrs. Dodd follow, and arrange it

in their apartment. Julia would stay behind to comfort Edward,

inconsolable herself. the auction come off. Most of the thing go for

cruelly little money compare to their value: and with the balance the

sad young pair come up to London, and be clasp in their mother's

arm. the tear be in her tender eye. “it be a poor place to receive

my treasure,” she say: Edward look round astonish: “it be a poor

place,” say he, “but you have make a little palace of it, somehow or

another.”



“my child's love can alone do that,” reply Mrs. Dodd, kiss they

both again.



next day they consult together how they be to live. Edward wish to

try and get his father into a public asylum; then his mother would have

a balance to live upon out of her income. but Mrs. Dodd reject this

proposal with astonishment. in vain Edward cite the _'Tiser_ that

public asylum be pattern of comfort, and cure twice as many patient

as the private one do. she be deaf alike to the _'Tiser_ and to

statistic. “do not argue I out of my common sense,” say she. “my

husband, your father, in a public asylum, where anybody can go and stare

at my darling!”



she then inform they she have write to her Aunt Bazalgette and her

Uncle Fountain, and invite they to contribute something towards David's

maintenance.



Edward be almost angry at this. “fancy asking favour of _they,_” say

he.



“oh, I must not sacrifice my family to false pride,” say Mrs. Dodd;

“besides they be entitle to know.”



while wait for their answer, a word about the party and their

niece.



our Mrs. Dodd, bear Lucy Fountain, be leave at nineteen to the care

of two guardian: 1, her Uncle Fountain, an old bachelor, who love

comfort, pedigree, and his own way; 2, her Aunt Bazalgette, who loved

flirting, dressing, and her own way; both charming people, when they get

their own way; verjuice, when they don't: and, to conclude, egotist

deep as ocean. from guardian they grow match-maker and rival by

proxy: uncle scheme to graft Lucy on to a stick call Talboys, that

come in with the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes, know in pedigree

as “the Norman Conquest.” Aunt, wife of a merchant of no descent,

except from a high stool, devote she to Richard Hardie. an unlooked-for

obstacle encounter both: Lucy be not amorous. she love these two

egotist and their quadruped; but there she stop dead short. they

persist; and, while they pull she to and fro and ruffle she

native calm, David Dodd, first mate of the something or other East

Indiaman--brown cheek, honest speech, heart of gold--fall deep in love

and worship she at a distance. his timidity and social insignificance

make he harmless; so egotist Fountain have he in to dessert to spin

yarn; egotist Bazalgette invite he to her house to flirt with. at

this latter place he find Hardie and Talboys both court Lucy; this

drive he mad, and in his fury he pop. Lucy decline he _secundum

artem:_ he go away bless she, with a manly sob or two. Lucy cry

a little and take a feminine spite against his rival, who remain to

pester she. now Talboys, spur by uncle, have often all but pop;

only some let, hindrance, or just impediment have still interpose: once

her pony keep prance at each effort he make towards Hymen; they do say

the subtle virgin keep probe the brute with a hair pin, and make he

caracole and spill the treacle as fast as it come her way. however,

now talboy elect to pop by sea. it be the element his ancestor have

invade fair England by; and on its tranquil bosom a lover be safe from

prance steed, and the myriad anti-pop of _terra firma._ Miss Lucy

consent to the water excursion demurely, design to bring her sickly

wooer to the point and so get rid of he for ever and ever. plot and

counter-plot be baffle by the element: there come an anti-pop out

of the south-west call a gale. talboy boat so skilfully that he and

his intend would have be unite without ceremony by Father Nep, at

the bottom of the British Channel, but for David Dodd, who be hover

near in jealous anguish and a cutter. he save they both, but in the

doing of it miss his ship, and professional ruin face he. then

good-hearted Lucy be miserable, and appeal to Mr. Bazalgette, and he

manage somehow to get David make captain of the _Rajah._ the poor girl

think she have square the account with David; but he refuse the ship

unless she would go half, and while her egotist bully and vex

she, he work so upon her pity, and tease she so, that to get rid

of his importunity she marry he. in time she learn to love he ten

time well than if she have begin all flame. Uncle and aunt cut

her tolerably dead for some year. Uncle come round the first; some

antiquarian show he that Dodd be a much more ancient family

than Talboys. “why, sir, they be lord of sixteen manor under the

Heptarchy, and hold some of they to this day.” Mrs. Bazalgette, too, have

long correspond with she periodically, and on friendly term.





the answer come on the same day, curiously enough. Uncle Fountain,

ruin by railway speculation, be live on an allowance from

creditor; but his house be at their service, if they like to live

with he--and board themselves.



Mrs. Bazalgette's be the letter of a smooth woman, who have hoard

imperishable spite. she remind her niece after all these year, that

her marriage with David be an act of disobedience and ingratitude. she

then enumerate her own heavy expense, all but the L. 400 a year she

spend in bedizen her carcass, and finally, amidst a multitude of

petty insult, she offer to relieve Mrs. Dodd of--Julia. now Poetry

have reconcile we to an asp in a basket of fig; but here be a scorpion

in a bundle of nettle. poor Mrs. Dodd could not speak after read

it. she hand it to Edward, and lay her white forehead wearily in her

hand. Edward put the letter in an envelope and send it back with a line

in his own hand decline all further correspondence with the writer.



“now then, lady,” say he, “don't you be cast down. let this be a

warning to we, never to ask favour of anybody. let we look the thing in

the face; we must work or starve: and all the well for we. hard work

suit heavy heart. come, have you any plan?”



“to be sure we have,” say Julia eagerly. “I mean to go for a governess,

and then I shall cost mamma nothing, and besides I can send she the

money the people give I.”



“a pretty plan!” say Edward sadly; “what! we three part company? don't

you feel lonely enough without that? I do then. how can we bear our

burden at all, if we be not to be all together to cheer one another

along the weary road? what! be we to break up? be it not enough to be

bereaved?”



he could say no more for the emotion his own word cause he; think

of Jane, he break down altogether, and run out of the room.



however, he come back in an hour with his eye red, but his heart

indomitable; determine to play a man's part for all their sake. “you

lady,” say he, with something of his old genial way, that sound

so strange to one look at his red eye, and inspire a desire to hug

he, “be full of talent, but empty of invention. the moment you be

ruin or that sort of thing, it be, _go_ for a governess, _go_ for a

companion, _go_ here, _go_ there, in search of what? independence? no;

dependence. besides all this _go_ be bosh. family be strong if

they stick together, and if they go to piece they be weak. I learn

one bit of sense out of that mass of folly they call antiquity; and that

be the story of the old bloke with his twelve son, and fagot to match.

'break 'em apart,' he say, and each son break his stick as easy as

shell pea. 'now break the twelve all tie together:' devil a bit

could the duffer break it then. now we be not twelve, we be but

three: easy to break one or two of we apart, but not the lot together.

no; nothing but death shall break this fagot, for nothing less shall

part we three.”



he stand like a colossus, and hold out his hand to they; they clung

round his neck in a moment, as if to illustrate his word; clung tight,

and bless he for stand so firm and forbid they to part.



Mrs. Dodd sigh, after the first burst of enthusiastic affection, and

say: “if he would only go a step far and tell we what to do in

company.”



“ay, there it be,” say Julia. “begin with I. what can I do?”



“why, paint.”



“what, to sell? oh dear, my daub be not good enough for that.”



“stuff! nothing be too bad to _sell._”



“I really think you might,” say Mrs. Dodd, “and I will help you.”



“no, no, mamma, I want you for something well than the fine art.

you must go in one of the great groove: female vanity: you must be a

dressmaker; you be a genius at it.”



“my mamma a dressmaker,” cry Julia; “oh Edward, how can you. how dare

you. poor, poor mamma!”



“do not be so impetuous, dear. I think he be right: yes, it be all I be

fit for. if ever there be a Heaven-bear dressmaker, it's I.”



“as for myself,” say Edward, “I shall look out for some business in

which physical strength go far than intellectual attainment.

luckily there be plenty such. break stone be one. but I shall try a

few other first.”



it be easy to settle on a business, hard to get a footing in one. Edward

convince that the dressmaking be their good card, search that mine

of various knowledge, the _'Tiser,_ for an opening: but none come. at

last one of those great miscellaneous house in the City advertise

for a lady to cut cloak. he propose to his mother to go with he. she

shrink from encounter stranger. no, she would go to a fashionable

dressmaker she have employ some year, and ask her advice. perhaps

Madame Blanch would find she something to do. “I have more faith in the

_'Tiser,_” say Edward, cling to his idol.



Mrs. Dodd find Madame Blanch occupy in try to suit one of those

heart-break idiot, to whom dress be the one great thing, and all

thing else, sin include, the little one. she have try on a scarf

three time; and it discontent she when on, and spoil all else when

off. Mrs. Dodd see, and say obligingly, “perhaps be I to put it on,

you could well judge.” Mrs. Dodd, you must know, have an admirable art

of put on a shawl or scarf. with apparent _nonchalance_ she settle

the scarf on her shapely shoulder so happily that the fish bit, and the

scarf go into its carriage; forty guineas, or so. Madame cast a rapid

but ardent glance of gratitude Dodd-ward. the customer begin to go, and

after fidget to the door and back for twenty minute actually go

somehow. then madame turn round, and say, “I'm sure, ma'am, I be much

oblige to you; you sell I the scarf: and it be a pity we couldn't put

she on your bust and shoulder, ma'am, then perhaps a scarf might please

she. what can I do for you, ma'am?”



Mrs. Dodd blush, and with subdue agitation tell Madame Blanch that

this time she be come not to purchase but to ask a favour. Misfortune

be heavy on she; and, though not penniless, she be so reduce by her

husband's illness and the loss of L. 14,000 by shipwreck, that she must

employ what little talent she have to support her family.



the woman explore she from head to foot to find the change of fortune

in some corner of her raiment: but her customer be as well, though

plainly dress as ever, and still look an easy-go duchess.



“could Madame Blanch find her employment in her own line? what talent I

have,” say Mrs. Dodd humbly, “lie in that way. I could not cut as

well as yourself, of course; but I think I can as well as some of your

people.”



“that I'll be bind you can,” say Madame Blanch drily. “but dear, dear,

to think of your having come down so. have a glass of wine to cheer you

a bit; do now, that be a good soul.”



“oh no, madam. I thank you; but wine cannot cheer I: a little bit of

good news to take back to my anxious child, that would cheer I,

madam. _will_ you be so good?”



the dressmaker colour and hesitate; she feel the fascination of

dignity don Humility, and speak music: but she resist. “it

willn't do, at least here. I shouldn't be mistress in my own place. I

couldn't drive you like I be force to do the rest; and, then, I should

be sure to favour you, be a real lady, which be my taste, and you

always will be, rich or poor; and then all my lady would be on the

bile with jealousy.”



“ah, madam,” sigh Mrs. Dodd, “you treat I like a child; you give I

sweetmeat, and refuse I food for my family.”



“no, no,” say the woman hastily, “I don't say I mightn't send you out

some work to do at home.”



“oh, thank you, madam.” _N.B._ the dressmaker have drop the Madam, so

the lady use it now at every word.



“now stop a bit,” say Madame Blanch. “I know a firm that's in want.

Theirs be easy work by mine, and they cut up a piece of stuff every two

or three day.” she then write on one of her own card, Messrs. Cross,

Fitchett, Copland, and Tylee, 11, 12, 13, and 14, Primrose Lane, City.

“say, I recommend you. to tell the truth, an old hand of my own be to

come here this very morning about it, but she haven't keep she time; so

this will learn her business don't stand still for lie-a-bed to catch

it.”



Mrs. Dodd put the card in her bosom and press the hand extend to she

by Madame Zaire Blanch; whose name be Sally White, spinster. she go

back to her child and show they the card, and sink gracefully into

a chair, exhaust as much by the agitation of asking favour as by

the walk. “Cross, Fitchett, Copland? why they be in the _'Tiser_

yesterday,” say Edward: “look at this; a day lose by be wise than

the _'Tiser._”



“I'll waste no more then,” say Mrs. Dodd, rise quietly from the

chair. they beg she to rest herself first. no, she would not. “I see

this lose by half an hour,” say she. “succeed or fail, I will have no

remissness to reproach myself with.” and she glide off in her quiet

way, to encounter Cross, Fitchett, Copland and Tylee, in the lane where

a primrose be catch grow--six hundred year ago. she decline

Edward's company rather peremptorily. “stay and comfort your sister,”

 say she. but that be a blind; the truth be, she could not bear her

child to mingle in what she be do. no, her ambition be to ply

the scissor and thimble vigorously, and so enable they to be lady and

gentleman at large. she be go, Julia make a parcel of water-colour

drawing, and sally forth all on fire to sell they. but, while she be

dress, Edward start on a cruise in search of employment. he fail

entirely. they meet in the evening, Mrs. Dodd resign, Edward dog,

Julia rather excited. “now, let we tell our adventure,” she say. “as

for I, shop after shop decline my poor sketch. they all want

something about as good, only a little different: nobody complain

of the grand fault, and that be, their utter badness. at last, one old

gentleman examine they, and oh! he be so fat; there, round. and he

twist his mouth so” (imitate he) “and squint into they so. then

I be full of hope; and say to myself; 'dear mamma and Edward!' and

so, when he end by say, 'no,' like all the rest, I burst out cry

like a goose.



“my poor girl,” cry Mrs. Dodd, with tear in her own eye, “why expose

yourself to these cruel rebuff?”



“oh, don't waste your pity, mamma; those great babyish tear be a

happy thought of mine. he buy two directly to pacify I; and there's

the money. thirty shilling!” and she lay it proudly on the table.



“the old cheat,” say Edward; “they be worth two guinea apiece, I

know.”



“not they; or why would not anybody else give twopence for they?”



“because picture be a drug.”



he add that even talent be not saleable unless it get into the Great

Grooves; and then look at Mrs. Dodd, she reply that unfortunately

those Grooves be not always accessible. the City firm have receive she

stiffly, and inquire for whom she have work. “child, my heart

fall at that question. I be oblige to own myself an amateur and beg a

trial. however, I give Madame Blanch's card: but Mr.--I don't know which

partner it be--say he be not acquaint with she: then he look a

little embarrassed, I think, and say the Firm do not care to send

its stuff to lady not in the business. I might cut it to waste, or--he

say no more; but I do really think he mean I might purloin it.”



“why ben't I there to look he into the earth? oh, mamma, that you

should be subject to all this!”



“be quiet, child; I have only to put on my armour; and do you know what

my armour be? think of my child. so I put on my armour, and say

quietly, we be not so poor but we could pay for a piece of cloth

should I be so unfortunate as to spoil it; and I offer in plain term

to deposit the price as security. but he turn as stiff at that as his

yard measure; 'that be not Cross and Co.'s way of do business,' he

say. but it be unreasonable to be deject at a repulse or two; and I

be not out of spirit; not much:” with this her gentle mouth smile; and

her patient eye be moist.



the next day, just after breakfast, be announce a gentleman from

the City. he make his bow and produce a parcel, which prove to be a

pattern cloak. “Order, lady,” say he briskly, “from Cross, Fitchett,

and Co., Primrose Lane. porter outside with the piece. you can come in,

sir.” Porter enter with a bale. “please sign this, ma'am.” Mrs. Dodd

sign a receipt for the stuff, with an undertaking to deliver it in

cloak, at 11 Primrose Lane, in such a time. Porter retreat. the other

say, “our Mr. Fitchett wish you to observe this fall in the pattern.

it be new.”



“I will, sir. be I to trouble you with any money--by way of deposit,

sir?”



“no order about it, ma'am. lady, your most obedient. good morning,

sir.”



and he be away.



all this seem like a click or two of City clock-work: follow by

rural silence. yet in that minute Commerce have walk in upon genteel

poverty, and leave honest labour and modest income behind she.



Great be the thankfulness, strange and new the excitement. Edward be

employ to set up a very long deal table for his mother to work on,

Julia to go and buy tailor' scissor. calculation be make how to cut

the stuff to advantage, and in due course the heavy scissor be hear

snick, snick, snick all day long.





Julia paint zealously, and Edward, without say a word to they,

walk twenty mile a day hunt for a guinea a week; and find it

not. not but what employment be often bob before his eye: but there

be no grasp it. at last he hear of a place peculiarly suit to

he; a pack foreman's in a warehouse at Southwark; he go there, and

be refer to Mr. A.'s private house. Mr. A. be in the country for

a day. try Mr. B. Mr. B. be dine with the Lord Mayor. return

belate, he fall in with a fire; and, sad to say, life be in jeopardy:

a little old man have run out at the first alarm, when there be no

danger, and, as soon as the fire be hot, have run in again for his
