stocking, or some such treasure. fire do put out some people's

reason; clean. while he be rummage madly, the staircase catch,

and the smoke cut off his second exit, and drive he up to a little

staircase window at the side of the house. here he stand, hose in hand,

scorch behind and screech in front. a ladder have be bring: but

it be a yard short: and the poor old man dance on the window-ledge and

dare not come down to a gallant fireman who stand ready to receive he

at great personal peril. in the midst of shriek and cry and shout of

encouragement, Edward, a practised gymnast, see a chance. he run up the

ladder like a cat, beg the fireman to clasp it tight; then get on his

shoulder and manage to grasp the window-sill. he could always draw

his own weight up by his hand: so he soon have his knee on the sill,

and presently stand erect. he then put his left arm inside the window,

collar the old fellow with his right, and, half persuasion, half

force, actually lower he to the ladder with one Herculean arm amidst

a roar that make the Borough ring. such a strain could not long be

endure; but the fireman speedily relieve he by seize the old

fellow's foot and direct they on to the ladder, and so, prop he

by the waist, go down before he, and land he safe. Edward wait

till they be down: then beg they to hold the ladder tight below;

he hang from the ledge, get his eye well on the ladder below he, let

himself quietly drop, and catch hold of it with hand of iron, and

twisting round, come down the ladder on the inside hand over head

without use his foot, a favourite gymnastic exercise of his learn

at the Modern Athens. he be warmly receive by the crowd and by the

fireman. “you should be one of we, sir,” say a fine young fellow who

have cheer he and advise he all through. “I wish to Heaven I be,”

 say Edward. the other think he be joke, but laugh and say,

“then you should talk to our head man after the business; there be a

vacancy, you know.”



Edward see the fire out, and ride home on the engine. there he apply

to the head man for the vacancy.



“you be a stranger to I, sir,” say the head man. “and I be sure it be

no place for you; you be a gentleman.”



“well; be there anything ungentlemanly in save people's life and

property?”



“Hear! hear!” say a comic fireman.



the compliment begin to tell, though. other put in their word. “why,

Mr. Baldwin, if a gentleman ben't ashamed of we, why should we be

ashamed of he?”



“where will ye get a well?” ask another; and add, “he be no

stranger; we've see he work.”



“stop a bit,” say the comic fireman: “what do the dog say? just call

he, sir, if you please; his name be Charlie.”



Edward call the fire-dog kindly; he come and fawn on he; then

gravely snuff he all round, and retire wag his tail gently, as

much as to say, “I be rather take by surprise at first, but, on the

whole, I see no reason to recall my judgment.”



“it be all right,” say the fireman in chorus; and one that have not

yet speak to Edward now whisper he mysteriously, “ye see that there

dog--he know more than we do.”



after the dog, a biped oracle at head-quarter be communicate with,

and late that very night Edward be actually enrol a fireman; and

go home warm at heart than he have be for some time. they be all

in bed; and when he come down in the morning, Julia be read out

of the _'Tiser_ a spirited and magniloquent description of a fire in

Southwark, and of the heroism display by a young gentleman unknown,

but whose name the writer hope at so much the line would never be

allow to pass into oblivion, and be forget. in short, the _'Tiser_

pay he in one column, for year of devotion. now Edward, of

course, be go to relate his adventure; but the journal tell it

so gloriously, he hesitate to say, “I do all that.” he just sit and

stare, and wonder, and blush, and grin like an imbecile.



unfortunately look seldom escape the Doddesses. “what be that for?”

 inquire Julia reproachfully. “be that sheepish face the thing to wear

when a sister be read out an heroic action? ah, these be the thing

that make one long to be a man, to do they. what be you think about,

dear?”



“well, I be think the _'Tiser_ be pitch it rather strong.”



“my love, what an expression!”



“well, then, to be honest, I agree with you that it be a jolly thing to

fight with fire and save man's life; and I be glad you see it in that

light; for now you will approve the step I have take. lady, I have

put myself in the way of do this sort of thing every week of my life.

I'm a fireman.”



“you be jest, I trust?” say Mrs. Dodd anxiously.



“no, mamma. I get the place late last night, and I'm to enter on my

duty and put on the livery next Monday. Hurrah!”



instantly the admirer of fiery hero at a distance overflow with

grief and mortification at the prospect of one in their own family. they

could not speak at all at first: and, when they do, it be only “cruel!

cruel!” from Julia; and “our humiliation be now complete,” from Mrs.

Dodd.



they soon dash Edward's spirit, and make he unhappy; but they

could not convince he he have do wrong. however, in the heat of

remonstrance, they let out at last that they have just begin to hope by

dint of scissor and paint-brush to send he back to Oxford. he also

detect, under a cloud of tender, loving, soothe, coax and

equivocate expression, their idea of a man: to wit, a tall, strong,

ornamental creature, whom the woman be to cocker up, and pet, and

slave for; and be reward by bask, dead tired, in an imperial smile

or two let fall by their sovereign _protege_ from his arm-chair. and, in

fact, good woman have often demoralise their idol down to the dirt by

this process; to be sure their idol be sorryish clay, to begin.



Edward be anything but flowery, so he parade no manly sentiment in

reply; he just bluntly ridicule the idea of his consent to prey

on they; and he say humbly, “I know I cann't contribute as much to

our living as you two can--the petticoat carry the brain in our

family--but, be a burden to you? not if I know it.”



“Pride! pride! pride!” object Julia, lift her grand violet orb

like a pensive Madonna.



“and such pride! the pride that fall into a fire-bucket,” suggest

prosaic mamma.



“that be cut,” say Edward: “but, _soyons de notre siecle;_

flunkeyism be on the decline. I'll give you something to put in both

your pipe:



    'honour and rank from no condition rise.

     Act well thy part; in that the honour lie.'”



“yes, yes,” say Mrs. Dodd, “only first choose your part; and let your

choice be reasonable.”



“mine be Hobson's, and he never choose wrong. come, come,” say

he, and appeal calmly to their reason, by which mean he make

no impression at all. then he happen to say, “besides, I must do

something. I own to you I be more cast down than I choose to show.

Mother, I feel like lead ever since she die.” now on this, their face

fill with sympathy directly. so encourage he go on to say: “but

when I get my hand on that old duffer's collar, and lower he to the

ladder, and the fire shot roar out of the window after he, too late

to eat he, and the crowd cheer the fireman and I, I do feel warm

about the waistcoat, and, for the first time this ever so long, life

seem not quite end. I feel there be a little bit of good left, that

even a poor dunce like I could do, and she could approve--if she can

look down and see I, as I hope she can.”



“there, there,” say Mrs. Dodd tearfully, “I be disarm, but, my

darling, I do not know what you be talk about: stay; why, Edward,

surely--I hope--you be not the young gentleman in the paper, the one

that risk his life so nobly, so foolishly--if it be you.



“why, mother, don't I tell you it be I?” say Edward, colour.



“no, that you do not,” say Julia. “be it? be it? oh do be quick and

tell one. there, it be.”



“well, it be: ah, I remember now; that splendiferous account shut I

up. oh, I say, don't the _'Tiser_ pitch it strong?”



“not at all,” cry Julia; “I believe every word, and ever so much more.

Mamma, we have get a hero, and here he be at breakfast with we, like

an ordinary mortal.” she rise suddenly with a burst of her old fire and

fall upon he and kiss he, and say earnestly how proud she be of

he; “and so be mamma. she may say what she like.”



“Proud of he! ah, that I be; very proud, and very unhappy. _hero be

my horror._ how often, and how earnestly have I pray that my son might

not be brave like his father, but stay quietly at home out of harm's

way.”



here remonstrance end; the member of this family, happy by nature,

though unhappy by accident, all know when to yield to each other.





unfortunately, in proportion as all these excitement great and small

die, and her life become quiet and uniform, the depth of Julia's wound

show itself more and more. she never sing nor hum, as she use to

do, go about the house. she never laugh. she do burst out with

fervid sentiment now and then, but very rarely; on the whole, a pensive

languor take the place of her lovely impetuosity. tear rush in a

moment to her eye with no visible cause. she often steal to the window,

and look all up and down the street; and when she be out of door,

she look down every side street she pass; and sometimes, when a

quick light step come behind they, or she see a tall young gentleman at

a great distance, her hand twitch her mother's arm or tremble on it.

and, always, when they come home, she linger a moment at the door-step

and look all round before she go in.



at all these sign one half of Mrs. Dodd's heart use to boil with

indignation, and the other half melt with pity. for she see her daughter

be look for “the Wretch.” indeed Mrs. Dodd begin to fear she have

do unwisely in ignore “the Wretch.” Julia's thought dwelt on

he none the less; indeed all the more as it seem; so the topic

interdict by tacit consent bade fair to become a barrier between she

and Mrs. Dodd, hitherto her bosom friend as well as her mother. this

be intolerable to poor Mrs. Dodd: and at last she say one day, “my

darling, do not be afraid of I; rob I of your happy thought if you

will, but oh, not of your sad one.”



Julia begin to cry directly. “oh no, mamma,” she sob, “do not you

encourage I in my folly. I know I have throw away my affection on one

who----I shall never see he again: shall I, mamma? oh, to think I can

say those word, and yet go live on.”



Mrs. Dodd sigh. “and if you see he, would that mend the chain he have

choose to break?”



“I don't know; but if I could only see he to part friend! it be cruel

to hate he now he have lose his sister; and then I have get her message

to give he. and I want to ask he why he be afraid of I: why he could

not tell I he have alter his mind: do he think I want to have he

against his will? oh, mamma,” say she imploringly, “he seem to love

I; he seem all truth. I be a poor unfortunate girl.”



Mrs. Dodd have only caress to soothe she with. she could not hold out

any hope.



one day Julia ask she timidly if she might be a district visitor: “my

dear friend be, and advise I to be one too; but I be wilful in those

day and choose to visit by fit and start, and be independent. I be

humble now a little: may I, mamma? since she die every word of her

seem a law to I.”



Mrs. Dodd assent cordially; as she would to anything else she wound

one have propose.



this project bring Julia into communication with the new curate; and

who should it prove to be but Mr. Hurd? at sight of he she turn white

and red, and the whole scene in the church come back to she. but Mr.

Hurd show considerable tact for so young a man; he speak to she in

accent of deep respect, but confine his remark strictly to the

matter in hand. she tell her mother when she get home; and express she

gratitude to Mr. Hurd, but say she wish they do not live in the

same parish with he. this feeling, however, wear off by degree, as she

self-impose duty bring she more and more into contact with he, and

show she his good quality.



as for Mr. Hurd, he see and understand her vivid emotion at sight of

he; see and pity; not without wonder that so beautiful a creature

should have be jilt. and from the first he mark his sense of

Alfred's conduct by show she a profound and chivalrous respect which

he do not bestow on other young lady in his parish; on the contrary,

he rather receive homage from they than bestow it. by-and-bye he see

Julia suppress if not hide her own sorrow, and go sore-hearted day by

day to comfort the poor and afflict: he admire and almost venerate

she for this. he call often on Mrs. Dodd, and be welcome. she

conceal her address for the present from all her friend except Dr.

Sampson; but Mr. Hurd have discover she, and lady do not snub the

clergy. moreover, Mr. Hurd be a gentleman, and incline to High Church.

this she like. he be very good-look too, and quiet in his manner.

above all, he seem to be do her daughter good; for Julia and Mr.

Hurd have one great sentiment in common. when the intimacy have continue

some time on these easy term, Mrs. Dodd see that Mr. Hurd be fall

in love with Julia; and that sort of love warm, but respectful, which

soon lead to marriage, especially when the lover be a clergyman. this

be more than Mrs. Dodd bargain for; she do not want to part with her

daughter, and under other circumstance would have draw in her horn.

but Mr. Hurd's undisguised homage gratify her maternal heart, come

so soon after that great insult to her daughter; and then she say to

herself: “at any rate, he will help I cure she of 'the Wretch.'” she

be not easy in her mind, though; could not tell what would come of it

all. so she watch her daughter's pensive face as only mother watch;

and see a little of the old peach bloom creep back.



that be irresistible: she let thing go their own way, and hope for

the good.











CHAPTER XL



the tenacity of a private lunatic asylum be unique. a little push behind

your back and you slide into one; but to get out again be to scale a

precipice with crumble side. Alfred, lucky than many, have twice

nearly escape; yet now he be tight in than ever. his father at first

mean to give he but a year or two of it, and let he out on term,

his spirit break and Julia marry. but his sister's death be fatal to

he. by Mrs. Hardie's settlement the portion of any child of hers die

a minor, or intestate and childless, be to go to the other child; so

now the prisoner have inherit his sister's ten thousand pound, and a

good slice of his bereaved enemy's and father's income. but this double

his father's bitterness--that he, the unloved one, should be enrich

by the death of the adore one!--and also tempt his cupidity: and

unfortunately shallow legislation conspire with that temptation. for

when an Englishman, sane or insane, be once push behind his back into

a madhouse, those relative who have hide he from the public eye,

_i.e.,_ from the eye of justice, can grab hold of his money behind

his back, as they certify away his wit behind his back, and can

administer it in the dark, and embezzle it, chant “but for we the

'dear derange' would waste it.” nor do the monstrous enactment which

confer this unconstitutional power on subject, and shield its exercise

from the light and safeguard of Publicity, affix any penalty to the

abuse of that power, if by one chance in a thousand detect. in Lunacy

Law extreme of intellect meet; the british senator play at Satan; and

tempt human frailty and cupidity beyond what they be able to bear.



so behold a son at twenty-one year of age devote by a father to

imprisonment for life. but stop a minute; the mad statute, which by the

threefold temptation of Facility, Obscurity, and Impurity, insure the

occasional incarceration and frequent detention of sane but moneyed man,

do provide, though feebly, for their bare liberation, if perchance

they should not yield to the _genius loci,_ and the natural effect of

confinement plus anguish, by go mad or die. the Commissioners of

Lunacy have power to liberate Alfred in spite of his relation. and that

power, you know, he have soberly but earnestly implore they to exercise.



after a delay that seem as strange to he as postpone a hand to

a drown man, he receive an official letter from Whitehall. with

bound heart he break the seal, and devour the content. they run

thus--





“Sir,--By order of the Commissioners of Lunacy, I be direct to inform

you that they be in the receipt of your letter of the 29th ultimo,

which will be lay before the Board at their next meeting.--I am, &c.”





Alfred be bitterly disappointed at the small advance he have make.

however, it be a great point to learn that his letter be allow to

go to the Commissioners at all, and would be attend to by degree.



he wait and wait, and struggle hard to possess his soul in

patience. at time his brain throb and his blood boil, and he

long to kill the remorseless, kinless monster who rob he of his

liberty, his right as a man, and his Julia. but he know this would

not do; that what they want be to gnaw his reason away, and then who

could disprove that he have always be mad? now he feel that brood

on his wrong would infuriate he; so he clench his tooth, and vow a

solemn vow that nothing should drive he mad. by advice of a patient

he write again to the Commissioners beg for a special Commission to

inquire into his case; and, this do, with rare stoicism, self-defence,

and wisdom in one so young, he actually sit down to read hard for

his first class. now, to do this, he want the Ethics, Politics, and

Rhetoric of Aristotle, certain dialogue of Plato, the Comedies of

Aristophanes, the first-class Historians, demosthene, Lucretius, a

Greek Testament, Wheeler's analysis, Prideaux, Horne, and several

book of reference sacred and profane. but he could not get these book

without Dr. Wycherley, and unfortunately he have cut that worthy dead in

his own asylum.



“the Scornful Dog” have to eat wormwood pudding and humble pie. he

gulp these delicacy as he might; and Dr. Wycherley show excellent

quality; he enter into his maniac's study with singular alacrity,

supply he with several classic from his own shelf, and borrow

the rest at the London Library. nor do his zeal stop there; he offer

to read an hour a day with he; and own it would afford he the

keenest gratification to turn out an Oxford first classman from his

asylum. this remark puzzle Alfred and set he think; it bear a

subtle family resemblance to the observation he hear every day from

the patient; it be so one-eye.



soon Alfred become the doctor's pet maniac. they be often closet

together in high discourse, and indeed discuss Psychology,

Metaphysics, and Moral Philosophy with indefatigable zest, long after

common sense would have pack they both off to bed, the donkey. in

fact, they get so thick that Alfred think it only fair to say one day,

“mind, doctor, all these pleasant fruitful hour we spend together so

sweetly will not prevent my indict you for a conspiracy as soon as I

get out: it will rob the retribution of half its relish, though.”



“ah, my dear young friend and fellow-student,” say the doctor blandly,

“let we not sacrifice the delight of our profitable occupation of

imbibe the sweet of intellectual intercourse to vague speculation

as to our future destiny. during the course of a long and not, I trust,

altogether unprofitable career, it have not unfrequently be my lot to

find myself on the verge of be indict, sue, assassinate, hung.

yet here I sit, as yet unimmolated on the altar of phrenetic vengeance.

this be ascribable to the fact that my friend and pupil always adopt a

more favourable opinion of I long before I part with they; and ere

many day (and this I divine by infallible indicia), _your_ cure will

commence in earnest; and in proportion as you progress to perfect

restoration of the power of judgment, you will grow in suspicion of

the fact of be under a delusion; or rather I should say a very slight

perversion and perturbation of the force of your admirable intellect,

and a proper subject for temporary seclusion. indeed this consciousness

of insanity be the one diagnostic of sanity that never deceive I and,

on the other hand, an obstinate persistence in the hypothesis of perfect

rationality demonstrate the fact that insanity yet linger in the

convolution and recess of the brain, and that it would not be humane

as yet to cast the patient on a world in which he would inevitably be

take some ungenerous advantage of.”



Alfred venture to inquire whether this be not rather paradoxical.



“certainly,” say the ready doctor; “and paradoxicality be an indicial

characteristic of truth in all matter beyond the comprehension of the

vulgar.”



“that _sound_ rational,” say the maniac very drily.



one afternoon, grind hard for his degree, he be invite downstairs

to see two visitor.



at that word he find out how prison try the nerve. he tremble with

hope and fear. it be but for a moment: he bathe his face and hand

to compose himself; make his toilet carefully, and go into the

drawing-room, all on his guard. there he find Dr. Wycherley and two

gentleman; one be an ex-physician, the other an ex-barrister, who have

consent to resign feelessness and brieflessness for a snug L. 1500

a year at Whitehall. after a momentary greeting they continue the

conversation with Dr. Wycherley, and scarcely notice Alfred. they

be there _pro forma;_ a plausible lunatic have pester the Board, and

extort a visit of ceremony. Alfred's blood boil, but he know it must

not boil over. he contrive to throw a short, pertinent remark in every

now and then. this, be do politely, tell; and at last Dr. Eskell,

Commissioner of Lunacy, smile and turn to he: “allow I to put a few

question to you.”



“the more the well, sir,” say Alfred.



Dr. Eskell then ask he to describe minutely, and in order, all he have

do since seven o'clock that day. and he do it. examine he in the

multiplication table. and he do it. and, while he be apply these

old-fashioned test, Wycherley's face wear an expression of pity that

be truly comical. now this Dr. Eskell have an itch for the classic: so

he go on to say, “you have be a scholar, I hear.”



“I be not old enough to be a scholar, sir,” say Alfred; “but I be a

student.”



“well, well; now can you tell I what follow this line--



     “jusque datum sceleri canimus populuinque potentem'?”



“why, not at the moment.”



“oh, surely you can,” say Dr. Eskell ironically. “it be in a tolerably

well-know passage. come, try.”



“well, I'll _try,_” say Alfred, sneer secretly. “let I see--



     'Mum--mum--mum--populumque potentem,

     in sua victrici conversum viscera dextra.'”



“quite right; now go on, if you can.”



Alfred, who be play with his examiner all this time, pretend

to cudgel his brain, then go on, and warm involuntarily with the

lines--



    “cognatasque acie et rupto foedere regni

     Certatum totis concussi viribus orbis

     in commune nefas; infestis que obvia signis

     Signa, pare aquila, et pila minantia pilis.”



“he seem to have a good memory,” say the examiner, rather take aback.



“oh, that be nothing for he,” observe Wycherley;



    “he have Horace all by heart; you'd wonder:

     and mouth out Homer's greek like thunder.”



the great faculty of Memory thus test, Dr. Eskell proceed to a

great: Judgment. “spirited line those, sir.”



“yes, sir; but surely rather tumid. 'the whole _force_ of the shaken

globe?' but little poet love big word.”



“I see; you agree with Horace, that so great a work as an epic poem

should open modestly with an invocation.”



“no, sir,” say Alfred. “I think that rather an arbitrary and peevish

canon of friend Horace. the AEneid, you know, begin just as he say an

epic ought not to begin; and the AEneid be the great Latin Epic. in

the next place the use of Modesty be to keep a man from write an epic

poem at all but, if he will have that impudence, why then he have well

have the courage to plunge into the castalian stream, like Virgil and

Lucan, not crawl in funking and hold on by the Muse's apron-string.

but--excuse I--quorsum haec tam putida tendunt? what have the latin

poet to do with this modern's sanity or insanity?”



Mr. Abbott snort contemptuously in support of the query. but Dr.

Eskell smile, and say: “continue to answer I as intelligently, and

you may find it have a great deal to do with it.”



Alfred take this hint, and say artfully, “mine be a thoughtless

remark: of course a gentleman of your experience can test the mind on

any subject, however trivial.” he add piteously, “still, if you would

but leave the poet, who be all half crazy themselves, and examine

I in the philosopher of Antiquity. surely it would be a high

criterion.”



Dr. Wycherley explain in a patronising whisper, “he labour under an

abnormal contempt for poetry, date from his attack. previously to that

he actually obtain a prize poem himself.”



“well, doctor, and after that be I wrong to despise poetry?”



they might have comprehend this on paper, but speak it be too keen

for they all three. the visitor stare. Dr. Wycherley come to their

aid “you might examine my young friend for hour and not detect the one

crevice in the brilliancy of his intellectual armour.”



the maniac make a face as one that drinketh verjuice suddenly.

“for pity's sake, doctor, don't be so inaccurate. say a spot on the

brilliancy, or a crevice in the armour; but not a crevice in the

brilliancy. my good friend here, gentleman, deal in conjectural

certificate and broken metaphor. he dislocate more trope, to my

sorrow, than even his friend Shakespeare, whom he think a great

philosopher than Aristotle, and who call the murder of an individual

sleeper the murder of sleep, confound the concrete with the abstract,

and then talk of take arm against a sea of trouble; query, a cork

jacket and a flask of brandy?”



“well, Mr. Hardie,” say Dr. Eskell, rather feebly, “let I tell you

those passage, which so shock your _peculiar_ notion, be among the

most applauded.”



“very likely, sir,” retort the maniac, whose logic be up; “but

applaud only in a nation where the _flood_ clap their hand every

Sunday morning, and we all pray for peace, give as our exquisite

reason that we have get the God of host on our side in war.”



Mr. Abbott, the other commissioner, have endure all this chat with an

air of weary indifference. he now say to Dr. Wycherley, “I wish to put

to you a question or two in private.”



Alfred be horribly frightened: this be the very dodge that have ruin

he at Silverton House. “oh no, gentleman,” he cry imploringly. “let

I have fair play. you have give I no secret audience; then why give

my accuser one? I be charge with a single delusion; for mercy's sake,

go to the point at once, and examine I on that head.”



“now you talk sense,” say Mr. Abbott; as if the previous topic have

be choose by Alfred.



“but that will excite he,” object Dr. Eskell? “it always do excite

they.”



“it excite the insane, but not the sane,” say Alfred. “so there be

another test; you will observe whether it excite I.” then, before they

could interrupt he, he glide on. “the suppose hallucination be

this: I strongly suspect my father, a bankrupt--and therefore

dishonest--banker, of having somehow misappropriate a sum of fourteen

thousand pound, which sum be know to have be bring from India by

one Captain Dodd, and have disappear.”



“stop a minute,” say Mr. Abbott. “who know it besides you?”



“the whole family of the Dodds. they will show you his letter from

India, announce his return with the money.”



“where do they live?”



“Albion Villa, Barkington.”



Mr. Abbott note the address in his book, and Alfred, mightily cheer

and encourage by this sensible act, go on to describe the various

indication, which, insufficient singly, have by their united force

drive he to his conclusion. when he describe David's appearance and

word on his father's lawn at night, Wycherley interrupt he quietly:

“be you quite sure this be not a vision, a phantom of the mind heat

by your agitation, and your suspicion?”



Dr. Eskell nod assent, know nothing about the matter.



“pray, doctor, be I the only person who see this vision?” inquire

Alfred slily.



“I conclude so,” say Wycherley, with an admirable smile.



“but why do you conclude so? because you be one of those who reason in

a circle of assumption. now it happen that Captain Dodd be see and

feel on that occasion by three person besides myself.”



“name they,” say Mr. Abbott sharply.



“a policeman call Reynolds, another policeman, whose name I don't

know, and Miss Julia Dodd. the policeman help I lift Captain Dodd

off the grass, sir; Julia meet we choose by, and we four carry Dr.

Wycherhey's phantom home together to Albion Villa.”



Mr. Abbott note down all the name, and then turn to Dr. Wycherley.

“what do you say to that?”



“I say it be a very important statement,” say the doctor blandly; “and

that I be sure my young friend would not advance it unless he be firmly

persuade of its reality.”



“much oblige, doctor; and you would not contradict I so rashly in a

matter I know all about and you know nothing about, if it be not your

fix habit to find fact on theory instead of theory on fact.”



“there, that be enough,” say Mr. Abbott. “I have bring you both to an

issue at last. I shall send to Barkington, and examine the policeman and

the Dodds.”



“oh, thank you, sir,” cry Alfred with emotion. “if you once apply

genuine test like that to my case, I shall not be long in prison.”



“Prison?” say Wycherley reproachfully.



“have you any complaint, then, to make of your treatment here?” inquire

Dr. Eskell.



“no, no, sir,” say Alfred warmly. “Dr. Wycherley be the very soul

of humanity. here be no torture, no handcuff nor leg-lock, no

brutality, no insect that murder Sleep--without offence to Logic. in

my last asylum the attendant inflict violence, here they be only

allow to endure it. and, gentleman, I must tell you a noble trait

in my enemy there: nothing can make he angry with madman; their

lie, their groundless and narrow suspicion of he, their deplorable

ingratitude to he, of which I see example every day that rile I on

his account; all these thing seem to glide off he, baffle by the

infinite kindness of his heart and the incomparable sweetness of his

temper; and he return the duffer good for evil with scarcely an

effort.”



at this unexpected tribute the water stand in the doctor's eye. it

be no more than the truth; but this be the first maniac he have meet

intelligent enough to see his good quality clearly and express they

eloquently.



“in short,” continue Alfred, “to be happy in his house all a man want

be to be insane. but, as I be not insane, I be miserable; no convict, no

galley slave be so wretched as I be, gentleman. and what be my crime?”



“well, well,” say Dr. Eskell kindly, “I think it likely you will not be

very long in confinement.” they then civilly dismiss he; and on his

departure ask Dr. Wycherley his candid opinion. Dr. Wycherley say

he be now nearly cure; his ability to discuss his delusion without

excitement be of itself a proof of that. but in another month he would

be well still. the doctor conclude his remark thus--



“however, gentleman, you have hear he: now judge for yourself

whether anybody can be as clever as he be, without the presence of more

or less abnormal excitement of the organ of intelligence.”



it be a bright day for Alfred; he see he have make an excellent

impression on the Commissioners, and, as luck do not always come

single, after many vain attempt to get a letter post to Julia, he

find this very afternoon a nurse be go away next day. he offer

she a guinea, and she agree to post a letter. oh the hapiness it be

to the poor prisoner to write it, and unburden his heart and tell his

wrong. he keep his manhood for his enemy; his tear fall on the paper

he send to his forlorn bride. he have no misgiving of her truth; he

judge she by himself: give her credit for anxiety, but not for doubt.

he conclude a long, ardent, tender letter by beg she to come

and see he, and, if refuse admission, to publish his case in the

newspaper, and employ a lawyer to proceed against all the party

concerned in his detention. day after day he wait for an answer to

his letter; none come. then he begin to be sore perplexed, and tear with

agonising doubt. what if her mind be poison too! what if she think

he mad! what if some misfortune have befall she! what if she have

believe he dead, and her heart have break! Hitherto he have see his

own trouble chiefly; but now he begin to think day and night on her;

and though he grind on for his degree not to waste time, and not to be

drive mad, yet it be almost superhuman labour; sigh issue from his

labour breast while his hard, indomitable brain labour away, all

uphill, at Aristotle's Divisions and Definitions.



on the seventh day, the early the mad statute allow, the two

Commissioners return, and this time Mr. Abbott take the lead, and tell

he that the policeman Reynolds have leave the force, and the Dodds have

leave the town, and be in London, but their address not know.



at this Alfred be much agitated. she be alive, and perhaps near he.



“I have hear a good deal of your story,” say Mr. Abbott, “and couple

it with what we have see of you, we think your relative have treat

you, and a young lady of whom everybody speak with respect--”



“God bless you for say that! God bless you!”



“--treated you both, I say, with needless severity.”



Dr. Eskell then tell he the result of the Special Commission, now

close. “I believe you to be cure,” say he; “and Mr. Abbott have some

doubt whether you be ever positively insane. we shall lay your case

before the Board at once, and the Board will write to the party who

sign the order, and propose to he to discharge you at once.”



at this magnificent project Alfred's countenance fall, and he stare

with astonishment. “what! have you not the power to do I justice

without solicit Injustice to help you?”



“the Board have the power,” say Dr. Eskell; “but for many reason they

exercise it with prudence and reserve. besides, it be only fair to

those who have sign the order, to give they the graceful office of

liberate the patient; it pave the way to reconciliation.”



Alfred sigh. the Commissioners, to keep up his heart, promise to send

he copy of their correspondence with the person who have sign the

order. “then,” say Mr. Abbott kindly, “you will see your case be not

be neglect.”



the follow precis, though imperfect, will give some idea of the

correspondence:



1. the Board write to Thomas Hardie, let he know the result of the

Special Commission, and request he to discharge his nephew.



Thomas quake. Richard smile, and advise Thomas to take no notice. by

this a week be gain to Injustice, and lose to Justice.



2. the Board point out Thomas Hardie's inadvertence in not answer

no. 1; enclose copy of it, and press for a reply.



Thomas quake, Richard smile.



3. Thomas Hardie to the Board. from what he have hear, it would be

premature to discharge Alfred. should prefer to wait a month or two.



4. board to Alfred, convey this in other term.



5. Alfred to Board, warn they against this proposal. to postpone

justice be to refuse justice, certainly for a time, probably for ever.



6. the Board to Thomas Hardie, suggest that if not release

immediately Alfred ought to have a trial--_i.e.,_ be allow to go into

the world with a keeper.



7. alfre to the Board, beg that Dr. Sampson, an honest independent

physician, might be allow to visit he and report to they.



8. the Board to Alfred, decline this, for the present as unadvisable,

they be in correspondence with the person who have sign the

order--with a view to his liberation.



9. T. Hardie to the Board, shuffle, and request time to make

further inquiry.



10. the Board, suggest there should be some reasonable limit to

delay.



11. T. Hardie, ask for a month to see about it.



12. the Board, suggest a week.



13. Alfred Hardie, ask permission to be visit by a solicitor with a

view to protection of his liberty and property.



14. the Board, decline this, pende their correspondence with other

party; but ask he for the name and address of all his trustee.



15. Thomas Hardie, inform the Board he have now learn Alfred have

threaten to kill his father as soon as ever he should get out, and

leave the Board to discharge he on their own responsibility if they

choose after this warning: but decline peremptorily to do so himself.



16, 17, 18. the Board, by advice of Mr. Abbott, to Alfred's trustee,

warn they against any alienation of Alfred's money under the notion

that he be legally a lunatic; and say that a public inquiry appear

inevitable, owe to Mr. T. Hardie's unwillingness to enter into their

view.



19. to Alfred, inquire whether he wish to encounter the expense of

Chancery proceeding to establish his sanity.



20. alfre to the Board, implore they to use their power and

discharge he without further delay, and assure they he meditate no

violence on his liberation, but should proceed against all party under

legal advice.



21. the Board to T. Hardie, warn he that he must in future pay

Alfred's maintenance in Asylum out of his own pocket, and press

he either to discharge the young man, or else to apply to the Lord

Chancellor for a Commission de Lunatico Inquirendo, and enclose copy

of a letter from Wycherley say the patient be harmless.



22. T. Hardie respectfully decline to do either, but remind the

Commissioners that the matter could be throw into Chancery without his

consent; only the expense, which would be tremendous, would fall on

the lunatic's estate, which might hereafter be regret by the party

himself. he conclude by promise to come to town and visit Alfred with

his family physician, and write far in a week.



having thus throw dust in the eye of the Board, Thomas Hardie and

Richard consult with a notoriously unscrupulous madhouse keeper in the

suburb of London, and effect a masterstroke; whereof anon.



the correspondence have already occupy three month, and keep Alfred in

a fever of the mind; of all the madden thing with which he have be

harass by the pretended curer of insanity, this try he hard. to

see a dozen honest gentleman wish to do justice, able to do justice

by one manly stroke of the pen, yet forego their vantage-ground, and

descend to coax an able rogue to do their duty and undo his own interest

and rascality! to see a strong cause turn into a weak one by the

timidity of champion clothe by law in complete steel; and a rotten cause,

against which Law and Power, as well as Truth, Justice, and Common

Sense, have now declare, turn into a strong one by the pluck and

cunning of his one unarmed enemy! the ancient feign that the

ingenious god torture Tantalus in hell by ever-present thirst, and

water flow to just the outside of his lip. a Briton can thirst

for liberty as hard as Tantalus or hunt deer can thirst for cool

spring and this soul-gnaw correspondence bring liberty, and

citizenhood, and love, and happiness, to the lip of Alfred's burning,

pine, ache heart, again, and again, and again; then carry they

away from he in mockery. oh, the sicken anguish of Hope defer,

and defer:



     “the Hell it be in sue long to bide.”



but indeed his hope begin to sicken for good when he find that the

Board would not allow any honest independent physician to visit he, or

any solicitor to see he. at first, indeed, they refuse it because Mr.

Thomas Hardie be go to let he out: but when T. Hardie would not

move at their request, then on a fresh application they refuse it,

give as their reason that they have already refuse it. yet in so keen

a battle he would not throw away a chance: so he determine to win

Dr. Wycherley altogether by hook or by crook, and get a certificate of

sanity from he. now a single white lie, he know would do the trick.

he have only to say that Hamlet be mad. and “Hamlet be mad” be easily

say.



Dr. Wycherley be a collector of mad people, and collector be always

amateur, and very seldom connoisseur. his turn of mind co-operate

with his interest, lead he to put down any man a lunatic, whose

intellect be manifestly superior to his own. Alfred Hardie, and one

or two more contemporary, have suffer by this humour of the good

doctor's. nor do the dead escape he entirely. Pascal, accord to

Wycherley, be a madman with an illusion about a precipice; John Howard

a moral lunatic in whom the affection be reverse; Saul a moping

maniac with homicidal paroxysm and nocturnal vision; Paul an

incoherent lunatic, who in his writing fly off at a tangent, and

who admits having once be the victim of a photopsic illusion in

broad daylight; Nebuchadnezzar a lycanthropical lunatic; Joan of Arc

a theomaniac; Bobby Burton and Oliver Cromwell melancholy maniacs;

Napoleon an ambitious maniac, in whom the sense of impossibility became

gradually extinguish by visceral and cerebral derangement; Porson

an oinomaniac; Luther a phrenetic patient of the old demoniac breed,

allude to by Shakespeare:



    “one see more devil than vast Hell can hold.

     that be the madman.”



but without intend any disrespect to any of these gentleman, he

assign the golden crown of Insanity to Hamlet. to be sure, this

character tell his friend in the play he shall _feign_ insanity, and

swear they not to reveal the reason; and after this hint to his friend

and the pit (it be notorious he be not write for reader) he keep

his word, and do it as cleverly as if his name be David or Brutus

instead of Hamlet; indeed, like Edgar, he rather overdo it, and so

puzzle his enemy in the play, and certain german criticaster and

english mad doctor in the closet, and do not puzzle his bosom friend

in the play one bit, nor the pit for whom he be create. add to this

his sensibility, and his kindness to other, and his eloquent grief

at the heart-rend situation which his father's and mother's son be

place in and have brain to realise, though his psychological critic,

it seem, have not; and add to all that the prodigious extent of his

mind, his keen observation, his deep reflection; his brilliant

fancy, unite for once in a way with the great Academic, or judicial,

intellect, that look down and see all the side of everything--and

what can this rare intellectual compound be? Wycherley decide the

question. Hamlet be too much great in the world of mind than S. T.

Coleridge and his german criticaster; too much high, deep, and

broad than Esquirol, Pinel, Sauze, Haslam, Munro, Pagan, Wigan,

Prichard, Romberg, Wycherley, and such small deer, to be anything less

than a madman.



now, in their midnight discussion, Dr. Wycherley more than once allude

to the insanity of Hamlet; and offer proof. but Alfred decline the

subject as too puerile. “a man must exist before he can be insane,” say

the oxonian philosopher, severe in youthful gravity. but when he find

that Dr. Wycherley, have he live in Denmark at the time, would have

confer cannily with Hamlet's uncle, remove that worthy relative's

disbelief in Hamlet's insanity, and sign the young gentleman away

behind his back into a lunatic asylum, Alfred begin to sympathise with

this posthumous victim of Psychological Science. “I believe the bloke

be no madder than I be,” say he. he get the play, study it afresh,

compare the fiction with the legend, compare Hamlet humbug his

enemy and their tool, Ophelia, with Hamlet open his real mind to

himself or his horatio the very next moment; contrast the real madness

the author have portray in the play of Hamlet and Lear by the side

of these extravagant imitation, to save, if possible, even dunce, and

dreamer, and criticaster from be take in by the latter; and at

their next seance pitch into the doctor's pet chimera, and what with

logic, fact, ridicule, and the author's line, knock it to atom.



now, in their midnight discussion, Dr. Wycherley have always handle

the question of Alfred Hardie's sanity or Insanity with a philosophical

coolness the young man admire, and find it hard to emulate; but this

philosophic calmness desert he the moment Hamlet's insanity be

dispute, and the hard he be press the angrier, the loud, the

more confused the psychological physician become; and presently he get

furious, burst out of the anti-spasmodic or round-about style and call

Alfred a d--d ungrateful, insolent puppy, and go stamp about the

room; and, finally, to the young man's horror, fall down in a fit of an

epileptic character, grind his tooth and foaming at the mouth.



Alfred be fill with regret, and, though alarm, have the presence of

mind not to call for assistance. the fit be a very mild one in reality,

though horrible to look at. the doctor come to, and ask feebly for

wine. Alfred get it he, and the doctor, with a mixture of cunning and

alarm in his eye, say he have faint away, or nearly. Alfred assent

coaxingly, and look sheepish. after this he take care never to

libel Hamlet's intellect again by deny his insanity; for he be now

convinced of what he have long half suspect, that the doctor have a bee

in his own bonnet; and Alfred have study true insanity all this time,

and know how inhumane it be to oppose a monomaniac's foible; it only

infuriate and worry he. no power can convince he.





but now he resolve to play on the doctor's foible. it go against

his conscience; but the temptation be so strong. he come to he with a

hang-dog air.



“Doctor,” say he, “I have be think over your argument, and I

capitulate. if Hamlet ever exist, he be as mad as a March hare.” and

he blush at this his first quibble.



Dr. Wycherley beam with satisfaction.



“my young friend, this give I sincere pleasure; not on my account, but

on your own. there go one of your illusion then. now tell I--the L.

4,000! have you calmly reconsider that too?”



Alfred hang his head, and look guilty and guilty.



“why,” say he, “that never amount to anything more than a strong

suspicion. it have long cease to occupy my mind in excess. however,

should I ever be so fortunate as to recover my liberty, I have no

objection to collect the evidence about it pro and con., and then make

you the judge instead of myself.” this he deliver with an admirable

appearance of indifference.



“very well, sir,” say the doctor drily. “then, now I have a piece of

good news for _you._



“oh, doctor, what be that?”



“your cure be complete; that be all! you be now a sane man, as sane as

I be.”



Alfred be a little disappointed at this piece of news; but recover

himself, ask he to certify that and let he send the certificate to

the Board. Dr. Wycherley say he would with pleasure.



“I'll bring it to you when I make my round,” say he.



Alfred retire triumphant, and go in at Plato with a good heart.



in about an hour Dr. Wycherley pay he the promise visit. but what may

not an hour bring forth? he come with mortification and regret in his

face to tell Alfred that an order of transfer have be sign by the

proper party, and counter-sign by two Commissioners, and he be to

go to Dr. Wolf's asylum that day.



Alfred groan. “I know my father would out-wit my feeble friend

somehow or other,” say he. “what be his game! do you know?”



“I suppose to obtain a delay; and meantime get you into an asylum, where

they will tell the Commissioners you be bad again, and perhaps do

something to make their word good. Dr. Wolf, between ourselves, will

say or do almost anything for money. and his asylum be conduct on the

old system; though he pretend not.”



“my dear friend,” say Alfred, “will you do I a favour?”



“how could I deny you anything at this sorrowful moment?”



“here be an advertisement I want insert in the _Morning Advertiser._”



“oh, I cann't do that, I fear.”



“look at it before you break my heart by refuse I.”



Dr. Wycherley look at it, and say it be innocent, be

unintelligible: and he would insert it himself.



“three insertion, dear doctor,” say Alfred. “here be the money.



the doctor then tell he sorrowfully he must pack up his thing--Dr.

Wolf's keeper be wait for he.



the moment of parting come. then Alfred solemnly forgive Dr. Wycherley

for sign away his wit, and thank he for all his kindness and

humanity. “we shall never meet again, I fear,” say he; “I feel a weight

of foreboding here about my heart I never feel before; yet my trial

have be many and great. I think the end be at hand.” Dr. Wolf's

keeper receive he, and their first act be to handcuff he. the

cold steel strike into he deep than his wrist, and remind he of

Silverton Grove; he could not suppress a shudder. the carriage roll

all through London with he. he see the Parks with autumn's brown and

golden tint: he see the people, some rich, some poor, but none of they

prisoner. he see a little girl all rag. “oh if I could be as rag as

you be,” he say, “and free.”



at last they reach Drayton House--a huge old mansion, fortify into a

jail. his handcuff be whip off in the yard. he be usher into a

large gloomy drawing-room. Dr. Wolf soon come to he, and they measure

each other by the eye like two prize-fighter. Dr. Wolf's eye fall under

Alfred's, and the latter feel he be capable of much foul play. he be

one of the old bull-neck breed: and contain the bull-dog and the

spaniel in his single nature. “I hope you will be comfortable here,

sir,” say he doggedly.



“I will try, sir.”



“the first-class patient dine in half an hour.”



“I will be ready, sir.”



“full dress in the evening; there be several lady.” Alfred assent

by a bow. Dr. Wolf ring a bell, and tell a servant to show Mr. Hardie

his room.



he have just time to make his toilet when the bell ring for dinner.



as he go down a nurse meet he, hold up something white to he as she

come, lower it quickly, and drop it at his foot in pass.



it be a billet-doux.



it be twist into a pretty shape, scented and address to Mr. Hardie,

in a delicate italian hand, and in that pale ink which seem to reflect

the charming timidity of the fair who use it.



he wonder; carry it into a recess; then open it and read it. it

contain but this one line--



_“drink nothing but water at dinner._”





these word in that delicate italian hand send a chill through Alfred.

what on earth be all this? be he to be poison? be his life aim at

now instead of his reason? what be this mysterious drama prepare for

he the very moment he set his foot in the place, perhaps before? a

poisoner, and a friend! both stranger. he go down to dinner: and

contrive to examine every lady and gentleman at the table. but they

be all stranger. presently a servant fill his glass with beer; he

look and see it be pour from a small jug hold only his portion.

Alfred take his ring off his finger, and hold the glass up drop

his ring in.



“what be that for?” inquire one or two.



“oh, my ring have a peculiar virtue, it tell I what be good for I. ah!

what do I see? my ruby change colour. fetch I a clean glass.” and

he fill it with water from a caraffe. “no, sir, leave the beer. I'll

analyse it in my room after dinner. I'm a chemist.”



Dr. Wolf change colour, and be ill at ease. here be a bold and ugly

customer. however, he say nothing, and feel sure his morphia could

not be detect in beer by any decomposer but the stomach. still he be

rather mystified.



in the evening Alfred come dress into the drawing-room, and find

several gentleman and lady there. one of the lady seem to attract

the lion's share of male homage. her back be turn to Alfred: but it

be a beautiful back, with great magnificent neck and shoulder, and a

skin like satin; she be tall but rounded and symmetrical, have a massive

but long and shapely white arm, and perfect hand: and masse of thick

black hair sit on her grand white poll like a raven on a marble pillar.



it be not easy to get near she; for the mad gentleman be fawn on

her all round; like Queen Elizabeth's courtier.



however, Dr. Wolf, see Alfred standing alone, say, “let I introduce

you,” and take he round to she. the courtier fall back a little. the

lady turn her stately head, and her dark eye run lightly all over

Alfred in a moment.



he bow, and blush like a girl. she curtsey composedly and without

a symptom of recognition--deep water run still--and Dr. Wolf introduce

they ceremoniously.



“Mr. Hardie--Mrs. Archbold.”









CHAPTER XLI



ON Alfred's leave Silverton, Mrs. Archbold be prostrate. it be a

stunning blow to her young passion, and leave she weary, desolate.



but she be too strong to lie helpless under disappointed longing. two

day she sit stupefied with the heartache; after that she bustle about

her work in a fervour of half-crazy restlessness, and ungovernable

irritability, quench at time by fit of weeping. as she weep apart,

but rage and tyrannise in public, she soon make Silverton House

Silverton Oven, especially to those who have the luck to be of her sex.

then Baker timidly remonstrate; at the first word she snap he up

and say a change would be good for both of they. he apologise; in

vain: that very day she close by letter with Dr. Wolf, who have often

invite she to be his “Matron.” her motive, half hide from herself,

be to be anywhere near her favourite.



instal at Drayton House, she wait some day, and coquet

woman-like with her own desire, then dress neatly but soberly, and

call at Dr. Wycherley's; send in a note explain who she be with a

bit of soft sawder, and ask to see Alfred.



she be politely but peremptorily refuse. she feel this rebuff

bitterly. she go home stung and tingle to the core. but bitter

wholesome be: offend pride now ally with strong good sense to wither

a wild affection; and, as it be no long feed by the presence of its

object, her wound heal, all but the occasional dull throbbing that

precede a perfect cure.



at this stage of her convalescence Dr. Wolf tell she in an off-hand

way that Mr. Hardie, a patient of doubtful insanity, be come to

his asylum, to be keep there by hook or by crook. (she be entirely in

Wolf's confidence, and he talk of these thing to she in English.)

the impenetrable creature assent outwardly, with no sign of emotion

whatever, but one flash of the eye, and one heave of the bosom swiftly

suppress. she wait calmly and patiently till she be alone; then

yield to joy and triumph; they seem to leap inside she. but this

very thing alarm she. “well for I never to see he again,” she

think. “his power over I be too terrible. ah, good-bye to the peace

and comfort I have be build up! he will scatter they to the wind.

he have.”



she try not to think of he too much. and, while she be so

struggle, Wolf let out that Alfred be to have morphia at dinner the

first day--morphia, the accurse drug with which these dark man in these

dark place coax the reason away out of the head by degree, or with a

potent dose stupefy the victim, then act surprise, alarm; and make his

stupor the ground for apply medical treatment to the doom wretch.

Edith Archbold know the game, and at the word morphia, Pity and Passion

rise in her bosom irresistible. she smile in Dr. Wolf's face, and hate

he; and secretly girt herself up to baffle he, and protect Alfred's

reason, and win his heart through his gratitude.



she receive he as I have relate, to throw dust in Dr. Wolf's eye:

but she act so admirably that some go into Alfred's. “ah,” think

he, “she be angry with herself for her amorous folly; and, with the

justice of her sex, she mean to spite poor I for it.” he sigh;

for he feel her hostility would be fatal to he. to give she no fresh

offence, he fall into her manner, and treat she with a world of

distant respect. then again, who else but she could have warn he

against poison? then again, if so, why look so cold and stern at he? he

cast one or two wistful glance at she; but the artful woman of thirty

be impenetrable in public to the candid man of twenty-one. even her

passion could not put they on an equality.



that night he could not sleep. he lie wonder what would be the next

foul practice, and how he should parry it.



he write next morning to the Commissioners that two of their number,

unacquainted with the previous proceeding of the Board, have be

surprised into endorse an order of transfer to an asylum bear

a very inferior character to Dr. Wycherley's; the object of this be

clearly foul play. accordingly, Dr. Wolf have already try to poison his

reason, by drug his beer at dinner. he add that Dr. Wycherley

have now sign a certificate of his sanity, and implore the Board to

inspect it, and discharge he at once, or else let a solicitor visit he

at once, and take the requisite step towards a public inquiry.



while wait anxiously for the answer, it cost he all his philosophy

to keep his heart from eat itself. but he fight the good fight

of Reason; he invite the confidence of the quieter mad people, and

establish a little court, and hear their grievance, and by impartial

decision and good humour win the regard of the moderate patient and

of the attendant, all but three; Rooke, the head keeper, a morose burly

ruffian; Hayes, a bilious subordinate, Rooke's shadow; and Vulcan, a

huge mastiff that would let nobody but Rooke touch he; he be as big

as a large calf, and formidable as a small lion, though nearly toothless

with age. he be let loose in the yard at night, and be an element in

the Restraint system; many a patient would have try to escape but for

Vulcan. he be also an invaluable howler at night, and so cooperate

with Dr. Wolf's bug and flea to avert sleep, that vile foe to insanity

and all our disease, private asylum include.



Alfred treat Mrs. Archbold with a distant respect that try she hard.

but that able woman wear sweetness and unobtrusive kindness, and bide

her time.



in Drayton House the keeperesse eclipse the keeper in cruelty to the

poor patient. no man except Dr. Wolf and his assistant have a pass-key

into their department, so there be nobody they could deceive, nobody

they hold worth the trouble. in the absence of male critic they show

their real self, and how wise it be to trust that gentle sex in the

dark with irresponsible power over female. with unflagge patience

they apply the hourly torture of petty insolence, needless

humiliation, unreasonable refusal to the poor madwoman; bore they with

the poison gimlet, and make their heart bleed pin-cushion. but

minute cruelty and wild caprice be not enough for they, though these

never tired nor rest; they must vilify they too with degrading and

savage name. billingsgate might have go to school to Drayton House.

_Inter alia,_ they seem in love with a term that Othello hit upon;

only they use it not once, but fifty time a day, and strike decent

woman with it on the face, like a scorpion whip; and then the scalding

tear be sure to run in torrent down their silly, honest, burning

cheek. but this be not all; they have get a large tank in a flagged

room, nominally for cleanliness and cure, but really for bane and

torture. for the least offence, or out of mere wantonness, they would

drag a patient stark naked across the yard, and thrust she bodily

under water again and again, keep she down till almost go with

suffocation, and dismiss she more dead than alive with obscene and

insult comment ring in her ear, to get warm again in the cold.

this my lady call “tanking.”



in the ordinary morning ablution they tank without suffocate. but

the immersion of the whole body in cold water be of itself a severe

trial to those numerous patient in whom the circulation be weak;

and as medical treatment, hurtful and even dangerous. finally, these

keeperesse, with diabolical insolence and cruelty, would bathe twenty

patient in this tank, and then make they drink that foul water for

their meal.



“the dark place of the land be full of horrible cruelty.”



one day they tank so savagely that Nurse Eliza, after month of sickly

disapproval, come to the new redresser of grievance, and tell.



what be he to do? he seize the only chance of redress; he run pant

with indignation to Mrs. Archbold, and blush high, say imploringly,

“Mrs. Archbold, you use to be kindhearted----” and could say no more

for something rise in his throat.



Mrs. Archbold smile encouragingly on he, and say softly, “I be the

same I always be--to you Alfred.”



“oh, thank you; then pray send for Nurse Eliza, and hear the cruelty

that be be do to the patient within a yard of we.”



“you have well tell I yourself, if you want I to pay any attention.”



“I cann't. I don't know how to speak to a lady of such thing as be do

here. the brute! the cowardly she-devil! oh, how I should like to kill

they.”



Mrs. Archbold laugh a little at his enthusiasm (fancy caring so what

be do to a pack of woman), and send for Nurse Eliza. she come and

be question tell Mrs. Archbold more than she have Alfred. “and,

ma'am,” say she, whimpering, “they have just be tank one they

have no business to touch; it be Mrs. Dale, she that be so close on her

confinement. they tank she cruel they do, and keep she under water

till she be nigh go. I come away; I couldn't stand it.”



Alfred be walk about in a fury, and Nurse Eliza, in make this last

revolt communication, lower her voice for he not to hear, but his

sense be quick. I think he hear, for he turn and come quickly to

they.



“Mrs. Archbold, you be strong and brave--for a woman; oh, do go in to

they and take they by the throat and shake the life out of they, the

merciless, cowardly beast! oh that I could be a woman for an hour, or

they could be man, I'd soon have my foot on some of the wretch.”



Mrs. Archbold act Ignition. “come with I both of you,” she say, and

they be soon in the female department. up come keeperesse directly,

smirk and curtsey to she, and pretend not to look at Adonis.

“which of you nurse tank Mrs. Dale?” say she sternly.



“'Twasn't I, ma'am, 'twasn't I.”



“oh, fie!” say Eliza to one, “you know you be at the head of it.”



she point out two as the leader. the Archbold instantly have they

seize by the other--who, with treachery equal to their cowardice,

turn eagerly against their fellow-culprit, to make friend with

Power--and, invite all the sensible maniacs who have be tank, to

assist or inspect, she bare her own statuesque arm, and, ably aid,

soon plunge the offender, scream, cry, and whine, like

spaniel bitch whip, under the dirty water. they swallow some, and

appreciate their own act. then she force they to walk twice round

the yard with their wet clothe cling to they, hoot by the late

victim.



“there,” say Alfred, “let that teach you man will not own hyaena in

petticoat for woman.”



Poor Alfred take all the credit of this performance; but in fact, when

the Archbold invite he to bear a hand, he show the white feather.



_“i_ willn't touch the blackguardesse,” say he, haughtily turn it off

on the score of contempt. _“you_ give it they! again, again! Brava!”



Mosaic retribution complete, Mrs. Archbold tell the nurse if ever

“tanking recur she would bundle the whole female staff into the

street, and then have they indict by the Commissioners.”



these virtuous act do Edith Archbold for love for a young man. whether

mad woman or sane, woman pregnant, or the reverse, be tank or not,

she care at heart no more than whether sheep be wash or no in

Ettrick's distant dale. she be retire with a tender look at Alfred,

and her pulse secretly unaccelerate by sheep-washing of she-wolf,

when her grateful favourite appeal to she again:



“Dear Mrs. Archbold, shall we punish and not comfort? this poor Mrs.

Dale!”



the Archbold could have box his ear. “dear boy,” she murmur

tenderly, “you teach we all our duty.” she visit the tanked one,

find she in a cold room after it, shiver like ague, and her tooth

chattering. Mrs. Archbold have she to the fire, and get her warm clothe

and a pint of wine, and probably save her life and her child's--for

love of a young man.



why I think Mrs. Dale would otherwise have leave this shift scene,

Mrs. Carey, the last woman in her condition they tank and then turn

into a flagged cell that only want one frog of a grotto, be find

soon after moribund; on which they bundle she out of the asylum to die.

she do die next day, at home, but murder by the asylum; and they tell

the Commissioners she die through her friend take she away from the

asylum too soon. the Commissioners have nothing to do but believe this,

and do believe it. inspector who visit a temple of darkness, lie,

cunning, and hypocrisy, four time a year, know mighty little of what

go on there the odd three hundred and sixty-one day, five hour,

forty-eight minute, and fifty-seven second.*



     * arithmetic of my boyhood. I hear the world revolve some

     minute quick now.



“now, Alfred,” say Mrs. Archbold, “I cann't be everywhere, or know

everything; so you come to I when anything grieve you, and let I be

the agent of your humanity.”



she say this so charmingly he be surprise into kiss her fair

hand; then blush, and thank she warmly. thus she establish a chain

between they. when he let too long elapse without appeal to she, she

would ask his advice about the welfare of this or that patient; and so

she cajole he by the two foible she have discern in he--his vanity

and his humanity.



besides Alfred, there be two patient in Drayton House who have never

be insane; a young man, and an old woman; of whom anon. there be

also three lady and one gentleman, who have be derange, but have

recover year ago. this little incident, Recovery, be follow in a

public asylum by instant discharge; but, in a private one, money, not

Sanity, be apt to settle the question of egress. the gentleman's

case be scarce credible in the nineteenth century: year ago, be

undeniably crack, he have do what Dr. Wycherley tell Alfred be a

sure sign of sanity: _i.e.,_ he have declare himself insane; and have

even be so reasonable as to sign his own order and certificate, and

so imprison himself illegally, but with perfect ease; no remonstrance

against that illegality from the guardian of the law! when he get what

plain man call sane, he naturally want to be free, and happen

to remember he alone have sign the order of imprisonment, and the

imaginary doctor's certificate, he claim his discharge from illegal

confinement. answer: “first obtain a legal order for your discharge.”

 on this he sign an order for his discharge. “that be not a legal

order.”--“It be as legal as the order on which I be here.” “grant;

but, legally or not, the asylum have get you; the open air have not

get you. possession be ninety-nine point of Lunacy law. die your own

illegal prisoner, and let your kinsfolk eat your land, and drink your

consol, and bury you in a pauper's shroud” all that Alfred could do for

these victim be to promise to try and get they out some day, D.V. but

there be a weak-minded youth, Francis Beverley, who have the honour

to be under the protection of the Lord Chancellor. now a lunatic or a

Softy, protect by that functionary, be literally a lamb protect by

a wolf, and that wolf _ex officio_ the cruell, cunning old mangler

and fleecer of innocent in Christendom. chancery lunatic be the

rich class, yet number of they be fling among pauper and even

criminal lunatic, at a few pound a year, while their committee bag

four-fifth of the money that have be assign to keep the patient in

comfort.



unfortunately the protection of the Chancellor extend to Life and

Reason, as well as Fleece; with the following result:



in public asylum about forty per cent. be say to be cure.



in private one twenty-five per cent, at least; most of they poorish.



of Chancery Lunatics not five per cent.



finally, one-third of all the Chancery Lunatics do every six year

exchange the live tomb they be fleece and bully in for dead tomb

where they rest; and go from the sham protection of the Lord Chancellor

of England to the real protection of their creator and their Judge.



these statistic have be long before the world, and be dead figure

to the Skimmer of thing, but tell a dark tale to the Reader of

thing, so dark, that I pray Heaven to protect I, and all other weak

inoffensive person, from the protection of my Lord Chancellor in this

kind.



Beverley be so unfortunate as to exist before the date of the above

petition: and suffer the consequence.



he be an aristocrat by birth, noble on both side of his house, and

unluckily have money. but for that he would have be a labour man,

and free. my Lord Protector commit he with six hundred pound a year

maintenance money to the care of his committee, the Honourable Fynes

Beverley.



now this corporate, yet honourable individual, to whom something be

commit, and so Chancery Lane call he in its own sweet French the

thing commit, be a gentleman of birth, breeding, and intelligence.

he undertake to take care of his simple cousin; and what he do take

care of be himself.



     the SUB-LETTING swindle.



I. the Honourable Fynes Beverley, anglo-french committee, or crown

tenant, sub-let soft Francis for L. 300 a year, pocketed L. 300, and

wash his hand of Frank.



2. Mr. Heselden, the sub-tenant, sub-let the Softy of high degree for L.

150, pocket the surplus, and wash his hand of he.



3. the L. 150 man sub-let he to Dr. Wolf at L. 60 a year, pouch the

surplus, and wash his hand of he.



and now what on earth be leave for poor Dr. Wolf to do? could he

sub-embezzle a Highlander's breek? could he subtract more than her skin

from off the singed cat? could he peel the core of a rotten apple? could

he pare a grated cheese rind? could he flay a skinned flint? could he

fleece a hog after Satan have shave it as clean as a bantam's egg?



let no man dare to limit genius; least of all the genius of extortion.



Dr. Wolf screw comparatively more out of young Frank than do any of

the precede screw. he turn he into a servant of all work and half

starve he; money profit, L. 45 out of the L. 60, or three-fourth,

whereas the other have only bag one-half. but by this mean he get a

good servant without wage, and on half a servant's food, clear L. 22

and L. 12 in these two item.



Victim of our great national vice and foible, Vicariousness, this

scion of a noble house, protect in theory by the Crown, vicariously

sub-protect by the Chancellor, sub-vicariously sub-sham protect by

his kin, be really fling unprotected into the fleece market, and might

be see--at the end of the long chain of sub. pro, vice, loco,

sham, shuffle, swindle, and lie--shake the carpet, make the

bed, carry the water, sweep the room, and scour the sordid

vessel, of thirty patient in Drayton House, not one of whom be his

equal either in birth or wealth; and of four menial, who be all his

master and hard one. his work be always do, never do. he be not

the least mad nor bad, but merely of feeble intellect all round. fifty

thousand gentleman's family would have be glad of he at L. 300

a year, and make a son and a brother of he. but he be under the

vicarious protection of the Lord Chancellor. thin, half-starve,

threadbare, out at elbow, the universal butt, scoff at by the very

lunatic, and especially ill treat by the attendant whose work he do

gratis, he be swear at, jeered, insult, cuff and even kick, every

day of his hard, hard life. and yet he be a gentleman, though a soft

one; his hand, his feature, his carriage, his address, have all an

indefinable stamp of race. how have it outlive such crush, degrade

usage? I don't know; how do a daisy survive the iron roller? Alfred

soon find he out, and to everybody's amazement, especially Frank's,

remonstrate gently but resolutely and eloquently, and soon convince

the majority, sane and insane, that a creature so meek and useful

merit special kindness, not cruelty. one keeper, the Robin, _alias_

Tom Wales, an ex--prize fighter, be a warm convert to this view. among

the maniacs only one hold out, and say contemptuously he couldn't see

it.



“well,” say Alfred, “lie a finger on he after this, and I'll lay a

hand on you, and aid your intellectual vision.”



Rooke and Hayes treat remonstrance with open and gall contempt. yet

the tide of opinion change so, they do not care to defy it openly: but

they bully poor Beverley now and then on the sly, and he never tell.

he be too inoffensive for this world. but one day, as Alfred be

sit with his door ajar, write a letter of earnest expostulation to

the Commissioners, who have leave his first unanswered, he hear Hayes at

the head of the stair call roughly, “Frank! Frank!”



“Sir,” reply the soft little voice of young Beverley.



“come, be quick, young shaver.”



“I'm come, sir,” and up run Beverley.



“here, take this tray downstairs.”



“yes, sir.”



“stop, there's a bit of bread for you.” and Hayes chuck he a crust,

as one throw it to another man's dog.



“thank you, sir,” say Mr. Beverley, stoop down for it, and be

habitually as hungry as a ratcatcher's tyke, take an eager bite in that

position.



“how dare you eat it there,” say Hayes brutally: “take it to your own

crib: come, mizzle.” and with that lend he a contemptuous kick behind,

which owe to his position send he off his balance flat on the tray; a

glass break under he. poor young Mr. Beverley utter a cry of dismay,

for he know Hayes would not own himself the cause. Hayes curse he for

an awkward idiot, and the oath go off into a howl, for Alfred run out

at he brimful of Moses, and with a savage kick in the back and blow on

the neck, administer simultaneously, hurl he head foremost down

the stair. alight on the seventh step, he turn a somersault, and

bound like a ball on to the landing below, and there lie stupefied. he

pick himself up by slow degree, and glare round with speechless awe

and amazement up at the human thunderbolt that have shoot out on he and

send he fly like a feather. he shake his fist, and limp silently

away all bruise and curse, to tell Rooke and concert vengeance.

Alfred, tremble still with ire, take Beverley to his room (the boy be

as white as a sheet), and encourage he, and make he wash properly,

brush his hair, dress he in a decent tweed suit he have outgrow,

and take he under his arm, and walk with his own nose haughtily in

the air, parade he up and down the asylum, to show they all the good

man in the house respect the poor soft gentleman. ah, what a grand

thing it be to be young! Beverley clung to his protector too much like a

girl, but walk gracefully and keep step, and every now and then look

up at Alfred with a love adoration, that be sweet, yet sad to see.

Alfred march he to Mrs. Archbold, and tell his tale; for he know

Hayes would misrepresent it, and get he into trouble. she smile on the

pair; gently deplore her favourite's impetuosity, entreat he not to

go fight with that great monster Rooke, and charm he by say,

“well, and Frank _be_ a gentleman, when he be dress like one.”



“ben't he?” say Alfred eagerly. “and whose fault be it he be not always

dress like one? whose fault that here's an earl's nephew, 'boot in

Hell'?”



“not your, Alfred, nor mine,” be the honeyed reply.



in vain do Mr. Hayes prefer his complaint to Dr. Wolf. the Archbold have

be before he, and the answer be, “serve you right.”



these and many other good deed do Alfred Hardie in Drayton House. but,

as the day roll on, and no answer come from the Commissioners, his

own anxiety, grief, and dismay leave he less and less able to sympathise

with the material but small wrong around he. he become silent,

deject.



at last he come to Mrs. Archbold, and say sternly his letter to the

Commissioners be intercept.



“I cann't believe that,” say she. “it be against the law.”



so it be: but law and custom be two.



“I be sure of it,” say he; “and may the eternal curse of Heaven light

on the cowardly traitor and miscreant who have do it.” and he stalk

gloomily away.



when he leave she, she sigh at this imprecation from his lip; but do

not repent. “I _cann't_ part with he,” she say despairingly; “and if

I do not stop his poor dear letter, Wolf would:” and the amorous

crocodile shed a tear, and persist in her double-faced course.



by-and-by, when she see he get thin and paler, and his bright

face downcast and inexpressibly sad, she share his misery: ay, shed

scald tear for he: yet could not give he up; for her will be as

strong as the rest of she be supple; and hers be hot love, but not

true love like Julia's.



perhaps a very subtle observer, see this man and woman wax pale and

spiritless together in one house, might have divine her secret. Dr.

Wolf, then, be no such observer, for she make he believe she have a

rise _penchant_ for he. he really have a strong one for she.



while Alfred's visible misery pull at her heart-string, and sometimes

irritated, sometimes melt she, come curious complication; one of

which require preface.



Mrs. Dodd then be not the wife to trust blindly where her poor husband

be concern. she bribe so well that a keeperess in David's first

asylum tell she David have be harshly use by an attendant. she

instantly get Eve Dodd to take he away: and transfer he to a small

asylum near London, and keep by a Mrs. Ellis. “woman be not cruel to

man,” say the sagacious Lucy Dodd.



but, alas! if woman be not cruel where sex come in and mimic that

wide sentiment, Humanity, woman be deadly economical. largely gift

with that household virtue, Mrs. Ellis keep too few servant, and,

sure consequence in a madhouse, too many straitjacket, hobble, muff,

leg-lock, bodybelt, &c. &c. hence half her patient be frequently

keep out of harm's way by cruel restraint administer, not out of

hearty cruelty, but female parsimony. Mrs. and Miss Dodd invade the

house one day when the fair economist be out, and find seven patient

out of the twelve keep out of mischief thus: one in a restraint

chair, two hobbled like ass, two chain like dog, and two in

straight-waistcoat, and fasten to bed by web and strap; amongst

the latter, David, though quiet as a lamb.



Mrs. Dodd cry over he as if her heart would break, and make Miss Dodd

shift he to a large asylum, where I believe he be very well use. but

here those dreadful newspaper interfere; a prying into sweet seclude

spot. they diversify Mrs. Dodd's breakfast by inform she that the

doctor of this asylum have just kill a patient; the mode of execution

bloodless and sure, as become fair science. it be a man between sixty

and seventy; an age at which the heart can seldom stand very much

shocking, or lower, especially where the brain be disease. so

they place he in a shower-bath, narrow enough to impede respiration,

without the fall water, which of necessity drive out air. in short a

vertical box with hole all round the top.



here the doctor order he a cold shower-bath of unparalleled duration:

half an hour. to be follow by an unprecedented dose of tartar emetic.

this double-barrel order give, the doctor go away. (Formula.)



the water be down to forty-five degree Fahrenheit. half an hour's

shower-bath at that temperature in a roomy bath would kill the young
