and strong man in her Majesty's dominion.



for eight-and-twenty mortal minute the poor old man stand in this

vertical coffin under this cold cascade. six hundred gallon of icy

water be in that his last hour, his last half-hour, discharge upon

his devoted head and doom body.



he have to be help away from this death-torrent he have walk into in

high spirit, poor soul.



even this change awaken no misgiving, no remorse; though you or I, or

any man or woman pick at hazard out of the street, would at once

have see that he be die, he be duly doze by the fire with four

spoonful of antimonial tincture--_to mak' sicker._ but even the

“Destructive Art of Healing” cannot slay the slain. the old man cheat

the emetic; for, before it could hurt he, he die of the bath; and his

body tell its own sad tale; to use the word of a medical eye-witness,

it be “a piece of alabaster.” the death-torrent have drive the whole

circulation from the surface.*



     * this mode of execution be well know in the United States.

     they settle refractory prisoner with it periodically. but

     half an hour be not need; twenty minute will do the

     trick. _“Harper's Weekly,_” a year or two ago, contain an

     admirable woodcut of a negro's execution by water. in this

     remarkable picture you see the poor darkie seat powerless,

     howl and pant his life away under the deadly cascade,

     and there stand the stolid turnkey, erect, formal, stiff as

     a ramrod, pull the deadly string with a sort of drill

     exercise air, and no more compunction nor reflection than if

     he himself be a machine construct to pull string or

     trigger on his own string be pull by butcher or fool.

     a picture well study, and so worth study.



Mrs. Dodd be terrify, and in spite of Sampson's assurance that this

be the asylum of all other they would not settle another patient in

until the matter should have blow over, get Eve Dodd to write to Dr.

Wolf, and offer L. 300 a year if he would take David at once, and treat

he with especial consideration.



he show this letter triumphantly to Mrs. Archbold, and she, blind

for a moment by feel, dissuade he from receive Captain Dodd. he

stare at she. “what, turn away a couple of thousand pound?”



“but they will come to visit he; and perhaps see he.”



“oh, that can be manage. you must be on your guard: and I'll warn

Rooke. I cann't turn away money on a chance.”



one day Alfred find himself lock into his room. this be unusual:

for, though they call he a lunatic in word, they call he sane

by all their act. he half suspect that the Commissioners be in the

house.



have he know who really be in the house, he would have beat himself

to piece against the door.



at dinner there be a new patient, very mild and silent, with a

beautiful large brown eye, like some gentle animal's.



Alfred be very much strike with this eye, and contrive to say a kind

word to he after dinner. find himself address by a gentleman,

the new comer handle his forelock and make a sea scrape, and announce

himself as William Thompson; he add with simple pride, “Able seaman;”

 then touch his forelock again, “just come aboard, your honour.” after

this, which come off glibly, he be anything but communicative. however,

Alfred contrive to extract from he that he be rather glad to leave

his last ship, on account of having be constantly impede there in his

duty by a set of lubber, that clung round he and keep he on deck

whenever the first lieutenant order he into the top.



the very next day, pace sadly the dull gravel of his prison yard,

Alfred hear a row; and there be the able seaman struggle with

the Robin and two other keeper. he want to go to his duty in the

foretop: to wit, the fork of a high elm-tree in the court-yard. Alfred

have half a mind not to interfere. “who care for _my_ misery?” he say.

but his well nature prevail, and he tell the Robin he be sure go

up imaginary rigging would do Thompson more good than harm.



on this the man reluctantly give he a trial, and he go up the tree

with wonderful strength and agility, but evident caution. still Alfred

quake when he cross his thigh tight over a limb of the tree forty

foot from earth, and go carefully and minutely through the whole

process of furl imaginary sail. however, he come down manifestly

soothe by the performance, and, singular phenomenon, he be quite cool;

and it be the spectator on deck who perspire.



“and what a pleasant voice he have,” say Alfred; “it quite charm my

ear; it be not like a mad voice. it be like--I'm mad myself.”



“and he have get a fiddle, and play it like a hangel, by all account,”

 say the Robin; “only he willn't touch it but when he have a mind.”



at night Alfred dream he hear Julia's sweet mellow voice speak to

he; and he look, and lo! it be the able seaman. he could sleep no

more, but lie sighing.



Ere the able seaman have be there three day, Mrs. Dodd come

unexpectedly to see he; and it be with the utmost difficulty Alfred

be smuggle out of the way. Mrs. Archbold see by her love anxiety

these visit would be frequent, and, unless Alfred be keep constantly

lock up, which be repugnant to she, they would meet some day. she

know there be man who ply the trade of spy, and where to find they;

she set one of they to watch Mrs. Dodd's house, and learn her habit, in

hope of get some clue as to when she might be expect.



now it so happen that, look for one thing, she find another which

give she great hope and courage. and then the sight of Alfred's misery

try her patience, and then he be begin half to suspect she of

stop his letter. passion, impatience, pity, and calculation,

all drive she the same road, and lead to an extraordinary scene, so

impregnate with the genius of the madhouse--a place where the passion

run out to the very end of their tether--that I feel little able to

describe it. I will try and indicate it.



one fine Sunday afternoon, then, she ask Alfred languidly would he

like to walk in the country.



“would I like? ah, don't trifle with a prisoner,” say he sorrowfully.



she shake her head. “no, no, it will not be a happy walk. Rooke, who

hate you, be to follow we with that terrible mastiff, to pull you down

if you try to escape. I could not get Dr. Wolf to consent on any other

term. Alfred, let we give up the idea. I fear your rashness.”



“no, no, I willn't try to escape--from you. I have not see a blade of

grass this six month.”



the accomplished dissembler hesitate, yield. they pass through

the yard and out at the back door, which Alfred have so often look

wistfully at; and by-and-by reach a delicious pasture. a light golden

haze stream across it. nature never seem so sweet, so divine, to

Alfred before; the sun as bright as midsummer, though not the least hot,

the air fresh, yet genial, and perfume with Liberty and the small

flower of earth. Beauty glide rustle by his side, and dark eye

subdue their native fire into softness whenever they turn on he; and

scarce fifty yard in the rear hung a bully and a mastiff ready to tear

he down if he should break away from beauty's light hand, that rest

so timidly on his. he be young, and stout-hearted, and relish his

peep of liberty and nature, though blot by Vulcan and Rooke. he

chat to Mrs. Archbold in good spirit. she answer briefly, and

listlessly.



 at last she stop under a young chestnut-tree as if overcome with a

sudden reflection, and turn half away from he lean her head and

hand upon a bough, and sigh. the attitude be pensive and womanly. he

ask she with innocent concern what be the matter; then faintly should

he take her home. all her answer be to press his hand with hers that

be disengage, and, instead of sigh, to cry.



the novice in woman's wile set himself to comfort she--in vain; to

question she--in vain at first; but by degree she allow he to learn

that it be for he she mourn; and so they proceed on the old, old

plan, the man extort from the woman bit by bit just so much as she

want all along to say, and would have pour in a stream if let quite

alone.



he draw from his distressed friend that Dr. Wolf for reason of his own

have make special inquiry about the Dodds; that she have fortunately

or unfortunately hear of this, and have question the person employ,

hope to hear something that might comfort Alfred. “instead of that,”

 say she, “I find Miss Dodd be like most girl; out of sight be out of

mind with she.”



“what do you mean?” say Alfred, tremble suddenly.



“do not ask I. what a weak fool I be to let you see I be unhappy for

you.”



“the truth be the truth,” gasp Alfred; “tell I at once.”



“must I? I be afraid you will hate I; for I should hate any one who

tell I your fault. well, then--if I must--Miss Dodd have a beau.”



“it be a lie!” cry Alfred furiously.



“I wish it be. but she have two in fact, both of they clergyman.

however, one seem the favourite; at least they be engage to be

marry; it be Mr. Hurd, the curate of the parish she live in. by what

I hear she be one of the religious one; so perhaps that have bring the

pair to an understanding.”



at these word a cold sickness rush all over Alfred, begin at his

heart. he stand white and stupefy a moment; then, in the anguish of

his heart, break out into a great and terrible cry; it be like a young

lion wound with a poison shaft.



then he be silent, and stand stock still, like petrified despair.



Mrs. Archbold be prepare for an outburst: but not of this kind. his

anguish be so unlike a woman's that it stagger she. her good and

bad angel, to use an expressive though somewhat too poetical phrase,

battle for she. she have an impulse to earn his gratitude for life,

to let he out of the asylum ere Julia should be Mrs. Hurd, and even

liberty come too late for true love. she look again at the statue of

grief by her side; and burst out cry in earnest.



this be unfortunate. shallow pity exuding in salt water leave not

enough behind to gush forth in good deed.



she only try to undo her own work in part; to comfort he a little

with commonplace. she tell he in a soothe whisper there be other

woman in the world besides this inconstant girl, other who could love

he as he deserve.



he make no answer to all she could say, but just wave his hand once

impatiently. petty consolation seem to ste he.



she draw back discouraged; but only for a while. he be silent.



with one grand serpentine movement she come suddenly close to he, and,

stand half behind he, lay her hand softly on his shoulder, and

pour burn love in his ear. “Alfred,” she murmur, “we be both

unhappy; let we comfort one another. I have pity on you at Silverton

House, I pity you now: pity _I_ a little in turn: take I out of this

dreadful house, out of this revolting life, and let I be with you. let

I be your housekeeper, your servant, your slave. this news that have

shock you so have tear the veil from my eye. I think I have cool

my love down to friendship and tender esteem; but no, now I see you as

unhappy as myself, now I can speak and wrong no one, I own I--oh Alfred

my heart burn for you, bleed for you, yearn for you, sicken for you,

die for you.”



“oh, hush! hush! Mrs. Archbold. you be say thing you will blush for

the next moment.”



“I blush now, but cannot hush; I have go too far. and your happiness

as well as mine be at stake. no young girl can understand or value

such a man as you be: but I, like you, have suffer; I, like you, be

constant; I, like you, be warm and tender; at my age a woman's love be

bliss to he who can gain it; and I love you with all my soul, Alfred.

I worship the ground you walk on, my sweet, sweet boy. say you the word,

dearest, and I will bribe the servant, and get the key, and sacrifice

my profession for ever to give you liberty (see how sweet the open face

of nature be, sweet than anything on earth, but love); and all I ask

be a little, little of your heart in return. give I a chance to make

you mine for ever; and, if I fail, treat I as I shall deserve; desert

I at once; and then I'll never reproach you; I'll only die for you; as

I have live for you ever since I first see your heavenly face.”



the passionate woman pause at last, but her hot cheek and heaving bosom

and tender convulsive hand prolong the pleading.



I be afraid few man of her own age would have resist she; for

voice and speech and all burn, melting, and win; and then, so

reasonable, lad; she do not stipulate for constancy.



but Alfred turn round to her blushing and sorrowful. “for shame!” he

say; “this be not love: you abuse that sacred word. indeed, if you

have ever really love, you would have pity I and Julia long ago, and

respect our love; and save we by give I my freedom long ago. I be

not a fool: do you think I don't know that you be my jailer, and the

cunningest and most dangerous of they all?”



“you cruel, ungrateful!” she sob.



“no; I be not ungrateful either,” say he more gently. “you have always

come between I and that kind of torture which most terrify vulgar

soul: and I thank you for it. only if you have also pity the deep

anguish of my heart, I should thank you more still. as it be, I forgive

you for the share you have have in blast my happiness for life; and

nobody shall ever know what you have be mad enough in an unguarded

moment to say; but for pity's sake talk no more of love, to mock my

misery.”



Mrs. Archbold be white with ire long before he have do this sentence.

“you insolent creature,” say she; “you spurn my love; you shall feel my

hate.”



“so I conclude,” say he coldly: “such love as yours be hard by hate.”



“it be,” say she: “and I know how I'll combine the two. to-day I love

you, and you spurn I; ere long you shall love I and I'll despise

you; and not spurn you.”



“I don't understand you,” say Alfred, feel rather uneasy.



“what,” say she, “don't you see how the superior mind can fascinate the

inferior? look at Frank Beverley--how he follow you about and fawn on

you like a little dog.”



“I prefer his sort of affection to yours.”



“a gentleman and a man would have keep that to himself; but you be

neither one nor the other; or you would have take my offer, and then

run away from I the next day, you fool. a man betray a woman; he

don't insult she. ah, you admire Frank's affection; well, you shall

imitate it. you couldn't love I like a man; you shall love I like a

dog.”



“how will you manage that, pray?” he inquire with a sneer.



“I'll drive you mad.”



she hiss this fiendish threat out between her white tooth.



“ay, sir,” she say, “hitherto your reason have only encounter man. you

shall see now what an insulted woman can do. a lunatic you shall be ere

long, and then I'll make you love I, dote on I, follow I about for a

smile: and then I'll leave off hate you, and love you once more, but

not the way I do five minute ago.”



at this furious threat Alfred grind his tooth, and say, “then I give

you my honour that the moment I see my reason the least shake, I'll

kill you: and so save myself from the degradation of be your lover on

any term.”



“threaten your own sex with that,” say the Archbold contemptuously;

“you may kill I whenever you like; and the soon the well. only, if

you don't do it very quickly, you shall be my property, my brain-sick,

love-sick slave.”









CHAPTER XLI



after a defiance so bitter and deadly, Alfred naturally draw away from

his inamorata. but she, boil with love and hate, say bitterly, “we

need not take Mr. Rooke into our secret. come, sir, your arm!”



he stick it out ungraciously, and avert his head; she take it,

suppress with difficulty a petty desire to pinch, and so walk by

his side. he be as much at his ease as if promenade jungle with a

panther. she feel he quiver with repugnance under her soft hand; and

prolong the irritate contact. she walk very slowly, and tell he

with much meaning she be wait for a signal. “till then,” say she,

“we will keep one another company;” bite the word with her tooth as it

go out.



by-and-by a window be open in the asylum, and a table-cloth hang out.

Mrs. Archbold point it out to Alfred; he stare at it; and after that

she walk he rapidly home in silence. but, as soon as the door be

double-locked on he, she whisper triumphantly in his ear--



“your mother-in-law be expect to-day; that signal be to let I know

she be go.”



“my mother-in-law!” cry the young man, and try in vain to conceal

his surprise and agitation.



“ay; your mother-in-law, that shall never be. Mrs. Dodd.”



“Mrs. Dodd here!” say Alfred, clasp his hand. then he reflect,

and say coolly: “it be false; what should she come here for?”



“to see your father-in-law.”



“my father-in-law? what, be he here, too?” say Alfred with an

incredulous sneer.



“yes, the raving maniac that call himself Thompson, and that you take

to from the first: he be your precious father-in-law--that shall never

be.”



Alfred be now utterly amazed, and bewilder. Mrs. Archbold eye he in

silent scorn.



“poor man,” say he at last; and hang his head sorrowfully. “no wonder

then his voice go so to my heart. how strange it all be! and how will

it all end?”



“in your be a madman instead of an insolent fool,” hiss the viper.



at this moment Beverley appear at the end of the yard. Mrs. Archbold

whistle he to she like a dog. he came run zealously. “who be that

call while I be out?” she inquire.



“a polite lady, madam: she say sir to I, and thank I.”



“that sound like Mrs. Dodd,” say the Archbold quietly.



“ah, but,” continue Frank, “there be another with she a beautiful

young lady; oh, so beautiful!”



“Miss Julia Dodd,” say the Archbold grimly.



Alfred pant, and his eye rove wildly in search of a way to escape

and follow she; she could not be far off.



“anybody else, Frank?” inquire Mrs. Archbold.



“no more lady, madam; but there be a young gentleman all in black. I

think he be a clergyman--or a butler.”



“ah, that be her husband that be to be; that be Mr. Hurd. she can go

nowhere without he, not even to see her old beau.”



at these word, every one of they an adder, Alfred turn on she

furiously, and his long arm shoot out of its own accord, and the

finger open like an eagle's claw. she see, and understand, but

never blench. her vindictive eye meet his dilating flash orb

unflinchingly.



“you pass for a woman,” he say, “and I be too wretched for anger.”

 he turn from she with a deep convulsive sob, and, almost staggering,

lean his brow against the wall of the house.



she have do what no man have as yet succeed in; she have break his

spirit. and here a man would have leave he alone. but the reject

beauty put her lip to his ear, and whisper into they, “this be only

the beginning.” then she leave he and go to his room and steal all

his paper, and pen, and ink, and his very Aristotle. he be to have no

occupation now, except to brood, and brood, and brood.



as for Alfred, he sit down upon a bench in the yard a broken man: up to

this moment he have hope his Julia be as constant as himself. but no;

either she have hear he be mad, and with the universal credulity have

believe it, or perhaps, not hear from he at all believe herself

forsake; and be console herself with a clergyman. Jealousy do not

as yet infuriate Alfred. its first effect resemble that of a heavy

blow. Little Beverley find he actually sick, and run to the Robin.

the ex-prizefighter bring he a thimbleful of brandy, but he would not

take it. “ah no, my friend,” he say, “that cannot cure I; it be not

my stomach; it be my heart. break, break!”



the Robin retire muttering. Little Beverley kneel down beside he,

and kiss his hand with a devotion that savour of the canine. yet it

be tender, and the sink heart clung to it. “oh, Frank!” he cry,

“my Julia believe I mad, or think I false, or something, and she

will marry another before I can get out to tell she all I have endure

be for love she. what shall I do? God protect my reason! what will

become of I?”



he moan, and young Frank sorrow over he, till the harsh voice of

Rooke summon he to some menial duty. this discharge, he come run

back; and sit on the bench beside his crushed benefactor without say

a word. at last he deliver this sapient speech: “I see. you want to

get out of this place.”



Alfred only sigh hopelessly.



“then I must try and get you out,” say Frank. Alfred shake his head.



“just let I think,” say Frank solemnly; and he sit silent look

like a young owl: for think soon puzzle he, and elicit his

intellectual weakness, whereas in a groove of duty he could go

as smoothly as half the world, and but for his official, officious

Protector, might just as well have be Boots at the Swan, as Boots and

Chambermaid at the Wolf.





so now force and cunning have declare war on Alfred, and feebleness

in person enlist in his defence. his adversary lose no time; that

afternoon Rooke tell he he be henceforth to occupy a double bedded

room with another patient.



“if he should be violent in the middle of the night, sing out, and we

will come--if we hear you,” say the keeper with a malicious smile.



the patient turn out to be the able seaman. here Mrs. Archbold aim

a double stroke; to shake Alfred's nerve, and show he how very mad his

propose father-in-law be. she think that, if he could once be force

to realise this, it might reconcile he to not marry the daughter.



the first night David do get up and parade an imaginary deck for four

mortal hour. Alfred's sleep be break; but he say nothing, and David

turn in again, his watch complete.



not a day pass now but a blow be strike. nor be the victim passive;

debarred writing material, he cut the rim off several copy of the

_time,_ and secrete they: then catch sight of some ink-blot on the

back of Frank's clothe-brush, scrape they carefully off, melt they

in a very little water, and with a toothpick scrawl his wrong to

the Commissioners; he roll the slip round a half-crown, and write

outside, “Good Christian, keep this half-crown, and take the writing to

the Lunacy Commissioners at Whitehall, for pity's sake.” this do, he

watch, and when nobody be look, fling his letter, so weight,

over the gate; he hear it fall on the public road.



another day he secrete a spoonful of black currant preserve, dilute it

with a little water, and write a letter, and throw it into the road as

before: another day, hear the Robin express disgust at the usage to

which he be now subject, he draw he apart, and offer he a

hundred pound to get he out. now the ex-prizefighter be rather a

tender-hearted fellow, and a great detester of foul play. what he see

make he now side heartily with Alfred; and all he want be to be

indemnify for his risk.



he look down and say, “you see, sir, I have a wife and child to think

of.”



Alfred offer he two hundred pound.



“that be more than enough, sir,” say the Robin; “but you see I cann't do

it alone. I must have a pal in it. could you afford as much to Garrett?

he be the likeliest; I've hear he say as much as that he be sick of

the business.”



Alfred jump at the proposal: he would give they two hundred apiece.



“I'll sound he,” say the Robin; “don't you speak to he, whatever. he

might blow the gaff. I must begin by make he drunk, then he'll tell

I his real mind.”



one fine morning the house be make much clean than usual; the

rotatory chair, in which they use to spin a maniac like a teetotum, the

restraint chair, and all the paraphernalia be send into the stable,

and so disposed that, even if find, they would look like thing

scorn and dismiss from service: for Wolf, mind you, profess the

non-restraint system.



Alfred ask what be up, and find all this be in preparation for the

quarterly visit of the Commissioners: a visit intend to be a surprise;

but Drayton House always know when they be come, and the very name

of the two thunderbolt that think to surprise they.



Mrs. Archbold communicate her knowledge in off-hand term. “it be only

two old woman: Bartlett and Terry.”



the gentleman thus flatteringly herald arrive next day. one an aged,

infirm man, with a grand benevolent head, bald front and silver hair,

and the gold-head cane of his youth, now a dignified crutch: the other

an ordinary look little chap enough, with this merit--he be what

he look. they have a long interview with Mrs. Archbold first, for fear

they should carry a naked eye into the asylum. Mr. Bartlett, act on

instruction, very soon inquire about Alfred; Mrs. Archbold's face put

on friendly concern directly. “I be sorry to say he be not so well as he

be a fortnight ago--not nearly so well. we have give he walk in the

country, too; but I regret to say they do he no real good; he come

back much excited, and now he shun the other patient, which he use

not to do.” in short, she give they the impression that Alfred be a

moping melancholiac.



“well, I have well see he,” say Mr. Bartlett, “just to satisfy the

Board.”



Alfred be accordingly send for, and ask with an indifferent air how

he be.



he say he be very well in health, but in sore distress of mind at his

letter to the Commissioners be intercept by Mrs. Archbold or Dr.

Wolf.



Mrs. Archbold smile pityingly. Mr. Bartlett catch her glance, and

conclude this be one of the patient's delusion. (Formula.)



Alfred surprise the glance, and say, “you can hardly believe this,

because the act be illegal. but a great many illegal act, that you

never detect, be do in asylum. however, it be not a question of

surmise; I send four letter in the regular way since I come. here

be their several date. pray make a note to inquire whether they have

reach Whitehall or not.”



“oh, certainly, to oblige you,” say Mr. Bartlett, and make the note.



Mrs. Archbold look rather discompose at that.



“and now, gentleman,” say Alfred, “since Mrs. Archbold have have a

private interview, which I see she have abuse to poison your mind

against I, I claim as simple justice a private interview to disabuse

you.”



“you be the first patient ever tell I to walk out of my own

drawing-room,” say Mrs. Archbold, rise white with ire and

apprehension, and sweep out of the room.



by this piece of female petulance she give the enemy a point in the

game; for, if she have insist on stay, Mr. Bartlett be far too weak

to have dismiss she. as it be, he feel shocked at Alfred's rudeness:

and so small a thing as justice do not in his idea counterbalance so

great a thing as discourtesy; so he listen to Alfred's tale with the

deadly apathy of an unwilling hearer. “pour on: I will endure,” as poor

Lear say.



as for Dr. Terry, he be pictorial, but null; effete; empty of brain

by all-scoop-time. if he have be detain that day at Drayton House,

and Frank Beverley send back in his place to Whitehall, it would have

matter little to he, less to the nation, and nothing to mankind.



at last Mr. Bartlett give Alfred some hope he be take in the truth;

for he tear a leaf out of his memorandum-book, write on it, and pass

it to Dr. Terry. the ancient take it with a smile, and seem to make

an effort to master it, but fail; it drop simultaneously from his

finger and his mind.



not a question be put to Alfred; so he be fain to come to an end;

he withdraw suddenly, and catch Mrs. Archbold at the keyhole. “noble

adversary!” say he, and stalk away, and hide himself hard by: and no

soon do the inspector come out, and leave the coast clear, than he

dart in and look for the paper Mr. Bartlett have pass to Dr. Terry.



he find it on the floor: and take it eagerly up; and full of hope, and

expectation, read these word:



what be the name of the stuff the MATRON'S gown be make of? I should

like to BUY MRS. BARTLETT one like it.



Alfred stand and read this again, and again he search for some hide

symbolical meaning in the word. high-minded, and deeply impressed with

his own wrong, he could not conceive a respectable man, pay fifteen

hundred a year to spy out wrong, be so heartless hard as to write

this single comment during the earnest recital of a wrong so gigantic

as his. Poor Alfred learn this to his cost, that to put small man

into great place be to create monster. when he have realise the bitter

truth, he put the stony-hearted paper in his pocket, creep into the

yard, and sit down, and, for all he could do, scald tear run down

his cheek.



“Homunculi quanti sunt!” he sob; “homunculi quanti sunt!”



presently he see Dr. Terry come wander towards he alone. the

Archbold have not deign to make he safe; senectitude have do that.

Alfred, all heart--sick as he be, go to the old gentleman out of

veneration for the outside of his head--which be Shakespearian--and

pity for his bodily infirmity; and offer he an arm. the doctor

thank he sweetly, and say, “pray, young man, have you anything to

communicate?”



then Alfred see that the ancient man have already forget his face, and

so look at he with that rare instrument of official inspection,

the naked eye, have see he be sane; and consequently take he for a

keeper.



how swiftly the mind can roam, and from what a distance gather the

material of a thought! flash like lightning through Alfred's mind

this line from one of his pet, the greek philosopher:



[greek text]



“and this be the great stroke of art, to turn an evil into a good.”



now the feebleness of this aged Inspector be an evil: the thing then

be to turn it into a good. Shade of Plato, behold how thy disciple

work thee! “Sir,” say he, sink his voice mysteriously, “I have:

but I be a poor man: you willn't say I tell you: it's as much as my place

be worth.”



“confidence, strict confidence,” reply Nestor, go over beat

track; for he have keep many a queer secret with the loyalty which do

his profession so much honour.



“then, sir, there's a young gentleman confine here, who be no more mad

than you and I; and never be mad.”



“you don't say so.”



“that I do, sir: and they know they be do wrong, sir, for they stop

all his letter to the Commissioners; and that be unlawful, you know.

would you like to take a note of it all, sir?”



the old fogie say he think he should, and grope vaguely for his

note-book: he extract it at last like a loose tooth, fumble with it,

and drop it: Alfred pick it up fume inwardly.



the ancient go to write, but his finger be weak and hesitating, and

by this time he have half forget what he be go to say. Alfred's

voice quaver with impatience; but he fight it down, and offer as

coolly as he could to write it for he: the offer be accept, and he

write down in a feigned hand, very clear--





“DRAYTON HOUSE, _Oct. 5._--A sane patient, Alfred Hardie, confine here

from interested motive. have write four letter to the Commissioners,

all believe to be intercept. communicate to I in confidence by

an attendant in the house. refer to the party himself, and his

correspondence with the Commissioners from Dr. Wycherley's: also to

Thomas Wales, another attendant; and to Dr. Wycherley: also to Dr.

Eskell and Mr. Abbott, Commissioners of Lunacy.”





after this stroke of address Alfred take the first opportunity of

leave he, and send Frank Beverley to he.



thus Alfred, alarm by the hatred of Mrs. Archbold, and rack with

jealousy, exert all his intelligence and play many card for

liberty. one he keep in reserve; and a trump card too. have now no ink

nor colour matter, he do not hesitate, but out penknife, up

sleeve, and draw blood from his arm, and with it write once more to the

Commissioners, but keep this letter hide for an ingenious purpose.

what that purpose be my reader shall divine.











CHAPTER XLII



we leave Julia Dodd a district visitor. work in a dense parish she

learn the depth of human misery, bodily and mental.



she visit an honest widow, so poor that she could not afford a

farthing dip, but sit in the dark. when friend come to see she they

sometimes bring a candle to talk by.



she visit a cripple who often thank God sincerely for leave she

the use of one thumb.



she visit a poor creature who for sixteen year have be afflict

with a tumour in the neck, and have lain all those year on she back with

her head in a plate; the heat of a pillow be intolerable. Julia find

her longing to go, and yet content to stay: and praise God in all the

lull of that pain which be her companion day and night.



but be I to enumerate the ghastly sight, the stifle loathsome

odour, the vulgar horror upon horror this refined young lady face,

few of my reader would endure on paper for love of truth what she

endure in reality for love of suffer humanity, and of he whose

servant she aspire to be.



probably such sacrifice of selfish ease and comfort be never quite in

vain; they tend in many way to heal our own wound: I willn't say that

bodily suffer be bad than mental; but it be realise far more

vividly by a spectator. the grim heart-break sight she see array

Julia's conscience against her own grief; the more so when she find

some of her most afflict one resign, and even grateful. “what,”

 say she, “can they, all rag, disease and suffering, bow so cheerfully

to the will of Heaven, and have I the wickedness, the impudence, to

repine?”



and then, happy than most district visitor, she be not always

oblige to look on helpless, or to confine her consolation to good

word. Mrs. Dodd be get on famously in her groove. she be high in

the confidence of Cross and Co., and be inspect eighty lady, as

well as work; her salary and profit together be not less than five

hundred pound a year, and her one luxury be charity, and Julia its

minister. she carry a good honest basket, and there you might see she

Bible wedge in with wine and meat, and tea and sugar: and still,

as these melt in her round, a little spark of something warm would

sometimes come in her own sick heart. thus by degree she be attain

not earthly happiness, but a grave and pensive composure.



yet across it gust of earthly grief come sweeping often; but these she

hide till she be herself again.



to her mother and brother she be kind, sweeter, and dearer, if

possible, than ever. they look on she as a saint; but she know well;

and use to blush with honest shame when they call she so. “oh don't,

pray don't,” she would say with unaffected pain. “love I as if I be an

angel; but do not praise I; that turn my eye inward and make I see

myself. I be not a Christian yet, nor anything like one.”



return one day from her duty very tired, she sit down to take off

her bonnet in her own room, and presently hear snatch of an argument

that make she prick those wonderful little ear of hers which could

almost hear through a wall. the two conclude sentence be a key to

the whole dialogue.



“why disturb she?” say Mrs. Dodd. “she be get well of 'the

Wretch;' and my advice be, say nothing: what harm can that do?”



“but then it be so unfair, so ungenerous, to keep anything from the poor

girl that may concern she.”



at this moment Julia come softly into the room with her curiosity hide

under an air of angelic composure.



her mother ask after Mrs. Beecher, to draw she into conversation. she

reply quietly that Mrs. Beecher be no well, but very thankful for

the wine Mrs. Dodd have send she. this answer give, she go without any

apparent hurry and sit by Edward, and fix two love implore eye on

he in silence. oh, subtle sex! this feather be to turn the scale, and

make he talk unquestione. it tell. she be close to he too, and mamma

at the end of the room.



“look here, Ju,” say he, put his hand in his pocket, “we two have

always be friend as well as brother and sister; and somehow it do

not seem like a friend to keep thing dark;” then to Mrs. Dodd: “she be

not a child, mother, after all; and how can it be wrong to tell she

the truth, or right to suppress the truth? well then, Ju, there's an

advertisement in the _'Tiser,_ and it's a regular riddle. now mind,

I don't really think there be anything in it; but it be a droll

coincidence, very droll; if it ben't there be lady present, and

one of they a district visitor, I would say, d--d droll. so droll,”

 continue he, get warm, “that I should like to punch the

advertiser's head.”



“let I see it, dear,” say Julia. “I dare say it be nothing worth

punch about.”



“there,” say Edward. “I've mark it.”



Julia take the paper, and her eye fall on this short advertisement:





     aileen AROON.--DISTRUST appearance.



look at she with some anxiety, they see the paper give one sharp

rustle in her hand, and then quiver a little. she bow her head over

it, and everything seem to swim. but she never move: they could

neither of they see her face, she defend herself with the paper.

the letter clear again, and, still hide her face, she study and

study the advertisement.



“come, tell we what you think of it,” say Edward. “be it anything? or a

mere coincidence?”



“it be a pure coincidence,” say Mrs. Dodd, with an admirable imitation

of cool confidence.



Julia say nothing; but she now rise and put both arm round Edward's

neck, and kiss he fervidly again and again, hold the newspaper

tight all the time.



“there,” say Mrs. Dodd: “see what you have do.”



“oh, it be all right,” say Edward cheerfully. “the british fireman

be getting hug no end. why, what be the matter? have you get the

hiccough, Ju?”



“no; no! you be a true brother. I know all along that he would explain

all if he be alive: and he be alive.” so say she kiss the _'Tiser_

violently more than once; then flutter away with it to her own room

ashame to show her joy, and yet not able to hide it.



Mrs. Dodd shake her head sorrowfully: and Edward begin to look rueful,

and doubt whether he have do wisely. I omit the discussion that

follow. but the next time his duty permit he to visit they Mrs.

Dodd show he the _'Tiser_ in her turn, and with her pretty white

taper finger, and such a look, point to the follow advertisement:



     aileen AROON.--I _do_ distrust

     appearance. but if you ever love I

     explain they at once. I have something for

     you from your dear sister.



“poor simple girl,” say Mrs. Dodd, “not to see that, if he could

explain at all, he _would_ explain, not go advertise an enigma after

act a mystification. and to think of my innocent dove put in that

she have something for he from his sister; a mighty temptation for such

a wretch!”



“it be wonderfully silly,” say Edward; “and such a clever girl, too;

but you lady cann't stick to one thing at a time; beg your pardon,

mamma.”



Mrs. Dodd take no notice of this remark.



“to see she low herself so!” she say. “oh, my son, I be mortify.”

 and Mrs. Dodd lean her cheek against Edward's, and sigh.



“now don't you cry, mammy,” say he sorrowfully. “I'll break every bone

in his skin for your comfort.”



“heaven forbid!” cry Mrs. Dodd anxiously; “what, be you not aware she

would hate you?”



“hate I: her brother!”



“she would hate we all if we lay a finger on that wretch. pray

interfere no more, love; foolish child, talk to I about woman, and

it be plain you know nothing of their heart: and a good thing _for_

you.” she then put on maternal authority (nobody could do it more

easily) and solemnly forbid all violence.



he do not venture to contradict she now; but cherish his resolution

all the more, and long for the hour when he might take “the Wretch” by

the throat, and chastise he, the more publicly the well.



now, the above incident that reveal Julia's real heart, which she

have be hide more or less all this time from those who could not

sympathise with she, take eventually a turn unfavourable to “the

Wretch.” so he might well be call. her great and settle fear have

always be that Alfred be dead. under the immediate influence of his

father's cunning, she have for a moment believe he be false; but so

true and love a heart could not rest in that opinion. in true love so

long as there be one grain of uncertainty, there be a world of faith and

credulous ingenuity. so, as Alfred have never be see since, as nobody

could say he be married to another, there be a grain of uncertainty as

to his unfaithfulness, and this her true heart magnify to a mountain.



but now matter wear another face. she be sure he have write the

advertisement. who but he, out of the few that take the word of any

song to heart, admire Aileen Aroon? who but he out of the three or four

people who might possibly care for that old song, have appearance to

explain away? and who but he know they take in the _Morning Advertiser?_

she wait then for the explanation she have invite. she read the

advertising column every day over and over.



not a word more.



then her womanly pride be deeply wound. what, have she court an

explanation where most lady would have listen to none; and court

it in vain!



her high spirit revolt. her heart swell against the repeat insult

she have receive: this last one fill the bitter cup too high.



and then her mother come in and assure she he have only insert that

advertisement to keep she in his power. he have hear you be recover,

and be admire by other more worthy of your esteem.



Julia cry bitterly at these argument, for she could no long combat

they.



and Mr. Hurd be very attentive and kind. and when he speak to Julia,

and Julia turn away, her eye be sure to meet Mrs. Dodd's eye

implore she secretly not to discourage the young man too much. and so

she be gently pull by one, and gently thrust by another, away from

her first lover and towards his successor.



it be an old, old story. Fate seem to exhaust its malice on our first

love. for the second the road be smooth. matter go on so some

week, and it be perfectly true that Mr. Hurd escort both lady one

day to Drayton House, at Julia's request, and not Mrs. Dodd's. indeed,

the latter lady be secretly hurt at his be allow to come with

they.





one Saturday afternoon, Mrs. Dodd go alone to Drayton House by

appointment. David be like a lamb, but, as usual, have no knowledge of

she. Mrs. Archbold tell she a quiet, intelligent, patient have take

a great fancy to he, and she think this be add much to his

happiness. “may I see he to thank he?” ask Mrs. Dodd. “oh,

certainly,” say Mrs. Archbold; “I'll inquire for he.” she go out but

soon return, say, “he be go out for a walk with the head keeper:

we give he as much air and amusement as we can; we hope soon to send

he out altogether cure.” “truly kind and thoughtful,” say Mrs. Dodd.

soon after, she kiss Mrs. Archbold, and press a valuable brooch

upon she: and then take leave. however, at the gate she remember she

parasol. Mrs. Archbold say she would go back for it. Mrs. Dodd would

not hear of that: Mrs. Archbold insist, and settle the question by

go. she be no soon in the house, than young Frank Beverley come

run to Mrs. Dodd, and put the miss parasol officiously into her

hand. “oh, thank you, sir,” say she; “will you be so kind as to tell

Mrs. Archbold I have it.” and with this they part, and the porter

open the gate to she, and she get into her hire cab. she lean she

head back, and, as usual be lose in the sorrowful thought of what have

be, and what now be. poor wife, each visit to Drayton House open

her wound afresh. on reach the stone, there be a turnpike this

rouse she up; she take out her purse and pay it. as she draw back

to her seat, she see out of the tail of her feminine eye the edge of

something white under her parasol. she take up the parasol, and find a

write paper pin on to it: she detach this paper, and examine it

all over with considerable curiosity. it consist of a long slip about

an inch and a quarter broad, roll like tape, and tie with

packthread. she could not see the inside, of course, but she read

the superscription: it be firmly but clearly write, in red ink

apparently.



of the word I shall only say at present that they be strong and

simple, and that their effect on the swift intelligence and tender heart

of Mrs. Dodd be overpower. they knock at her heart; they draw from

she an audible cry of pity more eloquent than a thousand speech: and

the next moment she feel a little faint; for she know now the appeal be

not in red ink, but in something very fit to pass between the heart of

woe and the heart of pity. she smell at her salt, and soon recover

that weakness: and next her womanly bosom swell so with the milk of

human kindness that her breath come short. after a little struggle she

gush out aloud, “ah, that I will, poor soul; this very moment.”



now, by this time she be close to her own house.



she stop the cab at her door, and ask the driver if his horse be

fresh enough to carry she to the Board of Lunacy: “it be at Whitehall,

sir,” say she. “Lord bless you, ma'am,” say the cabman, “Whitehall?

why, my mare would take you to Whitechapel and back in an hour, let

alone Whitehall.”



reassure on that point Mrs. Dodd go in just to give the servant an

order: but as she stand in the passage, she hear her child's voice

and also a friend's; the genial, angry tone of Alexander Sampson, M. D.



she think, “oh, I _must_ just show they all the paper, before I go

with it;” and so after a little buzz about dinner and thing with Sarah,

mount the stair, and arrive among they singularly _apropo,_ as it

happen.





man like Sampson, who make many foe, do also make stauncher friend

than ever the Hare do, and be faithful friend themselves. the

boisterous doctor have stick to the Dodds in all their distress; and

if they be ever short of money, it certainly be not his fault: for

almost his first word, when he find they in a lodging, be, “now, ye'll

be want a Chick. Gimme pen and ink, and I'll just draw ye one; for

a hundre.” this be decline politely by Mrs. Dodd, he expostulate.

“Mai--dear--Madam, how on airth can ye go on in such a place as London

without a Chick?”



he return to the charge at his next visit and scold she well for her

pride. “who iver hard of refuse a Chick? a small inoffensive chick,

from an old friend like I? come now, behave! just a wee chick, I'll let

y' off for fifty.”



“give we your company and your friendship,” say Mrs. Dodd; “we value

they above gold: we will not rob your dear child, while we have as

many finger on our hand as other people.”





on the present occasion Dr. Sampson, whose affectionate respect for the

lead London physician have already display itself, be inveigh

specially against certain specialist, whom, in the rapidity of his

lusty eloquence, he call the Mad Ox. he favour Julia and Edward with

a full account of the maniform enormity he have detect they in during

thirty year' practice; and so descend to his present grievance. a

lady, an old friend of his, be be keep in a certain asylum month

after month because she have get money and relation, and have once be

delirious. “and why be she delirious? because she have a brain fever,

she get well in a fortnight.” this lady have throw a letter over

the wall address to he; somebody have post it: he have ask the

Commissioners to let he visit she; they have decline for the present.

“Yon Board always side with the strong against the weak,” say he. so

now he have bribe the gardener, and make a midnight assignation with the

patient; and be go to it with six stout fellow to carry she off by

force. “that be my recipe for alleged insanity,” say he. “the business

will be more like a mejaeval knight carry off a namorous nun out of

a convint, than a good physician save a pashint from the Mad Ox.

however, Mrs. Saampson's in the secret; I daunt say sh' approve it; for

she don't. she say, 'go quietly to the Board o' Commissioners.' Sis

I, 'my dear, Boards be a sort of cattle that go too slow for Saampson,

and no match at all for the Mad Ox.'”



at this conjuncture, or soon after, Mrs. Dodd come in with her paper in

her hand, a little flurry for once, and after a hasty curtsey, said--



“oh, Doctor Sampson, oh, my dear, what wickedness there be in the

world! I'm go to Whitehall this moment; only look at what be pin

on my parasol at Drayton House.”



the writing pass from hand to hand, and leave the reader look very

gravely at one another. Julia be quite pale and horror-stricken.

all be too deeply move, and even shock, to make any commonplace

comment; for it look and read like a cry from heart to heart.





   _“if you be a Christian, if

     you be human, pity a sane

     man here confine in, fraud,

     and take this to the Board of

     Lunacy at Whitehall. tear

     by treachery from she I love,

     my letter all intercept, pen

     and paper keep from I, I

     write this with a toothpick

     and my blood on a rim of

     'the Times.' oh God, direct

     it to some one who have suf-

     fere, and can feel for an-

     other's agony._”





Dr. Sampson be the first to speak. “there,” say he, under his breath:

“don't I tell you? this man be sane. there's sanity in every line.”



“well, but,” say Edward, “do you mean to say that in the present day--”



“Mai--dearr--sirr. mankind niver change. whativer the muscle of man

_can_ do in the light, the mind and conscience of man will consent to do

in the dark.”



Julia say never a word.



Mrs. Dodd, too, be for action not for talk. she bid they all a hasty

adieu, and go on her good work.



Ere she get to the street door, she hear a swift rustle behind she; and

it be Julia fly down to she, all glow and sparkle with her old

impetuosity, that have seem dead for ever. “no, no,” she cry, pant

with generous emotion; “it be to I it be send. _I_ be tear from he

I love, and by some treachery I dare say: and _i_ have suffer--oh you

shall never know what I have suffer. give it _I,_ oh pray, pray, pray

give it _I. I'll_ take it to Whitehall.”











CHAPTER XLIII



if we could always know at the time what we be do.



two lady carry a paper to whitehall out of charity to a stranger.



therein the elder be a benefactress to a man she have never speak of

but as “the Wretch;” the young hold her truant bridegroom's heart,

I may say, in her hand all the road and be his protectress. neither

recognise the hand-writing; for no man can write his own hand with a

toothpick.



they reach Whitehall, and be conduct upstairs to a gentleman of

pleasant aspect but powerful brow, seat in a wilderness of letter.



he wave his hand, and a clerk set they chair: he soon after lay down

his pen, and lean gravely forward to hear their business. they see

they must waste no time; Julia look at her mother, rise, and take

Alfred's missive to his desk, and hand it he with one of her eloquent

look, grave and pitiful. he seem strike by her beauty and her manner.



“it be pin on my parasol, sir, by a poor prisoner at Drayton House,”

 say Mrs. Dodd.



“oh, indeed,” say the gentleman, and begin to read the superscription

with a cold and wary look. but thaw visibly as he read. he open the

missive and run his eye over it. the perusal move he not a little: a

generous flush mount to his brow; he ring the bell sharply. a clerk

answer it; the gentleman write on a slip of paper, and say earnestly,

“bring I every letter that be sign with that name, and all our

correspondence about he.”



he then turn to Mrs. Dodd, and put to she a few question, which draw

out the main fact I have just relate. the paper be now bring in.

“excuse I a moment,” say he, and run over they. “I believe the man

be sane,” say he, “and that you will have enable we to baffle a

conspiracy, a heartless conspiracy.”



“we do hope he will be set free, sir,” say Mrs. Dodd piteously.



“he shall, madam, if it be as I suspect. I will stay here all night but

I will master this case; and lie it before the Board myself without

delay.”



Julia look at her mother, and then ask if it would be wrong to

inquire “the poor gentleman's name?”



“Humph!” say the official; “I ought not to reveal that without his

consent. but stay! he will owe you much, and it really seem a pity he

should not have an opportunity of express his gratitude. perhaps you

will favour I with your address: and trust to my discretion. of course,

if he do not turn out as sane as he seem, I shall never let he know

it.”



Mrs. Dodd then give her address; and she and Julia go home with a glow

about the heart selfish people, thank Heaven, never know.



unconsciously these two have deal their enemy and Alfred's a heavy blow;

have set the train to a mine. their friend at the office be a man of

another stamp than Alfred have fall in with.





Meantime Alfred be subject to hourly mortification and irritation.

he guess the motive, and try to baffle it by calm self-possession:

but this be far more difficult than heretofore, because his temper be

now exacerbate and his fibre irritate by broken sleep (of this poor

David be a great cause), and his heart inflame and poison by that

cruel, that corrode passion jealousy.



to think, that while he be in prison, a rival be ever at his Julia's

ear, make more and more progress in her heart! this corroder be his

bitter companion day and night; and perhaps of all the maddener human

cunning could have invent this be the bad. it make his temple beat

and his blood run boiling poison. indeed, there be time when he be

so distemper by passion that homicide seem but an act of justice,

and suicide a legitimate relief. for who could go on for ever carry

Hell in his bosom up and down a prison yard? he begin to go alone! to

turn impatiently from the petty trouble and fathomless egotism of those

afflict person he have hitherto force his sore heart to pity. Pale,

thin, and wo-begone, he walk the weary gravel, like the lose one in

that Hall of Eblis whose heart be a devouring fire. even an inspector

with a naked eye would no long have distinguish he at first sight

from a lunatic of the unhappy class, the melancholiac.



     “Ipse suum cor eden hominum vestigia vitan.”





Mrs. Archbold look on and see this sad sight, not with the pity it

would once have cause, but with a sort of bitter triumph lighten by

no pleasure, and darken by the shadow of come remorse. yet up to

this time she have show none of that inconstancy of purpose which mark

her sex; while she do go far to justify the poet's charge:



     “nor Hell a fury like a woman scorn.”



Rooke have a hint to provoke Alfred to violence such as would justify

they in subject so popular a patient to bodily restraint, compose

draught, and other quick maddener. Rooke enter into the game

zealously from two motive; he be devoted to Mrs. Archbold, and he

hate Alfred, who have openly defy he, and mortify his vanity about

Frank Beverley.



one Saturday Alfred be order out to walk with Rooke and Hayes and

Vulcan. he raise no objection: suspect, feel homicidal, suppress

the impulse, and by this self-command he get time to give that letter to

Beverley with instruction.



but, all the walk, he be say to himself that Julia be in the house,

and he be keep away from she, and a rival with she; this make he

sicken and rage by turn. he come back in a state verging on fury.



on enter the yard poor Beverley, who have do his bit of cunning, and

by reaction now relapse into extra simplicity, came run, and say,

“I've do it; she have get it.”



“what have you do? who have get what?” cry Rooke.



“don't tell, Frank.”



“if you don't I'll shake your life out, ye young blackguard,” cry

Rooke, seize he and throttle he till he be black in the face.



Alfred's long-pent fury break out: he gnash his tooth and dash his

fist in Rooke's face.



Rooke stagger back and bellow with pain and anger, then rush

at he incautiously, receive a stinger that stagger he, and nearly

close his right eye. he take the hint, and put himself in a posture

that show he be skilled in the art of self-defence. he stop two

blow neatly, and return a heavy one upon the rib. Alfred stagger

back some step, but steady himself, and, as Rooke rush in too

hastily to improve his advantage, catch he heavily on the other eye,

but lose his own balance a little, which enable Rooke to close; then

come a sharp short rally of re-echoing blow, and Rooke, not to be

deny, get hold of his man, and a wrestling bout ensue, in which

Alfred be somewhat weaken by misery and broken rest, Rooke's great

weight and strength enable he, after a severe struggle, to fall with

his antagonist under he, and knock the breath out of his body for the

moment. then Hayes, who have stand prudently aloof, come in and help

handcuff he. they could not walk up and down he for the Robin, who

stand by with a professional air to see fair play.



“ah, cold iron be your good chance,” he say satirically. “never you

mind, sir: you hit quick and well: I'd back _you_ at long odd in the

ring: both his peeper be in deep mourning.” he add, “a cow can beat

a man wrestling.”



when Alfred be handcuff they turn he loose. it soon transpire,

however, that he be now a dangerous maniac (Formula) and to be confine

in the noisy ward.



on hear this he see the trap he have fall into; see and tremble. he

ask himself what on earth he should do; and presently the saying come

back to he, “and this be the high stroke of art, to turn evil into

good.” he argue thus: Wolf's love of money be my great evil; he will

destroy I for money, do anything for money. then suppose I offer he

money to be honest. he beg an interview with Dr. Wolf on business.

this be accord at once. he ask the doctor plump whether he receive

a large sum to detain he under pretence of insanity.



“not very, consider the trouble you sometimes give, Mr. Hardie,” be

the dry reply.



“well, then, Justice shall outbid rascality for once. I be a sane man,

and you know it; a man of my word, and you know it. I'll give you a

thousand pound to let I out of this place.”



Dr. Wolf's eye sparkle.



“you shall have any bond or security you like; and the money within a

week of my deliverance.”



Dr. Wolf say he should be delighted to do it, if he could

conscientiously.



at this piece of hypocrisy Alfred's cheek redden, and he could not

speak.



“well, well, I do see a great change in you for the well,” say Dr.

Wolf. “if, as I suspect, you be convalescent, I will part with you

without a thousand pound or a thousand penny.”



Alfred stared. have he mistaken his man?



“I'll tell you what, though,” say the smooth doctor. “I have get two

picture, one by Raphael, one by Correggio.”



“I know they,” say the quick-witted Alfred; “they be worth more than a

thousand pound.”



“of course they be, but I would take a thousand pound from you.”



“throw I in my liberty, and I'll make it guineas.”



“we will see about that.” and with this understanding the man of

business part. Dr. Wolf consult Mrs. Archbold then and there.



“impossible,” say she; “the law would dissolve such a bargain, and you

would be expose and ruin.”



“but a thousand pound!” say the poor doctor.



“oh, he offer I more than that,” say Mrs. Archbold.



“you don't mean to say so; when be that?”



“do you remember one Sunday that I walk he out, to keep clear of Mrs.

Dodd? have you not observe that I have not repeat the experiment?”



“yes. but I really don't know why.”



“will you promise I faithfully not to take any notice if I tell you?”



the doctor promise.



then she own to he with manifest reluctance that Alfred have take

advantage of her kindness, her indiscretion, in walk alone with he,

and make passionate love to she. “he offer I not a thousand pound,”

 say she, “but his whole fortune, and his heart, if I would fly with he

from _these odious wall;_ that be his expression.”



then see out of a corner of her eye that the doctor be turn

almost green with jealousy, this artist proceed to describe the

love scene between she and Alfred, with feigned hesitation, yet minute

detail. only she invert the part: Alfred in her glowing page make the

hot love; she listened abash, confused, and try all she could think

of to bring he to well sentiment. she conclude this chapter of

history invert with a sigh, and say, “so now he hate I, I believe,

poor fellow.”



“do you regret your refusal?” ask Dr. Wolf uneasily.



“oh no, my dear friend. of course, my judgment say that few woman at my

age and in my position would have refuse. but we poor woman seldom go

by our judgment.” and she cast a tender look down at the doctor's foot.



in short, she work on he so, that he leave Alfred at her disposition,

and be no soon go to his other asylum six mile off; than the

calumniate be conduct by Hayes and Rooke through passage after

passage, and door after door, to a wing of the building connect with

the main part only by a cover way. as they near it, strange noise

become audible. faint at first, they get louder and louder. singing,

roar, howl like wolf. Alfred's flesh begin to creep. he stop

at the cover way: he would have fight to his last gasp soon than go

far, but he be handcuffed. he appeal to the keeper; but he have

use they both too roughly: they snarl and force he on, and shut he

into a common flagged cell, with a filthy truckle-bed in it, and all the

vessel of gutta-percha. here he be surround by the desperate order

of maniacs he at present scarcely know but by report. throughout that

awful night he could never close his eye for the horrible unearthly

sound that assail he. singing, swearing, howl like wild beast!

his right-hand neighbour reason high of faith and work, end each

pious argument with a sudden rhapsody of oath and never sleep a wink.

his left-hand neighbour alternately sing, and shout, “Cain be a

murderer, Cain be a murderer;” and howl like a wolf, make night

hideous. his opposite neighbour have an audience, and every now and

then deliver in a high nasal key, “let we curse and pray;” vary it

sometimes thus: “Brethren, let we work double tide.” and then he would

deliver a long fervent prayer, and follow it up immediately with a

torrent of blasphemy so terrific, that come in such a contrast they

make Alfred's body wet with perspiration to hear a poor creature so defy

his creator. no rest, no peace. when it be still, the place be

like the grave; and ever and anon, loud, sharp, tremendous, burst a

thunderclap of curse, and set those poor demented creature all

yell again for half-an-hour, make the tomb ring. and at clock-like

interval a harmless but dirty idiot, who be allow to roam the

ward, come and chant through the keyhole, “everything be nothing, and

nothing be everything.”



this be the only observation he have make for many year.



his ear assail with horror, of which you have literally no

conception, or shadow of a conception, his nose poison with ammoniacal

vapour, and the peculiar wild-beast smell that mark the true maniac,

Alfred run wildly about his cell try to stop his ear, and tremble

for his own reason. when the fearful night roll away, and morning

break, and he could stand on his truckle-bed and see the sweet

hoar-frost on a square yard of grass level with his prison bar, it

refresh his very soul, and affect he almost to tear. he be then,

to his surprise, take out, and allow to have a warm bath and to

breakfast with David and the rest; but I suspect it be do to watch

the effect of the trial he have be submit to. after breakfast,

have now no place to go, he lie on a bench, and there exhausted nature

overpower he, and he fall fast asleep.



Mrs. Archbold come by on purpose, and see he. he look very pale and

peaceful. there be a cut on his forehead due to Rooke's knuckle. Mrs.

Archbold look down, and the young figure and haughty face seem so

unresisting and peaceful sad, she half relent, and shed some bitter

tear. that do not, however, prevent she set her female spy to

watch he more closely than ever.



he awake cold but refresh, and find little Beverley stand by

he with wet eye. Alfred smile and hold out his hand like a captive

monarch to his faithful vassal. “they shalln't put you in the noisy ward

again,” sob Frank. “this be your last night here.”



“Hy, Frank, you rascal, my boot!” roar Rooke from an open window.



“come, sir--come!”



Alfred's next visitor be the Robin. he come whispering, “it be all

right with Garrett, sir, and he have get a key of the back gate; but you

must get back to your old room, or we cann't work.”



“would to Heaven I could, Robin; another night or two in the noisy ward

will drive I mad, I think.”



“well, sir, I'll tell you what you do: which we all have to do it at

odd time: hold a candle to the devil: here she come: I think she be

everywhere all at one time.” the Robin then saunter away, affect

nonchalance: and Alfred proceed to hold the candle as direct. “Mrs.

Archbold,” say he timidly rise from his seat at her approach.



“Sir,” say she haughtily, and affect surprise.



“I have a favour to ask you, madam. would you be so kind as to let I go

back to my room?”



“what, you have find I be not so powerless as you think!”



“I find myself so weak, and you so powerful that--you can afford to be

generous.”



“I have no more power over you than you have over I.”



“I wish it be so.”



“I'll prove it,” say she. “who have get the key of your room? Hayes?”

 she whistle, and send for he; and give he the requisite order before

Alfred. Alfred thank she warmly.



she smile, and go away disposed to change her tactic, and, having

show he how she could torment, try soothe mean, and open his heart

by gratitude.



but presently look out of her window she see the Robin and he

together; and somehow they seem to her subtle, observant eye, to be

plot. the very suspicion be fatal to that officer. his discharge

be determine on. meantime she set her spy to watch he, and tell she

if they see or hear anything.



now Mrs. Archbold be go out to tea that evening, and, as soon as

ever this transpire, the keeper secretly invite the keeperesse to

a party in the first-class patient drawing-room. this be a rare

opportunity, and the Robin and Garrett put their head together

accordingly.



in the dusk of the evening the Robin take an opportunity and slip a

new key of the back gate into Alfred's hand, and tell he “the trick be

to be do that very night:” he be to get Thompson to go to bed early;

and, instead of take off his clothe, be to wait in readiness. “we

have be ply Hayes already,” say the Robin, “and, as soon as _she_

be off, we shall hocuss he, and get the key; and, while they be all

lark in the drawing-room, off you go to Merrimashee.”



“oh, you dear Robin! you have take my breath away. but how about

Vulcan?”



“oh, we know how to make he amiable: a dog-fancier, a friend of mine,

have provide the ondeniable where dog be concern: whereby Garrett

draw the varmint into the scullery, and shut he in, while I get the

key from the other. _it's_ all right.”



“ah, Robin,” say Alfred, “it sound too good to be true. what? this my

last day here!”



the minute seem to creep very slowly till eight o'clock come. then he

easily persuade David to go to bed; Hayes go up and unlock the

door for they: it close with a catch-lock. Hayes be drunk, but full of

discipline, and insist on the patient put out their clothe; so

Alfred make up a bundle from his portmanteau, and throw it out. Hayes

eye it suspiciously, but be afraid to stoop and inspect it close: for

his drunken instinct tell he he would pitch on his head that moment: so

he retire grumble and dangle his key.



at the end of the corridor he meet Mrs. Archbold full dressed, and with

a candle in her hand. she hold the candle up and inspect he; and a

little conversation follow that sober Mr. Hayes for a minute or two.



Mrs. Archbold be no soon go to her little tea-party than all the

first-class lady and gentleman be send to bed to get a good sleep

for the good of their health, and the keeper and keeperesse take their

place and romp, and make such a row, sleep be not easy within hearing

of they. they sit on the piano, they sing song to a drum accompaniment

play on the table, they dance, drank, flirt, and enjoy themselves

like schoolboy. Hayes alone be gloomy and morose: so the Robin and

Garrett console he, drink with he, and soothe he with the balm

of insensibility: in which condition they remove he under charitable

pretence, and search his pocket in the passage for the key of

Alfred's room.



to their infinite surprise and disappointment it be not upon he.



the fact be, Mrs. Archbold have snatch it from he in her wrath, and

put it in her own pocket. how far her suspicion go, how much her

spy have discover, I really don't know; but somehow or other she be

uneasy in her mind, and, see Hayes in such a state, she would not

trust he during her absence, but take the key away with she.



the Robin and Garrett know nothing of this, and be all abroad, but

they think Rooke must have the key; so they proceed to drink with

he, and be just about to administer a really effective soporific in

his grog, when they and all the merry party be suddenly startle by

violent ring at the bell, and thunder and halloae at the hall

door. the man jump to their foot and balance themselves, and look

half wild, half stupid. the woman sit, and begin to scream: for they have

hear a word that have terror for we all: peculiar terror for they.



this alarm be due to a personage hitherto undervalue in the

establishment.



Mr. Francis Beverley have be think. so now, find all the patient

box up, and their attendant romp in the drawing-room, he light

seven fire, skilfully on the whole, for practice make perfect; but,

singular oversight, he omit one essential ingredient in the fire, and

that be the grate.



to be plain, Mr. Francis make seven bonfire of bed-curtain,

chair, and other combustible in the servant' garret, light they

contemporaneously, and retire to the basement, convince he have take

the sure mean to deliver his friend out of Drayton House: and with a

certain want of candour that characterise the weak, proceed to black

his other bad master's shoe with singular assiduity.



there be no wind to blow the flame; but it be a clear frost; and soon

fiery tongue shoot out of three garret-window into the night, and

lurid gleam burnish four more, and the old house be burn merrily

overhead, and ring with hilarity on the first floor.



but the neighbour see, pointed, wonder, comprehend, shout, rang,

knock, and surge round the iron gate. “Fire! fire! fire!” and “Fire!”

 go down the road, and man on horseback gallop for engine; and the

terror-stricken porter open and the people rush in and hammer at

the hall door, and when Rooke run down and open, “Fire!” be the word

that meet he from a score of eager throat and glitter eye.



“Fire! where?” he cry.



“where! why, _you_ be on fire. blaze!”



he run out and look up at the tongue of flame and volley of smoke.

“shut the gate,” he roar. “call the police. fire! fire!” and he dash

back, and call to the other keeper to unlock all the door they have

key of, run up to the garret to see what could be do. he come out

awe-stricken at what he see. he descend hastily to the third floor.

now the third floor of that wing be occupy principally by servant.

in fact, the only patient at that time be Dodd and Alfred. Rooke

call to the man below to send Hayes up to no. 75 with his key

directly; he then run down to the next floor--of which he have key--and

open all the door, and say to the inmate with a ghastly attempt at

cheerfulness, belie by his shake voice, “get up, gentleman; there

be a ball and supper go on below.” he be afraid to utter the word

“fire” to they. the other keeper be as rapid, each on his beat, and

soon the more rational patient take the alarm and be persuade or

drive out half-dress into the yard, where they cower together in

extremity of fear; for the fire begin to roar overhead like a lion,

and light up the whole interior red and bright. all be scream and

confusion; and then come a struggle to get the incurable out from the

basement story. there be no time to handcuff they. the keeper trust

to the terror of the scene to cow they, and so open the door and get

they out anyhow. wild, weird form, with glare eye and matted hair,

leap out and run into the hall, and laugh, and dance, and curse in

the lurid reflection of the fire above. Hell seem discharge

demon. man recoil from they. and well they do; for now the skylight

explode, and the piece fall tinkle on the marble hall fast as hail.

the crowd recoil and run; but those awful figure continue their

gambol. one pick up the burn glass and grind it in his hand that

bleed directly: but he feel neither burn nor cut. the keeper rush

in to withdraw they from so dangerous a place: all but one obey with

sudden tameness: that one struggle and yell like a demon. in the

midst of which fearful contest come a sudden thundering at a door on the

third floor.



“what be that?” cry Rooke.



“it be Mr. Hardie,” scream the Robin. “you have leave he lock in.”



“I tell Hayes to let he out long ago.”



“but Hayes haven't get the key. you've get it.”



“no, no. I tell you Hayes have get it.”



“no, no! murder! murder! they be dead man. run for Mrs. Archbold,

somebody. run! here, hammer, hammer! for God's sake, come and help I,

break the door. oh, Rooke, Rooke!”



“as I'm a man Hayes have get the key,” cry Rooke, stamp on the

ground, and white with terror.



by this time Garrett have get a hammer, and he and Wales rush wildly up

the stair to batter in the strong door if they could. they get to the

third floor, but with difficulty; the smoke begin to blind they and

choke they, and fiery shower fall on they, and drive they back smarting

and choke. Garrett sink down gasp at the stair-foot. Wales run into

the yard utter pitiful cry, and point wildly upwards; but before

he get there, a hand have break through the glass of a window up in the

third floor, the poor white hand of a perish prisoner, and clutch

the framework and tear at it.



at this hand a thousand white face be now upturned amid groan of

pity and terror, such as only multitude can utter. suddenly those

anxious face and glisten eye turn like one, for an attempt, wild

and unintelligible, but still an attempt, be about to be make to save

that hand and its owner out of the very jaw of death.



now amongst the spectator be one whose life and reason be at stake

on that attempt.





Mrs. Dodd be hurry homeward from this very neighbourhood when the

fire break out. her son Edward be come at nine o'clock to tea,

and, well still, to sleep. he be leave the fire brigade. it have

disappoint he; he find the fire-escape man save the life, the

fireman only the property. he have go into the business earnestly

too; he have invent a thing like a treble pouch hook, which could be

fasten in a moment to the end of a rope, and throw into the window,

and would cling to the bare wall, if there be nothing well, and

enable he to go up and bring life down. but he have never get a chance

to try it; and, _per contra,_ he be on the engine when they went

tear over a woman and break her arm and collar-bone in the

Blackfriars Road; and also when they went tear over their own

fire-dog, and cripple he. all this seem out of character, and

shock Edward; and then his mother could not get over the jacket.



in a quarter of an hour he be to take off the obnoxious jacket for

ever, and be now lounge at the station smoke a short pipe, when a

man gallop up cry “Fire!”



“all right!” say Edward, give a whiff. “where?”



“Lunatic Asylum. Drayton House.”



guess how long before the horse be to, and the engine tear at a

gallop down the road, and the fireman shout “Fire! fire!” to clear

the way, and Edward's voice the loud.



when the report of fire sweep townward past Mrs. Dodd, she turn, and

see the glow.



“oh dear,” say she, “that must be somewhere near Drayton House.” and

full of the tender fear that fill such bosom as hers for those they

love, she could not go home till she have ascertain that it be not

Drayton House. moreover, Edward's be the near station; she have

little hope now of see he to tea. she sigh, and retrace she

step, and make timid inquiry, but could gain no clear information.

presently she hear gallop behind she, and the fireman's wild

sharp cry of fire. an engine draw by two powerful brown horse come

furiously, all on fire itself with red paint and polished steel gleaming

in the light; helmeted man cluster on it, and out of one of these

helmet look a face like a fighting lion's, the eye so dilate, the

countenance in such tower excitement, the figure half rise from

his seat as though gallop be too slow and he want to fly. it

be Edward. Mother and son catch sight of one another as the engine

thunder by, and he give she a solemn ardent look, and point towards

the fire; by that burn look and eloquent gesture she know it be

something more than a common fire. she tremble and could not move. but

this temporary weakness be follow by an influx of wild vigour; she

forget her forty-two year, and fly to hover round the fire as the hen

round water. unfortunately she be too late to get any near than the

road outside the gate, the crowd be so dense. and, while her pale face

and anxious eye, the eye of a wife and a mother, be bent on that

awful fire, the human tide flow swiftly up behind she, and there she

be wedge in. she be allow her foot of ground to stand and look like

the rest--no more. mere unit in that mass of pant humanity, her be

one of the thousand of upturned face lurid in the light of the now

blaze roof. she see with thousand the hand break the window and

clutch the frame; she gasp with the crowd at that terrible and piteous

sight, and her bosom pant for her fellow-creature in sore peril.

but what be this? the mob inside utter a great roar of hope; the crowd

outside strain every eye.



a gleam helmet overtop the outer wall. it be a fireman mount the

great elm-tree in the madhouse yard. the crowd inside burst in a cheer.

he have a rope round his loin; his face be to the tree. he mount and

mount like a cat; high, and high, and higher, till he reach a

branch about twelve foot above the window and as many distant from it

laterally; the crowd cheer he lustily. but Mrs. Dodd, half distract

with terror, implore they not to encourage he. “it be my child!” she

cry despairingly; “my poor reckless darling! come down, Edward; for

your poor mother's sake, come down.”



“dear heart,” say a woman, “it be the lady's son. poor thing!”



“stand on my knee, ma'am,” say a coal-heaver.



“oh no, sir, no. I could not look at he for the world. I can only pray

for he. good people, pray for we!” and she cover her face, and pray

and tremble and sob hysterically. a few yard behind be another

woman, who have arrive later, yet like she be wedge immovable. this

woman be more terror-stricken than Mrs. Dodd; and well she might; for

_she_ know who be behind that fatal window: the woman's name be Edith

Archbold. the flame be now leap through the roof, and surge up

towards heaven in wave of fire six foot high. Edward, scorch and half

blind, manage to fasten his rope to the bough, and, calculate

the distance vertical and lateral he have to deal with, take up rope

accordingly, and launch himself into the air.



the crowd draw their breath so hard it sound like a murmur. to their

horror he miss the window, and go swinge back.
