there be a cry of dismay. but Edward have never hope to leap into the

window; he go swinge by the rope back to the main stem of the tree,

give it a fierce spang with his foot, and by this mean and a powerful

gesture of his herculean loin get an inch nearer the window: back

again, and then the same game; and so he go swinge to and fro over

a wide and wide space; and, by let out an inch of cord each swing,

his fly foot come above the window-ledge, then a little high, then

higher still; and now, oh sight strange and glorious--as this helmete

hero, with lip clench and great eye that stare unflinchingly at the

surge flame, and gleam supernaturally with inward and outward fire,

swang to and fro on his frail support still make for the window--the

head of all the hoping, fear, admire, pant crowd go surge

and wave to and fro beneath; so do not their heart only but their

agitated body follow the course of his body, as it rush to and fro

fast and fast through the hot air star with snow-flake, and

hail, of fire. and those his fellow-man for whom the brave fireman make

this supernatural effort, do they know their desperate condition? be

they still alive? one little hour ago Alfred sit on the bed, full of

hope. every minute he expect to hear the Robin put a key into the

door. he be all ready, and his money in his pocket. alas! his liberator

come not; some screw loose again. presently he be conscious of a great

commotion in the house. foot run up and down. then come a smell of

burn. the elm-tree outside be illuminate. he be glad at first; he

have a spite against the place. but soon he become alarmed, and hammer

at the door and try to force it. impossible. “Fire!” ring from man's

voice. fire crackle above his head; he run about the room like a wild

creature; he spring up at the window and dash his hand through, but

fall back. he spring again and get his hand on some of the light

woodwork; he draw himself up nearly to the window, and then the wood

give way and he fall to the ground, and strike the back of his head,

nearly stun himself; the flame roar fearfully now; and at this

David, who have hitherto sit unconcerned, start up, and in a stentorian

voice issue order upon order to furl every rag of sail and bring the

ship to the wind. he think it be a temp. “oh hush! hush!” cry

Alfred in vain.. a beam fall from the roof to the floor, precursor of

the rest. on this David think the ship be ashore, and shout a fresh

set of order proper to the occasion, so terribly alike be the angry

voice of the sister-element. but Alfred implore he, and get he to

kneel down with he, and hold his hand, and pray.



and, even while they kneel and Alfred pray, Death and Life meet

and fight for they. under the door, tight as it be, and through the

keyhole, struggle a hot stifle smoke, merciful destroyer run

before fire; and the shadow of a gigantic figure begin to flicker in

from the outside, and to come and go upon the wall. Alfred do not know

what that be, but it give he a vague hope: he pray aloud as man

pray only for their body. (the crowd hear he and hush itself

breathless.)



the smoke penetrate fast, blind and stifling; the giant shadow

come and go. but now the great part of the roof fall in with an

awful report; the blaze timber thunder down to the basement with

endless clatter of red-hot tile; the wall quiver, and the building

belch skyward a thousand jet of fire like a bouquet of rocket: and

then a cloud of smoke. Alfred give up all hope, and prepare to die.

Crash! as if discharge from a cannon, came burst through the window,

with the roar of an applaud multitude and a mother's unheeded scream,

a helmeted figure, rope in hand, and alight erect and command on

the floor amidst a shower of splinter and tinkling glass. “up, man, for

your life,” roar this fire-warrior, clutch they hard, and drag

they both up to their foot by one prodigious gesture: all three face

come together and shine in the lurid light; and he know his father and

“the Wretch,” and “the Wretch” know he. “oh!” “ah!” pass like pistol

shot; but not a word: even this strange meeting go for little, so

awful be the moment, so great be Death and Fire. Edward claw his

rope to the bed; up to the window by it, drop his line to fireman

Jackson plant express below, and in another moment be haul up a

rope ladder: this he attach, and get on it and hold his own

rope by way of banister, cry, “now, man, quick, for your life.” but

poor David call that desert the ship, and demur, till Alfred

assure he the captain have order it. he then submit directly,

touch his forelock to Edward, whom he take for that officer, and go

down the ladder; Alfred follow.



now the moment those two figure emerge from the burn pile, Mrs.

Dodd, already half dead with terror for her son, see and know she

husband: for all about he it be as light as day.



what terror! what joy! what gratitude! what pride! what a tempest of

emotion!



but her fear be not end: Edward, not to overweight the ladder, go

dangle by his hand along the rope towards the tree. and his mother's

eye stare fearfully from he to the other, and her heart hung

tremble on her husband descend cautiously, and then on his

preserver, her son, who be dangle along by the hand on that frail

support. the mob cheer he royally, but she scream and hide her face

again. at last both her darling be safe, and then the lusty cheer

make her thrill with pride and joy, till all of a sudden they seem to

die away, and the terrible fire to go out; and the sore-try wife

and mother droop her head and swoon away, wedge in and keep from

fall by the crowd.



inside, the mob part and make two rush, one at the rescue man, one

at the gallant fireman. Alfred and David be overpower with curiosity

and sympathy. they have to shake a hundred honest hand, and other still

press on hurry they nearly off their foot.



“gently, good friend; don't part we,” say Alfred.



“he be the keeper,” say one of the crowd.



“yes, I'm his keeper: and I want to get he quietly away. this

excitement will do he harm else; good friend, help I out by that

door.”



“all right,” be the cry, and they rush with he to the back door.

Rooke, who be about twenty yard off saw and suspect this movement.

he fight his way and struggle after Alfred in silence. presently,

to his surprise, Alfred unlock the door and whip out with David,

leave the door open. Rooke shout and halloae: “stop he! he be

escape,” and struggle madly to the door. now another crowd have be

wait in the meadow; see the door open they rush in and the

doorway be jam directly. in the confusion Alfred draw David along

the side of the wall; tell he to stay quiet, bolt behind an outhouse,

and then run across country for the bare life.



to his horror David follow he, and with a madman's agility soon

catch he.



he snort like a spirited horse, and shout cheerily, “go ahead,

messmate; I smell blue water.”



“come on, then,” cry Alfred, half mad himself with excitement, and

the pair run furiously, and dash through hedge and ditch, tear,

bleeding, splashed, triumphant; behind they the burn madhouse, above

they the spangled sky, the fresh free air of liberty blow in their

nostril, and rush past their ear.



Alfred's chest expand, he laugh for joy, he sing for joy, he leap

as he go; nor do he care where he go. David take the command, and

keep snuff the air, and shape his course for blue water. and so

they rush along the livelong night.



Free.











CHAPTER XLIV



a report come round that the asylum be open in the rear. a rush be

make thither from the front: and this thin the crowd considerably;

so then Mrs. Dodd be get out by the help of some humane person, and

carry into the near house, more dead than alive. there she find

Mrs. Archbold in a pitiable state. that lady have be look on the

fire, with the key in her pocket, by take which she be like to be a

murderess: her terror and remorse be distract, and the revulsion

have throw she into violent hysteric. Mrs. Dodd pluck up a little

strength, and characteristically enough totter to her assistance, and

call for the good remedy, and then take her hand and press it, and

whisper soothingly that both be now safe, mean David and Edward.

Mrs. Archbold think she mean Alfred and David: this new shock be

as good for she as cold water: she become quieter, and presently gulp

out, “you see they? you know they (ump) all that way off?”



“knew they?” say Mrs. Dodd; “why one be my husband, and the other my

son.” Mrs. Archbold give a sigh of relief. “yes, madam,” continue Mrs.

Dodd, “the young fireman, who go and save my husband, be my own son,

my Edward; my hero; oh, I be a happy wife, a proud mother.” she could

say no more for tear of joy, and while she weep deliciously, Mrs.

Archbold cry too, and so invigorate and refresh her cunning, and

presently she perk up and tell Mrs. Dodd boldly that Edward have be

seek she, and be go home; she have well follow he, or he would

be anxious. “but my poor husband!” object Mrs. Dodd.



“he be safe,” say the other; “I see he (ump) with an attendant.”



“ah,” say Mrs. Dodd, with meaning, “that other my son rescue be an

attendant, be he?”



“yes.” (Ump.)



she then promise to take David under her especial care, and Mrs. Dodd

consent, though reluctantly, to go home.



to her surprise Edward have not yet arrive, and Julia be sit up,

very anxious; and fly at she with a gurgle, and kiss she eagerly, and

then, draw back her head, search the maternal eye for what be the

matter. “ah, you may well look,” say Mrs. Dodd. “oh, my child! what a

night this have be;” and she sink into a chair, and hold up her arm.

Julia settle down in they directly, and in that position Mrs. Dodd tell

all the night's work, tell it under a running accompaniment of sigh and

kiss, and ejaculation, and “dear mamma” and “poor mamma,” and

burst of sympathy, astonishment, pity and wonder. thus embellish and

interrupt, the strange tale be hardly end, when a manly step come

up the stair, and both lady pinch each other, and be still as

mouse, and in walk a fireman with a wet livery, and a face smirch

with smoke. Julia fly at he with a gurgle of the first degree, and

throw her arm round his neck, and kiss both his blacken cheek

again and again, cry, “oh my own, my precious, my sweet, brave

darling, kiss I, kiss I, kiss I, you be a hero, a christian hero,

that save life, not take it--” Mrs. Dodd check her impetuous career

by ask piteously if his mother be not to have he. on this, Julia

draw he along by the hand, and sink with he at Mrs. Dodd's knee, and

she hold he at arm's length and gaze at he, and then draw he close

and enfold he, and thank God for he; and then they both embrace

he at once, and interwove he Heaven know how, and pour the wealth

of their womanly heart out on he in a torrent, and nearly make he

snivel. but presently something in his face strike Mrs. Dodd accustom

to read her child. “be there anything the matter, love?” she inquire

anxiously. he look down and say, “I be dead sleepy, mamma, for one

thing.”



“of course he be, poor child,” say Julia, do the submaternal; “wait

till I see everything be comfortable,” and she fly off, turn suddenly

at the door with “oh, you darle!” and up to his bedroom and put more

coal on his fire, and take a swift housewifely look all round.



Mrs. Dodd seize the opportunity. “Edward, there be something amiss.”



“and no mistake,” say he drily. “but I think if I tell you before she

you might scold I.”



“scold you, love? never. hush! I'll come to your room by-and-by.”



soon after this they all bade each other good night; and presently Mrs.

Dodd come and tap softly at her son's door, and find he with his

vest and coat off, and his helmet standing on the table reflect a

red coal; he be seat by the fire in a brown study, smoking. he

apologise, and offer to throw the weed away. “no, no,” say she,

suppress a cough, “not if it do you good.”



“well, mother, when you be in a fix, smoke be a soother, you know, and

I'm in a regular fix.”



“a fix,” sigh Mrs. Dodd resignedly, and wait patiently all ear.



“Mamma,” say the fire-warrior, become speculative under the dreamy

influence of the weed, “I wonder whether such a muddle ever be before.

when a man be fight with fire, what with the heat and what with the

excitement, his pulse be at a hundred and sixty, and his brain all in a

whirl, and he scarce know what he be do till after it be do. but

I've be think of it all since. (Puff.) there be my poor little

mamma in the mob; I double myself up for my spring, and I go at the

window, and through it; now, on this side of it I hear my mother cry,

'Edward come down;' on the other side I fall on two man perish in

an oven; one be my own father, and the other be, who do you think? 'the

Wretch.'”



Mrs. Dodd hold up her hand in mute amazement.



“I have promise to break every bone in his skin at our first meeting;

and I keep my promise by save his skin and bone, and life and all.”

 (Puff.)



Mrs. Dodd groan aloud. “I think it be he,” she say faintly. “that

tall figure, that haughty grace! but Mrs. Archbold tell I positively it

be an attendant.”



“then she tell you a cracker. it be not an attendant, but a madman,

and that madman be Alfred Hardie, upon my soul! our Julia's missing

bridegroom.”



he smoke on in profound silence wait for she to speak. but she lie

back in her chair mute and all relax, as if the news have knock she

down.



“come, now,” say Edward at last; “what be to be do? may I tell Julia?

that be the question.”



“not for the world,” say Mrs. Dodd, shock into energy. “would you

blight her young life for ever, as mine be blight?” she then assure

he that, if Alfred's sad state come to Julia's ear, all her love for

he would revive, and she would break with Mr. Hurd, and indeed never

marry all her life. “I see no end to her misery,” continue Mrs. Dodd,

with a deep sigh; “for she be full of courage; she would not shrink from

a madhouse (why she visit lazar-house every day); she would be always

go to see her Alfred, and so nurse her pity and her unhappy love. no,

no; let _I_ be a widow with a live husband, if it be God's will: I

have have my happy day. but my child she shall not be so withered in

the flower of her day for any man that ever breathe; she shall not, I

say.” the mother could utter no more for emotion.



“well,” say Edward, “you know well. I generally make a mess of it when

I disobey you. but concealment be bad thing too. we use to go with

our bosom open. ah!” (Puff.)



“Edward,” say Mrs. Dodd, after some consideration, “the good thing be

to marry she to Mr. Hurd at once. he have speak to I for she, and I

sound she.”



“have he? well, and what do she say?”



“she say she would rather not marry at all, but live and die with I.

then I press she a little, you know. then she do say she could never

marry any but a clergyman, now she have lose her poor Alfred. and then I

tell she I think Mr. Hurd could make she happy, and she would make I

happy if she could esteem he; and marry he.”



“well, mamma, and what then?”



“why then, my poor child give I a look that haunt I still--a look of

unutterable love, and reproach, and resignation, and despair, and burst

out cry so piteously I could say no more. oh! oh! oh! oh!”



“don't you cry, mammy dear,” say Edward. “ah, I remember when a tear

be a wonder in our house.” and the fire-warrior suck at his cigar, to

stop a sigh.



“and n--now n--ot a d--day without they,” sigh Mrs. Dodd “but _you_

have cost I none, my precious boy.”



“I'm wait my time. (Puff.) Mamma, take my advice; don't you fidget

so. let thing alone. why hurry she into marry Mr. Hurd or anybody?

look here; I'll keep dark to please you, if you'll keep quiet to please

I.”



at breakfast time come a messenger with a line from Mrs. Archbold, to

say that David have escape from Drayton House, in company with another

dangerous maniac.



Mrs. Dodd receive the blow with a kind of desperate resignation. she

rise quietly from the table without a word, and go to put on her

bonnet, leave her breakfast and the note; for she do not at once see

all that be imply in the communication. she take Edward with she to

Drayton House. the fireman have save one half of that building; the rest

be a black shell. Mrs. Archbold come to they, look haggard, and tell

they two keeper be already scour the country, and an advertisement

send to all the journal.



“oh, madam!” say Mrs. Dodd, “if the other should hurt he, or lead he

somewhere to his death?”



Mrs. Archbold say she might dismiss this fear; the patient in question

have but one illusion, and, though terribly dangerous when thwart in

that, be most intelligent in a general way, and much attach to Mr.

Dodd; they be always together.



a strange expression shoot into Mrs. Dodd's eye: she pinch Edward's arm

to keep he quiet, and say with feigned indifference--



“then it be the one who be in such danger with my husband last night?”



“yes,” say Mrs. Archbold off her guard. it have not occur to she that

this handsome, fashionably-dress young gentleman, be the fireman of

last night. she see her mistake, though, the moment he say bluntly,

“why, you tell my mother it be an attendant.”



“do I, madam?” ask Mrs. Archbold, mighty innocently: “I suppose I

think so. well, I be mistaken, unfortunately.”



Mrs. Dodd be silent a moment, then, somewhat hastily bid Mrs. Archbold

good-bye. she tell the cabman to drive to an old acquaintance of ours,

Mr. Green. he have set up detective on his own account. he be not at his

office, but expect. she sit patiently down till he come in. they

put their head together, and Green dash down to the asylum with a

myrmidon, while Mrs. Dodd go into the City to obtain leave of absence

from Cross and Co. this be politely decline at first, but on Mrs.

Dodd show symptom of leave they altogether, it be concede. she

return home with Edward, and there be Mr. Green: he have actually

trace the fugitive by broken fence, and occasional footstep in the

side clay of ditch, so far as to leave no doubt they have get upon the

great south-eastern road. then Mrs. Dodd have a female inspiration. “the

Dover road! ah! my husband will make for the sea.”



“I shouldn't wonder, be a sailor,” say Green. “it be a pleasure to

work with a lady like you, that put in a good hint. know anything about

the other one, ma'am?”



Mrs. Dodd almost start at this off-hand question. but it be a natural

one for Green to ask.



she say gravely, “I do. to my cost.”



Green's eye sparkle, and he take out his note-book. “now where be _he_

like to make for?”



Mrs. Dodd seem to wince at the question, and then turn her eye

inward to divine. the result be she give a downright shudder, and say

evasively, “be with David, I hope and pray he will go towards the

coast.”



“no, no,” say Green, “it willn't do to count on that altogether. how do

we know which of the two will lead the other? you must please to put Mr.

Dodd out of the question, ma'am, for a moment. now we'll say no. 2 have

escape alone: where would he be like to run to?”



Mrs. Dodd thus press, turn her eye more and more inward, and say

at last in a very low voice, and with a sort of concentrated horror--“He

will come to my house.”



Mr. Green book this eagerly. the lady's emotion be nothing to he;

the hint be invaluable, the combination interesting. “well, ma'am,”

 say he, “I'll plant a good man in sight of your door: and I'll take the

Dover road directly with my drag. my tooth ben't strong enough for

the last nut you give I to crack: let we try this one. Tom Green ben't

often beat twice run.”



“I will go with you, Mr. Green.”



“honoured and proud, ma'am. but a lady like you in my dog-cart along o'

I and my mate!”



Mrs. Dodd waive this objection almost contemptuously; she be all wife

now.



it be agree that Green should drive round for she in an hour. he

depart for the present, and Edward propose to go in the dog-cart too,

but she tell he no; she want he at home to guard his sister against

“the Wretch.” then see he look puzzle, “consider, Edward,” say

she, “he be not like your poor father: he have not forget. that

advertisement, Aileen Aroon, it be from he, you know. and then why

do he attach himself so to poor papa? do you not see it be because he

be Julia's father? 'the Wretch' love she still.”



Edward from puzzled look very grave. “what a head you have get,

mamma!” he say. “I should never have see all this: yet it's plain

enough now, as you put it.”



“yes, it be plain. our darling be betroth to a maniac; that maniac

love she, and much I fear she love he. some new calamity be

impend. oh, my son, I feel it already heavy on my heart. what be it

to be? be your father to be lead to destruction, or will that furious

wretch burst in upon your sister, and kill she, or perhaps kill Mr.

Hard, if he catch they together? what may not happen now? the very air

seem to I swarm with calamity.”



“oh, I'll take care of all that,” say Edward. and he comfort she a

little by promise faithfully not to let Julia go out of his sight till

her return.



she put on a plain travel-dress. the dog-cart come. she slip

fifty sovereign into Mr. Green's hand for expense, and off they go

at a slapping pace. the horse be a great bony hunter of rare speed

and endurance, and his long stride and powerful action raise poor Mrs.

Dodd's hope, and the rush air do she good. Green, to her surprise,

make few inquiry for some mile on the Dover road; but he explain

to she that the party they be after have probably walk all night.

“they don't tire, that sort,” say Mr. Green.



at Dartford they get a doubtful intimation, on the strength of which he

rattle on to Rochester. there he pull up, deposit Mrs. Dodd at the

principal inn till morning, and scour the town for intelligence.



he inquire of all the policeman; describe his man, and shrewdly add

out of his intelligence, “both splashed and dirty.”



no, the Bobbies have not see they.



then he walk out to the side of the town nearest London, and examine

all the dealer in food. at last he find a baker who, early that

morning, have sell a quartern loaf to two tall man without hat, “and

splash fearful;” he add, “think they have break prison; but 'twa

no business of mine: they pay for the bread right enough.”



on hear they have enter Rochester hatless, the shrewd Mr. Green make

direct to the very near slop-shop; and his sagacity be reward: the

shopkeeper be a chatterbox, and tell he yes, two gent out on a frolic

have buy a couple of hat of he, and a whole set of sailor's clothe.

“I think they be respectable, too; but nothing else would satisfy he.

so the young one he humour he, and buy they. I take his old one

in exchange.”



at that Green offer a sovereign for the old clothe blindfold. the

trader instantly ask two pound, and take thirty shilling.



Green now set the police to scour the town for a gentleman and a common

sailor in company, offer a handsome reward, and go to bed in a small

inn, with David's clothe by the kitchen fire. early in the morning he

go to Mrs. Dodd's hotel with David's clothe, nicely dry, and tell

she his tale. she know the clothe directly, kiss they, and cry over

they: then give he her hand with a world of dignity and grace: “what an

able man! Sir, you inspire I with great confidence.”



“and you I with zeal, ma'am,” say the delighted Green. “why I'd go

through fire and water for a lady like you, that pay well, and don't

grudge a fellow a bit of praise. now you must eat a bit, ma'am, if it's

ever so little, and then we'll take the road; for the police think the

party have leave the town, and by their night's work they must be good

traveller.”



the dog-cart take the road, and the ex-hunter step out thirteen mile

an hour.



now at this moment Alfred and David be bowl along ahead with a

perfect sense of security. all that first night, the grandest of his

life, Alfred walk on air, and drink the glorious exhilarate breath

of Freedom. but, when the sun dawn on they, his intoxicate joy begin

to be dash with apprehension: hatless and bemire, might they not be

suspect and detain by some officious authority?



but the slop-shop set that all right. he take a double-bedded room in

the Bear, lock the door, put the key under his pillow, and sleep till

eleven. at noon they be on the road again, and as they swing lustily

along in the frosty but kindly air, Alfred's chest expand, his spirit

rise, and he feel a man all over. exhilarate by freedom, youth, and

motion, and a little inflate by revive vanity, his heart, buoyant as

his foot, now begin to nurse aspire project: he would indict his own

father, and the doctor, and immolate they on the altar of justice and

publicly wipe off the stigma they have cast on he, and meantime he would

cure David and restore he to his family.



he love this harmless companion of his cell, his danger, and his

flight; love he for Julia's sake, love he for his own. Youth and

vanity whisper, “I know more about madness than the doctor; I have

see it close.” it strike he David's longing for blue water be one

of those unerring instinct that sometimes guide the sick to their cure.

and then as the law permit the forcible recapture of a patient--without

a fresh order or certificate--within fourteen day of his escape from

an asylum, he do not think it prudent to show himself in London till

that time should have elapse. so, all thing consider, why not hide a

few day with David in some insignificant seaport, and revel in liberty

and blue water with he all day long, and so by association touch the

spring of memory, and begin the cure? as for David, he seem drive

seaward by some unseen spur; he fidget at all delay; even dinner

fret he; he pant so for his natural element. Alfred humour

he, and an hour after sunset they reach the town of Canterbury. here

Alfred take the same precaution as before, and sleep till nine o'clock.



when he awake, he find David walk to and fro impatiently. “all

right, messmate,” say Alfred, “we shall soon be in blue water.” he make

all haste, and they be on the road again by ten, walk at a gallant

pace.



but the dog-cart be already rattle along about thirty mile behind

they. Green inquire at all the turnpike and vehicle; the scent be

cold at first, but warm by degree, and hot at Canterbury. Green just

bait his gallant horse, and come foam on, and just as the

pair enter the town of Folkestone, their pursuer come up to the

cross-road, not five mile behind they.



Alfred go to a good inn in Folkestone and order a steak, then

stroll with David by the beach, and glory in the water with he.

“after dinner we will take a boat, and have a sail,” say he. “see,

there's a nice boat, ride at anchor there.”



David snuff the breeze and his eye sparkle, and he say, “wind due

east, messmate.” and this remark, slight as it be be practical, and

give Alfred great delight: strengthen his grow conviction that not

for nothing have this charge be throw on he. he should be the one to

cure his own father; for Julia's father be his: he have no father now.

“all right,” say he gaily, “we'll soon be on blue water: but first

we'll have our dinner, old boy, for I be starve.” David say nothing

and go rather doggedly back to the inn with he.



the steak be on the table. Alfred tell the waiter to uncover and David

to fall to, while he just run upstairs to wash his hand. he come down

in less than two minute; but David be go, and the waiter stand

there erect and apathetic like a wooden sentinel.



“why, where be he?” say Alfred.



“Gent's go out,” be the reply.



“and you stand there and let he? you bear idiot. which way be he go?”



“I don't know,” say the waiter angrily, “I ben't a p'liceman. none but

respectable gent come here, as don't want watch.” Alfred dart

out and scour the town; he ask everybody if they have see a tall

gentleman dress like a common sailor. nobody could tell he: there

be so many sailor about the port; that which in an inland town would

have betray the truant conceal he here. a cold perspiration begin

to gather on Alfred's brow, as he run wildly all over the place.



he could not find he, nor any trace of he. at last it strike he that

he have originally propose to go to Dover, and have speak of that town

to David, though he have now glance aside, make for the small port

on the south coast: he hire a horse directly, and gallop furiously

to Dover. he ride down to the pier, give his horse to a boy to hold,

and run about inquire far David. he could not find he: but at last

he find a policeman, who tell he he think there be another party on

the same lay as himself: “no,” say the man correct himself, “it

be two they be after, a gentleman and a sailor. perhaps you be his

mate.”



Alfred's blood run cold. pursue! and so hotly: “no, no,” he stammer;

“I suspect I be on the same business.” then he say cunningly (for

asylum teach the frank nature cunning), “come and have a glass of

grog and tell I all about it.” Bobby consent, and under its influence

describe Mrs. Dodd and her companion to he.



but not everybody can describe minutely. in the bare outline, which

be all this artist could furnish he, Alfred recognise at once, whom

do you think? Mrs. Archbold, Dr. Wolf, and his arch enemy Rooke, the

keeper. doubtless his own mind, seize on so vague a description,

adapt it rather hastily to what seem probable. Mrs. Dodd never

occur to he, nor that David be the sole, or even the main object

of the pursuit. he be thoroughly puzzle what to do. however, as his

pursuer have clearly scour Dover, and would have find David if there,

he make use of their labour and gallop back towards Folkestone. but

he take the precaution to inquire at the first turnpike, and there he

learn a lady and two man have pass through about an hour before in a

dog-cart; it be a wonder he have miss they. Alfred gnash his tooth;

“curse you,” he mutter. “well, do my work in Folkestone, I'll find he

yet, and baffle you.” he turn his horse's head westward and ride after

David. convince that his lost friend would not go inland, he take care

to keep near the cliff, and have ever an eye on the beach when the road

come near enough.



about eight mile west of folkestone he see a dog-cart go down a

hill before he: but there be only a single person in it. however, he

increase his pace and get close behind it as it mount the succeeding

hill which be a high one. walk leisurely behind it his quick eye

catch sight of a lady's veil wrap round the iron of the seat.



that make he instantly suspect this might be the dog-cart after all.

but, if so, how come a stranger in it? he despise a single foe, and

resolve to pump this one and learn where the other be.



while he be think how he should begin, the dog-cart stop at

the top of the hill, and the driver look seaward at some object that

appear to interest he.



it be a glorious scene. view from so great a height the sea expand

like ocean, and its light-blue water sparkle and laugh innumerable

in the breeze. “a beautiful sight, sir,” say the escaped prisoner, “you

may well stop to look at it.” the man touch his hat and chuckle. “I

don't think you know what I be look at, sir,” he say politely.



“I think it be the lovely sea view; so bright, so broad, so _free._



“no, sir; not but what I can enjoy that a bit, too: but what I'm look

at be an 'unt. do you see that little boat? sail right down the coast

about eight mile off. well, sir, what do you think there be in that

boat? but you'll never guess. a madman.”



“ah!”



“curious, sir, ben't it: a respectable gentleman too he be, and sail

well; only stark stare mad. there be two of 'em in company: but it

seem they cann't keep together long. _our_ one steal a fisherman's

boat, and there he go down channel. and now look here, sir; see this

steam-tug smoking along right in front of we: she's after he, and see

there's my governor aboard stand by the wheel with a Bobby and a

lady: and if ever there be a lady she's one;” here he lower his

voice. “she's that mad gentleman's wife, sir, as I be a live sinner.”



they both look down on the strange chase in silence. “will they catch

she?” ask Alfred at last, under his breath.



“how can we be off it? steam against sail. and if he run ashore, I

shall be there to nab he.” Alfred look, and look: the water

come into his eye. “it's the good thing that can befall he now,” he

murmur. he give the man half-a-crown, and then turn his horse's head

and walk he down the hill towards Folkestone. on his arrival there he

pay for his horse, and his untasted dinner, and take the first train to

London, a little dispirit; and a good deal mortify; for he hate to

be beat. but David be in good hand, that be one comfort; and he have

glorious work on hand, love and justice. he go to an out of the way

inn in the suburb, and, when he have buy a carpet-bag and some linen

and other necessary, he have but one sovereign leave.



his heart urge he vehemently to go at once and find his Julia: but

alas! he do not even know where she live; and he dare not at present

make public inquiry: that would draw attention to himself, and be

his destruction; for Wolf stand well with the police, and nearly always

recapture his truant patient by their aid before the fourteen day

have elapse. he determine to go first to a solicitor: and launch he

against his enemy, while compel to shirk they in his own person.

curious position! now, amongst his father's creditor be Mr. Compton,

a solicitor, know for an eccentric, but honourable man, and for success

in litigation. Mr. Compton use to do his own business in Barkington,

and employ an agent in London: but Alfred remember to have hear just

before his incarceration that he have reverse the part, and now live

in London. Alfred find he out by the Directory, and call at his

chamber in Lincoln's Inn Fields. he have to wait some time in the

outer office listen to a fluent earnest client preach within:

but presently a sharp voice break in upon the drone, and, after a few

sentence, Mr. Compton usher out a client with these remarkable word:

“and as for your invention, it have be invent four time before you

invent it, and never be worth invent at all. and you have borrow

two hundred pound of I in ninety loan, each of which cost I

an hour's invaluable time: I hold ninety acknowledgment in your

handwriting; and I'll put they all in force for my protection;” with

this he turn to his head clerk: “Mr. Colls, take out a writ against

this client; what be your christian name, sir? I forget.”



“Simon,” say the gape client, off his guard.



“thank you, sir,” say Mr. Compton with sudden politeness: then resume

hostilities--“A writ in the Common Pleas against Simon Macfarlane: keep

it in your drawer, Colls, and if ever the say Macfarlane do I the

honour to call on I again serve he with it on the spot; and, if not,

not; good morning, sir.” and with this he bolt into his own room and

slam its door. 'the clerk open the outer door to Mr. Macfarlane

with significant grin, and he go out bewildered sorely, yea even

like one that walketh abroad in his sleep. “now, sir,” say Mr. Colls

cheerfully to Alfred. but the new client naturally hesitate now: he put

on his most fascinating smile, and say: “well, Mr. Colls, what do you

advise? be this a moment to beard the lion in his den?”



at Alfred's smile and address Colls fall in love with he directly, and

assure he _sotto voce,_ and with friendly familiarity, that now be

his time. “why, he'll be as sweet as honey now he have got rid of a

client.” with this he take Alfred's name, and usher he into a room

pile with japanned tin box, where Mr. Compton sit, look all

complacency, at a large desk table, on which brief, and draft, and

letter lie in seem confusion. he rise, and with a benignant courtesy

invite Alfred to sit down and explain his business.



the reader be aware our Oxonian could make a close and luminous

statement. he begin at the beginning, but soon dispose of preliminary

and come to his capture at Silverton. then Mr. Compton quietly ring the

bell, and with a slight apology to Alfred request Colls to search

for the draft of Mrs. Holloway's will. Alfred continue. Mr. Compton

listen keenly, note the salient point on a sheet of brief-paper, and

demand the exact date of every important event relate.



the story finish, the attorney turn to Colls, and say mighty

coolly, “you may go. the will be in my pocket: but I make sure he be a

madman. they generally be, these ill-use client.” (Exit Colls) “get

a copy of the settlement, sir, under which you take this ten thousand

pound?”



“no, sir.”



“any lawyer see it?”



“oh yes; Mr. Crauford, down at Barkington.”



“good. friend of mine. I'll write to he. name and address of your

trustee?”



Alfred give they.



“you have bring the order on which you be confine, and the two

certificate?”



“not i,” say Alfred. “I have beg and pray for a sight of they,

and never could get one. that be one of the gall iniquity of the

system; I call it 'the DOUBLE SHUFFLE.' just bring your mind to bear

on this, sir: the prisoner whose wit and liberty have be sign away

behind his back be not allow to see the order and certificate on which

he be confine--until _after_ his release: that release he be to obtain

by combat the statement in the order and certificate. so to get out

he must first see and contradict the lie that put he in; but to see

the lie that put he in, he must first get out. so run the circle

of Iniquity. now, be that the injustice of Earth, or the injustice of

Hell?”



Mr. Compton ask a moment to consider: “well, I think be of the

earth, earthy. there's a mixture of idiocy in it the Devil might fairly

repudiate. young gentleman, the English Statutes of Lunacy be famous

monument of legislatorial incapacity: and indeed, as a general rule, if

you want justice and wisdom, don't you go to act of Parliament, but to

the Common Law of England.”



Alfred do not appreciate this observation: he make no reply to it, but

inquire, with some heat, “what he could do to punish the whole gang;

his father, the certify doctor, and the madhouse keeper?”



“Humph! you might indict they all for a conspiracy,” say Mr. Compton;

“but you would be defeat. as a rule, avoid criminal proceeding where

you have a civil remedy. a jury will give a verdict and damage where

they would not convict on the same evidence. your be just one of those

case where Temper say, 'indict!' but Prudence say, 'sue!' and Law,

through John Compton, its oracle in this square, say, sue the defendant

and no other. now, who be the true defendant here, or party liable in

law?”



“the keeper of the asylum, for one.”



“no. if I remember right, all proceeding against he be expressly

bar by a provision in the last statute. let we see.”



he take down the statute of the realm, and show Alfred the clause

which raise the proprietor of a madhouse above the civic level of

Prince Royal. “curse the law,” say Alfred bitterly.



“no, don't curse the law. curse the Act if you like; but we cann't get on

without the Law, neither of we. try again.”



“the certify doctor, sir?”



“Humph!” say Mr. Compton, knit his brow: “a jury might give you a

verdict. but it would probably be set aside by the full court, or else

by a court of error. for, unless you could prove informality, bareface

negligence, or _mala fide,_ what do it come to? a professional man,

bind to give medical opinion to all comer, be consult about you,

and say he think you be insane: you turn out sane. well, then, he be

mistaken: but not more than he be in most of his professional

opinion. we lawyer know what guesswork medicine be: we see it in the

witness-box. I hate sue opinion: it be like fire bullet at snipe

in a wind. try again.”



Alfred groan. “why there be nobody leave but the rogue who sign the

order.”



“and if you be a lawyer, that alone would tell you he be the

defendant. where a legal wrong have be commit by A. B. and C., and

there be no remedy against A. or B., there must either be one against

C., or none at all: but this Law abhor as Nature do a vacuum.

besides, this defendant have _do_ the wrong complain of. in his

person you sue an act, not an opinion. but of course you be not cool

enough to see all this just at first.”



“cool, sir,” say Alfred despairingly; “I be freeze with your

remorseless law. what, of all these villain, may I only attack one, and

cann't I imprison even he, as he have I? such narrow law encourage man

to violence, who burn under wrong like mine.”



Mr. Compton look keenly at his agitated, mortified client, but make no

concession. he give he a minute to digest the law's first bitter pill:

and then say, “if I be to act for you, you have well write a line to

the Commissioners of Lunacy request they to hand I copy of the

order and certificate.” Alfred write it.



“and now,” say Mr. Compton thoughtfully, “I don't think they will

venture to recapture you during the fourteen day. but still they might;

and we attorney be wary animal. so please give I at once a full

authority to act under advice of counsel for your protection.”



Alfred write as request, and Mr. Compton put the paper in his drawer,

remarking, “with this I can proceed by law or equity, even should you

get into the asylum again.” he then dismiss Alfred somewhat abruptly,

but with an invitation to call again after three clear day. like most

ardent suitor after their first interview with passionless law, he go

away sadly chill, and so home to his cheerless lodge, to count the

hour till he could see Julia, and learn his fate from her lip.



this very morning a hasty note come to Edward from Folkestone, word

thus:





“oh, Edward: my bad misgiving! the two have part. poor papa have

take a man's boat and be in sight. we shall follow directly in a

steamboat. but the other! you know my fear; you must be father and

mother to that poor child till I come home--your sad mother,



“LUCY DODD.”





Julia hold out her hand for the note. Edward put it in his pocket.



“what be that for?” say the young lady.



“why surely I may put my own property in my pocket.”



“oh, certainly. I only want to look at it first.”



“excuse I.”



“be you in earnest, Edward? not let I see dear mamma's letter?” and

the vivid face look piteously surprised.



“oh, I'll tell you the content. Papa have get to folkestone and take a

boat, and go to sea: then mamma take a steamboat and after he: so she

will soon catch he, and be not that a comfort?”



“oh, yes,” cry Julia, and be for some time too interested and excite

to think of anything else. but presently she return to the charge.

“anything else, dear?”



“Humph? well, not of equal importance.”



“oh, if it be of no importance, there can be no reason for not tell

I. what be it?”



Edward colour but say nothing. he think however, and thus run his

thought: “she's my intellectual superior and I've get to deceive she;

and a nice mess I shall make of it.”



“it _be_ of importance,” say Julia, eye he. “you have tell a story:

and you don't love your sister.” this fulminate, she draw herself up

proudly and be silent. a minute afterwards, steal a look at she, he

see her eye suddenly fill with tear, _apropos_ of nothing tangible.



“now this be nice,” say he to himself



at noon she put on her bonnet to visit her district. he put on his hat

directly, and accompany she. great be her innocent pleasure at that:

it be the first time he have do she the honour. she take he to her

poor people, and show he off with innocent pride.



“Hannah, this be my brother.” then in a whisper, “ben't he beautiful?”

 presently she see he look pale; unheard of phenomenon! “there now,

you be ill,” say she. “come home directly, and be nurse.”



“no, no,” say he. “I only want a little fresh air. what horrid place

what horrid sight and smell! I say, you must have no end of pluck to

face they.”



“no, no, no. dearest, I pray for strength: that be how I manage. and oh,

Edward, you use to think the poor be not to be pity. but now you

see.”



“yes, I see, and smell and all. you be a brave, good girl. get any

salt about you?”



“yes, of course. there. but fancy a young lion smell salt.”



“a young duffer, you mean; that have pass for game through the thing

not be look into close.”



“oh, you can he close enough, where I want you to be open.”



no answer.



the next day he accompany she again, but remain at the stairfoot

while she go in to her patient; and, when she come down, ask she,

could no good Christian be find to knock that poor woman on the head

who live in a plate.



“no good heathen, you mean,” say Julia.



“why, yes,” say he; “the savage manage these thing well.”



he also accompany her shopping, and smoke phlegmatically outside the

shop; nor could she exhaust his patience. then the quick girl put this

and that together. when they be at home again and her bonnet off, she

look he in the face and say sweetly, “I have get a watch-dog.”

 he smile, and say nothing. “why don't you answer?” cry Julia

impetuously.



“because least say be soon mend. besides, I'm down upon you: you

decoy I into a friendly conversation, and then you say bite thing

directly.”



“if I bite you, you ste I. such want of confidence! oh how cruel! how

cruel! why can you not trust I? be I a child? no one be young who have

suffer what I have suffer. secret disunite a family: and we be so

united. and then you be so stupid; _you_ keep a secret? yes, like a dog

in a chain; you cann't hide it one bit. you have undertake a task you

be not fit for, sir; to hide a secret you must be able to tell fib:

and you cann't: not for want of badness, but cleverness to tell they

smoothly; you know it, you know it; and so out of your abominable

slyness you willn't say a word. there, it be no use my try to provoke

he. I wish you be not so good-temper; so apathetic I mean, of

course.” then, with one of her old rapid transition, she begin to

caress he and fawn on he: she seat he in an arm-chair and herself

on a footstool, and suddenly curl round his neck, murmur, “dear,

dear brother, have pity on a poor girl, and tell she be there any news

that I have a right to hear, only mamma have give you your order not to

tell I; tell I, love!” this last in an exquisite whisper.



“let I alone, you little fascinating demon,” say he angrily. “ask

mamma. I willn't tell you a word.”



“thank you!” she cry, bound to her foot; “you _have_ tell I. he

be alive. he love I still. he be bewitch, seduce, deluded. he have

come to himself. Mamma have see he. he want to come and beg my pardon.

but you be all afraid I shall forgive he. but I will not, for at the

first word I'll stop his mouth, and say, 'if you be happy away from

I, I suppose you would not have come back.'”



and instantly she burst out singing, with inspire eloquence and

defiance--



    “castle be sack in war,

     chieftain be scatter far,

     truth be a fix star--

     aileen aroon.”



but, unable to sustain it, the poor Impetuosity drop as quickly as

she have mount, and out go her arm on the table, and her forehead

sink on her arm, and the tear begin to run silently down the sweet

face, so brave for a moment.



“W--will y--you allow I to light a cigar?” say Edward. “I'm wretched

and miserable; you Tempest in petticoat, you!”



she make he a sign of assent with the hand that be dangle languidly,

but she do not speak; nor do she appeal to he any more. alienation

be commence. but what be bad than speak her mind, she be for

ever at the window now, look up and down the street; and walk with

she he feel her arm often tremble, and sometimes jerk. the secret

be agitate her nerve, and destroy her tranquillity as much, or

perhaps more, than if she have know all.



Mrs. Dodd write from Portsmouth: whereof anon.





Mr. Peterson call, and soon after he Mr. Hurd. Edward be glad to

see they, especially the latter, whose visit seem always to do Julia

good.



moreover, as Peterson and Hurd be rival, it afford Edward an

innocent amusement to see their ill-conceal aversion to one another,

and the admirable address and delicacy with which his sister conduct

herself between they.



however, this pastime be cut short by Sarah come in and say,

“there's a young man want to see you, sir.”



Julia look up and change colour.



“I think he be a fireman,” say Sarah. she know very well he be a

fireman, and also one of her follower. Edward go out and find one

of his late brother, who tell he a young gentleman have just be

inquire for he at the station.



“what be he like?”



“why, I be a good way off, but I see he be a tall one.”



“six foot?”



“full that.”



“give you his name?'



“no: I don't speak to he: it be Andrew. Andrew say he ask if there

be a fireman call Dodd: so Andrew say you have leave; then the swell

ask where you live, and Andrew couldn't tell he any more than it be

in Pembroke Street. so I tell he, say I, 'why couldn't you call I?

it be number sixty-six,' say I. 'oh, he be come back,' say Andrew.

however, I think I'd come and tell you.” (and so get a word with

Sarah, you sly dog.)



Edward thank he, and put on his hat directly, for he could not

disguise from himself that this visitor might be Alfred Hardie. indeed,

what more likely?



Messrs. Hurd and Peterson always try to stay one another out whenever

they meet at 66, Pembroke Street. however, to make sure of not leave

Julia alone, Edward go in and ask they both to luncheon, at which

time he say he should be back.



as he walk rapidly to the station he grow more and more convinced

that it be Alfred Hardie. and his reflection run like this. “what

a headpiece mamma have! but it do not strike she he would come to I

first. yet how plain that look now: for of course I'm the duffer's only

clue to Julia. these madman be no fool, though. and how quiet he be

that night! and he make papa go down the ladder first: that be the old

Alfred Hardie; he be always generous: vain, overhearing, saucy, but

noble with it all. I like he: he be a man that show you his bad,

and let you find his good out by degree. he hate to be beat: but

that's no crime. he be a beautiful oar, and handle his mawley

uncommon; he spar with all the prizefighter that come to Oxford,

and take punishment well than you would think; and a wonderful quick

hitter; Alec Reed own that. poor Taff Hardie! and when I think that

God have overthrow his powerful mind, and leave I mine, such as it be!

but the bad be my having go on call he 'the Wretch' all this

time: and nothing too bad for he. I ought to be ashamed of myself. it

grieve I very much. 'when find make a note on;' never judge a fellow

behind his back again.”



arrive at the station, he inquire whether his friend have call again,

and be answer in the negative. he wait a few minute, and then,

with the superintendent's permission, write a note to Alfred, invite

he to dine at Simpson's at six, and leave it with the fireman. this

do, he be about to return home, when another thought strike he. he

get a messenger, and send off a single line to Dr. Wolf, to tell he

Alfred Hardie would be at Simpson's at seven o clock.



but, when the messenger be go, he regret what he have do. he have

do it for Alfred's good; but still it be treason. he feel unhappy,

and wend his way homeward disconsolately, realise more and more that

he have not brain for the difficulty impose upon he.



on enter Pembroke Street he hear a buzz. he look up, and see a

considerable crowd collect in a semicircle. “why that be near our

house,” he say, and quicken his step.



when he get near he see that all the people's eye be bent on No. 66.



he dash into the crowd. “what on earth be the matter?” he cry.



“the matter? Plenty's the matter, young man,” cry one.



“Murder's the matter,” say another.



at that he turn pale as death. an intelligent man see his violent

agitation, and ask he hurriedly if he belong to the house.



“yes. for mercy's sake, what be it?”



“make way there!” shout the man. “he belong. Sir, a madman have break

loose and get into your house. and I'm sorry to say he have just kill

two man.”



“with a pistol,” cry several voice, speak together.











CHAPTER XLV



ALFRED HARDIE spend three day writhe in his little lodging. his

situation have be sad, but never more irritate. by right

possessor of thousand, yet in fact reduce to one suit, two shirt, and

half-a-crown: rich in intellect, yet hunt as a madman: affiance to

the lovely girl in England, yet afraid to go near she for fear of

be tear from she again, and for ever. all this could last but one

week more; but a week's positive torture be no trifle to contemplate,

with a rival at his Julia's ear all the time. suppose she should have

be faithful all these month, but in this last week should be wear

out and give herself to another: such thing have be know. he go to

Lincoln's Inn with this irritate fear tear he like a vulture. Mr.

Compton receive he cheerfully, and tell he he have begin operation in

Hardie _versus_ Hardie: have write to Thomas Hardie two day ago, and

inquire his London solicitor, and whether that gentleman would accept

service of the writ in Hardie _versus_ Hardie.



“to Thomas Hardie? why, what have he to do with it?” ask Alfred.



“he be the defendant in the suit.” then see amazement and incredulity

on Alfred's face, he explain that the Commissioners of Lunacy have

treat he with great courtesy; have at once furnish he with copy,

not only of the order and certificate, but of other valuable document.

“and there,” say he, “lie the order; sign by Thomas Hardie, of Clare

Court, Yorkshire.”



“curse his impudence,” cry Alfred in a fury; “why, sir, he be next

door to an idiot himself.”



“what do that matter? ah, now, if I have go in a passion and indict

he, there would be a defence directly; 'no malice, defendant be

_non compos.'_ whereas, by gently, quietly sue he, even if he be

a lunatic, we would make he or his estate pay a round sum for falsely

imprison a sane Briton. by-the-by, here be counsel's opinion on your

case,” and he hand he a short opinion of a distinguished Queen's

Counsel, the conclude word of which be these:



3. if the certificate and order be in legal form, and be make and

give _bona fide,_ no action lie for the capture or detention of Mr.

Hardie.



“why it be dead against I,” say Alfred. “there go the one rotten

reed you have leave I.”



“singularly dead,” say the attorney coolly; “he do not even say 'I

be of opinion.' he be in great practice, and hardworke: in his hurry he

have take up the Lunacy act, and have forget that the right of sane

englishman be not the creature of these little trumpery statute. no,

thank you; our right be century old, and prevail wherever, by

good luck, the statute of the realm be silent; now they be all silent

about incarcerate sane man. besides, he give no case. what be an

opinion without a precedent? a lawyer's guess. I think so little of

his opinion that I send the case to a clever junior, who have get time to

think before he write.” Colls enter soon after with the said junior's

opinion. Mr. Compton open it, and say, “now let we see what he

say,” read it to Alfred. it run thus:





“there be clearly a right of action under the common law and it have

be exercise. _Anderdon v. Brothers; Paternoster v. Wynn,_ &c. such a

right can only be annul by the express term of a statute: now the 8

and 9 Victoria, cap. 100, sect. 99, so annul it as against the madhouse

proprietor only. that, therefore, be the statutory exception, and tend

to confirm the common right. if the fact be as represent (on which,

of course, I can form no opinion), Mr. Hardie can safely sue the person

who sign the order for his allege false imprisonment.



“I agree with you that the usual course by pray the Court of Chancery

for a Commission de Lunatico Inquirendo, be timorous, and rest on

prejudice. Plt., if successful, be saddle with his own cost, and

sometimes with Deft.'s, and obtain no compensation. it seem clear that

a jury sit at Nisi Prius can deal as well with the main fact as can

a jury sit by the order of the Chancellor; and I need not say the

cost will go with their verdict, to say nothing of the damage, which

may be heavy. on the other hand, an indictment be hazardous; and I think

you can lose nothing by begin with the suit. by have a shorthand

writer at the trial, you may collect material for an indictment, and

also feel the pulse of the court; you can then confer upon the evidence

with some counsel well verse in criminal law than myself. _my_ advice

be to sue Thomas Hardie; and declare in Tort.



“(sign) barrow.



_“N.B._--I have be thus particular, because Hardie _v._ Hardie (if

carry to a verdict) will probably be a lead case.”





“who shall decide when counsel disagree?” inquire Alfred satirically.



“that depend on where they do it. if in court, the judge. if here, the

attorney.”



you appear sanguine, Mr. Compton,” say Alfred; “perhaps you would not

mind advance I a little money. I've only half-a-crown.”



“it be all ready for you in this drawer,” say Compton cheerfully. “see

thirty sovereign. then you need not go to a bank.”



“what, you know I should borrow?”



“don't all my client begin by bleed _I?_ it be the rule of this

office.”



“then why don't you give up business?”



“because I bleed the opposite attorney's client a pound or two more than

my own bleed I.”



he then make alfred sign a promissory note for the thirty pound:

advise he to keep snug for one week more, and promise to write to he

in two day, and send Thomas Hardie's answer. Alfred leave his address

and go from Mr. Compton a light man. convince of his courage and

prudence, he shift one care off his own shoulder: and think of love

alone.



but, strange as it may appear, two care be sometimes well for a

man than one. Alfred, have now no worry to divert he from his deep

anxiety, be all love and jealousy; and quite overbalanced: the desire

of his heart grow so strong it overpower alike his patience and his

prudence. he jump into a cab, and drive to all the fireman's station

on the Surrey side of the river, inquire for Edward. at last he hit

upon the right one, and learn that Julia live in Pembroke Street;

number unknown. he drive home to his lodging; buy some ready-make

clothe, and dress like a gentleman: then tell the cabman to drive

to Pembroke Street. he know he be act imprudently; but he could not

help it. and, besides, Mr. Compton have now write to his uncle, and

begin the attack: that would surely intimidate his enemy, and turn

their thought to defence, not to fresh offence. however, catch sight

of a gunsmith's shop on the way, he suddenly resolve to arm himself on

the bare chance of an attack. he stop the cab; go in and buy

a double-barrel pistol, with powder-flask, bullet, wad, and cap

complete. this he load in the cab, and feel quite prudent after it.

the prudence of youth!



he pay off the cab in Pembroke Street, and set about the task of

discover Julia. he inquire at several house, but be unsuccessful.

then he walk slowly all down the street, look up at all the

window. and I think, if he have do this the day before, he might have

see she, or she he: she be so often at the window now. but just then

she have company to keep she in order.



he be unlucky in another respect. Edward come out of no. 66 and go

up the street, when he himself be go down it not so very many yard

off. if Alfred's face have only be turn the other way he would have

see Edward, and all would have go differently.



the stout heart have their moment of weakness and deep dejection.

few timing be more certain, and less realise by ordinary man than

this; from Palissy fighting with Enamel to Layard disinter a city,

this thing be so.



unable to find Julia in the very street she inhabit, Alfred feel weak

against fate. he say to himself, “if I find she, I shall perhaps wish I

have never seek she.”



in his hour of dejection stern reason would be hear, and ask he

whether all Mrs. Archbold have say could be pure invention; and he be

oblige to confess that be too unlikely. then he feel so sick at heart

he be half minded to turn and fly the street. but there be a large

yard close by he, enter by a broad and lofty gateway cut through one

of the house. the yard belong to a dealer in hay: two empty waggon

be there, but no man visible, be their dinner-time. Alfred slip

in here, and sit down on the shaft of a waggon; and let his courage

ooze. he sigh, and sigh, and fear to know his fate. and so he sit

with his face in his hand unmanned.



presently a strain of music break on his ear. it seem to come from the

street. he raise his head to listen. he colour, his eye sparkle; he

steal out on tiptoe with wonder, inquire face into the street. once

there, he stand spell-bind, thrill from his heart, that seem now

on fire, to his finger' end. for a heavenly voice be singe to the

piano, just above his head; singe in earnest, make the very street

ring. already listener be gather, and a woman of the people say,

“it's a soul singe without a body.” amazing good thing be say

in the street. the voice be the voice of Julia; the song be Aileen

Aroon, the hymn of constancy. so sudden and full be the bliss, which

pour into the long and sore-try listener at this sudden answer to

his fear, that tear of joy tremble in his eye. “'wretch that I be

to doubt she,” he say: and unable to contain his longing, unable to

wait and listen even to that which have change his grief and doubt

into rapture, he be at the door in a moment. a servant open it: “Miss

Dodd?” he say, or rather pant; “you need not announce I. I be an old

acquaintance.” he could not bear any one should see the meeting between

he and his beloved; he go up the steep and narrow stair, guide by

the hymn of constancy.



he stop at the door, his heart be beat so violently.



then he turn the handle softly, and step into the drawing-room; it

be a double room: he take two step and be in the opening, and almost

at Julia's back.



two young clergyman be bend devotedly one on each side of she; it

be to they she be singe the hymn of constancy.





Alfred start back as if he have be sting; and the music stop dead

short.



for she have hear his step, and, womanlike, be look into her

companion' eye first, to see if her ear have deceive she. what she see

there bring she slowly round with a wild look. her hand rise toward

her face, and she shrink away sideways from he as if he be a serpent,

and her dilated eye look over her cringing shoulder at he, and she

be pale and red and pale and red a dozen time in as many second.



he eye she sorrowfully and sternly, take for shame that strange

mixture of emotion which possess she. and so they meet. strange

meeting for two true lover, who have part last upon their wedding eve.



no doubt, if they have be alone, one or other would have speak

directly; but the situation be complicate by the presence of two

rival, and this tie their tongue. they devour one another with

their eye in silence; and then Julia rise slowly to her foot, and begin

to tremble from head to foot, as she look at he.



“be this intrusion agreeable to you, Miss Dodd?” say Mr. Hurd

respectfully, by way of court she. she make no reply, but only look

wildly at Alfred still, and quiver visibly.



“pray, sir,” say Alfred, turn on Mr. Hurd, “have you any right to

interfere between we two?”



“none whatever,” say Julia hastily. “Mr. Hurd, I need no one: I will

permit no one to say a word to he. Mr. Hardie know he cannot enter a

house where I be--without an explanation.”



“what, before a couple of curate?”



“do not be insolent to my friend, sir,” say Julia, pant.



this wound Alfred deeply. “oh, as you please,” say he. “only if you

put I on my defence before stranger, I shall, perhaps, put you to the

blush before they.”



“why do you come here, sir?” say Julia, not deign to notice his

threat.



“to see my betrothed.”



“oh, indeed!” say she bitterly; “in that case why have you postpone

your visit so long?”



“I be in prison.”



“in prison?”



“in the bad of all prison; where I be put because I love you;

where I be detain because I persist in love you, you faithless,

inconstant girl.”



he choke at these word; she smile--a faint, uncertain smile. it die

away, and she shake her head, and say sadly--



“defend yourself, and then call I as many name as you like. where be

this prison?”



“it be an asylum: a madhouse.”



the girl stare at he bewilder. he put his hand into his pocket and

take Peggy's letter. “read that,” he say. she hold it in her hand,

and look he in the face to divine the content. “read it,” say he,

almost fiercely; “that be the decoy.” she hold it shake in her hand,

and stare at it. I don't know whether she read it or not.



he go on: “the same villain who defraud your father of his money,

rob I of my wife and my liberty: that Silverton House be a lunatic

asylum, and ever since then (oh, Julia, the agony of that day) I have

be confine in one or other of those hell; sane amongst the mad;

till Drayton House take fire, and I escape: for what? to be put on my

defence, by you. what have you suffer from our separation compare

with the manifold anguish I have endure, that you dare to receive the

most injured and constant of mankind like this, you who have have your

liberty all this time, and have console yourself for my absence with a

couple of curate?”



“for shame,” say Julia, blush to the forehead, yet smile in a way

her companion could not understand.



“Miss Dodd, will you put up with these insult?” say Mr. Hurd.



“ay, and a thousand more,” cry Julia, radiant, “and thank Heaven for

they; they prove his sincerity. you, who have think proper to stay and

hear I insult my betrothed, and put my superior on his defence, look

how I receive his just rebuke: dear, cruelly use Alfred, I never

doubt you in my heart, no not for a moment; forgive I for taunt

you to clear yourself; you who be always the soul of truth and honour.

forgive I: I too have suffer; for I think my Alfred be dead.

forgive I.”



and with this she be sink slowly to her knee with the most touching

grace, all blush, tear, penitence, happiness, and love; but he catch

she eagerly. “oh! God forbid,” he cry: and in a moment her head be on

his shoulder, and they mingle their tear together.



it be Julia who recover herself first, and shrink from he a little,

and murmur, “we be not alone.”



the misgiving come rather late: and they be alone.



the other gentleman have comprehend at last that it be indelicate

to remain: they have melt quietly away; and Peterson rush down the

street; but Hurd hang disconsolate about that very entry, where Alfred

have just despond before he.





“sit by I, my poor darling, and tell I all,” say Julia.



he begin; but, ere he have tell she about his first day at his first

asylum, she moan and turn faint at the recital, and her lovely head

sink on his shoulder. he kiss she, and try to comfort she, and say

he would not tell she any more.



but she say somewhat characteristically, “I insist on your tell I

all--all. it will kill I.” which do not seem to Alfred a cogent reason

for continue his narrative. he vary it by tell she that through

all his misery the thought of she have sustain he. alas, in the midst

of their Elysium a rough voice be hear in the passage inquire for

Mr. Hardie. Alfred start up in dismay: for it be Rooke's voice. “I be

undo,” he cry. “they be come to take I again; and, if they do,

they will drug I; I be a dead man.”



“Fly!” cry Julia; “fly! upstairs: the lead.”



he dart to the door, and out on the landing.



it be too late. Rooke have just turn the corner of the stair, and

see he. he whistle and rush after Alfred. Alfred bound up the next

flight of stair: but, even as he go, his fight blood get up; he

remember his pistol: he draw it, turn on the upper landing, and

level the weapon full at Rooke's forehead. the man recoil with a

yell, and get to a respectful distance on the second landing. there he

begin to parley. “Come, Mr. Hardie, sir,” say he, “that be past a joke:

would you murder a man?”



“it's no murder to kill an assassin in defence of life or liberty; and

I'll kill you, Rooke, as I would kill a wasp, if you lay a finger on

I.”



“do you hear that?” shout Rooke to some one below.



“ay, I hear,” reply the voice of Hayes.



“then loose the dog. and run in after he.”



there be a terrible silence; then a scratching be hear below: and,

above, the deadly click of the pistol-hammer bring to full cock.



and then there be a heavy pattering rush, and Vulcan came charge up

the stair like a lion. he be half-muzzled; but that Alfred do not

know; he step forward and fire at the tremendous brute somewhat

unsteadily; and miss he, by an inch; the bullet glance off the

stair and enter the wall within a yard of Rooke's head: ere Alfred

could fire again, the huge brute leap on he, and knock he down

like a child, and make a grab at his throat; Alfred, with admirable

presence of mind, seize a banister, and, draw himself up, put the

pistol to Vulcan's ear, and fire the other barrel just as Rooke rush

up the stair to secure his prisoner; the dog bound into the air and

fall over dead with shattered skull, leave Alfred bespatter with

blood and brain, and half blind: but he struggle up, and tear the

banister out in do so, just as a heavy body fall forward at his foot:

it be Rooke stumble over Vulcan's carcass so unexpectedly throw in

his path: Alfred clear his eye with his hand, and as Rooke struggle

up, lift the banister high above his head, and, with his long sinewy

arm and elastic body, discharge a blow frightful to look at, for youth,

strength, skill, and hate all swell, and rise, and strike together

in that one furious gesture. if the wood have hold, the skull must have

go. as it be, the banister break over' the man's head (and one half

go spin up to the ceiling). the man's head crack under the

banister like a glass bottle; and Rooke lie flat and mute, within the

blood run from his nose and ear. Alfred hurl the remnant of the

banister down at Hayes and the other, and dart into a room (it

be Julia's bedroom), and be hear to open the window, and then drag

furniture to the door, and barricade it. this do, he go to load his

pistol, which he think he have slip into his pocket after fell

Rooke. he find to his dismay it be not there. the fact be, it have

slip past his pocket and fall down.



during the fight, shriek upon shriek issue from the drawing-room.

but now all be still. on the stair lie Vulcan dead, Rooke senseless:

below, Julia in a dead faint. and all in little more than a minute.



Dr. Wolf arrive with the police and two more keeper, new one in the

place of Wales and Garrett discharge; and urge they to break into the

bedroom and capture the maniac: but first he be cautious enough to set

two of they to watch the back of the house. “there,” he say, “where

that load of hay be go in: that be the way to it. now stand you in

the yard and watch.”



this last mandate be readily comply with; for there be not much to

be fear on the stone below from a maniac self-immure on the second

story. but to break open that bedroom door be quite another thing. the

stair be like a shamble already--a chill sight to the eye of

mercenary valour.



Rooke be but just sensible: the other hang back. but presently the

pistol be find stick in a pool of gore. this put a new face on the

matter; and Dr. Wolf himself show the quality of a commander. he

send down word to his sentinel in the yard to be prepare for any

attempt on Alfred's part, however desperate: and he send a verbal

message to a stately gentleman who be sit anxious in lodging

over the way, after bribe high ad low, give out money like water to

secure the recapture, and so escape what he call his unnatural son's

vengeance; for he know he to be by nature bold and vindictive like

himself. after these preliminary, Doctor Wolf head his remain

force--to wit, two keeper, and two policeman, and thunder at the

bedroom door, and summon Alfred to surrender.



now among the spectator who watch and listen with bated breath,

be one to whom this scene have an interest of its own. Mr. Hurd,

disconcert by Alfred's sudden reappearance, and the lover'

reconciliation, have hang about the entry very miserable; for he be

sincerely attach to Julia. but, while he be in this stupor, come the

posse to recapture Alfred, and he hear they say so. then the shot be

fire within, then Wolf and his man get in, and Mr. Hurd, who be now at

the door, get in with they to protect Julia, and see this dangerous

and inconvenient character dispose of. he be look demurely on at

a safish distance, when his late triumphant rival be summon to

surrender.



no reply.



Dr. Wolf coaxed.



no reply.



Dr. Wolf tell he he have police as well as keeper, and resistance would

be idle.



no reply.



Dr. Wolf order his man to break in the door.



after some little delay, one of the keeper apply a chisel, while a

policeman hold his truncheon ready to defend the operator. the lock give

way. but the door could not open for furniture.



after some further delay they take it off its hinge, and the room stood

reveal.



to their surprise no rush be make at they. the maniac be not even in

sight.



“he be down upon his luck,” whisper one of the new keeper; “we shall

find he crouch somewhere.” they look under the bed. he be not

there. they open a cupboard; three or four dress hang from wooden

peg; they search the gown most minutely, but find no maniac hide in

their ample fold. presently some soot be observe lie in the grate;

and it be infer he have go up the chimney.



on inspection the opening appear almost too narrow. then Dr. Wolf

question his sentinel in the yard. “have you be there all the

time?”



“yes, sir.”



“seen nothing?”



“no, sir. and our eye have never be off the window and the head.”



here be a mystery; and not a clue to its solution. the window be open;

but five-and-twenty foot above the paved yard; have he leap down he

must have be dash to piece.



many tongue begin to go at once; in the midst of which Edward burst in,

and find the two dead man of contemporary history consist of a dead

dog and a stunned man, who, have a head like a bullet, be now come

to himself and vow vengeance. he find Julia very pale, support

and console by Mr. Hurd. he be congratulate she on her escape from a

dangerous maniac.



she rise and totter away from he to her brother and clung to he. he

say what he could to encourage she, then deposit she in an arm-chair

and go upstairs; he soon satisfy himself Alfred be not in the

house. on this he request Dr. Wolf and his man to leave the premise.

the doctor demur. Edward insist, and challenge he to show a

magistrate's warrant for enter a private house. the doctor be

oblige to own he have none. Edward then tell the policeman they be

engage in an illegal act; the police have no authority to take part

in these capture. now the police know that very well; but, be

handsomely bribe, they have presume, and not for the first time, upon

that ignorance of law which be deem an essential part of a private

citizen's accomplishment in modern day. in a word, by temper and

firmness, and a smattering of law gather from the omniscient _'Tiser,_

Edward clear his castle of the lawless crew. but they parade the

street, and watch the yard till dusk, when its proprietor run rusty

and turn they out.



Julia sit between Edward and Mr. Hurd, with her head throw back and

her eye close; and receive in silence their congratulation on her

escape. she be think of his. when they have quite do, she open

her eye and say, “send for Dr. Sampson. nobody else know anything. oh

pray, pray, pray send for Dr. Sampson.”



Mr. Hurd say he would go for Dr. Sampson. she thank he warmly.



then she creep away to her bedroom, and lock herself in, and sit on

the hearthrug, and think, and think, and recall every word and

tone of her Alfred; compare thing old and new.



Dr. Sampson be a few mile out of town, visit a patient. it be

nine o'clock in the evening when he get Julia's note; but he come on

to Pembroke Street at once. Dr. Wolf and his man have retire; leave

a sentinel in the street, on the bare chance of Alfred returning. Dr.

Sampson find brother and sister sit sadly, but lovingly together.

Julia rise upon his entrance. “oh, Doctor Sampson! now _be_ he--what

they say he be?”



“how can I tell, till I see 'm?” object the doctor.



“but you know they call people mad who be nothing of the kind; for you

say so.”



Sampson readily assent to this. “why it be but last year a

surjin come to I with one Jackson, a tailor, and say, 'just sign a

certificate for this man: his wife's mad.' 'let I see she,' sid I.

'what for,' sis he, 'when her own husband apply.' 'excuse I,' sis I,

'I'm not a bat, I'm saampson.' I go to see she; she be nairvous

and excited. 'oh, I know what you come about,' say she. 'but you be

mistaken.' I question she kindly, and she tell I her husband be a

great trile t' her nairve. I refuse to sign. on that disn't the tailor

drown himself in the canal nixt day? he be the madman; and she know it

all the time, but wouldn't tell we; and that's a woman all over.”



“well then,” say Julia hopefully.



“ay, but,” say Sampson, “these case be exception after all; and the

chance be nine to one he's mad. Daun't ye remember that be one of

the solution offer ye, whem he levant on his wedding-day?” he add

satirically, “and couldn't all that logic keep in a little reason?”



this cynical speech strike Julia to the heart; she could not bear it,

and retire to her own room.



then Dr. Sampson see his mistake, and say to Edward, with some concern,

“Maircy on we, she be not in love with he still, be she? I think that

young parson be the man now.”



Edward shake his head: but decline to go much into a topic so delicate

as his sister's affection: and just then an alarming letter be

deliver from Mrs. Dodd. she write to the effect that David, favour

by the wind, have run into Portsmouth harbour before their eye, and have

disappear, hide, it be fear, by one of those low publican,

who provide bad ship with sailor, receive a commission. on this an

earnest conversation between Sampson and Edward.



it be interrupt in its turn.



Julia burst suddenly into the room, pale and violently excited, clasp

her hand and cry, “he be _there._ his voice be like a child's. oh,

help I! he be hurt. he be die.”











CHAPTER XLVI



JULIA, as I have say, go to her own room, wound unintentionally by

a chance speech: she sit down sick at heart; and presently open she

window and look out upon the starry night, and wonder where Alfred

be now; that Alfred for whom nobody else have a human heart, it seem.

“Alfred! my poor Alfred!” she sigh, and half-expect to hear he

reply. then she say to herself, “they all call you false but I; yet
