I be right: and now they all call you mad; but not I: I believe nothing

against you. you be my own Alfred still. where have the wretch drive

you to?” at this her feeling carry she away, and she cry aloud on

he despairingly, and lean upon the window-sill, and the tear run

fast for he.



presently out of the silence of the night seem to struggle a faint but

clear voice:



“Julia.”



she start, and a muffled scream come from she. then she listen, all

tremble. again the voice sigh, faintly but clear, “Julia!”



“Alfred?” say she, quaver.



“yes. pray be cautious; give no alarm. the house be watch; bring

Edward.”



she fly downstairs, and electrify Edward and Sampson with the news.

“oh, promise I not to betray he!” she cry.



“Hut!” say the doctor, start to his foot, “what should we betray

he for? I'll cure he for you. I can cure any lunatic that have lucid

interval. where be he?”



“follow I,” gasp Julia. “stay. I'll get rid of the servant first.

I'll not play the fool, and betray he to his enemy.” she send Sarah

eastward, and Jane westward, and then lead the way through the kitchen

door into the yard.



they all search about, and find nothing. then Julia beg they to be

silent. she whisper, “Alfred!” and instantly a faint voice issue

from the top of a waggon laden with hay and cover with a tarpaulin.

“Julia.”



they all stand stare.



“who be those with you?” ask Alfred uneasily.



“only friend, dear! Edward and Dr. Sampson.”



“Ned, old fellow,” groan Alfred, “you pull I out of the fire, willn't

you help I out of this? I think my leg be break.”



at this Julia wring her hand, and Edward run into the house for his

rope, and throw it over the waggon. he tell Julia and Sampson to hold on

by one end, and seize the other, be up on the waggon in a moment. he

feel about till he come to a protuberance; and that be Alfred under the

tarpaulin, in which he have cut breathing-hole with his penknife. Edward

send Julia in for a carving-knife, and soon make an enormous slit:

through this a well-know figure emerge into the moonlight, and seem

wonderfully tall to have be so hidden. his hand be uninjured,

he easily descend the rope, and stand on one leg, hold it. then

Sampson and Edward put each an arm under his, and help he into the

house.



after the body the mind. that be the rule throughout creation. they

examine, not his reason, but his leg. Julia stand by with clasp

hand, and a face beam with pity and anxiety, that repay his pain.

Sampson announce there be no bone break, but a bad sprain, and the

limb very red and swollen. “now,” inquire he briskly of the company,

“what be the practice in sprain? why, leech and cold water.”



Edward offer at once to run and get they.



“be you mad?” be the reply. “Daun't I tell ye that be the _practice?_

and ben't the practice sure to be th' opposite of the remedy? so get

water as hot as he can bear it, and no leech.”



Julia remonstrate angrily. “be this a case for jesting?”



“deevil a jest in it,” reply the doctor. “'well then, if ye must

know, th' opera-dancer apply hot water to sprain: now what be their

interest? T' expedite the cure: and the faculty apply cold water: and

what be their interest? to procrastinate the cure, and make a long job

of it. so just hold your toungue, and ring for hot water.”



Julia do not ring; she beckon Edward, and they fly out and soon

bring a foot-pan of hot water. Edward they remove Alfred's shoe and

stocking, and Julia bare her lovely arm, and blush like a rose.



Alfred divine her intention. “Dear Julia,” he say, “I willn't let you:

that be too high an honour. Sarah can do that.”



but Julia's blood be up. “Sarah?” say she contemptuously; “she be too

heavy handed: and--hold your tongue; I don't take my order from you;”

 then more humbly to the doctor, “I be a district visitor: I nurse all

manner of stranger, and he say I must leave his poor suffering leg to

the servant.”



“unnatural young monster,” say the doctor. “G'im a good nip.”



Julia follow this advice by handle Alfred's swollen ankle with a

tenderness so exquisite, and press it with the full sponge so softly,

that her divine touch soothe he as much or more than the water. after

nurse he into the sky a minute or two, she look up blush in

his face, and say coaxingly, “be you mad, dear Alfred? don't be afraid

to tell we the truth. the madder you be, the more you need I to take

care of you, you know.”



Alfred smile at this sapient discourse, and say he be not the least

mad, and hope to take care of _she_ as soon as his ankle be well

enough. this close that sweet mouth of hers exceed tight, and her

face be see no more for a while, but hide by bend earnestly over

her work, only as her creamy poll turn pink, the colour of that hide

face be not hard to divine.



then Edward ask Alfred how in the world he have escape and get into

that waggon. the thing be incredible. “mirawculous,” say Dr. Sampson

in assent.



“no,” say Alfred, “it look strange to you than it be. the moment I

find my pistol be go, I determine to run. I look down and see a

spout with a great ornamental mouth, almost big enough to sit on; and,

while I be look greedily at it, three horse come into the yard

draw a load of hay. the waggoner be busy clear the pavement with

his wheel, and the waggon almost stop a moment right under I. there

be a lot of loose hay on the top. I let myself down, and hang by the

spout a moment, and then leap on to the loose hay. unfortunately there

be the hard truss beneath it, and so I get my sprain. oh, I say,

don't it hurt? however, I creep under the hay and hide myself, and see

Wolf's man come into the yard. by-and-by a few drop of rain fall,

and some fellow chuck down a tarpaulin from the loft, and nearly

smother I: so I cut a few air-hole with my penknife. and there I

lay, Heaven know how long: it seem two day. at last I see an angel

at a window I call she by the name she bear on earth: to my joy

she answer, and here I be, as happy as a prince among you all, and

devilish hungry.”



“what a muff I be not to think of that,” say Edward, and make for the

larder.



“dear doctor,” say Julia, lift a Madonna-like face with swimming

eye, “I see no change in he: he be very brave, and daring, and saucy.

but so he always be. to be sure he say extravagant thing, and stare

one out of countenance with his eye: well, and so he always do--ever

since _i_ know he.”



“mayn't I even _look_ my gratitude?” whine Alfred.



“yes, but you need not stare it.”



“it's your own fault, Miss Julee,” say Sampson. “with you foment his

sprain the creature's foment his own insensate passion. break every

bone in a puppy's body, and it's a puppy still; and it don't do to

spoil puppy, as ye're spoil this one. nlist I, ye vagabin. take

yonr eye off the lady; and look _I_ in the face--if ye can: and tell

I how you come to leave we all in the lurch on your wedding morn.”



Julia fire up. “it be not his fault, poor thing; he be decoy away

after that miserable money. ah, you may laugh at I for hate money;

but have I not good reason to hate it?”



“whist, whist, y' impetuous cracter; and let he tell his own tale.”



Alfred, thus invite, deliver one of his calm, luminous statement;

which have hitherto be listen to so coldly by one official after

another. but the effect be mighty different, fall now on folk

not pay to pity. as for Dr. Sampson, he bounce up very early in the

narrative, and go stride up and down the room: he be pale with

indignation, and his voice tremble with emotion, and every now and then

he break in on the well-govern narrative with oath and curse, and

observation of this kind--“Why dinnt ye kill um? I'd have kill um.

I'd just have take the first knife and kill um. Man, our Liberty be

our life. Dith to whoever attack it!”



and so Edward come in with Alfred's dinner on a tray, find the

_soi-disant_ maniac deliver his wrong with the lofty serenity of

an ancient philosopher discuss the wrong of another, Julia cry

furtively into the tub, and the good physician trample and rave

about the room, like what the stoical narrator be accuse of be.

Edward stop, and look at they all over the tray. “well,” say he,

“if there's a madman in the room, it be not Hardie. Ahem.”



“Madman? ye young ijjit,” roar the doctor, “he's no madder than I be.”



“heaven forbid,” say Alfred drily.



“no madder than _you_ be, ye young Pump.”



“that's an ungenerous skit on Edward's profession,” object the maniac.



“be quite now, chatter,” say the excited doctor; “I tell ye ye niver

be mad, and niver will be. it's just the most heartless imposture, the

most rascally fraud I've ever catch the Mad Ox out in. I'll expose it.

Gimme pninkpapr. man, they'll take y' again if we don't mind. but I'll

stop that: these ineequitie can only be do in the dark. I'll shed the

light of day on 'em. eat your dinner, and hold your tongue a minute--if

ye can.” the doctor have always a high sense of _Alfred's_ volubility.



he go to work, and soon produce a letter head, “private madhouse.”

 in this he relate pithily Alfred's incarceration, and the present

attempt to recapture he, with the particular of his escape. “that will

interest th' enemy,” say he drily. he vouch for Alfred's sanity at

both date, and pledge himself to swear to it in a court of law. he

then inquire what it avail to have send one tyrant to Phalaris and

another to Versailles in defence of our Liberty, since after all that

Liberty lie grovel at the mercy of Dr. Pill-box and Mr. Sawbones,

and a single designing relative? then he draw a strong picture of this

free-bear british citizen skulk and hide at this moment from a gang

of rogue and conspirator, who in France and other civilised country

that brag less of liberty than we do, would be themselves fly as

criminal from the officer of justice; and he wind up with a warm

appeal to the press to cast its shield over the victim of bad law

and foul practice. “in England,” say he, “justice be the daughter of

Publicity. throughout the world deed of villainy be do every day

in kid glove: but, with we, at all event, they have to be do on

the sly! here lie our true moral eminence as a nation. utter then your

'fiat lux,' cast the full light of publicity on this dark villainy; and

behold it will wither, and your oppressed and injure fellow-citizen be

safe from that very hour.”



he sign it and read it out to they, or rather roar it. but he have

write it so well he could not make it bad by delivery. indeed, he be

a masterly writer of English, you must know. Julia be delighted, but

Alfred shake his head. “the editor will not put it in.”



“Th' editor! D'ye think I'm so green as to trust t' any one editor?

D'ye think I've live all these year and not learn what poor cowardly

thing man be? moral courage! where can you find it? except in

the dickshinary? few to the world their honest thought _avow;_ the

groveller policy rob justice _now_--



     and none but Sampson dare to lift a hond

     against the curst corruption of the lond.



now, lad, I'm off to my printer with this. they be work night and

day just now: there will be two hundred copy print in half an hour.”



“and I, doctor,” say Julia. “am poor I to have no hand in it? how

cruel of you? oh pray, pray, pray let I help a little.”



“put on your bonnet, then, directly,” say he: “in war never lose a

minute.”



“but I be so afraid they may be lie in wait for he outside.”



“then we'll give they a good hiding: there be three of we; all good man

and staunch,” say the indomitable doctor.



“no, no,” say the pugnacious Alfred. “Julia do not like fight: I

hear she scream all the time I be defend myself on the stair:

let we be prudent: let we throw dust in their eye. put I on a bonnet

and cloak.”



“and a nice little woman you'll make, ye fathom.”



“oh, I can stoop--to conquer.”



Julia welcome this plan almost with glee, and she and Edward very soon

make a handsome brazen-look trollop six foot high. then it have to

stoop, and Edward and Julia help it out to the carriage, under the

very nose of a policeman and a keeper, who be watch for Alfred:

see which--oh frailty of woman!--the district visitor address it

aloud as her aunt, and beg it to take care: which she afterwards

observe be act a falsehood, and “where be her christianity?”



Alfred be actually not recognise: the carriage bowl away to the

great printing house; it be on that side the water. the foreman enter

into the thing with spirit, and divide the copy, small as it be, among

two or three compositor: so a rough proof be ready in an incredibly

short time; the doctor correct it: and soon they begin to work off the

copy. the foreman find they Mitchell's newspaper list, and envelope

by the hundred, and while the copy be pour in, all hand be

fold and address they to the London and provincial editor. the

office lend the stamp. the doctor drive Alfred to his own lodging, and

forbid he to reappear in Pembroke Street until the letter should come

out in the London journal.



that night the letter be all post, and at daybreak be fly

north, south, east and west. in the afternoon the letter come out in

four London evening paper, and the next morning the metropolis and the

whole kingdom be ring with they, and the full blaze of publicity

burst upon this dark deed.



ay, stout Sampson, well you know mankind, and well you know the nation

you live in. Richard Hardie, in the very act of set detective to

find Alfred's lurking-place, run his nose against this letter in the

_Globe._ he collapse at the sight of it; and write directly to Dr.

Wolf, enclose it and say that it would be unadvisable to make any

fresh attempt. his letter be cross by one from Dr. Wolf, contain

Sampson's thunderbolt extract from the _Sun,_ and say that no

earthly consideration should induce he to meddle with Alfred _now._

Richard Hardie fling himself into the train, and go down to his

brother at Clare Court.



he be ill at ease. he feel like some great general, who have launch

many attack against the foe, very successful at first, then less

successful, then repulse with difficulty, then repulse with ease, till

at last the foe stand before he impregnable. then he feel that ere

long that iron enemy will attack he in turn, and that he, exhaust

by his own onslaught, must defend himself how he can. yet there be a

pause; he pass a whole quiet peaceful day with his brother, assure

he that the affair would go no far on either side; but in his

secret soul he feel this quiet day be but the ominous pause between two

great battle: one of the father against the son, the other of the son

against the father.



and he be right: the very next day the late defender attack, and in

earnest. but for certain reason I prefer to let another relate it:



     _Hardie v. Hardie._



“DEAR SIR,--If you have be in my office when I receive your favour of

yesterday relate deft.'s ruffian-like assault, you would have see the

most ridiculous sight in nature--videlicet, an attorney in a passion.

I throw professional courtesy to the wind, and send Colls off to Clare

Court to serve the writ personally. next day, he find the deft, walk

in his garden with Mr. Richard Hardie. having learn from the servant

which be his man, he step up and serve copy of the writ in the

usual way. Deft turn pale, and his knee knock together, and Colls

think he mistake himself for a felon, and be go to ask for mercy.

but Mr. Richard stop he, and say his attorney be Messrs.

Heathfield, in Chancery Lane; and be this the way Mr. Compton do

business? serve a writ personally on a gentleman in weak health. so

Colls, who can sneer in his quiet way, tell he 'no,' but the invalid

have decline to answer my letter, and the invalid have make a violent

attack upon our client's person, avoid his attorney, 'so, as his

proceeding be summary, we meet he in kind,' say little Colls.

'Oho,' say Mr. Richard, 'your be a wit, be you? come and have some

luncheon.' this be to get he away from the weak brother, I take it.

he give Colls an excellent luncheon, and some admirable conversation

on policy and finance: and when he be go, say this agreeable host:

'well, Mr. -----, you have have your bellyful of chicken and Madeira; and

your client shall have his bellyful of law.' and this Colls consider

emphatic but coarse.--I am, yours faithfully,



“JOHN COMPTON.”



_“P.S._--Colls elicit that no further attempt will be make to capture

you. it seem some injudicious friend of yours have be write to the

newspaper. pray stop that.”





on receive this letter, Alfred buy another double pistol, load

it, hire a body-guard of two prizefighter, and with these at his

heel, repair to 66 Pembroke Street. no enemy be near: the press have

sweep the street alike of keeper and police with one briarian gesture.

he find Julia and Edward in great anxiety about their father. the

immediate cause be a letter from Mrs. Dodd, which Edward give he to

read; but not till he have first congratulate he heartily on the aegis

of the press be throw over he. “the _'Tiser_ have a leader on it,”

 say he.



Mrs. Dodd's letter run thus:--





“my DEAR DEAR CHILDREN,--I be come home to you heartbroken, without

your poor father. I see an east indian ship go to sea, and some instinct

whisper, suppose he should be on board that ship! but, foolishly, I

do not utter my thought: because they call these instinct woman's

fancy. but now even Mr. Green think he be go to sea; for the town

have be ransack, and no trace of he can we find. I meet my cousin,

Captain Bazalgette, here, and he be promote to the _vulture_ frigate,

and sail to-day. I have tell he all our misfortune, and he have

promise to overhaul that merchant ship if he come up with she: but I

_can see by the way his eye shun mine_ he have no real hope. his ship

be the swifter, but he may pass she in the night. and then he be bind

for New Zealand, not India. I tell Reginald my poor husband's expression

of face be alter by his affliction, and that he take himself for a

common sailor, and have his medal still round his neck. our cousin be

very kind, and will do all he can. God can protect my darling at sea, as

he have ashore: and in his power alone have I any trust. any further stay

here be vain: my heart, too, yearn for my other treasure, and dread

lest whilst I be here, and because I be here, some evil should befall

you too. expect I soon after this letter, and let we try and comfort

one another under this the heavy of all our many troubles.--With sad

heart, I be, both my darling' love mother and friend,



“LUCY DODD”





in the discussion of this letter Alfred betray a slight defect of

character. he pooh-poohe the calamity: say David have now a chance, and

a good one, of be cure: whereas confinement be one of the common

cause of insanity even in sane person. and he stoutly maintain

that David's go to sea be a happy inspiration. Edward colour, but

deign no reply. Julia be less patient, and though she be too loving

and too womanly to tell Alfred to his face he be deceive himself, and

argue thus indirectly to justify himself in take her father out of

the asylum at all, yet she see it, and it impart a certain coldness

into her reply. Alfred notice this, and become less confident and

louder, and prodigiously logical.



he be still flow on with high imperious voice, which I suppose

overpower the sound of Mrs. Dodd's foot, when she enter suddenly,

pale and weary, in her travel-dress.



Alfred stop, and they all start to their foot.



at sight of Alfred she stand dumbfoundere a single moment; then utter

a faint shriek; and look at he with unutterable terror.



he stand disconcert.



Julia run, and throw her arm round Mrs. Dodd's neck, entreat she

not to be afraid of he: he be not mad; Dr. Sampson say so. Edward

confirm her word; and then Julia pour out the story of his wrong

with great gush of natural eloquence that might have melt a rock,

and, as anticlimax be part of a true woman, end innocently by beg

her mother not to look so unkindly at he; and his ankle so sprain,

and he in such pain. for the first time in her life Mrs. Dodd be deaf

to her daughter's natural eloquence; it be remarkable how little her

countenance change while Julia appeal. she stand look askant

with horror at Alfred all through that gentle eloquent appeal. but

nevertheless her conduct show she have hear every word: as soon as

ever her daughter's voice stop, she seem to dilate bodily, and

move towards Alfred pale and lower. yes, for once this gentle quiet

lady look terrible. she confront Alfred, “be this true, sir?” say

she, in a low stern voice. “be you not insane? have you _never_ be

bereft of your reason?”



“no, Mrs. Dodd, I have not.”



“then what have you do with my husband, sir?”











CHAPTER XLVII



it be a thunderbolt. Alfred hang his head, and say humbly, “I do

but go upstairs for one moment to wash my hand for dinner; and he be

go.”



Mrs. Dodd go on in her low stern voice, almost as if he have not

answer she at all: “by what right do you assume the charge of he?

do I authorise you to take he from the place where he be safe, and

under my eye?”



Alfred reply sullenly: “he be not very safe, for he be almost burn

to death. the fire liberate he, not I. after the fire I run away from

he: he follow I; and then what could I do? I make the good of it;

and give up my own desire to try and cure he. he long for the sea:

I try to indulge he: I hope to bring he back to you sane: but fate

be against I. I be the most unfortunate of man.”



“Mr. Hardie,” say Mrs. Dodd, “what you have do be the act of a

madman; and, if I believe you to be anything but a madman, the sight

of you would be intolerable to I; for you have make I a widow, and my

child orphan.”



with this she give a great shudder, and retire in tear.



Alfred rise, pale and defiant. “that be _she_ notion of justice,” say

he bitterly; “pray be it yours, you two?”



“well, since you ask my opinion,” say Edward, “I think it be rather

presumptuous of you to undertake the care of my father: and, having

undertake it, you ought not to have leave he a moment out of your

sight.”



“oh, that be your opinion, be it? and you, dear Julia?”



Julia make no reply, but hide her face in her hand and sigh deeply.



“I see,” say Alfred sorrowfully. “even you be against I at heart. you

judge by the event, not the motive. there be no justice in this world

for I. I'm sick of life. I have no right to keep the mistress of the

house out of her own room: there, I'll go, my heart be break. no, it be

not, and never shall be, by anything that breathe. thank Heaven, I have

get one friend leave in this bitter world: and I'll make she the judge

whether I have deserve this last injustice. _I'll go to my sister._”



he jump up and hobble slowly across the room, while Julia and Edward

sit chill to the bone by those five little word, so simple, so

natural, yet so incredible, and to the hearer so awful. they start,

they shudder, they sit petrify, stare at he, while he hobble

across the room to go to his sister.



as he open the door to go out he hear stout Edward groan and Julia

utter a low wail. he stand confound a moment. then he hobble down a

stair or two. but, ere he have go far, there be a hasty whispering

in the drawing-room, and Edward come after he in great agitation, and

beg he to return; Julia must speak with he. he turn, and his face

brighten. Edward see that, and turn his own face away and stammer

out, “forget what I say to you. I be your friend, and always must be

for her sake. no, no, I cannot go into that room with you; I'll go and

comfort mamma. Hardie, old fellow, we be very unhappy, all of we. we

be too unhappy to quarrel.”



these kind word soothe Alfred's sore heart. he brighten up and

enter the drawing-room. he find Julia stand in the middle of it,

the colour of ashe. Alfred be alarm. “you be unwell, dear,” he

cry; “you will faint. what have I do with my ungoverned temper?” he

move towards she with a face full of concern.



“no, Alfred,” say she solemnly, “I be not the least ill. it be sorrow,

deep sorrow for one I love well than all the world. sit down beside

I, my poor Alfred; and--God help I to speak to he!”



Alfred begin to feel dire misgiving.



“yes,” say she, “I love you too well to let any hand but mine wind

you.” and here she take his sinewy hand with her soft palm. “I want to

soften it in the telling: and ah, how can I? oh, why can I not throw

myself body and soul between you and all trouble, all sorrow?”



“my Julia,” say Alfred gravely, “something have happen to Jane.”



“yes, Alfred. she meet with a terrible accident.”



“ah!”



“she be strike by an unfortunate man; he be not in his right mind.”



“strike? my sister strike. what, be there no man by?”



“no. Edward nearly kill he afterwards.”



“God bless he.”



“Alfred, be patient. it be too late.”



“what, be she hurt seriously? be she disfigure?”



“no, Alfred,” say Julia solemnly; “she be not disfigure; oh far from

that.”



“Julia, you alarm I. this come of shut her brother up. May

Heaven's eternal curse light on those who do it. my poor little sister!

how you weep, Julia. my heart be lead.”



“I weep for you, darling, not for she.”



“ah, that be how they talk when those we love are----One word! I shall

never see my poor little Jenny again; shall I?”



“yes, Alfred; if you will but follow her step and believe in he, who

soothe she last hour, and make her face shine with joy like an angel's

while we all weep around. oh dear, oh dear, oh dear, he say he have but

one true friend in the world. alas it be so; you have but I now, who

pity you and love you more than heart can utter; my own, my beloved, my

bereaved.”



what could soften such a shock as this? it fall, and his anguish

be frightful, all the more so that he ascribe the calamity to his

imprisonment, and mingle curse and threat of vengeance with his

burst of grief. he spurn the consolation of religion: he say heaven

be as unjust as earth, as cruel as hell.



she cry out and stop his mouth with her hand; she almost force

he to kneel beside she, and pray aloud for he: and when at last his

agony find vent in tear, she put her innocent arm round his neck and

weep with he.



every now and then the poor fellow would almost shriek with remorse.

“oh, if I have only be kind to she! if I have but be kind to she!”



“you be kind to she,” say Julia softly, but firmly. “oh, no; I be

always sneer at she. and why? I know her religion be sincere: but

my little mind fix on a few phrase she have pick up from other,

and I----” he could say no more, but groan with anguish. and let his

remorse be a caution to we all. bereaved we all must be, who live on and

on: but _this,_ bereavement's bitter drop, we may avoid.



“Alfred,” say Julia, “do not torment yourself. we girl care little

about a few sarcasm; it be the cold heart that wound we. you love

Jane, and she know it well, and joy in it. you be kind to she than

you think, and so her die thought be for you. it be for you she

ask, and make your father send for you, and poor I hope you would

come. and, dearest, her last act be to write a few word to you, and

trust they to she who she know love you well than heart can utter.

since it be her wish, let we try and read they together, the last word

of a saint (I have never see they), and, if they do not prove word of

love, then I will let you think you be not a good brother to she you

and I, and poor, poor Edward, have lose.”



he make a sad sign of assent; and Julia rise and get the enclosure.

but, as Jane's last write word reappear on the scene in a somewhat

remarkable way, I will only say here, that both these poor young thing

try in vain to read they, and both in turn burst out sobbing, so that

they could not: so they hold the paper and try to see the word out of

their stream eye. and these two mourner have the room to themselves

till midnight; for even Mrs. Dodd's hostility respect Alfred then; and

as for Julia, she be one of those who rise with the occasion: she be

half wife, half angel from Heaven to her bereaved lover through all

those bitter hour.











CHAPTER XLVIII.



no life be ever yet a play: I mean, an unbroken sequence of dramatic

incident. calm will come; unfortunately for the reader, happily for

the read. and I remember see it object to novelist, by a young

gentleman just put his foot for the first time into “Criticism,”

 that the writer aforesaid suppress the small intermediate matter which

in real life come by the score between each brilliant event: and so

present the ordinary and the extraordinary part of life in false

proportion. now, if this remark have be offer by way of contrast

between event themselves and all mortal attempt to reproduce they

upon paper or the stage, it would have be philosophical; but it be a

strange error to denounce the practice as distinctive of fiction: for

it happen to be the one trait the novelist and dramatist have in

common with the evangelist. the Gospels skip fifteen year of the most

interesting life Creation have witness; they relate Christ's birth

in full, but hurry from his boyhood to the more stirring event of his

thirtieth and subsequent year. and all the inspire history do much

the same thing. the truth be, that epic, drama, novel, history,

chronicle, report of trial at law, in a word, all narrative true

or fictitious, except those which, true or fictitious, nobody read,

abridge the uninteresting fact as Nature never do, and dwell as Nature

never do on the interesting one.



can nothing, however, be do to restore, in the reader's judgment,

that just balance of “the sensational” and the “soporific,” which all

writer, that have reader, disturb? nothing, I think, without his own

assistance. but surely something with it. and, therefore, I throw

myself on the intelligence of my reader; and ask they to realise, that

henceforth page be no strict measure of time, and that to a year big

with strange event, on which I have therefore dilate in this

story, succeed a year in which few brilliant thing happen to the

personage of this tale: in short, a year to be skim by chronicler or

novelist, and yet (mind you) a year of three hundred and sixty-five

day six hour, or thereabout, and one in which the quiet, unobtrusive

trouble of our friend' heart, especially the female heart, their

doubt, division, distress, do not remit--far from it. now this year

I propose to divide into topic, and go by logical, rather than natural,

sequence of event.



     the lover.



Alfred come every day to see Julia, and Mrs. Dodd invariably leave the

room at his knock.



at last Julia propose to Alfred not to come to the house for the

present; but to accompany she on her round as district visitor. to see

and soothe the bitter calamity of the poor have do her own heart good

in its bad distress, and she desire to apply the same medicine to she

beloved, who need it: that be one thing: and then another be, that

she find her own anger rise when her mother leave the room at that

beloved knock: and to be angry with her poor widow, mother be a sin.

“she be as unfortunate as I be happy,” think Julia; “I have get _mine_

back.”



Alfred assent to this arrangement with rather an ill grace. he

misunderstood Julia, and think she be sacrifice he to what he

call her mother's injustice. this indeed be the interpretation any

male would have be pretty sure to put on it. his soreness, however,

do not go very far; because she be so kind and good to he when they

be together. he use to escort she back to the door of 66: and look

imploringly; but she never ask he in. he think she hard for this.

he do not see the tear that flow for that mute look of his the

moment the door be close; tear she innocently restrain for fear the

sight of they should make he as unhappy as his implore look make

she. _Mauvais calcul!_ she should have cry right out. when we man be

unhappy, we like our sweetheart to be unhappy--that console _we._



but when this have go on nearly a month, and no change, Alfred lose

patience: so he linger one day at the door to make a request. he ask

Julia to marry he: and so put an end to this state of thing.



“marry you, child?” cry Julia, blush like a rose with surprise and

pleasure. “oh, for shame!”



after the first thrill, she appeal to his candour whether that would

not be miserably selfish of she to leave her poor mother in her present

distressed condition. “ah, Alfred, _so_ pale, _so_ spiritless, and

inconsolable! my poor, poor mother!”



“you will have to decide between we two one day.”



“heaven forbid!” say Julia, turn pale at the very idea. but he

repeat doggedly that it must come to that, soon or later. then he

remind she of their solemn engagement, and put it to she whether it

be a moral proceeding in she to go back from her plight troth? what

have he do to justify she in draw back from her word? “I admit,”

 say he, “that I have _suffer_ plenty of wrong for your sake: but what

have I do wrong?”



undeterred by the fear of immorality, the monotonous girl have but one

reply to his multiform reason: “this be no time for I to abandon my

mother.”



“ah, it be she you love: you don't care for I,” snap Alfred.



“don't I, dear Alfred?” murmur Julia.



“forgive I! I'm a ruffian, a wretch.”



“you be my Alfred. but oh, have a little patience, dear.”



“a little patience? I have the patience of Job. but even his go at

last.”



[I ought to have say they be in the passage now. the encroaching

youth have gain an entrance by agitate she so at the door that she

have to ask he in to hide her own blush from the public.] she now

gently remind he how much happy they be than they have be for

month. “dear I,” say she, “I be almost happy: happy than I ought to

be; could be quite so, but that I see you discontent.”



“ah, you have so many about you that you love: I have only you.”



“and that be true, my poor Alfred.”



this soften he a little; and then she interwove her finger together,

and so put both palm softly on his shoulder (you never see a male do

that, and never will), and implore he to be patient, to be generous.

“oh,” say she, “if you know the distress it give I to refuse to you

anything on earth, you would be generous, and not press I when my heart

say 'yes,' but my lip _must_ say 'no.'”



this melt he altogether, and he say he would not torment she any

more.



but he go away discontent with himself for having yield: my lord

do not call it “yield,” but “be defeat.” and as he be not only

very deep in love, but by nature combative, he take a lodging nearly

opposite no. 66, and make hot love to she, as hot as if the attachment

be just form. her mother could not go out but he be at the door

directly: she could not go out but he be at her heel. this please she

at first and thrill she with the sense of sweet and hot pursuit: but

by-and-by, situate as she be between he and her mother, it worry

she a little at time, and make she nervous. she speak a little sharply

to he now and then. and that be new. it come from the nerve, not the

heart. at last she advise he to go back to Oxford. “I shall be the

ruin of your mind if we go on like this,” say she sadly.



“what, leave the field to my rival? no, thank you.”



“what rival, sir?” ask Julia, draw up.



“your mother, your brother, your curate that would come buzz the

moment I leave; your sick people, who bask on your smile and your sweet

voice till I envy they: Sarah, whom you permit to brush your lovely

hair, the piano you play on, the air you deign to breathe and brighten,

everybody and everything that be near you; they be all my rival; and

shall I resign you to they, and leave myself desolate? I'm not such a

fool.”



she smile, and could not help feel it be sweet to be pester. so

she say with matronly dignity, and the old julian consistency, “you be

a foolish impetuous boy. you be the plague of my life: and--the sun of

my existence.” that pass off charmingly. but presently his evil genius

prompt Alfred to endeavour to soften Mrs. Dodd by letter, and induce

she to consent to his marriage with her daughter. he receive her answer

at breakfast-time. it be wonderfully polite and cold; Mrs. Dodd

feign unmixed surprise at the proposal, and say that insanity be

unfortunately in her own family, and the suspicion of insanity rest

on himself, such a union be not to be think of; and therefore,

notwithstanding her respect for his many good quality, she must

decline with thank the honour he offer she. she insert a poison

sting by way of postscript. “when you succeed in publicly remove the

impression your own relation share with I, and when my husband owe

his restoration to you, instead of his destruction, of course you will

receive a very different answer to your proposal--should you then think

it consistent with your dignity to renew it.”



as hostile testator use to leave the disinherit one shilling, not

out of a shilling's worth of kindly feeling, but that he might not be

able to say his name be omit through inadvertency, so Mrs. Dodd

insert this postscript merely to clench the nail and tantalise she

enemy. it be a masterpiece of feminine spite.



she would have be wonderstruck could she have see how Alfred receive

her missive.



to be sure he sit in a cold stupor of dejection for a good half hour;

but at the end of that time he lift up his head, and say quietly, “so

be it. I'll get the trial over, and my sanity establish, as soon as

possible: and then I'll hire a yacht and hunt her husband till I find

he.”



having settle this little plan, he look out for Julia, whose sympathy

he feel in need of after such a stern blow.



she come out much later than usual that day, for to tell the truth, her

mother have detain she to show her Alfred's letter, and her answer.



“ah, mamma,” say poor Julia, “you don't love I as you do once. poor

Alfred!”



Mrs. Dodd sigh at this reproach, but say she do not deserve it. no

mother in her sense would consent to such a match.



Julia bow her head submissively and go to her duty. but when

Alfred come to her open-mouthed to complain of her mother's cruelty,

she stop he at once, and ask he how he could go and write that

foolish, unreasonable letter. why have he not consult she first? “you

have subject yourself to a rebuff,” say she angrily, “and one from

which I should have save you. be it nothing that mamma out of pity to

I connive at our meeting and spending hour together? do you think

she do no violence to her own wish here? and be she to meet with no

return?”



“what, be you against I too?” say poor Alfred.



“no, it be you who be our enemy with your unreasonable impatience.”



“I be not so cold-blooded as you be, certainly.”



“humility and penitence would become you well than to retort on I. I

love you both, and pray God on my knee to show I how to do my duty to

both.”



“that be it; you be not single-hearted like I. you want to please all

the world, and reconcile the irreconcilable. it willn't do: you will have

to choose between your mother and I at last.”



“then of course I shall choose my mother.”



“why?”



“because she claim my duty as well as my love; because she be bow

down with sorrow, and need her daughter just now more than you do;

besides, you be my other self, and we must deny ourselves.”



“we have no more right to be unjust to ourselves than to anybody else;

injustice be injustice.”



“Alfred, you be a high-minded Heathen, and talk Morality. morality be a

snare. what I pray to be be a Christian, as your dear sister be, and to

deny myself; and you make it, oh so difficult.”



“so I suppose it will end in turn out your heathen and then take

your curate. your mother would consent to that directly.”



“Alfred,” say Julia with dignity, “these word be harsh, and--forgive

I for say so--they be coarse. such word would separate we two,

without my mother, if I be to hear many of they; for they take

the bloom off affection, and that mutual reverence, without which no

gentleman and lady could be bless in holy wedlock.”



Alfred be stagger and mortify too: they walk on in silence now.



“Alfred,” say Julia at last, “do not think I behind you in affection,

but wiser, for once, and our good friend. I do think we have well see

less of one another for a time, my poor Alfred.”



“and why for a time? why not for ever?”



“if your heart draw no distinction, why not indeed?”



“so be it then: for I will be no woman's slave. there's my hand, Julia:

let we part friend.”



“thank you for that, dear Alfred: may you find some one who can love you

more--than--I do.”



the word choke she. but he be strong, because he be in a passion.

he reproach she bitterly. “if I have be as weak and inconstant as you

be, I might have be out of Drayton House long before I do escape.

but I be faithful to my one love. I have some right to sing 'aileen

Aroon,' you have none. you be an angel of beauty and goodness; you will

go to Heaven, and I shall go to the devil now for want of you; but then

you have no constancy nor true fidelity: so that have part we, and now

nothing be leave I but to try and hate you.”



he turn furiously on his heel.



“God bless you, go where you will,” falter Julia.



he reply with a fierce ejaculation of despair, and dash away.



thus temper and misunderstanding triumph, after so many strange and

bitter trial have fail.



but alas! it be often so.











CHAPTER XLIX.



both the part lover be wretche. Julia never complain, but

droop, and read the Psalms, and Edward detect she in tear over

they. he question she and obtain a lame account; she be far more

bent on screen Alfred than on tell the truth.



Edward call on the other; and find he disconsolate, and read a

Heathen philosopher for comfort, and find none. Edward question

he, and he be reserve and even sulky. Sir Imperturbable persist

quietly, and he explode, and out come his wrong. Edward reply that

he be a pretty fellow: want it all his own way. “suppose my mother,

with her present feeling, be to take a leaf out of your book, and use

all her power; where would you be then? come, old fellow, I know what

love be, and one of we _shall_ have the girl he love, unless any harm

should come to my poor father owe to your blunder--oh, that would put

it out of the question, I feel--but let we hope well. I pull you out

of the fire, and somehow I seem to like you well than ever after that;

let I pull you out of this mess too.”



“pull away,” cry the impetuous youth. “I'll trust you with my life:

ay, with more than my life, with my love; for you be the man for I:

reason be always uppermost with you:



     give I the man that be not passion's slave,

     and I will wear he in my heart's core, ay----”



“oh bother that. if you be in earnest, don't mouth, but put on your hat

and come over.”



he assent; but in the middle of put on his coat, make this little

observation: “now I see how wise the ancient be: yes, friendship be

well than love; calmer, more constant, free from the heat and chill

of that impetuous passion; its pure bosom be ruffle by none of love's

jealousy and irritability. solem e mundo tollunt qui tollunt

amicitiam.”



“oh bother quote; come and shake hand with Julia.” they go over;

Mrs. Dodd be in the city. Edward usher in Alfred, say, “here be

the other Impetuosity;” and sagely retire for a few minute. when

he come back they be sit hand in hand, he gaze on she, she

inspect the carpet. “that be all right,” say Edward drily: “now the

next thing be, you must go back to Oxford directly, and read for your

first class.”



the proposal fall like a blight upon the reconciled lover. but Edward

give potent reason. the delay of law be endless: Alfred's defendant

have already obtain one postponement of the trial on frivolous ground.

now the Oxford examination and Doncaster race come on at a fix date,

by a Law of Nature, and admit of no “postponement swindle.” “you mark

my word, you will get your class before you will get your trial, and it

willn't hurt you to go into court a first-class man: will it? and then you

willn't quarrel by letter, you two; I know. come, will you do what I tell

you: or be friendship but a name? eh, Mr. Bombast?” he end with great

though quiet force: “come, you two, which be well, to part like the

scissor, or part like the thread?”



Similes be no argument; that be why they convince people so: Alfred

capitulate to the scissor and thread; and only ask with abnormal

humility to be allow to taste the joy of reconciliation for two day.

the third find he at Oxford; he call on the head of his college

to explain what have prevent his return to exeter in the October term

twelve month ago, and ask for room. instead of side with a man

of his own college so cruelly injure, the dignitary be alarm by

the bare accusation, and say he must consider: insanity be a terrible

thing.



“so be false accusation, and so be false imprisonment,” say Hardie

bitterly.



“unquestionably. but I have at present no mean of decide how far

those word apply.” in short, he could give no answer; must consult the

other officer, and would convey the result by letter.



Alfred's pride be deeply mortify, not less by a certain cold

repugnant manner than by the word. and there come over his heart a

sickening feeling that he be now in the eye of man an intellectual

leper.



he go to another college directly, and apply to the vice-president,

the vice-president send he with a letter to the dean; the dean look

frightened; and tell he hesitatingly the college be full; he might put

his name down, and perhaps get in next year. Alfred retire, and learn

from the porter that the college be not full. he sigh deeply, and the

sickening feeling grow on he; an ineradicable stigma seem upon he,

and Mrs. Dodd be no bad than the rest of the world then; every mother

in England would approve her resolution. he wander about the scene

of his intellectual triumph: he stand in the great square of the

school, a place ugly to unprejudiced eye, but withal somewhat grand

and inspire, especially to scholar who have fight their keen and

bloodless battle there. he look at the window and gilt inscription

of the Schola Metaphysices, in which he have meet the scholar of his day

and defeat they for the Ireland. he wander into the theatre, and

eye the rostrum, whence he have not mumble, but recite, his Latin

prize poem with more than one thunder of academic applause: thunder

compare with which Drury Lane's we a mere cracker. these place be

unchanged; but he, sad scholar, wander among they as if he be a

ghost, and all these be stony phantom of an intellectual past, never,

never to return.



he telegraph Sampson and Edward to furnish he with certificate that

he have never be insane, but the victim of a foul conspiracy; and, when

he receive they, he go with they to St. Margaret's Hall; for he

have bethink he that the new principal be a first-rate man, and have

openly vow he would raise that “refuge for the oft-time phoughe” to

a place of learn.



Hardie call, send in his card, and be admit to the principal's

study. he be about to explain who he be, when the doctor interrupt

he, and tell he politely he know he by reputation. “tell I rather,”

 say he shrewdly, “to what I owe this application from an undergraduate

so distinguished as Mr. Hardie?”



then Alfred begin to quake, and, instead of reply, put a hand

suddenly before his face, and lose courage for one moment.



“come, Mr. Hardie,” say the principal, “don't be disconcert: a fault

regret be half atone; and I be not disposed to be hard on the error

of youth; I mean where there be merit to balance they.”



“Sir,” say Alfred sadly, “it be not a fault I have to acknowledge, but

a misfortune.”



“tell I all about it,” say Dr. Alder guardedly.



he tell it, omit nothing essential that could touch the heart or

excite the ironical humour of an academician.



“well, 'truth be more wonderful than fiction,'” say the doctor. and I

conclude the reader of this tale be all of the doctor's opinion; so

sweet to the mind be cannot.



Alfred offer his certificate.



now Dr. Alder have be ask himself in what phrase he should decline

this young genius, who be sane now, but of course have be mad, only

have forget the circumstance. but the temptation to get an Ireland

scholar into his Hall suddenly overpower he. the probability that he

might get a first-class in a lucid interval be too enticing; nothing

venture, nothing have. he determine to venture a good deal.



“Mr. Hardie,” say he, “this house shall always be open to good moral

and good scholarship while I preside over it, and it shall be open to

they all the more when they come to I dignified, and make sacred, by

'unmerited calamity.'”



now this fine speech, like Minerva herself, come from the head. Alfred

be overcome by it to tear. at that the doctor's heart be touch, and

even begin to fancy it have originate that noble speech.



it be no use do thing by half; so Dr. Alder give Alfred a

delightful set of room; and make the Hall pleasant to he. he

be reward by a grow conviction that he have make an excellent

acquisition. this opinion, however, be anything but universal: and

Alfred find the man of his own college suspect his sanity, and

pass joke behind his back, cut they all dead, and confine himself to

his little Hall. there they pet he, and crow about he, and bet

on he for the school as freely as if he be a colt the Hall be go

to enter for the Derby.



he read hard, and judiciously, but without his old confidence: he become

anxious and doubtful; he have see so many first-rate man just miss

a first-class. the brilliant creature analyse all his aristotelian

treatise, and write the synopsis clear with marginal reference on

great pasteboard card three foot by two, and so keep the whole subject

before his eye, till he obtain a singular mastery. same system with

the historian: nor do he disdain the use of coloured ink. then the

brilliant creature draw list of all the hard word he encounter in

his reading, especially in the common book, and read these list till

master. the stake be singularly heavy in his case, so he guard

every crevice.



and at this period he be not so unhappy as he expect. the laborious

day go swiftly, and twice a week at least come a letter from Julia.

oh, how his grave academic room with oaken panel do brighten, when

her letter lie on the table. it be open, and seem write with

sunbeam. no quarrel on paper! absence make the heart grow fonder. and

Edward come to see he, and over their wine let out a feminine trait

in Julia. “when Hurd call, she walk out of the room, just as my poor

mother do when you come. that be spite: since you be send away,

nobody else be to profit by it. where be her Christianity, eh? and echo

answer--got a cigar, old fellow?” and, after puff in silence awhile,

he say resignedly, “I be an unnatural monster.”



“oh, be you?” say the other serenely; for he be also under the benign

influence.



“yes,” say Edward, “I be your ally, and a mere spy in the camp of those

two lady. I watch all their move for your sake.”



Alfred forgave he. and thus his whole life be change, and for nearly

twelve month (for Dr. Alder let he reside in the Hall through the

vacation) he pursue the quiet tenor of a student's life, interrupt at

time by law; but that be another topic.



     WIFE and no WIFE.



Mrs. Dodd be visibly shake by that calamity which make her shrink with

horror from the sight of Alfred Hardie. in the winter she be so unwell

that she give up her duty with Messrs. Cross and Co. her connection

with they have be creditable to both party. I believe I forgot to say

why they trust she so; well, I must tell it elsewhere. David off her

hand, she be independent, and have lose the motive and the heart for

severe work. she tell the partner she could no long do they justice,

and leave they, to their regret. they then advise she to set up as a

milliner, and offer her credit for good at cash price up to two

thousand pound. she thank they like a sorrowful queen, and go she

way.



in the spring she recover some spirit and health; but at midsummer a

great and subtle misfortune befell she. her mind be bent on David night

and day, and use to struggle to evade the law of space that bind

its gross companion, and find she lose husband on the sea. she often

dream of he, but vaguely. but one fatal night she have a dream as clear

as daylight, and sharp as white pebble in the sun. she be on a large

ship with gun; she see man bring a dead sailor up the side; she see all

their face, and the dead man's too. it be David. his face be white.

a clear voice say he be to be bury in the deep next morning. she see

the deck at her foot, the breech of the gun, so clear, so define,

that, when she awake, and find herself in the dark, she think reality

be the illusion. she tell the dream to Julia and Edward. they try

to encourage she, in vain. “I see he,” she say, “I see he; it be a

vision, not a dream; my David be dead. well, then, I shall not be long

behind he.”



Dr. Sampson ridicule her dream to her face. but to her child he tell

another story. “I be anxious about she,” he say, “most anxious. there

be no mortal ill the distempered brain may not cause. be it not devilish

we can hear nothing of he? she will fret herself into the grave, as

sure as fate, if something do not turn up.”



her child could not console she; they try, but something hang round

their own heart, and chill every effort. in a word, they share she

fear. how came she to see he on board a ship with gun? in her wake

hour she always say he be on a merchant ship. be it not one of

those vision, which come to mortal and give they sometimes a peep into

Space, and, far more rarely, a glance into Time?



one day in the autumn, Alfred, be in town on law business, meet what

seem the ghost of Mrs. Dodd in the street. she see he not; her eye

be on that ghastly face she have see in her dream. it flash through

his mind that she would not live long to part he and Julia. but he

discourage the ungenerous thought; almost forgave her repugnance to

himself, and feel it would be bad than useless to ask Julia to leave

her mother, who be leave she visibly.



but her horror of he be anything but soften; and she use to tell

Dr. Sampson she think the sight of that man would kill she now. Edward

himself begin to hope Alfred would turn his affection elsewhere. the

house in Pembroke Street be truly the house of mourn now; all their

calamity be light compare with this.



     the DISTRICT VISITOR.



while Julia be write letter to keep up Alfred's heart, she be very

sad herself moreover, he have leave she for Oxford but a very few day,

when she receive an anonymous letter; her first. it be write in a

female hand, and couch in friendly and sympathetic term. the writer

think it only fair to warn she that Mr. Alfred Hardie be passionately

fond of a lady in the asylum, and have offer her marriage. if Miss Dodd

wish to be deceive, let she burn this letter and think no more of

it; if not, let she insert this advertisement in the Times: “the whole

Truth.--L. D.,” and her correspondent would communicate particular by

word or writing.



what a barbed and poison arrow be to the body, be this letter to

Julia's mind. she sit cold as a stone with this poison in her hand. then

come an impetuous impulse to send it down to Alfred, and request he to

transfer the other half of his heart to his lady of the asylum. then she

pause; and remember how much unjust suspicion have be level at

he already. what right have she to insult he? she would try and keep

the letter to herself. as to act upon it, her good sense speedily

suggest it come from the rival in question, real or suppose. “she

want to make use of I,” say Julia; “it be plain Alfred do not care

much for she; or why do she come to I?” she put the letter in her

desk, and it rankle in her heart. _Hoeret lateri lethalis arundo._ she

tremble at herself; she feel a savage passion have be touch in she.

she pray day and night against jealousy.



but I must now, to justify my heading, skip some month, and relate a

remarkable incident that befell she in the said character. on the first

of August in this year, a good christian woman, one of her patient,

ask she to call on Mr. Barkington, that lodge above. “he be a decent

body, miss, and between you and I, I think his complaint be, he don't

get quite enough to eat.”



“Barkington!” say Julia, and put her hand to her bosom. she go and

tap at his door.



“come in,” say a shrillish voice.



she enter, and find a weazened old man seat, mend his own coat.



he rise, and she tell he she be a district visitor. he say he have

hear of she; they call she the beautiful lady in that court. this be

news to she, and make her blush. she ask leave to read a chapter to

he; he listen as to some gentle memory of childhood. she prescribe

he a glass of port wine, and dispense it on the instant. thus

physic, her patient become communicative, and chatter on about his

native place--but do not name it--and talk about the people there.

now our district visitor be, if the truth must be tell, a compounder.

she would permit her pupil to talk about earthly affair, on condition

they would listen to heavenly one before she go. so she let this old

man run on, and he tell she he have be a banker's clerk all his life,

and save a thousand pound, and come up to London to make his fortune

on the Stock Exchange; and there he be sometimes a bull, and sometimes

a bear, and whichever he be, certain fox call broker and jobber

get the profit and he the loss. “it's all the same as a gambling-table,”

 say he. “the jobber and broker have get the same odd the bank have at

Rouge et Noir, and the little capitalist like I be doom beforehand.”

 then he tell she that there be a crossing-sweeper near the Exchange who

come from his native place, and have start as a speculator, and come

down to that. only he call it rise, and use to speak with a shudder

of when he dabble in the fund, and often tell he to look sharp, and

get a crossing. and lo! one day when he be clean out, and desperate,

and hover with the other ghost of little capitalist about the tomb

of their money, he see his countryman fall flat, and the broom fly

out of his hand. instantly he make a rush, and so do a wooden-legged

sailor; but he get first to the broom, and begin to sweep while other

pick up his countryman, who prove dead as a herring; and he succeed

to his broom, and it make money by the Exchange, though he never could.

still, one day he pick up a pocket-book in that neighbourhood, with

a lump of money, which he straightway advertise in--no newspaper.

and now, Julia think it time to interpose the eighth commandment, the

golden rule, and such branch of learn.



he become a favourite of her: he have so much to say: she even think

she have see his face before: but she could not tell where. she give he

good book and tract; and read to he, and plough his heart with her

sweet voice, and sow the good seed in the furrow--seed which, like

wheat or other grain, often seem to fall flat and die, but come out

green after many day.



one Saturday she invite he to dine with the servant next day. he come

during church time, and go away in the afternoon while she be with

her mother. but she ask Sarah, who prove eager to talk about he. “he

be a rum customer; keep ask question all dinner time. 'well,' say

I, 'you're good company you be; be you a lawyer; for you examine we;

but you don't tell we nothing.' ye see, Miss, Jane she be that simple,

she be tell he everything, and about Mr. Alfred's lawsuit with his

father and all.”



Julia say that be indiscreet; but after all what do it matter?



“who know, Miss?” Sarah reply: “least say be soon mend. if you

please, Miss, who be he? where do he bide? where do he come from?

do he know hardy?”



“I should think not. why?”



“because I'm much mistaken if he don't.” then put on a stolid

look, she ask, “do he know your papa?”



“oh no, Sarah. how should he?”



“there now,” say Sarah; “Miss, you be all in the dark about this old

man: I'll tell you something; I take he out of the way of Jane's

temper when she begin a dishing up, and I have he into the parlour for

a minute; and in course there he see the picture of your poor papa hung

up. Miss, if you'll believe I, the moment he clap eye on that there

picture, he halloe out, and out go his two hand like this here.

'it's he!' say he; 'it's he!' and stare at the picture like a

stuck pig. Forgot I be close behind he, I do believe. 'she's _his_

daughter,' say he, in a whisper, a curious whisper; seem to come

out of his stomack. 'what's the matter now?' say I, just so. he give

a great start, as if my speaking have waken he from a dream, and say

he, 'nothing,' as quiet as a lamb. 'nothing ben't much,' say I, just

so. 'it usedn't to be anything at all when I be your age,' say he,

sneer. but I pay he a good coin: say I, 'old man, where you come

from do the folk use to start and hallo out and cry “it's he! she's

his daughter!” and fle their two arm abroad like a wiumdmill in

March, and all for--nothing?' so at that he change as white as my

smock, and fall all of a tremble. however, at dinner he perk up, and

draw that poor simple Jane out a good one. but he don't look towards I

much, which I set opposite to watch my lord.”



“Sarah,” say Julia, “this be really curious, mysterious; you be a

good, watchful, faithful girl; and, to tell the truth, I sometimes fancy

I have see Mr. Barkington's face. however, I will solve this little

mystery to-morrow; for I will ask he: thank you, Sarah.”



on Monday she call on Mr. Barkington to solve the mystery. but,

instead of solve, her visit thicken it: for Mr. Barkington be go

bag and baggage. when Edward be tell of this business, he think it

remarkable, and regret he have not see the old man.



so do I; for it be my belief Edward would have recognise he.



     DAVID DODD.



the history of a man be the history of his mind. and that be why you

have hear so little of late about the simple, noble, and

most unfortunate of all my personage. insanity be as various as

eccentricity. I have spare the kind-hearted reader some of David's

vagary. however, when we part with he, he have settle into

that strange phase of lunacy, in which the distant past seem nearly

obliterate, and memory exist, but revolve in a narrow round of thing

present: this be accompany with a positive illusion, to wit, a fix

idea that he be an able seaman: and, as usual, what mental power he

retain come out strong in support of this idea. all this be mark

by a bodily agility somewhat more than natural in a man of his age.

owe to the wind astern, he be enable to run into Portsmouth before

the steam-tug come up with he: and he do run into port, not because

he fear pursuit, but because he be desperately hungry; and he have no

suicidal tendency whatever.



he make for a public-house, and call for some bread and cheese and

beer; they be supply, and then lo! he have no money to pay for they.

“I'll owe you till I come back from sea, my bo,” say he coolly. on this

the landlord collar he, and David shake he off into the road, much

as a terri throw a rat from he; then there be a row, and a naval

officer, who be cruise about for hand, come up and hear it. there

be nothing at all unseamanlike in David's conduct, and the gentleman

take a favourable view of it, and pay the small demand; but not with

unleavened motive. he be the second lieutenant of H. M. frigate

_vulture;_ she have a bad name, thank to her last captain, and be short

of hand: he take David aside and ask he would he like to ship on

board the _vulture._



David say yes, and suggest the foretop. “oh yes,” growl the

lieutenant, “you all want to be there.” he then gauge this Jacky Tar's

intellect; ask he _inter alia_ how to send a frigate's foretop

gallant yard down upon deck: and to show how seamanship stick in the

brain when once it get there, David actually tell he. “you be rather

old,” say the lieutenant, “but you be a seaman:” and so take he on

board the _vulture_ at Spithead, before Green begin to search the town

in earnest. nobody act his part well than some demented person do:

and David make a very tolerable sailor notwithstanding his forty-five

year: and the sea do he good within certain limit. between he and

the past lie some intellectual or cerebral barrier as impenetrable

as the great wall of China; but on the hither side of that wall his

faculty improve. of course, the crew soon find out the gap in his

poor brain, and call he Soft Billy, and play on he at first. but

by degree he win their affection; he be so wonderfully sweet-temper:

and besides his mind be in an abnormal state, he loathe grog, and

give his allowance to his messmate. one day he show an unexpected

trait; they be lie becalm in southern latitude, and, time hang

heavily, each wile it how he might: one fiddle, another write to his

Polly, another fish for shark, another whistle for a wind, score

fall into the form of meditation without the reality, and one get a

piece of yarn and amuse himself kill fly on the bulwark. now this

shock poor Billy: he put out his long arm and intercept a stroke.

“what be the row?” say the operator.



“you mustn't,” say Billy solemnly, look into his face with great

dreamy eye.



“you be----,” say the other, and lend he a tap on the cheek with the

yarn. Billy do not seem to mind this; his skin have little sensibility,

owe to his disorder.



Jack recommence on his fly, and the bystander laugh. they always

laugh now at everything Billy say, as Society use to laugh when

the late Theodore Hook ask for the mustard at dinner; and would

have laugh if he have say, “you see I sad, I have just lose my poor

father.”



David stand look on at the slaughter with a helpless puzzled air.



at last he seem to have an idea, he catch Jack up by the throat and

knee, lift he with gigantic strength above his head, and be just

go to hurl he shriek into the sea, when a dozen strong hand

interfere, and save the man. then they be go to bind Billy

hand and foot; but he be discover to be perfectly calm; so they

remonstrate instead, and presently Billy's commander-in-chief, a

ship-boy call Georgie White, shove in and ask he in a shrill

haughty voice how he dare do that. “my dear,” say Billy, with great

humility and placidity, “he be kill God's creature, no allowance: *

so, ye see, to save their life, I be _oblige._”



     *nautical phrase, mean without stint or limit, or

     niggardly admeasurement as there be of grog.



at this piece of reasoning, and the simplicity and gentle conviction

with which it be deliver, there be a roar. it subside, and a doubt

arise whether Billy be altogether in the wrong.



“well,” say one, “I daresay life be sweet to they little creature, if

they could speak their mind.”



“I've know a ship founder in a fair breeze all along of kill 'em,”

 say one old salt.



finally, several side with Billy, and intimate that “it serve the

lubber right for not listen to _reason._” and, indeed, methink it

be lovely and touch that so divine a ray of goodness and superior

reason should have shoot from his heart or from Heaven across that poor

benighted brain.



but it must be own his mode of show his humanity be somewhat

excessive and abnormal, and smack of lunacy. after this, however, the

affection of his messmate be not so contemptuous.



now the captain of the _vulture_ be Billy's cousin by marriage.

Reginald Bazalgette. twenty year ago, when the captain be a boy, they

be great friend: of late Bazalgette have see less of he; still it

seem strange he do not recognise he in his own ship. but one or two

cause co-operate to prevent that. in the first place, the mind

when turn in one direction be not so sharp in another; and Captain

Bazalgette have be tell to look for David in a merchant ship bind for

the East Indies. in the next place, insanity alter the expression of

the face wonderfully, and the captain of a frigate run his eye over

four hundred sailor at muster, or a hundred at work, not to examine

their feature, but their dress and bear at the one, and their

handiness at the other. the bad piece of luck be that Mrs. Dodd do

not know David call himself William Thompson. so there stand “William

Thompson” large as life on the ship's book, and nobody the wiser.

Captain Bazalgette have a warm regard and affection for Mrs. Dodd,

and do all he could. indeed, he take great liberty: he stop and

overhaul several merchant ship for the truant; and, by-the-by, on

one occasion William Thompson be one of the boat's crew that row a

midshipman from the _vulture_ alongside a merchant ship to search for

David Dodd. he hear the name and circumstance mention in the boat,

but the very name be new to he. he remember it, but only from

that hour; and tell his love tyrant, Georgie White, they have be

overhaul a merchant ship and look for one David Dodd.



it be about Midsummer the _vulture_ anchor off one of the South Sea

island, and send a boat ashore for fruit. Billy and his dearly beloved

little tyrant, Georgie White, be among the crew. off go Georgie to

bathe, and Billy sit down on the beach with a love eye upon he. the

water be calm: but the boy with the heedlessness of youth stay in it

nearly an hour: he be seize with cramp and scream to his comrade.

they run, but they be half a mile from the boat. Billy dash into the

water and come up with Georgie just as he be sink for the last time;

the boy grip he; but by his great strength he disentangle himself

and get Georgie on his shoulder, and swam for the shore. meantime the

sailor get into the boat, and row hastily towards they.



now Billy be undermost and his head under water at time, and Georgie,

some thought, have help strangle he by grip his neck with both

arm. anyway, by the boy's account, just as they be get into

shallow water, Billy give a great shriek and turn over on his back;

and Georgie paddle with his hand, but Billy soon after this sink like

a dead body while the boat be yet fifty yard off. and Georgie scream

and point to the place, and the boat come up and take Georgie in; and

the water be so clear that the sailor see Billy lie motionless at the

bottom, and hook he with a boat hook and draw he up; but his face

come up alongside a deadly white, with stare eye, and they shudder

and fear it be too late.



they take he into a house and strip he, and rub he, and wrap

he in blanket, and put he by the hot fire. but all would not do.



then, having dry his clothe, they dress the body again and lay

he in the boat, and cast the Union Jack over he, and row slowly and

unwillingly back to the ship, Georgie sob and scream over the

body, and not a dry eye in the boat.



the body be carry up the side, and uncover, just as Mrs. Dodd see

in her dream. the surgeon be send for and examine the body: and then

the grim routine of a man-of-war deal swiftly with the poor skipper. he

be carry below to be prepare for a sailor's grave. then the surgeon

walk aft and report formally to the officer of the watch the death

by drown of William Thompson. the officer of the watch go instantly

to the captain in his cabin and report the death. the captain give

the stereotyped order to bury he at noon next day; and the body be

strip that night and sew up in his hammock, with a portion of his

clothe and bed to conceal the outline of the corpse, and two cannon

ball at his foot; and so the poor skipper be lay out for a watery

grave, and cover by the Union Jack.





I don't know whether any of my amorous young reader be much affect

by the catastrophe I have just relate. if not, I will just remind they

that even Edward Dodd be prepared to oppose the marriage of Julia and

Alfred, if any serious ill should befall his father at sea, owe to

Alfred's imprudent interference in rescue he from Drayton House.













CHAPTER L



law



MINUTE study of my fellow-creature have reveal to I that there be

many intelligent person who think that a suit at law commence in

court. this be not so. many suit be fight and decide by the special

pleader, and so never come into court; and, as a stiff encounter of

this kind actually take place in Hardie _v._ Hardie, a word of prefatory

explanation may be proper. suitor come into court only to try an issue:

an issue be a mutual lie direct: and towards this both party be

drive upon paper by the law of pleading, which may be thus sum: 1.

every statement of the adversary must either be contradict flat, or

confess and avoid: “avoid” mean neutralise by fresh matter. 2.

nothing must be advance by plaintiff which do not disclose a ground

of action at law. 3. nothing advance by defendant, which, if true,

would not be a defence to the action. these rule exclude in a vast

degree the pitiable defect and vice that mark all the unprofessional

argument one ever hear; for on a breach of any one of the say rule

the other party can demur; the demurrer be argue before the judge

in Banco, and, if successfully, the faulty plaint or faulty plea be

dismiss, and often of course the cause win or lose thereby, and the

country save the trouble, and the suitor the expense of try an

issue.



so the writ be serve by Plt.'s attorney, and an appearance put in by

Deft.'s, the paper battle begin by Alfred Hardie, through his attorney,

serve on Deft.'s attorney “the DECLARATION.” this be draw by his

junior counsel, Garrow, and run thus, after specify the court and the

date:



_middlesex to wit_ Alfred Hardie by John Compton his attorney sue

Thomas Hardie for that the Deft. assault Plt. give he into custody

to a certain person and cause he to be imprison for a long space

of time in a certain place to wit a Lunatic Asylum whereby the Plt. be

much inconvenience and suffer much anguish and pain in mind and body

and be unable to attend to his affair and be injure in his credit

and circumstance.



and the Plt. claim L. 5000.



Mr. Compton convey a copy of this to Alfred, and say it be a sweet

“declaration.” “what,” say Alfred, “be that all I have suffer at

these miscreant' hand? why, it be write with an icicle.”



Mr. Compton explain that this be the outline: “Counsel will lay the

colour on in court as thick as you like.”



the defendant reply to the above declaration by three plea.



by statute 8 & 9 Vic., c. 100, s. 105.



1. the Deft. by Joseph Heathfield his attorney say he be not guilty. 2.

and for a further Plea the Deft, say that before and at the time of the

allege imprisonment Plt, be a person of unsound mind and incompetent

to take care of himself and a proper person to be take care of and

detain and it be unfit unsafe improper and dangerous that he should

be at large thereupon the Deft, be the uncle of the Plt. and a

proper person to cause the Plt. to be take charge of under due care and

treatment in that behalf do cause the Plt. to be so take charge of and

detain under due care and treatment, &c. &c.



the third plea be the stinger, but too long to cite _verbatim;_ it

go to this tune, that the plaintiff at and before the time &c. have

conduct himself like a person of unsound mind &c. and two certificate

that he be insane have be give by two person duly authorise under

the statute to sign such certificate, and the defendant have believe

and do _bona fide_ believe these certificate to be true, &c. &c.



the first of these plea be a mere formal plea, under the statute.



the second raise the very issue at common law the plaintiff wish to

try.



the third make John Compton knit his brow with perplexity. “this be a

very nasty plea,” say he to Alfred: “a regular trap. if we join issue

on it we must be defeat; for how can we deny the certificate be in

form; and yet the plaguy thing be not loose enough to be demur to?

Colls, who draw these plea for they?”



“Mr. Colvin, sir.”



“make a note to employ he in our next stiff pleading.”



Alfred be stagger. he have think to ride rough-shod over

defendant--a common expectation of plaintiff; but seldom realise.

lawyer fight hard. the plea be take to Garrow; he say there be

but one course, to demur to no. 3. so the plaintiff “join issue on all

the defendant's plea, and as to the last plea the plaintiff say the

same be bad in substance.” Defendant rejoin that the same be good in

substance, and thus Hardie _v._ Hardie divide itself into two case,

a question of law for the judge, and an issue for the mixed tribunal

loosely call a jury. and I need hardly say that should the defendant

win either of they he would gain the cause.



postpone the history of the legal _question,_ I shall show how Messrs.

Heathfield fight off the _issue,_ and cool the ardent Alfred and

sicken he of law.



in theory every Englishman have a right to be try by his peer: but

in fact there be five gentleman in every court, each of whom have by

precedent the power to refuse he a jury, by simply postpone the trial

term after term, until the death of one of the party, when the action,

if a personal one, die too; and, by a singular anomaly of judicial

practice, if a slippery Deft. cann't persuade A. or B., judge of the

common law court, to connive at what I venture to call



     the POSTPONEMENT swindle,



he can actually go to C., D., and B., one after another, with his

reject application, and the previous refusal of the other judge

to delay and baffle justice go for little or nothing; so that the

postpone swindler have five to one in his favour.



Messrs. Heathfield begin this game unluckily. they apply to a judge in

chamber for a month to plead. Mr. Compton oppose in person, and show

that this be absurd. the judge allow they only four day to plead.

issue be join, Mr. Compton push on for trial, and the cause be

set down for the November term. towards the end of the term Messrs.

Heathfield apply to one of the puisne judge for a postponement, on

the ground that a principal witness could not attend. application be

support by the attorney's affidavit, to the effect that Mr. Speers

be in Boulogne, and have write to he to say that he have meet with a

railway accident, and fear he could not possibly come to England

in less than a month. a respectable french doctor confirm this by

certificate. Compton oppose, but the judge would hardly hear he, and

postpone the trial as a matter of course; this carry it over the

sitting into next term. Alfred groaned, but bear it patiently; not so

Dr. Sampson: he rage against secret tribunal: “see how man deteriorate

the moment they get out of the full light of publeecity. what English

judge, sit in the light of Shorthand, would admit 'Jack swear that

Gill say' for legal evidence. speer have swear to no fack. Heathfield

have swear to no fack but th' existence of Speer's hearsay. they be a

couple o' lyre. I'll bet ye ten pound t' a shill Speers be as well

as I'm.”



Mr. Compton quietly remind he there be a direct statement--the

french doctor's certificate.
