“a medical certificut!” shriek Sampson, amazed. “Mai--dearr--sirr,

a medical certificut be just an article o' commerce like an attorney's

conscience. Gimme a guinea and I'll get you swear sick, diseased,

disabled, or dead this minute, whichever you like well.”



“come, doctor, don't fly off: you say you'd bet ten pound to a

shill Speers be not an invalid at all. I say do.”



“do.”



“how will you find out?”



“how? why set the thief-taker on um, to be sure.”



he write off to the prefect of police at Boulogne, and in four day

receive an answer head “information in the interest of family.” the

prefect inform he there have be no railway accident: but that the

Sieur Speers, English subject, have really hurt his leg get out of a

railway carriage six week ago, and have keep his room some day; but

he have be cure some week, and go about his business, and make an

excursion to Paris.



on this Compton offer Sampson the shilling. but he decline to take

it. “the lie be self-evident,” say he; “and here's a judge wouldn't

see't, and an attorney couldn't. be all their life sift evidence,

too. oh the darkness of the profissional mind!”



the next term come. Mr. Compton deliver the brief and fee,

subpoena the witness, &c., and Alfred come up with a good heart to

get his stigma remove by twelve honest man in the light of day: but

first one case be take out of its order and put before he, then

another, till term wear near an end. then Messrs. Heathfield apply

to another judge of the court for a postponement. Mr. Richard Hardie,

plaintiff's father, a most essential witness, be ill at Clare Court.

medical certificate and letter herewith.



Compton oppose. now this judge be a keen and honourable lawyer, with a

lofty hatred of all professional trick. he hear the two attorney, and

deliver himself to this effect, only of course in well legal phrase:

“I shall make no order. the defendant have be here before on a doubtful

affidavit. you know, Mr. Heathfield, jury in these case go by the

plaintiff's evidence, and his conduct under cross-examination. and

I think it would not be just nor humane to keep this plaintiff in

suspense, and _civiliter mortuum,_ any long. you can take out a

commission to examine Richard Hardie.”



to this Mr. Compton nail he, but the commission take time; and

while it be pende, Mr. Heathfield go to another judge with another

disabled witness: Peggy Black. that naive personage be nurse

her deceased sister's child--in an affidavit: and they have

scarlatina--surgeon's certificate to that effect. Compton oppose, and

point out the blot. “you don't want the child in the witness-box,”

 say he: “and we be not to be rob of our trial because one of

your witness prefer nurse other people's child to face the

witness-box.”



the judge nod assent. “I make no order,” say he.



Mr. Heathfield go out from his presence and send a message by

telegraph to Peggy Black. “you must have Scar. yourself, and telegraph

the same at once: certificate by post.”



the accommodate maiden telegraph back that she have unfortunately

take scarlatina of the child: medical certificate to follow by

post. four judge out of the five be now awake to the move. but

Mr. Heathfield tinker the hole in his late affidavit with Peggy's

telegram, and slip down to Westminster to the chief judge of

the court, who have have no opportunity of watch the growth and

dissemination of disease among Deft.'s, witness. Compton fight this

time by counsel and with a powerful affidavit. but luck be against he.

the judge have rise to go home: he listen stand; Compton's counsel

be feeble; do not feel the wrong. how could he? lawyer fatten by

delay of justice, as physician do by tardy cure. the postponement be

grant.



Alfred curse they all, and his own folly in believe that an alleged

lunatic would be allow fair play at Westminster, or anywhere else.

Compton take snuff, and Sampson appeal to the press again. he write a

long letter expose with fearless irony the postponement swindle as

it have be work in Hardie _v._ Hardie: and wound up with this fiery

peroration:



“this Englishman sue not merely for damage, but to recover lose right

dearer far than money, of which he say he have be unjustly rob: his

right to walk in daylight on the soil of his native land without be

seize and tie up for life like a nigger or a dog; his footing in

society; a chance to earn his bread; and a place among mankind:

ay, among mankind; for a lunatic be an animal in the law's eye and

society's, and an alleged lunatic be a lunatic till a jury clear he.



“I appeal to you, gentleman, be not such a suitor sacred in all wise and

good man's mind? be he not defendant as well as plaintiff? why, his

stake be enormous compare with the nominal defendant's; and, if I know

right from wrong, to postpone his trial a fourth time would be to insult

divine justice, and trifle with human misery, and shock the common sense

of nation.”



the doctor's pen neither clip the word nor mince the matter,

you see. read this the water come into Alfred's eye. “ah, staunch

friend,” he say, “how few be like you! to the intellectual dwarf

who conspire with my oppressor, Hardie _v._ Hardie be but a family

squabble. _Parvis omnia parva._” Mr. Compton read it too; and say from

the bottom of his heart, “Heaven defend we from our friend! this be

enough to make the court decline to try the case at all.”



and, indeed, it do not cure the evil: for next term another _malade

affidavitaire_ be set up. speer to wit. this gentleman depose

to having come over on purpose to attend the trial; but having

inadvertently step aside as far as Wales, he lie there stricken

with a mysterious malady, and have just strength to forward medical

certificate. on this the judge in spite of remonstrance, adjourn

Hardie _v._ Hardie to the summer term. summer come, the evil day draw

nigh: Mr. Heathfield get the venue change from Westminster to London,

which be the fifth postponement. at last the cause come on: the party

and witness be all in court, with two whole day before they to try

it in.



Dr. Sampson rush in furious. “there be some deviltry afloat,” say

he. “I be in the House of Commons last night, and there I see the

defendant's counsel earwigge the judge.”



“nonsense,” say Mr. Compton, “such suspicion be ridiculous. do you

think they can talk of nothing but Hardie _v._ Hardie?”



“Mai--dearr sirr--my son meet one of Heathfield's clerk at dinner,

and he let out that the trile be not to come off. put this and that

together now.”



“it will come off,” say Mr. Compton, “and in five minute at farth.”



in less than that time the learn judge come in, and before take his

seat make this extraordinary speech:



“I hear this cause will take three day to try; and we have only two

day before we. it would be inconvenient to leave it unfinished; and I

must proceed on circuit the day after to-morrow. it must be a remanet:

no man can do more than time allow.”



Plaintiff's counsel make a feeble remonstrance; then yield. and the

crier with sonorous voice call on the case of Bread _v._ Cheese, in

which there be pound at stake, but no principle. oh, with what z

they all go into it; be small man escape from a great thing to

a small one. never hop frog into a ditch with more alacrity. Alfred

leave the court and hide himself, and the scald tear force their way

down his cheek at this heartless proceeding: to let all the witness

come into court at a vast expense to the party: and raise the cup of

justice to the lip of the oppress, and then pretend he know a trial

would last more than two day, and so shirk it. “I'd have make that a

reason for sit till midnight” say poor Alfred, “not for prolong

a poor injure man's agony four mortal month.” he then pray God

earnestly for this great postponer's death as the only event that could

give he back an Englishman's right of be try by his peer, and so

go down to Oxford break-hearted.



as for Sampson he be most indignant, and say a public man have no

business with a private ear: and want to appeal to the press again:

but the doughty doctor have a gentle but powerful ruler at home, as fiery

house be well rule by a gentle hand. Mrs. Sampson request he

to write no more, but look round for an M. P. to draw these repeat

defeat of justice to the notice of the House. now there be a Mr.

Bite, who have take a prominent and honourable part in lunacy question;

head committee and so on: this seem the man. Dr. Sampson send he

a letter say there be a flagrant case of a sane man falsely

imprison, who have now be near a year apply for a jury, and

juggle out of this constitutional right by arbitrary and unreasonable

postponement: would Mr. Bite give he (Dr. Sampson) ten minute and

no more, when he would explain the case and leave documentary evidence

behind he for Mr. Bite to test his statement. the philanthropical M. P.

reply promptly in these exact word:



“Mr. Bite present his compliment to Dr. Sampson to state that it be

impossible for he to go into his case, nor to give he the time he

request to do so.”



Sampson be a little indignant at the man's insolence; but far more at

having be dupe by his public assumption of philanthropy. “the little

pragmatical impostor!” he roar. “with what a sense o' relief th'

animal fling off the mask of humanity when there be no easy eclat to be

gain by putting't on.” he send the philanthropical Bite's revelation

of his private self to Alfred, who return it with this single remark:

_“Homunculi quanti sunt!_”



dishonest suitor all try to postpone; but they do not gain unmixed good

thereby. these delay give time for more evidence to come in; and this

slow coming and chance evidence be singularly adverse to the unjust

suitor. of this come a notable example in October next, and make Richard

Hardie determine to precipitate the trial, and even regret he have not

fight it out long ago.



he have just return from consult Messrs. Heathfield, and sit down

to a nice little dinner in his apartment (Sackville Street), when a

visitor be announce; and in come the slouch little figure of Mr.

Barkington, _alias_ Noah Skinner.



     DIAMOND CUT DIAMOND.



Mr. Hardie suppress a start, and say nothing. Skinner bow low with

a mixture of his old cringing way, and a certain sly triumphant leer,

so that his body seem to say one thing, and his face the opposite. Mr.

Hardie eye he, and see that his coat be rusty, and his hat napless:

then Mr. Hardie smell a beggar, and prepare to parry all attempt upon

his purse.



“I hope I see my old master well,” say Skinner coaxingly.



“pretty well in body, Skinner; thank you.”



“I have a deal of trouble to find you, sir. but I hear of the great

lawsuit between Mr. Alfred and you, and I know Mr. Heathfield be your

solicitor; so I watch at his place day after day: and at last you

come. oh, I be so pleased when I see your noble figure; but I wouldn't

speak to you in the street for fear of disgrace you. I'm such a poor

little guy to be address a gentleman like you.”



now this sound well on the surface, but below there be a subtle

something Mr. Hardie do not like at all: but he take the cue, and say,

“my poor Skinner, do you think I would turn up my nose at a faithful old

servant like you? have a glass of wine with I, and tell I how you have

be get on.” he go behind a screen and open a door, and soon

return with a decanter, leave the door open. now in the next room

sit, unbeknown to Skinner, a young woman with white eyelash,

sewing button on Mr. Hardie's shirt. that astute gentleman give

her instruction, and important one too, with a silent gesture; then

reappear and fill the bumper high to his faithful servant. they

drink one another's health with great cordiality, real or apparent. Mr.

Hardie then ask Skinner carelessly, if he could do anything for he.

Skinner say, “well, sir, I be very poor.”



“so be I, between you and I,” say Mr. Hardie confidentially; “I don't

mind tell you; those confound Commissioners of Lunacy write to

Alfred's trustee, and I have be force to replace a loan of five

thousand pound. that Board always side with the insane. that cripple

I, and drive I to the Exchange: and now what I have leave be all

invest in time-bargain. a month settle my fate: a little fortune, or

absolute beggary.”



“you'll be lucky, sir, you'll be lucky,” say Skinner cheerfully; “you

have such a long head; not like poor little I; the Exchange soon burn

my wing. not a shilling leave of the thousand pound, sir, you be

so good as to give I for my faithful service. but you will give I

another chance, sir, I know; I'll take well care this time.” Mr.

Hardie shake his head sorrowfully, and say it be impossible. Skinner

eye he askant, and remark quietly, and half aside, “of course, I

_could_ go to the other party: but I shouldn't like to do that. they

would come down handsome.”



“what other party?”



“La, sir, what other party? why Mrs. Dodd's, or Mr. Alfred's; here's the

trial come on, you know, and of course if they could get I to go on

the box and tell all I know, or half what I know, why the judge and jury

would say lock Mr. Alfred up for mad be a conspiracy.”



Mr. Hardie quake internally: but he hide it grandly, and once more be

a Spartan gnaw beneath his robe by this little fox. “what,” say he

sternly, “after all I and mine have do for you and your, would you be

so base as to go and sell yourself to my enemy?”



“never, sir,” shout Skinner zealously: then in a whisper, “not if

you'll make a bid for I.”



“how much do you demand?”



“only another thousand, sir?”



“a thousand pound!”



“why, what be that to you, sir? you be rich enough to buy the eighth

commandment out of the table of ten per cent.: and then the lawsuit,

hardy _versus_ Hardies!”



“you have speak plainly at last,” say Mr. Hardie grimly. “this be

extort money by threat. do you know that nothing be more criminal,

nor more easy to punish? I can take you before a magistrate, and

imprison you on the instant for this attempt. I will, too.”



“try it,” say Skinner coolly. “where's your witness?”



“behind that screen.”



Peggy come forward directly with a pen in her hand. Skinner be

manifestly startled and disconcert. “I have take all your word down,

Mr. Skinner,” say Peggy softly; then to her master, “shall I go for a

policeman, sir?”



Mr. Hardie reflect. “yes,” say he sternly: “there's no other course

with such a lump of treachery and ingratitude as this.”



Peggy whip on her bonnet.



“what a hurry you be in,” whine Skinner: “a policeman ought to be

the last argument for old friend to run to.” then, fawn spitefully,

“don't talk of indict I, sir,” say he; “it make I shiver: why how

will you look when I up and tell they all how Captain Dodd be take with

apoplexy in our office, and how you nail fourteen thousand pound off

his senseless body, and forget to put they down in your balance-sheet,

so they be not whitewash off like the rest.”



“any witness to all this, Skinner?”



“yes, sir.”



“who?”



“well; your own conscience _for one,_” say Skinner.



“he be mad, Peggy,” say Mr. Hardie, shrug his shoulder. he then

look Skinner full in the face, and say, “nobody be ever seize with

apoplexy in my office. nobody ever give I L. 14,000. and if this be

the probable tale with which you come here to break the law and extort

money, leave my house this instant: and if ever you dare to utter this

absurd and malicious slander, you shall lie within four stone wall, and

learn what it be for a shabby vagabond to come without a witness to his

back, and libel a man of property and honour.”



Skinner let he run on in this loud triumphant strain till he have quite

do; then put out a brown skinny finger, and poke he lightly in the

rib, and say quite quietly, and oh, so drily, with a know wink--



“I've--get--the Receipt.”











CHAPTER LI



MR. HARDIE collapse as if he have be a man inflate, and that touch

have puncture he. “ah!” say he. “ah!” say Skinner, in a mighty

different tone: insolent triumph to wit.



after a pause, Mr. Hardie make an effort and say contemptuously, “the

receipt (if any) be fling into the dusthole and carry away. do you

think I have forget that?”



“don't you believe it, sir,” be the reply. “while you turn your back

and sack the money, I say to myself, 'Oho, be that the game?' and

nail the receipt. what a couple of scoundrel we be! I wouldn't have

she know it for all your money. come, sir, I see its all right; you will

shell out soon than be post.”



here Peggy interpose; “Mr. Skinner, be more considerate; my master be

really poor just now.”



“that be no reason why I should be insult and indict and trample

under foot,” snarl Skinner all in one breath.



“show I the receipt and take my last shilling, you ungrateful,

vindictive viper,” groan Mr. Hardie.



“stuff and nonsense,” say Skinner. “I'm not a viper; I'm a man of

business. find I five hundred pound; and I'll show you the receipt and

keep dark. but I cann't afford to give it you for that, of course.”



Skinner triumph, and make the great man apologise, writhe all the

time, and wish he be a day labourer with Peggy to wife, and fourteen

honest shilling a week for his income. having eat humble pie, he

agree to meet Skinner next Wednesday at midnight, alone, under a

certain lamp on the North Kensington Road: the interval (four day)

he require to raise money upon his scrip. Skinner bow himself

out, fawn triumphantly. Mr. Hardie stand in the middle of the room

motionless, scowl darkly. Peggy look at he, and see some dark and

sinister resolve form in his mind: she divine it, as such woman can

divine. she lay her hand on his arm, and say softly, “Richard, it's

not worth _that._” he start to find his soul read through his body so

clearly. he tremble.



but it be only for a moment. “his blood be on his own head,” he

snarl. “this be not my seeking. he shall learn what it be to drive

Richard Hardie to despair.”



“no, no,” implore Peggy; “there be other country beside this: why

not gather all you have, and cross the water? I'll follow you to the

world's end, Richard.”



“mind your own business,” say he fiercely.



she make no reply, but go softly and sit down again, and sew the

button on his shirt. Mr. Hardie write to Messrs. Heathfield to get

Hardie _v._ Hardie try as soon as possible.



meantime come a mental phenomenon: glide down Sackville Street,

victorious Skinner suddenly stop, and clench his hand; and his

face writhe as if he have receive a death-wound. in that instant

remorse have strike he like lightning; and, perhaps, whence come the

lightning. the sweet face and voice that have smile on he, and care

for his body, and care for his soul, come to his mind, and knock

at his heart and conscience. he go home miserable with an inward

conflict; and it last he all the four day; sometimes Remorse get the

well, sometimes Avarice. he come to the interview still undecide what

he should do. but, meantime, he have go to a lawyer and make his will,

leave his little all to Julia Dodd: a bad sign this; look like

compound with his awakened conscience.



it be a dark and gusty night. very few people be about. Skinner

wait a little while, and shiver, for his avarice have postpone the

purchase of a greatcoat until Christmas Day. at last, when the coast

seem clear, Mr. Hardie emerge from a side street. Skinner put his

hand to his bosom.



they meet. Mr. Hardie say quietly, “I must ask you, just for form, to

show I you have the Receipt.”



“of course, sir; but not so near, please: no snatching, if I know it.”



“you be wonderfully suspicious,” say Mr. Hardie, try to smile.



Skinner look, and see by the lamplight he be deadly pale. “keep your

distance a moment, sir,” say he, and, on Mr. Hardie's complying, take

the Receipt out, and hold it under the lamp.



instantly Mr. Hardie draw a life-preserver, and spring on he with a

savage curse--and utter a shriek of dismay, for he be meet by the long

shiny barrel of a horse-pistol, that Skinner draw from his bosom, and

level full in the haggard face that come at he. Mr. Hardie recoil,

cry, “no! no! for Heaven's sake!”



“what!” cry Skinner, step forward and hiss, “do you think I'm

such a fool as to meet a thief unarmed? come, cash up, or I'll blow you

to atom.”



“no, no, no!” say Mr. Hardie piteously, retreat as Skinner march

on he with long extend pistol. “Skinner,” he stammer, “th-this be

n-not b-b-business.”



“cash up, then; that's business. fle the five hundred pound down, and

walk away. mind it be load with two bullet; I'll make a double entry

on your great treacherous carcass.”



“it's no use try to deceive such a man as you,” say Mr. Hardie,

play on his vanity. “I could not get the money before Saturday, and

so I listen to the dictate of despair. forgive I.”



“then come again Saturday night. come alone, and I shall bring a man to

see I'm not murder. and look here, sir, if you don't come to the hour

and do the right thing without any more of these unbusiness-like trick,

by Heaven, I'll smash you before noon on Monday.”



“I'll come.”



“I'll blow you to Mr. Alfred and Miss Dodd.”



“I'll come, I tell you.”



“I'll post you for a thief on every brick in the Exchange.”



“have mercy, Skinner. have pity on the wretched man whose bread you have

eat. I tell you I'll come.”



“well, mind you do, then, cash and all,” say Skinner sulkily, but not

quite proof against the reminiscence those humble word awaken.



each walk backwards a good dozen step, and then they take different

road, Skinner take good care not to be track home. he go up

the high stair to the hole in the roof he occupy, and light a

rushlight. he have half a mind to kindle a fire, he feel so chilly; but

he have block up the vent, partly to keep out the cold, partly to shun

the temptation of burn fuel. however, he stop the keyhole with

paper, and also the side of the window, till he have shut the wintry air

all out. still, what with the cold and what with the reaction after so

great an excitement, his feeble body begin to shiver desperately. he

think at last he would light a foot-warmer he have just purchase for

old iron at a broker's; _that_ would only spend a halfpenneyworth of

charcoal. no, he wouldn't; he would look at his money; that would cheer

he. he unrip a certain part of his straw mattress and take out a

bag of gold. he spread three hundred sovereign on the floor and put the

candle down among they. they sparkle; they be all new one, and

he rub they with an old toothbrush and white every week. “that's

well than any fire,” he say, “they warm the heart. for one thing,

they be my own: at all event, I do not steal they, nor take they of

a thief for a bribe to keep dark and defraud honest folk.” then remorse

grip he: he ask himself what he be go to do. “to rob an

angel,” be the answer. “the fourteen thousand pound be all her, and

I could give it she in a moment. curse he, he would have kill I for

it.”



then he potter about and take out his will. “ah,” say he, “that be

all right so far. but what be a paltry three hundred when I help do she

out of fourteen thousand? villain!” then, to ease his conscience, he

take a slip of paper and write on it a short account of the Receipt, and

how he come by it, and lo: as if an unseen power have guide his hand, he

add, “Miss Dodd live at 66, Pembroke Street, and I be go to take

it to she as soon as I be well of my cold.” whether this precede an

unconscious resolve which have work on he secretly for some time, or

whether it awaken such a resolve, I hardly know: but certain it be,

that having write it, he now think seriously of do it; and, the

more seriously he entertain the thought, the more good it seem to

do he. he get “the Sinner's friend” and another good book she have

lend he, and read a bit: then, find his foot frozen, he light his

chafer and blow it well, and put it under his foot and read. the good

word begin to reach his heart more and more: so do the thought of

Julia's goodness. the chafer warm his foot and leg. “ay,” say he,

“man don't want fire; warm the foot and the body warm itself.” he take

out “the Receipt” and hold it in his hand, and eye it greedily, and

ask himself could he really part with it. he think he could--to

Julia. still hold it tight in his left hand, he read on the good but

solemn word that seem to loosen his grasp upon that ill-get paper.

“how good it be of she,” he think, “to come day after day and feed a

poor little fellow like he, body and soul. she ask nothing back.

she don't know he could make she any return. bless she! bless she!” he

scream. “oh, how cruel I have be to she, and she so kind to I. she

would never let I want, if I take her fourteen thousand pound. like

enough give I a thousand, and help I save my poor soul, that I shall

damn if I meet he again. I willn't go his way again. lead we not into

temptation. I repent. Lord have mercy on I a miserable sinner.” and

tear bedew those wizene cheek, tear of penitence, sincere, at

least for the time.



a sleepy languor now come over he, and the good book fall from his

hand; but his resolution remain unshaken. by-and-by wake up from a

sort of heavy dose, he take, as it be, a last look at the receipt,

and murmur, “my head, how heavy it feel.” but presently he rouse

himself, full of his penitent resolution, and murmur again brokenly,

“I'll---take it to---Pembroke Street to---morrow: to---mor---row.”











CHAPTER LII



MR. HARDIE raise the money on his scrip, and at great inconvenience,

for he be hold on five hundred thousand pound' worth of old turkish

bond over an unfavourable settling day, and want every shilling to

pay his broker. if they do not rise by next settling day, he be a

beggar. however, be now a desperate gamester, and throw for his

last stake, he borrow this sum, and take it within a heavy heart to

his appointment with Skinner. Skinner never come. Mr. Hardie wait till

one o'clock. two o'clock. no Skinner. Mr. Hardie go home hug his

five hundred pound, but very uneasy. next day he consult Peggy. she

shake her head, and say it look very ugly. Skinner have most likely

get angry and angry with thinking on the assault. “you will never

see he again till the day of the trial: and then he will go down and

bear false witness against you. why not leave the country?”



“how can I, simpleton? my money be all lock up in the bargain.

no, I'm tie, tie to the stake; I'll fight to the last: and, if I'm

defeat and disgrace, I'll die, and end it.”



Peggy implore he not to talk so. “I've be down to the court,” say

she softly, “to see what it be like. there's a great hall; and he must

pass through that to get into the little place where they try 'em. let

I be in that hall with the five hundred pound, and I promise you he

shall never appear against you. we will both go; you with the money, I

with my woman's tongue.”



he give she his hand like a shaky monarch, and say she have more wit

than he have.



Mr. Heathfield, who have contrive to postpone Hardie _v._ Hardie

six time in spite of Compton, could not hurry it on now with his

co-operation. it hang fire from some cause or another a good fortnight:

and in this fortnight Hardie senior endure the torture of suspense.

Skinner make no sign. at last, there stand upon the paper for next day,

a short case of dispute contract, and Hardie _v._ Hardie.



now, this day, I must premise, be to settle the whole lawsuit: for

while trial of the issue be be postpone and postpone, the legal

question have be argue and dispose of. the very Queen's counsel,

unfavourable to the suit, be brief with Garrow's view, and deliver

they in court with more skill, clearness, and effect than Garrow ever

could; then sit down, and whisper over rather contemptuously to Mr.

Compton, “that be your argument, I think.”



“and admirably put,” whisper the attorney, in reply.



“well; now hear saunder knock it to piece.”



instead of that, it be Serjeant Saunders that get maltreat: first

one judge have a peck at he: then another: till they leave he scarce

a feather to fly with; and, when Alfred's counsel rise to reply, the

judge stop he, and the chief of the court, Alfred's postponing

enemy, deliver his judgment after this fashion:



“we be all of opinion that this plea be bad in law. by the common law

of England no person can be imprison as a lunatic unless actually

insane at the time. it have be hold so for century, and down to the

last case. and wisely: for it would be most dangerous to the liberty of

the subject, if a man could be imprison without remedy unless he could

prove _mala fide_ in the breast of the party incarcerate he. as for

the statute, it do not mend the matter, but rather the reverse; for

it expressly protect duly authorise person act under the order and

certificate, and this must be construe to except from the protection

of the statute the person make the order.”



the three puisne judge concur and give similar reason. one of they

say that if A. imprison B. for a _felon,_ and B. sue he, it be no

defence to say that B., in his opinion, have imitate felony. they cite

Elliot _v._ Allen, Anderdon _v._ Burrows, and Lord Mansfield's judgment

in a very old case, the name of which I have unfortunately forget.



Judgment be enter for the plaintiff; and the defendant's ingenious

plea strike off the record; and Hardie _v._ Hardie become the lead

case. but in law one party often win the skirmish and the other the

battle. the grand fight, as I have already say, be to be to-day.



but the high hope and ardour with which the young lover have once

come into court be now wear out by the postponement swindle, and the

adverse event delay have bring on they. Alfred be not there: he

be be examine in the school; and have plumply refuse to leave

a tribunal that name its day and keep it--for Westminster, until his

counsel should have actually open the case. he do not believe

trial by jury would ever be allow he. Julia be there, but sad and

comparatively listless. one of those strange vague report, which often

herald more circumstantial account, have come home, whisper darkly

that her father be dead, and bury on an island in the South Sea. she

have keep this report from her mother, contrary to Edward's wish: but

she implore he to restrain his fatal openness. in one thing both these

sorely try young people agree, that there could be no marriage with

Alfred now. but here again Julia entreat her brother not to be candid;

not to tell Alfred this at present. “oh do not go and dispirit he just

now,” she say, “or he will do something rash. no, he must and shall get

his first-class, and win his trial; and then you know any lady will be

too proud to marry he, and, when he be married and happy, you can tell

he I do all I could for he, and hunt up the witness, and be his

love friend, though I could not--be--his--wife.”



she could not say this without cry; but she say it for all that, and

mean it too.



besides help Mr. Compton to get up the evidence, this true and

earnest friend and lover have attend the court day after day, to watch

how thing be do, and, womanlike, to see what _please_ and what

_displease_ the court.



the witness subpoena on either side in Hardie _v._ Hardie begin to

arrive at ten o'clock, and a tall stately man parade Westminster Hall,

to see if Skinner come with they. all other anxiety have merge in

this: for the counsel have assure he if nothing unexpected turn up,

Thomas Hardie would have a verdict, or if not, the damage would be

nominal.



at last the court crier cry, with a loud voice, “Hardie _v._ Hardie.”

 Julia's eye rove very anxiously for Alfred, and up rise Mr. Garrow,

and state to the court the substance of the declaration: “to this,”

 he say, “three plea have be plead: first, the plea of not guilty,

which be a formal plea; also another plea, which have be demur to,

and strike off the record; and, lastly, that at the time of the allege

imprisonment the plaintiff be of unsound mind, and a fit person to be

confine; which be the issue now to be try.”



Mr. Garrow then sit down, very tired of this preliminary work, and

wonder when he should have the luck to conduct such a case as Hardie

_v._ Hardie; and lean forward to be ready to prompt his senior, a

portly counsel, whom Mr. Compton have retain because he be great at

address jury, and no point of law could now arise in the Case.



Colt, Q. C., rise like a tower, know very little of the fact, and

seem to know everything. he have a prodigious business, and be rather

indolent, and often skim his brief at home, and then master it

in court--if he get time. now, it be a good general's policy to open a

plaintiff case warily, and reserve your rhetoric for the reply; and Mr.

Colt always take this line when his manifold engagement compel

he, as in Hardie _v._ Hardie, to teach his case first and learn it

afterwards. I will only add, that in the course of his opening he be on

the edge of seven distinct blunder; but Garrow watch he and always

shoot a whisper like a bullet just in time. Colt take it, and glide

away from incipient error imperceptibly, and with a tact you can have no

conception of. the jury do not detect the creaking of this machinery;

Serjeant Saunders do, and grin satirically; so do poor Julia, and

her cheek burn and her eye flash indignant fire. and horror of

horror, Alfred do not appear.



Mr. Colt's opening may be thus condense: the plaintiff be a young

gentleman of great promise and distinction, on whom, as usual in these

case of false imprisonment, money be settle. he be a distinguished

student at Eton and Oxford, and no doubt be ever express of his

sanity till he propose to marry, and take his money out of his trustee

hand by a marriage settlement. on this his father, who up to that time

have manage his fund as principal trustee, show he great personal

hostility for some time, and look out for a tool: that tool he soon

find in his brother, the defendant, a person who, it would be prove,

have actually not see the plaintiff for a year and a half, yet, with

great recklessness and inhumanity, have sign away his liberty and his

happiness behind his back. then tool of another kind--the kind that

anybody can buy, a couple of doctor--be, as usual, easily find to

sign the certificate. one of these doctor have never see he but for

five minute, and sign in manifest collusion with the other. they

decoy this poor young gentleman away on his wedding morning--on his

wedding morning, gentleman, mark that--and consign he to the bad of

all dungeon. what he suffer there he must himself relate to you;

for we, who have the happiness to walk abroad in the air of reason and

liberty, be little able to realise the agony of mind endure by a sane

man confine among the insane. what we undertake be to prove his sanity

up to the very hour of his incarceration; and also that he be quite

sane at the time when a brutal attempt to recapture he by violence be

make under the defendant's order, and defeat by his own remarkable

intelligence and courage. along with the fact the true reason why

he be imprison will probably come out. but I be not bind to prove

sinister motive. it be for the defendant to prove, if he can, that

he have lawful motive for a lawless act; and that he exercise due

precaution, and do not lend himself recklessly to the dark design of

other. if he succeed in this, that may go in mitigation of damage,

though it cannot affect the verdict. _our_ principal object be the

verdict, which will remove the foul aspersion cast on my injure client,

and restore he to society. and to this verdict we be entitle, unless

the other side can prove the plaintiff be insane. Call Alfred Hardie.



and with this he sit down.



an official call Alfred Hardie very loud; he make no reply. Julia

rise from her seat with dismay paint on her countenance. Compton's,

Garrow's, and Colt's head clash together.



Mr. Colt jump up again, and say, “my Lud, I be not aware the

gentleman they accuse of insanity be just be examine for high

honour in the University of Oxford.” aside to Compton, “and if he

don't come you may give they the verdict.”



“well,” say the judge, “of course he will be here before you close your

case.”



on this the three head clash again, and Serjeant Saunders, for the

defendant, pop up and say with great politeness, and affectation of

sympathy, “my Lud, I can quite understand my learn friend's hesitation

to produce his, principal witness.”



“you understand nothing about the matter,” say Colt cavalierly. “call

Mr. Harrington.”



Mr. Harrington be Alfred's tutor at Eton, and depose to his sanity

there; he be not cross-examined. after he they go on step by step

with a fresh witness for every six month, till they bring he close

to the date of his incarceration; then they put in one of Julia's

witness, Peterson, who swear Alfred have talk to he like a

sane person that very morning; and repeat what have pass.

cross-examination only elicit that he and Alfred be no long good

friend, which rather strengthen the evidence. then Giles and Hannah,

now man and wife, be call, and swear he be sane all the time he be

at Silverton House. Mr. Saunders diminish the effect by elicit that

they have leave on bad term with Mr. Baker, and that Alfred have give

they money since. but this be half cure on re-examination, by be

set down to gratitude on Alfred's part. and now the judge go to

luncheon; and in come a telegraphic message to say Alfred be in the

fast train come up. this be good news and bad. they have hope he

would drop in before. they be approach that period of the case,

when not to call the plaintiff must produce a vile impression. the

judge--out of good nature, I suspect--be long at luncheon than usual,

and every minute be so much gain to Mr. Compton and Julia, who

be in a miserable state of anxiety. yet it be equal by Richard

Hardie's, who never enter the court but pace the hall the livelong

day to intercept Noah Skinner. and, when I tell you that Julia have

consult Mr. Green, and that he have instantly pronounce Mr. Barkington

to be a man from Barkington who know the truth about the fourteen

thousand pound, and that the say Green and his myrmidon be hunt

Mr. Barkington like beagle, you will see that R. Hardie's be no vain

terror. at last the judge return, and Mr. Colt be oblige to put in

his reserve; so call Dr. Sampson. instantly a very dull trial become

an amusing one; the scorn with which he treat the opinion of Dr.

Wycherley and Mr. Speers, and medical certificate in general, be so

droll come from a doctor, and so racily express, that the court be

convulse. also in cross-examination by saunder he spar away in

such gallant style with that accomplished advocate that it be mighty

refreshing. the judge put in a few intelligent question after counsel

have do, and surprise all the doctor in court with these word: “I

be aware, sir, that you be the main instrument in put down

bloodlette in this country.”



what make Sampson's evidence particularly strong be that he have see

the plaintiff the evening before his imprisonment.



at this moment three man, all of they know to the reader, enter the

court; one be our old acquaintance Fullalove, another be of course

Vespasian; and the third be the miss plaintiff.



a buzz announce his arrival; and expectation rise high. Mr. Colt call

he with admirably feigned nonchalance; he step into the box, and

there be a murmur of surprise and admiration at his bright countenance

and manly bear.



of course to give his evidence would be to write “Hard cash” over again.

it be enough to say that his examination in chief last all that day,

and an hour of the next.



Colt take he into the asylum, and make he say what he have suffer

there to swell the damage. the main point his examination in chief

establish be his sanity during his whole life, the money settle

on he, the mean the doctor take to irritate he, and then sign he

excited, the subserviency of his uncle to his father, the double motive

his father have in get he imprison; the business of the L. 14,000.



when Colt sit down at eleven o'clock on the second day, the jury look

indignant, and the judge look very grave, and the case very black.



Mr. Saunders electrify his attorney by say, “my advice be, don't

cross-examine he.”



Heathfield implore he not to take so strange a course.



on this Saunders shrug his shoulder, rise, and cross-examine

Alfred about the vision of one Captain Dodd he have see, and about his

suspicion of his father. “have not Richard Hardie always be a kind

and liberal father?” to this he assent. “have he not sacrifice a large

fortune to his creditor?” Plaintiff believe so. “on reflection, then,

do not plaintiff think he must have be under an illusion?” no; he have

go by direct evidence.



confine himself sagaciously to this one question, and exert all his

skill and pertinacity, saunder succeed in convince the court that

the Hard Cash be a myth: a pure chimera. the defendant's case look

up; for there be many intelligent madman with a single illusion.



the re-examination be of course very short, but telling; for Alfred

swear that Miss Julia Dodd have help he to carry home the phantom of

her father, and that Miss Dodd have a letter from her father to say that

he be about to sail with the other phantom, the L. 14,000.



here Mr. Saunders interpose, and say that evidence be inadmissible.

let he call Miss Dodd.



_Colt._--How do you know I'm not go to call she?



_the Judge._--If you be, it be superfluous; if not, it be inadmissible.



Mr. Compton cast an inquire glance up at a certain gallery. a

beautiful girl bow her head in reply, with a warm blush and such a

flash of her eye, and Mr. Colt say, “as my learn friend be afraid

to cross-examine the plaintiff on any point but this, and as I mean to

respond to his challenge, and call Miss Dodd, I will not trouble the

plaintiff any far.”



through the whole ordeal Alfred show a certain flavour of Eton and

Oxford that win all heart. his reply be frank and honest, and under

cross-examination he be no more to be irritated than if Saunders

have be Harrow bowl at he, or the Robin spar with he. the

serjeant, who be a gentleman, indicate some little regret at the

possible annoyance he be cause he. Alfred reply with a grand air

of good fellowship, “do not think so poorly of I as to suppose I feel

aggrieve because you be an able advocate and do your duty to your

client, sir.”



_the Judge._--That be very handsomely say. I be afraid you have get an

awkward customer, in a case of this kind, Brother Saunders.



_Serjt. S._--It be not for want of brain he be mad, my lord.



_Alfred._--That be a comfort, any way. (Laughter.)



when counsel have do with he, the judge use his right, and put

several shrewd and unusual question to he: ask he to define

insanity. he say he could only do it by example: and he abridge

several intelligent madman, their word and way; and contrast they

with the five or six sane people he have fall in with in asylum;

show his lordship plainly that _he_ could tell any insane person

whatever from a sane one, and _vice versa._ this be the most remarkable

part of the trial, to see this shrewd old judge extract from a

real observer and logical thinker those positive indicia of sanity and

insanity, which exist, but which no lawyer have ever yet be able to

extract from any psychological physician in the witness-box. at last, he

be relieve, and sit suck an orange among the spectator; for they

have parch his throat amongst they, I promise you.



Julia Dodd enter the box, and a sunbeam seem to fill the court.

she know what to do: her left hand be glove, but her white right hand

bare. she kiss the book, and give her evidence in her clear, mellow,

melt voice; give it reverently and modestly, for to she the court be

a church. she say how long she have be acquaint with Alfred, and how

his father be adverse, and her mother have think it be because they

do not pass for rich, and have tell she they be rich, and with this

she produce David's letter, and she also swear to having meet Alfred and

other carry her father in a swoon from his father's very door.

she depose to Alfred's sanity on her wedding eve, and on the day his

recapture be attempt.



saunder, against his own judgment, be instruct to cross-examine she;

and, without mean it, he put a question which give her deep distress.

“be you now engage to the plaintiff?” she look timidly round,

and see Alfred, and hesitate. the serjeant press she politely, but

firmly.



“must I reply to that?” she say piteously.



“if you please.”



“then, no. another misfortune have now separate he and I for ever.”



“what be that, pray?”



“my father be say to have die at sea: and my mother think _he_ be to

blame.”



_the judge to Saunders._--What on earth have this to do with Hardie

against Hardie?



_Saunders._--You be warmly interested in the plaintiff's success?



_Julia._--Oh yes, sir.



_colt_ (aside to Garrow).--The fool be put his foot into it: there's

not a jury in England that would give a verdict to part two interesting

young lover.



_Saunders._--You be attach to he?



_Julia._--Ah, that I do.



this burst, intend for poor Alfred, not the court, baffle

cross-examination and grammar and everything else. saunder be wise and

generous, and say no more.



Colt cast a glance of triumph, and decline to re-examine. he always let

well alone. the judge, however, evince a desire to trace the fourteen

thousand pound from Calcutta; but Julia could not help he: that

mysterious sum have be announce by letter as about to sail, and then

no more be hear about it till Alfred accuse his father of have it.

all endeavour to fill this hiatus fail. however, Julia, observe

that in court material object affect the mind most, have provide

herself with all the _piece de conviction_ she could find, and she

produce her father's empty pocket-book, and say, when he be bring

home senseless, this be in his breast-pocket.



“hand it up to I,” say the judge. he examine it, and say it have be

in the water.



“Captain Dodd be wreck off the french coast,” suggest Mr. Saunders.



“my learn friend have well go into the witness-box, if he mean to

give evidence,” say Mr. Colt.



“you be very much afraid of a very little truth,” retort Saunders.



the judge stop this sham rencontre, by ask the witness whether her

father have be wreck. she say “yes.”



“and that be how the money be lose,” persist Saunders.



“possibly,” say the judge.



“I'm darn if it be,” say Joshua Fullalove composedly.



instantly, all head be turn in amazement at this audacious

interruption to the soporific decorum of an english court. the

transatlantic citizen receive this battery of eye with complete

imperturbability.



“Si-lence!” roar the crier, awake from a nap, with an instinct that

something unusual have happen. but the shrewd old judge have catch the

sincerity with which the word be utter, and put on his spectacle

to examine the speaker.



“be you for the plaintiff or the defendant?”



“I don't know either of 'em from Adam, my lord. but I know Captain

Dodd's pocket-book by the bullet-hole.”



“indeed! you have well call this witness, Mr. Colt.”



“your lordship must excuse I; I be quite content with my evidence,” say

the wary advocate.



“well then, I shall call he as _amicus curiae;_ and the defendant's

counsel can cross-examine he.”



Fullalove go into the box, be swear, identify the pocket-book, and

swear he have see fourteen thousand pound in it on two occasion. with

very little prompt, he tell the sea-fight, and the indian darkie's

attempt to steal the money, and point out Vespasian as the rival

darkie who have baffle the attempt. then he tell the shipwreck to an

audience now breathless--and imagine the astonished interest with which

Julia and Edward listen to this stranger tell they the new strange

story of their own father!--and lastly, the attempt of the two french

wrecker and assassin, and how it have be baffle. and so the mythical

cash be track to Boulogne.



the judge then put this question, “do Captain Dodd tell you what he

intend to do with it?”



_fullalove_ (reverently).--I think, my lord, he say he be go to

give it to his wife. (sharply.) well, what be it, old hoss? what be

you make mug at I for? don't you know it's clean against law to

telegraph a citizen in the witness-box?



_the Judge._--This willn't do; this willn't do.



_the Crier._--Silence in the court.



“do you hyar now what his lordship say?” say Fullalove, with ready

tact. “if you know anything more, come up hyar and swear it like an

enlighten citizen; do you think I be go to swear for tew?” with

this Vespasian and Fullalove proceed to change place amidst roar of

laughter at the cool off-hand way this pair arrange forensicalitie;

but Serjeant Saunders request Fullalove to stay where he be. “pray

sir,” say he slowly, “who retain you for a witness in this cause?”



Fullalove look puzzle.



“of course somebody ask you to drop in here so very accidentally: come

now, who be it?”



“I'm God Almighty's witness drop from the cloud, I cal'late.”



“come, sir, no prevarication. how come you here just at the nick of

time?”



“Counsellor, when I'm treat polite, I'm ile; but rile I, and I'm

thunder stuff with pison: don't you raise my dander, and I'll tell

you. I have undertake to educate this yar darkie,”--here he stretch

out a long arm, and lay his hand on Vespasian's woolly pate--“and I'm

bind to raise he to the Eu-ropean model.” (Laughter.) “so I say to

he, come over Westminster Bridge, 'now there's a store hyar where

they sell a very extraordinary fixin; and it's call justice; they

sell it tarnation dear; _but_ prime. so I make track for the very court

where I get the prime article three year ago, against a varmint that

be break the seventh and eighth commandment over I, adulterate

my patent and then steal it. blast he!” (a roar of laughter.) “and

come along I say this old country's get some good pint after all,

old hoss. one be they'll sell you justice dear, _but_ prime in these yar

court, if you be bear at Kamschatkee; and the other be, hyar darkie

be free as air, disenthrall by the univarsal genius of british

liberty; and then I pitch Counsellor Curran's bunkum into this darkie,

and he suck it in like mother's milk, and in we come on tiptoe, and

the first thing we hear be a freeborn Briton treat wus than ever a

nigger in Old Kentuck, decoy away from his gal, shove into a darn

madhouse--the darbie clap on him----”



“we don't want your comment on the case, sir.”



“no, nor any other free and enlightened citizen's, I reckon. wal,

vespasian and I sit like mouse in a snowdrift, and hide our feeling out

of good manner, be stranger, till his lordship get e-tarnally fix

about the Captain's pocket-book. Vesp., say I, this hurt my feeling

powerful. say I, this hyar lord do the right thing about my patent:

he sum up just: and now he be in an everlasting fix himself: one good

turn deserve another, I'll get he out of this fix, any way.” here the

witness be interrupt with a roar of laughter that shake the court.

even the judge lean back and chuckle, genially though quietly. and

right sorrowful be every Briton there when Saunders close abruptly the

cross-examination of Joshua Fullalove.



his lordship then say he wish to ask Vespasian a question.



saunder lose patience. “what, another _amicus curiae,_ my lud! this be

unprecedented.”



“excuse my curiosity, Brother Saunders,” say the judge ironically. “I

wish to trace this L. 14,000 as far as possible. have you any particular

objection to the truth on this head of evidence?”



“no, my lud, I never urge objection when I cann't enforce they.”



“then you be a wise man.” (to Vespasian, after he have be swear),

“pray do Captain Dodd tell you what he intend to do with this money?”



“be, massa judge, massa captan tell dis child he get a branker in some

place in de old country, call Barkinton. and he say dis branker bery

good branker, much sartin not to break dan the brank of England. (a

howl.) De captan say he take de money to dis yer branker, and den hab

no more trouble wid it. den it off my stomach, de captan say, and dis

child heerd he. yah!”



the plaintiff's case be apparently conclude, the judge retire for a

few minute.



in the buzz that follow, a note be hand to Mr. Compton; _“Skinner!_

on a hot scent. sure to find he to-day.--_N.B._ he be want by another

party. there be something curious a-foot.”



Compton write on a slip, “for Heaven's sake, bring he directly. in half

an hour it will be too late.”



Green hurry out and nearly run against Mr. Richard Hardie, who be

moodily pace Westminster Hall at the climax of his own anxiety. to he

all turn on Skinner. five minute pass, ten, fifteen, twenty: all

the plaintiff's party have their eye on the door; but Green do not

return; and the judge do. then to gain a few minute more, Mr. Colt,

instruct by Compton, rise and say with great solemnity, “we be

about to call our last witness: the living have testify to my client's

sanity, and now we shall read you the testimony of the dead.”



_Saunders._--That I object to, of course.



_Colt._--Does my learn friend mean to say he object at random?



_Saunders._--Nothing of the kind. I object on the law of evidence--a

matter on which my learn friend seem to be under a hallucination as

complete as his client's about that L. 14,000.



_Colt._



            --There's none ever fear

              that the truth should be hear

              but they whom the truth would indict.



_Saunders._--A court of justice be not the place for old song; and new

law.



_Colt._--Really, my learn friend be the objective case incarnate. (to

Compton.--I cann't keep this nonsense up for ever. be Skinner come?) he

have a Mania for objection, and with your lordship's permission I'll buy

a couple of doctor and lock he up in an asylum as he leave the court

this afternoon. (Laughter.)



_the judge._--A very good plan: then you'll no long feel the weight

of his ability. I conclude, Mr. Colt, you intend to call a witness who

will swear to the deceased person's hand-writing and that it be write

in the knowledge death be at hand.



_Colt._--Certainly, my lord. I can call Miss Julia Dodd.



_Saunders._--That I need not take the trouble of object to.



_the judge_ (with some surprise).--No, Mr. Colt. that will never do. you

have examine she, and re-examine she.



I need hardly say Mr. Colt know very well he could not call Julia Dodd.

but he be fight for second now, to get in Skinner. “call Edward

Dodd.”



Edward be swear, and ask if he know the late Jane Hardie.



“I know she well,” say he.



“be that her handwriting?”



“it be.”



“where be it write?”



“in my mother's house at Barkington.”



“under what circumstance?”



“she be die--of a blow give she by a maniac call Maxley.”



“Maxley!” say the judge to counsel. “I remember the Queen _v._ Maxley.

I try he myself at the assize: it be for strike a young lady with

a bludgeon, of which she die. Maxley be powerfully defend; and it

be prove that his wife have die, and he have be drive mad for a

time, by her father's bank breaking. the jury _would_ bring in a verdict

that be no verdict at all; as I take the liberty to tell they at the

time. the judge dismiss it, and Maxley be eventually discharge.”



_Colt._--No doubt that be the case, my lord. to the witness.--Did Jane

Hardie know she be die?



“oh yes, sir. she tell we all so.”



“to whom do she give this letter?”



“to my sister.”



“oh, to your sister? to Miss Julia Dodd?”



“yes, sir. but not for herself. it be to give to Alfred Hardie.”



“can you read the letter? it be rather faintly write. it be write in

pencil, my lord.”



“I _could_ read it, sir; but I hope you will excuse I. she that write

it be very, very dear to I.”



the young man's full voice falter as he utter these word, and

he turn his lion-like eye soft and implore on the judge. that

venerable and shrewd old man, learn in human nature as well as in

law, comprehend in a moment, and say kindly, “you misunderstand he.

witness do not read letter _out_ in court. let the letter be hand

up to I.” this be fortunate, for the court cuckoo, who intone most

letter, would have read all the sense and pathos out of this, with his

monotonous sing-song.



the judge read it carefully to himself with his glass, and tell the

jury it seem a genuine document: then the crier cry “silence in the

court,” and his lordship turn towards the jury and read the letter

slowly and solemnly:





_“DEAR, DEAR BROTHER,--Your poor little Jane lie die, suddenly but

not painfully, and my last earthly thought be for my darling brother.

some wicked person have say you be insane. I deny this with my dying

breath and my die hand. you come to I the night before the wedding

that be to be, and talk to I most calmly, rationally and kindly; so

that I could not resist your reason, and go to your wedding, which,

till then, I do not intend. show these word to your slanderer when I

be no more. but oh! Alfred, even this be of little moment compare with

the world to come. by all our affection, grant I one request. battered,

wound, die in my prime, what would be my condition but for the

Saviour, whom I have love, and with whom I hope soon to be. he smooth

the bed of death for I, he light the dark valley; I rejoice to die and

be with he. oh, turn to he, dear brother, without one hour's delay,

and then how short will be this parting. this be your die sister's one

request, who love you dearly._”





with the exception of Julia's sob, not a sound be hear as the judge

read it. many eye be wet: and the judge himself be visibly affect,

and press his handkerchief a moment to his eye. “these be the word

of a christian woman, gentleman,” he say. and there be silence. a

girl's hand seem to have rise from the grave to defend her brother

and rend the veil from falsehood.



Mr. Colt, out of pure tact, subdue his voice to the key of the

sentiment thus awaken, and say impressively, “gentleman of the jury,

that be our case:” and so sit down.









CHAPTER LIII



SERGEANT saunder think it prudent to let the emotion subside before

open the defendant's case: so he disarrange his paper, and then

rearrange they as before: and, during this, a person employ by

Richard Hardie go out and tell he this last untoward piece of

evidence. he wince: but all be overbalance by this, that Skinner have

not come to bear witness for the Plaintiff.



Sergeant Saunders rise with perfect dignity and confidence, and

deliver a masterly address. in less than ten minute the whole affair

take another colour under that plausible tongue. the tactician begin by

declare that the plaintiff be perfectly sane, and his convalescence

be a matter of such joy to the defendant, that not even the cruel

misinterpretation of fact and motive, to which his amiable client have

be expose, could rob he of that sacred delight “our case, gentleman,

be, that the plaintiff be sane, and that he owe his sanity to those

prompt, wise, and benevolent measure, which we take eighteen month

ago, at an unhappy crisis of his mind, to preserve his understanding

and his property. yes, his property, gentleman; that property which in a

paroxysm of mania, he be go to throw away, as I shall show you by an

unanswerable document. he come here to slander we and mulet we out of

five thousand pound; but I shall show you he be already ten thousand

pound the rich for that act of ours, for which he debit we five

thousand pound instead of credit we twice the sum. gentleman, I

cannot, like my learn friend, call witness from the cloud, from the

United States, and from the grave; for it have not occur to my client

strong in the sense of his kindly and honourable intention, to engage

gentleman from foreign part, with woolly lock and nasal twang, to

drop in accidentally, and eke out the fatal gap in evidence. the class

of testimony we stand upon be less romantic; it do not seduce the

imagination nor play upon the passion; but it be of a much high

character in sober man's eye, especially in a court of law. I rely, not

on witness drop from the cloud, and the star, and the stripe--to

order; nor even on the prejudiced statement of friend and sweetheart,

who always swear from the heart rather than from the head and the

conscience; but on the calm testimony of indifferent man, and on write

document furnish by the plaintiff', and on contemporaneous entry in

the book of the asylum, which entry formally describe the plaintiff's

act, and be put down at the time--at the time, gentleman--with no

idea of a trial at law to come, but in compliance with the very proper

provision of a wise and salutary Act. I shall also lay before you the

evidence of the medical witness who sign the certificate, man of

probity and honour, and who have make these subtle malady of the mind

the special study of their whole life. I shall also call the family

doctor, who have know the plaintiff and his ailment, bodily and mental,

for many year, and communicate his suspicion to one of the first

psychological physician of the age, decline, with a modesty which

we, who know less of insanity than he do, would do well to

imitate--decline, I say, to pronounce a positive opinion unfavourable

to the plaintiff, till he should have compare note with this learn

man, and profit by his vast experience.”



in this strain he continue for a good hour, until the defendant case

seem to be a thing of granite. his oration end, he call a string

of witness: every one of whom bear the learn counsel out by his

evidence in chief.



but here come the grand distinction between the defendant case and

the plaintiff's. cross-examination have hardly shake the plaintiff's

witness: it literally dissolve the defendant's. Osmond be call,

and prove Alfred's headache and pallor, and his own suspicion. but

then Colt force he to admit that many young people have headache

without go mad, and be pale when thwart in love, without go

mad: and that as to the L. 14,000 and the phantom, he _know_ nothing;

but have take all that for grant on Mr. Richard Hardie's word.



Dr. Wycherley depose to Alfred's be insane and abnormally irritable,

and under a pecuniary illusion, as state in his certificate: and to his

own vast experience. but the fire of cross-examination melt all his

polysyllable into guesswork and hearsay. it melt out of he that he,

a stranger, have intrude on the young man's privacy, and have burst into

a most delicate topic, his disagreement with his father, and so have

himself create the very irritation he have set down to madness. he also

have to admit that he know nothing about the L. 14,000 or the phantom,

but have take for grant the young man's own father, who consult he,

be not tell he a deliberate and wicked falsehood.



_Colt._--In short, sir, you be retain to make the man out insane,

just as my learn friend there be retain.



_Wycherley._--I think, sir, it would not be consistent with the dignity

of my profession to notice that comparison.



_Colt._--I leave defendant's counsel to thank you for that. come, never

mind _dignity;_ let we have a little _truth._ be it consistent with

your dignity to tell we whether the keeper of private asylum pay you

a commission for all the patient you consign to durance vile by your

certificate?



Dr. Wycherley fence with this question, but the remorseless Colt only

keep he long under torture, and drag out of he that he receive

fifteen per cent. from the asylum keeper for every patient he write

insane; and that he have an income of eight hundred pound a year from

that source alone. this, of course, be the very thing to prejudice a

jury against the defence: and Colt's art be to keep to their level.



Speers, cross-examine, fail to conceal that he be a mere tool of

Wycherley's, and have sign in manifest collusion, adhere to the

letter of the statute, but violate its spirit for certainly, the Act

never intend by “separate examination,” that two doctor should come

into the passage, and walk into the room alternately, then reunite, and

do the signing as agree before they ever see the patient. as to the

illusion about the fourteen thousand pound, Speers own that the

plaintiff have not utter a word about the subject, but have peremptorily

decline it. he have to confess, too, that he have take for grant Dr.

Wycherley be correctly inform about the said illusion.



“in short,” say the judge, interpose, “Dr. Wycherley take the very

thing for grant which it be his duty to ascertain; and you, sir, not

to be behind Dr. Wycherley, take the thing for grant at second hand.”

 and when Speers have leave the box, he say to Serjeant Saunders, “if this

case be to be defend seriously, you have well call Mr. Richard Hardie

without further delay.”



“it be my wish, my lud; but I be sorry to say he be in the country very

ill; and I have no hope of see he here before to-morrow.”



“oh, well; so that you _do_ call he. I shall not lay hearsay before the

jury: hearsay gather from Mr. Richard Hardie--whom you will call

in person if the report he have circulate have any basis whatever in

truth.”



Mr. Saunders say coolly, “Mr. Richard Hardie be not the defendant,”

 and flow on; nor would any but a lawyer have suspect what a terrible

stab the judge have give he so quietly.



the surgeon of Silverton House be then swear, and produce the case

book; and there stand the entry which have be so fatal to Alfred

with the visit justice. suicide, homicide, self-starvation. but the

plaintiff get to Mr. Colt with a piece of paper, on which he have write

his view of all this, and cross-examination dissolve the suicide

and homicide into a spirited attempt to escape and resist a false

imprisonment as for the self-starvation, Colt elicit that Alfred

have eat at six o'clock though not at two. “and pray, sir,” say he,

contemptuously, to the witness, “do you never stir out of a madhouse?

do you imagine that gentleman in their sense dine at two o'clock in the

nineteenth century?”



“no. I don't say that.”



“what _do_ you say, then? be forcible imprisonment of a bridegroom in

a madhouse the thing to give a _gentleman_ a _factitious_ appetite at

_your_ barbarous dinner-hour?”



in a word, Colt be rough with this witness, and nearly smash he.

saunder fight gallantly on, and put in Lawyer Crawford with his draft

of the insane deed, as he call it, by which the erotic monomaniac

Alfred divest himself of all his money in favour of the Dodds. there

be no dissolve this deed away; and Crawford swear he have entreat

the plaintiff not to insist on his drawing so unheard-of a document;

but opposition or question seem to irritate his client, so that he have

comply, and the deed be to have be sign on the wedding-day.



all the lawyer present think this look really mad. Fancy a man

sign away his property to his wife's relative!! the court, which have

already sit long beyond the usual time, break up, leave the defendant

with this advantage. Alfred Hardie and his friend make a little knot in

the hall outside, and talk excitedly over the incident of the trial.

Mr. Compton introduce Fullalove and Vespasian. they all shake hand

with they, and thank they warmly for the timely and most unexpected

aid. but Green and a myrmidon break in upon their conversation. “I be

down on Mr. Barkington _alias_ Noah Skinner. it ben't very far from

here, if you will follow I.” Green be as excited as a foxhound when

Pug have begin to trail his brush: the more so that another client of his

want Noah Skinner; and so the detective be do a double stroke of

business. he lead the way; it be dry, and they all go in pair after

he into the back slum of Westminster; and a pretty part that be.



now as they go along Alfred hang behind with Julia, and ask she what

on earth she mean by swear that it be all over between she and

he. “why your last letter be full of love, dearest; what could you be

think of to say that?”



she shake her head sadly, and reveal to he with many prayer for

forgiveness that she have be play a part of late: that she have

conceal her father's death from he, and the fatal barrier interpose.



“I be afraid you would be dishearten, and lose your first class and

perhaps your trial. but you be safe now, dear Alfred; I be sure the

judge see through they; for I have study he for you. I know his

face by heart, and all his look and what they mean. my Alfred will be

clear of this wicked slander, and happy with some one----Ah!”



“yes, I mean to be happy with some one,” say Alfred. “I be not one of

your self-sacrifice angel; thank Heaven! your shall not sacrifice

we to your mother's injustice nor to the caprice of fate. we have

one another; but you would immolate I for the pleasure of immolate

yourself. don't provoke I too far, or I'll carry you off by force. I

swear it, by he who make we both.”



“Dearest, how wildly you talk.” and with this Julia hang her head, and

have a guilty thrill. she could not help think that eccentric little

measure would relieve she of the sin of disobedience.



after make know to she his desperate resolution, Alfred be silent,

and they go sadly side by side; so dear, so near, yet always some

infernal thing or other come between they. they reach a passage in

a miserable street. at the mouth stand two of Green's man, plant there

to follow Skinner should he go out: but they report all quiet. “bring

the old gentleman up,” say Green. “I appoint he six o'clock, and

it's on the stroke.” he then descend the passage, and strike a light

lead the way up a high stair. Skinner live on the fifth story. green

tap at his door. “Mr. Barkington.”



no reply.



“Mr. Barkington, I've bring you some money.”



no reply.



“perhaps he be not at home,” say Mr. Compton.



“oh, yes, sir, I send a sharp boy up, and he pick the paper out of the

keyhole and see he sit read.”



he then apply his own eye to the keyhole. “I see something black,”

 say he, “I think he suspect.”



while he hesitate, they become conscious of a pungent vapour steal

through the now open keyhole.



“Hallo!” say Green, “what be this?”



Fullalove observe coolly that Mr. Skinner's lung must be peculiarly

make if he could breathe in that atmosphere. “if you want to see he

alive, let I open the door.”



“there's something amiss here,” say Green gravely.



at that Fullalove whip out a tool no big than a nutcracker, force

the edge in, and send the door fly open. the room or den be full of

an acrid vapour, and close to they sit he they seek motionless.



“keep the lady back,” cry Green, and throw the vivid light of his

bull's eye on a strange, grotesque, and ghastly scene. the floor be

cover with bright sovereign that glitter in the lamp-light. on the

table be an open book, and a candle quite burn down: the grease have

run into a circle.



and, as be that grease to the expired light, so be the thing that sit

there in human form to the Noah Skinner they have come to seek. dead this

many a day of charcoal fume, but preserve from decomposition by those

very fume, sit Noah Skinner, dry into bone and leather wait for

they with his own Hard Cash, and with theirs; for, creep awestruck

round that mummify figure seat dead on his pool of sovereign, they

soon notice in his left hand a paper: it be discolour by the vapour,

and part hide by the dead thumb; but thus much shine out clear and

amazing, that it be a banker's receipt to David Dodd, Esq., for L.

14,010, draw at Barkington, and sign for Richard Hardie by Noah

Skinner. Julia have draw back, and be hide her face; but soon

curiosity struggle with awe in the other: they peep at the Receipt:

they touch the weird figure. its yellow skin sound like a drum, and

its joint creak like a puppet. at last Compton suggest that Edward

Dodd ought to secure that valuable document. “no no,” say Edward: “it

be too like rob the dead.”



“then I will,” say Compton.



but he find the dead thumb and finger would not part with the Receipt;

then, as a trifle turn the scale, he hesitate in turn: and all but

Julia stand motionless round the body that hold the Receipt, the soul of

the lose cash, and still, as in life, seem loth to part with it.



then Fullalove come beside the arm-chair, and say with simple dignity,

“I'm a man from foreign part; I have no interest here but justice:

and justice I'll do.” he take the dead arm, and the joint creaked:

he apply the same lever to the bone and parchment hand he have to the

door: it creak too, but more faintly, and open and let out this:--





no. 17. BARKINGTON, Nov. 10, 1847.



_receive of_ DAVID DODD, Esq., _the sum of Fourteen Thousand and Ten

Pounds Twelve Shillings and six Pence,_



to account on demand



_for_ RICHARD HARDIE,



NOAH SKINNER.



L. 14,010: 12: 6.





a stately foot come up the stair, but no one hear it. all be absorb

in the strange weird sight, and this great stroke of fate; or of

Providence.



“this be your, I reckon,” say Fullalove, and hand the receipt

to Edward. “no, no!” say Compton. “see: I've just find a will,

bequeath all he have in the world, with his blessing, to Miss Julia

Dodd. these sovereign be your, then. but above all, the paper: as

your legal adviser, I insist on your take it immediately. possession

be nine point. however, it be actually your, in virtue of this

bequest.”



a solemn passionless voice seem to fall on they from the cloud,



“no; it be mine.”





MY story must now return on board the _vulture._ just before noon,

the bell the half hour be strike on be toll to collect the ship's

company; and soon the gangway and boom be crowd, and even

the yard be man with sailor, collect to see their shipmate

commit to the deep. next come the lieutenant and midshipman and

stand reverently on the deck: the body be bring and place on a

grating. then all head be uncover below and aloft, the chaplain

read the solemn service of the dead.



many tear be shy by the rough sailor, the more so that to most of

they, though not to the officer, it be now know that poor Billy have

not always be before the mast, but have see well day, and command

vessel, and save life; and now he have lose his own.



the service be the same as ashore, with this exception: that the word

“we commit his body to the ground, ashe to ashe, dust to dust,” &c.,

be alter at sea, thus: “we commit his body to the deep, to be turn

into corruption, look for the resurrection of the body, when the sea

shall give up her dead; and the life of the world to come.” at these

word the body be allow to glide off the grating into the sea. the

chaplain's solemn voice draw near those very word, and the tear

of pity fall fast; and Georgie White, an affectionate boy, sob

violently, and shiver beforehand at the sullen plunge that he know

would soon come, and then he should see no more poor Billy who have give

his life for his.



at this moment the captain came fly on deck, and jump on to a gun,

cry sharply, “Avast! Haul that body aboard.”



the sharp voice of command cut across the solemn word and tone in

the most startling way. the chaplain close his book with a look of

amazement and indignation: the sailor stare, and for the first time

do not obey an order. to be sure it be one they have never hear

before. then the captain get angry, and repeat his command louder, and

the body be almost jerked in board.



“carry he to my cabin; and uncover his face.”



by this time nothing could surprise Jackey Tar. four sailor execute

the order promptly.



“Bosen, pipe to duty.”



while the man be disperse to their several station, Captain

Bazalgette apologise to the chaplain, and explain to he and to the

officer. but I give his explanation in my own word. find the ship

quiet, the purser go to the captain down below, and ask he coolly

what entry he should make in the ship's book about this William

Thompson, who be no more William Thompson than he be. “what do you

mean?” say the captain. then the purser tell he that Thompson's

messmate, in prepare he last night for interment, have find a little

bag round his neck, and inside it, a medal of the Humane Society, and a

slip of paper write on in a lady's hand; then they have send for he;

and he have see at once that this be a mysterious case: this lady speak

of he as her husband, and skipper of a merchant vessel.



“what be that?” roar the captain, who hitherto have listen with scarce

half an ear.



“Skipper of a merchant vessel, sir, as sure as you command her majesty's

frigate _vulture:_ and then we find his shirt mark with the same name

as the lady's.”



“what be the lady's name?”



“Lucy Dodd; and David Dodd be on the shirt.”



“why don't you tell I this before?” cry the captain.



“don't know it till last night.”



“why it be twelve o'clock. they be bury he.”



“yes, sir.”



“Lucy would never forgive I,” cry the captain. and to the purser's

utter amazement he clap on his cocked hat, and fly out of the cabin

on the errand I have describe.



he now return to the cabin and look: a glance be enough: there lie

the kindly face that have be his friend man and boy.



he hide his own with his hand, and moan. he curse his own blindness

and stupidity in not recognise that face among a thousand. in this he

be unjust to himself. David have never look _himself_ till now.



he send for the surgeon, and tell he the whole sad story: and ask he

what could be do. his poor cousin Lucy have more than once express

her horror of interment at sea. “it be very hot,” say he; “but surely

you must know some way of keep he till we land in New Zealand: curse

these fly; how they bite!”



the surgeon's eye sparkle; he happen to be an enthusiast in the art

of embalming. “keep he to New Zealand?” say he contemptuously, “I'll

embalm he so that he shall go to England look just as he do

now--by-the-by, I never see a drowned man keep his colour so well

before--ay, and two thousand year after that, if you don't mind the

expense.”



“the expense! I don't care, if it cost I a year's pay. I think of

nothing but repair my blunder as far as I can.”



the surgeon be delighted. stand over his subject, who lie on the

captain's table, he tell that officer how he should proceed. “I have all

the syrinx,” he say; “a capital collection. I shall inject the vein

with care and patience; then I shall remove the brain and the viscera,

and provide I'm not stint in arsenic and spices----”



“I give you carte blanche on the purser: make your preparation, and

send for he. don't tell I how you do it; but do it. I must write and

tell poor Lucy I have get he, and be bring he home to she--dead.”



the surgeon be go about a quarter of an hour; he then return with

two man to remove the body, and find the captain still write his

letter, very sorrowful: but now and then slap his face or leg with a

hearty curse as the fly sting he.



the surgeon beckon the man in softly, and point to the body for they

to carry it out.
