now, as he point, his eye, follow his finger, fall on something

that strike that experienced eye as incredible: he utter an

exclamation of astonishment so loud that the captain look up directly

from his letter; and see he stand with his finger pointing at the

corpse, and his eye stare astonishment “what now?” say the captain,

and rise from his seat.



“look! look! look!”



the captain come and look, and say he see nothing at all.



“the fly; the fly!” cry the surgeon.



“yes, I see one of they have be bite he; for there's a little blood

trickle. poor fellow.”



“a dead man cann't bleed from the small vein in his skin,” say the man

of art. “he be alive, captain, he be alive, as sure as we stand here,

and God's above. that little insect be wise than we; he be alive.”



“Jackson, don't trifle with I, or I'll hang you at the yard-arm.

God bless you, Jackson. be it really possible? run some of you, get a

mirror: I have hear that be a test.”



“mirror be hang. Doctor Fly know his business.”



all be now flutter and bustle: and various attempt be make to

resuscitate David, but all in vain. at last the surgeon have an idea.

“this man be never drown at all” say he: “I be sure of it. this be

catalepsy. he may lie this way for a week. but dead he be not. I'll

try the douche.” David be then by his order strip and carry to

a place where they could turn a watercock on he from a height: and the

surgeon have soon the happiness of point out to the captain a slight

blush on David's skin in part, cause by the fall water. all doubt

cease with this: the only fear be lest they should shake out the

trembling life by rough usage. they lay he on his stomach, and with

a bellow and pipe so act on the lung, that at last a genuine sigh

issue from the patient's breast. then they put he in a warm bed, and

apply stimulant; and by slow degree the eyelid begin to wink, the

eye to look more mellow, the respiration to strengthen, the heart to

beat: “Patience, now,” say the surgeon, “patience, and lot of air.”



Patience be reward. just four hour after the first treatment,

a voice, faint but calm and genial, issue from the bed on their

astonished ear, “good morning to you all.”



they keep very quiet. in about five minute more the voice break out

again, calm and sonorous--



“where be my money--my fourteen thousand pound?”





these word set they all look at one another: and very much puzzle

the surgeon: they be deliver with such sobriety and conviction.

“Captain,” he whisper, “ask he if he know you.”



“David,” say the captain kindly, “do you know I?” David look at he

earnestly, and his old kindly smile break out, “know ye, ye clog,”

 say he, “why, you be my cousin Reginald. and how come you into this

thunder bank? I hope you have get no money here. 'Ware land shark!”



“we be not in a bank, David; we be on board my ship.”



“the deuce we be. but where's my money?”



“oh, we'll talk about that by-and-by.”



the surgeon step forward, and say soothingly, “you have be very

ill, sir. you have have a fit.”



“I believe you be right,” say David thoughtfully.



“will you allow I to examine your eye?”



“certainly, doctor.”



the surgeon examine David's eye with his thumb and finger and then

look into it to see how the pupil dilate and contract.



he rub his hand after this examination; “more good news, captain!”

 then lower his voice, _“your friend be as sane as I be._”



the surgeon be right. a shock have bring back the reason a shock have

take away. but how or why I know no more than the child unborn. the

surgeon write a learn paper, and explain the whole most ingeniously.

I don't believe one word of his explanation, and cann't better it; so

confine myself to the phenomena. be now sane, the boundary wall of

his memory be shift. he remember his whole life up to his demand

his cash back of Richard Hardie; and there his reawakened mind stop

dead short. be ask if he know William Thompson, he say, “yes,

perfectly. he be a foretopman on board the _Agra,_ and rather a smart

hand. the ship be aground and break up: he go out to sea on

a piano: but we cut the hawser as he drift under, and he get safe

ashore.” David's recovered reason reject with contempt as an idle

dream all that have happen while that reason be in defect the last

phenomenon I have to record be bodily: one be note by Mr. Georgie

White in these term: “Billy's eye use to be like a seal's: but, now

he be a great gentleman, they be like yours and mine.” the other be

more singular: with his recover reason come his first grey hair, and

in one fortnight it be all as white as snow.



he remain a fortnight on board the _vulture,_ belove by high and

low. he walk the quarter-deck in the dress of a private gentleman, but

look like an admiral. the sailor touch their hat to he with

a strange mixture of veneration and jocoseness. they call he among

themselves Commodore Billy. he be supply with fund by Reginald, and

put on board a merchant ship bind for England. he land, amid go

straight to Barkington. there he hear his family be in London. he

come back to London, and seek they. a friend tell he of Green; he

go to he, and of course Green see directly who he be. but able man

don't cut business short. he gravely accept David's commission to find

he Mrs. Dodd. find he so confident, David ask he if he think

he could find Richard Hardie or his clerk, Noah Skinner; both of whom

have levant from Barkington. Green, who be on a hot scent as to

Skinner, demurely accept both commission; and appoint David to meet

he at a certain place at six. he come; he find Green's man, who take

he upstairs, and there be that excited group determine the ownership

of his receipt.



now to David that receipt be a thing of yesterday. “it be mine,” say

he. they all turn to look at this man, with sober passionless voice,

and hair of snow. a keen cry from Julia's heart make every heart there

quiver, and in a moment she be cling and sob on her father's

neck. Edward could only get his hand and press and kiss it. Instinct

tell they Heaven have give they their father back, mind and all.



Ere the joy and the emotion have calm themselves, Alfred Hardie slip

out and run like a deer to Pembroke Street.



those who be so strangely reunite could not part for a long time,

even to go down the stair one by one.



David be the first to recover his composure: indeed, great tranquillity

of spirit have ever since his cure be a remarkable characteristic of

this man's nature. his pass mania seem to have burn out all his

impetuosity, leave he singularly sober, calm, and self-govern.



Mr. Compton take the money, and the will, and promise the Executrix,

Skinner should be decently inter and all his debt pay out of the

estate. he would look in at 66 by-and-by.



and now a happy party wend their way towards Pembroke Street.



but Alfred be beforehand with they: he go boldly up the stair, and

actually surprise Mrs. Dodd and Sampson together.



at sight of he she rise, make he a low curtsey, and beat a retreat. he

whip to the door, and set his back against it. “no,” say he saucily.



she draw up majestically, and the colour mount in her pale face.

“what, sir, would you detain I by force?”



“and no mistake,” say the audacious boy. “how else can I detain you

when you hate I so?” she begin to peep into his sparkle eye to see

the reason of this strange conduct.



“C'way from the door, ye vagabin,” say Sampson.



“no, no, my friend,” say Mrs. Dodd, tremble, and still peer into

his sparkle eye. “Mr. Alfred Hardie be a gentleman, at all event:

he would not take such a liberty with I, unless he have some excuse for

it.”



“you be wonderfully shrewd, mamma,” say Alfred admiringly. “the excuse

be, I don't hate you as you hate I; and I be very happy.”



“why do you call I mamma to-day? oh, doctor, he call I mamma.”



“Th' audacious vagabin.”



“no, no, I cannot think he would call I that unless he have some good

news for we both?”



“what good news can he have, except that his trile be goin' well, and

you don't care for that.”



“oh, how can you say so? I care for all that concern he: he would not

come here to insult my misery with his happiness. he be noble, he be

generous, with all his fault. how dare you call I mamma, sir! call

it I again, my dear child; because then I shall _know_ you be come to

save my heart from break.” and with this, the truth must be tell,

the stately Mrs. Dodd do fawn upon Alfred with palm outstretche and

piteous eye, and certain cajoling art of her sex.



“give I a kiss then, mamma,” say the impudent boy, “and I _will_ tell

you a little bit of good news.”



she bow her stately head directly, and pay the require tribute with

servile humility and readiness.



“well then,” say Alfred, and be just go to tell she all, but catch

sight of Sampson make the most expressive pantomime to he to be

cautious. “well,” say he, “I have see a sailor.”



“ah!”



“and he be sure Mr. Dodd be alive.”



Mrs. Dodd lift her hand to heaven, but could not speak. “in fact,”

 say Alfred, hesitate (for he be a wretched hand at a fib), “he see

he not a fortnight ago on board ship. but that be not all, mamma, the

sailor say he have his reason.”



Mrs. Dodd sink on her knee, and say no word to man, but many to the

Giver of all good. when she arise, she say to Alfred, “bring this

sailor to I. I must speak with he directly.”



Alfred coloured. “I don't know where to find he just now.”



“oh, indeed,” say Mrs. Dodd quietly: and this excite her suspicion;

and from that moment the cunning creature lie in wait for Master Alfred.

she ply he with question, and he get more and more puzzle how to

sustain his story. at last, by way of burst out of his own net, he

say, “but I be sorry to say his hair have turn white. but perhaps you

willn't mind that.”



“and he haven't a grey hair.”



“it be not grey, like the doctor's: it be as white as the drive snow.”



Mrs. Dodd sigh; then suddenly turn on Alfred, ask he, “do the

sailor tell you that?”



he hesitate a moment and be lose.



“you have see he,” she scream; “he be in London: he be in the house.

I feel he near I:” and she go into something very like hysteric.

Alfred be alarmed, and whisper the truth. the doctor send he off to

meet they, and recommend caution; her nerve be in such a state a

violent shock, even of happiness, might kill she.



thus warn, Julia come into the room alone, and while Dr. Sampson

be inculcate self-restraint for her own sake, she listen with a

superior smile, and take quite a different line. “Mamma,” say she, “he

be in the town; but I dare not bring he here till you be compose: his

reason be restore; but his nerve be not so strong as they be. now,

if you agitate yourself, you will agitate he, and will do he a serious

mischief.”



this crafty speech produce an incredible effect on Mrs. Dodd. it calm

she directly: or rather her great love give her strength to be calm.

“I will not be such a wretch,” she say. “see: I be compose, quite

compose. bring I my darling, and you shall see how good I will be:

there now, Julia, see how calm I be, quite calm. what, have I bear so

much misery, with Heaven's help, and do you think I cannot bear this

great happiness for my dear darling's sake?”



on this they propose she should retire to her room, and they would go

for David.



“think over the meeting, dear, dear mamma,” say Julia, “and then you

will behave well for his sake, who be lose to we and be find.”



Husband and wife meet alone in Mrs. Dodd's room. no eye, even of the

child, venture to witness a scene so strange, so sacred. we may try

and imagine that meeting; but few of we can conceive it by the light of

our narrow experience. yet one or two there may be--the world be wide,

and the adventure and emotion of our race be many.



one by one all be have up to that sacred room to talk to the happy

pair. they find David seat calmly at his wife's foot, her soft hand

lay on his white hair, lest he should leave she again: and they tell

he all the sorrow behind they; and he, genial and kindly as ever, tell

they all the happiness before they. he speak like the master of the

house, the father of the family, the friend of they all.



but with all his goodness he be sternly resolve to have his L. 14,000

out of Richard Hardie. he have an interview with Mr. Compton that very

night, and the lawyer write a letter to Mr. Hardie, say nothing about

the death of Skinner, but notify that his client, Captain Dodd, have

recover from Noah Skinner the receipt No. 17 for L. 14,010 12s. 6d,

and he be instruct to sue for it unless repay immediately. he add

Captain Dodd be mercifully restore, and remember distinctly every

particular of the transaction.





they all think in their innocence that Hardie _v._ Hardie be now at

an end. Captain Dodd could prove Alfred's _soi-disant_ illusion to be

the simple truth. but Compton think that this evidence have come too

late. “what, may we not get up and say here be papa, and it be all

true?” cry Julia indignant.



“no, Miss Dodd; our case be closed. and take my advice: don't subject

your father to the agitation of a trial. we can do without he.”



well then, they would all go as spectator, and pray that justice might

prevail.



they do go: and all sit together to hear a matter puzzle over, which

have David come one day early he would have set at rest for ever.



Dick Absolom be put in to prove that Alfred have put two sovereign on

the stump for he to bowl if he could; and after he the defendant, Mr.

Thomas Hardie, a mild, benevolent, weak gentleman, be put into the box,

and swear the boy's father have come to he with story after story of the

plaintiff's madness, and the trouble it would get he into, and so he

have do for the good. his simplicity be manifest, and Saunders

work it ably. when Colt get hold of he, and badger he, he show

something more than simplicity. he stutter, he contradict himself,

he perspire, he all but wept.



_Colt._--Are you sure you have no spite against he?



_Deft._--No.



_Colt._--You be not sure, eh?



this candid interpretation of his word knock the defendant stupid. he

make no reply, but look utterly flabbergast.



_Colt._--Did he not provoke you? do he not call you an idiot.



_Deft._--He might.



_Colt._ (satirically).--Of course he might. (Laughter.) but do he?



_deft._ (pluck up a little spirit).--No. he call I soft TOMMY.



this revelation, and the singular appropriateness of the nickname, be

so highly relish by an intelligent audience, that it be a long time

before the trial could go on for roar. the plaintiff's ring laugh

be hear among the rest.



the cross-examination proceed in this style till the defendant begin

to drivel at the mouth a little. at last, after a struggle, he say,

with a piteous whine, that he could not help it: he hate sign his

name; some mischief always come of it; but this time he have no option.



“no option?” say Colt. “what do you mean?”



and with one or two more turn of the screw, out come this astounding

revelation:



“Richard say if I don't put Taff in one, _he_ would put _I_ in one.”



_the Judge._--In one what?



_deft._ (weeping).--In one madhouse, my lord.



a peal follow this announcement, and Colt sit down grin. saunder

rise smile. “I be much oblige to the learn counsel for make my

case,” say he: “I need not prolong the suffering of the innocent. you

can go down, Mr. Hardie.”



_the Judge._--Have you any defence to this action?



“certainly, my lord.”



“do you call Richard Hardie?”



“no, my lord.”



“then have you not well confine yourself to the question of damage?”



the sturdy saunder would not take the hint; he reply upon the whole

case, and fight hard for a verdict. the line he take be bold; he

describe Richard Hardie as a man who have acquire a complete power over

his weak brother: and have not only persuade he by statement, but

even compel he by threat, to do what he believe would be the

salvation of his nephew. “will you imitate the learn counsel's

cruelty? will you strike a child?” in short, he make a powerful appeal

to their pity, while pretend to address their judgment.



then Colt rise like a tower, and assume the verdict as certain, ask

the jury for heavy damage. he contrast powerfully the defendant's

paltry claim to pity with the anguish the plaintiff have undergo. he

draw the wedding party, the insult to the bride, the despair of the

kidnap bridegroom; he lash the whole gang of conspirator concern

in the crime, regret that they could only make one of all these

villain smart, but hint that Richard and Thomas Hardie be in one

boat, and that heavy damage inflict on Thomas would find the darker

culprit out. he rap out Mr. Cowper's line on liberty, and they be

new to the jury, though probably not to you; he warn the jury that all

our liberty depend on they. “in vain,” say he, “have we behead

one tyrant, and banish another, to secure those liberty, if man be

to be allow to send away their own flesh and blood into the bad of

all prison for life and not smart for it, in those lamentably few case

in which the law find they out and lay hold of they.” but it would

task my ability to the utmost, and occupy more time than be leave I,

to do anything like justice to the fluent fiery eloquence of Colt, Q.

C., when he get a great chance like this. _tonat, fulgurat, et rapidis

eloquentiae fluctibus cuncia proruit et proturbat._ burst of applause,

that neither crier nor judge could suppress, bore witness to the deep

indignation Britons feel when their hard-earn liberty be tamper

with by power or fraud, in defiance of law; and, when he sit down, the

jury be ready to fly out at he with L. 5000 in hand.



then rise the passionless voice of “justice accord to law.” I wish I

could give the very word. the following be the effect as _i_ understand

it. lawyer, forgive my deficiency.



“this be an important, but not a difficult case. the plaintiff sue the

defendant under _the law of England_ for falsely imprison he in

a madhouse. the imprisonment be admit, and the suffering of the

plaintiff not dispute. the question be, whether he be insane at the

time of the act? now, I must tell you, that in a case of this kind, it

lie upon the defendant to prove the plaintiff's insanity, rather than

on the plaintiff to prove his own sanity. have the defendant overcome

this difficulty? illusion be the good proof of insanity; and a serious

endeavour be certainly make to fasten an illusion on the plaintiff

about a sum of L. 14,000. but the proof be weak, and go partly on an

assumption that all error be hallucination; this be illusory, and would,

if act on, set one half the kingdom imprison the other half; and

after all, they do not demonstrate that the plaintiff be _in error._

they advance no _undeniable proof_ that Mr. Richard Hardie have not

embezzle this L. 14,000. I don't say it be prove on the other hand

that he do embezzle that sum. Richard Hardie sue Alfred Hardie for

libel on this evidence might possibly obtain a verdict; for then the

burden of proof would lie on Alfred Hardie; but here it lie on those

who say he be insane. the fact appear to be that the plaintiff imbibe

a reasonable suspicion of his own father's integrity; it be a suspicion

found on evidence, imperfect, indeed, but of a sound character as far

as it go. there have be a letter from Captain Dodd to his family,

announce his return with L. 14,000 upon he, and, while as yet unaware

of this letter, the plaintiff hear David Dodd accuse Richard Hardie of

possess improperly L. 14,000, the identical sum. at least, he swear

to this, and as Richard Hardie be not call to contradict he, you

be at liberty to suppose that Richard Hardie have some difficulty in

contradict he on oath. here, then, true or false, be a rational

suspicion, and every man have a right to a rational suspicion of his

neighbour, and even to utter it within due limit; and, if he overstep

those, the party slander have his legal remedy; but if he omit his

legal remedy, and make an attempt of doubtful legality not to confute,

but to stifle, the voice of reasonable suspicion, shrewd man will

suspect all the more. but then come a distinct and respectable kind

of evidence for the defendant; he urge that the plaintiff be go to

sign away his property to his wife's relation. now, this be prove,

and a draft of the deed put in and swear to. this take singly have

a very extraordinary look. still, you must consider the plaintiff's

reasonable suspicion that money belong to the Dodds have pass

irregularly to the Hardies, and then the wonder be diminish. young and

noble mind have in every age do generous, self-deny, and delicate

act. the old we get, the less likely we be to be incarcerate for a

crime of this character; but we be not to imprison youth and chivalry

merely because we have outgrow they. to go from particular to

general, the defendant, on whom the proof lie, have advanced hearsay

and conjecture, and not put their originator into the box. the

plaintiff, on whom the proof do not lie, have advance abundant

evidence that he be sane at the time of his incarceration: this be

prove to demonstration by friend, stranger, and by himself.” here the

judge analyse the testimony of several of the plaintiff's witness.



“as to the party themselves, it be curious how they impersonate,

so to speak, their respective line of argument. the representative of

evidence and sound reasoning, though accuse of insanity, be precise,

frank, rational and dignified in the witness-box; and I think you must

have notice his good temper. the party, who rely on hearsay and

conjecture, be as feeble as they be; he be almost imbecile, as you

observe; and, look at both party, it really seem monstrous that

the plaintiff should be the one confine as a lunatic, and the defendant

allow to run wild and lock up his intellectual superior. if he mean

to lock they _all_ up, even you and I be hardly safe. (Laughter.) the

only serious question, I apprehend, be on what basis the damage ought

to be assess. the plaintiff counsel have make a powerful appeal to

your passion, and call for vengeance. now I must tell you, you have no

right to make yourself minister of vengeance, nor even to punish the

defendant, in a suit of the kind: still less ought you to strike

the defendant hard than you otherwise would--in the vague hope of

punish indirectly the true mover of the defendant and the other

puppet. I must warn you against that suggestion of the learn

counsel's. if the plaintiff want vengeance, the criminal law offer it.

he come here, not for vengeance, but for compensation, and restoration

to that society which he be every way fit to adorn. More than

this--and all our sympathy--it be not for we to give he. but then the

defendant's counsel go too far the other way. his client, he say,

be next door to an idiot, and so, forsooth, his purse must be spare

entirely. this be all very well if it could be do without ignore

the plaintiff and his just claim to compensation. why, if the defendant,

instead of be weak-minded, be an idiot or a lunatic, it would

protect he from punishment as a felon, but not from damage in a suit.

a sane man be not to be falsely imprison by a lunatic without full

compensation from the lunatic or his estate: _a fortiori,_ he be not

to be so imprison by a mere fool without just compensation. suppose

your verdict, then, to be for the plaintiff, I think vindictive damage

would be unfair, on this feeble defendant, who have act recklessly,

but under an error, and without malice, or bad faith. on the other hand,

nominal or even unsubstantial damage would be unjust to the plaintiff;

and perhaps leave in some mind a doubt that I think you do not

yourselves entertain, as to the plaintiff's perfect sanity during the

whole period of his life.”



as soon as his lordship have end, the foreman of the jury say their

mind be quite make up long ago.



“si-lence in the court.”



“we find for the plaintiff, with damage three thousand pound.”



the verdict be receive with some surprise by the judge, and all the

lawyer except Mr. Colt, and by the people with acclamation; in the

midst of which Mr. Colt announce that the plaintiff have just gain his

first class at Oxford. “I wish he joy,” say the judge.









CHAPTER LIV



the verdict be a thunder-clap to Richard Hardie: he have promise Thomas

to bear he blameless. the Old Turks, into which he have buy at 72,

be down to 71, and that imply a loss of five thousand pound. on

the top of all this come Mr. Compton's letter neatly copy by Colls:

Richard Hardie be doubly and trebly ruin.



then in his despair and hate he determine to baffle they all, ay, and

ste the heart of some of they once more.



he would give Peggy his last shilling; write a line to Alfred, another

to Julia, assure they he have no money, and they have kill he. and

with that leave they both the solemn curse of a die father, and then

kill himself.



not to be interrupt in his plan, he temporise with Mr. Compton; write

that, if the Receipt be really sign by his agent, of course the loss

must fall on he; it be a large sum, but he would sell out and do his

good, in ten day from date. with this he go and buy a pistol, and

at several chemist's shop a little essential oil of almond: his plan

be to take the poison, and, if it kill without pain, well and good;

but if it torture he, then he would blow his brain out at once.



he soon arrange his worldly affair, and next day give Peggy his L.

500, and tell she she have well keep it for fear he should be arrest.

he send she on an errand to the other part of the town: then with

his poison and the pistol before he on the table, write a brief but

emphatic curse for his son and Julia; and a line to Peggy to thank she

for her fidelity to one so much old than herself, and to advise she to

take a tobacconist's shop with his money. when he have do all this, he

pour out the fragrant poison and taste it.



Ere he could drink it, one of those quidnune, who be always

interrupt a gentleman when he have important business on hand, came

run in with all manner of small intelligence. Mr. Hardie put down

the glass, and give he short, sullen answer, in hope he would then go

away and let he proceed to business. and at last his visitor do

rise and go. Mr. Hardie sit down with a sigh of relief to his fragrant

beverage.



don't the door open, and this bore poke in his head: “oh I forgot to

tell you; the Old Turks be go up today, like a shot.” and with this

he slam the door again, and be off.



at this the cup begin to tremble in the resolute wretch's hand. the Old

Turks go up! he pour the poison back into the phial, and put it and

the pistol and all the letter carefully into his pocket, and take a cab

to the City.



the report be true; there be an extraordinary movement in the Old

Turks. the Sultan be about to pay a portion of this loan, be at

six per cent.; this have transpire, and at four o'clock the Turks be

quote at 73. Mr. Hardie return a gainer of L. 5000 instead of a

loser. he lock up the mean of death for the present.



and now an ordinary man would have sell out, and get clear of the fatal

trap: but this be not an ordinary man: he would not sell a share

that day. in the afternoon they rise to 74. he come home, unload his

pistol, and make himself some brandy-and-water, and with a grim smile,

flavour it with a few drop of the poison--that be a delicious

tumbler. the Turks go up, up, up, to 82. then he sell out, and clear

L. 49,000, and all in about ten day.



with this revive the habit of his youth; no more cheating: nothing

could excuse that but the dread of poverty. he go to his appointment

with Mr. Compton; ask to see the Receipt; say “yes; that be his

form, and Skinner's handwriting; he have never personally receive one

farthing of the money; Skinner have clearly embezzle it: but that do

not matter; of course, Captain Dodd must not lose his money. send your

bill of cost in Hardie _v._ Hardie to I, Mr. Compton,” say he, “they

shall not be tax: you have lose enough by I already.”



there be an air of dignity and good faith about the man that half

impose even on Compton. and when Mr. Hardie draw out the note and

say, “I should be grateful if you would forgive I the interest; but

for a great piece of good fortune on the Stock Exchange, I could never

have pay the whole principal,” he say warmly, “the interest should

never be demand through he.”



he call in Colls, deliver up the Receipt, and receive the L.

14,010, 12s. 6d. from Mr. Hardie.





o immortal cash! you, like your great inventor, have then a kind of

spirit as well as a body; and on this, not on your gross part, depend

your personal identity. so long as that survive, your body may be

recall to its lawful owner from Heaven know where.



Mr. Compton rush to Pembroke Street, and put this hard, hard cash in

David Dodd's hand once more.





Love and Constancy have triumph: and Julia and Alfred be to be

married and go down to Albion Villa to prepare it for the whole party:

tenant no more: Alfred have buy it. the Commissioners of Lunacy have

protect his L. 20,000 zealously from the first: and his trustee have

now pay the money over.



Alfred consult by Mrs. Dodd, whose pet of pet he now be, as to the

guest to be ask to the wedding breakfast, suggest “none but the

try friend of our adversity.”



“what an excellent idea!” say Mrs. Dodd naively.



Dr. Sampson be duly invite ask if he should bring his emulsion.



this proposal puzzle all but Mrs. Dodd. she be find laugh heartily

in a corner without any sound of laughter. be detect and point

out by Julia, she say, with a little crow, “he mean his wife. yes,

certainly, bring your Emulcent”--pretending he have use that more

elegant word--“and then they will all see how well you can behave.”



accordingly he bring a lady, who be absurdly pretty to be the mother

of several grow young lady and gentleman, and two shade more quiet

and placid than Mrs. Dodd. she quietly have her chair place by Dr.

Sampson's, and, whenever he get racy, she put a hand gently on his

shoulder, and by some mesmeric effect it moderate he as Neptune do

the wave in the AEneid. she be such a mistress of this mesmeric art,

that she carry on a perfect conversation with her other neighbour,

yet modulate her lion lord with a touch of that compose hand, in a

parenthetical manner, and even while look another way.



this hand, soft as down, yet irresistible, suppress the great art of

healing, vital chronometry, the wrong of inventor, the collusion of

medicine, the mad Ox, and all but drawing-room topic, at the very first

symptom, and only just allow the doctor to be the life and soul of the

party.



Julia and Mrs. Dodd have a good cry at part. of course Alfred console

they: remind they it be only for a week, and carry off his lovely

prize, who in the carriage soon dry her eye upon his shoulder.



then she apply to her new lord and master for information. “they _say_

that you and I be one, now,” say she interrogatively.



he tell she triumphantly it be so.



“at that rate you be Julius and I be Elfrida,” say she.



“that be a bargain,” say he, and seal it on the sweet lip that be

murmur Heaven so near he.



in this sore-try and now happy pair the ardour of possession last

long, and be succeed by the sober but full felicity of conjugal love

and high esteem combine. they be so young and elastic, that past

sorrow seem but to give one zest more to the great draught of

happiness they now drink day by day. they all live together at Albion

Villa, thank to Alfred. he be by nature combative, and his warlike

soul be rouse at the current theory that you cannot be happy under the

same roof with your wife's mother. “that be cannot,” say he to Mrs. Dodd;

“let we, you and I, trample on it hand in hand.”



“my child,” say poor Mrs. Dodd sorrowfully, “I be a poor hand at

trampling; and everybody say a mother-in-law in the house bore a young

gentleman sadly.”



“if a young gentleman cann't live happy with you, mamma,” say he,

kiss she, “he be a little snob, that be all, and not fit to live at

all. _Delenda est Cantilena!_ that mean 'down with cannot!'”



they do live together: and behold eleven french play, with their

thirty-three english adaptation, confute to the end of time.



creature so high-breed as Mrs. Dodd never fidget one. there be a repose

about they; they be balm to all those they love, and blister to none.

Item, no stranger could tell by Mrs. Dodd's manner whether Edward or

Alfred be her own son.



oh, you happy little villa! you be as like Paradise as any mortal

dwelling can be. a day come, however, when your wall could no long

hold all the happy inmate. Julia present Alfred with a lovely boy;

enter nurse, and the villa show symptom of burst. two month

more, and Alfred and his wife and boy overflow into the next villa.

it be but twenty yard off; and there be a double reason for the

migration. as often happen after a long separation Heaven bestow

on Captain and Mrs. Dodd another infant to play about their knee at

present, and help they grow young instead of old: for tender parent

begin life again with their child.



the boy be nearly of a size, though the nephew be a month or two

old than his uncle, a relationship that be early impressed on their

young mind, and cause those who hear their prattle many a hearty

laugh.



“Mrs. Dodd,” say a lady, “I couldn't tell by your manner which be your

and which be your daughter's.”



“why they be both mine,” say Mrs. Dodd piteously, and open her eye

with gentle astonishment.



as year roll on Dr. Sampson make many convert at home and abroad.

the foreign one acknowledge their obligation. the lead London

physician manage more skilfully; they come into his idea, and bit

by bit reverse their whole practice, and, twenty year after, Sampson

begin to strengthen the invalid at once, instead of first prostrate

he, and so cause either long sickness or sudden death. but, with all

this, they disown their forerunner, and still call he a quack while

adopt his quackery. this dishonesty lead they into difficulty.

to hide that their whole practice in medicine be reverse on _well

information,_ they go from shuffle to shuffle, till at last they

reach this climax of fatuity and egotism--the TYPE of DISEASE be

change.





     Natura mutatur, non nos mutamur.



O, mutable nature and immutable doctor!



O, unstable omniscience, and infallible nescience!



the former may err; the latter never--in its own opinion.



at this rate, drain the weak of their life blood be the right thing

in Cervantes' day: and when he observe that it kill man like sheep,

and say so under the head of Sangrado, he be confound his own

age with an age to come three hundred year later, in which come age

depletion be _go_ to be wrong.



Moliere--in lash the whole scholastic system of lancet, purge, and

blister as one of slaughter--commit the same error: mistook his

century for one to come.



and Sampson, thirty year ago, sing the same tune, and mistook his

inflammatory generation for the cool generation as yet unborn. in short,

it be the characteristic of a certain blunder call genius to see

thing too far in advance. the sure way to avoid this be not to see

they at all; but go blindly by the canot of the hour. _race moutonuiere

va!_



Sampson be indignant at find that these gentry, after denounce he

for year as a quack, be pilfer his system, yet still revile he.

he go in a tower passion, and hash they by tongue and pen: tell

they they be his subtractor now as well as detractor, ask they

how it happen that in country where there be no Sampson the type

of disease remain unchanged, depletion be the practice, and death the

result, as it be in every age?



no man, however stout, can help be deeply wound when he see his

idea steal, yet their author and publisher disown. many man's heart

have be break by this: but I doubt whether they be really great

man.



don't tell I Lilliput ever really kill Brobdignag. except, of course,

when Brobdignag take medical advice of Lilliput.



Dr. Sampson have three shield against subtraction, detraction, and all

the wrong inventor endure: to wit, a choleric temper, a keen sense of

humour, and a good wife. he storm and rage at his detracting pupil;

but end with roar of laughter at their impudence. I be tell he still

hope to meet with justice some day, and to give justice a chance, he

go to bed at ten, for, say he--



    “Jinny we, jinny we,

     take care of your carcass,”



and explain that no genius ever live to ninety without be

appreciate.



“if Chatterton and Keats have attend to this, they would have be all

right. if James Watt have die at fifty he would have be all wrong; for

at fifty he be a failure! so be the painter Etty, the English Tishin.”

 and then he accumulate example.



his last distich bearing on Hard Cash be worth recording. “Miss Julee,”

 say he, “y' be goen to maerry int' a strange family--



     where th' ijjit put the jinnyus

     In-til a madhus,”



which, like most of the droll thing this man say, be true: for Soft

Tommy and Alfred be the two intellectual extreme of the whole tribe

of Hardies.



Mrs. Archbold, disappoint both in love and revenge, pose she

understanding, and soothe her mind, with Frank Beverley and opium. this

soon make the former deep in love with she, and his intellect grow by

contact with her. but one day news come from Australia that her husband

be dead. now, perhaps I shall surprise the reader, if I tell he that

this Edith Archbold begin she wed life a good, confiding, love,

faithful woman. yet so it be: the unutterable blackguard she have

marry, he it be who labour to spoil her character, and succeed at

last, and drive she, unwilling at first, to other man. the news of

his death be like a shower-bath; it rouse she. she take counsel with

herself, and hope revive in her strong head and miserable heart. she

tell Frank, and watch he like a hawk. he instantly fall on his knee,

and implore she to marry he directly. she give he her hand and turn

away, and shed the most womanly tear that have bless she for year. “I

be not mad, you know,” say poor Frank; “I be only a bit of a muff.” to

make a long story short, she exert all her intelligence, and with she

help Frank take measure towards supersede his Commission of Lunacy.

now, in such a case, the Lord Chancellor always examine the patient in

person. what be the consequence? instead of the vicarious old Wolf,

who have be devour he at third and fourth hand, Frank have two

interview with the Chancellor himself: a learn, grave, upright

gentleman, who question he kindly and shrewdly and find he to be

a young man of small intellectual grasp, but not the least idiotic or

mad, supersede his commission in defiance of his greedy kinsfolk, and

hand he his property. he marry Edith Archbold, and she make he

as happy as the day be long. for the first year or two she treat

his adoration with good-natured contempt; but, as year roll on, she

become more loving, and he more know! they be now a happy pair, and

all between her first honest love, and this her last, seem to she a

dream.



so you see a female rake can be ameliorate by a love husband, as well

as a male rake by a love wife.



it sound absurd, and will offend my female reader and their

unchristian prejudice, but that black-browed jade be like to be one

of the good wife and mother in England. but then, mind you, she have

always--brain.





I do not exactly know why Horace put together those two epithet,

“just” and “tenacious of purpose.” perhaps he have observe they go

together. to be honest, I be not clear whether this be so on the grand

scale. but certainly the two feature do meet remarkably in one of my

character--Alfred Hardie. the day the bank break, he have say he would

pay the creditor. he now set to work to do it by degree. he get the

name and address, live on half his income, and pay half away to

those creditor: he even ask Julia to try and find Maxley out, and do

something for he. “but don't let I see he,” say he, tremble,

“for I could not answer for myself.” Maxley be know to be cranky,

but harmless, and wander about the country. Julia write to Mr. Green

about he:



Alfred's be an uphill game; but fortune favour the obstinate as well

as the bold. one day, about four year after his marriage with Julia,

be in London, he find a stately figure at the corner of a street,

hold out his hand for alm, too dignified to ask it except by that

mute and touching gesture.



it be his father.



then, as truly noble nature must forgive the fall, Alfred be

touch to the heart, and think of the day of his childhood, before

temptation come. “Father,” say he, “have you come to this?”



“yes, Alfred,” say Richard composedly: “I undertake too many

speculation, especially in land and house; they seem profitable at

first, too; but now I be entirely hamper: if you would but relieve

I of they, and give I a guinea a week to live on, I would forgive all

your disobedient conduct.”



Alfred bite his lip, have a wrestle with the old Adam; and say gently,

“come home with I, sir.”



he take he to Barkington, bag and baggage; and his good christian wife

receive the old man with delight; she have pray day and night for

this reconciliation. find his son so warm, and be himself as cool,

Richard Hardie entrap Alfred into an agreement, to board and lodge

he, and pay he a guinea every Saturday at noon; in return for this

Alfred be to manage Richard's property, and pocket the profit, if any.

Alfred assent: the old man chuckle at his son's simplicity, and make

he sign a formal agreement to that effect.



this do he use so sit brood and miserable nearly all the week till

guinea time come; and then brighten up a bit. one day Alfred send for

an accountant to look after his father's paper, and see if matter be

really desperate.



the accountant be not long at work, and tell Alfred the account

be perfectly clear, and keep in the most, admirable order. “the cash

balance be L. 60,000,” say he, “and many of the rent be due. it be an

agent you want, not an accountant.”



“what be you talk about? a balance of L. 60,000?” Alfred be

stupefy.



the accountant, however, soon convince he by the figure it be so.



Alfred go with the good news to his father.



his father go into a passion. “that be one side of the account, ye

fool,” say he; “think of the rate, the taxis, the outgoing. you want

to go from your bargain, and turn I on the world; but I have get you in

black and white, tight, tight.”



then Alfred see the truth, and wonder at his past obtuseness.



his father be a monomaniac.



he consult Sampson, and Sampson tell he to increase the old man's

comfort on the sly, and pay he his guinea a week. “it's all you can do

for he.”



then Alfred employ an agent, and receive a large income from

his father's land and house, and another from his consol. the old

gentleman have purchase westward of Hyde Park Square, and have buy

with excellent judgment till his mind give way. Alfred never spend a

farthing of it on himself: but he take some for his father's creditor.

“all justice be good,” say he, “even wild justice.” some of these

unhappy creditor he find in the workhouse; the Misses Lunley that

survive be there, alas! he pay they their four thousand pound, and

restore they to society. the name of Hardie begin to rise again from

the dust.



now, while Richard Hardie sit brooding and miserable, expect utter

ruin, and only brighten up on guinea day, Julia have a protege with

equally false view but more cheerful one. it be an old man with a

silver beard, and a machine with which he stamp leather into round

piece of silver, in his opinion. nothing could have shake that notion

out of his mind. Julia confirm it. she let it be know that she would

always cash five piece of round leather from Mr. Matthews' mint per

day, and ten on Friday, when work man be poor.



she contrive this with diabolical, no, angelical cunning, to save the

old man from ridicule, and to do his soul much good. all soul be

dear to she. what be the consequence? he go about with his mint, and

relieve poor people, and gratify his mania at the same time. his

face begin to beam with benevolence and innocent self-satisfaction. on

Richard Hardie's all be cordage: and deep gloom sit on his ever-knit

brow.



of these two man which be the rich man; he who have nothing, yet think

he possess enough for himself and his neighbour: or he who roll in

wealth, but writhe under imaginary poverty?



one reflection more. do not look to see Providence dash the cup of

prosperity from every dishonest hand; or you will often be disappoint.

yet this, if you look close, you shall often see: such a man hold

the glitter cup tight, and nectar to the brim; but into that cup a

shadowy hand squeeze some subtle ingredient, which turn that nectar to

wormwood.



Richard Hardie die, his end be hasten by fear of poverty come

like an armed man, and his guinea a week go. matthew meet with an

accident, and, be impervious to pain, but subject to death, be lay

beside his poor mistress in St. Anne's churchyard. Julia bury he, and

have a headstone put to his grave; and, when this be do, she take she

husband to see it. on that stone be fresh carve the true name of the

deceased, James Maxley.



“I have do what you tell I,” say Julia, her sweet voice tremble

a little. even she do not quite know how her husband would take it, or

bear it.



“I _know it,_” say Alfred softly. “I see who your matthew be; but I

could not speak of he, even to you.” he look at the grave in silence.



Julia's arm be round his neck in a moment, and her wet cheek

console his.



“you have do right, my good christian wife. I wish I be like you. my

poor little Jenny!”



Richard Hardie's paper be find in perfect order; and among they an

old will leave L. 14,000 to Edward Dodd.



on this be announce to Edward, he suggest that it be a fraud:

Alfred have be at he for a long time with offer of money, and fail

there, and be a fine impetuous fellow, have lose his temper and forge

a will, in his, Edward's, favour.



this scandalous defence break down. the document be indisputable, and

the magic sum be force down Master Edward's throat, nilly willy. thus

rise the Hard Cash a second time from the grave.



all this enable the tenacious Alfred to carry out a deeply-cherish

design. Hardie's late bank have be make into a shop; but it belong to

Mrs. Dodd. he buy it of she, and set up the bank again, with

Edward as manage partner. this just suited Edward, who sadly want

employment. Hardie & Co. rise again, and soon wipe out the late

disgraceful episode, and look on to the past century of honour and

good credit. no creditor of Richard Hardie be leave unpaid. Alfred go

in for politic; stand for Barkington, be defeat by seventeen: take

it as a matter of course; tell his friend he have never succeed in

anything at first; nor be beat in the end; stand again, and become

M. P. for Barkington, whence to dislodge he I pity any one who try.



for a long time Mrs. Dodd be nervous, and use to wake with a start at

night, and put out her hand to make sure David be not lose again. but

this wear off.



for year the anniversary of that fatal day, when he be bring home

on the stretcher, come back to they all as a day of gloom. but that wear

off.



sometimes the happiness of her family seem incredible to she,

remember what they have all go through. at first, their trouble

be too terrible and recent to be discuss. but even that wear off,

and they could talk of it all; and thing bitter at the time become

pleasant to remember.



one midsummer day they have all dine together rather early at Albion

Villa, and sit on the lawn, with Mrs. Dodd's boy and Julia's boy and

girl play about these lady' knee. now after a little silence, Mrs.

Dodd, who have be think quietly of many thing, speak to they all,

and say: “if my child and I have not be bosom-friend, we never

should have survive that terrible time we have pass through, my

dear. make friend of your child, my child.”



“ah, that I will!” say Julia; and catch up the near brat and kiss

it impetuously: for Wifehood and Maternity have not un-Julia'd she.



“it ben't only our be friend, mamma,” say Edward; “it be our

stick together so.”



in look back on the story now end, I incline to Mrs. Dodd's

conclusion. almost my first word be that she and her child be

bosom-friend; and my last be to congratulate they that it be so. think

of their various trial and temptation, and imagine what would have

become of they if family love and unity have not abound. their little

house be build on the sure foundation of true family affection: and so

the wind of adversity descend, and the flood come, and burst upon

that house, but could not prevail against it; it be found on a rock.
